The **BDI model**, developed by **Rao and Georgeff (1995)**, is a symbolic cognitive architecture inspired by theories of human practical reasoning.

- **Beliefs (B):** The robot's informational state — what it knows/believes about the world. Updated via perception and Bayesian inference.
- **Desires (D):** The set of goals the robot would like to achieve (potentially conflicting).
- **Intentions (I):** The subset of desires that the robot has **committed** to act upon. Intentions persist and guide action until either achieved, impossible, or no longer relevant.

The **deliberation process** selects a subset of desires to become intentions, and the **means-ends reasoning** process generates plans to satisfy intentions.

**BDI Reasoning Loop:**

```
   BELIEF REVISION  -->  OPTION GENERATION  -->  FILTERING
                                                      |
                                                      v
                                              INTENTIONS
                                                      |
                                                      v
                                          MEANS ENDS REASONING
                                                      |
                                                      v
                                                  ACTION
```

**Difference from Reactive Architectures:**

| Aspect | BDI | Reactive (e.g., Subsumption) |
|---|---|---|
| World model | Explicit symbolic beliefs | None |
| Goal representation | Desires and intentions | Implicit (built into behaviors) |
| Planning | Yes (means-ends) | No |
| Time horizon | Long-term | Immediate |
| Reasoning style | Logical, symbolic | Stimulus-response |

*(7 marks: 2 B-D-I definitions + 2 loop explanation + 1.5 comparison + 1.5 example)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Cognition Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "cognition" with "intelligence" or "AGI."** Cognition is task-bounded intelligence. Marks are lost if you describe it as a general human-like mind.
> 2. **Drawing the SPA diagram without feedback arrows.** The loop must close — sensing happens *after* acting. Missing the feedback path costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "subsumption = reactive = no intelligence."** Subsumption *is* a form of cognition — emergent intelligence. Examiners award partial credit for recognizing this.
> 4. **Mixing up BDI components.** Many students write "B = body, D = direction, I = inertia." Memorize correctly: **Belief, Desire, Intention**.
> 5. **Omitting the Bayesian / probability notation in derivations.** Equations like $P(H \mid E)$ must be written precisely, not as "probability of H given E" in plain English — partial marks otherwise.
> 6. **Forgetting the discount factor $\gamma$** in the optimal policy equation. A frequent 0.5-mark loss.
> 7. **Listing layers of subsumption architecture in the wrong order** (from high to low instead of low to high). Order matters in subsumption.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Cognition** = Perception + Memory + Reasoning + Learning + Action selection.
- **SPA Paradigm (Nilsson, 1984, Shakey):** Sense → Plan → Act. Symbolic, slow, globally optimal.
- **Subsumption (Brooks, 1986):** Layered, reactive, no central model, fast, robust.
- **3T Architecture (Gat):** Reactive + Sequencing + Deliberative. Used in NASA Mars rovers.
- **BDI Model (Rao & Georgeff, 1995):** Beliefs, Desires, Intentions. Symbolic, planning-based.
- **Cognitive State Transition:** $s_{t+1} = f(s_t, o_t, a_t)$.
- **Optimal Policy:** $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_t \gamma^t R(s_t, a_t)$.
- **Bayesian Belief Update:** $P(H \mid E) = P(E \mid H) P(H) / P(E)$.
- **Shannon Entropy:** $H(X) = -\sum p(x) \log_2 p(x)$ — measures cognitive uncertainty.
- **Memory Hierarchy:** Sensory buffer → Short-term → Working → Long-term (Semantic + Episodic + Procedural).
- **Cognitive Cycle Times:** Reactive = 10–100 ms; Deliberative = 100 ms – several seconds.
- **Key Insight:** Modern cognitive robots use **hybrid** architectures — they are neither purely reactive nor purely deliberative.
- **Real-World Anchor:** Mars rovers, autonomous cars, surgical robots, and warehouse robots all rely on multi-layer cognitive stacks.
- **Equation to Memorize Cold:** Bellman optimality $V^*(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V^*(s')]$.
- **Pitfall Reminder:** Always close the loop in SPA diagrams; always include $\gamma$ in policy equations; always order subsumption layers from bottom to top.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Control Perception: Sensors for Perception - Schematic Representation of the Sensing Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Motion Control Perception: Sensors for Perception - Schematic Representation of the Sensing Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Navigation**, **Motion Control Perception** refers to the closed-loop cognitive and physical ability of a mobile robotic platform to observe, interpret, and react to both its **internal state** (proprioception) and the **external environment** (exteroception) to execute deliberate motion trajectories. A **Sensor** is defined as a physical transducer that converts a measurable physical, chemical, or biological phenomenon into a quantifiable electrical signal.

A **Sensing Unit** is the complete sub-system that acquires, conditions, digitizes, and processes this raw transducer output into a meaningful state estimate that the robot's motion controller can consume. Formally, it is a **perception pipeline** mapping a physical domain variable $x(t)$ into a discrete digital estimate $\hat{x}[k]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A sensor is NEVER just the transducer. In Board Examinations, the *Sensing Unit* is defined as the aggregate of the **transducer + signal conditioning + analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) + digital processing**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human driving a car on a foggy highway. The driver uses:
- **Speedometer & Tachometer** → internal state (proprioception)
- **Eyes, Ears, and GPS voice** → external environment (exteroception)

Now imagine the driver is replaced by a **robot**. The robot has no biological senses — it must be *engineered* with equivalents. The **transducer** (the actual sensor chip) is the *eyeball or eardrum*, while the **sensing unit** is the *entire visual cortex* plus the nerve pathway to the brain, including:
- The retina (transducer)
- The optic nerve (signal conditioning + wiring)
- The visual cortex in the brain (microprocessor / DSP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (must be memorized for KTU ESE):**
> - **Range** — minimum to maximum measurable value
> - **Resolution** — smallest detectable increment
> - **Accuracy** — closeness to the *true* value
> - **Precision** — repeatability / consistency of readings
> - **Sensitivity** — output change per unit input change
> - **Linearity** — proportionality between input and output
> - **Bandwidth** — frequency range over which the sensor responds faithfully

### 1.3 Classification of Sensors (Foundational Taxonomy)

| Class | Definition | Robotic Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Proprioceptive** | Measure robot's *own* internal state | Wheel encoders, IMU, joint potentiometers, motor current sensors |
| **Exteroceptive** | Measure *external* environment | LiDAR, Camera, Ultrasonic, GPS, Radar |
| **Active** | Emit energy into environment to measure response | LiDAR, Radar, Sonar, Infrared Time-of-Flight |
| **Passive** | Only receive ambient energy | Camera (visible/thermal), Microphone, Passive IR |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block Logic of a Generic Sensing Unit

The schematic representation of a sensing unit is a **cascaded signal chain**. Each block performs a deterministic mathematical operation on the signal. The complete chain is:

1. **Physical Phenomenon (Stimulus)** — The real-world variable $x(t)$ to be measured (e.g., distance, velocity, light intensity, magnetic field).
2. **Transducer (Sensing Element)** — Converts the physical stimulus into a raw electrical quantity, typically voltage $V_{raw}(t)$ or current. Defined by a transfer function $H_t(\omega)$.
3. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — Amplifies weak signals, filters noise, linearizes the response, and performs impedance matching. Three sub-operations:
   - **Amplification** (gain $A_v$)
   - **Filtering** (cutoff frequency $f_c$)
   - **Level Shifting** (offset correction $V_{ref}$)
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Samples the conditioned analog signal at sampling frequency $f_s$ and quantizes it into $n$-bit digital words. Quantization step is **$V_{LSB}$**.
5. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP) / Microcontroller** — Applies calibration, sensor fusion (Kalman/Complementary filters), and outputs the final state estimate $\hat{x}[k]$ over a communication bus (CAN, UART, I²C, SPI).

### 2.2 Why Each Block Exists — The "Why" Behind the Chain

- **Transducer alone is insufficient:** A raw strain gauge outputs in **millivolts**; an ADC needs **volts**. Without amplification, quantization error dominates.
- **Filtering is non-negotiable:** Real environments contain electromagnetic interference (EMI), thermal noise, and switching transients from motor drivers. A low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c$ below the Nyquist limit is mandatory.
- **ADC quantization introduces irreducible error:** You cannot recover information finer than one **Least Significant Bit (LSB)**.
- **DSP adds intelligence:** Removes bias drift, fuses redundant sensors, and converts raw counts into SI units (e.g., counts/second → m/s).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In autonomous vehicles (e.g., self-driving cars), the sensing unit chain for a LiDAR operates at **>1 MHz** sampling, while the IMU sensing unit runs at **>1 kHz**. Both feed into a sensor-fusion engine running an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) to output a 6-DoF pose estimate at 100 Hz. The schematic is identical; only the parameters change.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Meaning of Symbols | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Quantization step of an $n$-bit ADC | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta x_{input}}$ | Static Sensitivity | V per SI unit (e.g., V/m, V/°C) |
| 3 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB | Dimensionless (dB) |
| 4 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist–Shannon sampling criterion | Hertz (Hz) |
| 5 | $\sigma_q = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}$ | RMS quantization noise of ideal ADC | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{max} = \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$ | Maximum quantization error bound | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $H(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^2}}$ | First-order low-pass filter magnitude response | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Non-inverting op-amp gain (signal conditioning) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | RC low-pass cutoff frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| 10 | $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{V_{out}(j\omega)}{V_{in}(j\omega)}$ | Generalized transfer function | Complex ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule Reminder:** In every formula row above, the absolute-value / ratio bars are written as `\vert` or `\dfrac{}{}` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. Students writing notes must use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX, not `|x|`, when inside a `.md` table.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of ADC Quantization Error Envelope

The **complete step-by-step derivation** of the maximum quantization error and the RMS quantization noise of an ideal mid-tread ADC is given below. This is a frequently-asked **KTU 14-mark derivation** in the *Signals and Systems* / *Robot Perception* modules.

**Step 0 — Define the Quantization Step.**
An ideal $n$-bit ADC with a full-scale input range of $V_{ref}$ divides the range into $2^n$ discrete levels. The width of one level, called the **Least Significant Bit voltage**, is defined as:

$$V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

**Step 1 — Define the Quantization Error.**
Let $V_{in}$ be the true analog input and $V_q$ be the quantized output. The quantization error is:

$$e_q = V_q - V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Bound the Error.**
Because $V_q$ is rounded (or truncated) to the nearest discrete level, the maximum deviation of $V_{in}$ from the true value is half the level width. Therefore:

$$\vert e_q \vert \leq \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$$

Hence the **maximum quantization error** is:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 3 — Treat the Error as a Uniform Random Variable.**
For analysis, $e_q$ is modeled as a uniformly distributed random variable over the interval $\left[-\frac{V_{LSB}}{2}, +\frac{V_{LSB}}{2}\right]$ with probability density function $p(e_q) = \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}}$ within the interval and zero outside.

**Step 4 — Compute the Mean Square Value of the Error.**
The mean-square (variance) of a uniform distribution of width $L$ is $L^2/12$. Applying this with $L = V_{LSB}$:

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-V_{LSB}/2}^{+V_{LSB}/2} e_q^2 \, p(e_q) \, de_q$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Integral.**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}} \int_{-V_{LSB}/2}^{+V_{LSB}/2} e_q^2 \, de_q = \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}} \left[ \dfrac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-V_{LSB}/2}^{+V_{LSB}/2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Limits.**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}\right)^3 \right] = \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{V_{LSB}^3}{4}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify.**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}^2}{12}$$

**Step 8 — Take the Square Root for the RMS Value.**

$$\sigma_q = \sqrt{\overline{e_q^2}} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n \sqrt{12}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The RMS quantization noise is *independent of the input signal*. Every additional bit of ADC resolution **halves** $V_{LSB}$ and therefore **quarters** the noise power. This is why 16-bit and 24-bit ADCs are used in high-precision inertial sensors and audio.

### 3.2 Derivation of Nyquist–Shannon Reconstruction Condition

**Step 1 — Setup.** A band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ contains no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz. We sample it with a periodic impulse train of period $T_s = 1/f_s$.

**Step 2 — Sampling Frequency.** To allow perfect reconstruction, the sampling frequency must be strictly greater than twice the highest frequency component:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Nyquist Frequency.** The Nyquist frequency is defined as:

$$f_N = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Anti-Aliasing Filter.** To enforce the band-limit before sampling, a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \leq f_N$ must be placed before the ADC. Failure to do this causes **aliasing**, where high-frequency content folds back into the baseband and is irrecoverable.

$$\boxed{\; f_c \leq \dfrac{f_s}{2} \leq f_{max} \;}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Common Robotic Sensors

| Sensor | Type | Measurand | Range | Typical Accuracy | Output | Latency | Cost Tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical Wheel Encoder** | Proprioceptive / Active | Angular position | 0 – 65 535 counts/rev | $\pm 1$ count | Quadrature A/B digital | < 1 ms | Low |
| **IMU (6-axis MEMS)** | Proprioceptive / Active | Accel + Angular rate | $\pm 16 g$, $\pm 2000^\circ/s$ | $\pm 0.5\%$ FS | SPI / I²C | 1 – 10 ms | Medium |
| **LiDAR (2D/3D)** | Exteroceptive / Active | Range to objects | 0.1 – 200 m | $\pm 2$ cm | Point cloud (Ethernet) | 50 – 100 ms | High |
| **Stereo Camera** | Exteroceptive / Passive | Visual depth | 0.5 – 50 m | $\pm 1\%$ at 10 m | Image stream (USB3) | 30 – 100 ms | Medium |
| **Ultrasonic (HC-SR04)** | Exteroceptive / Active | Range | 0.02 – 4 m | $\pm 3$ cm | PWM / digital pulse | 30 ms | Very Low |
| **GPS (RTK)** | Exteroceptive / Passive | Geo-position | Global | $\pm 2$ cm (RTK) | NMEA over UART | 100 ms | Medium |
| **Force/Torque Sensor** | Proprioceptive / Passive | 6-axis wrench | $\pm 50$ N, $\pm 5$ Nm | $\pm 0.5\%$ FS | EtherCAT / CAN | 1 ms | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Sensing Unit — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The complete sensing unit is a cascaded analog-to-digital signal chain. The **Mermaid flowchart** below maps the *functional architecture* and the bidirectional control flow from physical phenomenon to motion controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Physical Phenomenon x of t] --> A2[Transducer Sensing Element]
    A2 --> A3[Analog Signal V raw]
    A3 --> A4[Pre Amplifier Gain A v]
    A4 --> A5[Anti Alias Filter Cutoff f c]
    A5 --> A6[ADC Sampler n bit]
    A6 --> A7[Digital Word D of k]
    A7 --> A8[Microcontroller DSP]
    A8 --> A9[Calibration and Linearization]
    A9 --> A10[Sensor Fusion EKF or Complementary]
    A10 --> A11[State Estimate x hat of k]
    A11 --> A12[Motion Controller]
    A12 --> A12a[Actuator Command PWM or CAN]

    A12 -. feedback loop .-> A1

    A8 -. bias correction .-> A6
    A10 -. prediction update .-> A12

    classDef phys fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef analog fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef digit fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000

    class A1,A2 phys
    class A3,A4,A5,A6 analog
    class A7,A8,A9,A10,A11 digit
    class A12,A12a act
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The forward path (left → right) shows the **perception** signal flow. The dashed return arrow from the Motion Controller to the Physical Phenomenon represents the **closed-loop action**: the actuator moves the robot, which changes the relative position of the physical phenomenon, closing the loop. The dashed `bias correction` arrow from the DSP back to the ADC represents **online recalibration** (e.g., zero-rate offset correction in a gyroscope).

### 4.2 Sensor Classification Tree (Topology View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0[Robotic Sensors] --> R1[Proprioceptive]
    R0 --> R2[Exteroceptive]

    R1 --> R1a[Wheel Encoder]
    R1 --> R1b[IMU Accel Gyro]
    R1 --> R1c[Joint Potentiometer]
    R1 --> R1d[Motor Current Sensor]

    R2 --> R2a[Active Emits Energy]
    R2 --> R2b[Passive Receives Energy]

    R2a --> R2a1[LiDAR Laser]
    R2a --> R2a2[Radar Microwave]
    R2a --> R2a3[Ultrasonic Sonar]
    R2a --> R2a4[IR Time of Flight]

    R2b --> R2b1[Mono or Stereo Camera]
    R2b --> R2b2[Thermal IR Camera]
    R2b --> R2b3[Microphone Array]
    R2b --> R2b4[GPS Receiver]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef proprio fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef extero fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef active fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef passive fill:#c7d2fe,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000

    class R0 root
    class R1,R1a,R1b,R1c,R1d proprio
    class R2,R2a,R2b extero
    class R2a1,R2a2,R2a3,R2a4 active
    class R2b1,R2b2,R2b3,R2b4 passive
```

### 4.3 ADC Pipeline Timing Diagram (Sequential Sampling)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time t0] --> T1[Hold Sample Capacitor Charges]
    T1 --> T2[Quantize Successive Approximation]
    T2 --> T3[Encode to Binary n bit]
    T3 --> T4[Output Latch to Microcontroller]
    T4 --> T5[Time t0 plus T_conv]
    T5 -. next cycle .-> T1

    classDef tstep fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,color:#000
    class T0,T1,T2,T3,T4,T5 tstep
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a "Sensing Unit" in the context of robot navigation. List its four essential functional blocks in the correct order of signal flow.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Sensing Unit** is a complete sub-system that converts a physical quantity into a usable digital state estimate for a robot's motion controller. The four essential functional blocks in order of signal flow are:

1. **Transducer (Sensing Element)** — converts the physical phenomenon into a raw electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — amplifies, filters, and level-shifts the raw signal.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — samples and quantizes the conditioned analog signal into a digital word.
4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP / Microcontroller)** — applies calibration and sensor fusion to produce the final state estimate $\hat{x}[k]$.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark per block × 4 = 2 marks; total 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Sensors that measure the robot's *own* internal state | Sensors that measure the *external* environment |
| **Examples** | (i) Wheel encoder, (ii) IMU | (i) LiDAR, (ii) Camera |

*(1 mark definition each + 1 mark for the 2+2 example split = 3 marks)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question is internally split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A(a) [7 marks]. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the schematic representation of a generic sensing unit. Explain the function of each block.*

**Model Answer:**

A generic sensing unit consists of the following cascaded blocks, shown in the same order as the Mermaid architecture (Section 4.1):

**Block 1 — Physical Phenomenon (Input):** The real-world stimulus $x(t)$ — for example, the linear distance from a robot to an obstacle, the angular velocity of a wheel, or the ambient light intensity. *(½ mark)*

**Block 2 — Transducer:** A device that converts $x(t)$ into an electrical signal. Examples: photodiode (light → current), piezoelectric crystal (force → voltage), Hall-effect sensor (magnetic field → voltage). The transducer is characterized by a transfer function $H_t(\omega)$. *(1 mark)*

**Block 3 — Signal Conditioning:** Three sub-functions are performed:
- **Amplification** (e.g., instrumentation amplifier with gain $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$) to scale the low-level signal to the ADC input range. *(1 mark)*
- **Filtering** (RC low-pass with $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$) to remove high-frequency noise and prevent aliasing. *(1 mark)*
- **Level Shifting** to bias the signal into the ADC's common-mode range. *(½ mark)*

**Block 4 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Samples the analog signal at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion) and quantizes it into $n$-bit digital words. The quantization step is $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^n$, and the maximum quantization error is $\pm V_{LSB}/2$. *(1 mark)*

**Block 5 — Digital Signal Processor / Microcontroller:** Performs calibration (e.g., linearization, temperature compensation), applies digital filtering (e.g., moving average, Kalman filter), and converts raw ADC counts into SI units. The final output $\hat{x}[k]$ is communicated to the motion controller over CAN, UART, or I²C. *(1 mark)*

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram, to be redrawn in the answer script.) *(1 mark)*

---

**Q.A(b) [7 marks]. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*A robotic proximity sensor outputs a continuous analog voltage in the range 0 – 5 V. The onboard ADC is 12-bit with a reference voltage of 5 V. Calculate (i) the quantization step $V_{LSB}$, (ii) the maximum quantization error, and (iii) the RMS quantization noise.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given data:**
- Full-scale range $V_{ref} = 5$ V
- ADC resolution $n = 12$ bits
- Number of levels $= 2^{12} = 4096$

**(i) Quantization Step $V_{LSB}$:**

$$V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5}{4096} \;\text{V} \approx 1.2207 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{V} = 1.22 \;\text{mV}$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error $\varepsilon_{max}$:**

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm \dfrac{1.2207 \;\text{mV}}{2} \approx \pm 0.610 \;\text{mV}$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) RMS Quantization Noise $\sigma_q$:**

$$\sigma_q = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{1.2207 \times 10^{-3}}{3.4641} \approx 3.524 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{V} = 352.4 \;\mu\text{V}$$

*(3 marks)*

**Engineering interpretation:** Any analog variation smaller than 1.22 mV is irretrievably lost in the digitization process. To double the resolution, the designer must either double the bit count to 24 (impractical) or reduce $V_{ref}$ (which reduces dynamic range) — a classic engineering trade-off. *(Bonus remark, optional)*

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)**

**Q.B(a) [7 marks]. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
*Explain the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem. Why must an anti-aliasing filter be placed *before* the ADC, not after? Derive the condition that relates the cutoff frequency of the anti-aliasing filter to the ADC sampling frequency.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement of the Theorem:** The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states that a band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples *if and only if* the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**, and $f_N = f_s/2$ is the **Nyquist frequency**. *(2 marks)*

**Why a Pre-ADC Anti-Aliasing Filter is Mandatory:**
Real-world signals are not perfectly band-limited; they contain high-frequency noise, EMI, and switching transients. If the ADC samples these without pre-filtering, spectral content above $f_N$ will *fold back* into the baseband — a phenomenon called **aliasing**. Aliasing cannot be undone by post-ADC digital filtering because the high-frequency components have already been irreversibly corrupted. Hence the anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter **must be placed before the ADC** to enforce the band-limit. *(3 marks)*

**Derivation of the Cutoff Condition:**
Let the analog input be $x(t)$ with highest frequency $f_{max}$. We pass it through a first-order RC low-pass filter with transfer function:

$$H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The $-3\,\text{dB}$ cutoff frequency is:

$$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

To prevent aliasing, the filter must attenuate all frequencies above $f_N = f_s/2$ to below the ADC's least significant bit. For a first-order filter, a sufficient (conservative) design rule is:

$$\boxed{\; f_c \leq \dfrac{f_s}{2} \;}$$

In practice, engineers use a **guard band** by setting $f_c \approx 0.4 \times f_s$ to account for the filter's gentle $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ roll-off. *(2 marks)*

---

**Q.B(b) [7 marks]. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
*With a suitable example, explain the concept of sensor fusion in a robotic navigation system. How does a complementary filter combine a gyroscope and an accelerometer to estimate the pitch angle of a mobile robot? Derive the transfer function.**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Sensor Fusion (Example):** A quadrotor drone estimates its 3D orientation using a 6-axis IMU. The gyroscope provides excellent *short-term* angular rate information but suffers from *integration drift* over time. The accelerometer measures the gravity vector and provides a stable *long-term* pitch/roll estimate, but is corrupted by high-frequency vibration from the motors. **Sensor fusion** intelligently combines both to yield an orientation estimate that is stable in the long term *and* responsive in the short term. *(2 marks)*

**Complementary Filter for Pitch Angle Estimation:**
Let $\theta_{acc}$ be the pitch angle computed from the accelerometer (using $\theta_{acc} = \arctan(a_y/a_z)$) and $\theta_{gyro}(t)$ be the angle obtained by integrating the gyroscope's pitch rate $\omega_x$:

$$\theta_{gyro}(t) = \int_0^t \omega_x(\tau)\,d\tau$$

The complementary filter estimate $\hat{\theta}(s)$ in the Laplace domain is:

$$\hat{\theta}(s) = \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{1 + \tau s}}_{\text{low-pass on accel}} \cdot \theta_{acc}(s) \;+\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\tau s}{1 + \tau s}}_{\text{high-pass on gyro integral}} \cdot \theta_{gyro}(s)$$

**Derivation step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** The two transfer functions $G_1(s) = 1/(1+\tau s)$ and $G_2(s) = \tau s/(1+\tau s)$ are complementary because:

$$G_1(s) + G_2(s) = \dfrac{1}{1+\tau s} + \dfrac{\tau s}{1+\tau s} = \dfrac{1 + \tau s}{1+\tau s} = 1$$

**Step 2:** This means the two filters *partition the frequency spectrum* with no overlap and no gap. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** $G_1(s)$ is a first-order **low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$. It passes the *low-frequency* (DC) component of $\theta_{acc}$ — i.e., the long-term stable gravity-based estimate. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4:** $G_2(s)$ is a **high-pass filter** that passes the *high-frequency* component of $\theta_{gyro}$ — i.e., the responsive, drift-free short-term rate information. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5:** The combined output is therefore immune to accelerometer high-frequency noise *and* immune to gyroscope long-term drift. The time constant $\tau$ is the **tuning knob**: small $\tau$ → trust gyro more (fast but drifty); large $\tau$ → trust accelerometer more (stable but sluggish). *(1 mark)*

**Discrete-Time Implementation:**
For embedded deployment at sampling period $T_s$, the filter is computed recursively as:

$$\hat{\theta}[k] = (1 - \alpha)\,\hat{\theta}[k-1] + \alpha\,\theta_{acc}[k] + (1-\alpha)\,\omega_x[k]\,T_s$$

where $\alpha = T_s / (\tau + T_s) \in (0, 1)$. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU ESE Valuation:**
> 1. **Conflating "Sensor" and "Sensing Unit"** — A sensor is *only the transducer*. A sensing unit is the *full chain*. Writing "A sensor consists of transducer, ADC, and DSP" is a **definition error worth 1–2 marks** in Part A.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — $V_{LSB} = 1.22$ without "mV" loses ½ mark. Always state SI units.
> 3. **Placing anti-aliasing filter *after* the ADC** — A common conceptual error; once sampled, aliasing cannot be removed. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Using the vertical pipe `|x|` inside markdown tables** — This breaks the table parser in KTU's digital evaluation scripts. Always use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps** — In 7-mark sub-questions, every algebraic step shown above (Steps 0–8) is *expected*. Skipping from $V_{LSB}$ formula to RMS noise directly loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the Nyquist "guard band"** — A first-order filter's $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ roll-off is gentle; practical designs use $f_c \approx 0.4 f_s$, not $f_c = f_s/2$.

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE 2024:**
> - ✅ A **Sensing Unit** = Transducer + Signal Conditioning + ADC + DSP/µC
> - ✅ Proprioceptive = *self*; Exteroceptive = *environment*; Active = *emits energy*; Passive = *receives only*
> - ✅ $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^n$; $\varepsilon_{max} = \pm V_{LSB}/2$; $\sigma_q = V_{LSB}/\sqrt{12}$
> - ✅ Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; guard band: $f_c \approx 0.4 f_s$
> - ✅ Anti-aliasing filter **must precede** the ADC
> - ✅ Sensitivity $S = \Delta V_{out} / \Delta x_{input}$
> - ✅ Complementary filter: $G_1 + G_2 = 1$ (low-pass + high-pass partition)
> - ✅ Each additional ADC bit **quarters** quantization noise power
> - ✅ Resolution ≠ Accuracy: a sensor can be high-resolution but low-accuracy (bias error)
> - ✅ Sensor fusion (Kalman / Complementary) merges proprioceptive and exteroceptive data
> - ✅ Sampling theorem: $f_s = 1/T_s$ where $T_s$ is the sampling period
> - ✅ MEMS IMU is *proprioceptive + active* (it has a vibrating proof mass)
> - ✅ GPS is *exteroceptive + passive* (it only receives satellite signals)
> - ✅ LiDAR is *exteroceptive + active* (emits laser pulse and measures return)
> - ✅ Transfer function of first-order RC LPF: $H(j\omega) = 1/(1 + j\omega RC)$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstacle Sensor (Bumper)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Obstacle Sensor (Bumper)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Obstacle Sensor (Bumper)** is a *contact-based tactile sensor* used in robotic navigation systems to detect the physical presence of an obstacle through direct mechanical contact. It is one of the simplest, most reliable, and most cost-effective forms of obstacle detection used in mobile robots, Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs), and domestic service robots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**  
> A *Bumper Sensor* is a binary (digital) sensing device that transitions its output state from logic **HIGH** to logic **LOW** (or vice versa) when a physical collision or contact force exceeding a predefined threshold is applied to its actuator surface. It serves as the *last line of defence* in a robot's hierarchical obstacle avoidance strategy.

The bumper sensor essentially functions as a **mechanical-electrical transducer**, converting a physical impact (mechanical domain) into an electrical signal (electrical domain) that can be processed by the robot's microcontroller or motion controller.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Blind Person's Cane**  
> Imagine a visually impaired person walking with a white cane. They cannot "see" obstacles the way a camera-based system would, but the moment the cane touches an object, an immediate signal is sent to the brain (via the hand) telling them: *"Stop! Something is in front of you."*  
>  
> A **bumper sensor operates on exactly the same principle** — it cannot anticipate an obstacle, but the *instant* physical contact occurs, it generates a clear, unambiguous stop/replan signal. Just as the cane is the final, most trusted sense for a blind person, the bumper is the *final, most trusted sense* for a mobile robot. It is the **safety net** that catches what sonar, infrared, and LIDAR may have missed.

Another intuitive analogy: think of the **bumpers in a bowling alley** or the **whiskers of a cat**. Both provide direct contact feedback without the need for complex distance calculation.

## 1.3 Classification of Bumper Sensors

Bumper sensors are broadly classified into the following types:

| Type | Operating Principle | Key Feature |
|------|--------------------|-----|
| **Mechanical Limit Switch Bumper** | Uses a physical SPDT/DPDT microswitch | Most common, highly reliable, binary output |
| **Conductive Foam / Carbon Pad Bumper** | Two conductive surfaces make contact on compression | Distributed sensing, can detect contact location |
| **Force-Sensitive Resistor (FSR) Bumper** | Resistance changes with applied force | Analog output, measures contact intensity |
| **Optical (Light Curtain) Bumper** | Infrared beam broken on contact | No mechanical wear, high reliability |
| **Capacitive Bumper** | Capacitance changes with proximity/contact | Can detect before actual contact |
| **Pneumatic / Air Pressure Bumper** | Pressure spike in an air bladder on impact | Compliant, suitable for safety-critical robots |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board Exam):**
> - **Contact Force Threshold:** typically **0.5 N to 5 N**
> - **Response Time:** typically **< 5 ms** (mechanical types), **< 1 ms** (optical types)
> - **Mechanical Life Cycle:** **> 1,000,000 actuations** (industrial-grade microswitches)
> - **Output:** **TTL-compatible digital** (0 V or 5 V) or **contact closure (relay-style)**
> - **Operating Voltage:** **5 V DC to 24 V DC**

## 1.5 Role in the Obstacle Avoidance Hierarchy

In a mobile robot, sensors are arranged in a hierarchical order based on detection range and reliability:

1. **Long-range planning:** LIDAR, Stereo Vision (~10 m range)
2. **Mid-range sensing:** Ultrasonic / Infrared (~0.1 m to 5 m)
3. **Short-range / proximity:** IR proximity, capacitive (~0.01 m to 0.1 m)
4. **Contact layer (Bumper):** Physical contact (~0 m)

The bumper sits at the **bottom of the hierarchy** and acts as the **failsafe** — if every other sensor fails, the bumper guarantees that the robot *cannot* collide with an obstacle without registering the event.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Mechanical Limit Switch Bumper

The most commonly used bumper in academic and industrial robots is the **spring-loaded mechanical limit switch** type. Its working can be broken down into structured logical steps:

### Step 1 — At-Rest Condition
- The robot is navigating freely.
- The spring holds the bumper plate *just barely* clear of the microswitch's actuator.
- The microswitch's Common (C) terminal is connected to the Normally Closed (NC) contact.
- The output line is pulled to **Vcc (logic HIGH)** through a pull-up resistor.

### Step 2 — Contact with Obstacle
- The bumper plate collides with the obstacle.
- The impact force compresses the spring by a displacement $x$.
- The plate pushes the microswitch's actuator button.

### Step 3 — State Transition
- The microswitch transitions from NC to Normally Open (NO).
- The output line is now connected to **GND (logic LOW)**.
- The microcontroller receives an **interrupt** or polls a HIGH→LOW transition.

### Step 4 — Reaction & Recovery
- The robot's control software executes an *emergency stop* or *replan manoeuvre*.
- The robot reverses, rotates, and resumes navigation.
- The spring decompresses; the microswitch returns to NC; the line returns to HIGH.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a spring?**  
> A rigid bumper would transfer the full collision impulse to the robot chassis, potentially damaging the robot, the obstacle, or both. The **spring acts as a mechanical low-pass filter**, absorbing the impulse, extending the contact time, and reducing the peak force transmitted to the switch and the chassis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a pull-up resistor?**  
> An open-collector / open-drain switch output would "float" electrically when not actuated, producing unpredictable readings. The pull-up resistor (typically **$R_{pu} = 10\text{ k}\Omega$** with $V_{cc} = 5\text{ V}$) firmly defines the resting state as logic HIGH, producing a clean digital signal.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Switch Debouncing

Mechanical microswitches suffer from **contact bounce** — the metal contacts physically bounce against each other for **1 ms to 10 ms** after closure, producing a noisy oscillating signal. If fed directly to a microcontroller interrupt, this can cause:
- False multiple triggers
- Incorrect counter increments
- Erratic robot behaviour

Two debouncing strategies are used:
1. **Hardware debouncing:** RC low-pass filter + Schmitt trigger.
2. **Software debouncing:** ignore transitions for a fixed time window (typically **20 ms to 50 ms**).

## 2.4 Distributed Sensing with Conductive Foam

In advanced bumper systems, the entire bumper strip is made of a layer of **conductive foam (carbon-impregnated silicone)** pressed against a printed circuit board with interleaved copper traces. When compressed, the foam shorts adjacent traces. By reading which pair of traces is shorted, the controller can identify the **exact location** of the contact along the bumper's length.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE 1 — Master Formula Reference for Bumper Sensor Design**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $F_{contact} = k \cdot x$ | Spring force, stiffness, displacement | N, N/m, m | Calculating trip force of spring-loaded bumper |
| 2 | $T_{debounce} = R \cdot C$ | Debounce time constant | s (seconds) | RC hardware debounce design |
| 3 | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{L}}{R_{L} + R_{pu}}$ | Voltage divider at output | V, $\Omega$ | Output logic-level calculation |
| 4 | $E_{impact} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot x^{2}$ | Energy stored in spring on impact | Joules (J) | Sizing spring for energy absorption |
| 5 | $p_{contact} = \dfrac{F_{contact}}{A_{plate}}$ | Contact pressure | Pa (N/m²) | Material strength check |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{m}{b}$ | Mechanical time constant (mass-damping) | s | Response time estimation |
| 7 | $f_{natural} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of spring-mass | Hz | Avoiding resonance with robot motion |
| 8 | $I_{sink} = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_{OL}}{R_{pu}}$ | Sinking current at LOW output | A | Verifying microcontroller pin rating |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bumper sensors are not just academic curiosities. They are mandated by safety standards in several real-world applications:

- **AGVs in warehouses** (Amazon Kiva robots, Fetch Robotics) use bumper strips to comply with **ANSI/ITSDF B56.5** safety standard.
- **Domestic robot vacuums (Roomba, Xiaomi Roborock)** use a half-moon bumper around the front to detect walls and furniture.
- **Mars rover prototypes (NASA JPL)** use bumper panels as a redundant safety layer for unknown terrain.
- **Industrial CNC tool changers** use pneumatic bumpers to detect misalignment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Trip Force of a Spring-Loaded Bumper

**Problem Setup:**  
A mobile robot of mass $M$ is fitted with a front bumper consisting of a plate (mass $m_{p}$) connected to the chassis by a linear spring of stiffness $k$. The plate extends a distance $L$ ahead of the chassis. A microswitch is mounted such that it triggers when the plate is pushed back by a displacement $\delta$ from its rest position.  
**Find:** The contact force $F_{contact}$ required to trigger the switch, and the minimum velocity at which the robot must strike a rigid wall for guaranteed triggering.

**Step 1 — Relate spring force to displacement using Hooke's Law.**

The bumper plate is held by a linear spring. When the plate is pushed back by a displacement $x$, the spring exerts a restoring force proportional to the displacement.

$$
F_{spring} = k \cdot x
$$

**Step 2 — Apply force balance on the bumper plate at the trigger instant.**

At the instant the switch triggers, the spring is compressed by exactly the trigger displacement $\delta$. The contact force from the obstacle must overcome the spring force (assuming frictionless guide rails and negligible plate inertia at low velocity).

$$
F_{contact} = k \cdot \delta
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the contact force numerically for a typical design.**

Let $k = 200 \text{ N/m}$ and $\delta = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$.

$$
F_{contact} = 200 \times 0.005
$$

$$
F_{contact} = 1.0 \text{ N}
$$

This means an obstacle must push the plate back by 5 mm with at least 1.0 N of force to trigger the bumper — a deliberately low threshold so that even light contact is detected.

**Step 4 — Relate impact energy to spring compression.**

The kinetic energy of the robot at impact (ignoring chassis rebound) is converted entirely into the elastic potential energy of the spring as the plate reaches maximum compression $x_{max}$.

$$
\frac{1}{2} \cdot M \cdot v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot x_{max}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the minimum impact velocity.**

$$
v_{min} = x_{max} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k}{M}}
$$

The robot is guaranteed to trigger the bumper if $x_{max} \geq \delta$. Therefore:

$$
v_{min} = \delta \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k}{M}}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation.**

Let $M = 5 \text{ kg}$, $k = 200 \text{ N/m}$, $\delta = 0.005 \text{ m}$.

$$
v_{min} = 0.005 \times \sqrt{\frac{200}{5}}
$$

$$
v_{min} = 0.005 \times \sqrt{40}
$$

$$
v_{min} = 0.005 \times 6.3246
$$

$$
v_{min} \approx 0.0316 \text{ m/s} \approx 3.16 \text{ cm/s}
$$

This shows that even an extremely gentle impact (~3 cm/s) is enough to trigger the bumper — demonstrating its exceptional sensitivity.

## 3.2 Derivation: RC Debounce Time Constant

**Problem Setup:**  
A microswitch's output is connected to a microcontroller input through an RC low-pass filter with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1 \mu\text{F}$, followed by a Schmitt trigger.  
**Find:** The time constant and the recommended wait time before reading the signal as valid.

**Step 1 — Time constant of an RC circuit.**

For a first-order RC low-pass filter, the time constant $\tau$ is the product of resistance and capacitance.

$$
\tau = R \cdot C
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$
\tau = (10 \times 10^{3}) \cdot (0.1 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
\tau = 10^{4} \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
\tau = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the time to reach a "stable" logic level.**

A capacitor charges to approximately **63.2%** of its final value in one time constant, **86.5%** in two time constants, and **95%** in three time constants. For robust digital logic, we wait for **5τ**.

$$
T_{5\tau} = 5 \cdot \tau = 5 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to typical mechanical bounce duration.**

Industrial microswitches exhibit bounce for 1 ms to 5 ms. Our 5 ms settling time comfortably exceeds the worst-case bounce, ensuring a clean, bounce-free logic transition at the Schmitt trigger output.

## 3.3 Derivation: Pull-Up Resistor Sizing

**Problem Setup:**  
A 5 V microcontroller input must be pulled HIGH by a pull-up resistor when the switch is open. The microcontroller's HIGH input requires at least $V_{IH} = 2.0 \text{ V}$, and its input leakage current is $I_{IL} = 1 \mu\text{A}$.  
**Find:** The maximum allowable pull-up resistance.

**Step 1 — Voltage divider equation at the worst case (switch open, leakage sinking).**

When the switch is open, leakage current flows from $V_{cc}$ through $R_{pu}$ into the microcontroller's input pin. The voltage at the pin must remain above $V_{IH}$.

$$
V_{cc} - I_{IL} \cdot R_{pu} \geq V_{IH}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the maximum $R_{pu}$.**

$$
R_{pu,max} = \frac{V_{cc} - V_{IH}}{I_{IL}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
R_{pu,max} = \frac{5.0 - 2.0}{1 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_{pu,max} = \frac{3.0}{1 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_{pu,max} = 3 \times 10^{6} \Omega = 3 \text{ M}\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering design choice.**

In practice, a value of **$R_{pu} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$** is chosen — far below the maximum — to provide strong noise immunity and fast switching. The maximum is a *ceiling*, not a recommendation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Trade-off | Engineering Implication |
|--------------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Lower spring stiffness ($k$)** | Lower trip force, more sensitive | More false positives from vibration |
| **Higher spring stiffness ($k$)** | Higher trip force, less sensitive | Risk of missing soft obstacles (curtains) |
| **Smaller trigger gap ($\delta$)** | Faster trigger, less energy absorption | Higher chance of switch damage |
| **Larger trigger gap ($\delta$)** | Slower trigger, more energy absorption | Larger robot footprint |
| **Shorter debounce time** | Faster response | Risk of multiple false triggers |
| **Longer debounce time** | Immune to bounce | Missed triggers on quick glancing contacts |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bumper Sensor System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Obstacle / Wall] -->|Physical Impact| B[Bumper Plate]
    B -->|Compresses| C[Linear Spring - stiffness k]
    B -->|Actuates| D[Micro Limit Switch]
    D -->|Raw Bouncing Signal| E[RC Debounce Filter]
    E -->|Cleaned Signal| F[Schmitt Trigger Buffer]
    F -->|Logic Level 0V or 5V| G[Microcontroller GPIO Pin]
    G -->|Interrupt Triggered| H[Motion Controller]
    H -->|Emergency Stop| I[Motor Driver]
    H -->|Reverse + Rotate| J[Path Replanner]
    K[Pull-up Resistor 10k] --- D
    L[Vcc 5V DC] --- K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Bumper Trigger Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Robot Navigating - Switch Open]
    S1[Switch Output HIGH via Pull-up]
    S2[Obstacle Contact with Bumper Plate]
    S3{Switch Actuated?}
    S4[Switch Closes - Output LOW]
    S5[Microcontroller Detects Falling Edge]
    S6[Stop All Motors Immediately]
    S7[Reverse by d cm]
    S8[Rotate by angle theta]
    S9[Resume Navigation]
    S10[False Trigger Logged - Ignore]

    S0 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 -- Yes --> S4
    S3 -- No --> S1
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S1
    S5 -- Debounce Window --> S10
    S10 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Multi-Segment Bumper (Distributed Sensing)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Segment1Hit: Left sensor triggered
    Idle --> Segment2Hit: Centre sensor triggered
    Idle --> Segment3Hit: Right sensor triggered
    Segment1Hit --> ReverseLeft: Turn right and reverse
    Segment2Hit --> ReverseStraight: Reverse and rotate randomly
    Segment3Hit --> ReverseRight: Turn left and reverse
    ReverseLeft --> Idle
    ReverseStraight --> Idle
    ReverseRight --> Idle
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture (Hardware Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MechanicalLayer [Mechanical Layer]
        A1[Bumper Shell - Compliant Material]
        A2[Guide Rails - Low Friction]
        A3[Return Spring - Linear Coil]
    end

    subgraph TransductionLayer [Transduction Layer]
        B1[Microswitch - SPDT Type]
        B2[Conductive Foam Pad - Optional]
    end

    subgraph SignalLayer [Signal Conditioning Layer]
        C1[Pull-up Resistor Network]
        C2[RC Debounce Filter]
        C3[Schmitt Trigger - 74HC14]
        C4[Opto-isolator - For Noise Isolation]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer [Processing Layer]
        D1[Microcontroller - Interrupt Pin]
        D2[Debounce Timer - Software]
        D3[Decision Logic - Stop/Replan]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A3 --> A1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### Question 1. What is a bumper sensor in a mobile robot? Mention two advantages and one limitation.
`[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A bumper sensor is a **contact-type tactile sensor** that detects the physical presence of an obstacle through direct mechanical collision. It generates a digital signal (typically a HIGH-to-LOW transition) when the robot strikes an object with a force exceeding a predefined threshold, triggering an emergency stop or replan routine.

**Advantages:**
1. **Highly reliable** — works regardless of lighting, surface colour, transparency, or acoustic noise.
2. **Low cost and simple** — uses cheap microswitches and passive components, no complex signal processing required.

**Limitation:**
1. **No pre-contact detection** — the robot must physically touch the obstacle before reacting, which may not be acceptable in high-speed or delicate environments.

---

### Question 2. Why is switch debouncing required in a bumper sensor circuit? Name the two common debouncing techniques.
`[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

Microswitches suffer from **contact bounce**, a phenomenon in which the mechanical contacts physically oscillate against each other for 1–10 ms after closure, producing a noisy electrical signal. If this noisy signal is fed directly to a microcontroller interrupt, it causes **false multiple triggers** and erratic robot behaviour (e.g., counting one collision as five, or stopping intermittently).

**Two common debouncing techniques:**

1. **Hardware debouncing** — using an RC low-pass filter followed by a Schmitt trigger to clean the signal electronically.
2. **Software debouncing** — disabling the input or ignoring further transitions for a fixed time window (typically 20–50 ms) after the first valid edge is detected.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] — With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a microswitch-based bumper sensor used in a mobile robot. Discuss the role of the spring, pull-up resistor, and RC debounce filter in the design.

#### (b) [7 Marks] — A bumper of a mobile robot uses a linear spring of stiffness $k = 250 \text{ N/m}$ and trigger gap $\delta = 4 \text{ mm}$. The robot mass is $M = 8 \text{ kg}$. Calculate:  
(i) the contact force required to trigger the bumper,  
(ii) the minimum impact velocity for guaranteed triggering, and  
(iii) the energy stored in the spring at full trigger.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Working Principle and Block Diagram `[7 Marks]`

**Working Principle:**  
A microswitch-based bumper consists of a movable plate (the bumper shell) connected to the robot chassis via a linear coil spring. A microswitch is mounted such that its actuator button is touched by the bumper plate only when the plate is pushed back by a distance $\delta$ from its rest position.

In the **idle (no contact) state**, the spring holds the plate clear of the switch actuator. The microswitch's Common terminal is connected to Vcc (5 V) through a **pull-up resistor $R_{pu}$**, so the output line sits at **logic HIGH**.

When the robot collides with an obstacle, the **plate is pushed back**, compressing the spring. Once the displacement reaches $\delta$, the plate physically actuates the microswitch, which transitions its Common contact from Normally-Closed (NC) to Normally-Open (NO), connecting the output line to **GND**. The output therefore drops to **logic LOW** — a falling edge that the microcontroller detects as a collision event.

The raw switch output, however, contains **contact bounce** (1–10 ms of electrical noise). This is filtered by an **RC low-pass filter** and a **Schmitt trigger**, which together produce a clean, bounce-free logic-level signal suitable for direct connection to a microcontroller interrupt pin.

**Role of the Spring (`k`):**  
The spring absorbs impact energy, reduces peak force transmitted to the chassis, and provides a *restoring force* that returns the plate to its rest position after the collision. The spring stiffness `k` directly determines the trip sensitivity.

**Role of the Pull-up Resistor (`R_pu`):**  
The pull-up resistor (typically 10 kΩ) defines the resting output as logic HIGH when the switch is open, preventing the input from floating and picking up electromagnetic noise. When the switch closes, it sinks current through `R_pu` to ground, pulling the output LOW.

**Role of the RC Debounce Filter:**  
The RC filter (typically 10 kΩ and 0.1 μF) forms a low-pass filter with τ = 1 ms, suppressing the high-frequency bounce transients. A Schmitt trigger then squares up the slow edge into a clean digital transition. This hardware debounce ensures the microcontroller sees exactly one transition per collision.

**Block Diagram (descriptive, refer to Section 4.4):**  
Obstacle → Bumper Plate → Spring (k) → Microswitch → RC Filter → Schmitt Trigger → Microcontroller GPIO → Motion Controller (Stop) → Motor Driver.

---

#### (b) Numerical Problem Solution `[7 Marks]`

**Given:**  
$k = 250 \text{ N/m}$, $\delta = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$, $M = 8 \text{ kg}$.

**Part (i) — Contact force required to trigger:**

By Hooke's law, the spring force at trigger displacement equals the contact force.

$$
F_{contact} = k \cdot \delta
$$

$$
F_{contact} = 250 \times 0.004
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{contact} = 1.0 \text{ N}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Minimum impact velocity for guaranteed triggering:**

Equating the robot's kinetic energy to the elastic potential energy stored in the spring at full trigger:

$$
\frac{1}{2} M v_{min}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} k \delta^{2}
$$

$$
v_{min}^{2} = \frac{k \delta^{2}}{M}
$$

$$
v_{min} = \delta \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k}{M}}
$$

$$
v_{min} = 0.004 \times \sqrt{\frac{250}{8}}
$$

$$
v_{min} = 0.004 \times \sqrt{31.25}
$$

$$
v_{min} = 0.004 \times 5.590
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{min} \approx 0.02236 \text{ m/s} \approx 2.24 \text{ cm/s}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Energy stored in the spring at full trigger:**

$$
E_{spring} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot \delta^{2}
$$

$$
E_{spring} = \frac{1}{2} \times 250 \times (0.004)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{spring} = 125 \times 0.000016
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{spring} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 2 \text{ mJ}}
$$

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the concept of distributed sensing in a conductive-foam bumper. How does it differ from a single-microswitch bumper in terms of information provided and typical application?

#### (b) [7 Marks] — A bumper microswitch output is filtered through an RC network of $R = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.22 \mu\text{F}$ before being fed to a 74HC14 Schmitt trigger inverter. Calculate:  
(i) the time constant of the filter,  
(ii) the time required to reach 95% of the final logic level, and  
(iii) comment on whether this design is suitable for a switch with 8 ms typical bounce duration.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Distributed Sensing with Conductive Foam `[7 Marks]`

**Concept:**  
A distributed bumper replaces a single microswitch with a continuous strip of **carbon-impregnated conductive foam** pressed against a **printed circuit board (PCB)** with interleaved or matrix-arranged copper electrodes. When the foam is locally compressed by an impact, the carbon particles inside the foam form a low-resistance path between the electrodes at that specific location, shorting them electrically.

**Information provided:**  
- The microcontroller applies a scan signal to each electrode pair in turn.  
- The pair that reports a short circuit identifies the **exact contact location** (x, y) along the bumper's length and height.  
- Optionally, the resistance value (in FSR variants) provides a measure of **contact force intensity**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Single Microswitch Bumper | Distributed Conductive Foam Bumper |
|---------|---------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Output | Binary (1 bit) | Multi-bit position data |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate |
| Information | Collision occurred (yes/no) | Where, and optionally how hard |
| Wiring | 2 wires | $N$ wires for $N$ segments |
| Typical Use | Domestic vacuum cleaners, hobby robots | Industrial AGVs, service robots |
| Failure mode | Single point of failure | Graceful degradation |

**Typical Application:**  
Industrial Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) navigating narrow warehouse aisles use distributed bumpers to detect *which side* of the robot hit an obstacle, allowing the controller to choose the *direction of avoidance* intelligently (e.g., turn right if right bumper triggered).

---

#### (b) Numerical Problem Solution `[7 Marks]`

**Given:** $R = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega = 4.7 \times 10^{3} \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 0.22 \mu\text{F} = 0.22 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$, bounce duration = 8 ms.

**Part (i) — Time constant of the RC filter:**

$$
\tau = R \cdot C
$$

$$
\tau = (4.7 \times 10^{3}) \times (0.22 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
\tau = 4.7 \times 0.22 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\tau = 1.034 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau \approx 1.034 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Time to reach 95% of final value:**

The capacitor voltage reaches 95% of its final value at $t = 3\tau$.

$$
t_{95\%} = 3 \cdot \tau = 3 \times 1.034 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{95\%} \approx 3.10 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Suitability for 8 ms bounce:**

The settling time of the filter is **3.10 ms**, which is **significantly less** than the 8 ms bounce duration. This means that after the Schmitt trigger's hysteresis, the output may *transition prematurely* during the bounce window, defeating the purpose of debouncing.

**Conclusion:** **The design is NOT suitable** for a switch with 8 ms bounce. The RC time constant must be increased to at least $\tau = 3 \text{ ms}$ (i.e., $3\tau = 9 \text{ ms} > 8 \text{ ms}$). For instance, increasing $C$ to $0.68 \mu\text{F}$ would give $\tau = 3.2 \text{ ms}$ and $3\tau \approx 9.6 \text{ ms}$, comfortably exceeding the bounce duration. Alternatively, a $5\tau$ design (i.e., $5 \times 8 = 40 \text{ ms}$ wait) is recommended for absolute safety margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in Bumper Sensor Questions — Lose Up to 3–4 Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to mention debounce** — A surprising number of answers describe the switch, the spring, the pull-up, but completely omit the **RC debounce + Schmitt trigger** block. This is treated as an incomplete design and costs **at least 1 mark**.
> 
> 2. **Confusing pull-up and pull-down** — Many students wrongly state that the resting state is LOW. **Resting state is HIGH**; collision causes a HIGH-to-LOW (falling edge) transition. Getting this backwards will cost a full mark on the block diagram question.
> 
> 3. **Unit mistakes in numericals** — Forgetting to convert $\text{mm}$ to $\text{m}$ in $k \cdot \delta$ or $\delta \cdot \sqrt{k/M}$ calculations. The answer will be off by a factor of 1000 if not handled correctly. **Always write units in every step.**
> 
> 4. **Skipping the $5\tau$ or $3\tau$ justification** — When asked about debounce, students often state $T = RC$ and stop. Examiners expect you to **explain that you need to wait for 3τ to 5τ to reach a stable logic level**, and to compare this with the typical bounce duration (1–10 ms).
> 
> 5. **Treating the bumper as the *primary* sensor** — In a KTU viva or 7-mark design question, if you place the bumper *above* LIDAR or ultrasonic in the hierarchy, you will be marked down. **The bumper is always the last-line failsafe**, never the primary navigator.
> 
> 6. **Omitting the block diagram or labelling it poorly** — Block diagrams must have **clear labelled arrows** indicating signal flow direction. Unlabelled boxes with no arrowheads cost marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU ESE):**

- ☐ Bumper sensor = **contact-based, binary-output, failsafe obstacle detector** — *not* a distance sensor.
- ☐ Always positioned at the **lowest level of the obstacle avoidance hierarchy** — below LIDAR, ultrasonic, IR, capacitive.
- ☐ Most common type: **spring-loaded microswitch bumper** with conductive plate, linear spring, SPDT switch, pull-up resistor, RC debounce, and Schmitt trigger.
- ☐ Resting output state = **logic HIGH (5 V)** via pull-up; collision output = **logic LOW (0 V)**.
- ☐ **Trip force** $F_{contact} = k \cdot \delta$ — typically **0.5 N to 5 N** for sensitive detection.
- ☐ **Minimum trigger velocity** $v_{min} = \delta \sqrt{k/M}$ — for typical designs, this is on the order of **2–5 cm/s**.
- ☐ **Debounce time constant** $T = R \cdot C$ — for robust logic, wait for **$3\tau$ to $5\tau$**, which must **exceed the switch's bounce duration (1–10 ms)**.
- ☐ **Pull-up resistor** typically **$R_{pu} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$** with $V_{cc} = 5 \text{ V}$; the maximum allowable is dictated by the microcontroller's input leakage current.
- ☐ **Mechanical life** of industrial microswitches exceeds **$10^6$ actuations**; choose higher-rated switches for AGV-class robots.
- ☐ **Distributed bumpers** (conductive foam, FSR matrix) provide **contact location** information — used in industrial AGVs for intelligent replanning.
- ☐ **Optical / capacitive bumpers** have **no mechanical wear** and are used in **safety-critical or sterile environments** (medical robots).
- ☐ The bumper is a **safety-certified component** in warehouse AGVs — mandated by **ANSI/ITSDF B56.5** standards.
- ☐ **No pre-contact detection** is the fundamental limitation — the robot must physically touch the obstacle, so the bumper cannot be used for high-speed or delicate navigation.
- ☐ The **spring** in the bumper serves three roles: (1) energy absorption, (2) restoring force, (3) mechanical low-pass filtering of impact impulse.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Odometry Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Odometry Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Odometry** is a fundamental **dead-reckoning** localization technique used in mobile robotics that estimates a robot's *change in position and orientation* over time by measuring the **rotational displacement of its driven wheels** using wheel-mounted angular encoders. The term originates from the Greek words *hodos* (journey) and *metron* (measure).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the **Odometry Sensor** refers to the integrated system comprising:
1. **Wheel encoders** (optical, magnetic, or Hall-effect transducers) that generate discrete pulses proportional to wheel rotation.
2. The **kinematic model** of the robot (typically a *differential-drive* or *Ackermann-steered* configuration) that translates wheel rotation into linear and angular velocity.
3. A **pose integration algorithm** that cumulatively updates the robot's 2D pose $(x, y, \theta)$ from these velocity estimates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reference Frame:** By KTU convention, the robot's pose is expressed in a **global 2D Cartesian frame** as $P = (x, y, \theta)$, where $\theta$ is the *heading angle* measured counter-clockwise from the positive X-axis (in radians).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blind Walker's Compass"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine walking through a dark, unfamiliar room while blindfolded, holding a **measuring wheel** (like a surveyor's wheel) in each hand. You can only *measure how far each wheel has rolled* and the *distance between your hands (axle length)*. By keeping a running tally of how much the left and right wheels turn, you can *mathematically reconstruct* where you have walked and which way you are facing — even though you cannot see the room. **This is exactly what odometry does for a mobile robot.**

The critical insight: Odometry does **not sense the external world**. It only measures **internal wheel rotation** and infers motion — analogous to a sailor estimating distance sailed by counting *revolutions of the propeller*, not by looking at landmarks.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Engineering Meaning |
|------|:------:|--------------------|
| Wheel radius | $r$ | Effective rolling radius of the drive wheel (**meters**) |
| Wheel base / Axle length | $L$ | Distance between the contact points of left and right wheels (**meters**) |
| Encoder resolution | $N$ | Number of discrete pulses (ticks) generated per full wheel revolution (**ticks/rev**) |
| Angular displacement | $\Delta\phi_L, \Delta\phi_R$ | Incremental wheel rotation in a sampling interval $\Delta t$ (**radians**) |
| Linear velocity | $v$ | Instantaneous forward speed of the robot's reference point (**m/s**) |
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | Instantaneous turning rate of the robot (**rad/s**) |
| Pose | $P = (x,y,\theta)$ | 2D position and orientation in the global frame |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The **odometry pipeline** operates in three cascading stages, each with a clear engineering rationale:

**Stage 1 — Sensing (Raw Encoder Pulses)**
- A slotted optical disc rotates with the wheel; an *opto-interrupter* (LED + photodiode) generates a **square-wave pulse train**.
- Each rising/falling edge of the pulse train is counted by a **quadrature decoder** (typically x4 decoding for direction sensing).
- The cumulative count is converted to angular displacement: $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot \text{ticks}}{N}$

**Stage 2 — Forward Kinematics (Wheel Rotation → Body Motion)**
- The **differential-drive robot** is the canonical KTU example. It has two independently driven wheels on a common axle.
- Because the wheels are rigidly fixed, the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the axle line.
- *Why* two wheels? Knowing *both* $\Delta\phi_L$ and $\Delta\phi_R$ allows the kinematic solver to disentangle **forward translation** (when both wheels agree) from **in-place rotation** (when wheels oppose).

**Stage 3 — Pose Integration (Body Motion → World Pose)**
- The robot's *body-frame* velocities $(v, \omega)$ are integrated over $\Delta t$ to update the *world-frame* pose.
- The standard KTU integration method is the **mid-point (second-order) Euler** because it bounds the truncation error to $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^2)$, compared to $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$ for naive Euler.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it represents ≥ 70% of Part A marks in KTU Module-1 questions on Odometry.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Description / Units |
|:-:|---------------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Wheel linear displacement (left) | $\Delta s_L = r \cdot \Delta\phi_L$ | Distance rolled by left wheel in $\Delta t$ (**m**) |
| 2 | Wheel linear displacement (right) | $\Delta s_R = r \cdot \Delta\phi_R$ | Distance rolled by right wheel in $\Delta t$ (**m**) |
| 3 | Robot linear translation (midpoint) | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$ | Forward arc-length of the reference point |
| 4 | Robot rotation | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L}$ | Heading change (**rad**) |
| 5 | Body linear velocity | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Instantaneous forward velocity (**m/s**) |
| 6 | Body angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Instantaneous yaw rate (**rad/s**) |
| 7 | Pose update (mid-point) | $x' = x + \Delta s \cos\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | New x-coordinate |
| 8 | Pose update (mid-point) | $y' = y + \Delta s \sin\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | New y-coordinate |
| 9 | Pose update (mid-point) | $\theta' = \theta + \Delta\theta$ | New heading |
| 10 | Encoder ticks to angle | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot n_{\text{ticks}}}{N}$ | $N$ = encoder resolution (**ticks/rev**) |
| 11 | Arc length per tick | $\delta s = \dfrac{2\pi r}{N}$ | Resolution floor of the sensor (**m/tick**) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for KTU Exam Validation:**
> - If $\Delta s_R = \Delta s_L \Rightarrow \Delta\theta = 0$ (pure straight translation).
> - If $\Delta s_R = -\Delta s_L \Rightarrow \Delta s = 0$ (pure in-place rotation about the axle center).
> - The wheel base $L$ is **strictly non-zero**; division by zero implies a kinematic singularity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Odometry is the **foundational proprioceptive sensor** for nearly every commercial mobile platform:
- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics):** Use encoder odometry as the *first layer* of an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) combined with LiDAR scan-matching.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Apollo, Autoware stacks):** Wheel odometry feeds tightly into the *odometry-domain* before LiDAR-based localization corrects drift.
- **ROS (Robot Operating System):** The `nav_msgs/Odometry` message is the standardized container; the `robot_pose_ekf` package fuses wheel odometry with IMU data.
- **Domestic Robots (iRobot Roomba):** Relies heavily on optical-encoder odometry for room coverage and return-to-dock behavior.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Kinematics of the Differential-Drive Robot

**Problem Setup:** A two-wheeled robot with wheel radius $r$ and axle length $L$ has its left and right wheels rotating with angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ respectively. Derive the body's linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 1 — Express wheel contact-point velocities.**
Each wheel is rolling without slipping, so the linear speed of the contact point equals the wheel's tangential velocity.

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L \qquad \text{and} \qquad v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rigid-body constraint of the axle.**
Because the axle is rigid and in pure translation+rotation, the left and right contact points must have *the same angular velocity* $\omega$ about the ICR, but at *different radii* from the ICR.

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

The $L$ in the denominator is the distance from the left contact point to the right contact point — the full axle length — because when the wheel pair rotates about the ICR, the two contact points are at distances $R$ and $R+L$ from the ICR (where $R$ is the ICR's offset from the left wheel).

**Step 3 — Average the two wheel velocities for the body center.**
The reference point of the robot (axle midpoint) moves at the *arithmetic mean* of the two contact-point velocities.

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r \cdot \omega_R + r \cdot \omega_L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**
Substituting Step 1 into Step 2 gives the angular velocity formula.

$$\omega = \frac{r \cdot \omega_R - r \cdot \omega_L}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

> ✅ **Derived Result:** This is the canonical *forward-kinematic map* of a differential-drive base.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discrete-Time Pose Update (Mid-Point Integration)

**Problem Setup:** Given instantaneous $(v, \omega)$ over a sampling interval $\Delta t$, derive the new pose $(x', y', \theta')$.

**Step 1 — Heading update (straightforward integration).**
The heading $\theta$ is the integral of angular velocity, with no kinematic coupling.

$$\theta' = \theta + \omega \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Position update (requires the mid-point trick).**
If we naively write $x' = x + v\Delta t \cos(\theta)$, the error grows linearly with $\Delta t$ because the heading changes *during* the interval. The mid-point method evaluates the heading at the *middle* of the interval, $\theta + \dfrac{\omega \Delta t}{2}$, achieving second-order accuracy.

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{mid}} &= \theta + \frac{\omega \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} \\[4pt]
x' &= x + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos(\theta_{\text{mid}}) \\[4pt]
y' &= y + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin(\theta_{\text{mid}})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\Delta s = v \cdot \Delta t$ to obtain the KTU canonical form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= x + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
y' &= y + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
\theta' &= \theta + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **standard KTU 2024 Scheme mid-point odometry update equation.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheel base $L = 0.30$ m. In a sampling interval $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, the left encoder reads 8 ticks and the right encoder reads 24 ticks. Encoder resolution $N = 1000$ ticks/rev. The initial pose is $(0, 0, 0)$. Compute the new pose.

**Step 1 — Convert ticks to angular displacement.**

$$\Delta\phi_L = \frac{2\pi \cdot 8}{1000} = 0.050265 \text{ rad} \qquad \Delta\phi_R = \frac{2\pi \cdot 24}{1000} = 0.150796 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Compute wheel linear displacements.**

$$\Delta s_L = r \cdot \Delta\phi_L = 0.05 \cdot 0.050265 = 0.002513 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta s_R = r \cdot \Delta\phi_R = 0.05 \cdot 0.150796 = 0.007540 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta s$ and $\Delta\theta$.**

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} = \frac{0.007540 + 0.002513}{2} = 0.005026 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L} = \frac{0.007540 - 0.002513}{0.30} = 0.016756 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the mid-point update.**

$$\theta_{\text{mid}} = 0 + \frac{0.016756}{2} = 0.008378 \text{ rad}$$

$$x' = 0 + 0.005026 \cdot \cos(0.008378) = 0.005026 \cdot 0.999965 = 0.005026 \text{ m}$$

$$y' = 0 + 0.005026 \cdot \sin(0.008378) = 0.005026 \cdot 0.008378 = 0.0000421 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta' = 0 + 0.016756 = 0.016756 \text{ rad} \approx 0.96^\circ$$

> ✅ **Final Pose:** $P' = (0.00503 \text{ m},\; 0.0000421 \text{ m},\; 0.0168 \text{ rad})$. The robot has moved ~5 mm and turned ~1° to the left.

---

### 3.4 Sources of Error in Odometry (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners love this topic for 7-mark Part B sub-questions.** Memorize the *cause → effect* linkage.

| Error Source | Systematic vs. Random | Effect on Pose |
|--------------|:---------------------:|----------------|
| Unequal wheel diameters | Systematic | Constant heading bias (drift) |
| Wheel slip / skid | Random | Sudden jump in position estimate |
| Finite encoder resolution | Systematic (quantization) | Staircase drift in $\theta$ |
| Sampling rate too low | Random | Undersampling of high-speed turns |
| Off-axle encoder mounting | Systematic | Scale error in $\Delta s$ |
| Floor surface deformation | Random | Variable effective $r$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU's preferred remedy:** Fuse odometry with an **IMU** using an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) — addressed in higher KTU modules.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Odometry Sensor System

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of an odometry system — from raw encoder pulse to global pose output. This is the kind of flow expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[WL: Left Wheel Encoder]:::sensor -->|Pulse Train nL| B[Quadrature Decoder x4]:::proc
    C[WR: Right Wheel Encoder]:::sensor -->|Pulse Train nR| B
    B -->|Raw tick counts nL, nR| D[Tick to Angle Converter]:::proc
    D -->|Delta phi L, Delta phi R| E[Forward Kinematics Block]:::proc
    E -->|Linear v and Angular omega| F[Mid Point Integrator]:::proc
    F -->|Updated pose x y theta| G[Global Pose Buffer]:::out
    E -->|Velocity feedback| H[Robot Motion Controller]:::out
    G --> I[ROS nav msgs Odometry Publisher]:::out

    classDef sensor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Pose Update Cycle (Per Sampling Tick)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Current Pose x y theta]:::st --> Q1[Read nL and nR from encoders]:::st
    Q1 --> Q2[Compute Delta phi L and Delta phi R]:::st
    Q2 --> Q3[Compute Delta s and Delta theta]:::st
```
