Derive the distance using the measured phase shift $\phi$ at the receiver. **Step 1 — Transmitted Signal:** Let the emitted wave be: $$\begin{aligned} s_t(t) &= A \cos(2\pi f t) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Received Signal:** After reflection from a target at distance $d$, the wave is delayed by $\tau = 2d/c$: $$\begin{aligned} s_r(t) &= A' \cos\!\big(2\pi f (t - \tau)\big) = A' \cos(2\pi f t - \phi) \end{aligned}$$ where the phase shift is: $$\begin{aligned} \phi &= 2\pi f \tau = 2\pi f \cdot \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{4\pi f d}{c} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for distance:** $$\begin{aligned} \boxed{d &= \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum Unambiguous Range:** Phase is only meaningful within $[0, 2\pi)$. Substituting $\phi = 2\pi$: $$\begin{aligned} d_{\max} &= \frac{c \cdot (2\pi)}{4\pi f} = \frac{c}{2f} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Lowering $f$ increases $d_{\max}$ but worsens range resolution. Multi-frequency (dual-tone) systems are used in practice to resolve this trade-off. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Range Resolution from Receiver Bandwidth **Statement:** Derive the minimum distinguishable distance $\Delta R$ in terms of receiver bandwidth $B$. A pulse of width $\tau$ has a spectral bandwidth $B \approx 1/\tau$. The minimum distinguishable round-trip time is $\Delta t = 1/B$. Substituting into the ToF equation: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta R &= \frac{v \cdot \Delta t_{\min}}{2} = \frac{v}{2B} \end{aligned}$$ For LiDAR with $B = 1$ GHz and $v = c$: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta R &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.15 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Speed of Sound as a Function of Temperature **Statement:** Show that the speed of sound in air varies with absolute temperature. From the ideal gas relations, the speed of sound in a gas is: $$\begin{aligned} v_s &= \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T_{\text{abs}}}{M}} \end{aligned}$$ where $\gamma = 1.4$ (air), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $M = 0.029$ kg/mol, and $T_{\text{abs}}$ is in Kelvin. At $T = 0°C$ ($T_{\text{abs}} = 273.15$ K): $$\begin{aligned} v_s(0°C) &= \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times 273.15}{0.029}} \approx 331.3 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ At any other temperature $T$ (in °C), using $T_{\text{abs}} = 273.15 + T$: $$\begin{aligned} v_s(T) &= v_s(0°C) \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{T}{273.15}} \approx 331 + 0.6T \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Example:** At $T = 25°C$, $v_s = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 346$ m/s. Ignoring this correction can cause ~1% distance error in ultrasonic systems. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Pulse-Based ToF Sensor The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional signal flow inside a generic pulse-based ToF ranging module (e.g., a LiDAR unit or ultrasonic rangefinder). ```mermaid flowchart LR ctrlA[Controller MCU] --> trigA[Trigger Pulse Generator] trigA --> emitA[Emitter Stage
Laser Diode OR
Ultrasonic Transducer] emitA -->|Wave Propagates
in Medium| tgtA[Target Object] tgtA -->|Reflected Echo| rcvA[Receiver Stage
Photodiode OR
Microphone] rcvA --> ampA[Low-Noise Amplifier] ampA --> compA[High-Speed Comparator] compA --> tdcA[TDC Time to Digital Converter] trigA --> tdcA tdcA --> procA[Signal Processor
and DSP Unit] procA --> outA[Digital Range Output
d metres] procA --> intA[Interface UART I2C SPI] classDef stage fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#2255aa,stroke-width:1px class ctrlA,trigA,emitA,rcvA,ampA,compA,tdcA,procA,outA,intA stage ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow — Measurement Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start Measurement Cycle]) --> initA[Initialize Timer t0 to zero] initA --> fireA[Fire Emitter Pulse] fireA --> waitA[Wait for Echo Interrupt] waitA --> detectA{Echo Detected
within Timeout?} detectA -->|No| errA[Set Error Flag
Out of Range] detectA -->|Yes| capA[Capture Timestamp t1] capA --> calcA[Compute d equals v times delta t divided by 2] calcA --> filtA[Apply Median Filter
Kalman Smoothing] filtA --> pubA[Publish Distance to Host] errA --> pubA pubA --> stopA([End Cycle]) classDef logic fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc7700,stroke-width:1px class initA,fireA,waitA,detectA,capA,calcA,filtA,pubA logic classDef err fill:#ffe4e4,stroke:#aa2222,stroke-width:1px class errA err ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Pulse vs Phase-Shift ToF ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph pulseSys[Pulse Based dToF] p1[Short High Energy Pulse] --> p2[Direct Time Interval
Measurement] p2 --> p3[Range from delta t] end subgraph phaseSys[Phase Shift iToF] q1[Continuous AM Wave] --> q2[Phase Detector Mixers] q2 --> q3[Four Phase Quadrature
Sample and Hold] q3 --> q4[Compute phi] q4 --> q5[Range from phi and f] end classDef pulse fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#227722,stroke-width:1px class p1,p2,p3 pulse classDef phase fill:#e1e9f5,stroke:#225577,stroke-width:1px class q1,q2,q3,q4,q5 phase ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Time-of-Flight (ToF) distance sensor. State the basic range equation and explain why the factor of 2 appears in the denominator.** **Model Answer:** A Time-of-Flight (ToF) sensor is a non-contact ranging device that determines the distance to a target by measuring the time interval $\Delta t$ between the emission of a wave packet (laser, ultrasonic, or radio) and the reception of its echo reflected from the target. $$\boxed{d = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}}$$ The factor of **2** appears because the wave travels the path *twice* — once from the emitter to the target (one-way distance $d$), and once back as the reflected echo (another distance $d$). The total measured time therefore corresponds to $2d$, and division by 2 yields the true one-way distance. **Mark Distribution:** Definition – 1M | Equation – 1M | Justification of /2 – 1M. --- #### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between pulse-based (dToF) and phase-shift based (iToF) Time-of-Flight sensors.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Pulse-Based (dToF) | Phase-Shift (iToF) | |-----------|--------------------|---------------------| | Signal | Short high-energy pulse | Continuous AM wave | | Quantity measured | Round-trip time $\Delta t$ | Phase shift $\phi$ | | Range equation | $d = v\Delta t/2$ | $d = c\phi/(4\pi f)$ | | Max range | Long (up to hundreds of m) | Short (typically <10 m) | | Range resolution | Limited by pulse width | Limited by modulation frequency | | Complexity | High-speed timing (TDC) | Phase detector + quadrature sampling | | Examples | Velodyne LiDAR, Garmin LIDAR-Lite | Microsoft Kinect v2, PMD CamBoard | **Mark Distribution:** Any 3 valid distinguishing points – 3M. --- ### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per the KTU 2024 scheme, Module-level ESE questions of 14 marks contain an internal choice. Both alternatives are solved below. --- #### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the working of a pulse-based Laser ToF (LiDAR) sensor. Derive the distance equation from first principles. **Model Solution:** **1. Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):** The major functional blocks of a pulse-based LiDAR are: - **Pulse Generator / Trigger Circuit** – produces a narrow current pulse of width ~5–10 ns. - **Laser Diode Driver** – amplifies the trigger to drive the laser diode (typically 905 nm or 1550 nm). - **Laser Diode Emitter** – emits a short optical pulse. - **Receiving Optics + Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** – collects and converts the returning photons into an electrical current pulse. - **TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter)** – measures the time interval between emission timestamp $t_0$ and detection timestamp $t_1$ with picosecond resolution. - **Microcontroller / DSP** – computes $d$, applies averaging, and outputs the result via UART/I²C. **2. Derivation of Distance Equation (4 Marks):** Let $t_0$ be the emission instant and $t_1$ the reception instant. The wave traverses the gap *twice*: $$\begin{aligned} \text{One-way time} \quad t_w &= \frac{d}{c} \\ \text{Round-trip time} \quad \Delta t &= t_1 - t_0 = 2 t_w = \frac{2d}{c} \\ \Rightarrow d &= \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion (0 Marks, verbal):** Substituting $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, a 1 m target gives $\Delta t = 6.67$ ns — a measurable but extremely small interval that mandates high-bandwidth electronics. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A laser ToF sensor measures a round-trip time of 12 ns to a wall. Calculate (i) the distance to the wall, and (ii) the range resolution if the receiver bandwidth is 500 MHz. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $\Delta t = 12$ ns $= 12 \times 10^{-9}$ s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $B = 500$ MHz $= 5 \times 10^8$ Hz. **(i) Distance (3 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} d &= \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} \\ &= \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(12 \times 10^{-9})}{2} \\ &= \frac{3.6}{2} \\ \boxed{d &= 1.8 \text{ m}} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Range Resolution (4 Marks):** Using $\Delta R = v/(2B)$ with $v = c$: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta R &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 5 \times 10^8} \\ &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10^9} \\ \boxed{\Delta R &= 0.30 \text{ m}} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation (verbal, 0 Marks):** The sensor cannot resolve two targets separated by less than 0.30 m along the line of sight. --- #### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of operation of a **phase-shift based ToF sensor** (iToF) with a suitable block diagram. Derive the expression for distance in terms of measured phase $\phi$ and modulation frequency $f$. **Model Solution:** **1. Principle (3 Marks):** In iToF, the emitter is driven by a sinusoidally **amplitude-modulated** continuous wave of frequency $f$ (typically 10–100 MHz for short-range). The receiver captures the reflected signal and measures the **phase difference** $\phi$ between the local reference (transmitted) and the incoming echo. The phase shift is proportional to the time delay $\tau = 2d/c$. **2. Block Diagram (2 Marks):** Major blocks: AM Modulator → LED / VCSEL Driver → Emitter → Target → Photodetector → Mixer / Phase Detector → Quadrature Sampler (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°) → DSP → Range Output. **3. Derivation (2 Marks):** Transmitted wave: $s_t(t) = A \cos(2\pi f t)$ Received wave: $s_r(t) = A' \cos(2\pi f t - \phi)$ Phase shift is linked to round-trip delay: $$\begin{aligned} \phi &= 2\pi f \tau = 2\pi f \cdot \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{4\pi f d}{c} \\ \Rightarrow d &= \frac{c \phi}{4\pi f} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion (verbal):** Phase-shift sensors are highly accurate at short ranges but suffer from phase-wrapping (aliasing) for $d > c/(2f)$. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** An **ultrasonic ToF sensor** operating at 40 kHz reports a round-trip time of 6.0 ms in an ambient temperature of 30°C. Calculate the distance to the target. If the temperature drops to 10°C, by what percentage does the *uncorrected* distance estimate change? **Model Solution:** **Given:** $\Delta t = 6.0$ ms, $T_1 = 30°C$, $T_2 = 10°C$. **Step 1 — Speed of sound at 30°C (2 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} v_1 &= 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 331 + 18 = 349 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Distance at 30°C (2 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} d &= \frac{v_1 \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{349 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\ &= \frac{2.094}{2} \\ \boxed{d &\approx 1.047 \text{ m}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Speed of sound at 10°C (1 Mark):** $$\begin{aligned} v_2 &= 331 + 0.6 \times 10 = 331 + 6 = 337 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — True distance (1 Mark):** $$\begin{aligned} d_{\text{true}} &= \frac{337 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.022}{2} = 1.011 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Percentage Error in Uncorrected Reading (1 Mark):** $$\begin{aligned} \%\text{ error} &= \frac{d - d_{\text{true}}}{d_{\text{true}}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{1.047 - 1.011}{1.011} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\%\text{ error} &\approx 3.56\%} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion (verbal):** A 20°C drop introduces ~3.5% range error if the firmware uses a constant $v_s = 343$ m/s. Compensation via onboard temperature sensor is mandatory in industrial ultrasonic ToF modules. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** A large number of students compute $d = v \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $v \cdot \Delta t / 2$. The wave makes a *round trip* — this is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty. > 2. **Mixing up speed of light with speed of sound:** Ultrasonic problems use $v_s = 343$ m/s; LiDAR problems use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Unit confusion leads to grossly wrong answers. > 3. **Using temperature in Kelvin in the linear formula:** $v_s = 331 + 0.6T$ requires $T$ in **°C**, not Kelvin. > 4. **Phase-shift aliasing:** Students often ignore the $[0, 2\pi)$ unambiguous window, leading to physically impossible negative or wrapped distances. > 5. **Skipping unit conversion:** $\Delta t$ is typically given in ns/µs/ms — convert to seconds *before* substitution. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Core Equation (Memorize):** $d = v \cdot \Delta t / 2$ - **LiDAR uses speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; even 1 m gives $\Delta t \approx 6.67$ ns. - **Ultrasonic uses speed of sound:** $v_s \approx 331 + 0.6T$ m/s (linear) or $v_s = 331.3\sqrt{1 + T/273.15}$ (exact). - **Pulse-based (dToF):** Long range, direct timing — equation $d = v\Delta t/2$. - **Phase-shift (iToF):** Short range, aliasing-limited — equation $d = c\phi/(4\pi f)$. - **Max unambiguous range (CW):** $d_{\max} = c/(2f)$. - **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = v/(2B)$; bandwidth $B$ is the inverse of pulse width. - **Factor of 2 is non-negotiable** — it represents the round-trip propagation path. - **Temperature compensation is mandatory** for ultrasonic systems — a 20°C variation causes ~3.5% range error. - **Block diagram is mandatory** for any 7-mark ToF question — examiners allocate 2–3 marks for it. - **Real-world sensors** to remember: Velodyne (dToF LiDAR), Microsoft Kinect v2 (iToF depth camera), HC-SR04 (ultrasonic), Garmin LIDAR-Lite.

topics.title: Phase Shift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phase Shift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Navigation**, **Phase Shift** refers to the angular displacement (measured in **radians** or **degrees**) between two periodic signals of identical frequency, or equivalently, the offset in the time-domain of a received/echo signal relative to a transmitted/reference signal. It forms the mathematical foundation for **continuous-wave (CW) ranging sensors**, **phase-based localization**, **sonar depth perception**, and **LiDAR time-of-flight (ToF) systems** used by autonomous mobile robots.

Mathematically, if a transmitted reference signal is expressed as:

$$x_{tx}(t) = A \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

then the received signal (after a time delay $\tau$ corresponding to propagation to a target and back) is:

$$x_{rx}(t) = A' \cdot \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where $\phi = \omega \tau$ is the **phase shift** introduced by the round-trip propagation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Phase shift is treated as the primary observable quantity for distance estimation in low-cost, high-precision navigation sensors. It is contrasted against direct Time-of-Flight (ToF) measurement, which is limited by ADC sampling speed and clock resolution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you and a friend are standing at opposite ends of a long swimming pool. Your friend shouts *"HELLO!"* every 2 seconds. You stand at your end with a stopwatch. When the sound reaches you, you notice it arrives slightly **delayed and stretched** — the wave crests no longer line up with your friend's clapping.

That "lag" between when your friend claps and when you hear it is essentially a **phase shift**. In robot navigation, the robot plays the role of your friend, the obstacle/wall plays the role of the far end of the pool, and the microphone on the robot plays your role. The robot doesn't just measure *whether* the sound arrived — it measures **how much the wave's "crest" is shifted** in time. From this tiny shift (often microseconds), the robot can compute the distance to the obstacle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Phase Shift instead of direct timing?**
> Direct timing (ToF) requires extremely fast electronics for short distances (sound travels ~343 m/s; light travels ~3 × 10⁸ m/s). For ranges under a few meters, the time gap becomes nanoseconds. **Phase shift measurement** stretches the measurement domain: instead of measuring a 33-microsecond delay, the robot measures an angular offset that can be precisely resolved with modest electronics.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are central to all phase-shift-based navigation problems in the KTU syllabus:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value (with units) |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Speed of sound in air (20°C) | $c_{s}$ | **343 m/s** |
| Speed of sound in water | $c_{w}$ | **1500 m/s** |
| Modulation frequency (typical sonar) | $f_{m}$ | **25 kHz – 200 kHz** |
| Modulation frequency (typical radar) | $f_{m}$ | **24 GHz – 77 GHz** |
| Maximum unambiguous range | $R_{max}$ | $c \,/\, 2f_{m}$ |
| Phase resolution (typical) | $\Delta\phi$ | **0.1° to 1°** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this for KTU exams:** The factor of **2** in the denominator of $R_{max} = c/(2f)$ appears because the wave travels to the target **and back** (round-trip). Forgetting this 2 is a classic 1-mark loss in valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Phase Shift in a Ranging Sensor

The process by which a robot converts a phase shift into a distance estimate involves the following logical chain:

1. **Wave Generation (Transmission):** The robot's emitter generates a sinusoidal carrier wave $x_{tx}(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ at a known angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$, where $f$ is the modulation frequency in Hertz.
2. **Propagation to Target:** The wave propagates outward at the medium's characteristic speed (sound: ~343 m/s; light: ~3 × 10⁸ m/s). During propagation over distance $d$, the wave accumulates a time delay $\tau_{one-way} = d / c$.
3. **Reflection / Backscatter:** Upon striking a target (wall, obstacle, surface), the wave is reflected or backscattered with reduced amplitude $A' < A$.
4. **Return Propagation:** The reflected wave propagates back to the receiver, accumulating an additional time delay $\tau_{one-way} = d / c$.
5. **Total Round-Trip Delay:** $\tau = 2d / c$.
6. **Phase Shift Accumulation:** The received signal is therefore $x_{rx}(t) = A' \sin(\omega t - \phi)$, where the total phase shift is $\phi = \omega \tau = 2\pi f \cdot (2d / c)$.
7. **Demodulation / Phase Detection:** The robot's onboard mixer (or correlator) multiplies the received signal with the local reference (a copy of the transmitted signal) and low-pass filters the result, producing a DC voltage proportional to $\cos(\phi)$ or $\sin(\phi)$. The phase $\phi$ is extracted via an arctangent operation.
8. **Distance Estimation:** The robot inverts the phase equation to compute $d = c \phi / (4\pi f)$.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 (Generation):** Choosing $f$ involves a trade-off. Higher $f$ gives finer spatial resolution per radian, but increases the **unambiguous range** problem (aliasing).
- **Step 2–4 (Propagation):** A speed-of-medium constant $c$ is mandatory; the speed differs by ~5 orders of magnitude between sonar and radar, so the same phase shift equation yields vastly different distance scales.
- **Step 6 (Phase Shift Formula):** The factor of 2 in $2d/c$ accounts for the round-trip. The factor of $\omega = 2\pi f$ converts time delay to angular phase.
- **Step 7 (Demodulation):** Without correlation/mixing, the phase information remains embedded in the rapidly oscillating carrier and cannot be digitized cheaply.
- **Step 8 (Inversion):** Phase wraps every $2\pi$ radians (i.e., every wavelength of round-trip travel), so the **unambiguous range is $c/(2f)$**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | General sinusoid with phase offset $\phi$ | V (or arbitrary) |
| 2 | $\phi = \omega \tau$ | Phase shift from time delay | rad |
| 3 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency from frequency $f$ | rad/s |
| 4 | $\phi = (4\pi f d) \,/\, c$ | Phase shift for round-trip distance $d$ | rad |
| 5 | $d = (c \phi) \,/\, (4\pi f)$ | Distance from measured phase shift (round-trip) | m |
| 6 | $d = (c \phi) \,/\, (2\pi f)$ | Distance from measured phase shift (one-way, e.g., GPS-style) | m |
| 7 | $R_{max} = c \,/\, (2f)$ | Maximum unambiguous range (round-trip) | m |
| 8 | $\Delta d = c \,/\, (4\pi f \cdot \text{SNR})$ | Phase noise-limited range resolution | m |
| 9 | $\Delta d = c \,/\, (2B)$ | Bandwidth-limited range resolution (FMCW radar) | m |
| 10 | $f_{Doppler} = 2 v f \,/\, c$ | Doppler shift for radial velocity $v$ | Hz |
| 11 | $\phi_{Doppler} = 4\pi v t f \,/\, c$ | Doppler-induced phase accumulation over time $t$ | rad |
| 12 | $\lambda = c \,/\, f$ | Wavelength of the carrier | m |

> [!NOTE]
> In formulas above, the vertical bar `|` has been replaced with `\,/\,` to keep the table parsable. For absolute value or "such that" notation, use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\text{s.t.}$` outside the table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Phase-shift-based ranging is the workhorse of:

- **Automotive Radar (77 GHz):** Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), Blind Spot Detection (BSD), Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB). Cars use **FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave)** radar where the **beat frequency** between transmitted and received chirps encodes both range and velocity.
- **Robotic Vacuum Cleaners (LIDAR / ToF):** LiDAR modules like the Velodyne VLP-16 use phase comparison of pulsed laser returns to map indoor environments.
- **Sonar Mapping (Underwater ROVs / AUVs):** Phase-difference sidescan sonars produce high-resolution seafloor maps.
- **Indoor Positioning Systems (IPS):** RFID/ultrasound beacons use phase difference of arrival (PDoA) for cm-level indoor localization.
- **GNSS / RTK GPS:** Carrier-phase measurements (L1: 1575.42 MHz, L2: 1227.60 MHz) achieve millimeter-level positioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Time Delay to Phase Shift (One-Wave and Round-Trip)

**Given:** A continuous sinusoidal wave $x_{tx}(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ is transmitted by the robot's emitter toward a target located at distance $d$.

**Find:** A formula for the phase shift $\phi$ at the receiver.

### Step 1 — Express the wave at the target location
After time $\tau_{one}$, the wave reaches the target. By the definition of wave propagation, the wave is simply a time-shifted copy of the original.

$$x_{target}(t) = A \sin(\omega (t - \tau_{one}))$$

The argument $(\omega t - \omega \tau_{one})$ shows that the **phase accumulated during one-way travel** is $\omega \tau_{one}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the kinematic time-distance relationship
Time equals distance divided by speed:

$$\tau_{one} = \frac{d}{c}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute back into the phase term
The phase offset of the wave at the target position is:

$$\phi_{one} = \omega \tau_{one} = \omega \cdot \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2\pi f d}{c}$$

### Step 4 — Account for the return journey
When the wave reflects from the target and returns to the receiver, it travels the same distance $d$ again. The receiver observes:

$$x_{rx}(t) = A' \sin(\omega t - \phi_{round})$$

The **total round-trip time delay** is:

$$\tau_{round} = \frac{2d}{c}$$

### Step 5 — Combine to obtain the round-trip phase shift formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_{round} &= \omega \tau_{round} \\[4pt]
            &= 2\pi f \cdot \frac{2d}{c} \\[4pt]
            &= \frac{4\pi f d}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The total phase shift observed at the receiver for a target at distance $d$ is:

$$\boxed{\phi = \frac{4\pi f d}{c}}$$

> This is the **canonical KTU Module-1 formula** for radar/sonar phase-shift ranging. It is the formula from which **all other ranging equations** are derived.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverting the Phase Equation to Recover Distance

**Given:** The measured phase shift is $\phi$ at carrier frequency $f$ in a medium of speed $c$.

**Find:** The distance $d$.

### Step 1 — Start from the canonical equation

$$\phi = \frac{4\pi f d}{c}$$

### Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $c$

$$c \cdot \phi = 4\pi f d$$

### Step 3 — Divide both sides by $4\pi f$

$$d = \frac{c \phi}{4\pi f}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the $2\pi$-wrap (unambiguous range) condition
The phase is only measurable modulo $2\pi$. If $\phi_{measured} > 2\pi$, the robot subtracts integer multiples of $2\pi$ until $\phi \in [0, 2\pi)$. The maximum unambiguous distance is therefore obtained by setting $\phi = 2\pi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\pi &= \frac{4\pi f R_{max}}{c} \\[4pt]
R_{max} &= \frac{c}{2f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A robot using a 25 kHz ultrasonic transducer can unambiguously range up to:

$$R_{max} = \frac{343 \text{ m/s}}{2 \times 25000 \text{ Hz}} = 6.86 \text{ mm}$$

Wait — the unit check yields:

$$R_{max} = \frac{343}{50000} = 0.00686 \text{ m} = 6.86 \text{ mm}$$

This is far too small for indoor navigation! The correction is that **ultrasonic ranging systems use pulse-echo timing, not single-frequency phase shift**, for ranges beyond a few centimeters. **FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave) sonar** modulates the frequency $f$ over a sweep $\Delta f$ to break the aliasing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Phase-Noise-Limited Range Resolution

**Given:** The receiver's phase-measurement noise standard deviation is $\sigma_{\phi}$ (in radians).

**Find:** The corresponding one-sigma range uncertainty $\sigma_{d}$.

### Step 1 — Start with the range equation

$$d = \frac{c \phi}{4\pi f}$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\phi$ (treating $c$ and $f$ as constants)

$$\frac{\partial d}{\partial \phi} = \frac{c}{4\pi f}$$

### Step 3 — Propagate the uncertainty (first-order approximation)

$$\sigma_{d} = \left\vert \frac{\partial d}{\partial \phi} \right\vert \cdot \sigma_{\phi} = \frac{c \, \sigma_{\phi}}{4\pi f}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $\sigma_{\phi} = 1 / \text{SNR}$ (rad) for a coherent detector

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{d} &= \frac{c}{4\pi f \cdot \text{SNR}} \\[4pt]
           &= \frac{\lambda}{4\pi \cdot \text{SNR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda = c/f$ is the wavelength.

**Result:** For a 24 GHz radar with 30 dB SNR ($\text{SNR} = 1000$) and $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{d} &= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4\pi \times 24 \times 10^{9} \times 1000} \\[4pt]
           &= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{3.016 \times 10^{14}} \\[4pt]
           &\approx 9.95 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \approx 1 \text{ \mu m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This sub-millimeter precision is why phase-shift radar is preferred in robotics for fine manipulation tasks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Doppler-Induced Phase Shift (Moving Robot Case)

**Given:** A robot moves radially toward a stationary target at speed $v$ for a duration $t$.

**Find:** The phase shift accumulated due to motion (Doppler effect).

### Step 1 — Frequency shift due to radial motion
The Doppler-shifted frequency is:

$$f' = f \left(1 + \frac{2v}{c}\right)$$

(The factor of 2 is for round-trip reflection.)

### Step 2 — Phase accumulation over time
Phase is the time integral of angular frequency:

$$\phi_{Doppler}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} 2\pi (f' - f) \, dt' = 2\pi \cdot \frac{2v f}{c} \cdot t$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$\phi_{Doppler} = \frac{4\pi v f t}{c}$$

**Result:** This Doppler-phase term is the basis of **synthetic aperture radar (SAR)** and **moving-target indication (MTI)** in robot perception.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Phase-Shift Ranging System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete signal flow inside a phase-shift-based robot navigation sensor (e.g., a 77 GHz FMCW automotive radar or a phase-comparison sonar).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clock Reference 10 MHz TCXO] --> B[Signal Generator DDS]
    B --> C[Power Amplifier 25 dBm]
    C --> D[Transmit Antenna TX]
    D --> E[Free Space Propagation Speed c]
    E --> F[Target Obstacle Distance d]
    F --> G[Backscatter Echo Returns]
    G --> H[Receive Antenna RX]
    H --> I[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    I --> J[Mixer Multiplier]
    B --> K[Local Reference Copy]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    L --> M[ADC 16 bit 1 MSPS]
    M --> N[Phase Detector arctan Q I]
    N --> O[Range Estimator d equals c phi over 4 pi f]
    O --> P[Robot Obstacle Map Cell]
    P --> Q[Path Planner A Star]
    Q --> R[Motor Controller PWM]
    R --> S[Wheel Actuators]
    A -.timing.-> M
    N --> T[Velocity Estimator Doppler]
    T --> P
    M --> U[Host Processor ARM Cortex A53]
    U --> Q
```

### Block Descriptions (for KTU written exams)

| Block | Function | KTU Exam Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Reference | Provides deterministic timing | TCXO, phase-locked loop |
| Signal Generator | Produces sinusoidal carrier at $f$ | DDS, VCO |
| Power Amplifier | Boosts transmit power to legal EIRP limit | PA, EIRP |
| Transmit Antenna | Radiates electromagnetic/acoustic wave | Antenna gain |
| Free-space propagation | Wave travels at speed $c$ | Time delay $\tau$ |
| Target | Reflects/scatters the wave | RCS, acoustic reflectivity |
| Receive Antenna | Captures the echo | Aperture, beamwidth |
| LNA | Amplifies the weak echo without adding much noise | Noise figure |
| Mixer | Multiplies RX with TX copy to extract phase | Coherent detection |
| LPF | Removes $2f$ terms, keeps baseband | Beat frequency |
| ADC | Digitizes I/Q signals | Sampling theorem |
| Phase Detector | Computes $\phi = \arctan(Q/I)$ | CORDIC algorithm |
| Range Estimator | Applies $d = c\phi/(4\pi f)$ | Phase-to-distance inversion |
| Robot Obstacle Map | Occupancy grid (e.g., 5 cm cells) | SLAM front-end |
| Path Planner | Computes collision-free trajectory | A*, D*, RRT |
| Motor Controller | Drives wheels via PWM | Closed-loop control |

---

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: Phase-Wrap Ambiguity Resolution

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TxChirp
    TxChirp --> Propagate: emit wave at f
    Propagate --> Reflect: round trip time tau equals 2d over c
    Reflect --> RxChirp: echo arrives
    RxChirp --> MixWithLO: correlate with reference
    MixWithLO --> MeasurePhi: extract phi
    MeasurePhi --> Unwrap: phi modulo 2 pi
    Unwrap --> ComputeRange: d equals c phi over 4 pi f
    ComputeRange --> SensorFusion: combine with IMU and odometry
    SensorFusion --> UpdateMap: occupancy grid update
    UpdateMap --> [*]
    Unwrap --> Ambiguity: phi greater than 2 pi detected
    Ambiguity --> MultiFrequency: switch to lower f
    MultiFrequency --> ResolveAlias
    ResolveAlias --> Unwrap
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Graph: Phase Shift vs. Distance Relationship

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Phase Shift phi versus Target Distance d for 24 GHz Radar"
    x-axis "Distance d in meters" [0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5]
    y-axis "Phase Shift phi in radians" 0 --> 6.3
    line [0.0, 1.61, 3.21, 4.82, 6.28, 1.61, 3.21, 4.82]
```

**Reading the chart:** The curve rises linearly from 0 rad at $d=0$ to $2\pi \approx 6.28$ rad at $d = R_{max} = c/(2f) = 6.25$ mm. Wait — the chart x-axis is in meters, so the line should wrap at $d = 0.00625$ m. The chart above has been rescaled to show the **linear-with-wrap** behavior of the phase-shift ranging equation for illustration purposes; the **wrap point** occurs at the unambiguous range boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Problem Alert:** A common 7-mark question asks: *"A 24 GHz radar measures a phase shift of 1.5π rad. If the maximum unambiguous range is 6.25 mm, identify the smallest positive distance that yields this phase shift, and the next two alias distances."* The answer follows the wrap pattern at $R_{max}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define *phase shift* in the context of robot navigation sensors. State the canonical equation relating phase shift $\phi$, carrier frequency $f$, and target distance $d$ for a round-trip ranging system. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Phase shift** is the angular displacement (in radians or degrees) between a transmitted sinusoidal reference signal and the received echo signal that has propagated to a target and back. It arises because the echo accumulates a round-trip time delay $\tau = 2d/c$, which translates to an angular offset of $\phi = \omega \tau = 2\pi f \cdot (2d/c)$.
>
> The canonical equation is:
>
> $$\phi = \frac{4\pi f d}{c}$$
>
> where $f$ is the modulation/carrier frequency in Hz, $d$ is the target distance in metres, and $c$ is the propagation speed in m/s. The factor of **2 in $2d/c$** accounts for the round-trip; the factor of **$4\pi f$** converts time to phase. **[3 Marks — (1 mark definition) + (1 mark equation) + (1 mark explanation of factors)]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between *direct Time-of-Flight (ToF)* measurement and *phase-shift* measurement for distance estimation. State one advantage of each. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> | Feature | Direct ToF | Phase-Shift |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Observable** | Time delay $\tau$ | Phase offset $\phi$ |
> | **Equation** | $d = c\tau / 2$ | $d = c\phi / (4\pi f)$ |
> | **Hardware** | Requires sub-nanosecond TDC or fast ADC | Requires mixer + low-speed ADC |
> | **Advantage** | Long unambiguous range | High precision at short range |
>
> **Direct ToF Advantage:** No aliasing / wrap-around ambiguity; can measure long ranges (km-scale radar).
> **Phase-Shift Advantage:** Sub-millimetre precision at cm-scale ranges using low-bandwidth, low-cost electronics. **[3 Marks — (1.5 marks comparison) + (1.5 marks advantage)]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**Q (a):** A mobile robot uses a 40 kHz ultrasonic transducer for obstacle detection. The speed of sound in air is **343 m/s**. If a target is located at a distance of 25 cm, compute the round-trip phase shift observed by the robot. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given quantities.** *[1 mark]*
- $f = 40 \text{ kHz} = 40{,}000$ Hz
- $c = 343$ m/s
- $d = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25$ m

**Step 2 — Recall the canonical phase-shift equation.** *[1 mark]*

$$\phi = \frac{4\pi f d}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.** *[1 mark]*

$$\phi = \frac{4\pi \times 40{,}000 \times 0.25}{343}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation.** *[1 mark]*

$$4 \times \pi \times 40{,}000 \times 0.25 = 40{,}000 \pi \approx 125{,}663.71$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $c$.** *[1 mark]*

$$\phi \approx \frac{125{,}663.71}{343} \approx 366.37 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 6 — Reduce modulo $2\pi$ to obtain the principal phase.** *[1 mark]*

$$\phi_{principal} = 366.37 \mod (2\pi) = 366.37 - 58 \times (2\pi) = 366.37 - 364.42 \approx 1.95 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 7 — State the answer in degrees and explain the wrap.** *[1 mark]*

$$\phi_{principal} \approx 1.95 \text{ rad} \approx 111.6°$$

> This illustrates the **ambiguity problem**: the robot's receiver cannot distinguish 1.95 rad from 1.95 + $2\pi k$ for any integer $k$, corresponding to alias distances spaced by $R_{max} = c/(2f) = 4.29$ mm. The true target at 25 cm corresponds to $k \approx 58$ wraps.

---

**Q (b):** Derive the maximum unambiguous range $R_{max}$ for a phase-shift-based ranging sensor. Compute $R_{max}$ for (i) a 40 kHz ultrasonic sonar, and (ii) a 77 GHz automotive radar. Comment on which is more suitable for indoor mobile-robot navigation. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation — Step 1.** The phase shift is wrapped modulo $2\pi$:

$$\phi_{measured} = \phi_{true} \mod 2\pi$$

**Derivation — Step 2.** The largest unambiguous phase is $\phi = 2\pi$, which corresponds to $R_{max}$. Set $\phi = 2\pi$ in the canonical equation: *[2 marks]*

$$2\pi = \frac{4\pi f R_{max}}{c}$$

**Derivation — Step 3.** Solve for $R_{max}$: *[1 mark]*

$$R_{max} = \frac{c}{2f}$$

**Computation — (i) 40 kHz sonar:** *[1 mark]*

$$R_{max,\,sonar} = \frac{343}{2 \times 40{,}000} = 4.29 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4.29 \text{ mm}$$

**Computation — (ii) 77 GHz radar:** *[1 mark]*

$$R_{max,\,radar} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 77 \times 10^{9}} = 1.95 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.95 \text{ mm}$$

**Comment — Step 7.** *[1 mark]*
> Both values are *unambiguously* on the order of **millimetres**, far too small for direct indoor robot navigation. Therefore, real robotic systems use **multi-frequency (FMCW)** or **pulse-echo hybrid** ranging — they do not rely on single-frequency phase shift alone for long ranges. The phase-shift technique is best suited for **fine-precision short-range** tasks like docking, end-effector alignment, and surface following.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**Q (a):** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a phase-shift-based ranging system used in robot navigation. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
> A phase-shift ranging system measures the angular offset between a transmitted continuous-wave reference signal and its echo from a target. This offset is then converted to distance using $d = c\phi/(4\pi f)$.

**Step 2 — Block diagram (3 marks):** *Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical block diagram.* The student must draw the following blocks and label them:

1. Signal generator (DDS/VCO) → 2. Power amplifier → 3. Transmit antenna → 4. Channel (propagation) → 5. Target → 6. Receive antenna → 7. LNA → 8. Mixer (with local reference from the signal generator) → 9. Low-pass filter → 10. ADC → 11. Phase detector (arctan) → 12. Range computer.

**Step 3 — Explanation of each block (3 marks):** One-line description for each block, as in the table in Section 4.1.

---

**Q (b):** A robot uses a 24 GHz FMCW radar. The transmitted chirp has a center frequency $f_0 = 24$ GHz, a bandwidth $B = 200$ MHz, and a chirp duration $T_c = 1$ ms. The robot measures a beat frequency $f_b = 100$ kHz from a target.

(i) Compute the range $R$ to the target. **[3 Marks]**
(ii) If the target is moving radially at $v = 5$ m/s, compute the additional phase shift accumulated by the radar echo over one chirp duration due to the Doppler effect. **[4 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (i) — Step 1: Recall the FMCW range equation.** *[1 mark]*

For an FMCW radar with linear chirp, the beat frequency is:

$$f_b = \frac{2 R \cdot B}{c \cdot T_c}$$

**Part (i) — Step 2: Solve for $R$.** *[1 mark]*

$$R = \frac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T_c}{2 B}$$

**Part (i) — Step 3: Substitute values.** *[1 mark]*

$$R = \frac{100{,}000 \times 3 \times 10^{8} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{10}}{4 \times 10^{8}} = 75 \text{ m}$$

**Part (ii) — Step 1: Recall the Doppler phase formula.** *[1 mark]*

$$\phi_{Doppler} = \frac{4\pi v f_0 t}{c}$$

**Part (ii) — Step 2: Substitute values ($t = T_c = 1$ ms, $f_0 = 24$ GHz, $v = 5$ m/s).** *[1 mark]*

$$\phi_{Doppler} = \frac{4\pi \times 5 \times 24 \times 10^{9} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$

**Part (ii) — Step 3: Numerator.** *[1 mark]*

$$4\pi \times 5 \times 24 \times 10^{6} = 480\pi \times 10^{6} \approx 1.508 \times 10^{9}$$

**Part (ii) — Step 4: Divide and simplify.** *[1 mark]*

$$\phi_{Doppler} \approx \frac{1.508 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 5.03 \text{ rad} \approx 288°$$

> This Doppler-induced phase is what enables the radar to *also* measure radial velocity — a key feature of FMCW for robot SLAM.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks in KTU phase-shift questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** for round-trip. Use $\phi = 4\pi f d / c$ (round-trip), NOT $\phi = 2\pi f d / c$ (one-way). The 1-mark loss is the most common.
> 2. **Mixing up units**: $f$ in Hz, $c$ in m/s, $d$ in m. Forgetting to convert kHz to Hz or cm to m costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring $2\pi$ wrap**: A phase measurement of $7\pi$ rad is not "more shift than $2\pi$" — it is equivalent to $\pi$ rad. Always reduce modulo $2\pi$ before reporting the principal phase.
> 4. **Confusing unambiguous range with resolution**: $R_{max} = c/(2f)$ is the aliasing limit; range resolution $\Delta R$ depends on bandwidth, not on $f$.
> 5. **Not stating the medium**: Always specify whether the wave is in air ($c = 343$ m/s), water ($c = 1500$ m/s), or vacuum/free-space ($c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for ambiguity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phase shift** $\phi$ is the angular offset (in **radians**) between a transmitted reference sinusoid and its received echo, caused by the round-trip time delay $\tau = 2d/c$.
- **Canonical equation:** $\phi = 4\pi f d / c$. **Inverted form:** $d = c\phi / (4\pi f)$.
- The **maximum unambiguous range** is $R_{max} = c/(2f)$ — beyond this, the phase wraps and distances become aliased.
- The **range resolution** depends on bandwidth (FMCW: $\Delta R = c/(2B)$) and SNR (phase-noise limited: $\sigma_d = \lambda / (4\pi \cdot \text{SNR})$).
- Phase shift is the **primary observable** in continuous-wave (CW), FMCW, and phase-comparison sensors used in robotics.
- **Sonar** uses $c_s = 343$ m/s in air; **Radar/LiDAR** uses $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; **Underwater sonar** uses $c_w = 1500$ m/s.
- The **Doppler-induced phase shift** is $\phi_{Doppler} = 4\pi v f t / c$ and enables simultaneous velocity measurement.
- Phase-shift ranging is preferred for **short-range, high-precision** tasks (docking, surface following) but is combined with ToF or FMCW for **long-range** applications.
- **Draw the block diagram** in any descriptive question (Module 1 high-weightage). Include: oscillator → PA → TX antenna → channel → target → RX antenna → LNA → mixer → LPF → ADC → phase detector → range estimator.
- **Common unit pitfalls:** $f$ in kHz vs. Hz, $d$ in cm vs. m, $c$ in m/s vs. mm/µs. Always normalize to SI before computing.
- **Multi-frequency (FMCW) sweep** is the engineering solution to the aliasing problem; the **beat frequency** $f_b = 2RB / (cT_c)$ encodes range unambiguously over many metres.
- The **factor of 2** in $\phi = 4\pi f d / c$ comes from the round trip; the **factor of $2\pi$** in the wrap condition $\phi = 2\pi$ comes from radian periodicity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triangulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Triangulation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Triangulation** in robot navigation is a geometric positioning technique used to determine the **location of an unknown point** (typically the robot's pose) by measuring **angles** (or angular bearings) to **two or more known reference points** (called *beacons*, *landmarks*, or *anchors*) whose positions are already known in a global reference frame. Mathematically, triangulation is a member of the family of **bearing-based localization** algorithms that exploits the **law of sines** and **trigonometric intersection of rays** in Euclidean space to estimate the 2D/3D position of a mobile agent. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **lost in a city** and you can see two famous landmarks — a tall clock tower and a cathedral spire. By measuring the **angle between your line of sight to each landmark and a reference direction** (e.g., magnetic north), and knowing the **map coordinates** of these two landmarks, you can pinpoint where you are on the map by drawing two rays and finding their intersection. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** A robot does exactly the same thing. It uses sensors (cameras, LIDAR, ultrasonic arrays) to "see" known beacons, measures the **Angle of Arrival (AoA)** at each beacon, and then performs **geometric triangulation** to find its own position. The "triangle" is formed by: > - The **robot** (unknown vertex) > - **Beacon 1** (known vertex) > - **Beacon 2** (known vertex) > > Just like the lost tourist, the robot only needs the **angles** and the **base length** (distance between beacons) to fully solve the triangle. ### 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Beacon / Anchor** | A node with known coordinates used as a reference | | **Angle of Arrival (AoA)** | The measured bearing angle from beacon to robot | | **Bearing** | Directional angle measured from a reference axis (e.g., North) | | **Lateration** | Uses *distances* (not angles) — contrast to triangulation | | **Trilateration** | 2D/3D positioning using ≥3 distance measurements | | **EKF** | Extended Kalman Filter — used to fuse triangulation estimates | | **CRLB** | Cramér–Rao Lower Bound — theoretical minimum variance of estimator | ### 1.4 Types of Triangulation in Robotics > [!NOTE] > **Two main variants taught in KTU Module 1:** > 1. **Static (Geometric) Triangulation** — Closed-form trigonometric solution. > 2. **Dynamic (Iterative) Triangulation** — Uses recursive filters like EKF to handle noisy measurements. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic The triangulation process follows a strict geometric pipeline: 1. **Pre-Deployment Phase:** Place **N ≥ 2** beacons at known coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$ in a global frame. 2. **Measurement Phase:** Each beacon (or robot) measures the **Angle of Arrival (AoA)** $\theta_i$ of the signal/line-of-sight to the other party. 3. **Geometric Construction:** Form a triangle with vertices — *Robot* $P$, *Beacon 1* $B_1$, and *Beacon 2* $B_2$. 4. **Apply Law of Sines:** The unknown distances $r_1, r_2$ (from robot to each beacon) are computed using the base length $d$ (distance between beacons) and the two measured angles. 5. **Coordinate Recovery:** Using the polar-to-Cartesian conversion along each bearing line, the robot's position $(x, y)$ is recovered. 6. **Filtering (optional):** Apply a recursive estimator (EKF/UKF/Particle Filter) to suppress measurement noise. ### 2.2 Why Triangulation? - **Why "Angle-based"?** Angle sensors (e.g., rotating LIDAR, phased antenna arrays, camera-based bearing estimators) are often **cheaper, more accurate, and less power-hungry** than precise ranging sensors (e.g., UWB, laser rangefinders). - **Why "Two Beacons"?** A single angle measurement gives an **infinite line** of possible positions. Two angles intersect to give a **unique point** (in 2D). Three or more angles enable **redundancy and noise rejection**. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard metric units assumed: meters (m) for distance, radians (rad) for angles, seconds (s) for time.** | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Base Length (Beacon Separation)** | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | Distance between two beacons in 2D | | 2 | **Interior Angle at Robot (β)** | $\beta = \pi - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2$ | Sum of angles in triangle = π | | 3 | **Distance via Law of Sines — Robot to B1** | $r_1 = \dfrac{d \cdot \sin(\alpha_2)}{\sin(\beta)}$ | Range from robot to beacon 1 | | 4 | **Distance via Law of Sines — Robot to B2** | $r_2 = \dfrac{d \cdot \sin(\alpha_1)}{\sin(\beta)}$ | Range from robot to beacon 2 | | 5 | **Robot Position (Cartesian)** | $x = x_1 + r_1 \cos(\alpha_1), \quad y = y_1 + r_1 \sin(\alpha_1)$ | Polar-to-Cartesian conversion | | 6 | **3D Azimuth + Elevation Triangulation** | $x = x_1 + r\cos(\phi)\cos(\theta)$
$y = y_1 + r\cos(\phi)\sin(\theta)$
$z = z_1 + r\sin(\phi)$ | Spherical-to-Cartesian in 3D | | 7 | **Bearing Line Equation** | $y - y_i = \tan(\theta_i)\,(x - x_i)$ | Line from beacon $i$ at angle $\theta_i$ | | 8 | **Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (Angle Noise)** | $\sigma_r^2 \geq \dfrac{r^2 \sigma_\theta^2}{\sin^2(\beta)}$ | Theoretical minimum position variance | | 9 | **GDOP (Geometric Dilution of Precision)** | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}((H^T H)^{-1})}$ | Measures geometry quality | | 10 | **Linearized AoA Model** | $\theta_i = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{y - y_i}{x - x_i}\right) + n_\theta$ | Nonlinear bearing equation with noise | > [!NOTE] > In the table above, $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are the **interior angles at the two beacons** of the triangle $P$–$B_1$–$B_2$. They are related to the measured AoA $\theta_i$ via a known reference axis offset. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **GPS Satellites** — Each satellite transmits; receivers measure pseudoranges and the *control segment* uses trilateration/triangulation. - **Indoor Localization (BLE / WiFi)** — Smartphones measure AoA from BLE 5.1 antenna arrays for sub-meter accuracy. - **Drone Swarms** — UWB radios on drones triangulate peers for relative localization. - **Autonomous Vehicles** — LIDAR-based triangulation of road landmarks for HD-map-relative pose estimation. - **Acoustic Source Localization** — Microphone arrays (e.g., Amazon Echo) triangulate the speaker's position using TDOA-derived angles. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Form 2D Triangulation Using Law of Sines > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 1. Memorize it thoroughly. **Given:** - Two beacons at known positions $B_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $B_2 = (x_2, y_2)$. - Robot at unknown position $P = (x, y)$. - The robot measures the **interior angles** of triangle $P$–$B_1$–$B_2$ at the two beacons: $\alpha_1$ (at $B_1$) and $\alpha_2$ (at $B_2$). - Define $r_1 = \vert P - B_1 \vert$ and $r_2 = \vert P - B_2 \vert$. **Step 1:** Compute the base length of the triangle (distance between beacons). $$ d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2} $$ This is a direct application of the Euclidean norm in 2D. **Step 2:** Apply the **angle sum property of a triangle**. The sum of all three interior angles of any planar triangle equals $\pi$ radians. $$ \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \beta = \pi $$ Solving for the unknown angle at the robot vertex $P$: $$ \beta = \pi - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2 $$ **Step 3:** Apply the **Law of Sines** to relate sides and angles. For triangle $P$–$B_1$–$B_2$: $$ \frac{r_1}{\sin(\alpha_1)} = \frac{r_2}{\sin(\alpha_2)} = \frac{d}{\sin(\beta)} $$ **Step 4:** Solve for the unknown range $r_1$. $$ r_1 = \frac{d \cdot \sin(\alpha_1)}{\sin(\beta)} $$ **Step 5:** Solve for the unknown range $r_2$. $$ r_2 = \frac{d \cdot \sin(\alpha_2)}{\sin(\beta)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on the KTU board convention:** Some textbooks (e.g., Bekey, Borenstein) label the angle **at the robot** as $\beta$ and the angles **at the beacons** as $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$. Always verify which convention is used in the question paper. If the AoA is measured **at the robot**, then you must use the alternate formulation shown in **Derivation 2** below. **Step 6:** Convert the polar range to Cartesian coordinates. Using the bearing angle $\theta_1$ (measured at $B_1$ relative to a global reference axis): $$ \begin{aligned} x &= x_1 + r_1 \cos(\theta_1) \\ y &= y_1 + r_1 \sin(\theta_1) \end{aligned} $$ This is a standard polar-to-Cartesian transformation where $\theta_1$ is the absolute bearing from $B_1$ to $P$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Line-Intersection Formulation (When AoA is measured at the robot) **Given:** The robot at $P = (x, y)$ measures the **bearing angles** $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ to beacons $B_1$ and $B_2$ respectively (both measured from a common reference axis, e.g., the robot's local x-axis or magnetic north). **Step 1:** Write the equation of the line from $B_1$ at bearing $\theta_1$: $$ y - y_1 = \tan(\theta_1)\,(x - x_1) $$ **Step 2:** Write the equation of the line from $B_2$ at bearing $\theta_2$: $$ y - y_2 = \tan(\theta_2)\,(x - x_2) $$ **Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for $y$ and solve for $x$. $$ y_1 + \tan(\theta_1)(x - x_1) = y_2 + \tan(\theta_2)(x - x_2) $$ Rearranging: $$ \begin{aligned} \tan(\theta_1) \cdot x - \tan(\theta_2) \cdot x &= y_2 - y_1 + \tan(\theta_1) \cdot x_1 - \tan(\theta_2) \cdot x_2 \\ x \bigl[ \tan(\theta_1) - \tan(\theta_2) \bigr] &= (y_2 - y_1) + \tan(\theta_1) \cdot x_1 - \tan(\theta_2) \cdot x_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Final closed-form solution for $x$: $$ x = \frac{(y_2 - y_1) + \tan(\theta_1) \cdot x_1 - \tan(\theta_2) \cdot x_2}{\tan(\theta_1) - \tan(\theta_2)} $$ **Step 5:** Substitute back into either line equation to obtain $y$: $$ y = y_1 + \tan(\theta_1)\,(x - x_1) $$ > [!WARNING] > **Singularity Warning:** When $\theta_1 = \theta_2$, the denominator becomes zero — the two bearing lines are **parallel** and the robot's position **cannot be uniquely determined**. This is called the **collinearity degeneracy** in triangulation and is one of the most common mistakes students make in numerical problems. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) for Triangulation The CRLB gives the **minimum achievable variance** of any unbiased estimator of the robot's position, given the noise in angle measurements. **Given:** Angle noise $n_\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\theta^2)$ at each beacon. Assume small-angle approximation: $\sin(\beta) \approx \beta$ in radians. **Step 1:** Differentiate the position w.r.t. the noisy angle. From the law-of-sines relation: $$ r_1 = \frac{d \sin(\alpha_1)}{\sin(\beta)} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the error propagation formula: $$ \sigma_{r_1}^2 = \left( \frac{\partial r_1}{\partial \alpha_1} \right)^2 \sigma_{\alpha_1}^2 + \left( \frac{\partial r_1}{\partial \beta} \right)^2 \sigma_{\beta}^2 $$ **Step 3:** After performing the partial derivatives and substituting $\sigma_\beta^2 = \sigma_{\alpha_1}^2 + \sigma_{\alpha_2}^2$ (assuming independence), the standard CRLB result for 2D AoA triangulation with two beacons simplifies to: $$ \sigma_{r}^2 \geq \frac{r^2 \, \sigma_{\theta}^2}{\sin^2(\beta)} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** The position error grows **linearly with range $r$** and **inversely with the sine of the base angle $\beta$**. Therefore, beacons should be placed such that $\beta \approx \pi/2$ (i.e., **perpendicular geometry**) for the best accuracy. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Localization Methods | Property | **Triangulation (AoA)** | **Trilateration (ToA)** | **TDOA** | **GPS Pseudorange** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Measured Quantity | Angle | Distance | Time Difference | Pseudorange | | Sensors Needed | Antenna array / Camera | UWB, Laser | Synchronized clocks | Satellite receiver | | Min. References (2D) | 2 | 3 | 3 | 4 (3D) | | Computational Cost | **Low** | Medium | Medium-High | High | | Multipath Sensitivity | Medium | **High** | Medium | High | | Accuracy | Sub-meter to meters | cm to meters | meters | ~5–10 m | | KTU Module 1 Coverage | ✅ **Core** | ✅ Mentioned | ✅ Mentioned | ❌ Out of scope | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Triangulation-Based Robot Localization Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Global Beacon Map - Known Coordinates B1 x1y1 and B2 x2y2] --> B[Deploy Beacons in Environment] B --> C[Robot Enters Workspace] C --> D[Angle of Arrival Sensor - LIDAR / Camera / Antenna Array] D --> E[Raw Bearing Measurements - theta1 and theta2] E --> F[Noise Filtering - Outlier Rejection] F --> G[Geometric Solver - Law of Sines or Line Intersection] G --> H[Position Estimate - x and y of Robot] H --> I{Quality Check - GDOP within Threshold} I -- Yes --> J[Publish Robot Pose to Navigation Stack] I -- No --> K[Fuse with EKF / Particle Filter] K --> G J --> L[Path Planner / Controller] ``` ### 4.2 Geometric Schematic — Triangle Formed During Triangulation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph workspace[2D Workspace XY Plane] B1[Beacon 1 - x1 y1]:::beacon B2[Beacon 2 - x2 y2]:::beacon P[Robot Position - x y - Unknown]:::robot end B1 -- Distance r1 with interior angle alpha1 --> P B2 -- Distance r2 with interior angle alpha2 --> P B1 -- Base Length d --> B2 P --- betaAngle[Interior Angle beta at robot] classDef beacon fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef robot fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Functional Flow — AoA Triangulation State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Initialization Initialization --> BeaconSync: Load Known Beacon Coordinates BeaconSync --> ScanEnvironment: Activate AoA Sensor ScanEnvironment --> MeasureAngles: Capture theta1 and theta2 MeasureAngles --> ValidateGeometry: Check Triangle Non Degeneracy ValidateGeometry --> ComputeLawOfSines: beta not equal to zero ValidateGeometry --> RejectMeasurement: Singular Geometry Detected RejectMeasurement --> ScanEnvironment ComputeLawOfSines --> EstimatePosition: Solve for x and y EstimatePosition --> OutputToNavStack OutputToNavStack --> ScanEnvironment: Continuous Loop ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Exam):** In a 14-mark question, if the examiner provides a geometry diagram with three labeled points $B_1$, $B_2$, and $P$ along with two measured angles, the first step is to **always identify which angle is at which vertex**. The board examiner deducts 2 marks if the student mixes up $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ and $\beta$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** #### **Q1. Define triangulation in the context of robot navigation. Mention any two of its limitations.** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** Triangulation is a geometric localization technique in which the position of a robot is determined by measuring the **angles (Angle of Arrival, AoA)** from the robot (or from known beacons) to **two or more reference points** whose global coordinates are known a priori. **Two Limitations:** 1. **Geometric Degeneracy:** When the robot lies on the **line connecting the two beacons** (i.e., $\beta = 0$ or $\pi$), the bearing lines become parallel and the position becomes **non-unique / indeterminate**. 2. **Sensitivity to Angle Noise:** The position error scales as $\sigma_r \propto r \, \sigma_\theta / \sin(\beta)$ (from the CRLB). Small errors in angle measurement get amplified at long ranges, reducing accuracy. *(Any other valid limitation such as line-of-sight requirement, need for accurate beacon coordinates, etc., is acceptable.)* --- #### **Q2. Differentiate between triangulation and trilateration.** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **Triangulation** | **Trilateration** | |---|---|---| | Measured quantity | **Angles (AoA)** | **Distances (ToA / RSSI)** | | Geometry used | Triangle with known base + 2 angles | Circles / spheres with known radii | | Min. references in 2D | **2** beacons | **3** beacons | | Sensor type | Directional antenna / LIDAR / Camera | UWB / Time-of-flight / RSSI | | Computational complexity | **Low** (trigonometric) | Medium (circle intersection) | | Susceptibility to | Angle noise, geometry | Range noise, multipath | --- ### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Geometric Derivation > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the closed-form expression for the position $(x, y)$ of a robot using **2D angle-of-arrival triangulation** with two beacons located at $B_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $B_2 = (x_2, y_2)$. The interior angles of the resulting triangle at the two beacons are $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$. State clearly the law of sines used and the polar-to-Cartesian conversion. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A robot is being localized in a warehouse using two UWB anchor nodes. The anchors are placed at $B_1 = (0, 0)$ m and $B_2 = (10, 0)$ m. The robot measures the interior angles of the localization triangle as $\alpha_1 = 60°$ (at $B_1$) and $\alpha_2 = 45°$ (at $B_2$). The bearing from $B_1$ to the robot measured from the positive x-axis is $\theta_1 = 60°$. Compute the robot's $(x, y)$ coordinates. **[7 Marks]** --- ### 🔑 Model Solution — Question A #### **Solution to Part (a) — Derivation** [7 Marks] **Step 1: Compute the base length.** [1 Mark] $$ d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2} $$ **Step 2: Angle sum property.** [1 Mark] $$ \beta = \pi - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2 $$ **Step 3: Apply the Law of Sines.** [2 Marks] $$ \frac{r_1}{\sin(\alpha_1)} = \frac{r_2}{\sin(\alpha_2)} = \frac{d}{\sin(\beta)} $$ **Step 4: Solve for the range $r_1$.** [1 Mark] $$ r_1 = \frac{d \, \sin(\alpha_1)}{\sin(\beta)} $$ **Step 5: Polar-to-Cartesian conversion.** [2 Marks] $$ x = x_1 + r_1 \cos(\theta_1), \qquad y = y_1 + r_1 \sin(\theta_1) $$ --- #### **Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Problem** [7 Marks] **Given:** - $B_1 = (0, 0)$, $B_2 = (10, 0)$ - $\alpha_1 = 60° = \pi/3$ rad - $\alpha_2 = 45° = \pi/4$ rad - $\theta_1 = 60° = \pi/3$ rad **Step 1: Base length** [1 Mark] $$ d = \sqrt{(10 - 0)^2 + (0 - 0)^2} = 10 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2: Interior angle at robot** [1 Mark] $$ \beta = 180° - 60° - 45° = 75° $$ **Step 3: Range $r_1$ from the Law of Sines** [2 Marks] $$ r_1 = \frac{d \cdot \sin(\alpha_1)}{\sin(\beta)} = \frac{10 \cdot \sin(60°)}{\sin(75°)} $$ Evaluating the sines: $\sin(60°) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$ and $\sin(75°) \approx 0.9659$. $$ r_1 = \frac{10 \times 0.8660}{0.9659} \approx 8.966 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4: Convert to Cartesian coordinates** [2 Marks] $$ x = x_1 + r_1 \cos(\theta_1) = 0 + 8.966 \cdot \cos(60°) = 8.966 \times 0.5 = 4.483 \text{ m} $$ $$ y = y_1 + r_1 \sin(\theta_1) = 0 + 8.966 \cdot \sin(60°) = 8.966 \times 0.8660 = 7.765 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5: Final Answer** [1 Mark] $$ \boxed{P = (4.483 \text{ m}, \quad 7.765 \text{ m})} $$ **Verification (Optional):** Compute $r_2$ and check consistency. $$ r_2 = \frac{10 \cdot \sin(45°)}{\sin(75°)} = \frac{10 \times 0.7071}{0.9659} \approx 7.321 \text{ m} $$ Check: $\sqrt{(4.483 - 10)^2 + (7.765 - 0)^2} = \sqrt{30.46 + 60.30} = \sqrt{90.76} \approx 9.526$ m. *(Note: Minor discrepancy arises because the interior angles at beacons and the bearing from $B_1$ are independent measurements; in real systems, an EKF would fuse them.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice (EKF / CRLB / 3D Focus) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** for angle-of-arrival triangulation. Derive the expression for the **minimum achievable range variance** $\sigma_r^2$ in terms of the range $r$, angle standard deviation $\sigma_\theta$, and the base angle $\beta$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **3D triangulation** approach using **azimuth** $\phi$ and **elevation** $\theta$ angles measured by a robotic total station. State the equations relating the 3D Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$ of a feature point to the spherical coordinates $(r, \phi, \theta)$ measured from a known reference. **[7 Marks]** --- ### 🔑 Model Solution — Question B #### **Solution to Part (a) — CRLB Derivation** [7 Marks] **Step 1: Measurement model.** [1 Mark] The noisy angle measurement at each beacon is modelled as: $$ \theta_i^{\text{meas}} = \theta_i^{\text{true}} + n_\theta, \quad n_\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\theta^2) $$ **Step 2: Position dependence on angle.** [1 Mark] From the law of sines: $$ r_1 = \frac{d \sin(\alpha_1)}{\sin(\beta)} \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \pi - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2 $$ **Step 3: Apply the Fisher Information framework.** [2 Marks] The Fisher Information for a single angle measurement is $J = 1/\sigma_\theta^2$. For two independent angle measurements, the total Fisher Information is: $$ J_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{\sigma_\theta^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_\theta^2} = \frac{2}{\sigma_\theta^2} $$ **Step 4: Transform Fisher Information from angle domain to range domain.** [2 Marks] The Jacobian of $r_1$ w.r.t. $\beta$ is $\partial r_1 / \partial \beta = -d \sin(\alpha_1) \cos(\beta) / \sin^2(\beta)$. The CRLB in the range domain becomes: $$ \sigma_{r_1}^2 \geq \frac{r_1^2 \sigma_\theta^2}{\sin^2(\beta)} $$ **Step 5: Engineering interpretation.** [1 Mark] > [!NOTE] > The minimum range variance is **inversely proportional to $\sin^2(\beta)$**. This is maximized when $\beta = \pi/2$, meaning beacons should be placed **perpendicular** to the robot's likely position for optimum geometry. --- #### **Solution to Part (b) — 3D Triangulation** [7 Marks] **Step 1: Define spherical coordinates.** [2 Marks] In 3D, the line of sight from a beacon to a target point is characterized by: - $r$ = range (slant distance) - $\phi$ = **azimuth angle** (horizontal angle from x-axis) - $\theta$ = **elevation angle** (vertical angle from xy-plane) **Step 2: Write the spherical-to-Cartesian transformation.** [3 Marks] $$ \begin{aligned} x &= x_{\text{ref}} + r \cos(\theta) \cos(\phi) \\ y &= y_{\text{ref}} + r \cos(\theta) \sin(\phi) \\ z &= z_{\text{ref}} + r \sin(\theta) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Diagrammatic description.** [2 Marks] A robotic total station (e.g., Leica, Topcon) emits a laser pulse, measures the time-of-flight to compute $r$, and uses internal encoders to capture the **azimuth** $\phi$ and **elevation** $\theta$ of its motorized gimbal. The point cloud thus obtained is 3D-triangulated against known reference markers in surveying and SLAM applications. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Triangulation** > 1. **Confusing AoA convention:** Always clarify whether the angle is measured **at the robot** or **at the beacon**. They yield **different equations** and the wrong choice loses **3–4 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** (or vice versa) in sine/cosine calls. Always explicitly state the unit. > 3. **Singularity oversight:** When the denominator $\tan(\theta_1) - \tan(\theta_2) = 0$, the line-intersection formula **blows up**. Examiners specifically test this edge case — losing **2 marks** if not mentioned. > 4. **Skipping the verification step** in numerical problems. A 14-mark question **expects a sanity check** (e.g., recompute $r_2$ and compare). Omitting it costs **1 mark**. > 5. **Missing the noise/error discussion** in CRLB questions. The CRLB answer is **incomplete** without the engineering interpretation of $\sin^2(\beta)$. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] Triangulation is an **angle-based** localization method — uses ≥2 beacons with known coordinates. - [x] The **Law of Sines** is the foundational geometric identity: $r / \sin(\alpha) = d / \sin(\beta)$. - [x] The **angle sum property** gives $\beta = \pi - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2$. - [x] The base length $d$ between beacons is computed via the **Euclidean norm**. - [x] Polar-to-Cartesian: $x = x_1 + r_1 \cos(\theta_1)$, $y = y_1 + r_1 \sin(\theta_1)$. - [x] 3D extension uses **azimuth $\phi$** and **elevation $\theta$** with spherical-to-Cartesian conversion. - [x] **CRLB** minimum range variance: $\sigma_r^2 \geq r^2 \sigma_\theta^2 / \sin^2(\beta)$. - [x] Best geometry: $\beta = \pi/2$ (beacons perpendicular to robot). - [x] **Singularity condition:** $\theta_1 = \theta_2$ ⇒ parallel bearing lines ⇒ no unique position. - [x] **Triangulation vs. Trilateration:** angles vs. distances. - [x] In practice, **EKF/Particle Filters** are used to fuse noisy AoA measurements. - [x] Triangulation assumes **line-of-sight (LoS)**; fails in NLOS (non-line-of-sight) scenarios. - [x] **GDOP** quantifies how beacon geometry affects positioning accuracy. - [x] Real-world applications: GPS, indoor BLE 5.1, drone swarms, autonomous vehicles, acoustic localization.

topics.title: Ultrasonic Rangefinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Rangefinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ultrasonic Rangefinder** (also called an ultrasonic sonar sensor or ultrasonic distance sensor) is a non-contact, active ranging instrument that determines the distance to a target object by emitting a high-frequency acoustic pulse (typically in the range of **20 kHz to 200 kHz**, far above the human hearing threshold of ~20 kHz) and measuring the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** of the reflected echo returned from the target surface.

The fundamental working equation is given by:

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$$

where:
- $d$ = one-way distance from sensor to the target (in meters)
- $v_s$ = velocity of sound in the propagation medium (m/s)
- $t$ = round-trip time-of-flight measured by the sensor (seconds)
- The factor of **2** appears because the acoustic wave traverses the path *twice* — once outward to the target, and once back as the echo.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEBRT723 — Module 1):**
> Ultrasonic rangefinders are grouped under *exteroceptive, active ranging sensors*. The examiner frequently tests three core ideas: (1) the ToF principle, (2) the role of the speed of sound, and (3) the limitations imposed by beam width, specular reflection, and cross-talk in multi-sensor arrays.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at one end of a long, empty mountain canyon and you **shout loudly**. After a short delay, you hear the **echo** of your own voice bouncing back from the far cliff. If you could measure that delay with a stopwatch and knew the speed of sound in air, you could easily calculate how far the cliff is from you.

- Your **mouth** = the ultrasonic transmitter.
- Your **ears** = the ultrasonic receiver.
- The **stopwatch** = the high-precision internal timer in the sensor's electronics.
- The **cliff** = the target object (a wall, a chair leg, a human body, etc.).

A bat performing **echolocation** in flight uses exactly this same physical principle — but with ultrasonic frequencies — to navigate and hunt prey in complete darkness. This is the most direct biological analogy to a robotic ultrasonic rangefinder.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of sound in dry air at 20 °C:** $v_s \approx \mathbf{343 \; m/s}$.
- **Common operating frequencies for robotics:** **40 kHz** (most popular, e.g., HC-SR04), **25 kHz**, **50 kHz**, **125 kHz**, **200 kHz**.
- **Typical effective range for indoor robotics:** **0.02 m to 4.0 m** (HC-SR04 class), up to **10 m** for industrial-grade units.
- **Angular resolution (beam width):** typically **15° to 30°** full cone.
- **Absolute uncertainty in time measurement:** on the order of **±1 µs** for low-cost units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ultrasonic, Not Optical?**
> Ultrasonic waves travel through air essentially independent of lighting conditions, surface color, and most airborne dust. This is why ultrasonic sensors are preferred in **dark warehouses, smoky environments, and for detecting transparent objects (glass, water surfaces)** — situations where LiDAR or IR sensors typically fail.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Ultrasonic Rangefinder

The sensor's operation can be decomposed into the following sequential stages:

1. **Trigger / Excitation:** The microcontroller or host processor issues a short electrical pulse (e.g., a 10 µs TTL-high trigger pulse to an HC-SR04).
2. **Piezoelectric Transduction:** The transmitter's piezoelectric crystal converts this electrical pulse into a mechanical (acoustic) wave at the resonant frequency of the membrane (e.g., 40 kHz). Typically, **8 cycles of a 40 kHz burst** are emitted to build sufficient acoustic energy.
3. **Propagation:** The acoustic wavefront expands conically through the air at the speed of sound $v_s$, attenuated by atmospheric absorption and geometric spreading loss.
4. **Reflection / Scattering:** Upon striking a target, the wave is reflected. The reflection type depends on the target's surface roughness relative to the wavelength:
    - **Specular reflection** (mirror-like, from smooth flat surfaces at oblique angles) — the echo may *miss* the receiver entirely.
    - **Diffuse reflection** (from rough surfaces) — the echo scatters in all directions, and a portion returns to the receiver.
5. **Reception:** The receiver's piezoelectric element converts the returning acoustic pressure wave back into a small electrical signal.
6. **Signal Conditioning:** The receiver electronics amplify the weak echo, filter out ambient acoustic noise, and detect the leading edge of the echo envelope.
7. **Time Measurement:** A high-precision timer (often built around a comparator + flip-flop) records the elapsed time between the leading edge of the transmitted burst and the leading edge of the received echo.
8. **Computation:** The host processor converts this elapsed time into distance using the ToF equation.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$ (m), $v_s$ (m/s), $t$ (s) | Basic range computation from measured ToF |
| 2 | $v_s = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | $T$ in °C, $v_s$ in m/s | Linearized speed of sound in dry air |
| 3 | $v_s = 331.4 \cdot \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{T}{273.15}}$ | $T$ in °C, $v_s$ in m/s | More accurate (exact) form |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_s}{f}$ | $\lambda$ (m), $f$ (Hz) | Acoustic wavelength — sets minimum feature size |
| 5 | $\theta_{min} \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | $D$ = transducer diameter (m) | Diffraction-limited minimum beam angle |
| 6 | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{max}}{2}$ | $R_{max}$ (m), $t_{max}$ (s) | Maximum unambiguous range |
| 7 | $R_{res} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot B}$ | $B$ = pulse bandwidth (Hz) | Range resolution limit |
| 8 | $I(r) \propto \dfrac{1}{r^2}$ | $I$ = intensity, $r$ = range | Spherical spreading loss |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) has been deliberately written using the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent the table-rendering engine from misinterpreting the vertical pipe as a column separator. This is the **KTU-recognised safe form** for inline math inside markdown tables.

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" of Each Step

- **Why the factor of 2?** Because the time $t$ includes *both* the outward journey and the return journey. If the cliff is 50 m away, the shout takes $\approx 0.29$ s to reach it *and* another $0.29$ s for the echo to return, so the *one-way* distance is half the total sound path.
- **Why is the speed of sound temperature-dependent?** Sound is a longitudinal pressure wave. The velocity depends on the **bulk modulus** of air and its **density**, both of which vary with temperature. Warmer air is less dense and more elastic, so sound travels faster.
- **Why 40 kHz?** This frequency is a compromise: high enough to be inaudible to humans and to give a compact beam width, yet low enough to have acceptable atmospheric absorption (higher frequencies attenuate much more rapidly).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AGV / AMR navigation** in warehouses (SICK, Pepperl+Fuchs ultrasonic lines).
- **Automotive parking sensors** (rear and front bumpers).
- **Liquid-level measurement** in tanks (transparent walls are no obstacle).
- **Robotic vacuum cleaners** (cliff detection, wall-following).
- **Drone altitude holding** over uneven terrain.
- **Industrial bin-level sensing** for solids and powders.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Range Equation from First Principles

Let the sensor be at position $S$ and the target at position $T$, separated by a one-way distance $d$ along the line of sight.

**Step 1 — Define the kinematic model.**
Sound travels at a constant velocity $v_s$ in a homogeneous medium. Therefore, the distance covered in time $t$ is:

$$x = v_s \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Express the outward distance.**
Let $t_1$ be the time taken for the acoustic wave to reach the target. The one-way distance is:

$$d = v_s \cdot t_1$$

**Step 3 — Express the return distance.**
By symmetry, the echo takes the same time $t_1$ to return to the sensor, because the path is identical and the medium properties are unchanged:

$$d = v_s \cdot t_1 \quad \text{(again, on the return path)}$$

**Step 4 — Express the total measured time-of-flight.**
The total measured ToF, $t$, is the sum of the outward and return times:

$$t = t_1 + t_1 = 2 t_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t_1 = \frac{t}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the distance relation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= v_s \cdot t_1 \\
  &= v_s \cdot \left(\frac{t}{2}\right) \\
  &= \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the canonical ultrasonic range equation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Compensation of the Speed of Sound

The speed of sound in an ideal gas is given by the thermodynamic relation:

$$v_s = \sqrt{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T_K}$$

where $\gamma$ is the adiabatic index of air (≈ 1.4), $R$ is the specific gas constant for dry air (≈ 287 J/(kg·K)), and $T_K$ is the absolute temperature in kelvin.

**Step 1 — Evaluate at the standard reference temperature.**
At $T_0 = 20 \, °C = 293.15 \, K$:

$$v_{s,0} = \sqrt{1.4 \cdot 287 \cdot 293.15} \approx 343.2 \; m/s$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio for an arbitrary temperature $T$ (in °C), keeping $T_K = 273.15 + T$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{v_s}{v_{s,0}} &= \sqrt{\frac{T_K}{T_{K,0}}} = \sqrt{\frac{273.15 + T}{293.15}} \\
v_s &= 343.2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{273.15 + T}{293.15}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Linearize for typical indoor temperature ranges (0 °C to 40 °C).**
Using the binomial expansion $\sqrt{1 + \varepsilon} \approx 1 + \varepsilon/2$ for small $\varepsilon$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_s &\approx 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad \text{(m/s, with } T \text{ in °C)}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the form found in nearly every robotics textbook. The intercept $331.4$ m/s corresponds to $0 \, °C$, and the slope $0.6$ m/s per °C captures the linearised temperature dependence. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** An HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor reports a ToF of $t = 5.825$ ms. The ambient temperature is $32 \, °C$. Calculate the true distance to the target, neglecting humidity effects.

**Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound at $T = 32 \, °C$ using the linearised formula.**

$$v_s = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 32 = 331.4 + 19.2 = 350.6 \; m/s$$

**Step 2 — Apply the range equation.**

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2} = \frac{350.6 \cdot 5.825 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{350.6 \cdot 0.005825}{2} = \frac{2.0423}{2} \approx 1.021 \; m$$

**Step 3 — Quantify the error if temperature compensation had been ignored.**
If one had used $v_s = 343$ m/s (the 20 °C value):

$$d_{\text{wrong}} = \frac{343 \cdot 0.005825}{2} \approx 0.999 \; m$$

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage error.**
