$$\varepsilon = \frac{\vert 1.021 - 0.999 \vert}{1.021} \times 100\% \approx 2.15\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 12 °C temperature swing produces a **~2 % range error**. Over a 4 m working range, this corresponds to an absolute error of nearly **9 cm** — more than the wheel diameter of many small mobile robots. **Always apply temperature compensation in any real KTU design problem** unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Trade-offs at a Glance

| Design Parameter | Increase Effect on Range | Increase Effect on Beam Width | Increase Effect on Attenuation |
|------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Operating frequency $f$ | Decreases (higher $\alpha$) | **Decreases** (narrower beam, better angular resolution) | **Increases** (atmospheric absorption $\propto f^2$) |
| Transducer diameter $D$ | Slight increase (more acoustic power) | **Decreases** (better directivity) | Negligible |
| Pulse burst length $N$ | Slight increase (more energy) | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Surface roughness of target | Improves detection (diffuse reflection) | — | — |
| Ambient temperature | Increases $v_s$ → distances appear longer if uncorrected | Unchanged | Slight effect on absorption |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question template**. The examiner typically asks: *"Discuss the trade-offs involved in selecting the operating frequency of an ultrasonic rangefinder."* You should be ready to argue from the entries in this matrix.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of an Ultrasonic Rangefinder

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the internal signal-flow architecture of a typical ultrasonic rangefinder module (e.g., the HC-SR04 family). Every node is a functional block; arrows represent the direction of signal or data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MCU["HOST MCU"] -->|10 us TRIGGER PULSE| TRIGDRV["TRIGGER DRIVER"]
    TRIGDRV -->|EXCITE| TXCRYSTAL["PIEZO TX CRYSTAL 40 KHZ"]
    TXCRYSTAL -->|ACOUSTIC BURST| AIR["AIR MEDIUM"]
    AIR -->|REFLECTED ECHO| RXCRYSTAL["PIEZO RX CRYSTAL 40 KHZ"]
    RXCRYSTAL -->|WEAK ECHO SIGNAL| AMP["LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER"]
    AMP -->|AMPLIFIED ECHO| BANDPASS["BANDPASS FILTER 40 KHZ"]
    BANDPASS -->|FILTERED ECHO| COMPARATOR["THRESHOLD COMPARATOR"]
    TRIGDRV -->|START| TIMER["PRECISION TIMER"]
    COMPARATOR -->|STOP| TIMER
    TIMER -->|ELAPSED T IN US| ECHO_PIN["ECHO PWM OUTPUT"]
    ECHO_PIN --> MCU
    MCU -->|d = v t OVER 2| DISTANCE["DISTANCE VALUE IN METERS"]
```

### 4.2 Timing Diagram — Trigger, Burst, and Echo Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as HOST MCU
    participant SENS as ULTRASONIC SENSOR
    participant TGT as TARGET OBJECT
    MCU->>SENS: TRIGGER PULSE 10 us
    SENS->>SENS: GENERATE 8 CYCLE BURST AT 40 KHZ
    SENS->>TGT: ACOUSTIC WAVE TRAVELS OUTWARD
    TGT-->>SENS: ECHO RETURNS AFTER TIME T
    SENS->>MCU: ECHO PIN HELD HIGH FOR DURATION T
    MCU->>MCU: COMPUTE DISTANCE = V SOUND TIMES T DIVIDED BY 2
```

### 4.3 Beam-Pattern Schematic — Why Off-Axis Targets Behave Differently

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSOR["SENSOR APERTURE"] -->|NARROW CENTRAL LOBE| TARGETA["ON AXIS TARGET DETECTED"]
    SENSOR -->|EDGE OF CONE 15 DEG| TARGETB["OFF AXIS TARGET EDGE OF DETECTION"]
    SENSOR -->|OUTSIDE CONE 30 DEG| TARGETC["OFF AXIS TARGET NOT DETECTED"]
    SENSOR -->|SPECULAR GHOST| GHOST["SPECULAR REFLECTION FALSE ECHO FROM WALL EDGE"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams above:**
> - Block 1 shows the *internal signal chain*: from trigger input to amplified, filtered, thresholded echo and finally the digital pulse-width output that the MCU interprets.
> - Block 2 shows the *temporal sequence*: trigger → burst emission → propagation → echo → pulse-width encoding → computation.
> - Block 3 illustrates the **geometric reality** behind the "15°–30° beam width" specification: a target inside the cone is reliably detected, a target on the cone boundary is marginal, and a target outside the cone is *invisible* to the sensor — even if it is physically very close.

### 4.4 Common Failure Modes (Schematic Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MODE["POTENTIAL FAILURE MODE"] --> M1["SPECULAR SKIP — SMOOTH SURFACE AT OBLIQUE ANGLE"]
    MODE --> M2["CROSS TALK — NEIGHBOURING SENSORS TRIGGER EACH OTHER"]
    MODE --> M3["MULTIPATH — ECHO ARRIVES VIA WALL REFLECTION NOT DIRECT PATH"]
    MODE --> M4["SPEED OF SOUND DRIFT — UNCOMPENSATED TEMPERATURE ERROR"]
    MODE --> M5["DEAD ZONE — TARGET INSIDE 2 CM BLIND RANGE"]
    MODE --> M6["ELEVATED NOISE FLOOR — IN INDUSTRIAL OR WINDY ENVIRONMENTS"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEBRT723]**
> Define an *ultrasonic rangefinder*. Why is a factor of **2** present in the basic range equation $d = v_s t / 2$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An ultrasonic rangefinder is an active non-contact ranging sensor that determines the distance to a target by emitting a high-frequency acoustic burst (typically 20–200 kHz) and measuring the round-trip **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** of the reflected echo. **(1 mark)**
The range equation is $d = v_s t / 2$, where $d$ is the one-way distance, $v_s$ is the speed of sound in air, and $t$ is the measured round-trip time. **(1 mark)**
The factor of 2 is necessary because the measured time $t$ corresponds to the acoustic wave traversing the path **twice** — once from the transmitter to the target, and once from the target back to the receiver. The one-way distance is therefore half of the total sound path. **(1 mark)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEBRT723]**
> List **any four limitations** of ultrasonic rangefinders when used in mobile robot navigation.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each, or proportional):**
1. **Specular reflection from smooth surfaces** at oblique incidence — the echo misses the receiver.
2. **Wide beam width (15°–30°)** leading to poor angular resolution and ghost echoes from nearby objects.
3. **Cross-talk** in multi-sensor arrays if their firings are not properly time-multiplexed.
4. **Temperature and humidity dependence** of the speed of sound, requiring compensation.
5. *(Bonus alternative)* Short effective range (typically 4 m for low-cost units) and blind zone in the first 2 cm.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> ### Question A — 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, OEBRT723]**
>
> **(a)** With the aid of a functional block diagram, explain the **operating principle of an ultrasonic rangefinder**. Derive the range equation and discuss the role of temperature in the measurement. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A mobile robot uses an HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor pointing forward. The sensor reports a ToF of **6.84 ms**. The on-board thermometer reads **28 °C**. Compute the actual distance to the obstacle, and quantify the error if temperature compensation had been ignored (use $v_{s,\,20°C} = 343$ m/s as the reference). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(a)

1. **Definition and principle (2 marks):** An ultrasonic rangefinder operates on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle. A piezoelectric transducer emits a short burst of ultrasonic waves (typically 40 kHz, 8 cycles). The waves propagate through air, strike a target, and a portion of the acoustic energy is reflected back as an echo. A second transducer (or the same one in time-multiplexed mode) receives the echo, and the elapsed time is measured by a precision timer.

2. **Functional block diagram (2 marks):** The block diagram should include the following sub-blocks: Trigger Driver → TX Piezo Transducer → Air Medium → RX Piezo Transducer → Low-Noise Amplifier → Bandpass Filter → Threshold Comparator → Precision Timer → Echo Output → Host MCU → Distance Computation. *(See the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4 of these notes.)*

3. **Derivation of the range equation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, $d = v_s t / 2$, where $t$ is the round-trip ToF and $v_s$ is the speed of sound in the propagation medium.

4. **Role of temperature (1 mark):** The speed of sound depends linearly on temperature through $v_s = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$ m/s. An uncompensated sensor will report erroneous distances in environments with significant temperature variation (e.g., 2 % error per 12 °C).

#### Model Solution — Q(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the compensated speed of sound at $T = 28 \, °C$.**

$$v_s = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 28 = 331.4 + 16.8 = 348.2 \; m/s$$

**Step 2 — Compute the true distance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{true}} &= \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2} = \frac{348.2 \cdot 6.84 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
                 &= \frac{348.2 \cdot 0.00684}{2} = \frac{2.3817}{2} \\
                 &= 1.1908 \; m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the uncompensated distance using $v_{s,\,20°C} = 343$ m/s.**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{uncomp}} &= \frac{343 \cdot 0.00684}{2} = \frac{2.3461}{2} = 1.1731 \; m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Quantify the absolute and relative errors.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta d &= \vert 1.1908 - 1.1731 \vert = 0.0177 \; m \approx 1.77 \; cm \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{\text{rel}} &= \frac{0.0177}{1.1908} \times 100\% \approx 1.49\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The true distance is **1.19 m**. Ignoring the 8 °C temperature rise introduces an under-estimation of about **1.77 cm** (≈ 1.5 % relative error). For a robot traversing a doorway, this is a non-negligible error margin.

---

> ### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, OEBRT723]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **sources of measurement error** in an ultrasonic rangefinder. Explain the phenomenon of **specular reflection** and **cross-talk**, and suggest engineering remedies. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A robot is fitted with three forward-facing ultrasonic sensors, each with a beam half-angle of **15°**, mounted at the left, centre, and right of the bumper. The sensors are spaced **0.20 m** apart (centre-to-centre). At an ambient temperature of **25 °C**, the left sensor reports a ToF of **4.50 ms**, the centre sensor reports **4.62 ms**, and the right sensor reports **4.50 ms**. **(i)** Compute the three ranges. **(ii)** Comment on the angular geometry of the detected obstacle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(a)

**Sources of measurement error (3 marks):**
1. **Temperature-induced speed-of-sound drift** (typically ±2 % per ±10 °C).
2. **Humidity variations** (smaller effect; 0.1–0.3 %).
3. **Atmospheric turbulence and wind gradients** causing acoustic refraction.
4. **Timing quantisation** in low-cost sensors (often ±1 µs → ±0.17 mm, but cumulative).
5. **Surface reflectivity variations** (rough vs. smooth, absorbent materials).

**Specular reflection (2 marks):** When the acoustic wavelength is much smaller than the surface roughness, reflection is *diffuse* and a portion of the echo returns to the receiver. When the target is smooth relative to $\lambda$ (e.g., a polished metal panel or a flat wall struck at an oblique angle), the wave reflects *specularly* — like light off a mirror — and may never return to the sensor. **Engineering remedies:** use multiple sensors at different angles, mount the sensor on a rotating/panning servo, or fuse ultrasonic data with LiDAR/IR sensors in a sensor-fusion framework.

**Cross-talk (2 marks):** When two ultrasonic sensors operate simultaneously or in close temporal proximity, the receiver of one sensor can pick up the transmitted burst of its neighbour, leading to false short-range readings. **Engineering remedies:** (i) time-multiplex the sensors (sequential firing with at least 50 ms guard time), (ii) use sensors with different resonant frequencies and narrow bandpass filters, or (iii) use coded chirp signals with matched filtering.

#### Model Solution — Q(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound at $T = 25 \, °C$.**

$$v_s = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 25 = 331.4 + 15.0 = 346.4 \; m/s$$

**Step 2 — Compute the three ranges.**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_L &= \frac{346.4 \cdot 4.50 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{1.5588}{2} = 0.7794 \; m \\[4pt]
d_C &= \frac{346.4 \cdot 4.62 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{1.6004}{2} = 0.8002 \; m \\[4pt]
d_R &= \frac{346.4 \cdot 4.50 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 0.7794 \; m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Geometric interpretation.** The left and right sensors report an **equal range** of 0.7794 m, while the centre sensor reports a slightly larger range of 0.8002 m. This **parabolic profile** (with the apex at the centre) is the signature of an obstacle whose surface is **concave from the robot's perspective** — for example, the **inside corner of a wall** or a cylindrical object positioned centrally. The robot's bumper is at the vertex of a triangle with the obstacle, and the centre sensor sees slightly farther into the concavity.

> Alternatively, a **convex** object positioned centrally would yield the *opposite* profile (centre sensor reporting the *shortest* range), because the centre beam would strike the apex of the object first.

**Conclusion:** The three readings are consistent with the robot facing a **concave wall corner** at a range of approximately **0.78–0.80 m**, with the centre of the bumper pointed slightly into the corner. The robot should execute a turn to align with the corner's axis or stop and replan its path.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on this topic in the KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $d = v_s t / 2$. This single omission will cost you **3 marks instantly** on any 7-mark numerical.
> 2. **Using $v_s = 343$ m/s universally** without checking whether the question specifies a temperature. Even when the temperature is given, students often copy the standard value and lose the **"compensation applied" credit** (1–2 marks).
> 3. **Confusing the units of $t$** — leaving it in milliseconds instead of converting to seconds. This produces a range answer in the wrong order of magnitude (e.g., 1700 m instead of 1.7 m).
> 4. **Omitting the block diagram** in 7-mark descriptive questions. KTU examiners explicitly allocate **1.5–2 marks** for a labelled diagram; a wall of text without one will be penalised.
> 5. **Hand-waving the specular reflection / cross-talk answer.** You must state the *cause* AND a *remedy* to earn full marks.
> 6. **Mixing up ToF with one-way time.** Some students compute $d = v_s \cdot t$ without the factor of 2 — an easy trap when re-reading your own working under exam stress.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Core equation:** $d = v_s t / 2$ — the factor of 2 is *non-negotiable*.
- ✅ **Speed of sound in air:** $v_s = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$ m/s, with $T$ in °C (linearised, 0–40 °C range).
- ✅ **Wavelength relation:** $\lambda = v_s / f$. At 40 kHz and 20 °C, $\lambda \approx 8.6$ mm.
- ✅ **Beam width:** 15°–30° full cone for typical robotics-grade units.
- ✅ **Operating frequency:** 40 kHz is the de facto industry standard for indoor mobile robots.
- ✅ **Typical range:** 2 cm to 4 m for HC-SR04; up to 10 m for industrial sensors.
- ✅ **Temperature compensation is mandatory** in any non-trivial design — a 10 °C drift costs ~2 % range error.
- ✅ **Major failure modes:** specular reflection, cross-talk, multipath echoes, blind zone, and speed-of-sound drift.
- ✅ **Remedies for cross-talk:** time-multiplex sensors, use distinct resonant frequencies, or apply coded chirps with matched filtering.
- ✅ **Beam geometry rule of thumb:** a target *within* the cone is detected, a target *on* the cone boundary is marginal, and a target *outside* the cone is invisible to the sensor.
- ✅ **Comparison anchor:** ultrasonic sensors complement (not replace) LiDAR and IR — they are best at *close-range, low-cost, lighting-independent* ranging.
- ✅ **Conversion discipline:** always convert $t$ from **ms → s** before applying the formula; always quote $d$ in **m** unless otherwise specified.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ground-Based Beacons
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ground-Based Beacons

# Ground-Based Beacons in Robot Navigation

## 1. Core Technical Definition

**Ground-Based Beacons** are fixed, known reference transceivers or reflectors strategically placed within a robot's operational workspace. They transmit, reflect, or relay known signals (electromagnetic, acoustic, or optical) that a mobile robot uses to compute its absolute pose (position $\langle x, y, z \rangle$ and orientation $\langle \phi, \theta, \psi \rangle$) relative to a globally defined map frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Beacon-based localization is classified as an **absolute (global) localization** technique under the broader taxonomy of robot pose estimation. Unlike dead reckoning, beacon-based methods do not accumulate drift over time because measurements are made against externally fixed infrastructure.

### Types of Ground-Based Beacons

| Beacon Class | Operating Principle | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Beacons** | Self-powered, emit signals on demand or periodically | RF transponders, Ultrasonic pingers, IR LEDs, UWB anchors |
| **Passive Beacons** | Reflect incident signals from the robot | Retro-reflective tape, Bar-coded landmarks, QR tags |
| **Semi-passive** | Battery-assisted, transmit only when interrogated | RFID tags, NFC markers |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Lighthouse" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a ship lost in fog. It cannot see the coast, but it can hear three foghorns (the beacons) from known lighthouses. By measuring how loud each horn is and how long the sound takes to arrive, the ship captain triangulates his exact position on the chart.
>
> In the same way, a mobile robot in a warehouse, hospital, or factory listens to/watches fixed beacons on the ceiling or walls, measures the signal properties, and mathematically "fixes" its position on a pre-loaded map. The beacons act as artificial landmarks in a controlled, engineered environment.

### Why Beacons? — The Need for Absolute Referencing

Mobile robots suffer from **odometric drift** — small errors in wheel-rotation integration grow unbounded with distance traveled. Beacons provide a periodic **re-zeroing** mechanism. Key engineering metrics include:

- **Positioning Accuracy:** typically **$\pm 1$ cm to $\pm 30$ cm** depending on technology.
- **Update Rate:** **1 Hz to 1000 Hz**.
- **Operating Range:** **0.1 m to 100 m**.
- **Latency:** **1 ms to 100 ms**.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, always remember the trade-off triangle: **Accuracy ↔ Cost ↔ Range**. High-frequency UWB beacons are accurate but expensive; simple IR beacons are cheap but short-range and noisy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1 The Localization Pipeline

Every beacon-based localization system executes the following logical chain:

1. **Signal Emission / Reflection** — A beacon emits (or reflects) a known waveform characterized by carrier frequency $f_c$, bandwidth $B$, and modulation scheme.
2. **Propagation** — The signal travels through the medium (air, vacuum, or structured environment) at speed $v$ (where $v = c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s for EM waves, and $v \approx 343$ m/s for sound in air at **20 °C**).
3. **Measurement Acquisition** — The robot's onboard receiver samples one or more of the following physical observables:
   - **Time of Arrival (ToA)** or **Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA)**
   - **Angle of Arrival (AoA)**
   - **Received Signal Strength (RSS)** / **RSSI**
   - **Phase of Arrival (PoA)**
4. **Geometric Solution** — The measured observables are fed into a geometric solver (trilateration, triangulation, or multilateration) to yield $\hat{p} = \langle \hat{x}, \hat{y}, \hat{z} \rangle$.
5. **Filtering & Fusion** — A Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter, or Particle Filter smooths the estimate and fuses it with odometry.

### 2.2 Beacon Measurement Modalities

#### (a) Time of Flight (ToF) / Time of Arrival (ToA)
The robot measures the round-trip or one-way time $t$ for a signal. The range $d$ is:
$$d = v \cdot t$$
where $v$ is the propagation speed. ToF requires **precise clock synchronization** between robot and beacon, with sub-nanosecond accuracy for centimeter-level EM ranging.

#### (b) Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA)
The robot uses an array of beacons with synchronized clocks. The robot measures the *difference* in arrival times from multiple beacons. This eliminates the need for the robot's own clock to be synchronized.
$$\Delta t_{ij} = t_i - t_j = \frac{1}{c}\left(d_i - d_j\right)$$

#### (c) Angle of Arrival (AoA)
The robot uses a rotating directional antenna or a phased array to determine the bearing $\theta$ to each beacon. Only **two beacons** are needed for 2D position if bearings are measurable.

#### (d) Received Signal Strength (RSSI)
The signal strength decays with distance according to a path-loss model. The simplest free-space model is the **Friis transmission equation**:
$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^2$$
Indoor environments use the **log-distance path loss model**:
$$P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10 \, n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$
where $n$ is the path-loss exponent (typically 2 indoors to 4 in cluttered environments) and $X_\sigma$ is shadow fading noise.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = v \cdot t$ | ToA-based range from time-of-flight | m, s, m/s |
| 2 | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | Speed of EM waves in vacuum | m/s |
| 3 | $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \, T$ | Speed of sound in air at temperature $T$ °C | m/s |
| 4 | $P_r(d) = P_0 - 10n\log_{10}(d)$ | Log-distance path loss for RSSI ranging | dBm, m |
| 5 | $\Delta t_{ij} = (d_i - d_j)/c$ | TDoA between beacons $i$ and $j$ | s, m |
| 6 | $d_i^2 = (x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2$ | Trilateration circle/sphere equation | m² |
| 7 | $\tan(\theta_i) = (y - y_i)/(x - x_i)$ | Bearing from beacon $i$ to robot | rad |
| 8 | $R_{max} = v \cdot t_{max}$ | Maximum measurable range given timing budget | m |
| 9 | $P_{pos} = 6 \, \sigma_{range}^2$ | 2D position covariance from range noise (3 beacons) | m² |
| 10 | $\lambda = c / f_c$ | Wavelength of carrier for phase-based ranging | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In KTU answer sheets, examiners use the absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ which can confuse markdown renderers. When writing exam answers on paper, always use a clear vertical bar; in these notes we use $\vert$ to avoid breaking tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) in warehouses** use reflective tape beacons + LiDAR for lane tracking.
- **Hospital service robots** use ceiling-mounted IR beacons for room-level localization.
- **Autonomous mining trucks** use UWB beacons in tunnel environments where GPS fails.
- **Indoor drones** use UWB beacons for precision hovering in GPS-denied zones.
- **Subsea ROVs** use acoustic transponder beacons because EM waves are heavily attenuated in water.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 2D Trilateration from Three Beacons

**Problem Setup:** Three beacons $B_1, B_2, B_3$ are at known coordinates $\langle x_i, y_i \rangle$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$. The robot at unknown position $\langle x, y \rangle$ measures distances $d_1, d_2, d_3$ to each beacon using ToF.

**Step 1 — Write the three range equations.**
Using the Euclidean distance formula:
$$d_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3$$

**Step 2 — Square both sides to remove square roots.**
$$d_i^2 = (x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2$$

**Step 3 — Expand the squared terms.**
$$d_i^2 = x^2 - 2 x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2 y y_i + y_i^2$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the equation for $i = 1$ from the equations for $i = 2$ and $i = 3$ to eliminate the nonlinear $x^2 + y^2$ term.**

For $i = 2$ minus $i = 1$:
$$d_2^2 - d_1^2 = -2 x x_2 + x_2^2 + 2 x x_1 - x_1^2 - 2 y y_2 + y_2^2 + 2 y y_1 - y_1^2$$

Group terms:
$$d_2^2 - d_1^2 = 2 x (x_1 - x_2) + 2 y (y_1 - y_2) + (x_2^2 - x_1^2) + (y_2^2 - y_1^2)$$

Rearrange as a linear equation in $x$ and $y$:
$$2(x_2 - x_1) x + 2(y_2 - y_1) y = d_1^2 - d_2^2 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for $i = 3$ minus $i = 1$:**
$$2(x_3 - x_1) x + 2(y_3 - y_1) y = d_1^2 - d_3^2 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2$$

**Step 6 — Write the linear system in matrix form $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$.**

Let us define:
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_2 - x_1) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \\ 2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(y_3 - y_1) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} d_1^2 - d_2^2 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 \\ d_1^2 - d_3^2 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Solve using Cramer's Rule or matrix inversion.**
The solution is:
$$\mathbf{p} = A^{-1} \mathbf{b}$$
Provided $\det(A) \neq 0$, i.e., the three beacons are **not collinear**. This is the well-known "non-collinearity condition" examiners love to test.

$$\det(A) = 4 \left[(x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (x_3 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1)\right]$$

If $\det(A) = 0$, the beacons lie on a line, and the system is **rank-deficient** — no unique solution exists.

### 3.2 Derivation: Angle of Arrival (Triangulation) Using Two Beacons

**Problem Setup:** A robot at $\langle x, y \rangle$ measures the bearings $\alpha$ and $\beta$ to two beacons at known positions $\langle x_1, y_1 \rangle$ and $\langle x_2, y_2 \rangle$, all bearings measured relative to a common reference (e.g., the robot's heading or true North).

**Step 1 — Write the bearing equations as tangents.**

The bearing from the robot to beacon $i$ is:
$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{y_1 - y}{x_1 - x}, \quad \tan(\beta) = \frac{y_2 - y}{x_2 - x}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply to remove denominators.**
$$(x_1 - x)\tan(\alpha) = y_1 - y$$
$$(x_2 - x)\tan(\beta) = y_2 - y$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system.**
Expand:
$$x \tan(\alpha) - y = x_1 \tan(\alpha) - y_1$$
$$x \tan(\beta) - y = x_2 \tan(\beta) - y_2$$

Subtract the two equations:
$$x \left[\tan(\alpha) - \tan(\beta)\right] = x_1 \tan(\alpha) - x_2 \tan(\beta) - y_1 + y_2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$.**
$$x = \frac{x_1 \tan(\alpha) - x_2 \tan(\beta) - (y_1 - y_2)}{\tan(\alpha) - \tan(\beta)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain $y$.**
$$y = x_1 \tan(\alpha) - y_1 - x \tan(\alpha)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from Trilateration:** Triangulation uses **angles only** and requires **only two beacons** for 2D, but is **highly sensitive to bearing errors** at long range. Trilateration uses **distances only** and requires **three beacons minimum**, but is robust over long range.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Beacon Technology for Common Scenarios

| Scenario | Recommended Beacon Type | Reason | Expected Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indoor warehouse AGV | Reflective tape + LiDAR | Low cost, works in metallic clutter | $\pm 2$ cm |
| Hospital corridor robot | Ceiling RFID + RSSI | Cheap, no line-of-sight needed | $\pm 1$ m |
| GPS-denied drone | UWB pulse beacons | High bandwidth, cm-level ranging | $\pm 10$ cm |
| Underwater AUV | Acoustic transponders | EM waves absorbed in water | $\pm 0.5$ m |
| Outdoor autonomous car | None — uses GNSS | Beacons are not scalable outdoors | N/A |
| Underground mining vehicle | UWB + IMU fusion | Robust to dust and darkness | $\pm 30$ cm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Beacon-Based Localization System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Beacon Infrastructure Layer]:::infra --> B[Signal Propagation Channel]
    B --> C[Robot Onboard Receiver]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning and Preprocessing]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction Module]
    E --> F[Geometric Solver: Trilateration or Triangulation]
    F --> G[Bayesian Filter: EKF or Particle Filter]
    G --> H[Pose Estimate x y theta]
    H --> I[Motion Controller]
    I --> J[Wheel Actuators]
    H --> K[Map Overlay and Display]

    classDef infra fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Trilateration Geometric Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Beacon B1 at x1 y1]:::beacon
    P2[Beacon B2 at x2 y2]:::beacon
    P3[Beacon B3 at x3 y2]:::beacon
    R[Robot at x y]:::robot

    P1 ---|distance d1| R
    P2 ---|distance d2| R
    P3 ---|distance d3| R

    classDef beacon fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef robot fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The intersection of the three range circles (in 2D) or range spheres (in 3D) gives the unique robot position. Geometrically, the trilateration equation derived in Section 3.1 is the algebraic statement of this circle-intersection principle.

### 4.3 Beacon Measurement Modality Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Start: Choose Beacon Modality] --> Q1{Is environment GPS denied?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is precise range needed less than 1 m?}
    Q1 -->|No| N1[Use GNSS Satellite Beacons]

    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is line of sight available?}
    Q2 -->|No| N2[Use UWB TDoA Beacons]

    Q3 -->|Yes| N3[Use Optical or IR Beacons]
    Q3 -->|No| N4[Use RF or UWB Beacons]

    N1 --> END[End]
    N2 --> END
    N3 --> END
    N4 --> END
```

### 4.4 Complete Localization Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Beacons 1 to N]:::beacon --> B[Multiplexed Receiver Array]
    B --> C[Time Stamping Unit]
    B --> D[Bearing Estimator]
    B --> E[RSSI Sampler]

    C --> F[ToA Estimator]
    D --> G[AoA Estimator]
    E --> H[RSSI to Range Converter]

    F --> I[Trilateration Solver]
    G --> J[Triangulation Solver]
    H --> I

    I --> K[Measurement Vector z]
    J --> K

    K --> L[EKF Predict using Odometry]
    L --> M[EKF Update Step]
    M --> N[Final Pose x y theta]
    N --> O[Robot Controller]

    classDef beacon fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Define ground-based beacons in robot navigation. List the three main categories with one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ground-based beacons are **fixed reference devices** placed in a robot's workspace that emit or reflect known signals to enable absolute position estimation.

The three main categories are:

1. **Active Beacons** — self-powered, transmit signals autonomously. Example: ceiling-mounted **ultrasonic pinger** used in hospital robots.
2. **Passive Beacons** — do not transmit; reflect incident signals. Example: **retro-reflective tape** scanned by a LiDAR.
3. **Semi-passive Beacons** — transmit only upon external interrogation. Example: **RFID tag** activated by a robot's reader antenna.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners award the 3rd mark only if the example matches the category. Mixing categories is a common $-1$ mark error.

#### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> *Explain the difference between triangulation and trilateration in beacon-based localization. State the minimum number of beacons required for 2D positioning in each case.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Triangulation | Trilateration |
|---|---|---|
| **Measured Quantity** | Angles (bearings) | Distances (ranges) |
| **Sensor Complexity** | Requires directional antenna / camera | Requires ToF or RSSI measurement |
| **Min Beacons for 2D** | **2 beacons** | **3 beacons** |
| **Sensitivity to Error** | High at long range (tangent amplification) | Lower, scales with range noise $\sigma$ |
| **Typical Use** | Optical beacons with camera | UWB / ultrasonic ranging |

> [!NOTE]
> A common student error is to state "two beacons are enough for trilateration" — this is **wrong**, because the two circle intersection gives two symmetric points (mirror ambiguity). The third beacon disambiguates.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> **(a)** Derive the position-fix equations for a 2D trilateration system with three non-collinear beacons placed at known coordinates. State clearly the non-collinearity condition. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A robot in a warehouse uses three ultrasonic beacons placed at $B_1 = (0, 0)$, $B_2 = (10, 0)$ and $B_3 = (0, 8)$ (all in meters). The measured ToF distances (assuming $v_{sound} = 340$ m/s and one-way time) are $d_1 = 5$ m, $d_2 = \sqrt{41}$ m, $d_3 = 5$ m. Calculate the robot's $(x, y)$ position. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Write the three squared range equations, subtract to linearize, form the matrix $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$, and solve.

> *Full derivation provided in Section 3.1 above. Award 1 mark for setting up the three distance equations, 2 marks for the subtraction and linearization, 2 marks for the matrix form, 1 mark for stating $\det(A) \neq 0$ as the non-collinearity condition, and 1 mark for the final inversion formula.*

The non-collinearity condition is:
$$\det(A) = 4 \left[(x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (x_3 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1)\right] \neq 0$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Substitute the known beacon coordinates:
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2(10 - 0) & 2(0 - 0) \\ 2(0 - 0) & 2(8 - 0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 2 — Compute the right-hand side vector.

For $i = 1$: $d_1^2 = 25$, $x_1^2 + y_1^2 = 0$.
For $i = 2$: $d_2^2 = 41$, $x_2^2 + y_2^2 = 100$.
For $i = 3$: $d_3^2 = 25$, $x_3^2 + y_3^2 = 64$.

Row 1 (from $i=2$ minus $i=1$):
$$b_1 = 25 - 41 + 100 - 0 + 0 - 0 = 84$$

Row 2 (from $i=3$ minus $i=1$):
$$b_2 = 25 - 25 + 64 - 0 + 0 - 0 = 64$$

Step 3 — Solve $A \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & 16 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 84 \\ 64 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:
$$x = 84 / 20 = 4.2 \text{ m}, \quad y = 64 / 16 = 4.0 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** The robot is at $(x, y) = (4.2, 4.0)$ meters.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Note):** Students frequently substitute $d_1^2$ values incorrectly or forget to square coordinates. A $-1$ mark penalty is applied for any arithmetic slip in $b_1$ or $b_2$. Also, marks are deducted if the final answer is given without units.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the two main categories of beacon measurement: Time of Arrival (ToA) and Angle of Arrival (AoA). Compare their accuracy, hardware complexity, and required number of beacons for 2D fix. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** An indoor robot uses two RF beacons on the ceiling. The beacons are at coordinates $B_1 = (0, 0)$ and $B_2 = (6, 0)$ m. The robot measures the bearings to be $\alpha = 60°$ and $\beta = 120°$ measured from its own forward direction. Assuming the robot's heading is aligned with the global $x$-axis, compute the robot's $(x, y)$ position. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) Conceptual Comparison (7 marks):**
Provide a labeled block diagram of a ToA receiver and an AoA receiver, then tabulate the comparison. The complete comparison is:

| Parameter | Time of Arrival (ToA) | Angle of Arrival (AoA) |
|---|---|---|
| Measured Variable | Time $t$ → range $d = v t$ | Angle $\theta$ of incoming wavefront |
| Hardware | Clock + correlator | Phased array / rotating antenna / camera |
| Clock Sync Required | **Yes** (for one-way ToA) | No |
| Min Beacons 2D | 3 | 2 |
| Accuracy vs Range | Roughly constant in range | Degrades with range due to $\tan$ function |
| Multipath Sensitivity | High in indoor (NLOS errors) | Moderate |

*(Award 4 marks for the diagrams and 3 marks for the table content.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $B_1 = (0, 0)$, $B_2 = (6, 0)$, $\alpha = 60°$, $\beta = 120°$, heading aligned with global $x$-axis.

Step 1 — Apply the triangulation formula from Section 3.2:
$$x = \frac{x_1 \tan(\alpha) - x_2 \tan(\beta) - (y_1 - y_2)}{\tan(\alpha) - \tan(\beta)}$$

Step 2 — Substitute values.
$\tan(60°) = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$
$\tan(120°) = -\sqrt{3} \approx -1.732$

Numerator:
$$0 \cdot \sqrt{3} - 6 \cdot (-\sqrt{3}) - (0 - 0) = 6\sqrt{3}$$

Denominator:
$$\sqrt{3} - (-\sqrt{3}) = 2\sqrt{3}$$

Therefore:
$$x = \frac{6\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{3}} = 3 \text{ m}$$

Step 3 — Find $y$ from the bearing equation.
$$y = x_1 \tan(\alpha) - y_1 - x \tan(\alpha) = 0 \cdot \sqrt{3} - 0 - 3 \cdot \sqrt{3} = -3\sqrt{3}$$

**Final Answer:** The robot is at $(x, y) = (3, -3\sqrt{3}) \approx (3, -5.196)$ meters.

The negative $y$ indicates the robot is "below" the global $x$-axis (i.e., on the opposite side of the beacons from the assumed bearing reference).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Note):** Two very common errors here:
>
> 1. **Sign error on $\tan(120°)$** — students write $+ \sqrt{3}$ instead of $- \sqrt{3}$. This produces a wrong sign on $x$. Marks deducted: $-2$.
> 2. **Forgetting that bearings are global** — if the robot's heading is not aligned with the $x$-axis, students must first transform: $\theta_{global} = \theta_{robot} + \psi_{heading}$. Examiners deduct $-1$ for skipping this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Ground-Based Beacons**

- **Definition:** Beacons are **fixed external references** that enable **absolute (global) localization**, eliminating odometric drift.
- **Three categories:** Active (self-transmitting), Passive (reflective), Semi-passive (RFID-style).
- **Three main measurement modalities:** ToA, TDoA, AoA, plus RSSI as a fourth (less accurate) option.
- **Speed constants to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s for EM; $v_{sound} = 343$ m/s at 20 °C.
- **Trilateration math:** minimum **3 non-collinear beacons** in 2D; **4 non-coplanar** in 3D. Always state the non-collinearity determinant $\neq 0$ condition.
- **Triangulation math:** minimum **2 beacons** in 2D; **3** in 3D. Watch for $\tan(\theta)$ sign at angles in Q2 and Q3 quadrants.
- **Friis equation** for free-space RSSI; **log-distance model** for indoor RSSI with exponent $n \in [2, 4]$.
- **Trade-off triangle:** accuracy vs cost vs range. UWB > LiDAR > Ultrasonic > IR > RFID for accuracy.
- **Limitations:** beacons require pre-installation and maintenance; not scalable to outdoor large areas; multipath and NLOS errors degrade indoor accuracy.
- **Common examiner traps:** (1) stating 2 beacons suffice for trilateration, (2) sign error on $\tan$ of obtuse angles, (3) forgetting the line-of-sight assumption for optical beacons, (4) not specifying beacon placement geometry in design questions.
- **Industrial examples to quote in answers:** AGVs in Amazon warehouses, Kiva robots, hospital delivery robots (TUG), DJI indoor drones with UWB, mining trucks.
- **KTU-favorite one-liner:** *"Beacons provide the global anchor; odometry provides the local motion — the filter fuses both."*

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** "**A**BSOLUTE = **B**EACON, **R**ELATIVE = **O**DOMETRY" — if a question asks about drift-free localization, the answer is **beacons**. If it asks about short-term smooth motion, the answer is **odometry**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Field and Optical Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Motion Field and Optical Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motion Field — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motion Field:** The **2D velocity vector field** formed by the projection of the 3D velocities of all visible points in a scene onto the **image plane** of a camera. It describes *true* geometric motion in the image and is governed entirely by the **relative motion between the camera and the scene** and the scene's 3D structure (depth).

Mathematically, the motion field assigns a 2D velocity vector $\mathbf{v}(x,y)$ to every image point $(x,y)$ based on the underlying 3D motion field and depth.

## 1.2 Optical Flow — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optical Flow:** The **apparent 2D motion** of brightness (intensity) patterns in an image sequence. It is *estimated* from image intensity changes between consecutive frames. Optical flow approximates the motion field only under specific conditions (static scene, Lambertian surfaces, constant illumination).

Formally, optical flow is the velocity field $\mathbf{u}(x,y) = (u, v)$ that best describes how image intensity $I(x,y,t)$ moves from frame $t$ to frame $t + \delta t$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Train Window

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine sitting inside a moving train and looking out of the window.
> - **True 3D Scene:** Trees, poles, and houses are stationary in the world.
> - **Motion Field:** Geometrically, the *exact* 2D velocity at which every point on the window (image plane) would have to move so the world appears stable relative to the moving camera. Trees close by whip past quickly, distant mountains drift slowly. This is the *true* projection.
> - **Optical Flow:** What you *observe* — the apparent shifting of textures, edges, and brightness patches on your retina. It is computed from pixel intensity changes, not true geometry.
> - In an ideal world, **Motion Field = Optical Flow**. In the real world, they differ (e.g., a perfectly smooth rotating sphere with no texture has a motion field but zero optical flow — the **aperture problem**).

## 1.4 Why Does the Distinction Matter in Robot Navigation?

For a mobile robot, this distinction is **critical**:
- **Motion Field** tells the robot *how fast physical objects are sliding across its camera sensor* → essential for obstacle tracking, depth estimation, and visual odometry.
- **Optical Flow** is what the robot's vision algorithms can actually *compute* from pixel data → forms the input to obstacle avoidance (e.g., optical flow divergence for time-to-contact).

## 1.5 Key Relationship — The Equality Assumption

Under three assumptions:
1. **Lambertian surfaces** (brightness independent of view angle)
2. **Static scene** (no independently moving objects with brightness changes)
3. **Uniform illumination** (lighting does not change between frames)

The optical flow *equals* the motion field. This assumption underlies nearly all classical optical flow methods (Horn–Schunck, Lucas–Kanade).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Image Brightness Constancy Assumption

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** The intensity of a moving surface point does not change as it moves from one frame to the next (Lambertian + constant illumination).

For a point moving along a trajectory $(x(t), y(t))$, the image brightness is constant:

$$I(x(t), y(t), t) = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$ using the **chain rule**:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = 0$$

## 2.2 The Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)

Expanding the total derivative:

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} = 0$$

Defining the spatial and temporal gradients:

$$I_x = \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}, \quad I_y = \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}, \quad I_t = \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}$$

And the 2D velocity:

$$u = \frac{dx}{dt}, \quad v = \frac{dy}{dt}$$

We obtain the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation**:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

Or in compact vector form:

$$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u} + I_t = 0$$

where $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$ and $\mathbf{u} = (u, v)^T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Aperture Problem:** This single equation is **underdetermined** — it has **2 unknowns** $(u, v)$ but only **1 equation** per pixel. It can only determine the flow component *normal* to the intensity gradient (along the edge), not the component *parallel* to it. This is known as the **aperture problem**, and additional constraints (smoothness, local constancy) are needed to resolve it.

## 2.3 Geometric Interpretation of the OFCE

The OFCE states that the flow vector $\mathbf{u}$ lies on a **line in velocity space** perpendicular to the image gradient $\nabla I$. The component of flow *along* the gradient (normal flow) is uniquely determined:

$$u_n = -\frac{I_t}{\vert \nabla I \vert}$$

## 2.4 Two Classical Methods to Solve the OFCE

### 2.4.1 Horn–Schunck Method (1981) — Global Smoothness

Adds a **global smoothness constraint** that the flow field varies smoothly across the image. Minimizes:

$$E = \underbrace{\iint (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 \, dx\, dy}_{\text{Optical Flow Constraint}} + \alpha^2 \underbrace{\iint (\vert \nabla u \vert^2 + \vert \nabla v \vert^2) \, dx\, dy}_{\text{Smoothness Penalty}}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **regularization parameter** (controls smoothness weight).

The resulting Euler–Lagrange equations (per pixel):

$$I_x (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \alpha^2 \nabla^2 u = 0$$
$$I_y (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \alpha^2 \nabla^2 v = 0$$

where $\nabla^2 u$ and $\nabla^2 v$ are the Laplacians of $u$ and $v$ (approximated by $\bar{u} - u$ and $\bar{v} - v$, where $\bar{u}, \bar{v}$ are local neighborhood averages).

### 2.4.2 Lucas–Kanade Method (1981) — Local Constant Flow

Assumes the flow $(u, v)$ is **constant within a small local window** of $n \times n$ pixels. Stacks the OFCE over all pixels in the window and solves via **least squares**:

$$\mathbf{A} \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{b}$$

where

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} I_x(p_1) & I_y(p_1) \\ I_x(p_2) & I_y(p_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(p_n) & I_y(p_n) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = -\begin{bmatrix} I_t(p_1) \\ I_t(p_2) \\ \vdots \\ I_t(p_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

The least-squares solution is:

$$\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$$

Computed as:

$$u = \frac{-I_t I_x I_y^2 + I_y \sum I_x I_t}{\sum I_x^2 \sum I_y^2 - (\sum I_x I_y)^2}$$

(with similar expression for $v$).

## 2.5 Motion Field from 3D Geometry (Pinhole Camera Model)

For a pinhole camera with focal length $f$ and a 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ moving with velocity $\mathbf{V} = (V_X, V_Y, V_Z)$ relative to a camera translating with velocity $\mathbf{T} = (T_X, T_Y, T_Z)$ and rotating with angular velocity $\mathbf{\Omega} = (\Omega_X, \Omega_Y, \Omega_Z)$:

The motion field components are:

$$u(x,y) = \frac{T_Z x - T_X f}{Z} - \Omega_Y f + \Omega_Z y + \frac{\Omega_X x y}{f} - \frac{\Omega_Y x^2}{f}$$

$$v(x,y) = \frac{T_Z y - T_Y f}{Z} + \Omega_X f - \Omega_Z x - \frac{\Omega_Y x y}{f} + \frac{\Omega_X y^2}{f}$$

These decompose into a **translational component** (depth-dependent, $1/Z$) and a **rotational component** (depth-independent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The $1/Z$ dependence of translational flow is the foundation of **visual odometry**, **structure from motion**, and **time-to-contact estimation** in mobile robots.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|--------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Optical Flow Constraint Eq.** | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | Linear; 1 eqn, 2 unknowns |
| 2 | **Normal Flow Component** | $u_n = -I_t / \vert \nabla I \vert$ | Pixel/s |
| 3 | **Vector Form of OFCE** | $\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u} + I_t = 0$ | Dot product form |
| 4 | **Horn–Schunck Functional** | $E = \iint (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \alpha^2 (\vert \nabla u \vert^2 + \vert \nabla v \vert^2) \, dx\, dy$ | $\alpha$ = regularization weight |
| 5 | **Horn–Schunck E–L Eqns** | $I_x (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) = \alpha^2 (\bar{u} - u)$ | Iterative update form |
| 6 | **Lucas–Kanade Matrix** | $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A} \, \mathbf{u} = -\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ is $2 \times 2$ |
| 7 | **Lucas–Kanade Solution** | $\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$ | Solvable if $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ is invertible |
| 8 | **Translational Flow $u$** | $u_T = (T_Z x - T_X f)/Z$ | Depends on $1/Z$ |
| 9 | **Rotational Flow $u$** | $u_R = -\Omega_Y f + \Omega_Z y + \Omega_X x y/f - \Omega_Y x^2/f$ | Depth-independent |
| 10 | **Focus of Expansion (FOE)** | Image point where all translational flow vectors radiate from | Used in navigation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)

**Step 1 — State the Brightness Constancy Assumption.**

For a point in the image moving with trajectory $(x(t), y(t))$, the intensity remains constant:

$$I(x(t), y(t), t) = C$$

where $C$ is a constant independent of time $t$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to Time.**

Taking the total derivative with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{d}{dt} I(x(t), y(t), t) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Multivariable Chain Rule.**

Because $I$ is a function of three variables $(x, y, t)$, the chain rule gives:

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Notation.**

Let $I_x \triangleq \partial I / \partial x$, $I_y \triangleq \partial I / \partial y$, $I_t \triangleq \partial I / \partial t$ (spatial and temporal image gradients), and let $u \triangleq dx/dt$, $v \triangleq dy/dt$ (components of 2D velocity):

$$\boxed{I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0}$$

**Step 5 — Vector Form Rearrangement.**

Rewriting the equation as a dot product:

$$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u} = -I_t$$

where $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)^T$ and $\mathbf{u} = (u, v)^T$. This shows the flow component along $\nabla I$ is fixed.

## 3.2 Derivation of Normal Flow

Starting from the OFCE:

$$I_x u + I_y v = -I_t$$

The component of velocity $\mathbf{u}$ along the gradient $\nabla I$ is the projection:

$$u_n = \frac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla I}{\vert \nabla I \vert} = \frac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}} = -\frac{I_t}{\vert \nabla I \vert}$$

This is the **normal flow** — the only flow component that is uniquely recoverable from a single OFCE per pixel.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Lucas–Kanade Solution

**Step 1 — Local Constant Flow Assumption.**

In a small spatial neighborhood (window) of size $n \times n$ centered at pixel $\mathbf{p}_0$, assume:

$$u(\mathbf{p}_i) = u, \quad v(\mathbf{p}_i) = v \quad \forall\, i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n^2\}$$

**Step 2 — Write OFCE at Every Pixel in the Window.**

For each pixel $\mathbf{p}_i = (x_i, y_i)$:

$$I_x(\mathbf{p}_i) \cdot u + I_y(\mathbf{p}_i) \cdot v = -I_t(\mathbf{p}_i)$$

**Step 3 — Stack into Matrix Form.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_x(\mathbf{p}_1) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_1) \\ I_x(\mathbf{p}_2) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(\mathbf{p}_n) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_n) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} I_t(\mathbf{p}_1) \\ I_t(\mathbf{p}_2) \\ \vdots \\ I_t(\mathbf{p}_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{A} \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 4 — Least-Squares Solution.**

Minimize $\Vert \mathbf{A} \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2$ by setting derivative to zero:

$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A} \, \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 5 — Explicit Inversion.**

The $2 \times 2$ matrix $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ is:

$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The inverse exists when the determinant is non-zero:

$$\det(\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}) = \sum I_x^2 \sum I_y^2 - (\sum I_x I_y)^2 \neq 0$$

The flow solution:

$$\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Motion Field from 3D Motion

**Step 1 — Perspective Projection.**

For a pinhole camera with focal length $f$, a 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to image point $(x, y)$:

$$x = f \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to Time.**

$$\dot{x} = f \frac{\dot{X} Z - X \dot{Z}}{Z^2} = f \frac{\dot{X}}{Z} - f \frac{X \dot{Z}}{Z^2}$$

Substitute $X = xZ / f$:

$$\dot{x} = \frac{f \dot{X} - x \dot{Z}}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Express Point Velocities in Camera Frame.**

A 3D point fixed in the world, viewed by a moving camera, has velocity in the *camera frame*:

$$\dot{X} = -T_X - \Omega_Z Y + \Omega_Y Z$$
$$\dot{Y} = -T_Y + \Omega_Z X - \Omega_X Z$$
$$\dot{Z} = -T_Z - \Omega_Y X + \Omega_X Y$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Flow Equation.**

Plugging into $\dot{x} = (f \dot{X} - x \dot{Z}) / Z$ and using $X = xZ/f$, $Y = yZ/f$:

$$\dot{x} = \frac{T_Z x - T_X f}{Z} - \Omega_Y f + \Omega_Z y + \frac{\Omega_X x y}{f} - \frac{\Omega_Y x^2}{f}$$

$$\dot{y} = \frac{T_Z y - T_Y f}{Z} + \Omega_X f - \Omega_Z x - \frac{\Omega_Y x y}{f} + \frac{\Omega_X y^2}{f}$$

**Step 5 — Decompose into Translation and Rotation.**

$$u(x,y) = \underbrace{\frac{T_Z x - T_X f}{Z}}_{\text{Translational}} + \underbrace{\left(-\Omega_Y f + \Omega_Z y + \frac{\Omega_X x y}{f} - \frac{\Omega_Y x^2}{f}\right)}_{\text{Rotational}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The translational component scales as $1/Z$ — closer objects produce larger flow. The rotational component is **independent of depth** — pure rotation produces flow even for infinitely distant points.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — At a Glance

| Method | Constraints Added | Solves Aperture Problem? | Dense Flow? | Best Use Case |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Normal Flow** | None | ❌ No (only normal component) | ✅ Yes | Real-time, low-compute |
| **Horn–Schunck** | Global smoothness | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (dense) | Smooth flow fields, off-line |
| **Lucas–Kanade** | Local constant flow | ✅ Yes | ❌ Sparse (corners only) | Real-time tracking, SLAM |
| **Pyramid LK** | Multi-scale | ✅ Yes | ❌ Sparse | Large motions |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Optical Flow Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image Sequence I_t and I_t+1] --> B[Image Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Gaussian Smoothing to Reduce Noise]
    B --> B2[Optional Image Pyramid Construction]
    B1 --> C[Spatiotemporal Gradient Estimation]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1[Compute I_x via Sobel Operator]
    C --> C2[Compute I_y via Sobel Operator]
    C --> C3[Compute I_t via Frame Difference]
    C1 --> D[Apply OFCE: I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E{Choose Solver}
    E -->|Normal Flow| F1[Output u_n = -I_t / magnitude of grad I]
    E -->|Horn Schunck| F2[Iterative E-L with Smoothness Penalty alpha]
    E -->|Lucas Kanade| F3[Least Squares over Local Window]
    F1 --> G[Postprocessing]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Median Filter to Remove Outliers]
    G --> G2[Flow Visualization via Color Coding HSV]
    G1 --> H[Final Optical Flow Field]
    G2 --> H
```

## 4.2 Concept Map — Motion Field vs Optical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC[True 3D World]
        S1[Scene Points P with Depth Z]
        S2[Camera Motion Translation and Rotation]
    end
    subgraph MF[Motion Field]
        M1[Projection of 3D Velocities onto Image Plane]
        M2[Geometric and Depth Dependent]
    end
    subgraph OF[Optical Flow]
        O1[Apparent Motion of Brightness Patterns]
        O2[Estimated from Pixel Intensity Changes]
    end
    subgraph ASS[Equivalence Conditions]
        A1[Lambertian Surfaces]
        A2[Static Scene]
        A3[Constant Illumination]
    end
    S1 --> M1
    S2 --> M1
    M1 -->|Under Assumptions| O1
    O2 -->|Equals| M1
    ASS --> M1
    ASS --> O1
```

## 4.3 The Aperture Problem Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Local Aperture Window] --> P2[Image Edge Moving Inside Window]
    P2 --> P3[OFCE Measures Only Normal Component]
    P3 --> P4[Parallel Component along Edge Remains Ambiguous]
    P4 --> P5[Need Global Horn-Schunck or Local Lucas-Kanade Constraint]
    P5 --> P6[Resolve Full 2D Flow Vector]
```

## 4.4 Translational vs Rotational Flow Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Motion Field u,v] --> B[Translational Component scales as 1 over Z]
    A --> C[Rotational Component Independent of Z]
    B --> B1[FOE Focus of Expansion where flow vectors diverge]
    B --> B2[Used for Time to Contact and Visual Odometry]
    C --> C1[Pure Rotation produces flow at all depths]
    C --> C2[Must be Compensated before depth estimation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> Differentiate between **motion field** and **optical flow** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Motion Field | Optical Flow |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Definition | Projection of true 3D velocities onto image plane | Apparent motion of brightness patterns |
| Source | Geometric (3D motion + depth) | Image intensity changes |
| Computation | Requires knowledge of depth, camera motion, 3D structure | Estimated from image sequence directly |
| Example | A ball flying toward a camera has a true radial motion field | The same ball's pixels change brightness — producing a measurable optical flow |
| Equality | Equals optical flow only under Lambertian + static + constant illumination | Generally approximates motion field |

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Source (1) + Example with equality conditions (1).

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional 2]**
> What is the **aperture problem** in optical flow estimation? Why does the OFCE alone fail to resolve it?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **aperture problem** arises because the Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE), $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$, provides only **one equation per pixel** but has **two unknowns** $(u, v)$ — the horizontal and vertical velocity components.

When observing a small aperture (local window) around a uniform edge or line, only the flow component **perpendicular (normal)** to the edge is observable; the **parallel (tangential)** component cannot be determined from local intensity data alone.

**Failure Mode:** An OFCE at a single pixel cannot distinguish whether the edge is moving laterally or purely along its length. Additional global (Horn–Schunck smoothness) or local (Lucas–Kanade constancy) constraints are necessary to obtain the full 2D flow.

> **Mark Distribution:** Statement of aperture problem (1) + Under-determined nature (1) + Need for additional constraints (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *(Compulsory)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> **(a)** Derive the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)** from the brightness constancy assumption. Show all intermediate steps. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With neat derivation, explain the **Lucas–Kanade method** for computing optical flow. State the key assumption, derive the matrix equation, and explain how the aperture problem is resolved. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Derivation of OFCE *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Brightness Constancy Assumption (1 mark):**
The intensity of a moving image point is constant over time:

$$I(x(t), y(t), t) = C$$

**Step 2 — Total Differentiation (2 marks):**
Differentiating with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Chain Rule Expansion (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substituting Notation and Final Form (2 marks):**
Let $I_x = \partial I/\partial x$, $I_y = \partial I/\partial y$, $I_t = \partial I/\partial t$, $u = dx/dt$, $v = dy/dt$:

$$\boxed{I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0}$$

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Lucas–Kanade Method *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Key Assumption (1.5 marks):**
Lucas–Kanade assumes the flow $(u, v)$ is **constant within a small local neighborhood** (typically $5 \times 5$ or $7 \times 7$ window) of the pixel of interest.

**Step 2 — OFCE in the Window (1.5 marks):**
For each pixel $\mathbf{p}_i$ in the window:

$$I_x(\mathbf{p}_i) \cdot u + I_y(\mathbf{p}_i) \cdot v = -I_t(\mathbf{p}_i)$$

**Step 3 — Matrix Formulation (2 marks):**
Stacking the $n$ equations:

$$\mathbf{A} \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{b}, \quad \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} I_x(\mathbf{p}_1) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_1) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(\mathbf{p}_n) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_n) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = -\begin{bmatrix} I_t(\mathbf{p}_1) \\ \vdots \\ I_t(\mathbf{p}_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Least-Squares Solution (2 marks):**
Minimize $\Vert \mathbf{A} \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2$:

$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A} \, \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$$
$$\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$$

provided the $2 \times 2$ matrix $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ is invertible (i.e., at least two gradients in the window are linearly independent — a **corner/textured region**).

**Step 5 — Aperture Resolution (Bonus, conceptual):**
The local window provides multiple OFCEs (overdetermined system), and the corner-rich neighborhoods ensure the matrix is well-conditioned, yielding the full 2D flow.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Horn–Schunck method** for global optical flow estimation. Write the energy functional, derive the Euler–Lagrange equations, and discuss the role of the smoothness parameter $\alpha$. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A camera with focal length $f = 8$ mm is translating with velocity $\mathbf{T} = (0, 0, 5)$ m/s while observing a stationary object at depth $Z = 10$ m. Compute the **translational motion field** at image points $(x, y) = (200, 150)$ and $(0, 0)$ pixels. Assume pixel size = $5 \, \mu\text{m}$. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Horn–Schunck Method *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Energy Functional (2 marks):**

$$E(u, v) = \iint \left[ (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \alpha^2 (\vert \nabla u \vert^2 + \vert \nabla v \vert^2) \right] dx\, dy$$

The first term is the **OFCE data term**; the second is the **smoothness penalty**.

**Step 2 — Euler–Lagrange Equations (2.5 marks):**
Setting $\partial E/\partial u = 0$ and $\partial E/\partial v = 0$:

$$I_x (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \alpha^2 \nabla^2 u = 0$$
$$I_y (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \alpha^2 \nabla^2 v = 0$$

**Step 3 — Iterative Update (1.5 marks):**
With $\nabla^2 u \approx \bar{u} - u$:

$$u^{(k+1)} = \bar{u}^{(k)} - \frac{I_x (I_x \bar{u}^{(k)} + I_y \bar{v}^{(k)} + I_t)}{\alpha^2 + I_x^2 + I_y^2}$$
$$v^{(k+1)} = \bar{v}^{(k)} - \frac{I_y (I_x \bar{u}^{(k)} + I_y \bar{v}^{(k)} + I_t)}{\alpha^2 + I_x^2 + I_y^2}$$

**Step 4 — Role of $\alpha$ (1 mark):**
- Large $\alpha$ → smoother flow (less noise, more bias)
- Small $\alpha$ → flow tightly fits data (more noise, less bias)

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Translational Motion Field Calculation *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Convert Pixel Coordinates to Meters (1 mark):**
Pixel size $p = 5 \, \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m.

$$x_m = 200 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1 \text{ mm}$$
$$y_m = 150 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Translational Flow Equations (2 marks):**
For $T_X = 0, T_Y = 0, T_Z = 5$ m/s and $Z = 10$ m, $f = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m:

$$u_T = \frac{T_Z x - T_X f}{Z} = \frac{T_Z x}{Z}, \quad v_T = \frac{T_Z y - T_Y f}{Z} = \frac{T_Z y}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Compute at $(200, 150)$ (2 marks):**

$$u_T = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} = 0.5 \text{ mm/s}$$
$$v_T = \frac{5 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{10} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} = 0.375 \text{ mm/s}$$

In pixels per second (divide by pixel size):

$$u_T^{\text{pix}} = \frac{0.5 \text{ mm/s}}{5 \, \mu\text{m}} = 100 \text{ pix/s}$$
$$v_T^{\text{pix}} = \frac{0.375 \text{ mm/s}}{5 \, \mu\text{m}} = 75 \text{ pix/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute at $(0, 0)$ — the FOE (2 marks):**
At the origin, both $x = 0$ and $y = 0$:

$$u_T = 0, \quad v_T = 0$$

This point is the **Focus of Expansion (FOE)** — all translational flow vectors appear to emanate from it. Since the camera is moving along $+Z$ (forward), the FOE is at the image center.

> **Physical Insight:** The robot moving forward at 5 m/s will see the central image point stationary while all others flow outward. The flow magnitude increases linearly with image radius — a key input to time-to-contact algorithms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Motion Field with Optical Flow (–2 marks):** Many students use the terms interchangeably. Motion field is *geometric* (3D projection); optical flow is *perceptual/estimated* (pixel-based).
> 2. **Forgetting the Brightness Constancy Assumption (–1.5 marks):** Always state the assumption explicitly before deriving the OFCE.
> 3. **Inverting the $2 \times 2$ Matrix Correctly in Lucas–Kanade (–1 mark):** The determinant $\sum I_x^2 \sum I_y^2 - (\sum I_x I_y)^2$ must be non-zero; the inverse must be explicitly written.
> 4. **Skipping the Role of $\alpha$ in Horn–Schunck (–1 mark):** Examiners expect a clear discussion of the smoothness parameter's bias-variance trade-off.
> 5. **Forgetting the FOE in the motion field problem (–1 mark):** For pure forward translation $T_Z \neq 0$, $T_X = T_Y = 0$, the flow at the principal point is zero — explicitly identifying this as the FOE earns full marks.
> 6. **Unit Conversion Errors in Motion Field Problems (–1 mark):** Always convert pixels to meters using the pixel size before substituting into the flow equations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know Concepts for KTU Exam:**

- **Motion Field** = 2D projection of true 3D velocity onto image plane; depends on depth, translation, rotation.
- **Optical Flow** = apparent 2D motion of brightness; estimated from pixel data.
- **Equality Conditions** for Motion Field = Optical Flow: (1) Lambertian surfaces, (2) static scene, (3) constant illumination.
- **Brightness Constancy Assumption:** $dI/dt = 0$ along a moving point's trajectory.
- **OFCE:** $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ — one equation, two unknowns → **under-determined**.
- **Aperture Problem:** Local intensity data determines only the **normal flow** $u_n = -I_t / \vert \nabla I \vert$, not the parallel component.
- **Horn–Schunck:** Global smoothness; energy $E = \text{data} + \alpha^2 \text{smoothness}$; iterative solution using Euler–Lagrange equations; produces **dense flow**.
- **Lucas–Kanade:** Local constant flow in a window; least-squares $\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$; produces **sparse flow** at corners/textured regions.
- **Lucas–Kanade Invertibility:** $\det(\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}) \neq 0$ requires at least two linearly independent gradients — i.e., **corners**.
- **Translational Flow:** $u_T = (T_Z x - T_X f)/Z$ — depends on $1/Z$ (depth).
- **Rotational Flow:** $u_R = -\Omega_Y f + \Omega_Z y + \Omega_X x y / f - \Omega_Y x^2 / f$ — depth-independent.
- **Focus of Expansion (FOE):** Image point where translational flow vectors converge or diverge — determined by direction of $\mathbf{T}$.
- **Real-World Robot Applications:** Visual odometry, obstacle avoidance, time-to-contact, SLAM feature tracking, ego-motion estimation.
- **Practical Tip:** For large motions, use a **multi-scale image pyramid** (coarse-to-fine optical flow) to avoid violating the small-motion assumption inherent in Taylor expansion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Color Tracking Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Color Tracking Sensors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Color Tracking Sensor (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A color tracking sensor is an electro-optical transducer that converts incident visible-spectrum electromagnetic radiation (wavelength range **380 nm to 750 nm**) into measurable electrical signals (current, voltage, or frequency) through wavelength-selective photodiodes, enabling a robotic system to detect, classify, and follow objects based on their chromatic signature. In the context of **Robot Navigation**, color tracking sensors form the perceptual front-end of vision-based guidance loops, allowing autonomous mobile robots to perform path following, object identification, beacon-based localization, and pick-and-place sorting operations without relying on expensive CMOS/CCD imaging systems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — "The Robot with a Color Compass"** > Imagine you are blindfolded in a room and asked to walk toward a red ball. Someone hands you three flashlights — one with a red filter, one with a green filter, one with a blue filter. By pointing each flashlight at the ball and measuring how much light bounces back, you can deduce the *exact* color of the ball (because a red ball reflects mostly red light, a blue ball reflects mostly blue light). A color tracking sensor does exactly this — but in microseconds, and with silicon photodiodes instead of flashlights. Think of the sensor as the robot's **"Chromatic Eye"** — a tiny, cheap substitute for a full camera that can answer the question: *"What color is that thing in front of me, and roughly where is it?"* ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Spectral Bands > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Visible Spectrum Bands (CIE 1931 Reference):** > - **Red channel:** 620 nm – 750 nm (Long-wavelength cone, L-cone) > - **Green channel:** 495 nm – 570 nm (Medium-wavelength cone, M-cone) > - **Blue channel:** 450 nm – 495 nm (Short-wavelength cone, S-cone) > - **Clear (unfiltered) channel:** 380 nm – 750 nm (luminance reference) > - **Photon energy at 555 nm (peak photopic sensitivity):** $E = hc/\lambda \approx 3.58 \times 10^{-19}$ J > - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s ## 1.4 Classification of Color Tracking Sensors | **Sensor Class** | **Example IC** | **Output Type** | **Typical Use** | |---|---|---|---| | Light-to-Frequency Converter | TCS3200, TCS3210 | Square-wave frequency | Low-cost line/ball follower | | RGB Color Light-to-Digital | TCS34725, APDS-9960 | I²C digital (R, G, B, C) | High-fidelity color sorting | | CMOS Camera + DSP | OV7670 + STM32 | Pixel matrix (YUV/RGB) | Advanced visual servoing | | Spectroscopic Sensor | AS7262 (6-channel) | I²C spectral data | Precision agriculture | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics of Color Detection A color tracking sensor operates on three coupled physical phenomena: 1. **Spectral Reflectance:** A colored object absorbs certain wavelengths and reflects others. The reflected spectrum is a *fingerprint* of the object's surface chemistry. 2. **Photoelectric Effect:** Silicon photodiodes generate a photocurrent $I_{ph}$ proportional to the incident photon flux within their spectral sensitivity band. 3. **Wavelength Filtering:** Thin-film dichroic filters (or color filter arrays) pass only the target band (R, G, or B) to dedicated photodiodes. The governing photodiode current equation is: $$I_{ph} = R(\lambda) \cdot \Phi(\lambda) \cdot A \cdot q$$ where: - $R(\lambda)$ is the spectral responsivity (A/W) - $\Phi(\lambda)$ is the incident spectral radiant flux density (W/m²) - $A$ is the active photodiode area (m²) - $q$ is the elementary charge ($1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C) ## 2.2 The TCS3200 Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic The **TCS3200** is the workhorse IC in KTU lab courses. Its internal signal chain is: - **Step 1 — Light Collection:** A built-in white LED illuminates the target object at a fixed distance (typically 1–3 cm). - **Step 2 — Color Filtering:** An 8×8 photodiode array is split equally into 4 groups of 16, each group covered by a Red, Green, Blue, or Clear filter. - **Step 3 — Current-to-Frequency Conversion:** Each photodiode's photocurrent charges an internal capacitor; when the capacitor voltage reaches a threshold, it discharges through a comparator, producing a **50% duty-cycle square wave** whose frequency is directly proportional to the incident light intensity. - **Step 4 — Multiplexed Output:** The S2/S3 pins select which color filter bank is routed to the output pin OUT, allowing the microcontroller to read R, G, B sequentially. > [!NOTE] > **Why frequency output?** Frequency signals are **immune to cable noise, ground-loop interference, and ADC quantization errors** — making the TCS3200 ideal for noisy robotic environments with PWM motor drivers. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize for KTU ESE** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | |---|---|---| | $f_{OUT} = k \cdot I_{ph} + f_{0}$ | TCS3200 output frequency vs. photocurrent | Hz | | $R_{norm} = R \div (R+G+B)$ | Normalized red intensity (lighting-invariant) | dimensionless | | $G_{norm} = G \div (R+G+B)$ | Normalized green intensity | dimensionless | | $B_{norm} = B \div (R+G+B)$ | Normalized blue intensity | dimensionless | | $H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.5\,[(R-G)+(R-B)]}{\sqrt{(R-G)^{2}+(R-B)(G-B)}}\right)$ | Hue from RGB | degrees | | $S = 1 - \dfrac{3 \cdot \min(R,G,B)}{(R+G+B)}$ | Saturation from RGB | [0, 1] | | $V = \dfrac{\max(R,G,B)}{255}$ | Value (brightness) | [0, 1] | | $x_{c} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} x_{i} \cdot I_{i}}{\sum_{i} I_{i}}$ | Centroid x-coordinate of tracked blob | pixels | | $y_{c} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} y_{i} \cdot I_{i}}{\sum_{i} I_{i}}$ | Centroid y-coordinate of tracked blob | pixels | | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(R_{1}-R_{2})^{2}+(G_{1}-G_{2})^{2}+(B_{1}-B_{2})^{2}}$ | Euclidean color distance | RGB units | | $I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \dfrac{P_{opt}}{h\nu}$ | Photocurrent from optical power | A | > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas enable mobile robots to: > - Distinguish a **red ball** from a **green ball** under varying ambient light (using normalization $R_{norm}, G_{norm}, B_{norm}$). > - Track a **colored beacon** for indoor localization (using centroid $(x_c, y_c)$). > - Implement **HSV-based thresholding** that is robust to shadows and illumination changes (Hue $H$ is lighting-invariant). > - Perform **quality control sorting** on a conveyor (using color distance $\Delta E$). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: RGB-to-HSV Conversion The HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value) color space is **decoupled from lighting intensity**, making it the preferred domain for color tracking in robotics. Given normalized RGB inputs $\in [0,1]$: **Step 1 — Compute the maximum and minimum channel values.** Let $M = \max(R, G, B)$ and $m = \min(R, G, B)$. **Step 2 — Compute the Value channel (brightness).** $$V = M$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Saturation channel (color purity).** $$S = \begin{cases} \dfrac{M - m}{M} & \text{if } M \neq 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } M = 0 \end{cases}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Hue channel (dominant wavelength).** The raw hue offset is: $$H' = \begin{cases} \dfrac{G - B}{M - m} \pmod 6 & \text{if } R = M \\ \dfrac{B - R}{M - m} + 2 & \text{if } G = M \\ \dfrac{R - G}{M - m} + 4 & \text{if } B = M \end{cases}$$ The final hue in degrees is: $$H = 60^{\circ} \times H'$$ > **Logic explanation:** The numerator $(G-B)$, $(B-R)$, or $(R-G)$ captures the **direction of color shift** away from the dominant channel. The denominator $(M-m)$ normalizes by the *chromatic spread*, so a pure red $(M=R=255, m=0)$ yields a well-defined hue even under dim lighting. ## 3.2 Derivation: Centroid of a Tracked Color Blob For a vision system with $N$ pixels whose RGB values match a target color within threshold $\tau$, the object's location on the image plane is given by the **intensity-weighted centroid**: **Step 1 — Define the binary mask.** $$M(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Delta E\bigl((R_{x,y}, G_{x,y}, B_{x,y}),\, (R_t, G_t, B_t)\bigr) \le \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — Define the per-pixel weight as the mask value.** $$w_{i} = M(x_i, y_i)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the weighted sum of coordinates.** $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, x_i \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, y_i$$ **Step 4 — Compute the centroid (center-of-mass of the blob).** $$x_c = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, x_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i}, \qquad y_c = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, y_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i}$$ > **Logic explanation:** This is the discrete 2-D analogue of the physics formula for **center of mass**: $x_{cm} = \sum m_i x_i / \sum m_i$. Each "mass" $w_i$ is 1 if the pixel matches the target color, 0 otherwise. The result is the geometric center of the colored region — a robust estimate even if the blob is partially occluded. ## 3.3 Derivation: TCS3200 Output Frequency The TCS3200 contains a transimpedance amplifier followed by a current-controlled oscillator. The output frequency is given by: $$f_{OUT} = f_{0} + k_{f} \cdot I_{ph}$$ where $k_f$ is the current-to-frequency gain (Hz/A) and $f_0$ is the dark-frequency offset (typically < 10 Hz). After applying the S2/S3 filter selection, the raw RGB counts measured by a microcontroller with gate time $T_g$ are: $$C_R = f_{OUT,R} \cdot T_g, \quad C_G = f_{OUT,G} \cdot T_g, \quad C_B = f_{OUT,B} \cdot T_g$$ > **Logic explanation:** Since $I_{ph} \propto \Phi(\lambda) \cdot R(\lambda)$, the count $C_R$ is proportional to the red light reflected from the target. Longer gate times improve resolution but slow the sensing loop — a key trade-off in real-time robot navigation (typical $T_g$ = 10–100 ms). ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A TCS3200 measures raw counts $C_R = 2400$, $C_G = 800$, $C_B = 600$ over a 50 ms gate time. Determine the normalized color vector and identify the dominant hue. **Step 1 — Compute total intensity.** $$T = C_R + C_G + C_B = 2400 + 800 + 600 = 3800$$ **Step 2 — Normalize each channel.** $$R_{norm} = \frac{2400}{3800} = 0.6316$$ $$G_{norm} = \frac{800}{3800} = 0.2105$$ $$B_{norm} = \frac{600}{3800} = 0.1579$$ **Step 3 — Verify closure (sum should equal 1).** $$0.6316 + 0.2105 + 0.1579 \approx 1.0000 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Identify dominant channel.** Since $R_{norm} > G_{norm} > B_{norm}$, the dominant hue is in the **red region**. Using the RGB-to-Hue formula with $R$ as the maximum: $$H = 60 \times \frac{G_{norm} - B_{norm}}{R_{norm} - \min(G_{norm}, B_{norm})}$$ $$H = 60 \times \frac{0.2105 - 0.1579}{0.6316 - 0.1579} = 60 \times \frac{0.0526}{0.4737}$$ $$H \approx 6.66^{\circ}$$ > **Result:** Hue ≈ 6.66° falls in the **red sector** (0°–60°), confirming the sensor is tracking a reddish object — consistent with the raw count dominance. # 4.
