Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Color Tracking Robot ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Target Object
Colored Surface] --> B[White LED
Illuminator Module] B --> C[RGB Filter Array
Photodiode Bank] C --> D[Current to Frequency
Converter TCS3200] D --> E[Microcontroller
ATmega328 / STM32] E --> F[HSV Conversion
and Thresholding] F --> G[Centroid
Computation] G --> H{Pixel Error
Greater than Threshold} H -- Yes --> I[Steer Left or Right
Motor Driver] H -- No --> J[Drive Forward
Motor Driver] I --> K[Wheel Actuators
DC Gear Motors] J --> K K --> L[Robot Reorients
Toward Target] L --> A ``` ## 4.2 Internal Architecture of the TCS3200 ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1[Photodiode Array 8 by 8] P1[16x Red Filtered
Photodiodes] P2[16x Green Filtered
Photodiodes] P3[16x Blue Filtered
Photodiodes] P4[16x Clear No Filter
Photodiodes] end S1 --> MUX[Channel Select
Multiplexer S2 S3] MUX --> TIA[Transimpedance
Amplifier] TIA --> OSC[Current Controlled
Oscillator] OSC --> OUT[Square Wave
Output Pin OUT] OE[OE Pin Output Enable] --> MUX SCL[S0 S1 Frequency
Scaling Pins] --> OSC ``` ## 4.3 Color Tracking Algorithm Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Robot Power On]) --> Init[Initialize GPIO
and I2C Bus] Init --> Pulse[Generate LED
Illumination Pulse] Pulse --> ReadR[Read Red Channel
Frequency Count] ReadR --> ReadG[Read Green Channel
Frequency Count] ReadG --> ReadB[Read Blue Channel
Frequency Count] ReadB --> Norm[Normalize RGB
Divide by Sum] Norm --> HueCalc[Compute Hue H
Saturation S] HueCalc --> ThreshCheck{H within Target
Hue Window} ThreshCheck -- No --> Search[Spin Slowly
Searching Pattern] ThreshCheck -- Yes --> Centroid[Compute Object
Centroid] Centroid --> ErrorCalc[Compute Centroid
Offset Error] ErrorCalc --> Steer{Error Sign
Positive or Negative} Steer -- Positive --> SteerR[Steer Right PWM] Steer -- Negative --> SteerL[Steer Left PWM] SteerR --> Forward SteerL --> Forward Search --> Forward Forward[Drive Forward PWM] --> Pulse ``` ## 4.4 RGB Color Cube vs. HSV Color Cylinder Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RGB[RGB Cartesian Cube] A1[Origin 0,0,0 Black] A2[1,0,0 Pure Red] A3[0,1,0 Pure Green] A4[0,0,1 Pure Blue] A5[1,1,1 White] end subgraph HSV[HSV Cylinder] B1[Center Bottom V=0 Black] B2[H=0 deg S=1 V=1 Red] B3[H=120 deg S=1 V=1 Green] B4[H=240 deg S=1 V=1 Blue] B5[Top Center V=1 White] end A1 -. Non Linear Mapping .-> B1 A2 -. Hue Projection .-> B2 A3 -. Hue Projection .-> B3 A4 -. Hue Projection .-> B4 A5 -. Value Projection .-> B5 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a color tracking sensor and list its primary applications in mobile robot navigation. Mention any two key limitations of low-cost RGB color sensors.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A color tracking sensor is an electro-optical device that detects the chromatic content of incident light reflected from a target surface by using wavelength-selective photodiodes (Red, Green, Blue filters) and converts it into a processable electrical signal for a robotic controller. **Primary applications (1.5 marks):** 1. **Beacon-based localization** — robots follow colored markers for indoor navigation. 2. **Object identification and sorting** — conveyor robots separate items by color (e.g., fruit grading, waste sorting). 3. **Line following with color coding** — multi-lane AGVs follow red/green/blue tracks. 4. **Ball-following or target-chasing robots** — typical in KTU lab courses. **Key limitations (1.5 marks):** 1. **Ambient light sensitivity** — performance degrades under varying illumination; requires normalization or HSV thresholding. 2. **Limited spatial resolution** — single-point sensors cannot localize the target laterally without a camera-based centroid algorithm. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Explain why HSV color space is preferred over RGB for color tracking in robotic vision. State the formula for computing Hue $H$ when $R$ is the maximum channel.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Why HSV is preferred (2 marks):** The HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value) representation **decouples chromatic information (Hue $H$, Saturation $S$) from intensity (Value $V$)**. This decoupling makes the tracker robust to: - Shadows cast on the target - Changes in ambient lighting intensity - Variations in the LED illuminator's brightness In contrast, RGB channels shift uniformly with illumination changes, making a fixed RGB threshold unreliable. **Hue formula when $R$ is the maximum (1 mark):** $$H = 60^{\circ} \times \left[ \frac{G - B}{\max(R,G,B) - \min(R,G,B)} \pmod{6} \right]$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **With a neat block diagram, describe the internal architecture of the TCS3200 color sensor. Explain the role of the S2, S3, S0, and S1 control pins.** **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram Description (4 marks):** The TCS3200 consists of the following cascaded blocks: 1. **8×8 Photodiode Array** — divided into 4 groups of 16 photodiodes each, covered with Red, Green, Blue, and Clear filters respectively. 2. **Current-to-Frequency Converter** — converts the photocurrent from the selected photodiode group into a 50% duty-cycle square wave. 3. **Multiplexer** — controlled by S2 and S3 pins; routes one of the four color channels to the output. 4. **Output Enable (OE) pin** — active-low enable for the output driver. 5. **Frequency Scaling Prescaler** — controlled by S0 and S1; divides the output frequency by 1, 2, 5, or 10 to match the microcontroller's timer input range. **Pin Functions Table (3 marks):** | **Pin** | **Function** | **Logic Levels** | |---|---|---| | S0, S1 | Frequency scaling | 00 = Power-down, 01 = 2%, 10 = 20%, 11 = 100% | | S2, S3 | Filter selection | 00 = Red, 01 = Blue, 10 = Clear, 11 = Green | | OE | Output enable | LOW = enabled, HIGH = high-Z | | OUT | Square-wave output | Frequency proportional to light intensity | #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A TCS3200 sensor interfaced with an ATmega328 microcontroller produces the following raw frequency counts over a 100 ms gate window: Red = 5000 Hz, Green = 3200 Hz, Blue = 2800 Hz.** **(i) Compute the normalized RGB values.** **(ii) Determine the dominant Hue using the HSV conversion formula and identify the color band.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Normalized RGB (3 marks):** $$R_{norm} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 3200 + 2800} = \frac{5000}{11000} = 0.4545$$ $$G_{norm} = \frac{3200}{11000} = 0.2909$$ $$B_{norm} = \frac{2800}{11000} = 0.2545$$ **Verification:** $0.4545 + 0.2909 + 0.2545 = 1.0000$ ✓ **(ii) Hue Calculation (4 marks):** Since $R_{norm}$ is the maximum: $$H = 60^{\circ} \times \frac{G_{norm} - B_{norm}}{R_{norm} - \min(G_{norm}, B_{norm})}$$ $$H = 60^{\circ} \times \frac{0.2909 - 0.2545}{0.4545 - 0.2545}$$ $$H = 60^{\circ} \times \frac{0.0364}{0.2000} = 60^{\circ} \times 0.182$$ $$H \approx 10.91^{\circ}$$ **Color identification:** A hue of $10.91^{\circ}$ lies within the **red-to-orange band** (0°–30°). The robot's controller would therefore interpret the target as an **orange-colored object**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the principle of centroid-based color tracking. Derive the mathematical expression for the centroid $(x_c, y_c)$ of a tracked color blob in a 2-D image.** **Model Answer:** **Principle (3 marks):** Centroid-based color tracking is a vision algorithm that estimates the geometric center of a contiguous region of pixels whose color matches a predefined target. The algorithm: 1. Captures an image frame. 2. Applies a binary mask $M(x,y)$ — pixels inside the target color range get weight 1, others get 0. 3. Computes the intensity-weighted average of all $(x,y)$ coordinates inside the mask. 4. Returns the centroid $(x_c, y_c)$, which the robot uses as the direction vector to navigate toward. **Derivation (4 marks):** Consider $N$ pixels in the image with coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ and binary weights $w_i \in \{0, 1\}$. The sum of weights is: $$W = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i$$ The first moment of the pixel distribution along the $x$-axis is: $$M_x = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot x_i$$ Similarly along the $y$-axis: $$M_y = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot y_i$$ By the definition of the center of mass of a discrete system: $$x_c = \frac{M_x}{W} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot x_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i}$$ $$y_c = \frac{M_y}{W} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot y_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i}$$ This gives the **centroid** of the tracked blob — a sub-pixel accurate, noise-robust estimate of the target's position. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Compare the RGB and HSV color spaces for robotic color tracking. Mention three real-world scenarios where HSV-based tracking outperforms RGB-based tracking.** **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | **Parameter** | **RGB Space** | **HSV Space** | |---|---|---| | Coordinate system | Cartesian cube | Cylindrical | | Channels | R, G, B (additive primaries) | H (hue), S (saturation), V (value) | | Lighting sensitivity | High — all channels scale with light | Low — only V scales; H and S are stable | | Thresholding | Difficult (cube region) | Easy (hue window + saturation floor) | | Computational cost | Low | Moderate (requires max/min search) | | Best suited for | Color identification under controlled light | Real-world tracking under varying light | **Three scenarios where HSV wins (3 marks):** 1. **Outdoor navigation in sunlight** — sunlight intensity varies by 10× from morning to noon. HSV's hue channel remains stable, while RGB ratios shift dramatically. 2. **Indoor robots with mixed lighting** — fluorescent + LED + incandescent lights produce different white balances; HSV decouples the chromatic content from these. 3. **Tracking shiny or reflective objects** — specular highlights change the RGB values drastically but affect only the V (value) channel in HSV, allowing the tracker to ignore the highlight and follow the underlying object's color. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting to normalize RGB** — Students often write $(R, G, B) = (2400, 800, 600)$ as the "color" without dividing by the sum. This loses 1–2 marks because the same normalized vector under dim light looks identical to the raw counts under bright light, making the tracker non-robust. > 2. **Incorrect hue formula selection** — Using the wrong branch (e.g., applying the $R$-max formula when $G$ is actually the max) gives a meaningless hue value. Always identify the maximum channel first. > 3. **Confusing centroid with average bounding-box center** — The centroid is **intensity-weighted**, not just the geometric center of the bounding box. A blob with denser pixels on one side will have its centroid shifted toward that side. > 4. **Skipping the $\pmod 6$ in the hue formula** — For hues near $0°$ or $360°$, omission causes wrap-around errors. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this. > 5. **Not mentioning ambient-light compensation** — Any color-tracking answer that ignores lighting variation is considered incomplete in KTU's OBE-style marking. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Color Tracking Sensors** - **Definition (1-liner):** A color tracking sensor detects chromatic content using RGB-filtered photodiodes and outputs a usable electrical signal for robot navigation. - **Core IC for KTU labs:** TCS3200 (light-to-frequency converter) and TCS34725 (I²C digital). - **Output type of TCS3200:** **Square-wave frequency** (50% duty cycle); immune to analog noise. - **Control pins of TCS3200:** S0, S1 (frequency prescaler), S2, S3 (filter select), OE (output enable). - **Filter mapping:** 00=Red, 01=Blue, 10=Clear, 11=Green. - **Normalization formula:** $R_{norm} = R / (R+G+B)$ — makes the sensor lighting-invariant. - **HSV vs. RGB:** HSV decouples hue from brightness; **always prefer HSV for real-world robotic tracking**. - **Hue formula (R-max branch):** $H = 60 \times [(G-B) / (M-m)] \pmod 6$. - **Saturation formula:** $S = 1 - 3 \cdot \min(R,G,B) / (R+G+B)$. - **Centroid formula:** $x_c = \sum w_i x_i / \sum w_i$ and similarly for $y_c$. - **Color distance metric:** $\Delta E = \sqrt{(R_1-R_2)^2 + (G_1-G_2)^2 + (B_1-B_2)^2}$. - **Visible spectrum:** **380 nm – 750 nm**; red ≈ 700 nm, green ≈ 530 nm, blue ≈ 470 nm. - **Photodiode current:** $I_{ph} = q \eta P_{opt} / h\nu$. - **Applications in KTU syllabus:** ball-follower robots, line-follower with multiple colored tracks, AGV beacon localization, color-based sorting arms. - **Common pitfalls to avoid in exams:** forgetting normalization, wrong hue-branch selection, skipping $\pmod 6$, ignoring ambient-light compensation.

topics.title: Feature Extraction - Feature Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feature Extraction - Feature Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Navigation** and computer vision, a **Feature** is formally defined as a distinctive, measurable, and locally unique pattern or region in sensor data (image, point cloud, lidar scan) that can be reliably extracted and used as a stable reference landmark for tasks such as localization, mapping, obstacle avoidance, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), and place recognition.

Mathematically, a feature $\mathbf{f}$ extracted from a sensor measurement $\mathbf{I}$ is a function:
$$\mathbf{f} = \Phi(\mathbf{I}, \mathbf{x}, \sigma)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{I}$ is the input sensor data (image, scan)
- $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)$ is the spatial location in the sensor frame
- $\sigma$ is the scale parameter
- $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the feature extraction operator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *feature* in robot navigation is NOT a raw pixel or a single range value. It is a **derived, compact, and invariant representation** of the underlying environment that exhibits **repeatability, distinctiveness, and robustness** under varying viewpoints, illumination, and environmental conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a tourist in a new city without a GPS. How do you navigate? You look for **landmarks** — a tall red building, a uniquely shaped fountain, a specific statue at a junction. You do not remember every single brick on every wall; you remember **distinctive, memorable elements** that stand out.

A robot does exactly the same. Instead of storing millions of raw pixel values, it extracts **features** — compact, distinctive descriptors of its environment — that it can later match, recognize, and use to determine "I have been here before" (loop closure) or "I am at this specific location" (localization).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Human Face Recognition:** When you recognize a friend, you do not memorize every pixel of their face. You extract features: "round face, sharp nose, thick eyebrows." These are robust, distinctive, and invariant to lighting. Robot feature extraction follows the same biological principle.

## 1.3 Classification of Features in Robot Navigation

Features are broadly classified into the following categories:

| Category | Description | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Point Features** | Localized interest points (corners, blobs) | Visual SLAM, place recognition |
| **Line Features** | Straight edges, walls, corridors | Indoor mapping, structural environments |
| **Geometric Features** | Planes, corners, cylinders | 3D object recognition, lidar SLAM |
| **Appearance Features** | Color histograms, texture patches | Topological mapping, scene classification |
| **Topological Features** | Nodes, edges, connectivity | Graph-based navigation |
| **Semantic Features** | Doors, rooms, traffic signs | High-level decision making |

## 1.4 Why Features Matter in Robot Navigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Feature Utility in Navigation:**
> 1. **Data Compression:** Reduce gigabytes of raw sensor data to kilobytes of feature descriptors.
> 2. **Invariance:** Enable recognition despite rotation, scale, lighting, and viewpoint changes.
> 3. **Computational Efficiency:** Speed up matching, registration, and loop-closure detection for real-time navigation.

## 1.5 Desired Properties of an Ideal Feature

An ideal feature for robot navigation must satisfy:

- **Repeatability:** Extracted reliably from the same scene under different conditions.
- **Distinctiveness:** Carries unique information to enable correct matching.
- **Locality:** Occupies a small region for robustness to occlusion and clutter.
- **Quantity:** Dense enough to allow reliable navigation decisions.
- **Accuracy:** Precisely localized in the sensor frame.
- **Efficiency:** Real-time computable on embedded robotic hardware.
- **Invariance:** Robust to rotation, scale, illumination, and affine transformations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Feature Extraction Pipeline

The feature extraction process in robot navigation follows a structured pipeline:

### Step 1 — Sensor Data Acquisition
Raw data is captured from cameras, LiDAR, sonar, IMU, or RGB-D sensors. This forms the input space $\mathbf{I} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W}$ (for 2D images) or $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 3}$ (for 3D point clouds).

### Step 2 — Preprocessing
Noise reduction, normalization, and geometric corrections are applied:
$$\mathbf{I}_{\text{pre}} = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{I}, \mu, \sigma^2)$$
where $\mathcal{N}$ represents normalization, $\mu$ is the mean intensity, and $\sigma^2$ is the variance.

### Step 3 — Feature Detection (Interest Point Localization)
The detector locates candidate regions that exhibit high information content (e.g., corners, blobs, edges). This is done by computing a response function $R(x, y)$ and applying non-maximum suppression.

### Step 4 — Feature Description
Each detected keypoint is converted into a compact descriptor vector $\mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ that encodes the local appearance around the keypoint.

### Step 5 — Feature Matching
Descriptors from different sensor readings are compared using a similarity metric (e.g., Euclidean distance):
$$D(\mathbf{d}_1, \mathbf{d}_2) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(d_{1,i} - d_{2,i})^2}$$

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — The Feature Response Function

The most common formulation for feature detection uses the **second-moment matrix** (also called the structure tensor):

$$M(x, y) = \sum_{u,v} w(u, v) \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where:
- $I_x$ and $I_y$ are the partial image derivatives in the $x$ and $y$ directions
- $w(u, v)$ is a weighting window (typically Gaussian)

The eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of $M$ determine the local image structure:
- If both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are large $\Rightarrow$ **Corner Feature**
- If one is large and the other small $\Rightarrow$ **Edge Feature**
- If both are small $\Rightarrow$ **Flat Region (No Feature)**

## 2.3 The Harris Corner Detector Response

The Harris corner response is a rotation-invariant scalar function:
$$R_{\text{Harris}} = \det(M) - k \cdot \text{trace}(M)^2$$
$$R_{\text{Harris}} = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2$$

where $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ is an empirical sensitivity constant.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Application |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| $M = \sum w \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Structure Tensor (Second-Moment Matrix) | Corner & edge detection |
| $R = \det(M) - k \cdot \text{trace}(M)^2$ | Harris Corner Response | Interest point detection |
| $R = \frac{\det(M)}{\text{trace}(M) + \epsilon}$ | Shi-Tomasi Response | Improved corner selection |
| $D(\mathbf{d}_1, \mathbf{d}_2) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(d_{1,i} - d_{2,i})^2}$ | Euclidean Distance for Matching | Descriptor comparison |
| $\text{NMS}(R) = \{ p \, \vert \, R(p) \geq R(q) \, \forall q \in \mathcal{N}(p) \}$ | Non-Maximum Suppression | Keypoint selection |
| $L(x, y, \sigma) = G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y)$ | Scale-Space Representation | Multi-scale feature detection |
| $\text{DoG} = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma)$ | Difference of Gaussians (SIFT) | Blob detection |
| $H = p^T M p$ | Quadratic Form (Eigenvalue Analysis) | Region classification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always remember the **three cases** of eigenvalues of the structure tensor. This is a frequently asked question worth 3-7 marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Tesla, Waymo):** Feature extraction from camera feeds for lane detection, traffic sign recognition, and visual odometry.
- **Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva):** QR-code and visual feature extraction for shelf identification.
- **Aerial Drones (DJI):** SIFT/SURF features for terrain-relative navigation and visual SLAM.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Rock and terrain feature extraction for autonomous path planning.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Sonar-based feature extraction for navigation in murky waters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Image Patch to Feature Strength

Let us derive the Harris corner response from first principles to ensure complete clarity for KTU board examination.

### Step 1 — Define the Local Intensity Change

Consider a small window around a point $(x, y)$ that is shifted by $(u, v)$. The change in intensity is:
$$E(u, v) = \sum_{x, y} w(x, y) \left[ I(x+u, y+v) - I(x, y) \right]^2$$

This $E(u, v)$ measures how the image intensity changes when the window is shifted. A "good feature" is a point where $E(u, v)$ is **large for all shift directions**.

### Step 2 — Taylor Series Approximation

Using a first-order Taylor expansion:
$$I(x+u, y+v) \approx I(x, y) + I_x u + I_y v$$

Substituting into the expression for $E$:
$$E(u, v) \approx \sum_{x, y} w(x, y) \left[ I_x u + I_y v \right]^2$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Quadratic Form

Expanding the squared term:
$$E(u, v) \approx \sum_{x, y} w(x, y) \left[ I_x^2 u^2 + 2 I_x I_y u v + I_y^2 v^2 \right]$$

Reorganizing in matrix form:
$$E(u, v) \approx \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} M \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}$$

where $M$ is the structure tensor:
$$M = \sum_{x, y} w(x, y) \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Eigenvalue Decomposition

Let $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ be the eigenvalues of $M$. Then:
$$E(u, v) \approx \lambda_1 u'^2 + \lambda_2 v'^2$$

where $u'$ and $v'$ are the shifted coordinates along the principal axes.

### Step 5 — Classification of Image Structure

| Case | Eigenvalues | Structure | $E(u, v)$ Behavior |
|------|-------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Both $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ small | $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | Flat region | Small change in all directions |
| One large, one small | $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2$ or vice versa | Edge | Small change along one direction |
| Both large | $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ both large | Corner | Large change in all directions |

### Step 6 — Derive the Harris Response

To avoid explicit eigenvalue computation (which is expensive), Harris and Stephens proposed the corner response:
$$R_{\text{Harris}} = \det(M) - k \cdot \text{trace}(M)^2$$

Using the relationships:
$$\det(M) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2, \quad \text{trace}(M) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$$

We can write:
$$R_{\text{Harris}} = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2$$

This produces:
- $R > 0$ at corners
- $R < 0$ at edges
- $R \approx 0$ in flat regions

### Step 7 — Computational Simplification

For a small patch with $I_x^2 = a$, $I_y^2 = b$, $I_x I_y = c$:
$$\det(M) = ab - c^2, \quad \text{trace}(M) = a + b$$

Substituting:
$$R_{\text{Harris}} = (ab - c^2) - k(a + b)^2$$

This is the final computable form used in real-time robotic vision systems.

## 3.2 Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Given a $2 \times 2$ structure tensor $M = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$ and $k = 0.04$, classify the region.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute determinant:
$$\det(M) = (4)(3) - (1)(1) = 12 - 1 = 11$$

Step 2: Compute trace:
$$\text{trace}(M) = 4 + 3 = 7$$

Step 3: Compute Harris response:
$$R = 11 - 0.04 \times (7)^2 = 11 - 0.04 \times 49 = 11 - 1.96 = 9.04$$

Step 4: Compute eigenvalues for verification:
$$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 11 = 0$$
$$\lambda = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{49 - 44}}{2} = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$$
$$\lambda_1 \approx 4.618, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 2.382$$

Step 5: Classification — Since both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are **large and positive**, the region is classified as a **CORNER feature**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Feature Categories and Mathematical Signatures

| Feature Type | Mathematical Signature | Invariant To | Used In |
|--------------|------------------------|--------------|---------|
| **Corner** | $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 \gg 0$ | Rotation | Harris, FAST |
| **Blob** | $\nabla^2 I = 0$ at scale | Scale, Rotation | SIFT, SURF |
| **Edge** | One $\lambda \gg 0$, other $\approx 0$ | Rotation | Canny, Sobel |
| **Ridge** | $G(\sigma) * I$ local extremum | Scale | Fingerprint, road |
| **SIFT Keypoint** | DoG scale-space extremum | Scale, Rotation, Illumination | Visual SLAM |
| **ORB Feature** | FAST + BRIEF | Rotation, Limited Scale | ORB-SLAM |
| **LiDAR Corner** | High curvature in point cloud | Translation | LOAM, LIO-SAM |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Feature Extraction Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Data Input: Camera LiDAR or Sonar] --> B[Preprocessing: Noise Removal and Normalization]
    B --> C[Scale Space Construction: Gaussian Pyramid]
    C --> D[Feature Detection: Interest Point Localization]
    D --> E[Non Maximum Suppression: Keep Strongest Points]
    E --> F[Feature Description: Build Descriptor Vector]
    F --> G[Feature Matching: Compare Descriptors]
    G --> H[Navigation Output: Pose Map or SLAM]
    H --> I[Localization and Path Planning]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow for Region Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image Patch] --> B[Compute Image Gradients Ix and Iy]
    B --> C[Build Structure Tensor M]
    C --> D[Compute Harris Response R]
    D --> E{Is R greater than Threshold}
    E -->|Yes| F{Check Eigenvalues}
    F -->|Both Large| G[CORNER Feature Selected]
    F -->|One Large One Small| H[EDGE Feature Detected]
    E -->|No| I[FLAT Region Discarded]
    G --> J[Output to Descriptor Module]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Final Feature Map for Navigation]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Feature-Based Navigation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Perception_Layer
        P1[Camera Module]
        P2[LiDAR Module]
        P3[IMU Module]
    end
    subgraph Feature_Processing_Layer
        F1[Feature Detector]
        F2[Feature Descriptor]
        F3[Feature Matcher]
    end
    subgraph Navigation_Layer
        N1[Localization Engine]
        N2[Mapping Module]
        N3[Path Planner]
    end
    P1 --> F1
    P2 --> F1
    P3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> N1
    F3 --> N2
    N1 --> N3
    N2 --> N3
```

## 4.4 Concept Map of Feature Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ideal Feature for Robot Navigation] --> B[Repeatability]
    A --> C[Distinctiveness]
    A --> D[Locality]
    A --> E[Computational Efficiency]
    A --> F[Invariance Properties]
    B --> B1[Detected in same scene under varied conditions]
    C --> C1[Unique signature for matching]
    D --> D1[Small spatial footprint]
    E --> E1[Real time on embedded hardware]
    F --> F1[Rotation Invariance]
    F --> F2[Scale Invariance]
    F --> F3[Illumination Invariance]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a "Feature" in the context of robot navigation. List any four desirable properties of an ideal feature. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A feature in robot navigation is a **distinctive, measurable, and locally unique pattern** in sensor data that can be reliably extracted and used as a stable reference for localization, mapping, and navigation tasks. It is a compact representation derived from raw sensor measurements.

**Four Desirable Properties:**
1. **Repeatability** — Extracted reliably from the same scene under varying conditions.
2. **Distinctiveness** — Carries unique information enabling correct matching.
3. **Locality** — Occupies a small region for robustness to occlusion.
4. **Computational Efficiency** — Real-time computable on embedded hardware.

### Question 2: What is the significance of the structure tensor in feature extraction? State the Harris corner response formula. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The structure tensor (second-moment matrix) is a $2 \times 2$ matrix computed from image gradients that captures the local intensity structure around a point. Its eigenvalues $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are used to classify the local region as a **corner, edge, or flat area**.

The Harris corner response formula is:
$$R_{\text{Harris}} = \det(M) - k \cdot \text{trace}(M)^2 = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2$$

A point is classified as a corner when **R is large and positive**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of feature extraction in robot navigation. Discuss the classification of features with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Feature extraction is the process of identifying and representing distinctive, compact, and invariant structures from raw sensor data for use in navigation tasks such as localization, mapping, and obstacle avoidance.

**Classification of Features:**

1. **Point Features** — Localized interest points such as corners (Harris, FAST) and blobs (SIFT, SURF). Used in visual SLAM for tracking and place recognition.
2. **Line Features** — Straight edges, walls, and corridor boundaries. Used in indoor robot navigation where structural geometry dominates.
3. **Geometric Features** — Planes, cylinders, and 3D corners extracted from point clouds. Used in 3D object recognition.
4. **Appearance Features** — Color histograms and texture patches. Used in topological mapping where global scene appearance matters.
5. **Semantic Features** — High-level labels such as doors, rooms, or traffic signs. Used in human-robot interaction and symbolic navigation.

The choice of feature depends on the sensor type, environment, and task requirements.

**(b)** Derive the Harris corner detection response from the intensity change function. Explain how eigenvalues are used to classify image regions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Starting from the intensity change function for a shifted window:
$$E(u, v) = \sum_{x, y} w(x, y) [I(x+u, y+v) - I(x, y)]^2$$

Applying Taylor series approximation:
$$I(x+u, y+v) \approx I(x, y) + I_x u + I_y v$$

Substituting:
$$E(u, v) \approx \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} M \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}$$

where $M = \sum w \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$.

The Harris response is defined as:
$$R = \det(M) - k \cdot \text{trace}(M)^2 = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2$$

**Region Classification Using Eigenvalues:**

| Condition | Classification |
|-----------|----------------|
| $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | Flat region (no feature) |
| One $\lambda \gg 0$, other $\approx 0$ | Edge |
| $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ both large | Corner |

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the feature extraction pipeline used in robot navigation. Discuss the role of scale-space representation in this pipeline. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The feature extraction pipeline consists of the following stages:

1. **Sensor Data Acquisition** — Capturing raw data from cameras, LiDAR, or other sensors.
2. **Preprocessing** — Noise removal, normalization, and geometric correction.
3. **Scale-Space Construction** — Building a Gaussian pyramid to enable multi-scale feature detection.
4. **Feature Detection** — Locating interest points using gradient-based or intensity-based operators.
5. **Non-Maximum Suppression** — Retaining only the strongest keypoints.
6. **Feature Description** — Building a compact descriptor vector around each keypoint.
7. **Feature Matching** — Comparing descriptors across frames or with a map.

**Role of Scale-Space Representation:**

Scale-space representation is defined as:
$$L(x, y, \sigma) = G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y)$$

It allows features to be detected at multiple scales, ensuring **scale invariance** — the same physical feature can be detected whether the robot is near or far from it. This is critical for real-world robot navigation where distances vary continuously.

**(b)** A $2 \times 2$ structure tensor at a pixel is given by $M = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$. Using $k = 0.04$, compute the Harris response and classify the region. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Determinant**
$$\det(M) = (9)(4) - (2)(2) = 36 - 4 = 32$$

**Step 2: Compute Trace**
$$\text{trace}(M) = 9 + 4 = 13$$

**Step 3: Compute Harris Response**
$$R = \det(M) - k \cdot \text{trace}(M)^2$$
$$R = 32 - 0.04 \times (13)^2$$
$$R = 32 - 0.04 \times 169$$
$$R = 32 - 6.76 = 25.24$$

**Step 4: Compute Eigenvalues for Verification**
$$\lambda^2 - 13\lambda + 32 = 0$$
$$\lambda = \frac{13 \pm \sqrt{169 - 128}}{2} = \frac{13 \pm \sqrt{41}}{2}$$
$$\lambda_1 \approx 9.70, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 3.30$$

**Step 5: Classification**
Since both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are **large and positive**, and $R = 25.24 \gg 0$, the region is classified as a **STRONG CORNER FEATURE**.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing the determinant and trace formulas** — Students often write $\text{trace}(M) = \det(M)$. Remember: $\text{trace}$ is the **sum** of diagonal elements, $\det$ is the **product minus off-diagonal squared**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $k$ factor** — The Harris response is NOT just $\det(M) - \text{trace}(M)^2$. The empirical constant $k$ is **mandatory**.
> 3. **Misinterpreting eigenvalue behavior** — Many students write "both eigenvalues are large $\Rightarrow$ edge." This is **WRONG**. Both large $\Rightarrow$ **corner**. One large, one small $\Rightarrow$ **edge**.
> 4. **Skipping the Taylor approximation step** — In derivations, examiners expect the first-order Taylor expansion to be explicitly shown. Skipping it costs 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Not stating units or threshold values** — Always mention that $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ and that $R$ is a **dimensionless scalar response**.
> 6. **Mixing up features with raw pixels** — A feature is NOT a pixel. It is a **derived, semantic representation**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A feature is a distinctive, measurable, locally unique, and invariant representation of a sensor measurement used as a stable reference for robot navigation tasks.
- **Structure Tensor:** $M = \sum w \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$ — captures local image structure.
- **Harris Response:** $R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2$ — corner detector response.
- **Region Classification Rules:**
  - $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ both small $\Rightarrow$ Flat
  - One large, one small $\Rightarrow$ Edge
  - Both large $\Rightarrow$ Corner
- **Seven Desired Properties:** Repeatability, Distinctiveness, Locality, Quantity, Accuracy, Efficiency, Invariance.
- **Feature Types:** Point, Line, Geometric, Appearance, Topological, Semantic.
- **Scale-Space:** $L(x, y, \sigma) = G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y)$ — enables multi-scale detection.
- **Pipeline Order:** Sensor → Preprocess → Scale-Space → Detect → NMS → Describe → Match → Navigate.
- **Key Algorithms:** Harris, Shi-Tomasi, SIFT, SURF, ORB, FAST, BRIEF.
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** This topic typically carries 7-14 marks in Part B and 3 marks in Part A.
- **Real-Time Use:** Feature extraction is the **backbone of visual SLAM, visual odometry, and place recognition** in modern autonomous systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Target Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Target Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Navigation**, the **Target Environment** (also referred to as the *robot workspace*, *operational environment*, or *task space*) is formally defined as the complete, bounded physical and logical space within which a mobile robot is expected to perceive, plan, and execute its motion to accomplish a defined set of tasks.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework for OEBRT723, the Target Environment encapsulates every observable and non-observable entity, surface, obstacle, landmark, and dynamic agent that can influence the robot's motion-planning algorithms. It serves as the **fundamental referential universe** against which all localization, mapping (SLAM), path planning, and control decisions are computed.

Mathematically, the Target Environment $\mathcal{E}$ is represented as a tuple:

$$\mathcal{E} = (\mathcal{W}, \mathcal{O}, \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{T})$$

where:
- $\mathcal{W}$ = the workspace (free and occupied volumes)
- $\mathcal{O}$ = the set of static obstacles
- $\mathcal{D}$ = the set of dynamic obstacles / moving agents
- $\mathcal{S}$ = the sensor perception region
- $\mathcal{T}$ = the time-evolution parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Target Environment is *not* merely a physical map. It is a **multi-layered abstraction** combining geometric data, semantic labels, dynamic states, and sensor noise characteristics. Mastery of this topic is a direct prerequisite for modules covering SLAM, Probabilistic Robotics, and Motion Planning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded and dropped into your own house** for the first time. You cannot see anything, but you are given a **walking stick (sensor)**, a **notebook (memory)**, and the task *"find the kitchen and pick up a glass of water"*.

- The **house** = your Target Environment.
- The **walls, furniture, doors** = static obstacles ($\mathcal{O}$).
- A **pet cat or another person walking** = dynamic obstacles ($\mathcal{D}$).
- Your **walking stick's reach** = sensor perception range ($\mathcal{S}$).
- The **notebook sketches you make as you tap around** = the world model / map.

Every decision you make — turning left, going straight, avoiding the couch — is fundamentally a function of the **Target Environment's properties** (cluttered, dark, narrow, dynamic). The smarter your *model* of the environment, the more efficient your navigation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connection:** In KTU's autonomous vehicle research (e.g., the Kerala startup ecosystem focused on agri-robots and autonomous shuttles), the Target Environment is the **road, traffic, pedestrians, weather, and GPS-denied zones** — the entire bundle of realities a self-driving car must reason about simultaneously.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Scale of Operation:** $\mathcal{E}$ can range from **1 m** (micro-swarm robots in lab) to **$\mathbf{10^6}$ m** (planetary rovers like NASA's Perseverance).
- **Update Rate:** Dynamic environments demand sensor refresh rates of **10 Hz to 100 Hz**.
- **Map Resolution:** Typical occupancy grid cells are **$\mathbf{5}$ cm to $\mathbf{50}$ cm**.
- **Dimensionality:** 2D (planar) or 3D (volumetric).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Target Environments

The Target Environment is classified along **four primary axes** that are consistently tested in KTU examinations:

### 2.1.1 Axis 1 — Structure (Geometric Regularity)

| Type | Description | Example | Planning Complexity |
|------|-------------|---------|---------------------|
| **Structured** | Geometrically regular, predictable layout | Factory floors, hospital corridors | Low |
| **Semi-structured** | Partially known, partially unknown | Office buildings, urban sidewalks | Medium |
| **Unstructured** | Highly irregular, no prior model | Forests, disaster zones, planetary terrain | High |

### 2.1.2 Axis 2 — Dynamism (Temporal Variability)

- **Static Environment** $\mathcal{D} = \emptyset$: No moving objects; map remains valid permanently.
- **Quasi-static Environment**: Slow changes (e.g., parked cars, furniture rearrangement).
- **Dynamic Environment**: Fast-moving agents; map must be re-estimated continuously.

### 2.1.3 Axis 3 — Knowledge Availability (Information State)

- **Known Environment** $\rightarrow$ Robot has the complete a-priori map.
- **Partially Known** $\rightarrow$ Some prior map, but with unknown/unexplored regions.
- **Unknown Environment** $\rightarrow$ No prior map; exploration and mapping happen simultaneously.

### 2.1.4 Axis 4 — Media / Terrain

- **Aerial** (air, vacuum, water surface) — 3D free space.
- **Terrestrial** (ground) — 2.5D, often constrained to a plane.
- **Aquatic / Underwater** — fluid dynamics, acoustic sensing.
- **Space** — micro-gravity, GPS-denied, harsh radiation.

## 2.2 Mathematical Representation of the Workspace

A robot workspace is partitioned into three mutually exclusive subsets:

$$\mathcal{W} = \mathcal{W}_{\text{free}} \cup \mathcal{W}_{\text{occupied}} \cup \mathcal{W}_{\text{unknown}}$$

with the constraint:

$$\mathcal{W}_{\text{free}} \cap \mathcal{W}_{\text{occupied}} = \emptyset$$

The **configuration space** (C-space) $\mathcal{C}$ is a topologically related space where every point represents a unique pose $(x, y, \theta)$ of the robot:

$$\mathcal{C} = SE(2) \quad \text{or} \quad SE(3)$$

depending on whether the robot operates in 2D or 3D.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The transformation from workspace $\mathcal{W}$ to configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ is called the **Minkowski sum operation**, expressed as:
>
> $$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus \mathcal{R}$$
>
> where $\mathcal{R}$ is the robot's body footprint.

## 2.3 Sensor Model & Environmental Observability

The robot's sensor can observe the environment only within a bounded region $\mathcal{S}(p, t)$ centered at the robot's current pose $p$ and time $t$:

$$\mathcal{S}(p, t) = \{ q \in \mathcal{W} \mid d(p, q) \leq r_{\max} \;\wedge\; \text{LoS}(p, q) = \text{clear} \}$$

where $d(p, q)$ is the Euclidean distance and LoS is the line-of-sight condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In warehouse robotics (Amazon, Flipkart sortation hubs in Kerala), the environment is *known, structured, and dynamic* due to human pickers — making probabilistic planning essential.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Units | Application |
|---------|-----------------|-------|-------------|
| Workspace Partition | $\mathcal{W} = \mathcal{W}_{\text{free}} \cup \mathcal{W}_{\text{occupied}} \cup \mathcal{W}_{\text{unknown}}$ | Set notation | Map classification |
| Configuration Space | $\mathcal{C} = SE(n)$ | Manifold | Pose representation |
| C-obstacle Inflation | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus \mathcal{R}$ | Minkowski sum | Path planning |
| Sensor Field of View | $r_{\max}$ | meters (m) | LiDAR, camera range |
| Grid Resolution | $\Delta = L \mid N$ | meters/cell | Occupancy grids |
| Map Update Rule | $l_{t} = l_{t-1} + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(z_t)$ — log-odds | dimensionless | Occupancy Grid Mapping |
| Environment Scale | $L$ (m) | meters | Indoor (1–100) vs Outdoor (100–$10^6$) |
| Dynamic Obstacle Velocity | $v_{\text{dyn}} = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ | m/s | Tracking, prediction |
| Robot DOF | $n \in \{2, 3, 6\}$ | count | Holonomic vs non-holonomic |
| Time-to-Collision | $TTC = \frac{d}{v_{\text{rel}}}$ | seconds (s) | Dynamic obstacle avoidance |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse $\mathcal{W}$ (workspace) with $\mathcal{C}$ (configuration space). Remember: $\mathcal{W}$ is in *metric coordinates* $(x, y, z)$, while $\mathcal{C}$ is in *pose coordinates* $(x, y, z, \theta, \phi, \psi)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Occupancy Grid Mapping Update Rule

The **Occupancy Grid Map** (Moravec & Elfes, 1985) is the most widely taught environment representation in KTU-style syllabi. We derive its update equation step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define the binary random variable for each grid cell.**

Let $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$ represent the occupancy of cell $i$:

$$m_i = 1 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{cell } i \text{ is occupied}$$

$$m_i = 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{cell } i \text{ is free}$$

**Step 2 — Express the full map as a vector of cell states.**

$$m = \{m_1, m_2, m_3, \ldots, m_N\}$$

**Step 3 — Use Bayes' rule to estimate posterior occupancy given sensor measurement $z_{1:t}$ and robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$.**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 4 — Take the log-odds form for numerical stability (this is the standard KTU derivation).**

Define the **log-odds** representation:

$$l_i = \log \frac{p(m_i = 1)}{p(m_i = 0)} = \log \frac{p(m_i = 1)}{1 - p(m_i = 1)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply log to Bayes' rule to convert multiplicative update into additive update.**

$$\begin{aligned}
l_{i,t} &= \log \frac{p(m_i = 1 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{p(m_i = 0 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} \\[4pt]
&= \log \frac{p(m_i = 1 \mid z_{t}, x_{t})}{p(m_i = 0 \mid z_{t}, x_{t})} + \log \frac{p(m_i = 1 \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(m_i = 0 \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})} \\[4pt]
&= \underbrace{\log \frac{p(m_i = 1 \mid z_{t}, x_{t})}{p(m_i = 0 \mid z_{t}, x_{t})}}_{\text{inverse sensor model}} + l_{i,t-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Recover the probability from log-odds when needed.**

$$p(m_i = 1) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l_i}}$$

This additive log-odds form is computationally efficient and is the *de facto* standard in ROS (Robot Operating System) implementations worldwide.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Configuration Space Obstacle Inflation (Minkowski Sum)

**Step 1 — Define the robot as a 2D rigid body $\mathcal{R}$.**

For a circular robot of radius $r$, $\mathcal{R}$ is a disc:

$$\mathcal{R} = \{ (a, b) \mid a^2 + b^2 \leq r^2 \}$$

**Step 2 — Define a workspace obstacle $\mathcal{O} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$.**

**Step 3 — Define the Minkowski sum.**

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ c \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \exists r \in \mathcal{R} \text{ such that } c = o + r, \; o \in \mathcal{O} \}$$

**Step 4 — For a circular robot, this simplifies geometrically to a morphological dilation of the obstacle by $r$.**

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus \mathcal{B}(0, r)$$

**Step 5 — Therefore, the free C-space becomes:**

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$$

This is the foundation of all sampling-based planners (RRT, PRM) taught later in your KTU course.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Environment Type vs. Suitable Algorithm

| Environment Type | Representation | Recommended Algorithm | Computational Cost |
|------------------|---------------|-----------------------|-------------------|
| Static, Known, 2D | Geometric / Topological | A*, Dijkstra | $O(N \log N)$ |
| Static, Unknown, 2D | Occupancy Grid | Frontier-based Exploration | $O(N)$ per cell update |
| Dynamic, Known, 2D | Grid + Velocity Obstacles | DWA, VO | $O(K \cdot N)$ |
| Static, Known, 3D | Octree / Point cloud | RRT*, A* on voxel graph | $O(N \log N)$ |
| Dynamic, Unknown, 3D | TSDF / Voxel hashing | Dynamic SLAM (DynaSLAM) | $O(N^2)$ worst case |

where $N$ is the number of map cells / nodes and $K$ is the number of dynamic obstacles.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Target Environments

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Target Environment E] --> B[Structure Axis]
    A --> C[Dynamism Axis]
    A --> D[Knowledge Axis]
    A --> E[Media Axis]
    B --> B1[Structured]
    B --> B2[Semi-structured]
    B --> B3[Unstructured]
    C --> C1[Static]
    C --> C2[Quasi-static]
    C --> C3[Dynamic]
    D --> D1[Known]
    D --> D2[Partially Known]
    D --> D3[Unknown]
    E --> E1[Aerial]
    E --> E2[Terrestrial]
    E --> E3[Aquatic]
    E --> E4[Space]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Perception-to-Planning Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    TE[Target Environment E] --> SEN[Sensor Array: LiDAR, Camera, IMU, GPS]
    SEN --> PRC[Preprocessing: Filtering, Calibration]
    PRC --> LOC[Localization Module: Pose Estimation]
    PRC --> MAP[Mapping Module: Grid or Feature Map]
    LOC --> PLAN[Path Planner: A*, RRT*, DWA]
    MAP --> PLAN
    PLAN --> CTL[Motion Controller: PID, MPC]
    CTL --> ROB[Mobile Robot Actuators]
    ROB -->|Feedback| TE
    LOC -->|Feedback| MAP
```

## 4.3 Data Flow: Occupancy Grid Update Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Sensor Reading z_t] --> S2[Inverse Sensor Model]
    S2 --> S3[Log-odds Update l_t = l_t-1 + inverse_model]
    S3 --> S4[Cell Probability p_m1 = 1 - 1 over 1 + e^l]
    S4 --> S5[Updated Occupancy Grid Map m_t]
    S5 --> S6[Path Planner Input]
    S6 --> S7[Motion Command]
    S7 --> S8[Robot Action in Environment E]
    S8 --> S1
```

## 4.4 Environment Representation Trade-off Matrix

```mermaid
graph TB
    REP[Environment Representation] --> R1[Metric: Grid or Point Cloud]
    REP --> R2[Topological: Graph of Landmarks]
    REP --> R3[Semantic: Object-labeled Map]
    R1 --> R1A[High memory cost]
    R1 --> R1B[Accurate localization]
    R2 --> R2A[Compact storage]
    R2 --> R2B[Path planning friendly]
    R3 --> R3A[Human-interpretable]
    R3 --> R3B[Requires AI/ML inference]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Target Environment" in the context of mobile robot navigation. List any four characteristics used to classify it.*

**Model Answer:**
The Target Environment $\mathcal{E}$ is the bounded physical and logical space in which a mobile robot perceives, plans, and acts. It is the universe of discourse for all navigation algorithms.

Four classification characteristics:
1. **Structure:** structured, semi-structured, unstructured.
2. **Dynamism:** static, quasi-static, dynamic.
3. **Knowledge availability:** known, partially known, unknown.
4. **Operational media:** aerial, terrestrial, aquatic, space.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ and Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$. Why is the conversion necessary in path planning?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ | Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$ |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Coordinates | Metric $(x, y, z)$ | Pose $(x, y, \theta, \phi, \psi)$ |
| Robot shape | Explicit | Reduced to a point |
| Obstacles | Physical geometry | Inflated (Minkowski sum) |
| Dimension | 2D or 3D | $n$-DOF manifold $SE(2)$ or $SE(3)$ |

Conversion to $\mathcal{C}$ is necessary because it **reduces the robot to a point**, simplifying collision checking and enabling the use of standard graph-search algorithms like A*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Full-Length KTU ESE Pattern*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four-axis classification of Target Environments with suitable engineering examples. For each axis, state the planning complexity it introduces.

**b) [7 Marks]** Derive the log-odds update rule for an Occupancy Grid Map starting from Bayes' rule. State two advantages of the log-odds form over the direct probability form.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four-axis classification of the Target Environment is summarized below:

**Axis 1 — Structure (Geometric Regularity):**
- *Structured*: predictable layout (factory floor with fixed conveyor geometry).
- *Semi-structured*: known topology with unknown clutter (office with random chairs).
- *Unstructured*: irregular natural terrain (forest, disaster rubble).
- *Planning impact*: structured environments allow **offline global planning**; unstructured environments require **online reactive planning**.

**Axis 2 — Dynamism (Temporal Variability):**
- *Static*: no moving objects (museum at night).
- *Quasi-static*: slow changes (warehouse during shift change).
- *Dynamic*: fast-moving agents (urban traffic, pedestrian mall).
- *Planning impact*: dynamic environments need **time-parameterized trajectories** and re-planning at rates $\geq$ **10 Hz**.

**Axis 3 — Knowledge Availability:**
- *Known*: complete a-priori map (indoor with stored floor plan).
- *Partially known*: some prior info (GPS-tagged road map with unmapped alleys).
- *Unknown*: no prior info (Mars rover at new landing site).
- *Planning impact*: unknown environments mandate **simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM)**.

**Axis 4 — Media / Terrain:**
- *Aerial*: 3D free space, GPS available.
- *Terrestrial*: 2.5D, often planar-constrained.
- *Aquatic*: fluid dynamics, GPS-denied underwater.
- *Space*: micro-gravity, extreme sensor constraints.
- *Planning impact*: dictates the choice of **locomotion mechanism, sensor suite, and state estimator**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Starting from Bayes' rule:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

Apply the same to the complementary hypothesis $m_i = 0$ and form the ratio:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{p(m_i = 1 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{p(m_i = 0 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} &= \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i = 1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i = 0, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i = 1 \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(m_i = 0 \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$l_{i,t} = \log \frac{p(m_i = 1 \mid z_t, x_t)}{p(m_i = 0 \mid z_t, x_t)} + l_{i,t-1}$$

where $l_{i,t}$ is the log-odds of cell $i$ at time $t$.

**Two advantages of log-odds form:**
1. **Computational efficiency:** Multiplicative Bayes updates become *additive* operations, reducing floating-point cost on embedded robot CPUs.
2. **Numerical stability:** Avoids underflow/overflow that occurs when $p(m_i)$ approaches **0** or **1** in raw probability form.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to justify *why* log is taken. Do not just write the final equation — **state that log converts the multiplicative Bayes update into a memory-efficient additive recurrence**, which is the actual engineering motivation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Full-Length Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Alternative]**

**a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the Perception-to-Planning pipeline for a mobile robot navigating an unknown dynamic environment. Identify at least three sensors and the data each contributes.

**b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Configuration Space and derive the Minkowski sum formulation for obstacle inflation. Why is this step essential before applying A* on a grid map?

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The Perception-to-Planning pipeline (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2) consists of the following blocks:

1. **Sensor Array Layer:** Combines heterogeneous sensors.
   - **LiDAR**: produces 2D/3D point clouds $\to$ obstacle geometry.
   - **Camera (RGB-D)**: produces colored point clouds + semantic labels.
   - **IMU**: produces linear acceleration and angular velocity $\to$ dead reckoning.
   - **GPS**: produces global position fix (outdoor only).
   - **Wheel Odometry**: produces incremental pose estimates.

2. **Preprocessing:** Time synchronization, coordinate frame calibration, noise filtering (e.g., Voxel grid downsampling, outlier rejection).

3. **Localization Module:** Fuses sensor data using an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Particle Filter** to estimate pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$.

4. **Mapping Module:** Maintains an **Occupancy Grid Map** updated via the log-odds rule.

5. **Path Planner:** Generates a collision-free path from start to goal using **A*** (global) and **DWA** (local).

6. **Controller:** Translates path into wheel velocities via **PID** or **MPC**.

7. **Robot Actuators:** Execute the motion command in the Target Environment $\mathcal{E}$, closing the loop.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Configuration Space Definition:**
$\mathcal{C}$ is the space of all possible robot configurations. For a planar mobile robot, $\mathcal{C} = SE(2)$, parameterized by $(x, y, \theta)$.

**Minkowski Sum Derivation:**

Consider a rigid-body robot $\mathcal{R}$ and a workspace obstacle $\mathcal{O}$. The robot at configuration $q$ occupies the set:

$$\mathcal{R}(q) = \{ q + r \mid r \in \mathcal{R} \}$$

The robot collides with $\mathcal{O}$ at $q$ if:

$$\mathcal{R}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset$$

Therefore, the **C-obstacle region** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} &= \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{R}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \} \\
&= \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \exists r \in \mathcal{R} : q + r \in \mathcal{O} \} \\
&= \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})
\end{aligned}$$

For a symmetric circular robot, $-\mathcal{R} = \mathcal{R}$, so $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus \mathcal{R}$.

**Why essential before A\*:**
- A* treats the robot as a **point mass**.
- Without inflation, A* would generate paths that graze obstacle boundaries, causing **physical collision** in $\mathcal{W}$.
- Inflation pre-computes a **buffered obstacle map** in $\mathcal{C}$ that guarantees collision-free paths in $\mathcal{W}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students often draw the inflated obstacle as a *uniform rectangular halo*. For a circular robot, the inflation is a **true Euclidean dilation** (Minkowski sum with a disc), not a Manhattan-distance offset.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Target Environment**

- Target Environment $\mathcal{E} = (\mathcal{W}, \mathcal{O}, \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{T})$ is the **complete operational universe** for a robot.
- Classification follows **four axes**: Structure, Dynamism, Knowledge, Media.
- Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ is partitioned into **free, occupied, unknown** subsets (mutually exclusive).
- Configuration Space $\mathcal{C} = SE(2)$ (planar) or $SE(3)$ (3D); reduces the robot to a point.
- C-obstacle inflation uses the **Minkowski sum**: $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus \mathcal{R}$.
- Occupancy Grid uses the **log-odds update**: $l_{i,t} = l_{i,t-1} + \text{inv\_model}(z_t, x_t)$.
- Probability recovery: $p(m_i = 1) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l_i}}$.
- Sensor field $\mathcal{S}(p, t)$ is **bounded by $r_{\max}$** and requires **line-of-sight**.
- Time-to-Collision formula: $TTC = \frac{d}{v_{\text{rel}}}$.
- The Perception-to-Planning loop is: **Sensor $\to$ Localization + Mapping $\to$ Planning $\to$ Control $\to$ Action**.
- Dynamic environments need **re-planning at $\geq 10$ Hz**.
- Unknown environments require **SLAM**, not just path planning.
- KTU board exams test: **definitions, classification tables, log-odds derivation, and C-space transformation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks "explain the environment," always structure your answer around the **four axes** *and* the **mathematical representation** — this guarantees full marks per the KTU 2024 marking scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environment Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environment Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environment Representation** is the formal computational encoding of the robot's surrounding physical workspace into a structured data structure that enables a robot to *localize* itself, *plan* collision-free paths, and *reason* about its operational domain. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this forms the foundational layer of any autonomous navigation stack and precedes localization, mapping, and motion planning modules.

In mobile robotics, the environment (or *workspace*) is denoted as $\mathcal{W} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ for planar robots or $\mathcal{W} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^3$ for aerial or underwater platforms. The robot's configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ is derived from the workspace through transformations that account for the robot's geometry and kinematic constraints.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU-Aligned)

| Term | Formal Meaning | Operational Role |
|------|---------------|------------------|
| **Workspace** ($\mathcal{W}$) | The physical 2D/3D volume where the robot operates | Real-world operational boundary |
| **Free Space** ($\mathcal{W}_{free}$) | Subset of $\mathcal{W}$ with no obstacles | Valid region for path planning |
| **Obstacle Space** ($\mathcal{W}_{obs}$) | Subset of $\mathcal{W}$ occupied by static/dynamic obstacles | Forbidden region for the robot |
| **Configuration Space** ($\mathcal{C}$) | Complete set of robot configurations $\mathbf{q}$ | Search domain for planners |
| **Map** ($M$) | Abstracted computational model of $\mathcal{W}$ | Input to localization & planning |
| **Landmark** ($l_i$) | A distinguishable feature in $\mathcal{W}$ | Anchor for localization & SLAM |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Tourist's City Map**
> Imagine you are a tourist arriving in a new city. Before you can navigate, you need a *map*. But maps come in different forms:
> - A **paper street map** = *Geometric/Metric Map* (precise distances and coordinates).
> - A **subway diagram** = *Topological Map* (only stations and connecting lines, no distances).
> - A **grid of squares painted on the city** = *Occupancy Grid Map* (each square is "free" or "blocked").
> - A **list of famous landmarks** = *Landmark-Based Map* (just notable features with no grid).
>
> Similarly, a robot must choose *one* (or *hybrid*) representation depending on the task, sensor suite, and computational budget.

### 1.4 Primary Categories of Environment Representation

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the following four principal classes:

1. **Metric (Grid) Representations** — Discrete discretization of $\mathcal{W}$.
2. **Geometric Representations** — Continuous features (lines, polygons, points).
3. **Topological Representations** — Graph of places and connections.
4. **Semantic Representations** — Object-labeled maps with meaning.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the *comparative trade-offs* between metric and topological maps. Always be ready to discuss memory, accuracy, computational cost, and suitability for different environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification with Mathematical Foundation

#### A. Occupancy Grid Maps (Metric — Discrete)

Introduced by **Moravec and Elfes (1985)**, an occupancy grid divides $\mathcal{W}$ into $n \times m$ uniform cells. Each cell $c_{ij}$ stores the probability that the cell is occupied:

$$p(c_{ij}) = P(\text{occupied} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

where $z_{1:t}$ represents the sensor history and $x_{1:t}$ the robot pose history.

- **Cell value range:** $0$ (definitely free) to $1$ (definitely occupied).
- **Threshold:** Typically $0.5$ is the decision boundary.
- **Resolution trade-off:** Higher resolution $\Rightarrow$ higher memory but better accuracy.

#### B. Geometric Maps (Metric — Continuous)

The environment is modeled using geometric primitives:

$$\mathcal{W} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{K} \mathcal{P}_k, \quad \mathcal{P}_k \in \{\text{lines, circles, polygons, points}\}$$

- **Line maps** — suitable for corridor-like indoor spaces.
- **Polygon maps** — efficient representation of obstacle boundaries.
- **Point maps** — sparse landmark clouds (used in Visual SLAM).

#### C. Topological Maps (Non-Metric — Relational)

The environment is represented as a graph:

$$G = (V, E)$$

where:
- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ — nodes represent distinctive places (rooms, junctions).
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ — edges represent traversable connections.

- **Advantage:** Compact, scalable, fast planning via graph search (Dijkstra, A*).
- **Disadvantage:** Limited precision; difficult place recognition.

#### D. Landmark-Based Maps

A set of distinguishable features $\mathcal{L} = \{l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_m\}$ with associated poses. Often used in EKF-SLAM and visual SLAM:

$$l_i = (x_i, y_i, \theta_i) \in \mathbb{R}^3$$

#### E. Semantic Maps

Augment spatial maps with object class labels:

$$m_{sem} = \{(x_i, y_i, \text{class}_i) \mid i = 1, \ldots, m\}$$

Example: chair, door, wall — often produced by deep neural networks (YOLO, Mask R-CNN).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application |
|---|------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Workspace decomposition | $\mathcal{W} = \mathcal{W}_{free} \cup \mathcal{W}_{obs}$ | Path planning feasibility |
| 2 | Cell count for grid | $N = \lceil L/\Delta r \rceil \cdot \lceil W/\Delta r \rceil$ | Memory estimation |
| 3 | Memory for binary grid | $M = N \cdot 1\,\text{bit} = N / 8\,\text{bytes}$ | Storage calculation |
| 4 | Memory for probabilistic grid | $M = N \cdot 8\,\text{bytes}$ (float64) | Storage calculation |
| 5 | Log-Odds update | $l(c_{ij}) = l(c_{ij}) + l_{occ} - l_{free}$ | Bayes update in grid |
| 6 | Log-Odds to probability | $p = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l)}$ | Map update |
| 7 | Configuration space (circle robot) | $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{x \in \mathcal{W} \mid \text{dist}(x, \mathcal{W}_{obs}) \leq r\}$ | Path planning |
| 8 | Voronoi graph | $V(p) = \arg\min_{p_i \in \mathcal{W}_{obs}} \vert \vert p - p_i \vert \vert$ | Roadmap construction |
| 9 | Voronoi edges | $e = \{p \mid d(p, p_i) = d(p, p_j)\}$ | Maximum clearance path |
| 10 | Topological graph | $G = (V, E)$, $\vert V \vert = n$, $\vert E \vert = m$ | High-level navigation |
| 11 | Landmark position | $l_i = (x_i, y_i, \theta_i)$ | SLAM state vector |
| 12 | Path length (grid) | $L_{path} = \sum_{k=1}^{K-1} \vert \vert p_{k+1} - p_k \vert \vert$ | Performance metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Valuation:** When writing absolute value $\vert x \vert$ inside a table cell, always use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX. Never use the bare pipe `|` symbol — it will break the markdown table renderer.

### 2.3 Comparative Trade-Off Analysis (KTU Favourite)

| Representation Type | Memory | Accuracy | Computational Cost | Best Use Case |
|--------------------|--------|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Occupancy Grid | **High** | Medium | Medium | Indoor, static env. |
| Geometric (Polygons) | Low | High | Low | Structured indoor |
| Topological | **Very Low** | Low | Low | Large-scale outdoor |
| Landmark-based | Medium | High | High | SLAM, visual nav. |
| Semantic | High | High | **Very High** | Human–robot interaction |
| Voronoi | Low | Medium | Medium | Clearance-based planning |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva):** Use grid + topological hybrid maps.
- **Autonomous cars (Waymo, Tesla):** HD semantic + geometric maps.
- **Mars rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Sparse landmark + topological maps due to bandwidth limits.
- **Drones (DJI):** 3D occupancy voxel grids for obstacle avoidance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cell Count and Memory for an Occupancy Grid

**Problem Statement:** A rectangular room of dimensions $L \times W$ is discretized into a grid of resolution $\Delta r$ (meters per cell). Compute:
1. The total number of cells $N$.
2. The memory required for (a) a binary grid and (b) a probabilistic (float64) grid.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_x &= \left\lceil \frac{L}{\Delta r} \right\rceil \quad \text{(number of cells along length)} \\[4pt]
N_y &= \left\lceil \frac{W}{\Delta r} \right\rceil \quad \text{(number of cells along width)} \\[4pt]
N &= N_x \cdot N_y = \left\lceil \frac{L}{\Delta r} \right\rceil \cdot \left\lceil \frac{W}{\Delta r} \right\rceil \quad \text{(total cells)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

- **Row 1:** The length $L$ is divided into cells of side $\Delta r$. The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ ensures complete coverage.
- **Row 2:** Similarly, the width $W$ is divided.
- **Row 3:** The total number of cells is the product of row and column counts.

**Memory for a binary grid (1 bit per cell):**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{binary} &= N \cdot 1\,\text{bit} = \frac{N}{8}\,\text{bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Memory for a probabilistic grid (float64, 8 bytes per cell):**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{prob} &= N \cdot 8\,\text{bytes} = 8N\,\text{bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Let $L = 10\,\text{m}$, $W = 8\,\text{m}$, $\Delta r = 0.1\,\text{m}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_x &= \left\lceil \frac{10}{0.1} \right\rceil = 100 \\[4pt]
N_y &= \left\lceil \frac{8}{0.1} \right\rceil = 80 \\[4pt]
N &= 100 \cdot 80 = 8000\,\text{cells} \\[4pt]
M_{binary} &= 8000 / 8 = 1000\,\text{bytes} = 1\,\text{kB} \\[4pt]
M_{prob} &= 8 \cdot 8000 = 64000\,\text{bytes} = 64\,\text{kB}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Configuration Space Inflation for a Circular Robot

**Problem Statement:** Given a circular robot of radius $r$ and an obstacle region $\mathcal{W}_{obs}$, derive the configuration space obstacle $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$.

**Derivation:**

A circular robot of radius $r$ at configuration $q = (x, y)$ collides with an obstacle $obs \in \mathcal{W}_{obs}$ if and only if the Euclidean distance between the robot's center and the obstacle is less than or equal to $r$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Collision condition:} \quad & \min_{p \in obs} \sqrt{(x - p_x)^2 + (y - p_y)^2} \leq r \\[4pt]
\text{Equivalently:} \quad & d((x,y), obs) \leq r \\[4pt]
\text{Therefore:} \quad & \mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{ (x, y) \in \mathcal{W} \mid d((x,y), \mathcal{W}_{obs}) \leq r \}
\end{aligned}$$

**Geometric interpretation:** $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ is the Minkowski sum of the obstacle and a disk of radius $r$:

$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \mathcal{W}_{obs} \oplus \mathcal{B}(0, r)$$

where $\oplus$ denotes the Minkowski sum operator and $\mathcal{B}(0, r)$ is a ball of radius $r$ centered at origin.

**Step-by-step explanation:**

- **Line 1:** The robot collides if any point of the robot's body (a disk of radius $r$) intersects an obstacle. The minimum distance from the center to the obstacle must be $\leq r$.
- **Line 2:** This is a compact restatement using the distance function.
- **Line 3:** The set of all such configurations is $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$.
- **Minkowski sum:** Shrinking the robot to a point and growing each obstacle by $r$ yields an equivalent point-robot problem.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Log-Odds Bayesian Update for Occupancy Grid

**Problem Statement:** Derive the recursive log-odds update rule used in occupancy grid mapping.

**Derivation:**

Let $p(c)$ denote the probability that a cell $c$ is occupied. Define the **odds** as:

$$O(c) = \frac{p(c)}{1 - p(c)}$$

and the **log-odds** as:

$$l(c) = \log O(c) = \log \frac{p(c)}{1 - p(c)}$$

Applying Bayes' rule for an independent sensor observation $z_t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
p(c \mid z_{1:t}) &= \frac{p(z_t \mid c) \cdot p(c \mid z_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}
\end{aligned}$$

Now, the log-odds ratio update:

$$\begin{aligned}
l(c \mid z_{1:t}) &= \log \frac{p(c \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(c \mid z_{1:t})} \\[4pt]
&= l(c \mid z_{1:t-1}) + \log \frac{p(c \mid z_t)}{1 - p(c \mid z_t)} - \log \frac{p(c)}{1 - p(c)} \\[4pt]
&= l(c \mid z_{1:t-1}) + \Delta l_{obs}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\Delta l_{obs} = \log \frac{p(c \mid z_t)}{1 - p(c \mid z_t)} - l_0$ is the incremental log-odds update.

**Final recursive form:**

$$l_t(c) = l_{t-1}(c) + l_{occ} - l_{free}$$

where $l_{occ} = \log \frac{p_{occ}}{1 - p_{occ}}$ and $l_{free} = \log \frac{p_{free}}{1 - p_{free}}$ are constants.

**Recovering the probability:**

$$p(c) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l(c))}$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

- **Line 1:** The log-odds is a compact monotonic transformation of probability.
- **Line 2:** Bayes' rule expresses the posterior in terms of likelihood and prior.
- **Line 3:** A standard algebraic manipulation yields the additive update — this is the *key advantage* of log-odds: addition instead of multiplication.
- **Line 4:** Two constants $l_{occ}$ and $l_{free}$ are predefined for "occupied" and "free" sensor returns, making updates O(1) per cell.
- **Final formula:** The inverse transform recovers the probability.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Voronoi Diagram as a Roadmap

**Problem Statement:** Show that the Generalized Voronoi Diagram (GVD) provides maximum clearance paths.

**Derivation:**

The GVD is the set of all points equidistant to two or more obstacles:

$$\begin{aligned}
GVD &= \left\{ p \in \mathcal{W}_{free} \,\middle|\, \exists\, p_i, p_j \in \mathcal{W}_{obs},\; \vert \vert p - p_i \vert \vert = \vert \vert p - p_j \vert \vert \leq \vert \vert p - p_k \vert \vert\;\forall k \neq i,j \right\}
\end{aligned}$$

The clearance at point $p$ is:

$$C(p) = \min_{p_i \in \mathcal{W}_{obs}} \vert \vert p - p_i \vert \vert$$

On the GVD, by definition, this distance is locally maximized (equidistant to the two nearest obstacles), giving maximum local clearance.

**Path planning on the GVD:** The robot can follow edges of the GVD from start to goal, guaranteeing collision-free navigation with maximum safety margin. The GVD is computed in $O(n \log n)$ time, where $n$ is the number of obstacle sites (using Fortune's algorithm).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Representation

| Environment Type | Best Representation | Why? |
|-----------------|--------------------|------|
| Indoor office (structured walls) | Geometric polygon | Low memory, high accuracy |
| Outdoor unstructured (forest) | Topological / landmark | Robust to clutter |
| Dynamic warehouse | Occupancy grid (updated) | Real-time obstacle response |
| Mars surface (sparse features) | Landmark-based + topological | Bandwidth-efficient |
| Self-driving highway | HD semantic + grid | Multi-layer decision making |
| Underwater (low visibility) | Topological + sonar grid | Sensor-suited |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Environment Representations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ENVIRONMENT REPRESENTATION"] --> B["METRIC"]
    A --> C["NON METRIC"]

    B --> B1["OCCUPANCY GRID"]
    B --> B2["GEOMETRIC"]
    B --> B3["VORONOI"]
    B --> B4["VOXEL GRID 3D"]

    C --> C1["TOPOLOGICAL GRAPH"]
    C --> C2["LANDMARK BASED"]
    C --> C3["SEMANTIC MAP"]
    C --> C4["HYBRID"]

    B1 --> B1a["2D CELL BASED"]
    B1 --> B1b["PROBABILISTIC"]
    B1 --> B1c["BINARY"]

    B2 --> B2a["LINE SEGMENTS"]
    B2 --> B2b["POLYGONS"]
    B2 --> B2c["POINT CLOUDS"]

    C1 --> C1a["NODES PLACES"]
    C1 --> C1b["EDGES PATHS"]
```

### 4.2 Occupancy Grid Mapping Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["SENSORS LIDAR CAMERA SONAR"] --> F1["PRE PROCESSING NOISE FILTER"]
    F1 --> F2["INVERSE SENSOR MODEL"]
    F2 --> F3["LOG ODDS BAYES UPDATE"]
    F3 --> F4["OCCUPANCY GRID MAP"]
    F4 --> F5["PATH PLANNER A STAR"]
    F4 --> F6["LOCALIZATION MODULE"]
    F5 --> M1["MOTOR COMMANDS"]
    F6 --> M1
    M1 --> A1["ROBOT MOTION"]
    A1 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Topological Map Construction Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["RAW METRIC MAP"] --> P2["PLACE DETECTION JUNCTIONS ROOMS"]
    P2 --> P3["NODE CREATION V1 V2 V3"]
    P3 --> P4["EDGE INFERENCE TRAVERSABLE LINKS"]
    P4 --> P5["TOPOLOGICAL GRAPH G V E"]
    P5 --> P6["GRAPH SEARCH DIJKSTRA A STAR"]
    P6 --> P7["PATH COMMANDS"]
```

### 4.4 Configuration Space Inflation Diagram (Schematic Concept)

```mermaid
graph LR
    W["WORKSPACE W"] --> W1["OBSTACLE obs"]
    W --> W2["ROBOT DISK r"]
    W1 --> I["MINKOWSKI SUM"]
    W2 --> I
    I --> COBS["C OBSTACLE INFLATED REGION"]
    W --> CFREE["C FREE COLLISION FREE"]
    COBS --> P["PATH PLANNING IN C"]
    CFREE --> P
```

### 4.5 Hybrid Map Integration (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["LAYER 1 METRIC GRID LOCAL VIEW"] --> H["HYBRID FUSION MODULE"]
    L2["LAYER 2 TOPOLOGICAL GLOBAL VIEW"] --> H
    L3["LAYER 3 SEMANTIC OBJECTS"] --> H
    H --> O["UNIFIED REPRESENTATION"]
    O --> APPS["APPLICATIONS NAV SLAM HRI"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip (KTU Exam):** When asked to draw an environment representation, ensure your Mermaid diagrams have:
> - Distinct start/end nodes (no isolated nodes).
> - Clear directional flow with `--> `.
> - Uppercase text inside quoted labels to avoid formatting conflicts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Environment Representation in the context of mobile robot navigation. List any four major types with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> *Environment Representation is the process of encoding the robot's workspace into a computational data structure (e.g., grid, graph, geometric primitives) that supports localization, mapping, and path planning.*

Four major types:
1. **Occupancy Grid** — Discrete cell-based probabilistic map of free/occupied space.
2. **Geometric Map** — Continuous representation using lines, polygons, or points.
3. **Topological Map** — Graph of places (nodes) and connections (edges) with no metric information.
4. **Semantic Map** — Object-labeled map (chair, door, etc.) for high-level reasoning.

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for list with descriptions.)*

---
