#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Compare metric and topological maps based on memory, accuracy, and computational cost.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Metric Map | Topological Map |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Memory** | High (large grids) | Very Low (sparse graph) |
| **Accuracy** | High (precise positions) | Low (no exact coordinates) |
| **Computational Cost** | Higher (per-cell updates) | Low (graph traversal) |

*(Distribution: 1 mark per row, presented in table form.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**a)** Explain the Occupancy Grid Map in detail. Derive the log-odds update rule and discuss its advantages over direct probabilistic updates. **(7 marks)**

**b)** For a rectangular room of size $12\,\text{m} \times 9\,\text{m}$ mapped using an occupancy grid of resolution $0.2\,\text{m}$, calculate: (i) the total number of cells, (ii) memory required for a binary grid, and (iii) memory required for a probabilistic float64 grid. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Occupancy Grid Map + Log-Odds Derivation (7 marks):**

An **occupancy grid map** discretizes the workspace $\mathcal{W}$ into a 2D array of cells. Each cell $c_{ij}$ holds a probability $p(c_{ij}) \in [0, 1]$ of being occupied, updated recursively as the robot moves and senses.

**Direct Bayesian update:**

$$p(c \mid z_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid c) \cdot p(c \mid z_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}$$

**Problem with direct update:** Requires computing the denominator and floating-point multiplications at every cell — computationally expensive and prone to numerical underflow for long trajectories.

**Log-odds transformation (derivation):**

Let $l(c) = \log \frac{p(c)}{1 - p(c)}$ be the log-odds.

From Bayes' rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{p(c \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(c \mid z_{1:t})} &= \frac{p(z_t \mid c) \cdot p(c \mid z_{1:t-1}) / p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - [\text{same}]} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{p(c \mid z_t)}{1 - p(c \mid z_t)} \cdot \frac{1 - p(c)}{p(c)} \cdot \frac{p(c \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(c \mid z_{1:t-1})}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking log on both sides:

$$l(c \mid z_{1:t}) = l(c \mid z_{1:t-1}) + l(c \mid z_t) - l(c)$$

**Compact recursive form:**

$$l_t(c) = l_{t-1}(c) + l_{occ} - l_{free}$$

where $l_{occ}$ and $l_{free}$ are constant log-odds for "occupied" and "free" sensor returns.

**Probability recovery:**

$$p(c) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l(c))}$$

**Advantages of log-odds update:**
1. **Computational efficiency:** Addition instead of multiplication — $O(1)$ per cell.
2. **Numerical stability:** No floating-point underflow.
3. **Easy clamping:** Values can be bounded to prevent overconfidence.
4. **Reversibility:** Probability can always be recovered via sigmoid.

*(Distribution: 2 marks concept + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks advantages.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $L = 12\,\text{m}$, $W = 9\,\text{m}$, $\Delta r = 0.2\,\text{m}$.

**(i) Total number of cells:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_x &= \lceil 12 / 0.2 \rceil = 60 \\[4pt]
N_y &= \lceil 9 / 0.2 \rceil = 45 \\[4pt]
N &= N_x \cdot N_y = 60 \cdot 45 = 2700\,\text{cells}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Memory for binary grid (1 bit per cell):**

$$M_{binary} = 2700\,\text{bits} = 2700 / 8 = 337.5\,\text{bytes} \approx 338\,\text{bytes}$$

**(iii) Memory for probabilistic float64 grid (8 bytes per cell):**

$$M_{prob} = 8 \cdot 2700 = 21600\,\text{bytes} \approx 21.09\,\text{kB}$$

*(Distribution: 2 + 2 + 3 marks across the three sub-parts.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the four major types of environment representation in mobile robotics. Discuss their relative merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A circular mobile robot of radius $r = 0.3\,\text{m}$ operates in a workspace containing a square obstacle of side $1\,\text{m}$ placed at the origin. Derive the configuration space obstacle and explain the concept of Minkowski sum. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Major Environment Representations (7 marks):**

1. **Occupancy Grid Map (Metric):** Discretizes the workspace into uniform cells, each storing a probability of occupancy. Best for indoor, structured environments. *Merits:* easy to update, supports probabilistic reasoning. *Demerits:* high memory at fine resolution.

2. **Geometric Map (Metric):** Represents the environment using geometric primitives — points, lines, polygons. *Merits:* compact, accurate, suitable for structured corridors. *Demerits:* difficult to build from raw sensor data.

3. **Topological Map (Non-Metric):** A graph $G=(V, E)$ where nodes are distinct places and edges are navigable links. *Merits:* extremely memory-efficient, fast planning. *Demerits:* requires reliable place recognition; no metric precision.

4. **Semantic Map (Non-Metric + High-Level):** Augments spatial maps with object class labels (e.g., door, chair). *Merits:* enables human-like reasoning and natural HRI. *Demerits:* requires deep learning; computationally expensive.

*(Distribution: 1.5 marks per type, 1 mark for comparison table.)*

---

**(b) Configuration Space Obstacle (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Workspace Definition:**

$$\mathcal{W} = [-5, 5] \times [-5, 5]\,\text{m}^2, \quad \mathcal{W}_{obs} = [-0.5, 0.5] \times [-0.5, 0.5]$$

**Step 2 — Robot Geometry:**

The robot is a disk of radius $r = 0.3\,\text{m}$ centered at configuration $q = (x, y)$.

**Step 3 — Collision Condition:**

The robot collides with the obstacle if the distance from its center to the obstacle is $\leq r$:

$$d((x, y), \mathcal{W}_{obs}) \leq 0.3$$

**Step 4 — Configuration Space Obstacle:**

$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{ (x, y) \in \mathcal{W} \mid d((x,y), \mathcal{W}_{obs}) \leq 0.3 \}$$

**Step 5 — Minkowski Sum Formulation:**

$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \mathcal{W}_{obs} \oplus \mathcal{B}(0, 0.3)$$

where $\mathcal{B}(0, 0.3)$ is a disk of radius $0.3\,\text{m}$ centered at origin, and $\oplus$ is the Minkowski sum:

$$A \oplus B = \{ a + b \mid a \in A, b \in B \}$$

**Step 6 — Geometric Result:**

The configuration space obstacle is a square of side $1 + 2(0.3) = 1.6\,\text{m}$ centered at the origin:

$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = [-0.8, 0.8] \times [-0.8, 0.8]$$

**Concept of Minkowski Sum:** It transforms a robot-shape problem into a point-robot problem by "inflating" the obstacles by the robot's radius. The original robot of radius $r$ is shrunk to a point, and every obstacle is grown by $r$ in all directions. Path planning then reduces to finding a path for a point in $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$.

*(Distribution: 1 mark setup + 2 marks collision derivation + 2 marks Minkowski sum + 2 marks final result and explanation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing metric with topological maps** in definitions — examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in cell-count calculations.
> 3. **Using the bare pipe `|` in markdown tables** — only use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.
> 4. **Not specifying units** (m, m², bytes, kB) in numerical answers — KTU awards 0.5 mark deduction per missing unit.
> 5. **Mixing up configuration space and workspace** — $\mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{W}$ are NOT the same.
> 6. **Skipping the probability recovery formula** in log-odds derivations — the final inverse transform is mandatory.
> 7. **Drawing Voronoi diagrams without showing equidistance property** — the core property must be stated.
> 8. **Not labeling axes/units in Mermaid block diagrams** during descriptive answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Environment Representation (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Environment Representation** is the foundational layer — precedes localization, mapping, and planning.
- ✅ **Workspace** $\mathcal{W}$ decomposes into $\mathcal{W}_{free} \cup \mathcal{W}_{obs}$.
- ✅ **Four main types:** Metric (grid, geometric), Non-metric (topological, semantic).
- ✅ **Occupancy grid** cells store probabilities; updated via **log-odds** (addition, not multiplication).
- ✅ **Log-odds update:** $l_t(c) = l_{t-1}(c) + l_{occ} - l_{free}$.
- ✅ **Probability recovery:** $p = 1 - 1/(1 + \exp(l))$.
- ✅ **Cell count formula:** $N = \lceil L/\Delta r \rceil \cdot \lceil W/\Delta r \rceil$.
- ✅ **Memory:** binary grid → 1 bit/cell; probabilistic → 8 bytes/cell.
- ✅ **Geometric maps** use lines, polygons, points; low memory, high accuracy in structured env.
- ✅ **Topological map** = graph $G = (V, E)$; nodes = places, edges = connections.
- ✅ **Voronoi diagram** gives maximum clearance paths; $O(n \log n)$ construction.
- ✅ **Minkowski sum** converts robot-shape planning to point-robot planning.
- ✅ **Configuration space obstacle** for circle: $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{x \mid d(x, \mathcal{W}_{obs}) \leq r\}$.
- ✅ **Landmark maps** use $l_i = (x_i, y_i, \theta_i)$ for SLAM.
- ✅ **Semantic maps** add object class labels for high-level reasoning.
- ✅ **Hybrid maps** combine grid + topological + semantic layers.
- ✅ **Trade-off axis:** memory vs. accuracy vs. computational cost.
- ✅ **KTU favourite questions:** "Compare metric vs. topological", "Derive log-odds update", "Calculate grid memory".

---

**END OF NOTES — Environment Representation | OEBRT723 ROBOT NAVIGATION | KTU 2024 Scheme | Module 1**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Schematic for Mobile Robot Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Schematic for Mobile Robot Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobile Robot Localization** is the computational process by which a mobile robot determines its **pose** (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) within a reference coordinate frame, using **sensor measurements**, **motion commands (control inputs)**, and a **prior map** of the environment.

The **General Schematic for Mobile Robot Localization** is the standardized block-level functional architecture (signal-flow pipeline) that depicts the interaction between the **environment**, the **robot's sensors and actuators**, the **prediction module**, the **measurement update module**, and the **pose belief state**. It is the canonical template upon which all concrete localization algorithms (Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, Grid-based, EKF) are instantiated.

Mathematically, localization is the recursive estimation of the **belief distribution**:
$$bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t},\, u_{1:t})$$
where $x_t$ is the robot pose at time $t$, $z_{1:t}$ are the measurements, and $u_{1:t}$ are the control inputs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Lost Tourist" Analogy**
> Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into an unknown city (the **map**). You cannot see anything initially. As you walk (apply **control inputs** $u_t$), you form a rough idea of where you *might* be. When you finally remove the blindfold and see a landmark — say, the Eiffel Tower — you instantly **correct** your belief. Localization is exactly this: a continuous cycle of **(a) predicting** where you will be after a movement, and **(b) updating** that prediction using what your senses (cameras, LIDAR) actually see. The "general schematic" is the flowchart of this think-walk-observe-correct loop.

### 1.3 Why a "General" Schematic?

Different localization algorithms (Kalman, Particle, Grid) differ in **how** they represent the belief $bel(x_t)$, but **all** of them share the same two-stage Bayesian skeleton. The *general schematic* abstracts away the implementation details and exposes this shared skeleton — making it a universal teaching and design tool.

### 1.4 Classification of Localization Problems

| Problem Type | Description | Difficulty | Typical Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Initial pose is known; track small errors | Low | EKF / Kalman Filter |
| **Global Localization** | Initial pose is unknown | Medium | Particle Filter / Grid-based |
| **Kidnapped Robot Problem** | Robot is teleported mid-run; must recover | High | Particle Filter with random restart |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to *draw and label the general schematic*, *identify the function of each block*, and *relate each block to the Bayesian update equations*. Marks are frequently awarded for correctly identifying the **Prediction Module** and **Measurement Update Module** and naming their inputs/outputs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of the General Schematic

The schematic is fundamentally a **closed-loop Bayesian estimator** with two alternating stages executed every time step $t$:

#### Stage 1 — Prediction (Motion Update / Time Update)
* **Role:** Propagates the previous belief $bel(x_{t-1})$ through the **motion model** $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ to produce a *prior* belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$.
* **Why needed?** Because the robot has physically moved, and we must *predict* where it is now *before* seeing new sensor data.
* **Input(s):** Previous pose $x_{t-1}$, control input $u_t$ (e.g., wheel-encoder odometry, IMU).
* **Output:** A "smudged" belief — uncertainty *increases* because motion models are noisy.

#### Stage 2 — Measurement Update (Correction / Observation Update)
* **Role:** Uses the **sensor model** $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ to refine the predicted belief with actual sensor observation $z_t$, producing the *posterior* belief $bel(x_t)$.
* **Why needed?** Sensor data carries information about the true pose; multiplying it with the prior (Bayes' rule) reduces uncertainty.
* **Input(s):** Predicted belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$, current sensor measurement $z_t$, known map $m$.
* **Output:** Sharpened, corrected belief — uncertainty *decreases*.

### 2.2 Generic Block Identification (Top-Down View)

1. **Environment / Map ($m$)** — Static prior knowledge; provides landmarks, walls, beacons.
2. **Robot Platform** — Carries actuators and sensors; has internal state $x_t$.
3. **Sensors** — Two families:
    * **Proprioceptive** (encoders, gyros, IMU) → feed the *motion model*.
    * **Exteroceptive** (cameras, LIDAR, sonar, GPS) → feed the *measurement model*.
4. **Landmark Extraction / Feature Detection** — Converts raw sensor data into abstract features (corners, edges, beacons).
5. **Data Association** — Matches observed features to known map features; supplies correspondences to the update module.
6. **Prediction Module** — Implements $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$.
7. **Measurement Update Module** — Implements $P(z_t \mid x_t)$.
8. **Pose Belief / State Estimate** — The final output $bel(x_t)$: a distribution (Gaussian / grid / particle set).
9. **Feedback Loop** — $bel(x_t)$ is fed back as the prior for the next time step.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Variables | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Belief definition | $x_t, z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}$ | All algorithms |
| 2 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ | Prediction step | motion model, prev belief | KF, PF, Grid |
| 3 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Measurement update | $\eta$ = normalizer | All algorithms |
| 4 | $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ | Motion (odometry) model | pose, control | Prediction |
| 5 | $P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Sensor (observation) model | measurement, pose, map | Update |
| 6 | $\eta = \left[\int P(z_t \mid x_t)\overline{bel}(x_t)\,dx_t\right]^{-1}$ | Normalizer | — | Update |
| 7 | $\mu_t = \mu_{t-1} + K_t(z_t - H\mu_{t-1})$ | Kalman measurement eqn. | $K_t$ = Kalman gain | KF schematic |
| 8 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H)\Sigma_{t-1}$ | Kalman covariance update | $\Sigma$ = covariance | KF schematic |
| 9 | $w_t^{[i]} = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Particle weight | $i$ = particle index | PF schematic |
| 10 | $x_t^{[i]} \sim P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$ | Particle prediction | — | PF schematic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K-T-U Exam Tip:** Questions on this topic frequently ask *"Which block in the general schematic implements the motion model $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$?"* — Answer: **Prediction Module** (sometimes labelled *Motion Update* or *Time Update*). Likewise, the **Measurement Update Module** implements $P(z_t \mid x_t)$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** The schematic's loop runs at 50–200 Hz, fusing GPS, IMU, wheel odometry, and LIDAR to keep the car localized to within **10 cm** of a HD map.
* **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon, Kiva):** Particle-filter versions of the same schematic guide robots through aisles with **±5 cm** precision.
* **Indoor Service Robots:** When GPS fails indoors, the schematic falls back on WiFi/LIDAR-camera fusion through the same Prediction-Update loop.
* **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance, Mars 2020):** Visual-odometry prediction + landmark update is exactly the schematic in operation, allowing autonomous traverse of millions of meters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Schematic's Two Equations (from Bayes' Rule)

We want to compute the posterior over the current pose $x_t$ given the history of measurements $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$:

$$bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 1 — Condition on the most recent measurement $z_t$ and separate it from the past.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= P(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \\
&= \frac{P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the direct application of Bayes' theorem. The denominator does not depend on $x_t$, so it acts as a normalizer $\eta$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov assumption.** The current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$ and the map $m$, not on past measurements or controls.

$$
P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t, m)
$$

*Explanation:* This is the **sensor model** (right-hand block of the schematic).

**Step 3 — Introduce the previous pose $x_{t-1}$ and marginalize (Law of Total Probability).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, dx_{t-1} \\
&= \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The first factor inside the integral is the **motion model** (left-hand block of the schematic). The second factor is the previous belief, fed back from the prior cycle.

**Step 4 — Combine and identify the normalizer.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \underbrace{\int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}}_{\overline{bel}(x_t)\ \text{(Prediction)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The result is the **General Schematic Equation**. The term $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ is implemented by the **Prediction Module**, and the multiplication by $P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ followed by normalization $\eta$ is implemented by the **Measurement Update Module**.

### 3.2 Specialization 1 — Kalman Filter Localization (Gaussian Belief)

If $bel(x_{t-1}) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$ and the models are linear-Gaussian, the integral collapses to a closed-form Gaussian update.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\mu}_t &= A\,\mu_{t-1} + B\,u_t \quad &\text{(Predicted mean)} \\
\overline{\Sigma}_t &= A\,\Sigma_{t-1}\,A^T + R \quad &\text{(Predicted covariance)} \\
K_t &= \overline{\Sigma}_t H^T (H \overline{\Sigma}_t H^T + Q)^{-1} \quad &\text{(Kalman gain)} \\
\mu_t &= \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - H\overline{\mu}_t) \quad &\text{(Updated mean)} \\
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H)\overline{\Sigma}_t \quad &\text{(Updated covariance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* These five lines are precisely the *inner workings* of the general schematic when specialized to the Kalman filter. $A, B$ instantiate the prediction module; $H, K_t$ instantiate the measurement update module.

### 3.3 Specialization 2 — Particle Filter Localization (Monte Carlo Belief)

The belief is represented by $N$ weighted samples (particles) $\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^N$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Prediction:}\quad \bar{x}_t^{[i]} = x_t^{[i]} \sim P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]}) \\
&\textbf{Importance weight:}\quad w_t^{[i]} = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid \bar{x}_t^{[i]}, m) \\
&\textbf{Resampling:}\quad \text{Draw } x_t^{[i]} \text{ from } \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\} \text{ with replacement}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Again, the first line is the prediction module, the second line is the measurement update, and the third line combats particle depletion. The *same schematic* governs this algorithm — only the belief representation changes.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Kalman / EKF | Grid-based | Particle Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Belief representation** | Unimodal Gaussian | Discrete grid cells | Set of weighted samples |
| **Prediction implementation** | Matrix multiply $A\mu$ | Convolution of grid | Sample from motion model |
| **Update implementation** | Kalman gain $K_t$ | Cell-wise multiplication | Weight = likelihood |
| **Multi-modal?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Computational cost** | $O(d^2)$ | $O(\vert G \vert)$ | $O(N)$ |
| **Map type** | Feature-based | Occupancy grid | Either |
| **Best suited for** | Tracking (local) | Global (small maps) | Global + tracking |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the *block names* in the general schematic — Prediction, Update, Belief — remain identical across all three columns. **This invariance is precisely why KTU examiners emphasize the "General" schematic**: it is algorithm-agnostic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Schematic — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below is the **canonical general schematic** that the KTU syllabus expects students to reproduce in the exam. Each block has a definite mathematical correspondence that we will annotate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment and Map M] --> B[Robot Platform]
    B --> C[Proprioceptive Sensors Encoders IMU Gyro]
    B --> D[Exteroceptive Sensors Camera LIDAR Sonar GPS]
    C --> E[Prediction Module Motion Model P xt given ut and xPrev]
    E --> F[Prior Belief belBar xt]
    D --> G[Landmark Extraction and Data Association]
    A --> G
    G --> H[Measurement Update Module Sensor Model P zt given xt and M]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Posterior Belief bel xt]
    I --> J[Pose Estimate mu t or particle set Xt]
    J --> E
```

**Block-by-block mapping to the math (this is the high-yield KTU content):**

| Schematic Block | Mathematical Role | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Proprioceptive Sensors + Prediction Module | Motion update | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ |
| Exteroceptive Sensors + Data Association | Observation input | $z_t$ extracted, matched to map |
| Measurement Update Module | Bayes correction | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ |
| Pose Estimate | Output of the loop | $bel(x_t)$ — Gaussian mean, grid arg-max, or particle set |

### 4.2 The Two-Stage Bayesian Loop (Conceptual View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start at time t] --> S2[Input: control ut and prior belief bel xPrev]
    S2 --> S3[Prediction: compute prior belBar xt using motion model]
    S3 --> S4[Input: sensor measurement zt and map m]
    S4 --> S5[Measurement Update: compute posterior bel xt using sensor model]
    S5 --> S6[Output: pose estimate bel xt]
    S6 --> S7[Feed bel xt as new prior for time t plus 1]
    S7 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Information-Flow Schematic (Signals Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Prior_Loop
        P1[bel xPrev] --> P2[Prediction Module]
        U1[Control ut] --> P2
        P2 --> P3[belBar xt]
    end
    subgraph Update_Loop
        P3 --> U2[Measurement Update Module]
        M1[Map m] --> U2
        Z1[Measurement zt] --> U2
        U2 --> U3[bel xt]
    end
    U3 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Drawing Tip:** KTU evaluators reward a clean **closed loop** with **two clearly separated dashed rectangles** (Prediction box and Update box). Always label the *input arrows* ($u_t$, $z_t$, $m$) and the *output arrow* ($bel(x_t)$). A schematic missing the feedback arrow from $bel(x_t)$ back to the Prediction Module typically loses **2 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q2(a)]**
*Define mobile robot localization. With the help of a block diagram, explain the general schematic of mobile robot localization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Mobile robot localization is the process of estimating the pose $(x, y, \theta)$ of a mobile robot within a known map using sensor measurements and control inputs. The general schematic consists of a closed Bayesian loop with two main blocks: the **Prediction Module** (using the motion model $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$) that propagates the prior belief, and the **Measurement Update Module** (using the sensor model $P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$) that refines it using current observations. The output is the belief $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, which is fed back as the prior for the next time step. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for schematic description.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, S2 B.Tech]**
*Differentiate between position tracking and global localization. Which algorithm is best suited for global localization and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Position tracking* assumes the initial pose of the robot is known and only compensates for small incremental errors during motion — it is essentially a local estimation problem. *Global localization*, in contrast, does not assume knowledge of the initial pose and must determine the robot's location from scratch, often yielding a multi-modal belief. The **Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)** is best suited for global localization because it represents the belief as a set of weighted samples, naturally supports **multi-modal** distributions, and can recover from localization failure (e.g., the kidnapped robot problem). EKF, being unimodal Gaussian, cannot represent the multi-modal beliefs required at startup. **(3 marks — 1.5 for differentiation, 1.5 for algorithm choice with justification.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**
**(a)** Draw the general schematic of mobile robot localization and explain the function of each block. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Derive the two fundamental equations of the general localization schematic starting from the definition $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. Clearly identify the Prediction step and the Measurement Update step. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Schematic (5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation):**
*Refer to Section 4.1.* The schematic must include:
* Environment/Map block (1 mark)
* Robot Platform with Proprioceptive + Exteroceptive sensors (1 mark)
* Prediction Module fed by $u_t$ and previous belief (1 mark)
* Measurement Update Module fed by $z_t$ and map (1 mark)
* Closed feedback loop to next time step (1 mark)
* Two marks for correctly stating the function of *each* block in one sentence.

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**
We start with $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ and apply Bayes' rule (1 mark):

$$
bel(x_t) = \frac{P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

Apply the **Markov assumption** — the measurement $z_t$ depends only on $x_t$ and $m$ (1 mark):

$$
P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t, m)
$$

Introduce $x_{t-1}$ via the **Law of Total Probability** (1 mark):

$$
P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}
$$

Combine and recognize the **normalizer** $\eta$ (2 marks):

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \underbrace{\int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}}_{\overline{bel}(x_t)}
$$

Final identification (2 marks):
* The integral is the **Prediction** $\overline{bel}(x_t)$, implemented by the Prediction Module.
* The multiplication by $P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ followed by normalization is the **Measurement Update**, implemented by the Update Module.

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice – Alternative]**
**(a)** Explain the three main paradigms of probabilistic localization: Kalman Filter, Grid-based, and Monte Carlo (Particle Filter). Compare them in terms of belief representation, ability to handle multi-modal distributions, and computational complexity. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A mobile robot operates in a 1-D corridor. Its belief is uniform on $[0,\, 10]$ (i.e., $bel(x_{0}) = 0.1$ for $x_0 \in [0, 10]$). The motion model is $P(x_1 \mid u_1, x_0) = \mathcal{N}(x_0 + 2,\, 0.5^2)$ truncated to $[0, 10]$, and the robot then makes a single observation $z_1 = 5$ from a beacon at $x = 5$ with likelihood $P(z_1 \mid x_1) = \mathcal{N}(x_1,\, 1^2)$. Compute the prior belief $\overline{bel}(x_1)$ at $x_1 = 4$ and the posterior belief $bel(x_1)$ at $x_1 = 4$ (up to a normalizer $\eta$). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Paradigms Comparison (7 marks — 2 for KF, 2 for Grid, 2 for PF, 1 for comparison table):**

* **Kalman Filter Localization:** Represents belief as a single Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. Best for unimodal, continuous tracking. Prediction is a matrix multiplication, update uses the Kalman gain. Cannot represent multi-modal beliefs — fails in global localization. Cost: $O(d^2)$ for $d$-dimensional state.
* **Grid-Based (Histogram) Localization:** Discretizes the state space into cells, each holding a probability. Naturally supports multi-modal beliefs. Prediction is a convolution with the motion kernel; update is a cell-wise product with the sensor model. Cost: $O(\vert G \vert)$ where $\vert G \vert$ is the number of grid cells. Resolution limited by grid size.
* **Monte Carlo / Particle Filter Localization:** Represents belief as $N$ weighted samples. Supports multi-modal beliefs, handles non-linear/non-Gaussian models trivially, and recovers from the kidnapped robot problem. Cost: $O(N)$ per step. Suffers from particle depletion in high-dimensional states.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Prediction step** — compute $\overline{bel}(4)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(4) &= \int_0^{10} P(4 \mid u_1, x_0)\,bel(x_0)\,dx_0 \\
&= \int_0^{10} \frac{1}{0.5\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(4 - x_0 - 2)^2}{2(0.5)^2}\right) \cdot 0.1 \, dx_0 \\
&= 0.1 \cdot \mathcal{N}(4; \mu = x_0 + 2,\, \sigma^2 = 0.25) \text{ averaged over } x_0 \in [0, 10]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $bel(x_0)$ is uniform, the integral becomes the mean of a Gaussian over the prior interval. Evaluating at the mean of the prior ($x_0 = 5$):

$$
\overline{bel}(4) \approx 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{0.5\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(4 - 7)^2}{2 \cdot 0.25}\right) = 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{0.5\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-18}
$$

This is essentially negligible, indicating that with uniform prior the prediction is also uniform (≈ 0.1). **(3 marks)**

**Update step** — compute $bel(4)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(4) &= \eta \cdot P(z_1 = 5 \mid x_1 = 4) \cdot \overline{bel}(4) \\
P(z_1 = 5 \mid x_1 = 4) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(5 - 4)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-0.5} \\
bel(4) &= \eta \cdot \frac{e^{-0.5}}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot 0.1 \approx 0.1 \cdot 0.2420 \cdot \eta
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4 marks)** — The value $\eta$ is the inverse of the integral of the un-normalized posterior over $[0, 10]$ and is left symbolic.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in this topic:**
> 1. **Drawing a schematic without the feedback arrow** from $bel(x_t)$ back to the Prediction Module — KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** because the loop is the *defining feature* of recursive Bayesian estimation.
> 2. **Confusing the motion model $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ with the sensor model $P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$** — they go into *different* blocks. Mixing them up costs 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$** in the update equation — it is mandatory for $bel(x_t)$ to integrate to 1.
> 4. **Calling the output a single number (e.g., "the robot is at (3,4)")** — the output is a *distribution* (Gaussian / grid / particle set), not a point. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this conceptual error.
> 5. **In derivation questions, skipping the Markov assumption line** — the step $P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is worth **1 full mark** and is the most frequently missed line.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Localization** = estimating pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$ of a robot on a map using sensors and controls.
- ✅ **General schematic = 2-block closed Bayesian loop:** Prediction (motion model) → Update (sensor model) → output belief → feedback.
- ✅ **Two key equations** must be memorized: $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ and $bel(x_t) = \eta\,P(z_t \mid x_t, m)\,\overline{bel}(x_t)$.
- ✅ **Proprioceptive sensors** (encoders, IMU) → motion model → Prediction Module.
- ✅ **Exteroceptive sensors** (camera, LIDAR, sonar, GPS) → sensor model → Measurement Update Module.
- ✅ **Three localization paradigms** (KF, Grid, PF) all conform to the *same* general schematic; only the **belief representation** differs.
- ✅ **Position tracking** = local, unimodal → Kalman / EKF.
- ✅ **Global localization** = no initial pose, multi-modal → Particle Filter.
- ✅ **Kidnapped robot problem** = global localization + recovery from mid-run displacement → Particle Filter with random particle injection.
- ✅ **Data Association** is the bridge between raw exteroceptive measurements and the map landmarks; it is a critical *and* failure-prone block.
- ✅ Always draw the **feedback arrow** from $bel(x_t)$ back to the Prediction Module.
- ✅ The **Markov assumption** ($z_t$ depends only on $x_t$ and $m$) is the linchpin that makes the derivation tractable.
- ✅ **Normalization constant** $\eta$ is mandatory in the update equation to keep $bel(x_t)$ a valid probability distribution.
- ✅ The schematic is **algorithm-agnostic** — this universality is its primary pedagogical and engineering value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenge of Localization - Sensor Noise and Sensor Aliasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Challenge of Localization - Sensor Noise and Sensor Aliasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Challenge of Localization – Sensor Noise and Sensor Aliasing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Localization Problem (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its pose $\mathbf{x}_t = (x, y, \theta)^\top$ (position and orientation) within a global reference frame, given a map $m$ of the environment and a stream of sensor observations $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ and control inputs $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$.

Mathematically, it is the problem of computing the posterior belief:

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)$$

The two principal challenges that prevent a robot from achieving perfect, deterministic localization are:
1. **Sensor Noise** – unavoidable random and systematic errors in the measurement process.
2. **Sensor Aliasing** – the phenomenon where distinct physical locations produce indistinguishable sensor signatures.

### 1.2 Sensor Noise – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensor Noise** is the stochastic perturbation superimposed on the true physical signal being measured. It arises from the finite resolution, electronic interference, environmental fluctuations, and physical limitations of the transducer.

It is typically modeled as a probability distribution added to the true value:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t) + \epsilon_t$$

where:
- $h(\cdot)$ is the (deterministic) sensor model.
- $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R}_t)$ is the **zero-mean Gaussian noise** with covariance $\mathbf{R}_t$.

### 1.3 Sensor Aliasing – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor Aliasing** (also called *perceptual aliasing*) occurs when the sensor measurement function $h(\cdot)$ is **non-injective** — i.e., two or more distinct robot poses $\mathbf{x}_i \neq \mathbf{x}_j$ produce identical or near-identical sensor readings $h(\mathbf{x}_i) \approx h(\mathbf{x}_j)$.

This violates the uniqueness assumption and creates *ambiguity* in the belief.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogies (Plain English)

| Real-world Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Trying to recognize your location at night using only street-lamp glow (you cannot tell two lit blocks apart) | **Sensor Aliasing** – two rooms look identical to a low-resolution camera. |
| Asking three different people the time and getting answers differing by ±1 minute | **Sensor Noise** – random scatter around the true value. |
| A clock that always runs 5 minutes slow | **Systematic Bias** – a deterministic, repeatable error (a special class of noise). |
| Standing in the middle of a long, featureless white corridor with no doors in view | **Extreme Aliasing** – the sensor provides no distinguishable information about pose. |

### 1.5 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners frequently test whether a student can **distinguish between noise (uncertainty around a value) and aliasing (ambiguity between values)**. This distinction forms the conceptual basis for justifying the design of probabilistic localization algorithms such as the **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** and the **Kalman Filter Localization (KFL)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Sensor Noise

Sensor noise is conventionally decomposed into three independent categories:

1. **White (Independent) Noise**
   - Zero-mean, uncorrelated between successive samples.
   - Modeled as $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I})$.
   - Reduced by averaging multiple independent measurements.

2. **Systematic Bias**
   - A deterministic offset that repeats every measurement.
   - Example: wheel-encoder counts 1.2% high due to wheel-diameter tolerance.
   - Cannot be reduced by averaging; requires **calibration**.

3. **Drift / Time-Correlated Noise**
   - Slowly varying random process (e.g., gyroscope bias).
   - Modeled using stochastic processes such as *random walks*.
   - The dominant error source in **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)**.

### 2.2 How Sensor Noise Breaks Localization

- **Why?** Because each measurement has finite precision, the belief $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ is not a point mass but a *probability distribution* whose spread grows as noise accumulates.
- **How?** Each integration step (e.g., odometry update) compounds the prior covariance matrix:

$$\mathbf{P}_{t+1} = \mathbf{A}_t \mathbf{P}_t \mathbf{A}_t^\top + \mathbf{B}_t \mathbf{Q}_t \mathbf{B}_t^\top$$

- **Consequence:** Without absolute reference (a *global fix*), **dead-reckoning error grows without bound** — the well-known $O(t^2)$ error of double-integrated accelerometer noise and $O(t)$ error of integrated wheel-encoder noise.

### 2.3 Taxonomy of Sensor Aliasing

1. **Geometric Aliasing** – Symmetric environments (e.g., two identical rooms, regular pillar patterns).
2. **Reflective Aliasing** – Multipath returns in ultrasonic / radar sensors.
3. **Range-Based Aliasing** – Two obstacles at the same distance produce identical range scans.
4. **Visual Aliasing** – Repetitive textures, lack of texture (corridor walls), or limited field of view.

### 2.4 Why Sensor Aliasing is Fundamentally Harder than Noise

> [!NOTE]
> **Noise** is a problem of *precision* (we know roughly where we are, just not exactly).
> **Aliasing** is a problem of *correctness* (we may be confident but entirely wrong).

Aliasing can produce a **multi-modal belief distribution** — multiple disjoint peaks each corresponding to a plausible location. A simple unimodal estimator (such as the EKF) cannot represent this and will diverge to one peak, often the wrong one.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for sensor noise and aliasing-related problems. **Absolute value bars are written as `\vert` to preserve the markdown table format.**

| Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Measurement model with noise | $\mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t) + \epsilon_t$ | $\epsilon_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R}_t)$ |
| Gaussian (Normal) PDF | $p(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma^2$ = variance | $\text{unit: m}^{-1}$ for 1-D spatial |
| Bayesian belief update | $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$ | $\eta$ = normalizer | Posterior $\propto$ likelihood $\times$ prior |
| Prediction (motion update) | $\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ | $\mathbf{u}_t$ = control | Chapman–Kolmogorov equation |
| EKF linearization | $\mathbf{H}_t = \left. \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right\vert_{\mathbf{x}=\mu_t}$ | Jacobian evaluated at $\mu_t$ | Used when $h(\cdot)$ is nonlinear |
| EKF covariance update | $\mathbf{S}_t = \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t \mathbf{H}_t^\top + \mathbf{R}_t$ | Innovation covariance | $\mathbf{R}_t$ = measurement noise covariance |
| Kalman gain | $\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_t \mathbf{H}_t^\top \mathbf{S}_t^{-1}$ | $\mathbf{K}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ | Minimizes posterior covariance |
| Odometry drift (wheel slip) | $\delta_d = \delta_{d,\text{true}} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_d^2)$ | $\sigma_d \approx 0.01 \, d$ | Empirical rule (0.1% of travel) |
| Gyro drift | $\omega = \omega_{\text{true}} + b + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\omega^2)$ | $b$ = bias in rad/s | Random-walk model for $b$ |
| Aliasing condition | $\exists \, \mathbf{x}_i \neq \mathbf{x}_j : h(\mathbf{x}_i) = h(\mathbf{x}_j)$ | existence of duplicates | Violates injectivity of $h$ |
| Multi-modal belief (MCL) | $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) \approx \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta(\mathbf{x}_t - \mathbf{x}_t^{(i)})$ | $N$ = particle count | Sum of Dirac masses |
| Resampling weight | $w_t^{(i)} \propto p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t^{(i)})$ | Importance weight | Sum normalized to 1 |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Sensor noise dominates at high speed (LiDAR returns scatter); aliasing dominates in tunnels or parking garages (repeated pillars). Both must be addressed via sensor fusion (LiDAR + camera + GNSS + IMU).
- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Reflective floor surfaces cause range-sensor aliasing; uneven flooring causes wheel-odometry noise.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Acoustic noise is severe due to multipath; aliasing occurs when the robot passes near symmetrical sonar reflectors.
- **Aerial Drones (UAVs):** GPS-denied environments suffer both noise (IMU drift) and aliasing (featureless terrain), which is why visual-inertial odometry (VIO) is a research hotspot.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Propagation of Gaussian Sensor Noise Through a Linear Sensor Model

We wish to derive the probability density of the noisy measurement $\mathbf{z}$ when the true measurement is a linear function of the state $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 1 — Define the noise-free measurement.**
Assume the true state is $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and the linear sensor model is:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}$$

where $\mathbf{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the observation matrix.

**Step 2 — Add the noise term.**
The actual sensor reading is $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$, hence:

$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the linear transformation property of Gaussians.**
If $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_x, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_x)$ and $\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R})$ are independent, then the sum of two independent Gaussians is Gaussian with:

$$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{z}] = \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu}_x + \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\epsilon}] = \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu}_x$$

**Step 4 — Compute the covariance using the bilinearity of covariance.**

$$\text{Cov}(\mathbf{z}) = \text{Cov}(\mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}) = \mathbf{C}\,\text{Cov}(\mathbf{x})\,\mathbf{C}^\top + \text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}) + 2\,\text{Cov}(\mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\epsilon})$$

**Step 5 — Use the independence of $\mathbf{x}$ and $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ to drop the cross-term.**

$$\text{Cov}(\mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\epsilon}) = \mathbf{0}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Cov}(\mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_x \mathbf{C}^\top + \mathbf{R}$$

**Step 6 — Write the final result as a complete Gaussian distribution.**

$$p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{z} \,;\, \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x},\, \mathbf{R}\right)$$

**Conclusion:** Sensor noise modelled as additive Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R})$ produces a measurement likelihood that is also Gaussian, but the *posterior* covariance grows as $\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_x \mathbf{C}^\top + \mathbf{R}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Posterior Belief under the Bayes Filter (Tackling Aliasing)

The Bayes filter produces a *complete* posterior distribution, which is the natural mathematical tool for handling **multi-modal** (aliased) beliefs.

**Step 1 — Begin with Bayes' rule.**

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) = \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \, p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)}{p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov assumption.**
Since $\mathbf{x}_t$ summarizes the entire past given the most recent control $\mathbf{u}_t$, the prediction simplifies to the *motion model*:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \, p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t-1}, m) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the integral into Bayes' rule.**

$$\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)\, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})\, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta\, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)\, \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$$

**Step 4 — Discuss why this solves the aliasing problem.**
The prediction $\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$ can be **multi-modal** if $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ is non-injective (e.g., the robot entered a symmetric corridor and the motion model maps multiple past poses onto a single distribution). The likelihood $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$ sharpens each mode according to the new measurement. The normalization constant $\eta$ ensures a valid probability mass.

**Step 5 — Express the multi-modal nature explicitly.**
Suppose two distinct poses $\mathbf{x}_A$ and $\mathbf{x}_B$ generate identical sensor readings. Then $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_A, m) = p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_B, m)$, and both peaks in $\overline{bel}$ survive the update. Only the relative weights change. This is the *defining advantage* of probabilistic localization.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: A 1-D Numerical Worked Example of Sensor Noise

A wheel-encoder reports the robot has moved $d = 2.00$ m. The encoder is known to have a Gaussian error with standard deviation $\sigma = 0.05$ m.

**Step 1 — Construct the measurement PDF.**

$$p(d_{\text{meas}} \mid d_{\text{true}}) = \frac{1}{0.05\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(d_{\text{meas}} - d_{\text{true}})^2}{2(0.05)^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the 95% confidence interval.**
For a 1-D Gaussian, the 95% interval is $\mu \pm 1.96\sigma$:

$$\Delta_{95} = 1.96 \times 0.05 = 0.098 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the effective error bound.**

$$d_{\text{reported}} = 2.00 \pm 0.098 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the relative precision.**

$$\frac{\Delta_{95}}{d} \times 100 = \frac{0.098}{2.00} \times 100 = 4.9\%$$

**Step 5 — Discuss the implication for the next 10 meters of travel.**
If errors accumulate independently, the standard deviation after 10 independent 2-m steps is:

$$\sigma_{10} = \sqrt{10} \times 0.05 = 0.158 \text{ m}$$

and the 95% bound grows to $1.96 \times 0.158 = 0.310$ m. This is the *fundamental reason* why odometry alone cannot localize a robot over long distances — a key insight expected in 14-mark KTU answers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Noise vs. Aliasing

| Property | Sensor Noise | Sensor Aliasing |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Physical sensor imperfections | Ambiguity of measurement function $h(\cdot)$ |
| Effect on belief | Single mode, growing spread | Multiple disjoint modes |
| Mathematical form | Continuous distribution widening over time | Multi-modal / degenerate likelihood |
| Reducible by averaging? | **Yes** (independent noise) | **No** |
| Reducible by better hardware? | **Yes** | **No** (requires more *information*) |
| Solved by EKF? | **Yes** (unimodal Gaussian) | **No** (single-mode collapse) |
| Solved by MCL (particle filter)? | **Yes** | **Yes** (multi-modal representation) |
| Detector | Residual analysis, Allan variance | Hypothesis testing, ambiguity index |
| Example in KTU paper | "Why does gyro drift accumulate over time?" | "Why does a corridor with identical doors confuse a vision-based localizer?" |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Causes, Effects, and Mitigation of Sensor Noise and Sensor Aliasing

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual flow of how each challenge originates, propagates through the localization pipeline, and is finally mitigated in real engineering systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical World State x_t] --> B[Sensor Measurement Process h]
    B --> C[True Sensor Reading y]
    C --> D1[Add Zero-Mean Noise eps1]
    C --> D2[Add Systematic Bias b]
    C --> D3[Add Drift eta_t]
    D1 --> E[Noisy Measurement z_t]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    B --> F{Distinct Poses Same Reading?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Sensor Aliasing Occurs]
    F -- No  --> H[Unique Likelihood]
    E --> I[Belief Update Bayes Filter]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Belief Shape}
    J -- Unimodal Spread --> K[EKF Suitable]
    J -- Multi-Modal Peaks --> L[MCL Particle Filter Required]
    K --> M[Final Pose Estimate]
    L --> M
    E --> N[Noise Mitigation Layer]
    N --> N1[Calibration for Bias]
    N --> N2[Filtering for Drift]
    N --> N3[Sensor Fusion for White Noise]
    G --> O[Aliasing Mitigation Layer]
    O --> O1[Distinct Feature Selection]
    O --> O2[Multi-Hypothesis Tracking]
    O --> O3[Information Rich Sensors]
    N1 --> M
    N2 --> M
    N3 --> M
    O1 --> M
    O2 --> M
    O3 --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The top branch shows how a single physical state produces three statistically distinct noise components. The middle branch captures the *deterministic* ambiguity of the sensor function. The right-side classification chooses the appropriate filter (EKF vs. MCL). The bottom layers show the *engineering mitigations*.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Belief Evolution under Pure Noise vs. Pure Aliasing

This block diagram contrasts the *shape* of the posterior belief for the two extreme scenarios.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Initial Belief] --> A2[After Motion Update: Unimodal Spread Widened]
    A2 --> A3[After Measurement: Single Peak Sharpened]
    A1 --> B1[After Motion Update: Multiple Peaks Spawned]
    B1 --> B2[After Measurement: Peaks Weights Re-Weighted]
    B2 --> B3[If One Peak Dominates: Robot Localized]
    B2 --> B4[If Multiple Peaks Survive: Aliasing Persists]
    A3 --> Z1[Noise Only Scenario]
    B3 --> Z2[Aliasing Resolved Scenario]
    B4 --> Z3[Aliasing Unresolved Scenario]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Multi-Sensor Fusion as a Universal Remedy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Wheel Odometry] --> F1[Kalman Filter Front-End]
    S2[IMU Gyro + Accel] --> F1
    S3[LiDAR Scan Matching] --> F2[Probabilistic Filter]
    S4[Camera Visual Features] --> F2
    S5[GNSS Global Fix] --> F3[Outlier Rejection Module]
    F1 --> M[Centralized Pose Estimator]
    F2 --> M
    F3 --> M
    M --> O[Robot Pose x_t and Covariance P_t]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Multi-modal sensor fusion combines sensors that are *complementary*. For example, GNSS reduces the long-term drift of odometry, while LiDAR resolves the aliasing that a low-resolution camera cannot. The central estimator outputs both the pose **and** its uncertainty — a hallmark of OBE-style problem statements.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`
**Differentiate between sensor noise and sensor aliasing in the context of robot localization. Provide one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Sensor Noise | Sensor Aliasing |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Random perturbations added to the true measurement. | Two or more distinct poses yield identical sensor readings. |
| **Belief Effect** | Widens a single mode (loss of precision). | Splits a single mode into multiple modes (loss of correctness). |
| **Example** | Wheel-encoder returns 2.00 m $\pm$ 0.05 m for a 2 m travel. | A robot in a corridor with three identical doors cannot tell them apart using a low-resolution camera. |

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example of each.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why does the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) localization fail in environments with severe sensor aliasing?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. The EKF represents the belief $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ as a **single Gaussian** characterized by a mean $\mu_t$ and covariance $\mathbf{P}_t$ (1 mark).
2. In an aliased environment, the true posterior is **multi-modal** — multiple distinct locations are plausible. A single Gaussian cannot represent multiple disjoint peaks (1 mark).
3. Consequently, the EKF will collapse to *one* of the modes (often the wrong one), and the covariance $\mathbf{P}_t$ will falsely indicate high confidence. This phenomenon is called **filter divergence** (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Question (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module-2 long-answer question **must offer an internal choice**. Two fully independent alternatives are given below. The student is expected to answer **exactly one** of the two.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`

**(a)** Derive the measurement update equation of the Bayes filter from first principles. Show clearly how the resulting posterior distribution can become multi-modal when the sensor exhibits aliasing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and axle length $L = 0.30$ m is commanded to drive straight for $\Delta t = 1$ s. The left and right wheel velocities are measured as $v_L = 0.25$ m/s and $v_R = 0.25$ m/s. Both encoders exhibit zero-mean Gaussian noise with standard deviation $\sigma_v = 0.005$ m/s. Compute the mean and standard deviation of the robot's linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$. Briefly discuss the implication for long-term localization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State Bayes' rule.**

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) = \eta\, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)\, p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Markov assumption to the prediction term.**

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)\, p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t-1}, m)\, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a single recursive update equation.**

$$\boxed{bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta\, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1})\, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})\, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Discuss multi-modality due to aliasing.**
If the measurement model $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$ has the same value at two distinct poses $\mathbf{x}_A \neq \mathbf{x}_B$, then both peaks of the prediction survive the update. The posterior is therefore a *mixture* of Gaussians rather than a single Gaussian. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Final justification.**
This is why **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** is the preferred filter: it represents the belief as a finite set of weighted samples $\{(\mathbf{x}_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, which can naturally capture multiple modes.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Write the differential-drive forward kinematics.**

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(v_L + v_R)$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{L}(v_R - v_L)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the mean values.**
With $v_L = v_R = 0.25$ m/s:

$$\bar{v} = \frac{0.05}{2}(0.25 + 0.25) = 0.05 \times 0.25 = 0.0125 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\bar{\omega} = \frac{0.05}{0.30}(0.25 - 0.25) = 0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Linearize the variance for small Gaussian inputs.**
For $v = \frac{r}{2}(v_L + v_R)$, both inputs are independent $\mathcal{N}(0.25, 0.005^2)$. Variance of the sum is the sum of variances:

$$\sigma_v^2 = \left(\frac{r}{2}\right)^2 (\sigma_{v_L}^2 + \sigma_{v_R}^2) = (0.025)^2 (0.005^2 + 0.005^2) = 0.000625 \times 5\times 10^{-5}$$

$$\sigma_v^2 = 3.125 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\sigma_v = \sqrt{3.125 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.768 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute variance of $\omega$.**
With $v_L, v_R$ independent:

$$\sigma_\omega^2 = \left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2 (\sigma_{v_L}^2 + \sigma_{v_R}^2) = \left(\frac{0.05}{0.30}\right)^2 (2 \times 0.005^2) = 0.02778 \times 5\times 10^{-5}$$

$$\sigma_\omega^2 = 1.389 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\sigma_\omega = 1.179 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Implication for long-term localization.**
The orientation estimate $\theta_t = \int_0^t \omega\, d\tau$ accumulates variance linearly:

$$\text{Var}(\theta_t) = t \cdot \sigma_\omega^2$$

Hence $\sigma_\theta$ grows as $\sqrt{t}$, and the position covariance grows as $t^2$ (double integration). After 1 hour (3600 s):

$$\sigma_\theta = \sqrt{3600} \times 1.179 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0707 \text{ rad} \approx 4.05°$$

and the corresponding position uncertainty is on the order of metres. This confirms that **odometry cannot be the sole localization source over long missions**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set C]`

**(a)** With suitable block diagrams, explain how sensor noise and sensor aliasing act as the two principal challenges in mobile robot localization. How does the Bayes filter framework address each? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot uses an ultrasonic range sensor with measurement model $z = d + \epsilon$, where $d$ is the true distance and $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma = 0.10$ m. The robot obtains two independent readings $z_1 = 1.50$ m and $z_2 = 1.62$ m of the same obstacle. Compute the maximum-likelihood estimate $\hat{d}_{\text{MLE}}$ and the standard deviation of the fused estimate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define each challenge.**

- **Sensor Noise** (2 marks): Random perturbation modelled as $\mathbf{z} = h(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$. Causes the belief $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ to spread. Quantified by the covariance matrix $\mathbf{R}_t$.
- **Sensor Aliasing** (2 marks): Non-injectivity of $h(\cdot)$ causing distinct poses to map to identical measurements. Causes $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ to become multi-modal.

**Step 2 — Bayes filter response to noise (1.5 marks).**
The Bayesian update averages over the noise via the likelihood $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$, yielding a precise unimodal posterior after sufficient measurements (variance reduction by factor $1/N$).

**Step 3 — Bayes filter response to aliasing (1.5 marks).**
The Bayesian update preserves multiple modes if the measurement cannot disambiguate them. The posterior is a mixture distribution, naturally represented by Monte Carlo Localization particles. This is a decisive advantage over EKF.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Write the joint log-likelihood.**

$$\mathcal{L}(d) = \log p(z_1, z_2 \mid d) = \log\!\left[\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(z_1-d)^2/(2\sigma^2)}\right] + \log\!\left[\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(z_2-d)^2/(2\sigma^2)}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Drop constants and simplify.**

$$\mathcal{L}(d) = -\frac{(z_1 - d)^2 + (z_2 - d)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \text{const}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $d$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d\mathcal{L}}{dd} = \frac{(z_1 - d) + (z_2 - d)}{\sigma^2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve.**

$$z_1 + z_2 - 2d = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \hat{d}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{z_1 + z_2}{2} = \frac{1.50 + 1.62}{2} = 1.56 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the variance of the fused estimate.**
For two independent Gaussian measurements with equal variance $\sigma^2$:

$$\sigma_{\text{fused}}^2 = \left(\frac{1}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\right)^{-1} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{fused}} = \frac{0.10}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.0707 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**
Averaging two independent noisy measurements reduces the standard deviation by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, demonstrating the **central limit theorem** and the importance of multiple independent measurements in combating noise.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating noise and aliasing** (–2 marks). Examiners expect a *clear distinction*. Use the words "precision" (noise) versus "correctness / ambiguity" (aliasing).
> 2. **Forgetting the Markov assumption** in the Bayes-filter derivation (–1 mark). Always state it before writing the integral.
> 3. **Unit mistakes** in numericals. Standard deviations must be in metres or rad/s — *not* seconds.
> 4. **Omitting the explanation of why EKF fails** under aliasing (–1 to –2 marks). A bare statement "EKF cannot handle multi-modal" gets only partial credit; explicitly mention the *single-Gaussian assumption* and the resulting *filter divergence*.
> 5. **Skipping the linearization** in EKF problems. Show the Jacobian $\mathbf{H}_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\vert_{\mathbf{x}=\mu_t}$ explicitly.
> 6. **Missing the $\sqrt{N}$ averaging rule.** Saying "averaging reduces noise" without stating the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}$ factor loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist as the last-minute revision sheet for the topic **"Challenge of Localization – Sensor Noise and Sensor Aliasing"**.

- [ ] Localization = estimation of pose $\mathbf{x}_t = (x, y, \theta)^\top$ given map $m$ and sensor/control history.
- [ ] The belief is the posterior $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)$.
- [ ] **Sensor Noise** = random additive perturbation $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R}_t)$ that *widens* a unimodal belief.
- [ ] **Sensor Aliasing** = non-injectivity of $h(\cdot)$ that *splits* a unimodal belief into multiple modes.
- [ ] Noise is reducible by averaging (variance $\propto 1/N$); aliasing is **not** reducible by averaging.
- [ ] The three noise categories: **white noise**, **systematic bias**, **drift**.
- [ ] Drift errors grow unbounded in dead-reckoning ($\sigma_\theta \propto \sqrt{t}$, $\sigma_{xy} \propto t$).
- [ ] The Bayes filter update: $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta\, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)\, \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$.
- [ ] The EKF represents beliefs as a **single Gaussian** — it **fails** under multi-modal aliasing.
- [ ] The Monte Carlo Localization (particle filter) represents beliefs as **weighted samples** — it handles multi-modal aliasing naturally.
- [ ] Multi-sensor fusion is the universal engineering remedy: combine complementary sensors (e.g., odometry + LiDAR + GNSS).
- [ ] The Kalman gain $\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_t \mathbf{H}_t^\top (\mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t \mathbf{H}_t^\top + \mathbf{R}_t)^{-1}$ minimizes the posterior covariance.
- [ ] For two independent Gaussian measurements of the same quantity, the fused variance is $\sigma^2_{\text{fused}} = \sigma^2 / 2$ (general: $\sigma^2 / N$).
- [ ] Common aliasing examples: identical rooms, symmetric corridors, repetitive pillars, multipath returns, featureless walls.
- [ ] Common noise examples: wheel slip, gyro drift, LiDAR speckle, camera exposure flicker, ADC quantization.
- [ ] Key KTU 2024 buzzwords to mention in answers: **Markov assumption**, **Chapman–Kolmogorov equation**, **filter divergence**, **multi-modal belief**, **importance sampling**, **resampling**, **covariance propagation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effector Noise
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effector Noise

# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In mobile robotics, **Effector Noise** refers to the stochastic uncertainty, error, or stochastic perturbation introduced into a robot's state when the **control action** (velocity, angular velocity, force, or torque command) issued by the motion planner is actually executed by the physical **effectors** — i.e., the wheels, drive motors, steering actuators, and mechanical transmission systems of the robot.

Mathematically, if a control vector $u_t = (v_t, \omega_t)^T$ is commanded at time $t$, the *actual* executed control becomes:

$$u_t^{actual} = u_t + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

where $\varepsilon_t$ is the **effector (actuation) noise**, modeled as a zero-mean **Gaussian random variable** with covariance matrix $R_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Effector noise is the *primary source of error* in **dead reckoning / odometry-based localization**. Unlike sensor noise (which is observation-side), effector noise is **actuation-side** and propagates forward in time, causing the *belief* about the robot pose to diverge from the true pose.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy 🚗

Imagine you are driving a car with **your eyes closed** (no GPS, no cameras — this is the dead-reckoning scenario). Your brain commands: *"turn the steering wheel 30° left, press the accelerator for 2 seconds."*

But what actually happens?

- The **steering mechanism** has mechanical backlash → the wheel turns only **27°**.
- The **engine** has fuel injection noise → the car accelerates slightly **more** or **less** than expected.
- One **wheel slips** on a wet patch → the car turns a bit **more** than commanded.
- The **encoder** on the wheel has finite resolution → you *think* you moved 1 meter, but it was actually **1.02 meters**.

All of these are **effector noise** — the *difference between what you commanded and what actually happened*. Over time, these tiny errors accumulate, and after 10 minutes you might be **30 meters away** from where you actually thought you were. **This drift is the fundamental problem of mobile robot odometry.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Sensor noise affects *what the robot perceives*; Effector noise affects *what the robot does*. Both are equally important, but effector noise is harder to detect because the robot has no direct way to measure how poorly its own motors responded.

---

## 1.3 The Two Major Categories of Effector Noise

| Category | Description | Examples | Correctable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Systematic Errors (Biases)** | Deterministic, repeatable deviations | Wheel diameter mismatch, axle misalignment, encoder offset | Yes, via calibration |
| **Non-Systematic Errors (Noise)** | Stochastic, non-repeatable perturbations | Wheel slip, floor unevenness, motor current fluctuations | No, modeled probabilistically |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Give an example of a non-systematic effector error."* — The answer is **wheel slip** (it varies randomly with terrain friction).

---

## 1.4 Standard Noise Magnitude Metrics (Bold Constants)

For a typical **differential-drive** wheeled robot, the effector noise parameters are:

- **Translation noise standard deviation:** $\sigma_v \approx \mathbf{0.05 \ m/s}$ to $\mathbf{0.2 \ m/s}$
- **Rotation noise standard deviation:** $\sigma_\omega \approx \mathbf{0.02 \ rad/s}$ to $\mathbf{0.1 \ rad/s}$
- **Cross-covariance term:** typically $\rho_{v\omega} \approx \mathbf{0}$ (assumed independent in basic models)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Motion Model (Foundation)

In the **probabilistic robotics paradigm** (Thrun, Burgard, Fox), the robot pose evolves according to a **motion model** that explicitly incorporates effector noise:

$$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \varepsilon_t$$

where:
- $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ = robot pose at time $t$
- $u_t = (v, \omega)^T$ = commanded control
- $f(\cdot)$ = kinematic motion function
- $\varepsilon_t$ = effector noise vector, $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$

The probability of reaching state $x_t$ given previous state and control is:

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^n \vert R_t \vert}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x_t - f(x_{t-1}, u_t))^T R_t^{-1} (x_t - f(x_{t-1}, u_t))\right)$$

This is the **Gaussian posterior over robot motion** — and $R_t$ is what we call the **effector noise covariance matrix**.

---

## 2.2 Kinematic Motion Function (Velocity Motion Model)

For a **differential drive** robot with control $(v, \omega)$ applied for time $\Delta t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= x_{t-1} - \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta_{t-1}) + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t) \\
y_t &= y_{t-1} + \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta_{t-1}) - \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t) \\
\theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

When this motion is perturbed by noise $\varepsilon_v, \varepsilon_\omega$, the resulting **Jacobian** propagates the uncertainty into the pose covariance:

$$P_t = F_x P_{t-1} F_x^T + F_\varepsilon R_t F_\varepsilon^T$$

where $F_x, F_\varepsilon$ are Jacobians of $f$ with respect to state and noise.

---

## 2.3 Parameterized Error Model (Proportional Noise)

A widely-used engineering model (Siegwart, Nourbakhsh) parameterizes effector noise as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_v^2 &= K_v \cdot \vert v \vert + \sigma_{v,0}^2 \\
\sigma_\omega^2 &= K_\omega \cdot \vert v \vert + \sigma_{\omega,0}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

- $K_v, K_\omega$ = **proportional gain constants** (scale with commanded velocity)
- $\sigma_{v,0}, \sigma_{\omega,0}$ = **additive noise floors** (independent of velocity)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why proportional?** Because at higher speeds, the *same absolute slip distance* translates to a larger *fractional* error — proportional models capture this empirically.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet / Quick Reference

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u_t^{actual} = u_t + \varepsilon_t$ | Noisy executed control | (m/s, rad/s) |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ | Gaussian noise assumption | — |
| 3 | $R_t = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_v^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_\omega^2 \end{pmatrix}$ | Effector noise covariance (diagonal) | $m^2/s^2$, $rad^2/s^2$ |
| 4 | $\sigma_v^2 = K_v \vert v \vert + \sigma_{v,0}^2$ | Velocity noise variance (proportional) | $m^2/s^2$ |
| 5 | $\sigma_\omega^2 = K_\omega \vert v \vert + \sigma_{\omega,0}^2$ | Rotation noise variance | $rad^2/s^2$ |
| 6 | $P_t = F_x P_{t-1} F_x^T + F_\varepsilon R_t F_\varepsilon^T$ | EKF pose covariance propagation | $m^2$, $rad^2$ |
| 7 | $D_{drift} = \sigma_\omega \cdot d \cdot \Delta t$ | Expected drift distance | m |
| 8 | $\theta_{odom} = \theta_{true} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\theta^2)$ | Heading uncertainty | rad |
| 9 | $v_{actual} = v_{nominal} \cdot (1 + \eta_v)$ | Multiplicative slip model | m/s |
| 10 | $R_{eff} = R_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{wheel}$ | Mechanical error chain | — |

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering 🏭

| Application Domain | Role of Effector Noise |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Wheel slip during braking/turning creates dangerous odometry drift |
| **Warehouse AGVs** | Floor imperfections cause systematic misalignment over long corridors |
| **Planetary Rovers (NASA)** | Sandy/rocky terrain produces massive slip (60%+ in extreme cases) |
| **Surgical Robots** | Even $\mu m$-scale actuator noise matters for precision cutting |
| **Drone Swarms** | Motor RPM noise compounds across formation flight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice of $R_t$ directly determines how much an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** will trust dead-reckoning versus GPS/camera updates. A larger $R_t$ makes the filter *trust sensors more*; a smaller $R_t$ makes it *trust odometry more*. This is a critical tuning parameter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Deriving the Effector Noise Covariance Update (EKF-Style)

**Starting Premise:** We have a motion model with state transition:

$$x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t + \varepsilon_t)$$

where $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$. We want to compute the **posterior covariance** $P_t = \mathrm{cov}(x_t)$ using **first-order Taylor linearization** (EKF).

---

### Step 1: Expand the state transition via first-order Taylor series

We Taylor-expand $g$ around the nominal point $(x_{t-1}, u_t)$:

$$x_t \approx g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \underbrace{\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x_{t-1},u_t}}_{F_x} (x_{t-1} - \hat{x}_{t-1}) + \underbrace{\frac{\partial g}{\partial u}\bigg\vert_{x_{t-1},u_t}}_{F_\varepsilon} \varepsilon_t$$

> **Conversion logic:** The first term $g(x_{t-1}, u_t)$ is the deterministic prediction. The second term captures how state-estimation error propagates. The third term captures how effector noise propagates.

---

### Step 2: Identify the two Jacobian matrices

For the **differential-drive kinematic model** $g(x_{t-1}, u_t)$ with $u = (v, \omega)$:

$$
F_x = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & -\frac{v}{\omega}\cos\theta + \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta + \omega\Delta t) \\
0 & 1 & -\frac{v}{\omega}\sin\theta + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta + \omega\Delta t) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
F_\varepsilon = \begin{pmatrix}
\frac{-\sin\theta + \sin(\theta+\omega\Delta t)}{\omega} & \frac{v}{\omega^2}\left[\sin\theta - \sin(\theta+\omega\Delta t)\right] + \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta+\omega\Delta t)\Delta t \\
\frac{\cos\theta - \cos(\theta+\omega\Delta t)}{\omega} & -\frac{v}{\omega^2}\left[\cos\theta - \cos(\theta+\omega\Delta t)\right] + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta+\omega\Delta t)\Delta t \\
0 & \Delta t
\end{pmatrix}
$$

---

### Step 3: Form the posterior covariance

The covariance of a linear combination of independent random variables $(x_{t-1} - \hat{x}_{t-1})$ with covariance $P_{t-1}$ and $\varepsilon_t$ with covariance $R_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= F_x \, P_{t-1} \, F_x^T + F_\varepsilon \, R_t \, F_\varepsilon^T
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The first term is **state-estimation uncertainty propagation**, and the second term is the **new effector noise injection**. This is the **additive uncertainty update** law.

---

### Step 4: Substitute the diagonal noise matrix

Since we assume velocity noise and rotation noise are **uncorrelated**, we have:

$$
R_t = \begin{pmatrix}
\sigma_v^2 & 0 \\
0 & \sigma_\omega^2
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_\varepsilon R_t F_\varepsilon^T = \sigma_v^2 \cdot \mathbf{c}_v \mathbf{c}_v^T + \sigma_\omega^2 \cdot \mathbf{c}_\omega \mathbf{c}_\omega^T
$$

where $\mathbf{c}_v, \mathbf{c}_\omega$ are the **columns** of $F_\varepsilon$. This is the **rank-1 decomposition** showing how each noise axis independently injects uncertainty into the pose.

---

### Step 5: Special case — Pure translation ($\omega = 0$)

When the robot moves straight, the Jacobian $F_\varepsilon$ reduces to:

$$
F_\varepsilon \approx \begin{pmatrix}
\Delta t \cos\theta & 0 \\
\Delta t \sin\theta & 0 \\
0 & \Delta t
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Thus:

$$
P_t = P_{t-1} + \begin{pmatrix}
\sigma_v^2 \Delta t^2 \cos^2\theta & \sigma_v^2 \Delta t^2 \cos\theta \sin\theta & 0 \\
\sigma_v^2 \Delta t^2 \cos\theta \sin\theta & \sigma_v^2 \Delta t^2 \sin^2\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \sigma_\omega^2 \Delta t^2
\end{pmatrix}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Notice that $x$ and $y$ uncertainties grow as $\sigma_v^2 \Delta t^2$, but heading uncertainty grows as $\sigma_\omega^2 \Delta t^2$. After $N$ steps of size $\Delta t$, total elapsed time $T = N \Delta t$, so position variance grows as $\sigma_v^2 T^2$ — this is **quadratic error growth** characteristic of dead reckoning!

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Positional Drift Over Time

For a robot commanded to drive in a straight line at velocity $v$ with rotational noise $\sigma_\omega$, the **heading error** after time $T$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta(T) &= \sigma_\omega \sqrt{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **lateral drift** due to heading error is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{drift} &= v \cdot T \cdot \sin(\sigma_\theta T) \approx v \cdot T \cdot \sigma_\omega \sqrt{T} = v \sigma_\omega T^{3/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Lateral drift grows as $T^{3/2}$ — **faster than linear but slower than quadratic**. This is why straight-line odometry is *more reliable* than turning odometry, and why long missions require periodic GPS/LiDAR corrections.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example
