**Problem:** A differential-drive robot is commanded to move forward at $v = 0.5$ m/s for 10 seconds. Given:
- Velocity noise: $\sigma_v = 0.05$ m/s
- Rotational noise: $\sigma_\omega = 0.02$ rad/s
- Initial heading: $\theta_0 = 0$
- Time step: $\Delta t = 0.1$ s

**Find:** The 3$\sigma$ position uncertainty ellipse after 10 seconds.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute accumulated velocity noise (assuming white noise integration):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x,acc}^2 &= \sigma_v^2 \cdot \Delta t^2 \cdot N = (0.05)^2 \cdot (0.1)^2 \cdot 100 \\
&= 0.0025 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 100 = 0.0025 \ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute heading noise growth:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta &= \sigma_\omega \sqrt{T} = 0.02 \cdot \sqrt{10} = 0.0632 \ \text{rad} \ (\approx 3.62^\circ)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute lateral drift:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{y,drift} &= v \cdot T \cdot \sigma_\theta = 0.5 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.0632 = 0.316 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** 3$\sigma$ uncertainty envelope:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_x^{3\sigma} &= 3 \cdot \sqrt{0.0025} = 0.15 \ \text{m} \\
\sigma_y^{3\sigma} &= 3 \cdot 0.316 = 0.948 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** After just 10 seconds of dead reckoning, the 99.7% confidence ellipse spans **~1.5 m laterally** — this is the practical reason GPS/landmark corrections are needed every few seconds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Effector Noise Injection Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram shows how effector noise propagates from the planner to the physical robot pose.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Planner Command v_nom, w_nom] --> B[Add Effector Noise eps_v, eps_w]
    B --> C[Actual Control v_act, w_act]
    C --> D[Motor Driver PWM Signals]
    D --> E[Gearbox Transmission]
    E --> F[Wheel Encoder Feedback]
    F --> G[Wheel Slip and Terrain Interaction]
    G --> H[Actual Robot Pose x, y, theta]
    H --> I[Pose Covariance P_t Updated]

    classDef planner fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef noise fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#7A0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef actuator fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#005F5A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef robot fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#006B5F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#C9A0FF,stroke:#3D0066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A planner
    class B noise
    class C,D,E,F actuator
    class G,H robot
    class I output
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each box injects an additional layer of stochastic uncertainty. The noise $R_t$ aggregates all these layers into a single covariance matrix used by the EKF/SLAM algorithm.

---

## 4.2 Cause-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[Effector Noise]:::root
    N --> S[Systematic]
    N --> NS[Non-Systematic]

    S --> S1[Wheel Diameter Mismatch]
    S --> S2[Axle Misalignment]
    S --> S3[Encoder Resolution Limit]
    S --> S4[Gearbox Backlash]
    S --> S5[Bearing Friction Variation]

    NS --> NS1[Wheel Slip on Wet Surface]
    NS --> NS2[Floor Bump Impact]
    NS --> NS3[Motor Current Ripple]
    NS --> NS4[Unknown Payload Shift]
    NS --> NS5[Temperature Drift of Components]

    classDef root fill:#FFE082,stroke:#B27300,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef systematic fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nonsyst fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#7F0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class N root
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 systematic
    class NS1,NS2,NS3,NS4,NS5 nonsyst
```

---

## 4.3 Covariance Propagation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Previous Covariance P_t-1]:::input --> M[Linearize Motion Model]
    B[Effector Noise Covariance R_t]:::input --> M
    M --> Jx[Compute Jacobian F_x]
    M --> Je[Compute Jacobian F_epsilon]
    Jx --> P1[F_x P_t-1 F_x^T]
    Je --> P2[F_epsilon R_t F_epsilon^T]
    P1 --> Sum[Matrix Addition]
    P2 --> Sum
    Sum --> Out[Updated Covariance P_t]:::output

    classDef input fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Uncertainty Growth Over Time Plot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Positional Uncertainty vs Mission Duration"
    x-axis "Time T (seconds)" [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30]
    y-axis "Uncertainty (meters)" 0 --> 5
    line [0.0, 0.25, 0.6, 1.1, 1.85, 2.75, 3.85]
```

> **Note:** The curve is *super-linear* (looks roughly like $T^{3/2}$), demonstrating dead-reckoning drift — the longer the mission without correction, the faster the uncertainty grows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define *effector noise* in the context of mobile robot navigation. Differentiate it from sensor noise with one example of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Effector noise** is the stochastic uncertainty introduced when a robot executes a motion command via its actuators (motors, wheels, gearboxes). It is modelled as additive Gaussian noise on the control vector: $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$, where $R_t$ is the effector noise covariance matrix.
>
> **Sensor noise** is uncertainty in the robot's *observations* (e.g., LiDAR range measurement error $\pm 2$ cm).
>
> **Effector noise example:** Wheel slip causing actual travelled distance to differ from commanded distance by 5%.
>
> **Sensor noise example:** Camera pixel quantization producing $\pm 1$ pixel uncertainty in landmark detection.
>
> *(1 mark each: definition, difference, example)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any four sources of non-systematic effector noise in a wheeled mobile robot.**

> **Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):**
> 1. **Wheel slip** on wet, icy, or sandy terrain
> 2. **Floor unevenness** causing wheel bouncing and momentary loss of contact
> 3. **Motor current/torque ripple** producing micro-speed variations
> 4. **Unknown payload shifting** altering the effective wheel radius under dynamic loads
> 5. *(Bonus)* Temperature drift in motor windings changing effective voltage-to-RPM ratio

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) prediction step covariance update** $P_t = F_x P_{t-1} F_x^T + F_\varepsilon R_t F_\varepsilon^T$ starting from the noisy motion model $x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t + \varepsilon_t)$, clearly identifying each Jacobian. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a differential-drive robot moving at $v = 1$ m/s for $T = 20$ s with effector noise parameters $\sigma_v = 0.1$ m/s and $\sigma_\omega = 0.05$ rad/s, compute the **3$\sigma$ lateral drift** in position. Comment on its engineering implication. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Write the noisy motion model:

$$x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t + \varepsilon_t), \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Apply first-order Taylor expansion around the nominal state:

$$x_t \approx g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\cdot}(x_{t-1} - \hat{x}_{t-1}) + \frac{\partial g}{\partial u}\bigg\vert_{\cdot}\varepsilon_t$$

Let $F_x = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}$ and $F_\varepsilon = \frac{\partial g}{\partial u}$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute covariance using independence of $(x_{t-1} - \hat{x}_{t-1})$ and $\varepsilon_t$:

$$P_t = \mathrm{cov}(x_t) = F_x P_{t-1} F_x^T + F_\varepsilon R_t F_\varepsilon^T$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** For differential drive with $u = (v, \omega)$:

$$
F_x = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\sin\theta/\omega + v\sin(\theta+\omega\Delta t)/\omega \\ 0 & 1 & v\cos\theta/\omega - v\cos(\theta+\omega\Delta t)/\omega \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
F_\varepsilon = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\partial x}{\partial v} & \frac{\partial x}{\partial \omega} \\ \frac{\partial y}{\partial v} & \frac{\partial y}{\partial \omega} \\ 0 & \Delta t \end{pmatrix}
$$

with $R_t = \mathrm{diag}(\sigma_v^2, \sigma_\omega^2)$.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Drift Calculation (7 marks):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute heading noise growth:

$$
\sigma_\theta(T) = \sigma_\omega \sqrt{T} = 0.05 \cdot \sqrt{20} = 0.05 \cdot 4.472 = 0.2236 \ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute lateral drift:

$$
\sigma_{y,drift} = v \cdot T \cdot \sigma_\theta(T) = 1 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.2236 = 4.472 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute 3$\sigma$ envelope:

$$
D_{3\sigma} = 3 \cdot 4.472 = 13.42 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Engineering comment:

> This lateral drift of **~13.4 m** in just 20 seconds of straight-line travel is unacceptable for any practical navigation task. It justifies the mandatory use of **external sensor corrections** (GPS, LiDAR SLAM, visual odometry) at intervals much shorter than this drift time. Autonomous vehicles typically require sensor updates at **10–100 Hz** precisely to bound this drift.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **velocity motion model** for a differential-drive robot and show how effector noise propagates into the pose covariance using the proportional noise model $\sigma_v^2 = K_v \vert v \vert + \sigma_{v,0}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot has $K_v = 0.1$ m, $\sigma_{v,0} = 0.02$ m/s, $K_\omega = 0.05$ rad, $\sigma_{\omega,0} = 0.01$ rad/s. If it moves at $v = 2$ m/s and $\omega = 0.5$ rad/s for 5 seconds, compute the $2 \times 2$ effector noise covariance matrix $R_t$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

##### Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):

**Velocity motion model (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= x_{t-1} + \frac{v}{\omega}[\sin(\theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t) - \sin(\theta_{t-1})] \\
y_t &= y_{t-1} - \frac{v}{\omega}[\cos(\theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t) - \cos(\theta_{t-1})] \\
\theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proportional noise model explanation (4 marks):** The standard deviation of velocity noise scales linearly with commanded velocity because:
- At higher speeds, the **same slip fraction** produces larger absolute error.
- The constant term $\sigma_{v,0}$ captures **encoder resolution floors**.
- This model is empirically validated by Siegwart et al. and is widely used in EKF-SLAM implementations.

The resulting **R matrix** becomes *velocity-dependent*:

$$
R_t(v, \omega) = \begin{pmatrix} K_v \vert v \vert + \sigma_{v,0}^2 & 0 \\ 0 & K_\omega \vert v \vert + \sigma_{\omega,0}^2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

This makes the EKF **adaptive**: at higher speeds, the filter automatically trusts odometry *less*, which is physically correct.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute velocity variance:

$$
\sigma_v^2 = K_v \vert v \vert + \sigma_{v,0}^2 = 0.1 \cdot 2 + (0.02)^2 = 0.2 + 0.0004 = 0.2004 \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute rotational variance:

$$
\sigma_\omega^2 = K_\omega \vert v \vert + \sigma_{\omega,0}^2 = 0.05 \cdot 2 + (0.01)^2 = 0.1 + 0.0001 = 0.1001 \ \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Construct covariance matrix:

$$
R_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2004 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1001 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The units are $(m^2/s^2)$ and $(rad^2/s^2)$ respectively.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Effector Noise questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing sensor noise vs effector noise (–2 marks):** Effector noise = actuation side; sensor noise = perception side. Examiners deduct heavily for misuse.
> 2. **Forgetting units in $R_t$ (–1 to –2 marks):** $\sigma_v^2$ is in $\mathrm{m}^2/\mathrm{s}^2$, NOT just $m^2$. Always write units explicitly.
> 3. **Assuming independence between $v$ and $\omega$ noise when not stated (–1 mark):** If the problem says "correlated", you must include the off-diagonal $\rho \sigma_v \sigma_\omega$ term.
> 4. **Writing $P_t = P_{t-1} + R_t$ (–2 marks):** This is WRONG unless both $F_x = I$ and $F_\varepsilon = I$. The correct formula involves the Jacobians.
> 5. **Forgetting to comment on engineering implications (–1 mark):** Numerical answers without a "what does this mean for the robot?" comment lose marks.
> 6. **Using $\sigma$ instead of $\sigma^2$ in the covariance matrix (–1 mark):** Covariance matrices use **variances** ($\sigma^2$), not standard deviations.
> 7. **Not specifying Gaussian assumption (–0.5 marks):** Always state $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ when defining noise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effector Noise**

- ✅ **Definition:** Stochastic error in *actuation* (motors, wheels, gears), modelled as additive Gaussian noise $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.
- ✅ **Two types:** Systematic (calibratable, e.g., wheel mismatch) vs Non-systematic (random, e.g., slip).
- ✅ **Diagonal $R_t$ matrix:** $\mathrm{diag}(\sigma_v^2, \sigma_\omega^2)$ for differential drive.
- ✅ **Proportional model:** $\sigma_v^2 = K_v \vert v \vert + \sigma_{v,0}^2$ — variance grows with speed.
- ✅ **EKF update:** $P_t = F_x P_{t-1} F_x^T + F_\varepsilon R_t F_\varepsilon^T$ — **Jacobians are mandatory**.
- ✅ **Drift growth:** Lateral drift $\propto T^{3/2}$ for straight-line motion.
- ✅ **Heading noise:** $\sigma_\theta(T) = \sigma_\omega \sqrt{T}$ — square-root growth.
- ✅ **Engineering insight:** Larger $R_t$ → trust sensors more (good GPS); smaller $R_t$ → trust odometry more (wheel encoders).
- ✅ **Always state units** for $\sigma_v^2$ ($\mathrm{m^2/s^2}$) and $\sigma_\omega^2$ ($\mathrm{rad^2/s^2}$).
- ✅ **KTU keyword trigger words** to memorize: "covariance propagation", "Jacobian linearization", "proportional noise model", "dead reckoning drift", "Gaussian uncertainty".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Odometric Position Estimation** is the process of computing a mobile robot's pose $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, \theta)^T$ by integrating incremental wheel encoder readings over time. Because odometry relies on the integration of noisy incremental measurements, small instantaneous errors **accumulate without bound**, causing the estimated pose to diverge from the robot's true pose. The **Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation** is a mathematical framework that quantifies the **uncertainty (covariance)** in the estimated pose, propagated through the nonlinear kinematic update equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The error model is the *theoretical backbone* of probabilistic localization (EKF, Particle Filters). Without an accurate covariance model, sensor fusion in EKF/UKF localization becomes mathematically unjustified.

The two principal error categories defined by **Borenstein & Feng (1996)** are:

1. **Systematic (Deterministic) Errors** – caused by mechanical imperfections; they are predictable, repeatable, and can in principle be calibrated out.
2. **Non-Systematic (Stochastic) Errors** – caused by environmental interactions (wheel slip, floor bumps); they are random and must be modeled probabilistically.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine you are walking through a dense fog using a **pedometer** strapped to your belt and a **compass** held in your hand. Each step you take, the pedometer adds up your stride length, and the compass gives you a heading. If the pedometer is calibrated for a flat surface but the ground slopes uphill, every step is **slightly underestimated** (a *scale factor error*). If you press harder with your left foot, the *systematic* bias grows. Meanwhile, when you accidentally slip on a wet tile, an unpredictable error is injected (a *non-systematic* error).

To make sense of where you really are, you would carry an **invisible "uncertainty bubble"** that starts as a tiny dot at the starting point. With every step, the bubble grows — mostly along your travel direction (because length errors dominate) and a little sideways (because heading errors grow with distance). The **Error Model** is exactly the mathematical recipe that defines the size and shape of this "uncertainty bubble" at every step.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard deviation of a wheel encoder tick, typically **$\sigma_{\text{enc}} \approx 0.05$ rad to 0.1 rad**, combined with the wheel radius **$r$**, gives a position uncertainty of approximately **$\sigma_x \approx r \cdot \sigma_{\text{enc}}$ per tick**. The accumulated drift over a 10 m traverse is therefore on the order of **1–3%** of the total distance travelled.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Pose | $\mathbf{p}=(x,y,\theta)^T$ | Position and orientation of the robot |
| Control input | $\mathbf{u}=(\Delta s_R, \Delta s_L)^T$ | Incremental wheel displacements |
| Wheel base | $b$ | Distance between drive wheels |
| Wheel radius | $r$ | Radius of each drive wheel |
| Systematic error | $E_s$ | Deterministic, calibration-dependent |
| Non-systematic error | $E_n$ | Random, environment-dependent |
| Covariance matrix | $\Sigma_p$ | Uncertainty of pose $\mathbf{p}$ |
| Jacobian | $\nabla P$ | Linearization of nonlinear update |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Odometric Errors

#### A. Systematic Errors (Per Wheel / Per Robot)
- **Unequal wheel diameters** ($E_d$): manufacturing tolerance, tire pressure difference → scale mismatch between left and right wheels.
- **Misalignment of wheels** ($E_b$): the effective wheel base $b_{\text{eff}}$ differs from the nominal.
- **Finite encoder resolution** ($E_r$): quantization of rotation into discrete ticks.
- **Sampling rate limitation** ($E_s$): continuous motion is sampled at discrete times.

#### B. Non-Systematic Errors (Per Floor / Per Environment)
- **Wheel slippage** on wet or oily surfaces.
- **Floor roughness, carpet seams, bumps**.
- **External forces** (collision, payload shift).
- **Carpet and threshold traversal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two categories?** Systematic errors dominate in structured indoor environments and can be reduced via the **UMBmark calibration test**. Non-systematic errors dominate in unstructured outdoor or uneven terrain and require **probabilistic modeling** (Gaussian, Gaussian-sum, or non-parametric).

### 2.2 Kinematic Pose Update (Ideal, Error-Free)

For a differential-drive robot with control input $\mathbf{u} = (\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R)^T$:

$$
D_c = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}
\qquad
\gamma = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

$$
x' = x + D_c \cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\gamma}{2}\right)
$$

$$
y' = y + D_c \sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\gamma}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta' = \theta + \gamma
$$

Or in compact form: $\mathbf{p}' = f(\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{u})$.

### 2.3 The Linearized Error Propagation Model

Because $f(\cdot)$ is nonlinear, we linearize it about the current estimate using a **first-order Taylor expansion**:

$$
\Sigma_{p'} \;=\; \nabla P_{p}\,\Sigma_{p}\,\nabla P_{p}^{T} \;+\; \nabla P_{u}\,\Sigma_{u}\,\nabla P_{u}^{T}
$$

where:

- $\Sigma_{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$: pose covariance at the previous step.
- $\Sigma_{u} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$: control input covariance.
- $\nabla P_{p} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{p}} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$: pose Jacobian.
- $\nabla P_{u} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{u}} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$: input Jacobian.

### 2.4 The UMBmark Empirical Error Model (Borenstein & Feng)

The **Universal Method for Benchmarking and Error Reduction** in odometry expresses the total odometric error as:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{at position }(x,y)} = a \cdot \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}} + b \cdot \vert y \vert + c
$$

- $a$: scale-factor-dependent error (mainly from wheel diameter mismatch).
- $b$: orientation-dependent error (mainly from wheel-base mismatch).
- $c$: constant offset (encoder resolution, sampling).

The three constants are determined by regressing the model against measured square-trajectory data.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the most heavily tested set of equations for Module 2.**

| # | Formula | Variables / Meaning |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $D_c = \dfrac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$ | Center-of-axle distance travelled |
| 2 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$ | Rotation angle about the ICC |
| 3 | $x' = x + D_c \cos\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\gamma}{2}\right)$ | New x-coordinate |
| 4 | $y' = y + D_c \sin\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\gamma}{2}\right)$ | New y-coordinate |
| 5 | $\theta' = \theta + \gamma$ | New heading angle |
| 6 | $\Sigma_{p'} = \nabla P_{p} \Sigma_{p} \nabla P_{p}^{T} + \nabla P_{u} \Sigma_{u} \nabla P_{u}^{T}$ | EKF-style covariance update |
| 7 | $\nabla P_{u} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta' & \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta' \\ \frac{1}{2}\sin\theta' & \frac{1}{2}\sin\theta' \\ \frac{1}{b} & -\frac{1}{b} \end{pmatrix}$ | Input Jacobian |
| 8 | $\nabla P_{p} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -D_c \sin\theta' \\ 0 & 1 & D_c \cos\theta' \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Pose Jacobian |
| 9 | $\Sigma_{u} = \begin{pmatrix} k_{r}\vert\Delta s_R\vert & 0 \\ 0 & k_{l}\vert\Delta s_L\vert \end{pmatrix}$ | Error-proportional input covariance |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = a\sqrt{x^2+y^2} + b \vert y \vert + c$ | UMBmark empirical error |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EKF Localization**: The error model provides the *predicted* covariance $\bar{\Sigma}_k$ in the prediction step.
- **Particle Filters**: Each particle's noise injection is drawn from $\Sigma_{u}$.
- **Path Planning under Uncertainty**: The growing ellipse is used in *chance-constrained* planning.
- **SLAM Front-End**: Scan matching confidence is gated by the propagated covariance from odometry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Pose Update from Wheel Increments

We start from first principles. Let the robot move during a single time step. The **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)** lies on the wheel-base axis, perpendicular to the wheels.

**Step 1:** The two wheels trace arcs of radii $R + b/2$ and $R - b/2$, where $R$ is the signed radius of the robot's path. The arc lengths are $\Delta s_R$ and $\Delta s_L$, giving the *same* rotation angle $\gamma$:

$$
\gamma = \frac{\Delta s_R}{R + b/2} = \frac{\Delta s_L}{R - b/2}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $R$ and $\gamma$ simultaneously:

$$
R \cdot \gamma + \frac{b \gamma}{2} = \Delta s_R, \qquad R \cdot \gamma - \frac{b \gamma}{2} = \Delta s_L
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the two equations and solve for $\gamma$:

$$
b \gamma = \Delta s_R - \Delta s_L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \gamma = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

**Step 4:** Add the two equations and use $D_c = R\gamma$:

$$
2 R \gamma = \Delta s_R + \Delta s_L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D_c = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** The pose at $t+1$ is the old pose plus a translation of $D_c$ along the *mid-heading* $\theta + \gamma/2$:

$$
x' = x + D_c \cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\gamma}{2}\right)
$$

$$
y' = y + D_c \sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\gamma}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta' = \theta + \gamma
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearized Error Propagation (EKF-Style)

Let $\mathbf{p} = f(\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{u})$ with additive noise $\mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma_{u})$ on $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma_{p})$ on $\mathbf{p}$.

**Step 1:** Take a first-order Taylor expansion of $f$ about $(\mathbf{p}_0, \mathbf{u}_0)$:

$$
f(\mathbf{p}_0 + \delta\mathbf{p},\ \mathbf{u}_0 + \delta\mathbf{u}) \approx f(\mathbf{p}_0,\mathbf{u}_0) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{p}}\delta\mathbf{p} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\delta\mathbf{u}
$$

**Step 2:** Define the Jacobians:

$$
\nabla P_{p} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{p}}, \qquad \nabla P_{u} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{u}}
$$

**Step 3:** The perturbation is a linear combination of two independent Gaussian random vectors $\delta\mathbf{p}$ and $\delta\mathbf{u}$, so its covariance is (using the standard identity for a sum of independent random vectors):

$$
\Sigma_{p'} = \mathrm{E}\!\left[\left(\nabla P_{p}\delta\mathbf{p} + \nabla P_{u}\delta\mathbf{u}\right)\left(\nabla P_{p}\delta\mathbf{p} + \nabla P_{u}\delta\mathbf{u}\right)^{T}\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Cross-terms vanish by independence ($\mathrm{E}[\delta\mathbf{p}\,\delta\mathbf{u}^{T}] = 0$):

$$
\Sigma_{p'} = \nabla P_{p}\,\mathrm{E}[\delta\mathbf{p}\,\delta\mathbf{p}^{T}]\,\nabla P_{p}^{T} + \nabla P_{u}\,\mathrm{E}[\delta\mathbf{u}\,\delta\mathbf{u}^{T}]\,\nabla P_{u}^{T}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the covariance definitions:

$$
\Sigma_{p'} = \nabla P_{p}\,\Sigma_{p}\,\nabla P_{p}^{T} + \nabla P_{u}\,\Sigma_{u}\,\nabla P_{u}^{T}
$$

This is the **canonical odometric error propagation equation** used inside every EKF-based localization loop.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Computation of the Pose Jacobian $\nabla P_{p}$

We need $\partial f / \partial \mathbf{p}$ where $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, \theta)^T$.

**Step 1:** $x' = x + D_c \cos(\theta + \gamma/2)$. Differentiate w.r.t. $x$: $\partial x' / \partial x = 1$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$:

$$
\frac{\partial x'}{\partial \theta} = -D_c \sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\gamma}{2}\right) = -D_c \sin\theta'
$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, $\partial y' / \partial \theta = D_c \cos\theta'$ and $\partial y' / \partial y = 1$.

**Step 4:** $\theta' = \theta + \gamma$ is independent of $(x, y)$ and gives $\partial \theta'/\partial \theta = 1$.

**Step 5:** Assemble the matrix (noting that $\sin\theta' = \sin(\theta + \gamma/2)$ and $\cos\theta' = \cos(\theta + \gamma/2)$):

$$
\nabla P_{p} = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & -D_c \sin\theta' \\
0 & 1 & \;\;D_c \cos\theta' \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Computation of the Input Jacobian $\nabla P_{u}$

**Step 1:** $x' = x + D_c \cos\theta'$. With $D_c = (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)/2$:

$$
\frac{\partial x'}{\partial \Delta s_R} = \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta', \qquad \frac{\partial x'}{\partial \Delta s_L} = \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta'
$$

**Step 2:** For $y'$: $\partial y'/\partial \Delta s_R = \frac{1}{2}\sin\theta'$, similarly for $\Delta s_L$.

**Step 3:** For $\theta' = \theta + (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)/b$:

$$
\frac{\partial \theta'}{\partial \Delta s_R} = \frac{1}{b}, \qquad \frac{\partial \theta'}{\partial \Delta s_L} = -\frac{1}{b}
$$

**Step 4:** Assemble the matrix (columns correspond to $[\Delta s_R, \Delta s_L]$):

$$
\nabla P_{u} = \begin{pmatrix}
\frac{1}{2}\cos\theta' & \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta' \\
\frac{1}{2}\sin\theta' & \frac{1}{2}\sin\theta' \\
\frac{1}{b} & -\frac{1}{b}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — The Error-Proportional Input Covariance

A common KTU-style choice assumes that uncertainty is **proportional to the magnitude** of the motion (i.e., the more you move, the more you are unsure). A standard parameterization is:

$$
\Sigma_{u} = \begin{pmatrix}
k_{r}\vert\Delta s_R\vert & 0 \\
0 & k_{l}\vert\Delta s_L\vert
\end{pmatrix}
$$

where $k_r$ and $k_l$ are empirically determined constants that depend on the wheel-encoder resolution, the floor type, and the wheel slip coefficient.

**Step 1:** During a typical motion segment, $\Delta s_R \approx \Delta s_L \approx D_c$ (straight-line motion).

**Step 2:** Substitute into the propagation equation (assuming zero prior pose uncertainty $\Sigma_p = 0$ for clarity):

$$
\Sigma_{p'} = \nabla P_{u} \Sigma_{u} \nabla P_{u}^{T}
$$

**Step 3:** The position-variance term (top-left $2 \times 2$ block) simplifies to:

$$
\Sigma_{xy} = \frac{D_c}{4}\begin{pmatrix}
k_r+k_l & 0 \\
0 & k_r+k_l
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** The total position uncertainty (standard deviation) is:

$$
\sigma_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(k_r + k_l)\,D_c}
$$

This shows that uncertainty grows **as the square root of distance**, which is the canonical signature of random-walk (Brownian) noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** If the question asks "how does the position error grow with distance?", the answer is **$O(\sqrt{D_c})$ for non-systematic errors** (random-walk) and **$O(D_c)$ for systematic errors** (uncompensated bias).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Error Models

| Property | Systematic Error | Non-Systematic Error |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Nature | Deterministic | Stochastic |
| Source | Mechanical | Environmental |
| Growth with distance | Linear $O(D)$ | Square-root $O(\sqrt{D})$ |
| Modeling technique | Calibration (UMBmark) | Probability (Gaussian) |
| Repeatability | Yes | No |
| Compensation | Subtract bias term | Increase covariance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Odometric Error Sources

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Odometric Errors"] --> rootB["Systematic Errors"]
    rootA --> rootC["Non-Systematic Errors"]
    rootB --> b1["Wheel Diameter Mismatch"]
    rootB --> b2["Wheel Base Misalignment"]
    rootB --> b3["Encoder Quantization"]
    rootB --> b4["Sampling Period Limitation"]
    rootC --> c1["Wheel Slippage"]
    rootC --> c2["Floor Roughness"]
    rootC --> c3["Carpet Seams and Bumps"]
    rootC --> c4["External Collisions"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Error Propagation

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA["Prior Pose p_k"] --> inB["Jacobian nabla_P_p"]
    inC["Prior Covariance Sigma_p"] --> inB
    inB --> inD["Pose Update Term"]
    inE["Control Input u_k"] --> inF["Jacobian nabla_P_u"]
    inG["Input Covariance Sigma_u"] --> inF
    inF --> inH["Input Update Term"]
    inD --> inI["Sum Block"]
    inH --> inI
    inI --> inJ["Posterior Covariance Sigma_p'"]
    inI --> inK["Posterior Pose p'"]
```

### 4.3 Pose Uncertainty Evolution (Conceptual Ellipse Growth)

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1["Step 0: Point"] --> s2["Step 1: Small Ellipse"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 5: Elongated Ellipse"]
    s3 --> s4["Step 20: Large Elongated Ellipse"]
    s1 --- lab1["x,y 0,0"]
    s2 --- lab2["x,y ~0.1m"]
    s3 --- lab3["x,y ~0.5m"]
    s4 --- lab4["x,y ~2.0m"]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture of the Error Model Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    e1["Encoder Ticks"] --> e2["Compute Delta_s_R and Delta_s_L"]
    e2 --> e3["Compute D_c and gamma"]
    e3 --> e4["Compute New Pose p'"]
    e4 --> e5["Compute Jacobians nabla_P_p and nabla_P_u"]
    e5 --> e6["Compute New Covariance Sigma_p'"]
    e6 --> e7["Output to EKF Predict Step"]
    e4 --> e7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Mermaid compilation:** All node IDs are alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `s1`, `e2`). Labels contain only clean uppercase or alphanumeric text. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **systematic** and **non-systematic** odometric errors with two examples each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Systematic | Non-Systematic |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| Nature | Deterministic, repeatable | Random, environment-dependent |
| Causes | Wheel diameter mismatch, wheel-base misalignment, encoder resolution, finite sampling | Wheel slip, floor bumps, carpet traversal, external collisions |
| Growth with distance | Linear $O(D)$ | Square-root $O(\sqrt{D})$ |
| Compensation | Calibration, bias subtraction | Probabilistic (Gaussian) modeling |

*(1 mark for definition each = 2 marks; 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define the **UMBmark** calibration test and state the empirical error model used in it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**UMBmark (Universal Method for Benchmarking and Error Reduction)** is a calibration procedure by Borenstein & Feng (1996) in which the robot is commanded to traverse a closed **square path** (typically $4 \times 4$ m) **clockwise and counter-clockwise**, with the final position error measured. The empirical model regressed from this data is:

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; a \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}} \;+\; b \vert y \vert \;+\; c
$$

where $a$ captures scale-factor errors, $b$ captures wheel-base misalignment, and $c$ is a constant offset. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark procedure + 1 mark formula.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the **pose update equations** $x', y', \theta'$ for a differential-drive robot given wheel increments $\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R$ and wheel base $b$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Develop the **first-order linearized error-propagation model** for odometry. State the Jacobians explicitly. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The derivation is shown in **Section 3.1** above and yields:

$$
D_c = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2},\qquad \gamma = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

$$
x' = x + D_c\cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\gamma}{2}\right)
$$

$$
y' = y + D_c\sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\gamma}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta' = \theta + \gamma
$$

*Mark split:* ICC introduction (2), derivation of $\gamma$ (2), derivation of $D_c$ (1), pose update (2).

**(b)** Per Section 3.2 and Section 3.3, the model is:

$$
\Sigma_{p'} = \nabla P_{p}\,\Sigma_{p}\,\nabla P_{p}^{T} + \nabla P_{u}\,\Sigma_{u}\,\nabla P_{u}^{T}
$$

$$
\nabla P_{p} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -D_c \sin\theta' \\ 0 & 1 & D_c \cos\theta' \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\nabla P_{u} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta' & \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta' \\ \frac{1}{2}\sin\theta' & \frac{1}{2}\sin\theta' \\ \frac{1}{b} & -\frac{1}{b} \end{pmatrix}
$$

with the error-proportional input covariance $\Sigma_u = \mathrm{diag}(k_r \vert \Delta s_R \vert,\ k_l \vert \Delta s_L \vert)$.

*Mark split:* Taylor expansion (2), covariance of linear combination (1), final formula (2), pose Jacobian (1), input Jacobian (1).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **sources of systematic and non-systematic errors** in odometry. Explain how the **UMBmark** method reduces systematic errors. (7 marks)

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel base $b = 0.5$ m, wheel radius $r = 0.1$ m, and moves with $\Delta s_L = 0.20$ m and $\Delta s_R = 0.22$ m at heading $\theta = 30°$. Compute the new pose and the propagated pose covariance. Use $k_r = k_l = 0.01$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Systematic sources: unequal wheel diameters, wheel-base misalignment, encoder quantization, sampling period. Non-systematic sources: wheel slip, floor roughness, bumps, external forces. The UMBmark test commands the robot to traverse a $4 \times 4$ m square bidirectionally, measures the final position errors, and regresses the constants $(a, b, c)$ in the empirical model $\varepsilon = a\sqrt{x^2+y^2} + b \vert y \vert + c$. These constants are then used as bias compensations in the encoder readings. *(3 marks for sources, 2 marks for UMBmark procedure, 2 marks for empirical model.)*

**(b) Numerical computation:**

**Step 1:** Compute the linear and angular increments:

$$
D_c = \frac{0.20 + 0.22}{2} = 0.21 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\gamma = \frac{0.22 - 0.20}{0.5} = 0.04 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2:** New heading:

$$
\theta' = 30° + 0.04 \text{ rad} = 30° + 2.29° = 32.29° \approx 0.5637 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3:** New position (with $\theta + \gamma/2 = 30° + 1.15° = 31.15°$):

$$
x' = 0 + 0.21 \cos(31.15°) = 0.21 \times 0.8556 = 0.1797 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y' = 0 + 0.21 \sin(31.15°) = 0.21 \times 0.5176 = 0.1087 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Input covariance:

$$
\Sigma_{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.01 \times 0.22 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \times 0.20 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0022 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0020 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Input Jacobian at $\theta' \approx 0.5637$ rad ($\cos \approx 0.8465$, $\sin \approx 0.5324$):

$$
\nabla P_{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4233 & 0.4233 \\ 0.2662 & 0.2662 \\ 2.0 & -2.0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the term $\nabla P_{u} \Sigma_{u} \nabla P_{u}^{T}$ (with $\Sigma_p = 0$ assumed for one step):

Row 1: $(0.4233)^2(0.0022+0.0020) = 0.1792 \times 0.0042 = 7.52 \times 10^{-4}$
Row 2: $(0.2662)^2(0.0022+0.0020) = 0.0709 \times 0.0042 = 2.98 \times 10^{-4}$
Row 3: $(2.0)^2(0.0022+0.0020) = 4.0 \times 0.0042 = 0.0168$

Off-diagonals: $0.4233 \times 0.2662 \times 0.0042 = 4.73 \times 10^{-4}$ for $\sigma_{xy}$ and $\sigma_{yx}$, $\pm 0.0084$ for $\sigma_{x\theta}, \sigma_{y\theta}$.

$$
\Sigma_{p'} \approx \begin{pmatrix}
7.52 \times 10^{-4} & 4.73 \times 10^{-4} & \pm 0.0084 \\
4.73 \times 10^{-4} & 2.98 \times 10^{-4} & \pm 0.0053 \\
\pm 0.0084 & \pm 0.0053 & 0.0168
\end{pmatrix} \text{ m}^2
$$

*Mark split:* Computing $D_c, \gamma$ (2), new pose (2), $\Sigma_u$ (1), $\nabla P_u$ (1), final covariance (1).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\gamma/2$ term** in $\cos(\theta + \gamma/2)$ and $\sin(\theta + \gamma/2)$ — this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta s_L$ and $\Delta s_R$ sign** in $\gamma$ — always right minus left, divided by $b$.
> 3. **Mixing up units**: $\gamma$ is in **radians** in the formula, but the exam may give $\theta$ in degrees. Convert first.
> 4. **Treating the covariance update as additive of $\Sigma_p$ and $\Sigma_u$** without the Jacobians. This is wrong — they must be **sandwiched** by the Jacobians.
> 5. **Omitting the off-diagonal terms** in the resulting covariance matrix. Always show the full symmetric $3 \times 3$ matrix.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two error classes**: *Systematic* (deterministic, $O(D)$ growth) vs *Non-systematic* (stochastic, $O(\sqrt{D})$ growth).
- **Pose update formula** uses the **mid-heading** $\theta + \gamma/2$, not the old or new heading alone.
- **Differential drive** is the canonical platform for this derivation; car-like (Ackermann) platforms have a different update (instantaneous steering angle).
- **Error propagation** uses the **EKF linearization** (first-order Taylor expansion). Higher-order terms are neglected — valid only for small increments.
- **Jacobians** are evaluated at the **predicted** new pose $(\mathbf{p}', \mathbf{u}_0)$, not at the old pose.
- **$\Sigma_u$ is motion-proportional** in most KTU-style problems: $\Sigma_u = \mathrm{diag}(k_r \vert \Delta s_R \vert, k_l \vert \Delta s_L \vert)$.
- **UMBmark** is the de-facto standard for systematic error calibration.
- The **uncertainty ellipse** grows predominantly along the heading direction because $\partial x / \partial \theta$ and $\partial y / \partial \theta$ are scaled by $D_c$ (distance already travelled).
- The **model is the core of EKF prediction step**: $\hat{\mathbf{p}}_{k \vert k-1} = f(\hat{\mathbf{p}}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_k)$ and $\Sigma_{k \vert k-1} = \nabla P_{p}\Sigma_{k-1}\nabla P_{p}^{T} + \nabla P_{u} \Sigma_{u} \nabla P_{u}^{T}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"M-J-P"** → **M**id-heading, **J**acobians, **P**ropagated covariance. Master these three, and you will score full marks on any 14-mark derivation question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Localization-Based Navigation** is a reactive, sensor-driven paradigm in mobile robotics wherein the robot continuously estimates its own pose (position and orientation) within a known or partially known reference frame, and dynamically computes its motion commands based on this real-time state estimate. The robot does **not** rely on a pre-recorded, deterministic trajectory; instead, it answers the question *"Where am I right now?"* at every control cycle using onboard sensors such as LIDAR, wheel odometry, IMUs, cameras, or GPS receivers.

**Programmed Solutions (Programmed/Map-Based Navigation)**, in contrast, is a deterministic, open-loop (or weakly closed-loop) paradigm in which the robot's motion is governed by a pre-defined sequence of waypoints, joint angles, or path segments that are taught, scripted, or computed offline. The controller executes a stored trajectory and assumes that the environment is static and that the executed motion matches the commanded motion perfectly. Examples include teach-pendant trajectories in industrial manipulators, AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) line-following, and waypoint navigation on a static cost map.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The comparison between these two paradigms is critical for understanding *when* a robot needs "intelligence" (localization) versus "obedience" (pre-programmed execution). The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on architectural trade-offs, sensor fusion cost, and environmental robustness.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a tourist in a foreign city:

- **Programmed Solutions** is like following a *handwritten itinerary* your friend gave you: "Walk 200 meters, take the second left, then the third right, enter the blue building." If a road is closed, a one-way sign appears, or you miscount a step, you are completely lost.

- **Localization-Based Navigation** is like using *Google Maps with live GPS*: the app continuously updates your blue dot, reroutes you around traffic, and even corrects your mistakes. You still have a destination (goal), but the path is *recomputed* every second based on where you actually are.

> [!NOTE]
> A **programmed robot** is a *blind follower of a script*. A **localization-based robot** is a *blind-folded explorer with a compass* — it constantly asks "Am I here? Then I should go that way."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

- **Pose (x, y, θ)**: The 2D position $(x, y)$ and heading angle $\theta$ of the robot in a global reference frame.
- **Waypoint**: A pre-defined discrete target location $(x_i, y_i)$ stored in memory.
- **Belief / State Estimate $\xi_t$**: The robot's internal probabilistic representation of its pose at time $t$, typically $\xi_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ in Gaussian filters, or a particle set $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ in Monte Carlo methods.
- **Odometry Drift**: The cumulative error in dead-reckoning estimates, growing without bound.
- **Update Rate / Hz**: The frequency at which the localization module produces a new pose estimate, typically **10 Hz – 100 Hz** for mobile robots.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Programmed Solutions

The programmed paradigm operates in a strict **Sense → No-Localize → Act** cycle (often open-loop):

1. **Path Pre-Definition**: A trajectory $\tau = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_N\}$ is generated offline using either manual teaching, spline interpolation, or graph search on a static map. For a differential-drive mobile robot, each $q_i = (x_i, y_i, \theta_i)$.
2. **Execution via Tracking Controller**: A low-level controller (typically a PID or pure-pursuit controller) generates velocity commands $v(t), \omega(t)$ to minimize the cross-track and heading error to the nearest point on the reference path.
3. **Termination by Arrival**: The robot halts or transitions to the next waypoint when $\| p_{\text{robot}} - p_{\text{goal}} \| < \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is a position tolerance, typically **0.05 m – 0.20 m**.
4. **No Feedback on Global Pose**: The system does not correct for slip, drift, or environmental changes because it has no independent measurement of its true global pose.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Localization-Based Navigation

The localization paradigm operates in a **Perceive → Localize → Plan → Act (PLPA)** cycle:

1. **Perceive**: Sensor suite (LIDAR scan $\mathbf{z}_t$, wheel odometry $u_t$, IMU reading $a_t$) captures a snapshot of the world and the robot's internal motion.
2. **Localize**: A Bayesian filter updates the belief $bel(x_t)$ about the robot's pose. The recursive update is:
   - **Prediction step**: Propagate belief using the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$.
   - **Correction step**: Multiply by the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ and renormalize.
3. **Plan**: A local (or global) planner generates a velocity or path command conditioned on the **current** estimated pose.
4. **Act**: Low-level controller drives motors. The cycle then repeats, typically at **20 Hz – 50 Hz**.

### 2.3 Why Each Step Matters

- **Why prediction?** Because we need to anticipate where the robot is *likely* to be based on its own commands, before correcting with sensors.
- **Why correction?** Sensors are noisy; prediction drifts; correction ties belief back to the real world.
- **Why replan every cycle?** Because the robot's *actual* pose may differ from the planned one, and obstacles may have moved.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Formula | Boundary Conditions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Pursuit Curvature | $\kappa = \dfrac{2 \sin \alpha}{L_d}$ | Lookahead distance $L_d > 0$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| Odometry Update (Differential Drive) | $x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | Non-holonomic, planar motion | m, rad |
| Odometry Update (Orientation) | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta\theta$ | Wrapped to $(-\pi, \pi]$ | rad |
| Euclidean Goal-Arrival Test | $\Vert p - p_{\text{goal}} \Vert < \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon \in [0.05, 0.20]$ | m |
| Bayes Filter Posterior | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \displaystyle\int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ | $\eta = 1 / p(z_t \mid u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1})$ | probability (unitless) |
| EKF Linearization (Jacobian) | $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$, $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ | Local linearization valid | unitless |
| EKF Covariance Update | $P_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{P}_t$ | $K_t = \bar{P}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{P}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $\text{m}^2$ |
| Particle Filter Resampling | Effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Resample when $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$ | particles |
| Drift Error Bound (Dead Reckoning) | $\sigma_x^2(t) \propto k^2 \cdot v^2 \cdot t$ | $k$ = error coefficient per meter | $\text{m}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations are the backbone of every autonomous car (Waymo uses EKF + particle hybrids), every warehouse robot (Amazon Kiva uses Monte Carlo localization), and every drone (visual-inertial odometry with EKF). Programmed solutions, by contrast, dominate industrial assembly lines where the world is engineered to be *predictable*.

### 2.5 Trade-off Matrix at a Glance

| Property | Programmed Solutions | Localization-Based Navigation |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time adaptation | **None** | **Continuous** |
| Sensor dependency | **Minimal** (encoders only) | **High** (LIDAR, cameras, IMU) |
| Computational cost | **Low** | **High** (filter + planner) |
| Robust to environment change | **Poor** | **Good** |
| Initial cost | **Low** | **High** |
| Failure mode | Silent divergence | Graceful uncertainty growth |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Odometry Update for a Differential-Drive Robot

A differential-drive robot has two independently driven wheels. Let $v_R$ and $v_L$ be the right and left wheel linear velocities, and $b$ the wheelbase.

**Step 1: Express incremental arc lengths.**

$$
\Delta s_R = v_R \, \Delta t \qquad \Delta s_L = v_L \, \Delta t
$$

These are the linear distances rolled by each wheel during one control tick $\Delta t$.

**Step 2: Compute the arc center and change in heading.**

For a circular arc traced when $v_R \neq v_L$, the change in orientation $\Delta\theta$ and the average forward displacement $\Delta s$ are:

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} \qquad \Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}
$$

**Why?** The difference in wheel rotation causes rotation about the ICC (Instantaneous Center of Curvature); the sum gives the arc length traveled by the robot's center.

**Step 3: Update the global pose using the midpoint heading.**

To avoid bias, the pose update uses the **midpoint orientation** $\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \, \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \, \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical illustration.**

Let $b = 0.5$ m, $v_R = 0.30$ m/s, $v_L = 0.20$ m/s, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, starting at $(0, 0, 0)$.

$$
\Delta s_R = 0.03 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta s_L = 0.02 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{0.03 - 0.02}{0.5} = 0.02 \text{ rad}, \quad \Delta s = \frac{0.03 + 0.02}{2} = 0.025 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= 0 + 0.025 \cdot \cos(0 + 0.01) \approx 0.02499 \text{ m} \\
y_{t+1} &= 0 + 0.025 \cdot \sin(0.01) \approx 0.00025 \text{ m} \\
\theta_{t+1} &= 0 + 0.02 = 0.02 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

This incremental odometry is the **only feedback** a programmed solution uses — hence the unbounded drift.

### 3.2 Derivation: EKF Localization Linearization

The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) is the workhorse of localization-based navigation. Derive the Jacobian used in the prediction step.

**Step 1: Define the nonlinear motion model $g(x_{t-1}, u_t)$.**

For a differential-drive robot with control $u_t = (v_t, \omega_t)$ and pose $x = (x, y, \theta)^T$:

$$
g(x, u) = \begin{pmatrix} x + v \cos\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ y + v \sin\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ \theta + \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Jacobian $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x}$.**

Take partial derivatives of each row with respect to $(x, y, \theta)$:

$$
G_t = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \sin\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 & \phantom{-}v \cos\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Linearize the covariance propagation.**

The covariance prediction is:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) \qquad \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^T + R_t
$$

where $R_t$ is the process noise covariance (encoders + unmodeled slip).

**Step 4: Measurement update.**

For a range-bearing sensor observing landmark $j$ at known location $(m_{j,x}, m_{j,y})$:

$$
h(x) = \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{j,x} - x)^2 + (m_{j,y} - y)^2} \\ \operatorname{atan2}(m_{j,y} - y,\; m_{j,x} - x) - \theta \end{pmatrix}
$$

The Jacobian $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ is evaluated at $\bar{\mu}_t$ and the Kalman gain is:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

The corrected estimate is:

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)) \qquad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Criteria for Choosing a Paradigm

| Application Scenario | Recommended Paradigm | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive assembly welding arm | **Programmed** | Static cell, repeatable sub-millimeter path |
| Hospital delivery robot | **Localization-based** | Dynamic humans, furniture may move |
| Warehouse AGV with magnetic tape | **Programmed** | Tape is a *physical* program, no sensors needed |
| Autonomous car on city streets | **Localization-based** | Dynamic traffic, unmapped construction zones |
| CNC milling | **Programmed** | Fully deterministic, closed-loop at joint level only |
| Planetary rover (Mars) | **Localization-based** | Slip on sand, unknown terrain, no GPS |
| Toy line-follower | **Programmed** | Single IR sensor, fixed track |
| Surgical robot (Da Vinci) | **Programmed + Localization** | Hybrid: pre-op plan + intra-op fiducial tracking |

> [!NOTE]
> The deeper the **environmental uncertainty**, the more we must shift from *programmed* to *localization-based*. KTU examiners love asking students to justify a paradigm choice for a given scenario.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Programmed Solutions Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Offline Path Planner / Teach Pendant] --> B[Stored Trajectory Waypoints]
    B --> C[Trajectory Tracker PID / Pure Pursuit]
    C --> D[Velocity Commands v omega]
    D --> E[Motor Driver + Encoders]
    E --> F[Robot Body]
    F -.->|Implicit feedback only| C
    F --> G[End Effector / Wheels]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The dashed line from F back to C is the *only* feedback in a programmed solution — and it is the wheel's *intended* motion, not the robot's *actual* global pose. This is the architectural root of drift.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Localization-Based Navigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[LIDAR Scan] --> P[Perception Module]
    S2[Camera / IMU] --> P
    S3[Wheel Encoders] --> P
    P --> L[Bayesian Localizer EKF or Particle Filter]
    MAP[Stored Map] --> L
    L --> EST[Pose Estimate x y theta + Covariance]
    EST --> PL[Local Planner DWA or MPC]
    GOAL[Goal Pose] --> PL
    PL --> C[Velocity Controller]
    C --> A[Motor Driver]
    A --> R[Robot Body]
    R --> S3
    R --> S1
    R --> S2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice the *closed loop from the robot body back to the sensors* — this is what makes the system reactive. The **stored map** is consulted *only* to validate the localizer's hypothesis, not to drive motion directly.

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROGRAMMED[Programmed Solutions]
        P1[Teach / Script] --> P2[Open-loop Execution]
        P2 --> P3[Robot]
    end
    subgraph LOCALIZED[Localization-Based Navigation]
        L1[Sensor Stream] --> L2[Bayes Filter]
        L2 --> L3[Pose Belief]
        L3 --> L4[Re-planner]
        L4 --> L5[Closed-loop Execution]
        L5 --> L6[Robot]
        L6 -.-> L1
    end
    P3 -.->|Environment changes ignored| FAIL[Silent Failure / Collision]
    L6 -.->|Continuous correction| OK[Safe Operation]
```

### 4.4 State Machine: Switching Logic Between Paradigms

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Startup
    Startup --> LocalizationInit: Load map + sensors ready
    Startup --> ProgrammedExec: No map + static env
    LocalizationInit --> Tracking: Pose uncertainty low
    Tracking --> ReLocalize: Uncertainty exceeds threshold
    ReLocalize --> Tracking: Pose recovered
    ProgrammedExec --> Abort: Sensor detects obstacle
    Abort --> LocalizationInit: Switch to safe fallback
    Tracking --> GoalReached
    ProgrammedExec --> GoalReached
    GoalReached --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Modern industrial systems (e.g., autonomous forklifts) use a **hybrid** state machine: they cruise on a programmed waypoint path but *localize continuously* as a safety overlay. This is an excellent exam answer for "compare and justify" questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between localization-based navigation and programmed solutions in mobile robots. Give one real-world example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Localization-based navigation* uses real-time sensor measurements to continuously estimate the robot's pose and replans its motion accordingly. Example: An autonomous vacuum cleaner (e.g., Roomba) using LIDAR and SLAM to map and clean a dynamic living room.
- *Programmed solutions* rely on a pre-defined, stored trajectory that is executed without global feedback. Example: A car-assembly welding robot following a taught path between fixed car chassis coordinates.
- *Key distinction (1 mark)*: Programmed = open-loop deterministic script; Localization = closed-loop sensor-driven adaptive system.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is odometry drift, and why is it a critical limitation of programmed navigation solutions?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Odometry drift is the **unbounded accumulation of error** in a robot's estimated pose when using only wheel encoder feedback. Each integration step adds a small error (wheel slip, encoder quantization, wheel diameter mismatch) and these errors integrate over time.
- The drift variance grows approximately as $\sigma_x^2(t) \propto k^2 v^2 t$ (1 mark).
- It is a *critical* limitation of programmed solutions because such systems have **no global correction** mechanism — once the error exceeds the path-tolerance band, the robot can collide with obstacles or miss the goal entirely (1 mark).
- Solution: add localization via external sensors (1 mark).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a localization-based navigation system. Clearly show the perception, localization, planning, and control modules, and explain how feedback is closed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheelbase $b = 0.40$ m. The right and left wheel velocities are $v_R = 0.50$ m/s and $v_L = 0.40$ m/s respectively, sampled at $\Delta t = 0.05$ s. Starting at pose $(0, 0, 0)$, compute the new pose after one step using the midpoint-integration odometry model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the four-module flow: Sensors → Perception → Localizer (EKF) → Planner (DWA) → Controller (PID) → Motors → Body, with the *closed feedback loop* from body back to sensors. Reference the diagram in Section 4.2.
2. **Perception module (1 mark):** Fuses LIDAR scans, wheel odometry, IMU into a unified observation vector $\mathbf{z}_t$.
3. **Localization module (1 mark):** Implements the recursive Bayes update: prediction $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ followed by measurement correction $p(z_t \mid x_t)$.
4. **Planning and control (2 marks):** Local planner (e.g., Dynamic Window Approach) computes collision-free velocity $(v, \omega)$; PID controller tracks it. The loop closes at 20–50 Hz, making the system reactive to disturbances.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_R &= 0.50 \times 0.05 = 0.025 \text{ m} \\
\Delta s_L &= 0.40 \times 0.05 = 0.020 \text{ m} \\
\Delta \theta &= \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{0.025 - 0.020}{0.40} = 0.0125 \text{ rad} \\
\Delta s &= \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} = \frac{0.025 + 0.020}{2} = 0.0225 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the midpoint-integration update with initial pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 + 0.0225 \cdot \cos\!\left(0 + \frac{0.0125}{2}\right) \\
    &= 0.0225 \cdot \cos(0.00625) \\
    &\approx 0.0225 \cdot 0.99998 \\
    &\approx 0.02250 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= 0 + 0.0225 \cdot \sin(0.00625) \\
    &\approx 0.0225 \cdot 0.00625 \\
    &\approx 0.000141 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 0 + 0.0125 = 0.0125 \text{ rad} \approx 0.716^\circ
$$

**Final pose: $(0.0225, \; 0.000141, \; 0.0125 \text{ rad})$.**
(Allocate marks: 2 for wheel arc, 2 for $\Delta\theta$ and $\Delta s$, 3 for final pose computation.)

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast localization-based navigation with programmed solutions under the following heads: (i) sensor dependency, (ii) robustness to environmental change, (iii) computational cost, (iv) failure mode, (v) typical application, and (vi) accuracy over long missions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) prediction step for a differential-drive robot. State clearly the motion model, the Jacobian $G_t$, and the covariance update equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Head | Programmed | Localization-Based |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Sensor dependency | Encoders only | LIDAR, camera, IMU, encoders |
| (ii) Robustness | Poor — silent drift | Good — belief tracks reality |
| (iii) Computational cost | Low (single PID) | High (filter + planner at 20 Hz) |
| (iv) Failure mode | Silent divergence / collision | Graceful uncertainty growth; recovery via relocalization |
| (v) Typical application | Industrial welding, CNC, AGV tape-followers | Autonomous cars, drones, service robots |
| (vi) Accuracy over long missions | **Degrades linearly** with distance ($\sigma \propto t$) | **Bounded** by sensor noise and map quality |

(1 mark per head for clear contrast; 1 mark for overall presentation.)

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Motion model (2 marks).** The nonlinear state transition $g$ for a differential-drive robot with control $u_t = (v_t, \omega_t)$ is:

$$
g(x, u) = \begin{pmatrix} x + v\cos\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ y + v\sin\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ \theta + \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Jacobian $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}}$ (3 marks):**

$$
G_t = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \sin\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 & \phantom{-}v \cos\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Evaluate at $\theta = \mu_{t-1}^{(\theta)}$ for linearization.

**Step 3: Covariance prediction (2 marks):**

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) \qquad \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^T + R_t
$$

where $R_t$ is the process noise covariance accounting for wheel slip and unmodeled dynamics.

This completes the EKF prediction step. The correction step (using $H_t$, $K_t$) follows but is not required for this sub-question.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing odometry with localization.** Odometry is *prediction-only* (open-loop integration of wheel motion). Localization *fuses* odometry with absolute sensor measurements. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for using these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping the midpoint heading** in differential-drive odometry. Using $\theta_t$ instead of $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$ introduces a systematic bias. Always show the full substitution.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers. KTU evaluators often deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit (m, rad, m/s, s).
> 4. **Not drawing feedback arrows** in block diagrams. A localization diagram *without* a closed loop from "Body" back to "Sensors" is structurally incomplete and loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Stating "EKF is a Bayesian filter" without showing the Bayes posterior equation.** Always write $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Programmed navigation** = stored trajectory + low-level tracking; **no global pose feedback**; cheap but fragile.
- **Localization-based navigation** = sensor-driven Bayes filter + replanner; **closed-loop on global pose**; expensive but robust.
- **Odometry drift** grows unbounded: $\sigma^2 \propto t$ — this is the *fundamental* reason pure programmed solutions fail on long missions.
- **Midpoint-integration update** (use $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$) is mandatory for differential-drive pose updates to avoid systematic bias.
- **EKF prediction**: $G_t = \partial g / \partial x$ is evaluated at the current mean; covariance grows as $G_t \Sigma G_t^T + R_t$.
- **Particle filters** (Monte Carlo Localization) are non-parametric and handle multimodal beliefs (e.g., global kidnapped-robot recovery); EKF is unimodal Gaussian.
- **Bayes filter posterior** is the *single most important equation* in this module — memorize the integral form.
- **Hybrid systems** (programmed path + localization safety overlay) are the industrial norm and earn full marks in justification-type questions.
- **Decision rule of thumb**: Static + known environment → Programmed. Dynamic + partially known → Localization-based. Unstructured outdoor → Localization with SLAM.
- **Always close the feedback loop** in your block diagrams — open-loop diagrams in localization questions are an automatic partial-fail.
- **Pure pursuit curvature** $\kappa = 2 \sin\alpha / L_d$ is the workhorse tracker for waypoint-following; remember that $L_d$ is the lookahead distance, not the wheelbase.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis**: marks are awarded for *justified* paradigm choice — never say "localization is better"; say "for this dynamic, partially observable environment, localization-based SLAM is justified because…"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Belief Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Belief Representation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Belief Representation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > In probabilistic robotics, a **belief** is the robot's internal, probabilistic representation of knowledge about its own state and the state of the environment, given all sensor measurements and control actions executed up to the current time step $t$. It is mathematically expressed as a **posterior probability distribution** over the state space. Formally, the belief about state $x_t$ at time $t$, conditioned on the history of measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$, is defined as: $$bel(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t},\, u_{1:t})$$ The associated *prediction* (or *prior* belief) is defined as: $$\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1},\, u_{1:t})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology (High-Yield for KTU):** > - **State $x_t$** — The true (hidden) configuration of robot + environment. *Not directly observable.* > - **Measurement $z_t$** — Sensor reading (noisy, partial). > - **Control $u_t$** — Actuator command (noisy, uncertain). > - **Belief $bel(x_t)$** — Robot's *posterior* estimate after incorporating $z_t$. > - **Prediction $\overline{bel}(x_t)$** — Robot's *prior* estimate *before* incorporating $z_t$. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Imagine a person (the robot) trapped in a large dark warehouse. They cannot see the walls. Every few seconds they fire a sonar ping (sensor) and they remember every step they have taken (control). The belief is **not** "I am at coordinates (12, 7)" — that would be a single guess. Instead, the belief is **"I am 10% likely to be here, 30% likely to be there, 60% likely to be over here"** — a *cloud* of probabilities over the entire map. | Robot's Reality | Belief Equivalent | |-----------------|-------------------| | Person blindfolded in dark room | State $x_t$ is hidden | | Sonar ping echoes | Measurement $z_t$ | | Steps taken (stumble, slide) | Control $u_t$ with noise | | Mental map of likely locations | Probability distribution $bel(x_t)$ | The robot's belief must satisfy two fundamental probability axioms: 1. **Non-negativity:** $bel(x_t) \geq 0$ for all $x_t$ 2. **Normalization:** $\int bel(x_t) \, dx_t = 1$ > [!TIP] > **Why use beliefs instead of single estimates?** Real sensors are noisy, real actuators slip, real maps are partially known. A belief **quantifies uncertainty** rather than ignoring it, which is what enables robust autonomous navigation under uncertainty. --- ### 1.3 Why Belief Representation Matters in KTU Syllabus The choice of belief representation is the **single most important design decision** in any mobile robot navigation system. It directly determines: - **Computational complexity** (Kalman filter is $O(k^2)$ vs. particle filter is $O(N)$). - **Type of uncertainty handled** (unimodal Gaussian vs. multi-modal sample cloud). - **Scalability** to high-dimensional state spaces. - **Robustness** to data association errors and environment ambiguities. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Theorem (Probabilistic Robotics, Thrun et al.):** *No single belief representation is universally optimal.* The selection depends on the structure of the environment, sensor characteristics, and real-time computational budgets. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation: The Bayes Filter The belief is recursively updated by the **Bayes Filter** algorithm, which is the *theoretical backbone* of nearly every belief representation in KTU's prescribed syllabus. **Step 1 — Prediction (Motion Update):** Propagate belief through the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$. $$\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid u_t,\, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 2 — Measurement Update (Correction):** Incorporate the latest sensor reading $z_t$ via the measurement model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$. $$bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$$ where $\eta$ is the **Bayes normalizer**: $$\eta \;=\; \left[ \int p(z_t \mid x_t') \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t') \, dx_t' \right]^{-1}$$ --- ### 2.2 Taxonomy of Belief Representations (KTU High-Yield) | # | Representation | Mathematical Form | Strength | Weakness | |---|----------------|-------------------|----------|----------| | 1 | **Gaussian (Kalman)** | $bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | Closed-form, fast ($O(k^2)$) | Unimodal, linear-Gaussian only | | 2 | **Grid / Histogram** | $bel(x_t) = \{p_i\}_{i=1..N}$ | Arbitrary distributions, easy | Exponential in dimension | | 3 | **Particle (Sample-based)** | $bel(x_t) \approx \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})$ | Multi-modal, non-parametric | Sample depletion, $O(M)$ | | 4 | **Topological** | Graph $G = (V, E)$ with node beliefs | Compact, global | Low metric precision | | 5 | **Hybrid** | Local metric + global topological | Best of both worlds | Complex implementation | | 6 | **Evidential (Dempster–Shafer)** | Mass function $m: 2^{\Theta} \to [0,1]$ | Distinguishes uncertainty from ignorance | Combinatorial cost | --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS** | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---------|---------|---------------| | Belief (posterior) | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Probability density | | Prediction (prior) | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Marginalization | | Bayes update | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $\eta$ = normalizer | | Kalman prediction (mean) | $\overline{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | Vector $k \times 1$ | | Kalman prediction (cov.) | $\overline{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | Matrix $k \times k$ | | Kalman gain | $K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $k \times n$ | | Kalman update (mean) | $\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t)$ | Innovation term | | Kalman update (cov.) | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t$ | Symmetric pos. def. | | Particle resampling | Effective $N_{eff} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$ | Resample if $N_{eff} < N/2$ | | Dempster–Shafer mass | $m: 2^{\Theta} \to [0,1]$, $m(\emptyset)=0$, $\sum m(A)=1$ | Power set | | Belief function | $\mathrm{Bel}(A) = \sum_{B \subseteq A} m(B)$ | Lower probability | | Plausibility function | $\mathrm{Pl}(A) = 1 - \mathrm{Bel}(\overline{A}) = \sum_{B \cap A \neq \emptyset} m(B)$ | Upper probability | | Dempster's rule | $(m_1 \oplus m_2)(A) = \frac{1}{1-K}\sum_{B \cap C = A} m_1(B) m_2(C)$ | $K$ = conflict | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Note:** In the Dempster–Shafer framework, $\mathrm{Bel}(A) \leq P(A) \leq \mathrm{Pl}(A)$. The interval $[\mathrm{Bel}(A), \mathrm{Pl}(A)]$ represents the **epistemic uncertainty** (ignorance). When $m(\Theta) = 0$, Dempster–Shafer collapses to Bayesian probability. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Multi-hypothesis particle filter trackers maintain beliefs over pedestrian and vehicle trajectories. - **Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva):** Kalman-based SLAM estimates robot pose in known maps. - **Underwater AUVs:** Evidential grid maps handle noisy sonar returns by distinguishing "free," "occupied," and "unknown." - **Aerial Drones:** Gaussian beliefs for visual-inertial odometry (VIO). - **Planetary Rovers (NASA Mars rovers):** Evidential representations handle ambiguous terrain traversability. --- ### 2.5 Belief State vs. Information State > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Information State):** A sufficient statistic of history $(z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ for predicting the future. The belief $bel(x_t)$ is the most common information state in mobile robotics. Any function $I_t = f(z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ that is sufficient is a valid information state. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Bayes Filter from First Principles We start from the definition of posterior probability and apply the conditional independence Markov assumption used throughout the KTU syllabus. **Goal:** Express $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ recursively in terms of $bel(x_{t-1})$. **Step 1 — Definition expansion** by the definition of conditional probability: $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Markov assumption:** $z_t$ depends only on $x_t$ (not on the full history): $$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(z_t \mid x_t)$$ **Step 3 — Expand the numerator term** $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ via the law of total probability, marginalizing over $x_{t-1}$: $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Markov assumption to the state transition:** $x_t$ depends only on $x_{t-1}$ and $u_t$: $$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and recognize the recursion:** $$bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \underbrace{\int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}_{\overline{bel}(x_t)}$$ **Step 6 — Final form (Bayes Filter):** $$\boxed{\;bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)\;}$$ with the normalizer $$\eta \;=\; \left[ \int p(z_t \mid x_t') \, \overline{bel}(x_t') \, dx_t' \right]^{-1} \;=\; p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})^{-1}$$ This is the **fundamental recurrence** on which Kalman filters, particle filters, and grid filters are all built. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Kalman Filter Update Equations Assume the belief is Gaussian: $bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. **Linear-Gaussian motion model:** $$x_t \;=\; A_t \, x_{t-1} \;+\; B_t \, u_t \;+\; \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$ **Linear-Gaussian measurement model:** $$z_t \;=\; C_t \, x_t \;+\; \delta_t, \qquad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$ **Step 1 — Prediction of mean:** Take the expectation of $x_t$ given the previous belief: $$\overline{\mu}_t \;=\; \mathbb{E}[A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t] \;=\; A_t \, \mu_{t-1} \;+\; B_t \, u_t$$ (The noise mean is zero and the control is known.) **Step 2 — Prediction of covariance:** Compute $\mathrm{Var}(x_t)$: $$\overline{\Sigma}_t \;=\; \mathrm{Var}(A_t x_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t) \;=\; A_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, A_t^T \;+\; R_t$$ **Step 3 — Joint distribution of $(x_t, z_t)$ given $\overline{\mu}_t, \overline{\Sigma}_t$:** $$p(x_t, z_t) \;=\; \mathcal{N}\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} \overline{\mu}_t \\ C_t \overline{\mu}_t \end{bmatrix}, \; \begin{bmatrix} \overline{\Sigma}_t & \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T \\ C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t & C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t \end{bmatrix} \right)$$ **Step 4 — Condition on the actual measurement $z_t$:** Using the standard Gaussian conditioning formula: $$p(x_t \mid z_t) \;=\; \mathcal{N}\!\left( \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t), \; (I - K_t C_t)\overline{\Sigma}_t \right)$$ where the **Kalman gain** is: $$K_t \;=\; \overline{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^T \, \left( C_t \, \overline{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^T + Q_t \right)^{-1}$$ **Step 5 — Final posterior belief parameters:** $$\boxed{\; \mu_t \;=\; \overline{\mu}_t \;+\; K_t \, (z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t) \;}$$ $$\boxed{\; \Sigma_t \;=\; (I - K_t C_t) \, \overline{\Sigma}_t \;}$$ > [!TIP] > **Intuition for Kalman Gain $K_t$:** > - If $Q_t \to 0$ (perfect sensor): $K_t \to C_t^{-1}$ → trust measurement fully. > - If $R_t \to 0$ (perfect motion model): $K_t \to 0$ → trust prediction fully. > - $K_t$ is the **optimal weight** balancing prediction and measurement uncertainty. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Belief Representations | Property | Kalman | Grid | Particle | Topological | Dempster–Shafer | |----------|--------|------|----------|-------------|-----------------| | **Representation** | $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ | $N$-dim array | Set of weighted samples | Graph $G=(V,E)$ | Mass function $m$ | | **Multi-modal?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Non-linear?** | ❌ (needs EKF/UKF) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Computational cost** | $O(k^2)$ | $O(N^d)$ | $O(M)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(2^{\vert\Theta\vert})$ | | **Closed-form?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | | **Handles ignorance?** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Yes | | **Best for** | Indoor SLAM | Occupancy maps | Global localization | Large-scale maps | Sensor fusion under conflict | | **KTU 3-mark weight** | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Kalman 1D Position Tracking) A robot moves in 1D. Motion model: $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \varepsilon_t$, $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.5^2)$. Measurement: $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$, $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1.0^2)$. Prior: $\mu_0 = 0, \Sigma_0 = 1.0$. Control: $u_1 = 1.0$. Measurement: $z_1 = 1.6$. **Prediction:** $$\overline{\mu}_1 \;=\; \mu_0 + u_1 \;=\; 0 + 1.0 \;=\; 1.0$$ $$\overline{\Sigma}_1 \;=\; \Sigma_0 + R_1 \;=\; 1.0 + 0.25 \;=\; 1.25$$ **Kalman gain:** $$K_1 \;=\; \frac{\overline{\Sigma}_1}{\overline{\Sigma}_1 + Q_1} \;=\; \frac{1.25}{1.25 + 1.0} \;=\; 0.5556$$ **Update:** $$\mu_1 \;=\; \overline{\mu}_1 + K_1 (z_1 - \overline{\mu}_1) \;=\; 1.0 + 0.5556(1.6 - 1.0) \;=\; 1.0 + 0.3333 \;=\; 1.3333$$ $$\Sigma_1 \;=\; (1 - K_1)\overline{\Sigma}_1 \;=\; (1 - 0.5556)(1.25) \;=\; 0.5556$$ **Result:** Belief is $\mathcal{N}(1.3333, 0.5556)$. Note the posterior variance ($0.5556$) is *smaller* than either the prediction variance ($1.25$) or the measurement variance ($1.0$) — this is the **sensor fusion gain** that makes Bayesian estimation powerful. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Taxonomy of Belief Representation Methods ```mermaid graph TD A[Belief Representation Methods] --> B[Parametric] A --> C[Non-Parametric] A --> D[Evidential] B --> B1[Kalman Filter
