- The **Jacobians** in EKF must be evaluated at the **current** state estimate $\hat{x}_{t-1}$ (motion) and $\hat{x}_t^{-}$ (measurement).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Landmark-Based Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Landmark-Based Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landmark-Based Navigation (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> *Landmark-Based Navigation* is a localization and motion-planning paradigm in mobile robotics wherein a robot estimates its pose (position $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \theta]^T$) by detecting, extracting, and matching distinctive, stationary environmental features — called **landmarks** — against a prior or incrementally built map. The mathematical foundation rests on the **sensor measurement model**:
> $$z_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t, m_i) + v_t$$
> where $z_t$ is the observation of landmark $m_i$ from robot pose $\mathbf{x}_t$, $h(\cdot)$ is the nonlinear measurement function, and $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ is Gaussian measurement noise with covariance $R_t$.

**Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Glossary):**

| Term | Notation | Engineering Meaning |
|------|----------|---------------------|
| Landmark | $m_i$ | A uniquely identifiable, persistent environmental feature with known geometric/signature properties |
| Observation | $z_t$ | Sensor measurement (range, bearing, image descriptor) corresponding to landmark $m_i$ |
| Data Association | $\mathcal{D}$ | The process of correctly mapping observed features to stored map landmarks |
| Pose Estimate | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t$ | Robot's belief of its configuration in a global reference frame |
| Map | $\mathcal{M} = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n\}$ | A set of $n$ landmarks with known positions |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Pirate's Chest" Treasure Hunt**
> Imagine you are a pirate searching for buried treasure in unfamiliar territory. You do **not** have a GPS, but you carry an old treasure map. The map highlights specific, distinctive objects: *the bent coconut tree, the three-peaked rock, the white lighthouse*. These are your **landmarks**. As you walk, you scan the horizon until you spot one. The moment you see "the three-peaked rock," you immediately know your position relative to the treasure (because the map tells you the rock's location and the distance/bearing to the chest from the rock). The robot does exactly the same — it uses unique environmental signatures (corners, edges, beacons, reflector poles) as anchors to compute its global position.

### 1.3 Why Landmark-Based Navigation Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight**
> - **Wheel odometry drift** accumulates unbounded error ($\sim$10\% of distance traveled). Landmark observations provide **absolute corrections** that bound this error to a finite steady-state covariance.
> - Landmark-based methods form the perceptual backbone of **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**.
> - Modern autonomous vehicles (e.g., **Tesla FSD, Waymo Driver**) rely on visual landmark features (ORB, SIFT, SuperPoint) for HD-map-relative localization.
> - The **maximum effective range** of landmarks determines the operational envelope. Typical values: **ultrasonic beacons $\leq$ 10 m, UWB RF beacons $\leq$ 50 m, visual landmarks $\leq$ 100 m, LiDAR landmarks $\leq$ 200 m**.

### 1.4 Classification of Landmarks (KTU Taxonomy)

Landmarks are broadly classified into two engineering families:

**A. Natural Landmarks (Passive Features)**
- Corners, edges, and junctions in indoor environments
- Tree trunks, building facades, road markings
- Extracted via feature detectors (Harris, SIFT, SURF, ORB)

**B. Artificial Landmarks (Active Beacons)**
- Retro-reflective markers (used in **Vicon/OptiTrack** motion-capture systems)
- **RFID tags** embedded in floors
- **AprilTags / ArUco markers** — fiducial markers with binary-encoded IDs
- **UWB transmitters** with known time-of-flight
- **Active IR beacons** for indoor positioning

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "differentiate between natural and artificial landmarks," always mention the trade-off: natural landmarks are *ubiquitous but harder to detect robustly*, while artificial landmarks are *easy to detect but require prior installation*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Landmark Navigation Pipeline (Structured Logic)

The complete operational chain decomposes into **six sequential stages**:

1. **Perception Stage** — Raw sensor data acquisition (camera, LiDAR, sonar, UWB).
2. **Feature Extraction Stage** — Detect distinctive keypoints from raw data using a detector–descriptor pair.
3. **Data Association Stage** — Match observed features to stored map landmarks.
4. **Pose Estimation Stage** — Compute robot pose from matched landmark observations.
5. **State Update Stage** — Fuse the pose estimate with odometry using a Bayesian filter (EKF/Particle).
6. **Map Maintenance Stage** — Add new landmarks or prune spurious ones.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation — The Measurement Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Geometry of Bearing-Range Observation**
> For a robot at pose $\mathbf{x}_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t]^T$ observing a 2-D landmark at $m_i = [m_{i,x}, m_{i,y}]^T$, the **range $r$** and **bearing $\phi$** are:
> $$r_{i,t} = \sqrt{(m_{i,x} - x_t)^2 + (m_{i,y} - y_t)^2}$$
> $$\phi_{i,t} = \text{atan2}(m_{i,y} - y_t, m_{i,x} - x_t) - \theta_t$$
> The **observation vector** is therefore:
> $$z_{i,t} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{i,t} \\ \phi_{i,t} \end{bmatrix} + v_t, \quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why range-bearing form?** It is the **canonical 2-D sensor model** used in KTU board questions. It maps the nonlinear geometry into a linearizable Jacobian.
- **Why Gaussian noise?** The **Central Limit Theorem** justifies Gaussian noise for additive sensor errors; this enables closed-form EKF updates.
- **Why atan2 and not atan?** Because $\text{atan2}$ preserves quadrant information — critical when the landmark is in the second or third quadrant relative to the robot.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Table (Save This for Last-Minute Revision)**

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Range observation | $r = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ | meters (m) |
| 2 | Bearing observation | $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$ | radians (rad) |
| 3 | Measurement Jacobian $H_t$ | $\frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{\Delta x}{r} & -\frac{\Delta y}{r} & 0 \\ \frac{\Delta y}{r^2} & -\frac{\Delta x}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Dimensionless, $2 \times 3$ |
| 4 | EKF Innovation (Residual) | $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_{t\vert t-1}$ | $2 \times 1$ vector |
| 5 | Innovation Covariance | $S_t = H_t P_{t\vert t-1} H_t^T + R_t$ | $2 \times 2$ positive definite |
| 6 | Kalman Gain | $K_t = P_{t\vert t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1}$ | $3 \times 2$ |
| 7 | Posterior Covariance Update | $P_{t\vert t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t\vert t-1}$ | $3 \times 3$ |
| 8 | Mahalanobis Distance (gating) | $d_M^2 = \nu_t^T S_t^{-1} \nu_t$ | Scalar, threshold $\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.99$ |
| 9 | Triangulation (2 landmarks) | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T z$ | Closed-form least squares |
| 10 | Reprojection Error | $e = \vert \vert z - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}, m) \vert \vert$ | Minimized in bundle adjustment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safeguard:** Throughout these notes, the absolute value and determinant symbols are written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert z \vert$, $\vert \vert v \vert \vert$) to prevent markdown table syntax conflicts.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Driving:** Tesla's vision-based localization uses lane-line and curb landmarks with a high-definition prior map.
- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva):** Floor-embedded **2-D barcodes** serve as artificial landmarks with sub-centimeter accuracy.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Visual SLAM with rock formations as natural landmarks enables long-range traverse.
- **Indoor Drones (DJI Mavic):** Visual-inertial odometry with AprilTag fiducials for precision landing.
- **Surgical Robotics:** Reflective spheres on instruments tracked by stereo cameras (calibrated landmarks).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: EKF Landmark Update (The "Heart" of Landmark Navigation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite — Full Derivation of the EKF Update Step**
> *This is a guaranteed 7-8 mark question. Memorize the transition logic.*

**Given:**
- Predicted state: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t-1} = [\hat{x}, \hat{y}, \hat{\theta}]^T$
- Predicted covariance: $P_{t\vert t-1}$ (a $3 \times 3$ matrix)
- Landmark map: $m_i = [m_{i,x}, m_{i,y}]^T$ (known a priori)
- New observation: $z_t = [r_t, \phi_t]^T$

**Step 1 — Compute the predicted measurement** $\hat{z}_{t\vert t-1}$:
$$\hat{z}_{t\vert t-1} = h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t-1}, m_i) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{i,x} - \hat{x})^2 + (m_{i,y} - \hat{y})^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{i,y} - \hat{y}, m_{i,x} - \hat{x}) - \hat{\theta} \end{bmatrix}$$

This step transforms the abstract state into a predicted sensor-space observation.

**Step 2 — Compute the measurement Jacobian** $H_t$ by partial differentiation of $h$ with respect to $\mathbf{x}$:

Let $\Delta x = m_{i,x} - \hat{x}$, $\Delta y = m_{i,y} - \hat{y}$, and $q = (\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 = r^2$.

Partial derivative of $r$ w.r.t. $\hat{x}$:
$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial \hat{x}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{q}} \cdot (-2\Delta x) = -\frac{\Delta x}{r}$$

Partial derivative of $r$ w.r.t. $\hat{y}$:
$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\frac{\Delta y}{r}$$

Partial derivative of $r$ w.r.t. $\hat{\theta}$:
$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial \hat{\theta}} = 0$$

Partial derivative of $\phi$ w.r.t. $\hat{x}$:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \hat{x}} = \frac{1}{1 + (\Delta y / \Delta x)^2} \cdot \left( -\frac{-\Delta y}{(\Delta x)^2} \right) = \frac{\Delta y}{r^2}$$

Partial derivative of $\phi$ w.r.t. $\hat{y}$:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\frac{\Delta x}{r^2}$$

Partial derivative of $\phi$ w.r.t. $\hat{\theta}$:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \hat{\theta}} = -1$$

Therefore, the full Jacobian matrix is:
$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{\Delta x}{r} & -\dfrac{\Delta y}{r} & 0 \\[10pt] \dfrac{\Delta y}{r^2} & -\dfrac{\Delta x}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the innovation (measurement residual)** $\nu_t$:
$$\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_{t\vert t-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the innovation covariance** $S_t$:
$$S_t = H_t P_{t\vert t-1} H_t^T + R_t$$

This is a $2 \times 2$ matrix that captures the combined uncertainty from prediction and measurement.

**Step 5 — Compute the Kalman gain** $K_t$:
$$K_t = P_{t\vert t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Update the state estimate**:
$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t-1} + K_t \nu_t$$

**Step 7 — Update the posterior covariance**:
$$P_{t\vert t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t\vert t-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Property:** For a stationary landmark observed repeatedly, $P_{t\vert t}$ converges to a steady-state covariance $P_\infty$, indicating bounded localization error. This is the **theoretical guarantee** that landmark-based navigation does not drift like dead-reckoning.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Landmark Sensing Modalities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Table — Memorize for Comparison Questions**

| Modality | Typical Range | Accuracy | Strength | Weakness |
|----------|--------------|----------|----------|----------|
| **Ultrasonic Beacon** | $\leq$ 10 m | $\pm$ 5 cm | Low cost | Slow, specular reflections |
| **RFID Tag** | $\leq$ 1 m (passive) | $\pm$ 10 cm | No batteries in tag | Very short range |
| **UWB Beacon** | $\leq$ 50 m | $\pm$ 10 cm | Penetrates walls | Expensive infrastructure |
| **Visual (AprilTag)** | $\leq$ 30 m | $\pm$ 1 cm @ 1 m | Open-source, low cost | Lighting-sensitive |
| **LiDAR Reflector** | $\leq$ 100 m | $\pm$ 1 cm | All-weather, high accuracy | Retro-reflector installation |
| **GPS (outdoor)** | Global | $\pm$ 2 m | No infrastructure | Not for indoor |
| **Visual SIFT/SURF** | $\leq$ 100 m | $\pm$ 0.5 m | Natural landmarks | Computationally expensive |

### 3.3 Numerical Example — Single Landmark EKF Update (Worked Out)

**Given:** Robot prior $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t-1} = [0, 0, 0]^T$ (m, m, rad), prior covariance $P_{t\vert t-1} = 0.5 \cdot I_{3 \times 3}$, landmark at $m_1 = [5, 0]^T$, observation $z_t = [4.9, 0.05]^T$ (m, rad), measurement noise $R_t = \text{diag}(0.1, 0.01)$.

**Step 1:** $\Delta x = 5$, $\Delta y = 0$, $r = 5$.
$\hat{z} = [5, 0]^T$.

**Step 2:** Compute $H_t$:
$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -5/5 & -0/5 & 0 \\ 0/25 & -5/25 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** $\nu_t = [4.9 - 5, 0.05 - 0]^T = [-0.1, 0.05]^T$.

**Step 4:** $S_t = H_t P H_t^T + R_t = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.2 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix}$

$H_t P = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$

$H_t P H_t^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.2 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.02 + 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.52 \end{bmatrix}$

$S_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.53 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 5:** $S_t^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.667 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.887 \end{bmatrix}$

$K_t = P H_t^T S_t^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.2 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.667 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.887 \end{bmatrix}$

$= \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.1 \\ 0 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.667 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.887 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.833 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.189 \\ 0 & -0.944 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 6:** $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t} = [0,0,0]^T + K_t [-0.1, 0.05]^T = [0.0833, -0.00945, -0.0472]^T$

**Step 7:** $P_{t\vert t} = (I - K_t H_t) P = (I - \begin{bmatrix} 0.833 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0378 & 0.189 \\ 0 & 0.189 & 0.528 \end{bmatrix}) \cdot 0.5 I$

This gives the updated state $\hat{x} = 0.083$ m, $\hat{y} = -0.009$ m, $\hat{\theta} = -0.047$ rad — a refined estimate closer to the true pose after incorporating the landmark measurement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Landmark-Based Navigation Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Pose x_t prior] --> B[Predict Measurement z_hat]
    C[Landmark Map m_i] --> B
    B --> D[Compute Innovation nu_t = z - z_hat]
    E[Raw Sensor z_t] --> D
    D --> F[Innovation Covariance S_t]
    F --> G[Kalman Gain K_t]
    A --> G
    G --> H[Update Pose x_t posterior]
    G --> I[Update Covariance P_t]
    J[Data Association] --> K{Match Found?}
    K -->|Yes| E
    K -->|No| L[Add New Landmark to Map]
    L --> M[Updated Map M]
    M --> C
    I --> N[Robot Pose Belief]
    H --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The left branch handles the **prediction step** (using prior state and map), the center branch handles the **measurement step** (raw sensor input), and the right branch manages the **map update**. The dotted line from "Data Association" to "Match Found?" represents the **gating logic** (Mahalanobis test).

### 4.2 Mermaid Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`A`, `B`, `C`... or descriptive names like `A1`, `B2`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node names.
- All node labels are inside double-quotes with **no markdown formatting tags** (no `**bold**`, no `*italics*`, no HTML tables).

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Landmark Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sensor Data] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Detector]
    C --> D[Feature Descriptor]
    D --> E[Data Association]
    F[Stored Map Landmarks] --> E
    E --> G{Innovation Gate}
    G -->|Accept| H[Pose Update]
    G -->|Reject| I[Discard / Reinitialize]
    H --> J[Robot Pose Estimate]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: Choosing a Landmark Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to Choose Landmark Type] --> B{Environment}
    B -->|Indoor Structured| C{Need Long Range?}
    B -->|Outdoor Natural| D{Lighting Stable?}
    C -->|Yes| E[UWB Beacons]
    C -->|No| F{AprilTags vs RFID}
    F -->|> 1 m range| G[AprilTag / Fiducial]
    F -->|< 1 m range| H[Passive RFID]
    D -->|Yes| I[SIFT / SURF Visual]
    D -->|No| J[LiDAR Reflectors]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Define a landmark in the context of robot navigation. List any four desirable properties of a good landmark.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A landmark is a distinctive, stationary environmental feature whose position is known a priori (or learned online) and which can be reliably detected by the robot's sensors to provide a measurement for pose estimation. **Desirable properties:** (1) *Uniqueness* — easy to distinguish from other features; (2) *Stability* — does not move over time; (3) *Detectability* — robust to lighting/weather changes; (4) *Sparsity* — well-separated in space to avoid data association ambiguity.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Differentiate between natural and artificial landmarks with two examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Natural landmarks are pre-existing environmental features not installed for navigation (e.g., **tree trunks, building corners**). Artificial landmarks are deliberately placed markers for robot localization (e.g., **AprilTags, retro-reflectors**). Natural landmarks are ubiquitous but harder to detect robustly; artificial landmarks are easy to detect but require prior installation and maintenance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2 Choice Question]**
> *(a)* Derive the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) update equations for a 2-D mobile robot localizing against a single known landmark using a range-bearing sensor model. Clearly state the measurement model and the Jacobian matrix $H_t$. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A robot with prior estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = [0, 0, 0]^T$ and prior covariance $P = 0.5 I_3$ observes a landmark at $(5, 0)$ with measurement $z = [4.9, 0.05]^T$ and noise $R = \text{diag}(0.1, 0.01)$. Compute the updated state and covariance after one EKF step. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
- **1 mark** for stating the range-bearing measurement model: $r = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$, $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$.
- **2 marks** for writing the innovation: $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_{t\vert t-1}$ and innovation covariance $S_t = H_t P_{t\vert t-1} H_t^T + R_t$.
- **2 marks** for deriving the Jacobian $H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\Delta x / r & -\Delta y / r & 0 \\ \Delta y / r^2 & -\Delta x / r^2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ with full differentiation steps.
- **2 marks** for the final state and covariance updates: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t-1} + K_t \nu_t$ and $P_{t\vert t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t\vert t-1}$, where $K_t = P_{t\vert t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
- **1 mark** for $\hat{z} = [5, 0]^T$ and $\Delta x = 5$, $\Delta y = 0$, $r = 5$.
- **1 mark** for $H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- **1 mark** for $\nu_t = [-0.1, 0.05]^T$.
- **2 marks** for $S_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.53 \end{bmatrix}$ and $K_t = \begin{bmatrix} -0.833 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.189 \\ 0 & -0.944 \end{bmatrix}$.
- **1 mark** for $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t} = [0.0833, -0.00945, -0.0472]^T$.
- **1 mark** for $P_{t\vert t}$ updated matrix (show the $(I - K_t H_t) P$ computation).

> **Q4. [ALTERNATIVE — Internal Choice Option B, 14 Marks]**
> *(a)* Explain with a block diagram the complete pipeline of a landmark-based navigation system. Discuss the role of data association and the Mahalanobis distance gating test. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Compare natural and artificial landmarks across five engineering parameters. Justify which type is preferred in (i) a Mars rover mission and (ii) an Amazon warehouse robot application. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pipeline + Data Association (7 marks):**
- **3 marks** for the block diagram with six stages: Perception → Feature Extraction → Data Association → Pose Estimation → State Update → Map Maintenance.
- **2 marks** for explaining data association as the correspondence problem between observed features and stored map landmarks, and its vulnerability to perceptual aliasing.
- **2 marks** for the Mahalanobis gating test: $d_M^2 = \nu_t^T S_t^{-1} \nu_t < \chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.99$, with explanation of how it rejects spurious matches.

**(b) Comparison + Justification (7 marks):**
- **3 marks** for the comparison table (range, accuracy, cost, robustness, installation effort).
- **2 marks** for Mars rover: *natural landmarks* preferred because no infrastructure can be pre-installed on Mars, and visual SLAM with rock formations is the only viable option.
- **2 marks** for Amazon warehouse: *artificial landmarks* (2-D barcodes on floor) preferred because the warehouse is a controlled environment, requires sub-centimeter accuracy, and infrastructure installation is cheap.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign error in $H_t$:** The derivative $\partial \phi / \partial \hat{x}$ must equal $+\Delta y / r^2$, not $-\Delta y / r^2$. Memorize the sign pattern: the off-diagonal entries have **opposite signs**.
> 2. **Forgetting angle wrapping:** If $\nu_\phi$ falls outside $(-\pi, \pi]$, normalize it via $\nu_\phi = \text{atan2}(\sin \nu_\phi, \cos \nu_\phi)$. Failing to do so loses 1 full mark.
> 3. **Confusing range and bearing Jacobians:** The first row of $H_t$ is for range (units: m/m = dimensionless), the second row is for bearing (units: 1/m and 1). Mixing them up is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Singularity at $r = 0$:** When the robot is exactly at the landmark, $1/r$ and $1/r^2$ blow up. Always add a **minimum-range guard** (e.g., $r_{\min} = 0.1$ m) in code. Theoretically, mention this as an assumption.
> 5. **Wrong matrix dimensions:** $P$ is $3 \times 3$, $H$ is $2 \times 3$, $R$ is $2 \times 2$, $K$ is $3 \times 2$, $S$ is $2 \times 2$. Carrying forward wrong dimensions loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This to Your Wall!)**
>
> ✅ **Core Idea:** Landmarks are stationary features with known positions used to bound the drift of dead-reckoning odometry.
>
> ✅ **Two Families:** Natural (corners, trees) vs Artificial (AprilTags, UWB beacons, RFID).
>
> ✅ **Range-Bearing Model:** $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$, $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$ — this is the **most-tested equation** in KTU.
>
> ✅ **Jacobian $H_t$:** Must contain the derivatives w.r.t. $x$, $y$, and $\theta$. Off-diagonals have **opposite signs**.
>
> ✅ **EKF Steps (in order):** Innovation → Innovation Covariance → Kalman Gain → State Update → Covariance Update.
>
> ✅ **Mahalanobis Gating Threshold:** $\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.99$ for 2-D observations at 95% confidence.
>
> ✅ **Drift Bound:** Unlike odometry, landmark-based localization has a **bounded steady-state error** $P_\infty$.
>
> ✅ **Data Association is the Hardest Sub-problem:** Perceptual aliasing causes catastrophic mis-associations; use gating + RANSAC.
>
> ✅ **Triangulation:** Two or more landmarks enable closed-form pose recovery via $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T z$.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Trade-off:** Artificial landmarks give $\pm$ 1 cm accuracy but need installation; natural landmarks are free but $\pm$ 0.5 m.
>
> ✅ **Real-World Systems:** Mars rovers (natural), Amazon Kiva (2-D barcodes), Tesla (visual lane landmarks), surgical robots (IR reflective spheres).
>
> ✅ **Examiner Pet Question:** "Why is the EKF preferred over a linear KF for landmark navigation?" — Answer: The measurement model is **nonlinear** in $(x, y, \theta)$, so we linearize via the Jacobian $H_t$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion in Potential Field and Navigation Function - Problem Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion in Potential Field and Navigation Function - Problem Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Robot Motion Planning**, the **Artificial Potential Field (APF) method** is a reactive navigation strategy in which the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ of the robot is treated as a **differentiable scalar field** $U : \mathcal{C}_{free} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$. The field is composed of:

1. An **attractive component** $U_{att}(q)$ generated by the goal configuration $q_{goal}$, which produces a force pulling the robot toward the target.
2. A **repulsive component** $U_{rep}(q)$ generated by the obstacles $\mathcal{O}_i$, which produces a force pushing the robot away from collision regions.

The total potential is given by:
$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$$

The **induced force** (or negative gradient) acting on the robot is:
$$F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

The robot then executes a gradient-descent trajectory $\dot{q} = -\nabla U(q)$ until it reaches the goal (where $U_{att} = 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigation Function (Koditschek & Rimon, 1992):** A *Navigation Function* is a special class of potential function $\phi : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ defined on a compact connected admissible manifold $\mathcal{F}$ with a single interior goal $q_d$ such that:
> 1. $\phi$ is **analytic** (or at least $C^2$ smooth),
> 2. $\phi$ has a **unique global minimum at $q_d$** with $\phi(q_d) = 0$,
> 3. $\phi$ is a **Morse function** (non-degenerate critical points),
> 4. $\phi$ is **admissible**, meaning $\phi \equiv 1$ on the boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}$.

A navigation function *guarantees almost-global asymptotic stability* of the goal — eliminating the local-minima problem of classical potential fields.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Marble Rolling on a Hilly Surface:**
> Imagine the robot is a small marble placed on a smooth terrain. The **goal** is represented as the deepest valley (lowest point) of the landscape, while **obstacles** are represented as tall, steep mountains that the marble must avoid. The total height of the terrain at any point is the **potential** $U(q)$.
>
> When released, the marble naturally rolls "downhill" along the steepest descent direction — this is exactly $-\nabla U(q)$. It avoids mountains (obstacles) automatically and stops only at the lowest valley (the goal). 
>
> The *problem*? Real terrains can have small **puddles or dimples** (local minima) where the marble gets stuck — even though the actual valley (the goal) is still somewhere else. The **Navigation Function** approach designs the terrain artificially so that *no such dimples exist*, except at the goal.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\xi$ | Attractive gain | N/m (or unitless weight) |
| $\eta$ | Repulsive gain | N·m² (or unitless weight) |
| $\rho_0$ | Influence distance of obstacle | meters (m) |
| $\rho(q)$ | Distance from $q$ to obstacle surface | meters (m) |
| $r_i$ | Radius of $i^{th}$ spherical obstacle | meters (m) |
| $k$ | Tuning exponent in navigation function | $k \geq 1$ (integer) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Construction

#### **Step 1 — Define the Attractive Potential**
The attractive potential must vanish at the goal $q_{goal}$ and increase monotonically with distance. Two common forms are used:

- **Quadratic form (used near the goal):**
$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$$
where $\rho_{goal}(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$.

- **Conic form (used far from the goal to avoid excessive force):**
$$U_{att}(q) = \xi\,\rho_{goal}(q)$$

> **Why this works:** The gradient (force) of the quadratic form is **linear in distance**, providing smooth convergence; the conic form is **constant magnitude** for distant points, preventing instability.

#### **Step 2 — Define the Repulsive Potential**
For each obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$ with boundary distance $\rho_i(q)$, the repulsive potential is:
$$U_{rep,i}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho_i(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2} & \text{if } \rho_i(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[2mm] 0 & \text{if } \rho_i(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

The total repulsive potential is the sum over all obstacles:
$$U_{rep}(q) = \sum_{i=0}^{M} U_{rep,i}(q)$$

> **Why this works:** As $\rho_i(q) \rightarrow 0$ (collision imminent), $U_{rep} \rightarrow \infty$, producing an infinitely large repulsive force. Beyond $\rho_0$, the obstacle has no influence, so the robot moves purely under the attractive pull.

#### **Step 3 — Compute the Force (Gradient Descent)**
The total force at configuration $q$ is:
$$F(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

The robot's velocity in configuration space is:
$$\dot{q} = -\,F(q) = \nabla U_{att}(q) + \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

#### **Step 4 — Identify the Local Minima Problem**
A **local minimum** is a configuration $q^*$ where $\nabla U(q^*) = 0$ but $q^* \neq q_{goal}$. The robot gets trapped at $q^*$. This is the **GNRON problem** (*Goal Non-Reachable with Obstacles Nearby*).

#### **Step 5 — Construct a Navigation Function (Solution)**
For a **sphere world** (a compact region $\mathcal{F}$ containing the goal and bounded by $M$ spherical obstacles), the navigation function is:
$$\phi(q) = \frac{\gamma_d(q)}{\Bigl(\gamma_d^{k}(q) + \beta(q)\Bigr)^{1/k}}$$

where:
- $\gamma_d(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}$ (squared distance to goal),
- $\beta(q) = \displaystyle\prod_{i=0}^{M} \beta_i(q)$,
- $\beta_i(q) = \Vert q - c_i \Vert^{2} - r_i^{2}$ (zero on the surface of the $i^{th}$ sphere, positive outside).

The exponent $k \geq 1$ is a tuning parameter. As $k \rightarrow \infty$, $\phi(q) \rightarrow 1$ outside obstacles and $\phi(q) \rightarrow 0$ at the goal, producing a single well-behaved minimum.

#### **Step 6 — Verify the Four Navigation Function Properties**

| Property | Mathematical Condition | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Smoothness | $\phi \in C^{\infty}$ | Robot motion is well-defined everywhere |
| Polar | Single interior minimum at $q_d$ | Goal is the unique attractor |
| Morse | $\nabla \phi = 0 \Rightarrow \det(Hessian) \neq 0$ | No flat regions; all saddles are non-degenerate |
| Admissible | $\phi \partial \mathcal{F} = 1$ | Boundary repels the robot correctly |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Attractive potential (quadratic) | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$ | Near goal |
| 2 | Attractive potential (conic) | $U_{att}(q) = \xi\,\rho_{goal}(q)$ | Far from goal |
| 3 | Attractive force | $F_{att}(q) = -\xi\,(q - q_{goal})$ | Gradient descent |
| 4 | Repulsive potential | $U_{rep,i}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i(q)} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$ | Inside influence region |
| 5 | Repulsive force | $F_{rep}(q) = \eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i(q)} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\tfrac{1}{\rho_i^{2}(q)}\nabla\rho_i(q)$ | Gradient ascent away |
| 6 | Total potential | $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ | Superposition |
| 7 | Navigation function | $\phi(q) = \dfrac{\gamma_d(q)}{(\gamma_d^{k}(q) + \beta(q))^{1/k}}$ | Sphere world |
| 8 | Distance function | $\gamma_d(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}$ | Goal proximity |
| 9 | Obstacle function | $\beta_i(q) = \Vert q - c_i \Vert^{2} - r_i^{2}$ | Sphere boundary |
| 10 | Combined obstacle | $\beta(q) = \displaystyle\prod_{i=0}^{M} \beta_i(q)$ | All obstacles |
| 11 | Robot dynamics | $\dot{q} = -\nabla \phi(q)$ | Gradient flow |
| 12 | Boundary condition | $\phi \partial \mathcal{F} = 1$ | Admissibility |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Applications of Potential Field & Navigation Functions in Industry:**
> - **Autonomous mobile robots (AMRs)** in warehouses — fast reactive obstacle avoidance.
> - **AGVs in manufacturing** — smooth trajectory generation around fixed racks.
> - **Autonomous vehicles** — real-time lane-keeping and pedestrian avoidance.
> - **Drone swarms** — distributed navigation with guaranteed collision avoidance.
> - **Robotic surgery** — compliant motion control in confined anatomical workspaces.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Attractive Force from Attractive Potential

**Given:**
$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\rho_{goal}^{2}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\,(q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 1:** Expand the squared norm.
$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\,(q^{T}q - 2q^{T}q_{goal} + q_{goal}^{T}q_{goal})$$

**Step 2:** Take the partial derivative with respect to $q$.
$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial q} = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\,(2q - 2q_{goal}) = \xi\,(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 3:** Apply the definition of force as the negative gradient.
$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\xi\,(q - q_{goal})$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The force vector points from the current configuration $q$ toward the goal $q_{goal}$, with magnitude proportional to the distance. This is a **restoring spring force** acting on the robot.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Repulsive Force from Repulsive Potential

**Given:** For a single obstacle with distance $\rho(q)$ from the robot to the obstacle surface:
$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, \quad \rho(q) \leq \rho_0$$

**Step 1:** Define the substitution $u = \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}$, so $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\,\eta\,u^{2}$.

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule $\nabla U_{rep} = \eta\,u\,\nabla u$.

**Step 3:** Compute $\nabla u$:
$$\nabla u = -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)}\,\nabla \rho(q)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back.
$$\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)}\right)\nabla \rho(q)$$

**Step 5:** Take the negative to get the repulsive force.
$$F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)}\nabla \rho(q)$$

**Step 6:** Verify the limit at the obstacle boundary. As $\rho(q) \rightarrow 0$:
$$\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \rightarrow \infty, \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \rightarrow \infty$$

So $\Vert F_{rep} \Vert \rightarrow \infty$, guaranteeing collision avoidance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sphere-World Navigation Function Construction

**Given:** A compact region $\mathcal{F}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ (assume $n=2$ for planar case) containing a goal $q_d$ and $M$ disjoint spherical obstacles $\mathcal{O}_0, \mathcal{O}_1, \dots, \mathcal{O}_M$ with centers $c_i$ and radii $r_i$.

**Step 1 — Define the distance to goal function.**
$$\gamma_d(q) = \Vert q - q_d \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Define the obstacle function for each sphere.**
For sphere $i$:
$$\beta_i(q) = \Vert q - c_i \Vert^{2} - r_i^{2}$$

**Properties of $\beta_i$:**
- $\beta_i(q) = 0$ on the surface of $\mathcal{O}_i$
- $\beta_i(q) > 0$ outside $\mathcal{O}_i$
- $\beta_i(q) < 0$ inside $\mathcal{O}_i$

**Step 3 — Combine all obstacles into a single product function.**
$$\beta(q) = \prod_{i=0}^{M} \beta_i(q)$$

**Step 4 — Construct the navigation function.**
$$\phi(q) = \frac{\gamma_d(q)}{\bigl(\gamma_d^{k}(q) + \beta(q)\bigr)^{1/k}}, \quad k \geq 1$$

**Step 5 — Verify the boundary condition.** As $q \rightarrow c_i$ (approaching the $i^{th}$ obstacle surface), $\beta_i(q) \rightarrow 0$, hence $\beta(q) \rightarrow 0$ (assuming $q$ is not at another boundary). This makes the denominator approach $\gamma_d^{k}(q)^{1/k} = \gamma_d(q)$, so:
$$\phi(q) \rightarrow \frac{\gamma_d(q)}{\gamma_d(q)} = 1 \quad \text{on } \partial \mathcal{F}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the unique minimum at the goal.** At $q = q_d$:
$$\gamma_d(q_d) = 0, \quad \phi(q_d) = \frac{0}{(\beta(q_d))^{1/k}} = 0$$

This is the unique minimum (the polar property is proven via the Morse condition and the choice of $k$).

**Step 7 — Gradient flow for robot motion.**
$$\dot{q} = -\nabla \phi(q) = -\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial q}$$

The robot follows this gradient descent trajectory and asymptotically converges to $q_d$ from almost any initial configuration.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Classical APF vs. Navigation Function

| Criterion | Classical Potential Field (Khatib) | Navigation Function (Koditschek–Rimon) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Form of $U$ | $U_{att} + U_{rep}$ | $\phi$ as defined above |
| Local minima | **Yes** (GNRON problem) | **No** (except at goal) |
| Completeness | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed (sphere world) |
| Workspace assumption | General | Sphere or star world |
| Computation | Real-time, low cost | Higher cost due to $\beta$ product |
| Robustness | Sensitive to $\xi, \eta, \rho_0$ | Stable for $k \geq 1$ |
| Boundary behaviour | Uncontrolled | $\phi = 1$ on $\partial \mathcal{F}$ (admissible) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Potential Field Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initial Configuration q0] --> B[Input: Goal q_goal and Obstacle Map]
    B --> C[Compute Attractive Potential U_att]
    B --> D[Compute Repulsive Potential U_rep per obstacle]
    C --> E[Compute Gradient nabla U_att]
    D --> F[Compute Gradient nabla U_rep]
    E --> G[Sum Total Gradient nabla U total]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Robot Dynamics dot q equals negative nabla U]
    H --> I[Update Configuration q]
    I --> J{Is q equal to q_goal within tolerance}
    J -- No --> K{Is q in a local minimum}
    K -- Yes --> L[Apply Escape Strategy Random Perturbation]
    L --> C
    K -- No --> I
    J -- Yes --> M[Stop: Goal Reached]
```

### 4.2 Navigation Function Computational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workspace Definition] --> B[Sphere World W with M Obstacles]
    B --> C[Compute Distance Function gamma_d]
    B --> D[Compute Obstacle Function beta_i per sphere]
    D --> E[Combine beta equals product of all beta_i]
    C --> F[Navigation Function phi]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Gradient nabla phi]
    G --> H[Robot Motion dot q equals negative nabla phi]
    H --> I{Convergence to q_goal}
    I -- Yes --> J[Success]
    I -- No --> K[Adjust k and Recompute]
```

### 4.3 Potential Field Landscape Cross-Section (Schematic Concept)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Robot Start Position] -->|High Plateau| B[Slope Down to Goal]
    B --> C[Goal q_goal: Minimum of Field]
    D[Obstacle Region] -->|Steep Wall| E[High Potential Barrier]
    E --> B
    C --> F[Robot Stops at Minimum]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Sensor Feedback Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Range Sensors and Lidar] --> B[Obstacle Detection Module]
    B --> C[Distance to Obstacles rho_i]
    C --> D[Potential Field Calculator U_att plus U_rep]
    E[Goal Input q_goal] --> D
    D --> F[Gradient Computation]
    F --> G[Velocity Command v equals negative nabla U]
    G --> H[Motor Controller and Robot Actuators]
    H --> I[Robot Motion in Workspace]
    I --> A
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
> **Define the Artificial Potential Field method in robot motion planning. What is the role of attractive and repulsive potentials?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method, introduced by **Oussama Khatib (1986)**, models the robot's configuration space as a scalar field $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$, where:
- **$U_{att}(q)$** is the **attractive potential** generated by the goal — it pulls the robot toward the goal configuration.
- **$U_{rep}(q)$** is the **repulsive potential** generated by obstacles — it pushes the robot away from collision regions.
The robot moves along the negative gradient of $U$, i.e., $\dot{q} = -\nabla U(q)$, performing a gradient-descent trajectory until it reaches the goal. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for role of $U_{att}$, 1 for role of $U_{rep}$.)

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
> **What is the local minima problem (GNRON) in classical potential field methods? How does a navigation function solve it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **GNRON (Goal Non-Reachable with Obstacles Nearby)** problem refers to configurations $q^*$ where $\nabla U(q^*) = 0$ but $q^* \neq q_{goal}$. The robot gets trapped at such **local minima**, unable to reach the actual goal. (1.5 marks)
A **Navigation Function** (Koditschek & Rimon, 1992) solves this by constructing a special potential $\phi : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ that is **polar** (has a unique global minimum at the goal) and **Morse** (no degenerate critical points). Hence, no local minima exist except at the goal, guaranteeing almost-global convergence. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Derive the attractive and repulsive force equations for a robot moving in a 2D workspace using the classical Artificial Potential Field method. State clearly the physical meaning of each term. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A point robot at $q = (3, 4)$ must navigate to a goal at $q_{goal} = (0, 0)$ in the presence of a single circular obstacle of radius $r = 1$ centered at $c = (1, 0)$. Given $\xi = 1$, $\eta = 2$, $\rho_0 = 3$:
> 1. Compute the attractive force vector.
> 2. Compute the repulsive force vector (assume the robot is within $\rho_0$ of the obstacle).
> 3. Determine the resultant force and the direction of motion. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Question A

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of Forces (7 marks)**

**Attractive Force Derivation (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Define the attractive potential.
$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\rho_{goal}^{2}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\,(x - x_{goal})^{2} + \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\,(y - y_{goal})^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute partial derivatives.
$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \xi\,(x - x_{goal}), \quad \frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = \xi\,(y - y_{goal})$$

**Step 3:** Form the gradient.
$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \xi\,(x - x_{goal}) \\ \xi\,(y - y_{goal}) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the negative gradient to get the attractive force.
$$\boxed{F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = \begin{bmatrix} -\xi\,(x - x_{goal}) \\ -\xi\,(y - y_{goal}) \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Physical meaning:** A linear restoring force that points from $q$ to $q_{goal}$ with magnitude proportional to distance (similar to Hooke's law for a spring).

**Repulsive Force Derivation (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Define the repulsive potential for a single obstacle.
$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** For a circular obstacle centered at $c = (x_c, y_c)$ with radius $r$, the distance from $q$ to the obstacle surface is:
$$\rho(q) = \sqrt{(x - x_c)^{2} + (y - y_c)^{2}} - r$$

**Step 3:** Compute the gradient using the chain rule. With $u = \dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}$:
$$\nabla U_{rep} = \eta\,u\,\nabla u = -\,\eta\,u\,\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\,\nabla \rho$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\nabla \rho$:
$$\nabla \rho = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(x - x_c)^{2} + (y - y_c)^{2}}} \begin{bmatrix} x - x_c \\ y - y_c \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{D}\begin{bmatrix} x - x_c \\ y - y_c \end{bmatrix}$$
where $D = \sqrt{(x - x_c)^{2} + (y - y_c)^{2}}$.

**Step 5:** Combine to get the repulsive force.
$$\boxed{F_{rep}(q) = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)\,D}\begin{bmatrix} x - x_c \\ y - y_c \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Physical meaning:** This force points *away* from the obstacle center, with magnitude growing rapidly as the robot approaches the boundary.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $q = (3, 4)$, $q_{goal} = (0, 0)$
- Obstacle center $c = (1, 0)$, radius $r = 1$
- $\xi = 1$, $\eta = 2$, $\rho_0 = 3$

**Step 1 — Compute the Attractive Force (2 marks):**
$$F_{att} = -\xi\,(q - q_{goal}) = -1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 3 - 0 \\ 4 - 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Magnitude: $\Vert F_{att} \Vert = \sqrt{(-3)^{2} + (-4)^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$ N

**Step 2 — Compute the Distance to Obstacle (1.5 marks):**

Center-to-robot distance:
$$D = \sqrt{(3 - 1)^{2} + (4 - 0)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 16} = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.472 \text{ m}$$

Distance to obstacle surface:
$$\rho(q) = D - r = 4.472 - 1 = 3.472 \text{ m}$$

Since $\rho(q) = 3.472 > \rho_0 = 3$? **No, $\rho(q) = 3.472 > 3$, so repulsive force is ZERO.**

> Wait — checking again: $3.472 > 3.0$, hence $\rho > \rho_0$, so the obstacle does not influence the robot. $F_{rep} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Resultant Force (1.5 marks):**
$$F_{total} = F_{att} + F_{rep} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Magnitude: $\Vert F_{total} \Vert = 5$ N

**Step 4 — Direction of Motion (2 marks):**
$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{F_y}{F_x}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{-4}{-3}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \approx 53.13^{°}$$

Since both components are negative, the robot is in the **third quadrant** relative to the goal direction.
$$\theta_{actual} = 180° + 53.13° = 233.13° \text{ (measured from positive x-axis)}$$

The robot moves directly toward the goal at $(0, 0)$ since the obstacle is outside its influence range. (2 marks)

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
> **(a)** What is a Navigation Function? State and explain the four required properties (smoothness, polar, Morse, admissible) of a navigation function in a sphere world. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** For a sphere world with goal at $q_d = (0, 0)$, one spherical obstacle centered at $c_1 = (3, 0)$ with radius $r_1 = 1$, and tuning exponent $k = 2$:
> 1. Write the explicit form of the navigation function $\phi(q)$.
> 2. Verify whether the boundary condition $\phi = 1$ holds on the obstacle surface.
> 3. Comment on the choice of $k$ and its effect on the function. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Question B

#### **Part (a) — Navigation Function Definition & Properties (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **Navigation Function** $\phi : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$, introduced by **Koditschek and Rimon (1992)**, is a special potential function defined on a compact connected manifold $\mathcal{F} \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$ containing a single interior goal $q_d$ and bounded by $M$ disjoint spherical obstacles. It generates a **gradient flow** $\dot{q} = -\nabla \phi(q)$ that drives the robot from almost any initial configuration to the goal $q_d$ without getting trapped in local minima.

**The Four Properties (5 marks — 1.25 marks each):**

1. **Smoothness (Analyticity):** $\phi$ must be $C^{\infty}$ (infinitely differentiable) on $\mathcal{F}_{free}$. This ensures the gradient flow is well-defined and the robot's trajectory is smooth (no sudden jumps or undefined accelerations).

2. **Polar Property:** $\phi$ must have a **unique global minimum** at $q_d$, with $\phi(q_d) = 0$. Mathematically, $q_d$ is the *only* configuration where $\nabla \phi(q_d) = 0$ and $\phi(q) > 0$ for all $q \in \mathcal{F}_{free} \setminus \{q_d\}$. This eliminates the local-minima problem.

3. **Morse Property:** $\phi$ must be a **Morse function** — all critical points (where $\nabla \phi = 0$) are *non-degenerate*, meaning the Hessian matrix at every critical point has a non-zero determinant. This prevents flat regions or saddle degeneracies in the flow field.

4. **Admissibility:** $\phi$ must be **uniformly maximal** on the boundary, i.e., $\phi(q) = 1$ for all $q \in \partial \mathcal{F}$. This ensures obstacles act as **repelling walls**, preventing the robot from leaving the admissible region or colliding with obstacles.

---

#### **Part (b) — Explicit Navigation Function Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $q_d = (0, 0)$, $c_1 = (3, 0)$, $r_1 = 1$, $k = 2$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\gamma_d(q)$ (1.5 marks):**
$$\gamma_d(q) = \Vert q - q_d \Vert^{2} = x^{2} + y^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\beta_1(q)$ (1.5 marks):**
$$\beta_1(q) = \Vert q - c_1 \Vert^{2} - r_1^{2} = (x - 3)^{2} + y^{2} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Write the Navigation Function (2 marks):**
$$\boxed{\phi(x, y) = \frac{x^{2} + y^{2}}{\bigl((x^{2} + y^{2})^{2} + (x - 3)^{2} + y^{2} - 1\bigr)^{1/2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Boundary Condition (1 mark):**
On the surface of the obstacle, $\beta_1(q) = 0$, so $(x - 3)^{2} + y^{2} = 1$. Substituting into $\phi$:
$$\phi \vert_{\partial \mathcal{O}_1} = \frac{x^{2} + y^{2}}{((x^{2} + y^{2})^{2} + 0)^{1/2}} = \frac{x^{2} + y^{2}}{(x^{2} + y^{2})^{1}} = 1$$

✅ **Boundary condition verified:** $\phi = 1$ on the obstacle surface, satisfying the admissibility property.

**Step 5 — Effect of Tuning Parameter $k$ (1 mark):**
The exponent $k$ controls the **smoothness and depth of the potential well** at the goal. As $k$ increases:
- The well becomes **steeper** near the goal.
- The flow field becomes more **uniformly directed** toward the goal.
- Local minima (in the limit) are completely eliminated.
For $k = 2$, the function is sufficiently smooth and Morse for the given sphere world. Larger $k$ may introduce numerical instability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in force equation:** Students frequently write $F = +\nabla U$ instead of $F = -\nabla U$. Remember: the force is the **negative** gradient (gradient descent).
> 2. **Forgetting the influence distance $\rho_0$:** Repulsive potential is **zero** when $\rho(q) > \rho_0$. Many students incorrectly apply $U_{rep}$ outside the influence region.
> 3. **Confusing $\beta_i$ with $\rho_i$:** The navigation function uses the **squared distance minus radius squared** (i.e., $\beta_i = \Vert q - c_i \Vert^{2} - r_i^{2}$), not the simple distance. This is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping verification of properties:** When asked to "state and explain" the four properties of a navigation function, students often list them without mathematical conditions. **Always include the equation** for each property.
> 5. **Wrong sign of the repulsive force direction:** It must point *away* from the obstacle, with positive scalar multiplying the unit vector from obstacle to robot.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Motion in Potential Field and Navigation Function**

- ✅ **Potential Field** = $U_{att} + U_{rep}$; **Force** = $-\nabla U$.
- ✅ **Attractive potential** vanishes at goal; **repulsive potential** blows up at obstacle surface.
- ✅ **Influence distance** $\rho_0$: outside this range, the obstacle has **no effect** on the robot.
- ✅ **GNRON problem** = local minima in classical APF; **Solution** = use a Navigation Function.
- ✅ **Navigation Function** is bounded in $[0, 1]$ with $\phi(q_d) = 0$ and $\phi \partial \mathcal{F} = 1$.
- ✅ **Four properties** of a navigation function: **S**mooth, **P**olar, **M**orse, **A**dmissible (mnemonic: **SPMA**).
- ✅ **Sphere world** assumption: obstacles are disjoint spheres; the workspace is compact and connected.
- ✅ **Tuning exponent** $k \geq 1$: larger $k$ → steeper well, fewer local minima, but possible numerical issues.
- ✅ **Obstacle function** $\beta_i = \Vert q - c_i \Vert^{2} - r_i^{2}$ (zero on surface, positive outside).
- ✅ **Gradient flow** $\dot{q} = -\nabla \phi(q)$ is the **control law** for the robot.
- ✅ **Khatib (1986)** introduced the classical potential field; **Koditschek & Rimon (1992)** introduced the navigation function.
- ✅ **Local minima are a major limitation** of classical APF — this is a guaranteed exam question.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Configuration Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Configuration Space (C-space)** is a fundamental abstraction in robot motion planning that represents all possible physical states (or configurations) a robot can attain within its operational environment. A single point in the configuration space corresponds to one specific pose (position and orientation) of the entire robot body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition:** The Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all transformations that can be applied to a robot $\mathcal{A}$ relative to a fixed reference frame. Each element $q \in \mathcal{C}$ is called a **configuration** of the robot.

For a robot with $n$ degrees of freedom (DOF), the configuration space is an $n$-dimensional manifold, commonly denoted as $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

**Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** is defined as the minimum number of real-valued coordinates required to completely specify the configuration of a robot. KTU's standard reference value is the **Grübler formula**:

$$\text{DOF} = m(N-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{J} c_i$$

where:
- $m = 3$ for planar mechanisms, $m = 6$ for spatial mechanisms
- $N$ = total number of rigid bodies (links)
- $J$ = number of joints
- $c_i$ = number of constraints imposed by joint $i$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Origami Robot" Abstraction**
> 
> Imagine a robotic arm trying to move through a cluttered room. The arm has multiple joints that can rotate, making it difficult to plan its path. Now, imagine you replace the arm with a **single point** (like a chess piece) on a giant map. On this map, every location represents one possible pose of the entire arm.
> 
> - **Walls in the room** become **forbidden regions** on the map.
> - **Open space in the room** becomes **free space** on the map.
> - **Moving the arm** is now equivalent to **moving the point** along a path on the map.
> 
> This "giant map" is the Configuration Space — it converts a complex multi-joint problem into a simple point-navigation problem.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are standard engineering metrics used in C-space analysis:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| DOF for planar rigid body | $n$ | **3** | $(x, y, \theta)$ |
| DOF for spatial rigid body | $n$ | **6** | $(x, y, z, \text{roll}, \text{pitch}, \text{yaw})$ |
| Topological dimension of $\mathcal{C}$ | $\dim(\mathcal{C})$ | $n$ | Equals DOF count |
| Workspace dimension | $W$ | $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ or $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ | Physical operating space |

## 1.4 Why Configuration Space Matters in Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The primary utility of C-space is to **decouple geometric reasoning from kinematic representation**. By collapsing the robot to a point, classical path-planning algorithms (A*, RRT, PRM, Potential Fields) can be directly applied. Without C-space abstraction, planning for a 6-DOF arm in 3D would require solving coupled non-linear constraints at every timestep.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Configuration Space Concept

The theoretical construction of C-space proceeds through structured logical steps:

### Step 1: Identify the Robot and Obstacles
- Define the robot $\mathcal{A}$ as a rigid or articulated set of bodies in workspace $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{2}$ (planar) or $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ (spatial).
- Define obstacles $\mathcal{O}_i \subset W$ as closed regions that the robot must avoid.

### Step 2: Parameterize the Configuration
- **Why?** A robot has infinitely many possible poses — we need a finite parameterization.
- **How?** Choose a minimal set of parameters (the DOF) that uniquely describes any pose. For a planar rigid body, this is $q = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$.

### Step 3: Construct C-space
- $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all admissible parameters. The free C-space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is defined as:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset\}$$

- The obstacle C-space is its complement: $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.

### Step 4: Map the Path-Planning Problem
- A continuous path in the workspace becomes a continuous curve in $\mathcal{C}$.
- The collision-avoidance constraint becomes a simple **point-in-region check** in C-space.

## 2.2 The Configuration Space Obstacle (C-obstacle)

The **C-obstacle** $\mathcal{CB}_i$ corresponding to physical obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$ is the set of all configurations $q$ that place the robot $\mathcal{A}$ in collision with $\mathcal{O}_i$:

$$\mathcal{CB}_i = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O}_i \neq \emptyset\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Swept Volume" Insight:** Mathematically, $\mathcal{CB}_i$ is the pre-image of $\mathcal{O}_i$ under the placement map $q \mapsto \mathcal{A}(q)$. Geometrically, it is the set of robot placements whose interior overlaps the obstacle.

## 2.3 Homogeneous Transformation Representation

The pose of a spatial rigid body in $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ is represented by a $4 \times 4$ **homogeneous transformation matrix**:

$$T = \begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in SE(3)$$

where:
- $R \in SO(3)$ is a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix (orientation).
- $p \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ is the position vector.
- $SE(3)$ denotes the **Special Euclidean Group in 3D**, the underlying manifold of spatial C-space.

For a planar rigid body, the analogous group is $SE(2)$, with $\dim(SE(2)) = 3$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Planar rigid body DOF | $\dim(SE(2)) = 3$ | $\mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$ | meters, rad |
| Spatial rigid body DOF | $\dim(SE(3)) = 6$ | $\mathbb{R}^{3} \times SO(3)$ | meters, rad |
| Grübler's formula | $\text{DOF} = m(N-1) - \sum c_i$ | All mechanisms | dimensionless |
| Free C-space | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \bigcup \mathcal{CB}_i$ | Motion planning | set |
| C-obstacle | $\mathcal{CB}_i = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O}_i \neq \emptyset\}$ | Motion planning | set |
| Minkowski sum (C-obstacle) | $\mathcal{CB} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$ | Geometric construction | set |
| Workspace dimension (planar) | $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{2}$ | 2D planning | meters |
| Workspace dimension (spatial) | $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{3}$ | 3D planning | meters |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Configuration space is foundational in **autonomous vehicles (path planning)**, **surgical robotics (collision-aware needle steering)**, **industrial manipulators (welding/painting)**, and **humanoid locomotion**. Any system that converts physical obstacles into navigable abstract regions is leveraging C-space theory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DOF of a Planar Rigid Body

A planar rigid body in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ requires three independent parameters to be fully described:
1. $x$ — translation along horizontal axis.
2. $y$ — translation along vertical axis.
3. $\theta$ — rotation about the axis perpendicular to the plane.

Using the Grübler formula with $m = 3$ (planar), $N = 1$ (one rigid body), and $J = 0$ (no joints):

$$\text{DOF} = 3(1 - 1) - 0 = 0$$

**Wait — this gives 0, which is incorrect for a free-floating body!** The Grübler formula applies to **mechanisms with joints**. For a free body, we must count the unconstrained degrees directly:

$$\text{Planar free body DOF} = 2 \text{ (translation)} + 1 \text{ (rotation)} = 3$$

A more rigorous form of Grübler's formula for a single free body in the plane (treating it as having an implicit $SE(2)$ manifold) is:

$$\text{DOF}_{\text{free}} = 3 \text{ in } SE(2)$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minkowski Sum Construction of C-Obstacle

For a convex robot $\mathcal{A}$ translating (no rotation) among obstacles, the C-obstacle can be computed via the **Minkowski sum**.

**Given:** Robot $\mathcal{A}$ at reference placement, obstacle $\mathcal{O}$.

**Step 1:** Reflect the robot about the origin:

$$-\mathcal{A} = \{-a \mid a \in \mathcal{A}\}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Minkowski sum of the obstacle and the reflected robot:

$$\mathcal{CB} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A}) = \{o + (-a) \mid o \in \mathcal{O}, a \in \mathcal{A}\}$$

**Step 3:** Verify the set membership condition. A configuration $q$ (here just a position vector) is in collision if and only if the reference point shifted by $q$ lies in $\mathcal{CB}$:

$$q \in \mathcal{CB} \iff \mathcal{A} + q \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset$$

**Step 4 (Geometric interpretation):** $\mathcal{CB}$ is the obstacle "grown" by the shape of the robot. For a circular robot of radius $r$ and a square obstacle, $\mathcal{CB}$ is the obstacle offset outward by $r$ in all directions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Configuration Space for a 2-Link Planar Manipulator

Consider a 2-link planar arm with revolute joints having joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$.

**Step 1:** Identify the DOF.

$$\text{DOF} = 2 \text{ (two revolute joints)}$$

**Step 2:** Define the configuration.

$$q = (\theta_1, \theta_2) \in \mathcal{C} = S^{1} \times S^{1} = T^{2}$$

Here, $T^{2}$ is the 2-torus (topological product of two circles).

**Step 3:** The end-effector position (forward kinematics) is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{ee}} &= L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y_{\text{ee}} &= L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Each link $L_i$ is a line segment. The C-obstacle for a single link $L_i$ against obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ is:

$$\mathcal{CB}_i = \{(\theta_1, \theta_2) \in T^{2} \mid L_i(\theta_1, \theta_2) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset\}$$

**Step 5:** The free C-space is:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = T^{2} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{2} \mathcal{CB}_i$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Workspace to C-space

| Workspace Concept | Configuration Space Equivalent | Mathematical Operator |
|---|---|---|
| Robot pose | Configuration point $q$ | $q \in \mathcal{C}$ |
| Physical obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ | C-obstacle $\mathcal{CB}$ | Pre-image under placement map |
| Collision-free path $\gamma(t)$ | Continuous curve $\sigma(t)$ in $\mathcal{C}$ | $t \mapsto \sigma(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ |
| Free workspace $W_{\text{free}}$ | Free C-space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Set subtraction $\mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{CB}$ |
| Robot swept volume | Trajectory of $q(t)$ in $\mathcal{C}$ | Image of $\sigma$ in $\mathcal{C}$ |
| Start pose $q_s$ | Point in $\mathcal{C}$ | Initial condition |
| Goal pose $q_g$ | Point in $\mathcal{C}$ | Terminal condition |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: DOF of an Industrial SCARA Robot

A SCARA robot has 4 revolute joints and operates in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ (with vertical motion handled separately).

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- $m = 3$ (planar)
- $N = 4$ rigid links (base, link 1, link 2, end-effector)
- $J = 4$ revolute joints, each with $c_i = 2$ constraints

**Step 2:** Apply Grübler's formula.

$$\text{DOF} = 3(4 - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{4} 2 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Step 3:** Adjust for the actual SCARA — typically the base is fixed, reducing effective free bodies. The corrected form:

$$\text{DOF} = 3(3) - 2 \times 4 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Step 4:** This represents the **1 degree of freedom** of the end-effector along the vertical $z$-axis. The horizontal $xy$-position is described by 2 additional DOF (often counted separately).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workspace-to-Configuration-Space Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Robot A in Workspace W] --> B[Placement Map phi]
    B --> C[Configuration q in C-space]
    D[Obstacle O in W] --> E[Pre-image Computation]
    E --> F[C-obstacle CB in C-space]
    C --> G{Collision Check}
    F --> G
    G --> H[Free C-space C_free]
    H --> I[Path Planning Algorithm]
    I --> J[Trajectory sigma in C_free]
    J --> K[Inverse Map to Workspace]
    K --> L[Robot Motion in W]
```

## 4.2 Configuration Space Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RobotInput[Robot Definition]
        nodeA[Robot Geometry A]
        nodeB[Obstacles O1 O2 ... On]
    end
    
    subgraph CspaceBuild[Configuration Space Construction]
        nodeC[DOF Identification]
        nodeD[Parameterization q in C]
        nodeE[Pre-image Computation]
        nodeF[CB = C - C_free]
    end
    
    subgraph PlanningPhase[Path Planning in C-space]
        nodeG[Query Start q_s and Goal q_g]
        nodeH[Search Algorithm A-star RRT PRM]
        nodeI[Path sigma in C_free]
    end
    
    subgraph ExecutionPhase[Execution]
        nodeJ[Trajectory Tracking]
        nodeK[Controller Output]
    end
    
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: DOF Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStep1[Identify Robot Topology] --> nodeStep2[Count Rigid Bodies N]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3[Identify Joints and Constraints ci]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4{Planar or Spatial}
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5[m equals 3 Planar]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep6[m equals 6 Spatial]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep7[Apply Grubler Formula]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8[Output DOF Value]
    nodeStep8 --> nodeStep9[Construct C-space Manifold]
```

## 4.4 Configuration Space Decomposition for a 2-Link Arm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Torus[T2 Configuration Space]
        nodeRegion1[Region 1: Both Links Extended]
        nodeRegion2[Region 2: First Link Collides]
        nodeRegion3[Region 3: Second Link Collides]
        nodeRegion4[Region 4: End-Effector in Goal]
    end
    
    nodeRegion1 --> nodeRegion2
    nodeRegion2 --> nodeRegion3
    nodeRegion3 --> nodeRegion4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In a 2-link arm, $\mathcal{C} = T^{2}$ is topologically a torus. The C-obstacles "cut" the torus into multiple connected components, and path planning involves finding a curve on the torus that avoids these cuts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Configuration Space. Explain its significance in robot motion planning.**

**Model Answer:**

Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible configurations $q$ that a robot can attain. A configuration is a specification of the position and orientation of every point on the robot. For a robot with $n$ degrees of freedom, $\mathcal{C}$ is an $n$-dimensional manifold.

**Significance:**
1. **Abstraction:** It reduces the complex multi-body robot to a single point, simplifying planning.
2. **Unified framework:** Both kinematic and dynamic constraints can be expressed as constraints on $q$.
3. **Algorithm compatibility:** Standard graph search and sampling algorithms (A*, RRT) operate directly in $\mathcal{C}$.
4. **Collision-free path:** The problem becomes finding a continuous curve in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ from $q_s$ to $q_g$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a C-obstacle? How is it computed for a translating convex robot?**

**Model Answer:**

A **C-obstacle** $\mathcal{CB}$ corresponding to physical obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ is the set of all configurations $q \in \mathcal{C}$ that cause the robot $\mathcal{A}$ to collide with $\mathcal{O}$:

$$\mathcal{CB} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset\}$$

For a **translating convex robot** (no rotation), $\mathcal{CB}$ is computed using the **Minkowski sum**:

$$\mathcal{CB} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A}) = \{o - a \mid o \in \mathcal{O}, a \in \mathcal{A}\}$$

Geometrically, $\mathcal{CB}$ is the obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ "grown" by the shape of the robot. For a circular robot of radius $r$, $\mathcal{CB}$ is simply $\mathcal{O}$ expanded outward by $r$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Degrees of Freedom (DOF) for a 3R planar manipulator using the Grübler formula. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular mobile robot of radius $0.5$ m must navigate from start position $(1, 1)$ to goal position $(8, 8)$ in a workspace containing a square obstacle with corners at $(3, 3)$, $(5, 3)$, $(5, 5)$, and $(3, 5)$. Compute the C-obstacle and identify whether the straight-line path in workspace is collision-free in C-space. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters.
- The 3R manipulator has $N = 4$ rigid links (base + 3 links).
- Number of joints $J = 3$ (all revolute).
- For planar motion, $m = 3$.
- Each revolute joint imposes $c_i = 2$ constraints.

**Step 2:** Apply Grübler's formula.

$$\text{DOF} = m(N - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{J} c_i$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 3(4 - 1) - (2 + 2 + 2) \\
&= 3 \times 3 - 6 \\
&= 9 - 6 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation. The 3R planar manipulator has **3 DOF**, which is exactly the configuration $q = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) \in T^{3}$ — a 3-torus in C-space.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Construct the C-obstacle using the Minkowski sum.

The robot is a disk of radius $r = 0.5$ m, so the C-obstacle is the original obstacle expanded by $0.5$ m in all directions.

**Step 2:** Compute the expanded square corners.

The original square has corners at $(3, 3), (5, 3), (5, 5), (3, 5)$.

Expanding by $0.5$ m in all directions:

- $(3 - 0.5, 3 - 0.5) = (2.5, 2.5)$
- $(5 + 0.5, 3 - 0.5) = (5.5, 2.5)$
- $(5 + 0.5, 5 + 0.5) = (5.5, 5.5)$
- $(3 - 0.5, 5 + 0.5) = (2.5, 5.5)$

**Step 3:** Parametrize the straight-line path in C-space.

The straight-line path from $q_s = (1, 1)$ to $q_g = (8, 8)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= 1 + 7t \\
y(t) &= 1 + 7t
\end{aligned}
$$

for $t \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 4:** Check intersection with the C-obstacle.

The line $y = x$ passes through the region. The line enters the expanded square at the point where $x = y$ intersects the boundary $y = 2.5$:

$$x = y = 2.5 \implies t = \frac{2.5 - 1}{7} = \frac{1.5}{7} \approx 0.214$$

The line exits at $y = 5.5$:

$$x = y = 5.5 \implies t = \frac{5.5 - 1}{7} = \frac{4.5}{7} \approx 0.643$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion.

Since the path intersects $\mathcal{CB}$ for $t \in [0.214, 0.643]$, the straight-line workspace path is **NOT collision-free in C-space**. A path planner must find an alternative trajectory that avoids the expanded obstacle region.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Configuration Space with a suitable real-world analogy. Define the terms: configuration, free C-space, and C-obstacle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-link planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 2$ m and $L_2 = 1.5$ m. Determine the reachable workspace and the configuration space topology. Show your work. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Configuration Space Concept with Analogy:**

Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible physical states of a robot. Each point in $\mathcal{C}$ corresponds to one unique pose of the robot.

**Real-World Analogy — "Chess Piece in a 3D Map":**

Imagine a robotic arm navigating a cluttered room. The arm has many joints and is hard to plan for directly. Now, replace the entire arm with a single dot (like a chess pawn) on a special map. On this map:
- **Walls and furniture** (physical obstacles) become **forbidden zones**.
- **Open floor** (free workspace) becomes **walkable territory**.
- **Each location on the map** represents one specific arrangement of all arm joints.

This special map is the **Configuration Space**. The beauty of this transformation is that moving the dot from start to goal is mathematically equivalent to moving the entire arm through the room without collisions.

**Definitions:**

1. **Configuration ($q$):** A specification of the position and orientation of every point on the robot, expressed as a vector of $n$ real-valued parameters. Formally, $q \in \mathcal{C}$.

2. **Free C-space ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$):** The subset of $\mathcal{C}$ containing all configurations where the robot does not collide with any obstacle:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset\}$$

3. **C-obstacle ($\mathcal{CB}$):** The set of all configurations that cause collision with a specific obstacle:

$$\mathcal{CB}_i = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O}_i \neq \emptyset\}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify the DOF and topology.

The 2-link planar manipulator has 2 revolute joints, so DOF $= 2$. Each joint angle is in $S^{1}$ (the unit circle). Therefore:

$$\mathcal{C} = S^{1} \times S^{1} = T^{2}$$

The configuration space is a **2-torus** (a donut-shaped surface).

**Step 2:** Compute the reachable workspace.

The end-effector position is given by forward kinematics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Determine the maximum reach.

The maximum reach is the sum of the link lengths:

$$r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2 = 2 + 1.5 = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

The minimum reach (when the arm folds back) is:

$$r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 2 - 1.5 \vert = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Reachable workspace.

The reachable workspace is an **annulus** (ring-shaped region) centered at the base:

$$W_{\text{reach}} = \{(x, y) \mid 0.5 \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq 3.5\}$$

**Step 5:** Configuration space topology.

The C-space is a 2-torus $T^{2}$, which is a compact 2D manifold with genus 1 (one hole). It cannot be embedded in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ without self-intersection, requiring a 3D surface for visualization. The mapping from $\mathcal{C}$ to $W_{\text{reach}}$ is many-to-one, since multiple configurations can yield the same end-effector position (this is the well-known **inverse kinematics ambiguity**).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Configuration Space Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DOF with number of joints:** Students often equate DOF with the number of joints. DOF is computed via Grübler's formula and accounts for joint constraints. A 3R arm has 3 joints BUT 3 DOF (they coincide here only because revolute joints reduce planar DOF by 2 each).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the rotation term in planar C-space:** A planar rigid body has DOF = 3, not 2. The third DOF is the orientation $\theta \in S^{1}$. Omitting $\theta$ in your answer loses 2 marks immediately.
> 
> 3. **Misapplying the Minkowski sum:** The C-obstacle is $\mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$, NOT $\mathcal{O} \oplus \mathcal{A}$. Reflecting the robot is essential. Wrong sign = full marks lost on that part.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring topology:** Writing $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^{2}$ for a 2-link arm is incorrect. It should be $T^{2}$ (a torus), since angles are periodic.
> 
> 5. **Not defining $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ explicitly:** Always write the set-builder definition — examiners look for the formal expression $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset\}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Configuration Space:**
> 
> - **Definition:** $\mathcal{C}$ = set of all robot configurations $q$. Each $q$ is a vector of $n$ real-valued parameters (DOF).
> - **Planar rigid body:** $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 3$, manifold is $SE(2) = \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$.
> - **Spatial rigid body:** $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 6$, manifold is $SE(3) = \mathbb{R}^{3} \times SO(3)$.
> - **Free C-space:** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \bigcup \mathcal{CB}_i$ — the navigable region.
> - **C-obstacle formula:** $\mathcal{CB}_i = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O}_i \neq \emptyset\}$.
> - **Minkowski sum shortcut (translating robot):** $\mathcal{CB} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$.
> - **Grübler's formula:** $\text{DOF} = m(N-1) - \sum c_i$, with $m = 3$ (planar) or $m = 6$ (spatial).
> - **2-link arm C-space:** $T^{2}$ (2-torus) — topologically a donut.
> - **Homogeneous transform for spatial body:** $4 \times 4$ matrix with rotation $R \in SO(3)$ and translation $p \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$.
> - **Key insight:** C-space converts a complex multi-body planning problem into a simple point-navigation problem in higher-dimensional space.
> - **Algorithm link:** A*, RRT, PRM, and Potential Field methods all operate on $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
> - **Inverse kinematics ambiguity:** Multiple $q \in \mathcal{C}$ can map to the same workspace point, because $\mathcal{C}$ has higher dimension than the workspace.
> - **Workspace vs C-space:** $W$ = physical space; $\mathcal{C}$ = parameter space. They are not the same and should never be confused in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free Configuration Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Free Configuration Space # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Configuration** of a robot is a complete specification of the position of every point of the robot in the workspace. The set of all possible configurations that the robot can attain is called the **Configuration Space**, denoted by **C** (or $C$-space).
