The **Free Configuration Space**, denoted $C_{free}$, is the subset of the configuration space $C$ in which the robot is **collision-free**, i.e., it does not intersect any obstacle and satisfies all joint limits. Mathematically: $$C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$$ where $C_{obs}$ is the **C-space obstacle** (the set of all configurations that cause the robot to collide with a workspace obstacle or with itself). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In KTU Board examinations, $C_{free}$ is **always** the **destination** of a motion-planning algorithm. Path planning is equivalent to finding a continuous curve in $C_{free}$ between an initial configuration $q_{start}$ and a goal configuration $q_{goal}$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are driving a long bus through a narrow, twisting street filled with parked cars. - In the **real world (workspace)**, you constantly have to think about the *front bumper, the rear bumper, and the overhang at every turn*. The bus is huge, the obstacles are fixed, and the geometry is painful. - Now, **shrink the bus to a single point** and **"inflate" every parked car** by the exact shape of the bus. Suddenly, you are a point in a much simpler space — and you only need to check whether the point touches anything. That simpler space is the **Free Configuration Space**. The bus is no longer a complex shape; it is a single point, and the obstacles have been pre-sculpted to represent "all the positions where the bus *would* have hit something." The "free" portion is the safe region where the point can travel. > [!NOTE] > **Why this trick works:** A motion-planning problem for a *rigid body* of non-zero size in the workspace is mathematically identical to a motion-planning problem for a *point* in a transformed space called $C_{free}$. This is the cornerstone of the classical *Configuration Space Approach* introduced by Lozano-Pérez and Wesley (1979). ## 1.3 Key Parameters (KTU Convention) | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | $n$ | Degrees of freedom (DOF) of the robot | dimensionless | | $q$ | A configuration (a point in $C$) | varies | | $W$ | Workspace (2-D or 3-D Euclidean space) | $\text{m}^2$ or $\text{m}^3$ | | $O_i$ | $i^{\text{th}}$ workspace obstacle | subset of $W$ | | $R(q)$ | Set of points occupied by the robot at configuration $q$ | subset of $W$ | | $A \oplus B$ | Minkowski sum of sets $A$ and $B$ | set operation | > [!NOTE] > **Physical constant used:** None — configuration space is a purely geometric/topological construct. It exists in $\mathbb{R}^n$, where $n$ is the number of DOF. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Logic — How $C_{free}$ is Built The construction of the free configuration space proceeds in **four ordered steps**: 1. **Identify the robot's degrees of freedom.** Count the minimum number of independent parameters required to specify every point of the robot. For a free-flying rigid body in the plane, this is **3 DOF** $(x, y, \theta)$. For a point robot in the plane, this is **2 DOF** $(x, y)$. For a planar 3R manipulator (three revolute joints), this is **3 DOF** $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$. 2. **Define the configuration $q$ formally.** A configuration $q$ is a vector of $n$ real numbers, $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$, that uniquely determines the placement of the robot. The configuration space is the set of all admissible configurations: $C = \mathbb{R}^n$ (in the unrestricted case) or $C = S^1 \times S^1 \times \cdots$ for a system of revolute joints. 3. **Map the workspace obstacles into $C$-space.** Each workspace obstacle $O_i$ is transformed into a **$C$-obstacle** $C B_i$, defined as: $$C B_i = \{\, q \in C \mid R(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset \,\}$$ 4. **Take the complement.** The free configuration space is everything else: $$C_{free} = C \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} C B_i = C \setminus C_{obs}$$ ## 2.2 The Role of the Minkowski Sum The most important algebraic tool in $C_{free}$ construction is the **Minkowski sum**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Minkowski Sum):** For two sets $A$ and $B$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$, > $$A \oplus B = \{\, a + b \mid a \in A,\ b \in B \,\}$$ For a **translating** robot of shape $R$ (with a reference point attached to $R$), the $C$-obstacle generated by a workspace obstacle $O$ is: $$C B = O \oplus (-R) = \{\, o - r \mid o \in O,\ r \in R \,\}$$ The **"minus"** arises because we conceptually drag the robot *around* the obstacle: shifting the obstacle by $-r$ for every $r \in R$ gives the locus of forbidden reference-point locations. For a **rotating and translating** planar robot, the situation is more complex: the $C$-obstacle in 3-D configuration space $(x, y, \theta)$ is built by *sweeping* the grown obstacle over all orientations $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$, producing a 3-D "tube" in $C$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Configuration of a free planar rigid body | $q = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ | 3 DOF | | 2 | Configuration of a point robot in plane | $q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ | 2 DOF | | 3 | Configuration of a planar 3R arm | $q = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) \in S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1$ | 3 DOF, joint space | | 4 | Free C-space | $C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$ | Main definition | | 5 | C-obstacle | $C B_i = \{q \in C \mid R(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset\}$ | Collision set | | 6 | Translating rigid body in plane | $C B_i = O_i \oplus (-R)$ | Minkowski sum | | 7 | Point robot in plane | $C_{free} = W \setminus \bigcup_i O_i$ | Trivial case | | 8 | Disc robot of radius $r$ in plane | $C_{free} = W \setminus \bigcup_i (O_i \oplus D(0,r))$ | Inflate obstacles | | 9 | Reflex angle range for polygon | $\theta \in [\theta_{min}, \theta_{max}]$ | For a vertex $v$, the orientation range where $v$ sees a reflex edge | | 10 | Continuous path | $\tau : [0,1] \to C_{free}$ | $\tau(0) = q_{start},\ \tau(1) = q_{goal}$ | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe-symbol alert:** In the above table, $C B_i$, $D(0,r)$, etc., use parentheses and commas — never the **vertical bar `|`** — to avoid breaking markdown rendering. KTU examiners penalize **illegible formula sheets**. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering | Domain | Use of $C_{free}$ | |---|---| | **Autonomous Vehicles** | Path planning in $(x, y, \theta, v)$ space for lane keeping & parking | | **Industrial Robotic Arms** | Joint-space motion planning avoiding self-collision and fixtures | | **CNC Machining** | Tool-path synthesis where the tool is reduced to a point and the part is "grown" by the tool radius | | **Computer-Aided Surgery** | Needle-steering paths in deformable configuration spaces | | **Warehouse Robots (AMR)** | Lattice planners operate entirely on discrete approximations of $C_{free}$ | | **Drone Swarms** | Collision avoidance in $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ 6-DOF configuration space | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Case Study 1 — Point Robot in a 2-D Polygonal Workspace A **point robot** has zero size. Its configuration is simply its position $q = (x, y)$. **Step 1 — Express the workspace.** The workspace is a 2-D region $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ that contains a finite set of polygonal obstacles $\{O_1, O_2, \dots, O_m\}$. **Step 2 — Identify $C$.** For a free-flying point, every $(x, y) \in W$ is admissible, so $C = W$. **Step 3 — Find $C_{obs}$.** The point is in collision if and only if it lies inside at least one obstacle. Therefore: $$C_{obs} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} O_i$$ **Step 4 — Compute $C_{free}$.** $$C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs} = W \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} O_i$$ **Interpretation.** A point robot's free configuration space is literally the *empty* part of the workspace. No transformation is needed because there is nothing to "grow." --- ## 3.2 Case Study 2 — Translating Disc Robot of Radius $r$ in 2-D A **disc robot** of radius $r$ is a planar circular body. The reference point is taken at its center. **Step 1 — Identify the configuration.** $q = (x, y)$ — the center of the disc. So $C = \mathbb{R}^2$ (or a bounding box of $W$). **Step 2 — Express the occupied set at configuration $q$.** The points occupied by the disc are: $$R(q) = \{\, (x + u,\ y + v) \mid u^2 + v^2 \leq r^2 \,\} = q + D(0,r)$$ where $D(0,r)$ is the closed disc of radius $r$ centered at the origin. **Step 3 — Collision condition.** The disc collides with obstacle $O_i$ iff there exists a robot point coinciding with an obstacle point, i.e., iff: $$R(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset$$ **Step 4 — Convert to a condition on the reference point $q$.** Substituting: $$(q + D(0,r)) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset \iff q \in O_i \oplus (-D(0,r))$$ **Reasoning:** The intersection is non-empty iff we can pick a robot point $d \in D(0,r)$ and an obstacle point $o \in O_i$ with $q + d = o$, i.e., $q = o - d$. As $d$ ranges over the disc and $o$ over the obstacle, $q$ ranges over $O_i \oplus (-D(0,r))$. **Step 5 — Since $-D(0,r) = D(0,r)$ (the disc is centrally symmetric),** we obtain: $$C B_i = O_i \oplus D(0,r)$$ This is the "grown" obstacle. Geometrically, every point in $O_i$ is replaced by a disc of radius $r$ and the union of all those discs forms the new forbidden region. **Step 6 — Final $C_{free}$:** $$C_{free} = W \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \bigl(O_i \oplus D(0,r)\bigr)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** The reference point is your *anchor* — every Minkowski sum is built around it. Always state the reference point explicitly in the exam; otherwise the answer is incomplete and loses 1–2 marks. --- ## 3.3 Case Study 3 — Translating & Rotating Convex Polygon Robot A convex polygon robot with $k$ vertices, free-flying in the plane. **Step 1 — Configuration space.** $q = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$, so $C$ is **3-dimensional**. **Step 2 — Occupied set at $q$.** Let $V$ be the set of vertex coordinates in the robot's body frame. Then in the world frame: $$R(q) = \{\, R_\theta \, v + (x,y) \mid v \in V \,\}$$ where $R_\theta$ is the planar rotation matrix: $$R_\theta = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Collision condition for a single vertex $v$.** A vertex $v$ collides with obstacle $O_i$ when: $$R_\theta v + (x,y) \in O_i \iff (x,y) \in O_i - R_\theta v$$ **Step 4 — The $C$-obstacle for one vertex $v$ is a "ribbon":** $$C B_i(v) = \{\, (x, y, \theta) \mid (x,y) \in O_i - R_\theta v \,\}$$ In 3-D $C$-space, this is the union over $\theta$ of a 2-D translated copy of $O_i$, swept continuously as $\theta$ varies. The result is a curved, twisted 3-D "tube." **Step 5 — Aggregate over all vertices (and edges) and all obstacles:** $$C_{obs} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{m}\ \bigcup_{v \in V}\ C B_i(v)$$ and $$C_{free} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1 \setminus C_{obs}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** For a convex polygon, the *vertex-based* test is sufficient — edges are automatically safe. For a *concave* polygon, both vertices *and* edges must be tested. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A disc robot of radius $r = 1$ m moves in a square workspace of side $10$ m with a single square obstacle of side $3$ m placed with its lower-left corner at $(4, 4)$. Find $C_{free}$ if the reference point is at the center of the disc. **Solution.** **Step 1 — Parameterize.** Disc reference point: $q = (x, y)$. Workspace: $W = [0, 10] \times [0, 10]$. Obstacle: $O_1 = [4, 7] \times [4, 7]$. **Step 2 — Minkowski growth.** The grown obstacle is the obstacle dilated by a disc of radius $1$: $$O_1 \oplus D(0,1) = [4, 7] \times [4, 7] \oplus D(0,1)$$ This is the set of all points within distance $\leq 1$ from $O_1$. Because $O_1$ is axis-aligned, the result is simply: $$O_1' = [3, 8] \times [3, 8]$$ (each face pushed outward by $r = 1$; rounded corners are filled in, but for an axis-aligned square the disc dilation produces a square of side $3 + 2r = 5$, hence $[3, 8] \times [3, 8]$). **Step 3 — Free configuration space.** $$C_{free} = \bigl([0, 10] \times [0, 10]\bigr) \setminus \bigl([3, 8] \times [3, 8]\bigr)$$ $$C_{free} = \bigl([0, 3] \times [0, 10]\bigr) \cup \bigl([8, 10] \times [0, 10]\bigr) \cup \bigl([3, 8] \times [0, 3]\bigr) \cup \bigl([3, 8] \times [8, 10]\bigr)$$ This is a 10 m × 10 m square with a 5 m × 5 m forbidden block in the center. The disc robot can be placed at any configuration $(x, y) \in C_{free}$. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | Robot Type | DOF $n$ | $C$-space topology | $C_{obs}$ construction | |---|---|---|---| | Point in $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | $C_{obs} = \bigcup O_i$ | | Disc in $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | Minkowski growth: $O_i \oplus D(0,r)$ | | Polygon (translate only) | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | $O_i \oplus (-R)$ | | Polygon (translate + rotate) | 3 | $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ | Swept 3-D tubes | | 3R planar arm | 3 | $S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1$ (torus) | Algebraic C-obstacles in joint space | | Free-flying body in $\mathbb{R}^3$ | 6 | $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ | High-dim $C$-obstacles | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transformation from Workspace to Free C-Space ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Workspace W
with robot R and obstacles O] --> B[Choose reference point
on the robot] B --> C[Determine degrees of freedom n
of the robot] C --> D{Is the robot a point?} D -- Yes --> E["C_free = W \\ minus obstacles O_i"] D -- No --> F[Apply Minkowski Sum
O_i OR minus R] F --> G[Grown obstacles
CB_i in C-space] E --> H[Combine: C_free = C \\ minus C_obs] G --> H H --> I[Identify start config q_start
in C_free] I --> J[Identify goal config q_goal
in C_free] J --> K[Path planner searches
a continuous curve in C_free] K --> L[Trajectory tau in C_free
connecting q_start to q_goal] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Algorithmic Pipeline for $C_{free}$ Construction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IN1[Input Stage] I1[Robot shape R] I2[Workspace map W] I3[Obstacle set O1 to Om] end subgraph PROC[Processing Stage] P1[Select reference point p on R] P2[Compute DOF n] P3[For each obstacle Oi build CB_i] P4[Union of all C-obstacles = C_obs] P5[Compute C_free = C minus C_obs] end subgraph OUT1[Output Stage] O1[Graph or grid representation
of C_free] O2[Connectivity map
for path planning] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P2 I3 --> P3 P1 --> P3 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> O1 O1 --> O2 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map — Topology of $C_{free}$ ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TOPO[Configuration Space C] A1[Continuous region C_free] A2[Forbidden region C_obs] end A2 -. Complement of A1 .- A1 A1 --> B1[Component 1: Room A] A1 --> B2[Component 2: Room B] A1 --> B3[Component N: Room N] B1 -. separated by C_obs .- B2 B2 -. separated by C_obs .- B3 B1 --> P1[Path tau1 within component 1] B2 --> P2[Path tau2 within component 2] B1 -. NO PATH EXISTS in C_free .-> B2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A configuration space may consist of **multiple disconnected components** in $C_{free}$. If $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$ lie in different components, **no collision-free path exists** — the planning problem is *infeasible*. This is a key KTU 2024 viva question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. Define Free Configuration Space. Why is it important in robot motion planning? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Free Configuration Space** ($C_{free}$) is the set of all configurations $q$ of a robot such that the robot, when placed at $q$, occupies no obstacle in the workspace and satisfies all kinematic constraints. $$C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$$ where $C$ is the configuration space and $C_{obs} = \bigcup_{i} C B_i$ is the union of all $C$-space obstacles. **Importance (1 mark):** Motion planning reduces to finding a continuous curve $\tau : [0,1] \to C_{free}$ from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$. Without $C_{free}$, the planner would have to reason about the robot's geometry and the obstacles simultaneously, which is computationally expensive. --- ### Q2. What is a $C$-space obstacle? How is it constructed for a translating disc robot? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **$C$-space obstacle** ($C$-obstacle) is the set of all configurations that cause a collision between the robot and a particular workspace obstacle. Formally, for obstacle $O_i$: $$C B_i = \{\, q \in C \mid R(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset \,\}$$ where $R(q)$ is the set of workspace points occupied by the robot at configuration $q$. **For a translating disc of radius $r$ (1.5 marks):** Taking the reference point at the disc's center, the $C$-obstacle is the Minkowski sum of the obstacle and a disc of radius $r$ centered at the origin: $$C B_i = O_i \oplus D(0,r) = \{\, o + d \mid o \in O_i,\ d \in D(0,r) \,\}$$ Geometrically, this "inflates" the obstacle by $r$ in all directions. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** OR. --- ### Question A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` **(a) For a translating convex polygon robot in a 2-D workspace, derive an expression for the C-space obstacle using the Minkowski sum. Show that for a point robot the result reduces to the workspace obstacle itself. (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the reference point.** Choose a reference point $p$ on the robot body. Every configuration $q = (x, y)$ specifies the location of $p$ in the world frame. **Step 2 — Express the occupied set.** Let $R$ denote the set of points in the robot's body frame, relative to $p$. The world-frame occupied set is: $$R(q) = \{\, p + (x, y) + r \mid r \in R \,\} = (x, y) + (p + R)$$ **Step 3 — Collision condition.** The robot collides with obstacle $O_i$ iff: $$(x, y) + (p + R) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange.** This is equivalent to: $$(x, y) \in O_i \oplus (-(p + R))$$ **Step 5 — Conclude the C-obstacle.** $$C B_i = O_i \oplus (-R') \quad \text{where } R' = p + R$$ (We absorb the constant offset $p$ into the robot shape; or, if we choose $p$ as the body origin, $R' = R$.) **Step 6 — Reduction to a point robot.** A point robot has $R' = \{0\}$, a singleton set. Then $O_i \oplus (-R') = O_i \oplus \{0\} = O_i$, recovering the trivial result. $\blacksquare$ **(b) A disc robot of radius $r = 0.5$ m operates in a square workspace $[0, 8] \times [0, 8]$ m. There is a single rectangular obstacle with vertices $(2,2), (5,2), (5,3), (2,3)$. Compute $C_{free}$ for the disc robot and determine whether the configuration $q = (1, 1)$ is collision-free. (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify inputs.** $W = [0,8] \times [0,8]$, $O_1 = [2,5] \times [2,3]$, $r = 0.5$. **Step 2 — Compute the Minkowski-grown obstacle.** For an axis-aligned rectangle dilated by a disc of radius $r$: $$O_1 \oplus D(0, r) = [2 - r,\ 5 + r] \times [2 - r,\ 3 + r]$$ This is exact because the rectangle has no convex corners that need rounding (the disc fills the corners and the resulting shape is a rectangle of expanded size). $$O_1' = [1.5,\ 5.5] \times [1.5,\ 3.5]$$ **Step 3 — Compute $C_{free}$.** $$C_{free} = \bigl([0, 8] \times [0, 8]\bigr) \setminus \bigl([1.5, 5.5] \times [1.5, 3.5]\bigr)$$ This is the workspace minus a $4$ m $\times$ $2$ m forbidden strip in the center. **Step 4 — Test the configuration $q = (1, 1)$.** Is $q \in C_{free}$? Check whether $q \in O_1'$: - $x = 1 < 1.5$? Yes. So $(1, 1) \notin O_1'$, hence $(1, 1) \in C_{free}$. **The configuration is collision-free.** $\blacksquare$ --- ### Question B — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a) Explain the configuration space approach to robot motion planning. Discuss the role of the reference point in constructing $C$-obstacles. (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Motivation.** The configuration space approach, introduced by Lozano-Pérez and Wesley (1979), transforms a *geometric* planning problem (collision of a shaped robot with obstacles) into a *point* planning problem (collision of a reference point with grown obstacles). **Step 2 — Configuration.** A configuration $q$ is the smallest vector that specifies the placement of every point of the robot. For a free-flying planar rigid body, $q = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ — a 3-D space. **Step 3 — Mapping.** The mapping $\mathcal{M} : C \to 2^W$ takes a configuration to the set of workspace points occupied by the robot. A workspace obstacle $O_i$ becomes the $C$-obstacle $C B_i = \mathcal{M}^{-1}(O_i) = \{q \mid \mathcal{M}(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset\}$. **Step 4 — Reference point.** A reference point $p$ is a single distinguished point on the robot (e.g., the center of a disc, a vertex of a polygon, the end-effector). The entire $C$-obstacle is built around this point: $$C B_i = O_i \oplus (-(p + R))$$ **Step 5 — Why it matters.** (i) The reference point choice affects the *shape* of $C_{obs}$ but not its *existence* — any consistent choice works. (ii) For a translating *and* rotating robot, the reference point must be the pivot of rotation; otherwise the mapping $q \mapsto R(q)$ becomes nonlinear. $\blacksquare$ **(b) For a planar 3R manipulator with link lengths $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = 1$ m and joint limits $\theta_i \in [0, \pi]$, describe the configuration space $C$ and identify at least two sources of $C$-obstacles. (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Configuration space.** A configuration is $q = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) \in C$, where: $$C = [0, \pi] \times [0, \pi] \times [0, \pi] \subset S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1$$ Topologically, $C$ is a **3-D cube with toroidal identification** of opposite faces (because revolute joints are periodic), but with joint-limit boundaries it is a 3-D cube. Dimension: $n = 3$. **Step 2 — Forward kinematics.** The end-effector position (and link positions) is determined by: $$\begin{aligned} x_1 &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 \\ y_1 &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 \\ x_2 &= x_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ y_2 &= y_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ x_3 &= x_2 + L_3 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3) \\ y_3 &= y_2 + L_3 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Source 1: Workspace obstacles.** A workspace obstacle $O_i$ is mapped to: $$C B_i = \{\, (\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) \mid \text{link}_k(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset,\ k = 1, 2, 3 \,\}$$ This is a (typically) non-convex 3-D set inside the joint cube. **Step 4 — Source 2: Self-collision.** If link 3 can collide with link 1 (e.g., when the arm folds back on itself), the self-collision set is: $$C_{self} = \{\, q \in C \mid \text{link}_1(q) \cap \text{link}_3(q) \neq \emptyset \,\}$$ **Step 5 — Free space.** $$C_{free} = C \setminus \left( \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} C B_i \cup C_{self} \right)$$ $\blacksquare$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common marks lost on Free Configuration Space questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the reference point** (–1 mark). Without it, the Minkowski sum is undefined. > 2. **Conflating workspace and configuration space** (–2 marks). A square workspace obstacle is *not* a square $C$-obstacle for a non-point robot. > 3. **Ignoring joint limits** in manipulator problems (–1 mark). $C \neq \mathbb{R}^n$ for a real robot. > 4. **Forgetting self-collision** in multi-link arms (–1 mark). $C_{free} \neq W \setminus \bigcup C B_i$ alone. > 5. **Writing $q \in R$ instead of $q \in C$** (–0.5 mark). A configuration belongs to *configuration* space, not workspace. > 6. **Skipping the final set-builder expression** (–0.5 mark). Always close the derivation with a clear $C_{free} = \dots$ statement. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Configuration** = full specification of robot placement; vector $q \in C$. - [ ] **Configuration space $C$** = set of *all* admissible configurations; dimension = DOF. - [ ] **Free configuration space $C_{free}$** = $C \setminus C_{obs}$; the safe region. - [ ] **$C$-obstacle** $C B_i$ = set of $q$ where robot at $q$ touches obstacle $O_i$. - [ ] **Point robot** ⇒ $C_{free} = W \setminus \bigcup O_i$ (no transformation). - [ ] **Translating disc of radius $r$** ⇒ $C_{free} = W \setminus \bigcup (O_i \oplus D(0,r))$. - [ ] **Translating polygon** ⇒ $C_{obs} = \bigcup_i (O_i \oplus (-R))$, where $R$ is the robot shape. - [ ] **Translating + rotating polygon** ⇒ $C$ is 3-D ($\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$); $C$-obstacles are swept tubes. - [ ] **Minkowski sum** $A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A, b \in B\}$ — central to obstacle growth. - [ ] **Reference point** is mandatory: the $C$-obstacle is *about* this point. - [ ] **DOF count** = minimum independent parameters: point = 2, disc = 2, planar rigid body = 3, 3R arm = 3, free body in 3-D = 6. - [ ] **Topology of $C$** varies: $\mathbb{R}^n$ for free-flying, $S^1 \times S^1 \times \cdots$ for revolute arms, a bounded cube with joint limits. - [ ] **Path existence** ⇔ $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$ lie in the *same connected component* of $C_{free}$. - [ ] **KTU board answer must always end with a boxed $C_{free} = \dots$ expression.** - [ ] **Lozano-Pérez & Wesley (1979)** is the historical reference for the $C$-space approach.

topics.title: Gradient Descent Method of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradient Descent Method of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Gradient Descent Method (GDM)** is a first-order, iterative optimization algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable objective (cost) function $f(\mathbf{x}): \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. The method updates the parameter vector $\mathbf{x}$ in the direction of the **steepest negative gradient** of the function, scaled by a positive scalar known as the **learning rate** $\alpha > 0$.

The canonical update rule is expressed as:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ denotes the gradient (vector of partial derivatives) of the cost function evaluated at the current iterate $\mathbf{x}_k$, and $\alpha_k$ is the step-size at iteration $k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Robot Navigation, gradient descent is the *engine* behind trajectory optimization, SLAM back-end optimization, Iterative Closest Point (ICP) registration, and learning-based perception modules. It is a **mandatory prerequisite concept** for understanding any optimization-driven navigation pipeline.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Hiker"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
>
> Imagine a hiker stranded on a foggy mountain at night who wants to reach the valley floor (lowest point). The hiker **cannot see the terrain** but can feel the slope of the ground beneath their feet.
>
> 1. The hiker checks the local slope (gradient) at the current position.
> 2. They take a step **downhill**, proportional to how steep the slope is.
> 3. They repeat until the ground is flat (gradient $\approx 0$).
>
> - The **steepness of the slope** $\equiv$ magnitude of the gradient.
> - The **size of each step** $\equiv$ learning rate $\alpha$.
> - The **flat regions** $\equiv$ local minima, global minima, or saddle points.
> - Taking **too large a step** $\equiv$ the hiker overshoots and ends up on the opposite slope.
> - Taking **too small a step** $\equiv$ the hiker takes forever to reach the valley.

In robotics, the "mountain" is the **cost function** that encodes how "bad" a robot's current state/trajectory is. Examples include:
- Distance from a goal pose
- Energy expenditure along a path
- Mismatch between observed and predicted sensor readings (SLAM)
- Collision penalty in path planning

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Learning rate | $\alpha$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$ | Controls step size |
| Convergence tolerance | $\epsilon$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Stopping criterion |
| Maximum iterations | $N_{\max}$ | $10^{3}$ to $10^{6}$ | Computational budget |
| Gradient norm | $\Vert \nabla f \Vert$ | Approaches 0 at optimum | Stationarity check |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Gradient Descent Works — The Logic Chain

The update rule is derived directly from the **first-order Taylor expansion** of $f(\mathbf{x})$ around the current iterate $\mathbf{x}_k$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta\mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \Delta\mathbf{x}$$

To minimize $f$, we must choose $\Delta\mathbf{x}$ such that the linear term is maximally negative. The most negative inner product occurs when:

$$\Delta\mathbf{x} = -\alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

because $\nabla f^\top (-\nabla f) = -\Vert \nabla f \Vert^2 \leq 0$, with equality **only** when $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$.

### 2.2 The Three Core Components (Exam Hot-Spot)

1. **Search Direction** $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ — points along steepest descent.
2. **Step Size (Learning Rate)** $\alpha_k > 0$ — controls magnitude of the update.
3. **Stopping Criterion** — usually one of:
   - $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert < \epsilon$ (gradient vanishes)
   - $\vert f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) - f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert < \epsilon$ (cost stalls)
   - $k \geq N_{\max}$ (iteration budget exhausted)

### 2.3 Variants of Gradient Descent (High-Yield for KTU)

| Variant | Update Rule | Key Idea | Robotics Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) = -\alpha \sum_i \nabla f_i$ | Uses **full dataset** per step | Offline trajectory smoothing |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f_i(\mathbf{x}_k)$ for random $i$ | Uses **one sample** per step | Online learning for perception |
| **Mini-batch GD** | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j \in B} \nabla f_j$ | Uses **batch of $m$** samples | SLAM back-end, NN training |
| **Momentum GD** | $\mathbf{v}_{k+1} = \beta \mathbf{v}_k + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ ; $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{v}_{k+1}$ | Accumulates velocity to escape plateaus | Deep visual odometry |
| **Adam** | Adaptive moments (1st & 2nd) | Self-tuning $\alpha$ per parameter | End-to-end navigation policies |

### 2.4 Convergence Behavior (Theoretical Boundaries)

- **Convex + Lipschitz gradient + small enough $\alpha$** ⟹ **global convergence** to the unique minimum.
- **Non-convex (typical in robotics)** ⟹ convergence to a **local minimum or saddle point**, **not guaranteed global**.
- **If** $\alpha$ **is too large** ⟹ divergence / oscillation.
- **If** $\alpha$ **is too small** ⟹ excruciatingly slow convergence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Rule:** For a function with $L$-Lipschitz gradient (smoothness constant $L$), a **safe constant learning rate** is:
> $$0 < \alpha < \frac{2}{L}$$
> This guarantees that each step **does not overshoot** the minimum along a quadratic valley.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Symbol | Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Update rule | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Core iteration |
| Gradient | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \left[\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \dots, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right]^\top$ | Direction of steepest ascent |
| Direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\dfrac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert}$ | Unit descent direction |
| Armijo (backtracking) condition | $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) \leq f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c_1 \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \mathbf{d}_k$, $0 < c_1 < 1$ | Sufficient decrease test |
| Lipschitz condition | $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) - \nabla f(\mathbf{y}) \Vert \leq L \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert$ | Bounds gradient change rate |
| Convergence rate (strong convex) | $f(\mathbf{x}_k) - f^* \leq \left(1 - \tfrac{\alpha \mu}{1}\right)^k \left(f(\mathbf{x}_0) - f^*\right)$ | Linear convergence |
| Momentum velocity | $\mathbf{v}_{k+1} = \beta \mathbf{v}_k + (1-\beta)\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Smoothed gradient |
| Stopping criteria | $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert < \epsilon$ **OR** $\vert f_k - f_{k-1} \vert < \epsilon$ | Algorithmic termination |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Robotics

Gradient descent is the silent workhorse in nearly every modern robot stack:

1. **SLAM Back-End (Graph Optimization)** — minimizing reprojection error using Gauss-Newton / Levenberg-Marquardt, which itself is a *modified gradient* approach.
2. **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** — minimizing point-to-point or point-to-plane distances for LiDAR/visual scan registration.
3. **Inverse Kinematics** — solving for joint angles that achieve a desired end-effector pose by minimizing a pose-error cost.
4. **Trajectory Optimization (CHOMP, STOMP)** — smoothing paths in configuration space by minimizing obstacle and smoothness costs.
5. **Deep Visual Odometry / Learning-based Control** — backpropagation (a fancy form of gradient descent) trains CNNs and policies end-to-end.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule

We start from a smooth scalar function $f(\mathbf{x})$ with $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The goal is to find $\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x})$.

**Step 1 — Taylor Series Linearization.**
The first-order Taylor expansion of $f$ around the current point $\mathbf{x}_k$ is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \Delta \mathbf{x} + \mathcal{O}(\Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert^2)$$

**Step 2 — Neglect the Higher-Order Term.**
For a small step $\Delta \mathbf{x}$, the second-order remainder is negligible, giving the linear approximation:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \Delta \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 3 — Minimize the Linear Model.**
The expression $f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \Delta \mathbf{x}$ is minimized (for a fixed step norm) by choosing $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ **opposite** to the gradient direction.

This follows from the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which states that for any vectors $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$:

$$\mathbf{a}^\top \mathbf{b} \geq -\Vert \mathbf{a} \Vert \, \Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert$$

The minimum of $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \Delta \mathbf{x}$ is therefore $-\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert \, \Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert$, achieved when:

$$\Delta \mathbf{x} = -\alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

where the scalar $\alpha_k > 0$ is the **step size (learning rate)**.

**Step 4 — Assemble the Update Rule.**
Substituting $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ gives the canonical gradient descent update:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \quad \square$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A mobile robot's cost function is $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 5$. Starting from $x_0 = 0$ with learning rate $\alpha = 0.3$, perform **three iterations** of gradient descent and report the values.

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient.**
The gradient (in 1-D, just the derivative) is:

$$\nabla f(x) = \frac{df}{dx} = 2x - 4$$

**Step 2 — Iteration $k = 0$.**
Evaluate the gradient at $x_0 = 0$:

$$\nabla f(x_0) = 2(0) - 4 = -4$$

Apply the update rule with $\alpha = 0.3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= x_0 - \alpha \cdot \nabla f(x_0) \\
x_1 &= 0 - 0.3 \times (-4) \\
x_1 &= 0 + 1.2 \\
x_1 &= 1.2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Iteration $k = 1$.**
Evaluate the gradient at $x_1 = 1.2$:

$$\nabla f(x_1) = 2(1.2) - 4 = 2.4 - 4 = -1.6$$

Apply the update:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_2 &= x_1 - \alpha \cdot \nabla f(x_1) \\
x_2 &= 1.2 - 0.3 \times (-1.6) \\
x_2 &= 1.2 + 0.48 \\
x_2 &= 1.68
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Iteration $k = 2$.**
Evaluate the gradient at $x_2 = 1.68$:

$$\nabla f(x_2) = 2(1.68) - 4 = 3.36 - 4 = -0.64$$

Apply the update:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_3 &= x_2 - \alpha \cdot \nabla f(x_2) \\
x_3 &= 1.68 - 0.3 \times (-0.64) \\
x_3 &= 1.68 + 0.192 \\
x_3 &= 1.872
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**
The true minimum of $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 5$ occurs at:

$$x^* = -\frac{b}{2a} = -\frac{(-4)}{2(1)} = 2$$

with minimum value $f(x^*) = 4 - 8 + 5 = 1$. The three iterations $0 \rightarrow 1.2 \rightarrow 1.68 \rightarrow 1.872$ are correctly marching **toward** $x^* = 2$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — 2D Vector Case (Robotic Trajectory)

> **Problem:** A robot's pose error cost is $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 3)^2 + (x_2 + 1)^2$. Starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^\top$ with $\alpha = 0.25$, perform **two iterations**.

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient Vector.**

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_1 - 3) \\ 2(x_2 + 1) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Iteration 0.**
Evaluate at $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^\top$:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0-3) \\ 2(0+1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Update:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - 0.25 \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{x}_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{x}_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 1.**
Evaluate at $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1.5, -0.5)^\top$:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(1.5 - 3) \\ 2(-0.5 + 1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Update:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix} - 0.25 \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{x}_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} -0.75 \\ 0.25 \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{x}_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.75 \\ -0.25 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Target Check.**
The unique global minimum is $\mathbf{x}^* = (3, -1)^\top$, with $f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$. Iterates $(0,0) \rightarrow (1.5, -0.5) \rightarrow (2.25, -0.75)$ are correctly **halving the residual** each step, consistent with the quadratic cost geometry. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Convergence Behavior Comparison

| Property | Batch GD | SGD | Mini-Batch GD | Momentum GD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data per step** | All $N$ | 1 | $m$ ($1 < m < N$) | All (with momentum) |
| **Convergence speed per step** | Slow (full pass) | Fast (one sample) | Balanced | Fast |
| **Noise in updates** | None | High | Medium | Low |
| **Escapes local minima?** | No | Yes (partially) | Partially | Yes |
| **Memory cost** | High | Low | Medium | High |
| **Typical $\alpha$** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $\alpha$ + $\beta \approx 0.9$ |
| **Robotics use** | Trajectory planning | Online adaptation | SLAM back-end, DL | Deep visuomotor policies |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of Gradient Descent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize x0, alpha, epsilon, Nmax] --> B[Compute gradient gx at current xk]
    B --> C{Is norm of gx less than epsilon?}
    C -- Yes --> Z[Converged: Report xk as solution]
    C -- No --> D[Check iteration counter: k less than Nmax]
    D -- No --> Y[Stop: Maximum iterations reached]
    D -- Yes --> E[Compute descent direction dk = minus gx]
    E --> F[Determine step size alpha_k via line search or backtracking]
    F --> G[Update xk plus 1 = xk plus alpha_k times dk]
    G --> H[Increment k = k plus 1]
    H --> B
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Robotic Navigation Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Input: LiDAR / Camera / IMU] --> B[Cost Function Module]
    B --> C[Compute Error Metric]
    C --> D[Gradient Computation Block]
    D --> E[Learning Rate Scheduler]
    E --> F[Update Rule Engine]
    F --> G[Robot State: Pose / Trajectory / Joint Angles]
    G --> H[Feedback to Sensor Input]
    H --> A
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#B0E0E6
    style C fill:#B0E0E6
    style D fill:#98FB98
    style E fill:#98FB98
    style F fill:#FFB6C1
    style G fill:#DDA0DD
    style H fill:#F0E68C
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Descent Trajectory (Cost Surface Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Point x0 on cost surface] -->|Largest step| B[x1: gradient steepest here]
    B -->|Smaller step| C[x2: descending slope]
    C -->|Even smaller step| D[x3: gentle slope near minimum]
    D -->|Tiny step| E[x4: very close to minimum]
    E -->|Convergence| F[Local minimum x star: gradient equals 0]
    style A fill:#FF6347
    style B fill:#FF8C00
    style C fill:#FFA500
    style D fill:#FFD700
    style E fill:#ADFF2F
    style F fill:#32CD32
```

### 4.4 Effect of Learning Rate on Convergence (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start x0] --> B{Learning Rate alpha}
    B --|alpha too small| C[Convergence: Very slow, many iterations]
    B --|alpha optimal| D[Convergence: Fast, monotonic decrease]
    B --|alpha too large| E[Oscillation: Bouncing across valley]
    B --|alpha extreme| F[Divergence: Cost grows unbounded]
    style C fill:#FFFFE0
    style D fill:#90EE90
    style E fill:#FFA07A
    style F fill:#FF6347
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define the Gradient Descent method for optimization. Write its iterative update rule and explain the role of the learning rate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Gradient Descent is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm that minimizes a differentiable function $f(\mathbf{x})$ by updating parameters in the direction opposite to the gradient of the function.
- **(1 mark) Update rule:** $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$
- **(1 mark) Role of learning rate $\alpha_k$:** It controls the **step size** of each update. A small $\alpha$ gives slow but stable convergence; a large $\alpha$ may overshoot or diverge; a properly chosen $\alpha$ ensures monotonic cost reduction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Batch Gradient Descent and Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Batch GD:** Computes the gradient using the **entire dataset** at each iteration. Update rule: $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla f_i(\mathbf{x}_k)$. Gives **deterministic, smooth** convergence but is **computationally expensive** for large $N$.
- **(1 mark) SGD:** Computes the gradient using **one randomly selected sample** at each iteration. Update rule: $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f_i(\mathbf{x}_k)$. Much **faster per iteration** and the noise helps **escape shallow local minima**, but convergence is **noisy**.
- **(1 mark) Advantage distinction:** Batch GD gives stable convergence on convex problems; SGD is preferred for online / streaming robotic data.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. **Provide answer to either Option A or Option B.**

---

### **OPTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**For the cost function** $f(x) = 2x^2 - 6x + 10$, **perform four iterations of gradient descent starting from** $x_0 = 1$ **with learning rate** $\alpha = 0.2$. **Tabulate your results and comment on convergence.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gradient calculation:**
$$\nabla f(x) = \frac{df}{dx} = 4x - 6$$

**Step 2 — Iterations:**

| Iteration $k$ | $x_k$ | $\nabla f(x_k) = 4x_k - 6$ | $x_{k+1} = x_k - 0.2 \cdot \nabla f(x_k)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.0000 | $4(1) - 6 = -2.0000$ | $1 - 0.2(-2) = 1.4000$ |
| 1 | 1.4000 | $4(1.4) - 6 = -0.4000$ | $1.4 - 0.2(-0.4) = 1.4800$ |
| 2 | 1.4800 | $4(1.48) - 6 = -0.0800$ | $1.48 - 0.2(-0.08) = 1.4960$ |
| 3 | 1.4960 | $4(1.496) - 6 = -0.0160$ | $1.496 - 0.2(-0.016) = 1.4992$ |

**Step 3 — Verification:** The true minimum is at $x^* = \dfrac{6}{4} = 1.5$, with $f(1.5) = 2(2.25) - 9 + 10 = 5.5$. The iterates $1.0 \to 1.4 \to 1.48 \to 1.496 \to 1.4992$ rapidly approach $1.5$.

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):** Convergence is **linear** for this strongly convex quadratic cost, with the residual shrinking by a factor of approximately $0.2 \times 4 = 0.8$ per step (as expected from the convergence rate formula). $\blacksquare$

---

**Q.A (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Explain the convergence properties of gradient descent for:**
**(i) Convex quadratic functions, and**
**(ii) Non-convex functions commonly encountered in robot navigation.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Convex Quadratic Functions [3.5 marks]:**
For a quadratic $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^\top A \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^\top \mathbf{x} + c$ with $A$ symmetric positive-definite (SPD):
- The gradient is $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$, a **linear** map.
- The optimal constant learning rate is $\alpha^* = \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{\max}(A) + \lambda_{\min}(A)}$, where $\lambda_{\max}$ and $\lambda_{\min}$ are the largest and smallest eigenvalues of $A$.
- Convergence is **linear** with rate $\rho = \dfrac{\kappa(A) - 1}{\kappa(A) + 1}$, where $\kappa(A) = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ is the **condition number**.
- **Ill-conditioning** ($\kappa \gg 1$) causes zig-zag trajectories and slow convergence.

**(ii) Non-Convex Functions in Robot Navigation [3.5 marks]:**
Robot navigation cost surfaces are often non-convex because of:
- Obstacle fields creating multiple local minima
- Non-holonomic constraints producing infeasible basins
- Sensor noise introducing spurious minima

Key properties:
- Gradient descent converges to a **stationary point** (local minimum, local maximum, or saddle point), **not** the global minimum.
- The algorithm **cannot distinguish** between these stationary points using only first-order information.
- In practice, **multi-start** strategies (running GD from many initial points) and **stochastic** variants are used to mitigate local-minima trapping.
- For non-convex SLAM costs, **second-order methods** (Gauss-Newton) or **trust-region** methods are preferred over vanilla gradient descent. $\blacksquare$

---

### **OPTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Derive the gradient descent update rule from the first-order Taylor approximation of the cost function. Clearly state all assumptions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption (1 mark):** $f(\mathbf{x})$ is continuously differentiable (at least $C^1$) in a neighborhood of the current iterate $\mathbf{x}_k$.

**Step 1 — Taylor Expansion (2 marks):** Around $\mathbf{x}_k$, the function can be expanded as:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \Delta \mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2}\Delta \mathbf{x}^\top H(\mathbf{x}_k) \Delta \mathbf{x} + \cdots$$

Truncating after the linear term gives the first-order approximation:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \Delta \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 — Minimization of the Linear Model (2 marks):** For a fixed norm $\Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert$, the linear term is minimized when $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ is **anti-parallel** to $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. By Cauchy-Schwarz:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \Delta \mathbf{x} \geq -\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert \cdot \Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert$$

The minimum is achieved by:

$$\Delta \mathbf{x} = -\alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k), \quad \alpha_k > 0$$

**Step 3 — Update Rule (1 mark):** Substituting:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):** The derivation assumes $\alpha_k$ is small enough that the dropped second-order term is negligible — i.e., the higher-order term $\frac{1}{2}\Delta \mathbf{x}^\top H \Delta \mathbf{x}$ does not dominate.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain with a suitable diagram how the Momentum-based Gradient Descent method improves upon vanilla gradient descent. Write its update equations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Vanilla GD Limitation (2 marks):** Vanilla gradient descent oscillates significantly in narrow valleys (high condition number) because each step is purely a function of the **current** gradient. When the gradient zig-zags across the valley walls, the algorithm struggles to make forward progress along the valley axis.

**Momentum GD Idea (2 marks):** Momentum GD introduces a **velocity** term that accumulates a decaying sum of past gradients, allowing the algorithm to:
- Dampen oscillations across the narrow valley walls.
- Accelerate progress along the gentle valley floor.

**Update Equations (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{v}_{k+1} = \beta \mathbf{v}_k + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{v}_{k+1}$$

where $\beta \in [0, 1)$ is the **momentum coefficient** (typically $0.9$). When $\beta = 0$, this reduces to vanilla GD.

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vanilla GD: zig-zag path] -->|Add momentum| B[Momentum GD: smoothed path]
    B --> C[Faster convergence]
    style A fill:#FF6347
    style B fill:#90EE90
    style C fill:#87CEEB
```

The zig-zag of vanilla GD is **smoothed out** by momentum into a more direct trajectory. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Missing the learning-rate sign:** Many students mistakenly write $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \nabla f$ (gradient **ascent**!). Always use a **minus** sign for descent.
> 2. **Confusing the gradient with the direction:** The gradient $\nabla f$ points **uphill**; the **descent direction** is $-\nabla f$.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify convergence:** A complete answer must include a stopping-criterion check and a remark on whether the cost is monotonically decreasing.
> 4. **Skipping the iteration table:** For numerical problems, the **table of $(x_k, \nabla f(x_k), x_{k+1})$** is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Using absolute-value pipes `\|` in markdown tables:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table formatting during online evaluation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- The **update rule** is $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ — memorise it **verbatim**.
- Gradient descent is a **first-order** method; it uses only gradient information, no Hessian.
- The **learning rate** $\alpha$ controls step size: too small ⟹ slow; too large ⟹ diverges.
- **Convex functions** ⟹ global minimum (unique). **Non-convex** ⟹ local minimum / saddle point.
- For a quadratic with Hessian $A$ (SPD), the optimal $\alpha = \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{\max} + \lambda_{\min}}$.
- **SGD** uses one sample per step — fast but noisy; **Batch GD** uses all samples — stable but slow.
- **Momentum GD** adds a velocity term to dampen oscillations in narrow valleys.
- **Stopping criteria:** $\Vert \nabla f \Vert < \epsilon$ **OR** $\vert \Delta f \vert < \epsilon$ **OR** iteration limit reached.
- For SLAM, ICP, and trajectory optimization, gradient descent (and its second-order cousins) is the **core algorithm**.
- In a 1-D quadratic, the iterates form a **geometric sequence** converging to the optimum — useful for quick exam verification.
- **Armijo backtracking line search** guarantees monotonic cost decrease per step.
- The **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** underpins why $-\nabla f$ is the steepest descent direction.
- Always **state assumptions** (differentiability, step size bound) when deriving or applying GD.
- **Mermaid pitfall:** Never use reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, or `style` as node IDs; always prefix IDs alphabetically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradient Descent Without and With Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradient Descent Without and With Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Gradient Descent** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable objective function $f(\mathbf{x}): \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. In the context of **Robot Navigation**, it is the foundational numerical technique used to compute optimal trajectories, plan collision-free paths, tune cost functions for SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), and minimize energy expenditure during motion planning.

The method works by repeatedly updating the control variable $\mathbf{x}$ (which may represent robot pose, joint angles, waypoints, or actuator commands) in the **direction opposite to the gradient** of the cost function, since the negative gradient points toward the steepest descent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition:**
> *"Gradient descent is an iterative optimization scheme that minimizes a scalar-valued cost function $J(\mathbf{x})$ by taking steps proportional to the negative of the gradient $\nabla J(\mathbf{x})$, with or without equality/inequality constraints on the feasible region of $\mathbf{x}$."*

### 1.1.1 Unconstrained Gradient Descent

When the feasible set $\mathcal{X} = \mathbb{R}^n$, the algorithm freely minimizes $f(\mathbf{x})$ over the entire $n$-dimensional Euclidean space without any boundary restrictions.

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

where $\alpha_k > 0$ is the **learning rate** (also called the **step size**).

### 1.1.2 Constrained Gradient Descent

When the optimization variable must satisfy constraints (e.g., robot cannot cross obstacle regions, joint angle limits, velocity bounds), the feasible set $\mathcal{X}$ is restricted:

$$\mathcal{X} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$$

The constrained problem is formally stated as:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}} f(\mathbf{x})$$

This becomes:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \; f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Hiker

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a **blindfolded hiker** standing on a fog-covered mountain slope, trying to reach the lowest valley. They cannot see the terrain, but they can **feel the slope beneath their feet**. At every step, they probe the ground and move a small distance in the direction where the slope descends most steeply. They repeat this process — **probing, deciding, stepping** — until the ground becomes flat (gradient is zero).
> - **Without constraints** → The hiker can walk anywhere on the mountain.
> - **With constraints** → There are **fenced boundaries** (cliffs, walls, restricted zones) the hiker must avoid; if a proposed step would cross a fence, they must project the step back inside the safe region.

This is *exactly* how gradient descent works in robot navigation: the **cost landscape** represents terrain elevation, the **gradient** is the slope, and the **constraints** represent obstacles or robot physical limits.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Robot Navigation

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Step size (learning rate) | $\alpha$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| Convergence tolerance | $\epsilon$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| Gradient norm threshold | $\Vert \nabla f \Vert$ | $< 10^{-4}$ for typical stop |
| Obstacle margin | $d_{\text{safe}}$ | **0.3 m** – **0.5 m** |
| Joint angle limit (typical arm) | $\theta_{\min}, \theta_{\max}$ | $-180°$ to $+180°$ |
| Maximum linear velocity | $v_{\max}$ | **0.5 m/s** – **2.0 m/s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step-by-Step Operating Principle

### 2.1.1 Unconstrained Gradient Descent Logic Flow

The algorithm follows a strict, deterministic iterative procedure:

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose an initial guess $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \mathbb{R}^n$, a step size $\alpha > 0$, and a tolerance $\epsilon > 0$. Set iteration counter $k = 0$.

- **Step 2 — Gradient Evaluation:** Compute the gradient vector of the cost function at the current point:
$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) = \left[ \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right]^{\mathsf{T}}$$

- **Step 3 — Stopping Criterion Check:** If $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert < \epsilon$ or $\vert f(\mathbf{x}_k) - f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}) \vert < \epsilon$, declare convergence and return $\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{x}_k$.

- **Step 4 — Update Rule:** Compute the next iterate by moving in the negative gradient direction:
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

- **Step 5 — Increment and Repeat:** Set $k \leftarrow k+1$ and return to Step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why move in the direction $-\nabla f$?** Because $\nabla f$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent** (greatest increase). To **minimize** $f$, we must move **opposite** to the gradient, i.e., along $-\nabla f$.

### 2.1.2 Why the Update Rule Works — First-Order Taylor Approximation

The update rule is **derived** from a first-order Taylor expansion of $f$ around $\mathbf{x}_k$. For an infinitesimal displacement $\Delta \mathbf{x}$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x}$$

To **minimize** the right-hand side, we want $\nabla f^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x}$ to be **most negative**. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, this is achieved when:

$$\Delta \mathbf{x} = -\alpha \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k), \quad \alpha > 0$$

giving the canonical gradient descent update.

## 2.2 Constrained Gradient Descent — The Three Classical Methods

When $\mathbf{x}$ must remain inside a feasible set $\mathcal{X}$, three classical approaches are taught in KTU 2024:

### 2.2.1 Method 1 — Projected Gradient Descent

After each unconstrained step, project the result back onto $\mathcal{X}$:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{X}}\left(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\right)$$

where $\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{X}}(\cdot)$ is the Euclidean projection operator:

$$\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{X}}(\mathbf{y}) = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert_2^2$$

This is **ideal for simple box constraints** like $a_i \le x_i \le b_i$, where projection is element-wise clipping.

### 2.2.2 Method 2 — Penalty Method

Augment the objective with a penalty term that becomes large when constraints are violated:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \; \Phi_{\mu}(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{\mu}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \max(0, g_i(\mathbf{x}))^2 + \frac{\mu}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{p} h_j(\mathbf{x})^2$$

where $\mu \gg 1$ is the **penalty parameter**. As $\mu \to \infty$, the solution of the unconstrained penalty problem converges to the constrained optimum.

### 2.2.3 Method 3 — Lagrangian (Augmented) Method

Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\lambda_i, \nu_j$ for each inequality and equality constraint:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \lambda, \nu) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \nu_j \, h_j(\mathbf{x})$$

The **KKT (Karush–Kuhn–Tucker) conditions** for optimality are:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j} \nu_j^* \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0, \quad h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

$$\lambda_i^* \ge 0, \quad \lambda_i^* g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \quad \text{(complementary slackness)}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are the core of every KTU Module 3 numerical.**

| # | Concept | Equation / Condition | Typical Use in Robot Navigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unconstrained update | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Trajectory smoothing, calibration |
| 2 | Convergence condition | $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) \Vert = 0$ | Stationary point reached |
| 3 | Line search (Armijo) | $f(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k) - c \alpha \Vert \nabla f \Vert^2$ | Adaptive step size selection |
| 4 | Projection operator | $\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{X}}(\mathbf{y}) = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert$ | Obstacle-avoidance box projection |
| 5 | Penalty objective | $\Phi_{\mu}(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{\mu}{2} \sum \max(0, g_i)^2$ | Soft constraint enforcement |
| 6 | KKT gradient stationarity | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{0}$ | Constrained optimality check |
| 7 | Complementary slackness | $\lambda_i^* g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Active/inactive constraint test |
| 8 | Step size bound (convex) | $0 < \alpha < \frac{2}{L}$ (where $L$ is Lipschitz constant of $\nabla f$) | Guaranteed convergence |
| 9 | Cost in path planning | $J = \int_0^T \left( \Vert \mathbf{v}(t) \Vert^2 + w \, d_{\text{obs}}(t)^{-1} \right) dt$ | Smooth, obstacle-aware trajectories |
| 10 | Obstacle repulsion gradient | $\nabla d_{\text{obs}}(\mathbf{x})$ points away from obstacle | Reactive collision avoidance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world KTU-aligned robotics projects (autonomous ground vehicles, UAV swarms, manipulator arms), gradient descent is embedded inside **MPC (Model Predictive Control)** loops, where the cost function penalizes tracking error, control effort, and proximity to obstacles — solved online at **50–200 Hz**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Minimizing a cost combining lane deviation, fuel use, and time-to-collision.
- **Robotic Manipulators:** Inverse kinematics solved by gradient descent on end-effector pose error.
- **Drone Path Planning:** Smooth trajectory generation in cluttered 3-D environments with obstacle constraints.
- **SLAM Front-End:** Pose graph optimization is gradient descent on a non-linear least-squares cost.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Policy gradient methods (REINFORCE, PPO) descend the expected reward landscape.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gradient Descent Update Rule from Taylor Series

**Goal:** Show rigorously that $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ is the optimal small-step direction to reduce $f$.

### Step 1 — Write the First-Order Taylor Expansion

For a differentiable scalar function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, the first-order Taylor expansion around the current point $\mathbf{x}_k$ with perturbation $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x} + \mathcal{O}(\Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert^2)$$

**Logic:** When $\Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert$ is small, the higher-order terms are negligible compared to the linear term.

### Step 2 — Drop the Higher-Order Term

For infinitesimal steps, the residual $\mathcal{O}(\Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert^2) \to 0$. So we approximate:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x}$$

### Step 3 — Decide the Direction of $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ that Decreases $f$

We want $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) < f(\mathbf{x}_k)$, which requires:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x} < 0$$

### Step 4 — Apply Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality

By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality:

$$\left\vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x} \right\vert \le \Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert \, \Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert$$

The **most negative** value of $\nabla f^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x}$ is achieved when $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ is **anti-parallel** to $\nabla f$, i.e.:

$$\Delta \mathbf{x} = -\alpha \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k), \quad \alpha > 0$$

Substituting:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x} = -\alpha \, \Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert^2 < 0$$

This is the **maximum possible decrease** for a step of length $\alpha \Vert \nabla f \Vert$.

### Step 5 — Final Update Rule

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}$$

**Interpretation:** The step size $\alpha_k$ scales how aggressively we follow the negative gradient direction at each iteration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Example: Unconstrained Minimization

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = x^2 + 2x + 5$ starting from $x_0 = 5$ with step size $\alpha = 0.1$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Gradient

The derivative (1-D gradient) is:

$$\nabla f(x) = \frac{df}{dx} = 2x + 2$$

### Step 2 — Apply Update Rule Iteratively

The general update is:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha (2 x_k + 2)$$

Substituting $\alpha = 0.1$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - 0.1 (2 x_k + 2) = 0.8 x_k - 0.2$$

### Step 3 — Compute Iterations

| Iteration $k$ | $x_k$ | $\nabla f(x_k) = 2x_k + 2$ | $x_{k+1} = x_k - 0.1 \nabla f$ | $f(x_k)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 5.0000 | 12.0000 | 5.0000 − 1.2000 = 3.8000 | 40.0000 |
| 1 | 3.8000 | 9.6000 | 3.8000 − 0.9600 = 2.8400 | 25.0400 |
| 2 | 2.8400 | 7.6800 | 2.8400 − 0.7680 = 2.0720 | 18.6656 |
| 3 | 2.0720 | 6.1440 | 2.0720 − 0.6144 = 1.4576 | 14.6050 |
| 4 | 1.4576 | 4.9152 | 1.4576 − 0.4915 = 0.9661 | 11.9650 |
| 5 | 0.9661 | 3.9322 | 0.9661 − 0.3932 = 0.5729 | 10.0790 |
| 10 | ≈ −0.7133 | ≈ 0.5734 | ≈ −0.7707 | ≈ 4.3472 |
| 20 | ≈ −0.9878 | ≈ 0.0244 | ≈ −0.9903 | ≈ 4.0002 |
| 50 | ≈ −0.99999 | ≈ 0.00002 | ≈ −1.00000 | ≈ 4.00000 |

### Step 4 — Analytical Solution Verification

Setting $\nabla f = 0$:

$$2x + 2 = 0 \implies x^* = -1$$

$$f(x^*) = (-1)^2 + 2(-1) + 5 = 1 - 2 + 5 = 4$$

**Conclusion:** Gradient descent converges to $x^* = -1$, $f^* = 4$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Example: Constrained Minimization via Penalty Method

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x, y) = (x-2)^2 + (y-2)^2$ subject to $x + y \ge 3$, starting from $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$, with penalty $\mu = 50$.

### Step 1 — Rewrite Constraint in Standard Form

The inequality $x + y \ge 3$ becomes $g(x, y) = 3 - x - y \le 0$.

### Step 2 — Formulate the Penalty Function

$$\Phi_{\mu}(x, y) = (x-2)^2 + (y-2)^2 + \frac{\mu}{2} \max(0, 3 - x - y)^2$$

For points violating $x + y \ge 3$ (i.e., where $x + y < 3$):

$$\Phi_{\mu} = (x-2)^2 + (y-2)^2 + \frac{\mu}{2} (3 - x - y)^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute Gradient of the Penalty Function

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x} = 2(x-2) + \mu(3 - x - y)(-1) = 2(x-2) - \mu(3 - x - y)$$

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial y} = 2(y-2) + \mu(3 - x - y)(-1) = 2(y-2) - \mu(3 - x - y)$$

Substituting $\mu = 50$:

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x} = 2(x-2) - 50(3 - x - y)$$

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial y} = 2(y-2) - 50(3 - x - y)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Gradient Descent Update

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_{k+1} \\ y_{k+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \end{bmatrix} - \alpha \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_k - 2) - 50(3 - x_k - y_k) \\ 2(y_k - 2) - 50(3 - x_k - y_k) \end{bmatrix}$$

Using $\alpha = 0.02$:

**Iteration 0:** $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$. Compute penalty gradient components:

$$2(0 - 2) = -4, \quad 50(3 - 0 - 0) = 150$$

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x} = -4 - 150 = -154$$

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial y} = -4 - 150 = -154$$

Update:

$$x_1 = 0 - 0.02(-154) = 3.08$$

$$y_1 = 0 - 0.02(-154) = 3.08$$

### Step 5 — Analytical Constrained Optimum

Without constraints: minimum at $(2, 2)$, where $x + y = 4 \ge 3$ → constraint **not active**. Therefore, the constrained solution equals the unconstrained solution: $(x^*, y^*) = (2, 2)$, with $f^* = 0$.

Let us verify via continued iteration; the algorithm overshoots due to large $\mu$, but converges toward $(2, 2)$ on the boundary or just inside it.

### Step 6 — Active vs. Inactive Constraint Test

If unconstrained optimum satisfied constraint → **inactive constraint**, no penalty effect at optimum.
If unconstrained optimum violated constraint → **active constraint**, penalty shifts optimum onto feasible boundary.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Feature | Unconstrained Gradient Descent | Constrained Gradient Descent |
|---|---|---|
| Feasible set $\mathcal{X}$ | $\mathbb{R}^n$ (entire space) | Subset defined by $g_i, h_j$ |
| Update rule | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f$ | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{X}}(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f)$ or penalty |
| Convergence check | $\Vert \nabla f \Vert \approx 0$ | KKT conditions satisfied |
| Optimality condition | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i + \sum \nu_j \nabla h_j = 0$ |
| Computation cost per step | Low (gradient only) | Higher (projection or multiplier update) |
| Robot navigation use case | Free-space trajectory smoothing | Obstacle-aware, joint-limit-aware planning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Unconstrained Gradient Descent Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize x0, alpha, epsilon] --> B[Set k = 0]
    B --> C[Compute gradient gradFx = grad f at xk]
    C --> D{norm gradFx less than epsilon?}
    D -- Yes --> Z[Converged: Return x star = xk]
    D -- No --> E[Update: xk+1 = xk - alpha times gradFx]
    E --> F[Increment k = k + 1]
    F --> C
    Z --> H[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Constrained Gradient Descent (Projected Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize x0 in feasible set X] --> B[Set k = 0]
    B --> C[Compute gradient gradFx = grad f at xk]
    C --> D[Compute candidate step: y = xk - alpha times gradFx]
    D --> E[Project y onto feasible set X]
    E --> F[xk+1 = Projection of y on X]
    F --> G{xk+1 inside X?}
    G -- No --> H[Apply penalty correction]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I{norm gradFx less than epsilon?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Converged: Return x star]
    I -- No --> K[Increment k = k + 1]
    K --> C
    J --> L[End]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Robot Navigation Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Robot State xk: pose, velocity, joint angles] --> S2[Cost Function f: tracking error, control effort, obstacle proximity]
    S2 --> S3[Gradient Computation: grad f at xk]
    S3 --> S4{Constraints Active?}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Unconstrained Update: xk+1 = xk - alpha grad f]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6[Constrained Module]
    S6 --> S7[Penalty Method]
    S6 --> S8[Projected Gradient]
    S6 --> S9[KKT Lagrangian]
    S7 --> S10[Augmented Objective Phi mu]
    S8 --> S11[Project onto Feasible Set]
    S9 --> S12[Multipliers lambda, nu]
    S10 --> S13[Apply unconstrained gradient descent]
    S11 --> S13
    S12 --> S13
    S5 --> S14[New Robot State xk+1]
    S13 --> S14
    S14 --> S15[Robot Actuator Commands]
    S15 --> S16[Sensor Feedback]
    S16 --> S1
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Diagram — Cost Landscape with and without Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph UC[Unconstrained Landscape]
        U1[Start point x0 on slope] --> U2[Descend freely along neg grad f]
        U2 --> U3[Reach global minimum x star at valley bottom]
    end
    subgraph CC[Constrained Landscape with Wall]
        C1[Start point x0] --> C2[Descend along neg grad f]
        C2 --> C3[Hit wall constraint g less than 0]
        C3 --> C4[Project step onto feasible boundary]
        C4 --> C5[Slide along boundary to constrained optimum x constrained]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**
**Q:** *Define gradient descent. Write the update rule used in unconstrained gradient descent and explain the role of the learning rate $\alpha$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** **Definition:** Gradient descent is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm that minimizes a differentiable scalar cost function $f(\mathbf{x})$ by moving the variable in the direction opposite to the gradient.
- **(1 Mark)** **Update Rule:** 
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$
- **(1 Mark)** **Role of $\alpha$:** The learning rate $\alpha$ controls the step size at each iteration. If $\alpha$ is too large, the algorithm diverges; if $\alpha$ is too small, convergence is very slow. For a convex $L$-Lipschitz gradient function, $\alpha \in (0, 2/L)$ guarantees convergence.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** *State the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions for a constrained optimization problem with inequality constraints.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For the problem $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, $i = 1, \ldots, m$, the KKT conditions are:
- **(1 Mark) Stationarity:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$
- **(1 Mark) Primal Feasibility:** $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0$ for all $i$
- **(1 Mark) Dual Feasibility + Complementary Slackness:** $\lambda_i \ge 0$ and $\lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ for all $i$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 3]`**

**Q:** With the help of a suitable cost function used in robot navigation:
**(a)** Derive the unconstrained gradient descent update rule from first principles using Taylor series expansion. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Apply the algorithm to minimize $f(x, y) = (x - 3)^2 + (y - 1)^2$ starting from $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$ with step size $\alpha = 0.1$. Perform 4 iterations and verify the analytical solution. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

The first-order Taylor expansion of $f$ around $\mathbf{x}_k$ is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x}$$

**(2 Marks)** For infinitesimal steps, higher-order terms vanish. To **decrease** $f$, we require:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x} < 0$$

**(2 Marks)** By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, this inner product is most negative when $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla f$:

$$\Delta \mathbf{x} = -\alpha \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k), \quad \alpha > 0$$

**(2 Marks)** Substituting yields the canonical update:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

**(1 Mark)** Justification: the chosen $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ maximizes the rate of decrease $\nabla f^{\mathsf{T}} \Delta \mathbf{x} = -\alpha \Vert \nabla f \Vert^2$.

**(b) Numerical Application (7 Marks):**

Compute the gradient:

$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x - 3) \\ 2(y - 1) \end{bmatrix}$$

Update rule with $\alpha = 0.1$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - 0.1 \cdot 2(x_k - 3) = x_k - 0.2 x_k + 0.6 = 0.8 x_k + 0.6$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k - 0.1 \cdot 2(y_k - 1) = y_k - 0.2 y_k + 0.2 = 0.8 y_k + 0.2$$

**(1 Mark) Iteration Table:**

| $k$ | $x_k$ | $y_k$ | $\nabla f_x$ | $\nabla f_y$ | $x_{k+1}$ | $y_{k+1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | −6.0000 | −2.0000 | 0.6000 | 0.2000 |
| 1 | 0.6000 | 0.2000 | −4.8000 | −1.6000 | 1.0800 | 0.3600 |
| 2 | 1.0800 | 0.3600 | −3.8400 | −1.2800 | 1.4640 | 0.4880 |
| 3 | 1.4640 | 0.4880 | −3.0720 | −1.0240 | 1.7712 | 0.5904 |
| 4 | 1.7712 | 0.5904 | −2.4576 | −0.8192 | 2.0170 | 0.6723 |

**(2 Marks)** Pattern observation: $x_k \to 3$ and $y_k \to 1$ geometrically with ratio $0.8$.

**(2 Marks)** Analytical verification: setting $\nabla f = 0$ gives $x^* = 3$, $y^* = 1$, $f^* = 0$. The trajectory confirms convergence.

**(2 Marks)** Closed-form solution: $x_k = 3 - 3(0.8)^k$, $y_k = 1 - (0.8)^k$, showing geometric convergence.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 3]`**

**Q:** In robot navigation, a mobile robot must minimize the cost $f(x, y) = (x - 5)^2 + (y - 5)^2$ subject to the obstacle constraint $x + y \ge 4$ (i.e., $g(x, y) = 4 - x - y \le 0$):
**(a)** Explain the **penalty method** for constrained optimization. Formulate the penalty objective for this problem using penalty parameter $\mu = 20$. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Starting from $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$ with step size $\alpha = 0.05$, perform 3 iterations of gradient descent on the penalty function. Comment on whether the unconstrained optimum is feasible. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Penalty Method Formulation (7 Marks):**

**(2 Marks)** The penalty method converts a constrained problem into an unconstrained one by adding a term that grows large when constraints are violated:

$$\Phi_{\mu}(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{\mu}{2} \sum_{i} \max(0, g_i(\mathbf{x}))^2$$

**(2 Marks)** Substituting the given $f$ and $g$:

$$\Phi_{\mu}(x, y) = (x - 5)^2 + (y - 5)^2 + \frac{\mu}{2} \max(0, 4 - x - y)^2$$

With $\mu = 20$:

$$\Phi_{20}(x, y) = (x - 5)^2 + (y - 5)^2 + 10 \cdot \max(0, 4 - x - y)^2$$

**(1 Mark)** Since $(0, 0)$ violates the constraint ($0 + 0 = 0 < 4$), the active form is:

$$\Phi_{20}(x, y) = (x - 5)^2 + (y - 5)^2 + 10 (4 - x - y)^2$$

**(2 Marks)** Compute the gradient of $\Phi_{20}$:

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x} = 2(x - 5) + 10 \cdot 2(4 - x - y)(-1) = 2(x - 5) - 20(4 - x - y)$$

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial y} = 2(y - 5) - 20(4 - x - y)$$

**(b) Iterative Application (7 Marks):**

**(1 Mark)** Update rule:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_{k+1} \\ y_{k+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \end{bmatrix} - 0.05 \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_k - 5) - 20(4 - x_k - y_k) \\ 2(y_k - 5) - 20(4 - x_k - y_k) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 0 → 1:** $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$.

$$2(0 - 5) = -10, \quad 20(4 - 0 - 0) = 80$$

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x}\bigg|_0 = -10 - 80 = -90$$

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial y}\bigg|_0 = -10 - 80 = -90$$

Update:

$$x_1 = 0 - 0.05(-90) = 4.5, \quad y_1 = 0 - 0.05(-90) = 4.5$$

**(2 Marks)** Check constraint: $4.5 + 4.5 = 9 \ge 4$ ✓ (constraint now satisfied, penalty becomes inactive).

**Iteration 1 → 2:** $(x_1, y_1) = (4.5, 4.5)$. Constraint inactive, so penalty gradient is **zero** for the constraint term. Only $f$ gradient remains:

$$2(4.5 - 5) = -1, \quad 2(4.5 - 5) = -1$$

Update:

$$x_2 = 4.5 - 0.05(-1) = 4.55, \quad y_2 = 4.5 - 0.05(-1) = 4.55$$

**(2 Marks)** Iteration 2 → 3: $(x_2, y_2) = (4.55, 4.55)$.

$$2(4.55 - 5) = -0.9$$

$$x_3 = 4.55 - 0.05(-0.9) = 4.595$$

$$y_3 = 4.595$$

**(2 Marks)** **Feasibility Comment:** The unconstrained optimum $(5, 5)$ satisfies $5 + 5 = 10 \ge 4$, so the **constraint is inactive** and the constrained solution equals the unconstrained one: $(x^*, y^*) = (5, 5)$. The penalty method correctly handles this — once inside the feasible region, it reverts to standard gradient descent.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
