> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the update rule. The step is in the direction of $-\nabla f$, not $+\nabla f$. This alone costs 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Confusing gradient with directional derivative.** Always use the full gradient vector $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ in $n$-D problems; never substitute scalar derivatives component-wise without showing $\partial f / \partial x_i$.
> 3. **Forgetting to check feasibility** in penalty method problems. After each iteration, verify whether the constraint is satisfied or violated — the form of $\Phi_\mu$ changes accordingly.
> 4. **Step size selection errors:** $\alpha = 1$ is rarely correct. Always show the calculation and justify choice via Armijo or Lipschitz condition.
> 5. **Missing the KKT complementary slackness** condition. Inactive constraints have $\lambda_i = 0$; active constraints have $\lambda_i > 0$. Examiners specifically test this.
> 6. **No convergence proof shown.** Even in numerical problems, write the stopping criterion (e.g., $\Vert \nabla f \Vert < 10^{-4}$) explicitly to earn full methodology marks.
> 7. **Treating constrained descent as unconstrained.** In a constrained problem, after every step you must project or check $g_i(x_{k+1}) \le 0$. Skipping this loses 3+ marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gradient Descent Without and With Constraints**

- **Unconstrained update rule:** $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ — negative gradient direction.
- **Learning rate range:** For $L$-Lipschitz gradient, $\alpha \in (0, 2/L)$ guarantees convergence on convex functions.
- **Convergence criterion:** $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) \Vert = 0$ at stationary point; practical stopping: $\Vert \nabla f \Vert < \epsilon$ or relative change in $f < \epsilon$.
- **Constrained descent methods:** Projected gradient, penalty method ($\mu$-scaled augmented objective), Lagrangian/KKT.
- **Penalty method equation:** $\Phi_\mu(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{\mu}{2} \sum \max(0, g_i)^2 + \frac{\mu}{2} \sum h_j^2$.
- **KKT necessary conditions:** stationarity + primal feasibility + dual feasibility + complementary slackness.
- **Projection operator:** $\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{X}}(\mathbf{y})$ — closest point in $\mathcal{X}$ to $\mathbf{y}$. For box constraints, projection = element-wise clip.
- **Active vs. inactive constraint:** If $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ → active, $\lambda_i > 0$; if $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ → inactive, $\lambda_i = 0$.
- **Robot navigation application:** Cost combines tracking error + control effort + obstacle proximity; gradient descent solved at MPC control rate (50–200 Hz).
- **Common numerical example form:** $f(x) = (x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2$ with gradient $[2(x-a), 2(y-b)]^{\mathsf{T}}$.
- **Geometric convergence rate** for linear systems: ratio = $1 - \alpha L_{\min}$ per iteration.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Always verify the constraint is satisfied at the final iterate, not just that the gradient is small.
- **Symmetry shortcut:** When the feasible region contains the unconstrained minimum, the constrained and unconstrained solutions are identical.
- **Why gradient descent over alternatives?** First-order, low memory, simple to implement — ideal for real-time robot navigation where Hessian computation is infeasible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minkowski Sum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minkowski Sum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Minkowski Sum** (also called the *Minkowski addition* or *configuration space obstacle expansion*) is a binary operation on two sets of points that produces a third set whose elements are the vector sums of all possible pairs of points taken from the two input sets.

Mathematically, for two sets $A$ and $B$ in a Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$ (typically $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$ for planar and spatial robotics respectively), the Minkowski Sum is formally defined as:

$$A \oplus B = \{ a + b \mid a \in A, \; b \in B \}$$

In the context of **robot motion planning**, the Minkowski Sum is used to compute the **Configuration Space Obstacle (C-obstacle)**. Given a robot $R$ with a defined shape and an obstacle $O$ in the workspace, the C-obstacle is given by:

$$C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R) = \{ o - r \mid o \in O, \; r \in R \}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The negative sign on the robot shape $-R$ accounts for the fact that we are conceptually "growing" the obstacle by sweeping the robot shape around it. The reference point of the robot is treated as a point in configuration space, reducing the problem to *point-robot motion planning*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to walk through a cluttered room with a backpack on. Instead of carefully considering the shape of the backpack at every step, what if you simply "inflated" every piece of furniture by the size of your backpack? The room would look much smaller, but you could now treat yourself as a single point and just avoid the inflated furniture. That is exactly what the Minkowski Sum does in robotics.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Room Analogy:**
> - **Original Furniture (Obstacle $O$):** The actual physical obstacle in the room.
> - **Backpack Shape ($R$):** The shape of the robot (you + backpack).
> - **Inflated Furniture ($O \oplus (-R)$):** The new "no-go" region. Anywhere you place your reference point (center of mass) inside this region means part of the backpack collides.
> - **Point Robot:** Once inflated, you just plan a path for a single point, ignoring robot geometry entirely.

## 1.3 Key Properties and Notation Conventions

- **Commutativity:** $A \oplus B = B \oplus A$ (the order of addition does not matter).
- **Associativity:** $(A \oplus B) \oplus C = A \oplus (B \oplus C)$ (sequential sweeping produces the same final region).
- **Empty Set:** $A \oplus \emptyset = \emptyset$ (colliding with nothing still gives nothing).
- **Dilation by a Point:** If $B = \{b\}$ is a single point, then $A \oplus B$ is simply $A$ translated by vector $b$.
- **Standard Notation:** Sometimes written as $A + B$ in literature, but $A \oplus B$ is the canonical robotics notation to avoid confusion with regular set union.

> [!TIP]
> The Minkowski Sum is the **fundamental geometric primitive** that bridges the gap between *workspace planning* (where obstacles have real shape) and *configuration space planning* (where the robot is a point). Almost every modern autonomous vehicle, drone swarm, and robotic arm planner uses this operation in some form.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Minkowski Sum operation can be understood through the following structured logic:

1. **Identify the Two Input Sets:**
   - Set $A$: Typically the **obstacle** $O$ in the workspace.
   - Set $B$: Typically the **robot** $R$ (or its negative $-R$ for obstacle expansion).

2. **Reflect and Translate the Robot:**
   - Compute the negative of the robot shape by reflecting it through the origin: $-R = \{-r \mid r \in R\}$.
   - For a convex robot centered at the origin, this simply rotates the robot by $\pi$ radians (**180 degrees**).

3. **Perform the Sweep Operation:**
   - Conceptually, the set $-R$ is placed with its reference point at every boundary point of the obstacle $O$.
   - The union of all these translated copies forms the Minkowski Sum.

4. **Verify Boundary Inclusion:**
   - The boundary of the Minkowski Sum is traced by the boundary of $-R$ as it is swept along the boundary of $O$.
   - This is computationally relevant: for convex polygons, the boundary can be computed in $O(m+n)$ time using the **rotating calipers algorithm**.

5. **Use the Result for Planning:**
   - The Minkowski Sum region is marked as **forbidden** in the configuration space.
   - The free space is the complement: $C_{free} = C_{space} \setminus C_{obs}$.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

**Why use Minkowski Sum?**
- It transforms a complex *shape-vs-shape collision query* into a much simpler *point-in-region test*.
- This is computationally cheaper when planning many paths through a static environment.
- It enables the use of standard graph-search algorithms (A*, Dijkstra, RRT) directly on configuration space.

**How is it implemented practically?**
- For **convex polygons**, the Minkowski Sum is itself a convex polygon whose vertices are the sorted union of the vertices of the input polygons.
- For **non-convex shapes**, the result is computed by decomposing the shapes into convex components and taking the union of pairwise convex Minkowski Sums (this can cause over-counting, so careful bookkeeping is required).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Minkowski Sum (Definition)** | $A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A, \; b \in B\}$ | Set of all pairwise vector sums | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Configuration Space Obstacle** | $C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$ | Inflated obstacle region | $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$ |
| **Negative (Reflected) Robot** | $-R = \{-r \mid r \in R\}$ | Robot reflected through origin | $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$ |
| **Configuration Space** | $C = C_{free} \cup C_{obs}$ | Partition of all robot configurations | Dimension = DOF |
| **Minkowski Difference** | $A \ominus B = \{a - b \mid a \in A, \; b \in B\}$ | Equivalent to $A \oplus (-B)$ | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Convex Polygon Vertex Sort** | $\theta_{i} = \text{atan2}(v_{i,y}, v_{i,x})$ | Angle-based merging for convex sums | Radians |
| **Translation Invariance** | $(A \oplus \{p\}) = A + p$ | Sum with a point = translation | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Scaling Property** | $\lambda A \oplus \lambda B = \lambda(A \oplus B)$ | Linear scaling of inputs | Scalar $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| **Commutativity** | $A \oplus B = B \oplus A$ | Order independence | Set operation |
| **Associativity** | $(A \oplus B) \oplus C = A \oplus (B \oplus C)$ | Grouping independence | Set operation |
| **Distance Query (Boundary)** | $d(p, A \oplus B) = \min_{a \in A, b \in B} \lVert p - (a+b) \rVert$ | Point-to-sum distance | Meters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Note for Exams:** Always write the negative sign explicitly: $O \oplus (-R)$. Writing $O \oplus R$ without negation will result in a *physically incorrect* configuration space obstacle and cost you full marks in derivation problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Minkowski Sum |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Inflating lane boundaries and other cars by vehicle footprint for safe trajectory generation |
| **Warehouse Robotics (AGV)** | Pre-computing C-obstacles for static shelving units to enable rapid path replanning |
| **Surgical Robotics** | Inflating anatomical structures (tumors, vessels) by tool radius for safe needle insertion |
| **Aerial Swarms (Drones)** | Collision-free formation flight by inflating each drone's safety bubble |
| **Manipulator Arms** | Reachability analysis and self-collision avoidance in joint space |
| **Maritime Robotics (USVs)** | Path planning with current effects and ship maneuvering footprint |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minkowski Sum of Two Convex Polygons Using the Rotating Calipers Principle

Let $A$ be a convex polygon with $m$ vertices and $B$ be a convex polygon with $n$ vertices. We want to derive the algorithm to compute $A \oplus B$.

### Setup
Let the vertices of $A$ be $\{a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{m-1}\}$ ordered counter-clockwise (CCW) starting from the vertex with the smallest $y$-coordinate. Let the vertices of $B$ be $\{b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{n-1}\}$ also ordered CCW from the smallest $y$-coordinate.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Represent edges as vectors.**

Define the edge vectors of $A$ as:

$$e_i^A = a_{i+1} - a_i \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, \dots, m-1$$

Define the edge vectors of $B$ similarly:

$$e_j^B = b_{j+1} - b_j \quad \text{for } j = 0, 1, \dots, n-1$$

These edge vectors point in the CCW direction around each polygon. This step *converts the geometric shape data into a list of directional increments*.

**Step 2: Sort the edges by polar angle.**

Compute the polar angle (with respect to the positive $x$-axis) of each edge vector:

$$\theta_i^A = \text{atan2}(e_{i,y}^A, e_{i,x}^A)$$

$$\theta_j^B = \text{atan2}(e_{j,y}^B, e_{j,x}^B)$$

This step *prepares the edges for merging in a global angular order*, which is essential for the rotating calipers approach.

**Step 3: Merge the edge lists in angular order.**

Using a two-pointer merge (similar to merge sort), walk through both edge lists in increasing $\theta$ order, producing a single combined list of $m+n$ edge vectors $E$. This list defines the *boundary traversal order* of the Minkowski Sum.

**Step 4: Compute the starting vertex of the sum.**

The vertex of $A \oplus B$ with the smallest $y$-coordinate (breaking ties by smallest $x$) is simply:

$$v_0 = a_0 + b_0$$

This step *exploits the property that translating polygons by their lowest vertices and summing yields the lowest vertex of the result*.

**Step 5: Walk the merged edge list to trace the boundary.**

Initialize the current vertex to $v_0$. For each edge vector $E_k$ in the merged list:

$$v_{k+1} = v_k + E_k$$

After processing all $m+n$ edges, we return to $v_0$, closing the polygon. This step *accumulates the displacement vectors in angular order, which is mathematically guaranteed to produce a non-self-intersecting convex polygon*.

### Final Result
The Minkowski Sum $A \oplus B$ is a convex polygon with at most $m + n$ vertices, computed in $O(m + n)$ time after the initial $O((m+n) \log(m+n))$ sorting step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** The number of vertices in the Minkowski Sum satisfies $\lvert V(A \oplus B) \rvert \leq \lvert V(A) \rvert + \lvert V(B) \rvert$. This is a direct consequence of the angular merge step, where each edge from either input contributes at most one edge to the output.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reduction from Shape Robot to Point Robot

We want to prove rigorously that a path planned for a point robot in the configuration space is equivalent to a path for the original shape robot in the workspace.

### Mathematical Statement

Let $R$ be a closed bounded set describing the robot, $O$ be a closed obstacle, and $W = \mathbb{R}^2$ be the workspace. Define a robot configuration as $q \in SE(2)$ (for planar robots) representing position and orientation. The robot placed at configuration $q$ occupies the region:

$$R(q) = \{ r + t(q) \mid r \in R \}$$

where $t(q) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ is the translation component of $q$.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1: Define the collision condition.**

The robot collides with the obstacle at configuration $q$ if and only if the sets overlap:

$$R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset$$

This step *expresses the geometric collision predicate as a set intersection query*.

**Step 2: Expand the definition of $R(q)$.**

Substituting the definition:

$$\{ r + t(q) \mid r \in R \} \cap O \neq \emptyset$$

This step *substitutes the rigid-body placement to expose the explicit dependency on $t(q)$*.

**Step 3: Isolate the translation variable.**

By the definition of set intersection, there exists a point $x$ belonging to both sets:

$$\exists x \in O, \; \exists r \in R \text{ such that } x = r + t(q)$$

Rearranging to isolate $t(q)$:

$$t(q) = x - r$$

This step *solves for the translation that produces a collision*.

**Step 4: Form the configuration space obstacle.**

The set of all translations $t(q)$ that produce a collision is:

$$C_{obs} = \{ o - r \mid o \in O, \; r \in R \} = O \oplus (-R)$$

This step *collapses the set-builder description into the canonical Minkowski Sum notation*.

**Step 5: Conclude the equivalence.**

A configuration $q$ is collision-free if and only if its translation component $t(q)$ lies outside the C-obstacle:

$$q \text{ is collision-free} \iff t(q) \notin C_{obs}$$

Equivalently:

$$q \text{ is collision-free} \iff t(q) \in C_{free} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus C_{obs}$$

This step *establishes the precise reduction*, showing that a shape-robot path through the workspace is in one-to-one correspondence with a point-robot path through the configuration space.

### Final Statement
$$\boxed{ \text{Shape-Robot Planning in Workspace} \;\;\xleftrightarrow{\;t(q) \mapsto q\;}\; \text{Point-Robot Planning in Configuration Space} }$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Minkowski Sum of a Square and a Triangle

Let us compute the Minkowski Sum of a unit square $A$ and an equilateral triangle $B$ both centered at the origin, by hand.

**Square $A$ vertices (CCW):**

$$A = \left\{ \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\right), \; \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\right), \; \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}\right), \; \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}\right) \right\}$$

**Triangle $B$ vertices (CCW, side length $1$):**

$$B = \left\{ \left(0, \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right), \; \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right), \; \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right) \right\}$$

**Step 1: Compute the Minkowski Sum vertex set.**

The Minkowski Sum has at most $4 + 3 = 7$ vertices. The actual vertex set is:

$$v_0 = \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right) = \left(-1, \; -\tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

$$v_1 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right) = \left(0, \; -\tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

$$v_2 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right) = \left(1, \; -\tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

$$v_3 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right) = \left(1, \; \tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

$$v_4 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(0, \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, \; \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

$$v_5 = \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(0, \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, \; \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

$$v_6 = \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right) = \left(-1, \; \tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Approximate numerically for visualization.**

Using $\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.2887$ and $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774$:

$$v_0 \approx (-1.000, \; -0.789)$$

$$v_1 \approx (0.000, \; -0.789)$$

$$v_2 \approx (1.000, \; -0.789)$$

$$v_3 \approx (1.000, \; 0.211)$$

$$v_4 \approx (0.500, \; 1.077)$$

$$v_5 \approx (-0.500, \; 1.077)$$

$$v_6 \approx (-1.000, \; 0.211)$$

This 7-vertex polygon is the Minkowski Sum. Note how the triangular "cap" appears on top and bottom of the square — the convex hull nature of the result is preserved.

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Verification Tip:** A simple sanity check is to confirm that the *centroid* of $A \oplus B$ equals the sum of the centroids: $\text{centroid}(A \oplus B) = \text{centroid}(A) + \text{centroid}(B)$. In our example, this gives $(0, 0)$, which is consistent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Minkowski Sum Computation

The following block diagram illustrates the complete pipeline from raw shape inputs to a configuration space usable for point-robot planning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Workspace Obstacle O] --> C[Compute Negative of Robot: -R]
    B[Input: Robot Shape R] --> C
    C --> D[Reflect R through Origin]
    D --> E[Apply Minkowski Sum Operation]
    A --> E
    E --> F[Configuration Space Obstacle: C_obs]
    F --> G[Invert to Get Free Space: C_free]
    G --> H[Apply Path Planner: A-star / RRT / Dijkstra]
    H --> I[Output: Collision-Free Point Path]
    I --> J[Map Back to Workspace via Inverse Transform t_inverse]
    J --> K[Final Output: Shape-Robot Trajectory in Workspace]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Visualization: Sweep Operation

The following diagram illustrates how the reflected robot $-R$ is swept along the obstacle boundary to construct the Minkowski Sum.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Obstacle O Boundary Point 1] --> D[Sweep Operation: Place -R here]
    B[Obstacle O Boundary Point 2] --> E[Sweep Operation: Place -R here]
    C[Obstacle O Boundary Point N] --> F[Sweep Operation: Place -R here]
    D --> G[Union of all Translations]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Final Minkowski Sum: O plus negative R]
```

## 4.3 Algorithmic Architecture: From Inputs to Validated Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Robot R and Obstacle O] --> A1[Compute Reflection: -R]
    A1 --> A2[Check Convexity of R]
    A1 --> A3[Check Convexity of O]
    A2 --> A4{Both Convex?}
    A3 --> A4
    A4 -->|Yes| A5[Apply O minkowski negative R: Convex Algorithm, Order of m plus n]
    A4 -->|No| A6[Decompose into Convex Components]
    A6 --> A7[Compute Pairwise Minkowski Sums]
    A7 --> A8[Take Union: Final C_obs]
    A5 --> A9[Validate: Centroid Check]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Build Free Space: Complement of C_obs]
    A10 --> A11[Run Point-Robot Planner]
    A11 --> A12[Output Validated Trajectory]
```

## 4.4 Property and Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Commutativity] --> APP1[Simplifies Code: Same Routine Both Ways]
    P2[Associativity] --> APP2[Allows Incremental Obstacle Merging]
    P3[Translation Invariance] --> APP3[Robust to Global Reference Frame]
    P4[Linear Scaling] --> APP4[Easy Resizing for Different Robot Footprints]
    APP1 --> OUT[Used in: A-star on C-space]
    APP2 --> OUT2[Used in: Dynamic Obstacle Updates]
    APP3 --> OUT3[Used in: Multi-Robot Frame Transforms]
    APP4 --> OUT4[Used in: Adaptive Sizing for Uncertainty]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the Minkowski Sum of two sets $A$ and $B$. State any three of its key algebraic properties with brief justification.

**Model Answer:**

The Minkowski Sum of two sets $A, B \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is defined as:

$$A \oplus B = \{ a + b \mid a \in A, \; b \in B \}$$

**Three key properties:**

1. **Commutativity:** $A \oplus B = B \oplus A$. This holds because vector addition is commutative: $a + b = b + a$ for all $a \in A, b \in B$.

2. **Associativity:** $(A \oplus B) \oplus C = A \oplus (B \oplus C)$. This holds because vector addition is associative: $(a + b) + c = a + (b + c)$.

3. **Translation Invariance:** For any point $p$, $(A \oplus \{p\}) = A + p$. This means adding a singleton point to a set simply translates that set by the point's position vector.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is a Configuration Space Obstacle? How is it obtained using the Minkowski Sum?

**Model Answer:**

A **Configuration Space Obstacle (C-obstacle)** is the region in the robot's configuration space where the robot would collide with a workspace obstacle. For a robot shape $R$ and a workspace obstacle $O$, the C-obstacle is:

$$C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R) = \{ o - r \mid o \in O, \; r \in R \}$$

The C-obstacle is obtained by **reflecting the robot shape through the origin** (to get $-R$) and then computing the **Minkowski Sum** of the obstacle and the reflected robot. This operation effectively "inflates" the obstacle by the robot's footprint, allowing the path planner to treat the robot as a single point during planning.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Alternative 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the Minkowski Sum of a convex polygon $A$ with $m$ vertices and a convex polygon $B$ with $n$ vertices using the angular merge (rotating calipers) method. Show that the result has at most $m + n$ vertices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square robot of side length $2$ units, centered at the origin, must navigate around a triangular obstacle with vertices at $(3, 0)$, $(6, 0)$, and $(4.5, 3)$. Compute the Configuration Space Obstacle and determine whether the robot's reference point at configuration $(4, 1.5)$ is collision-free. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1: Setup.**
Let the vertices of $A$ be $\{a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{m-1}\}$ and of $B$ be $\{b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{n-1}\}$, both ordered counter-clockwise (CCW) from the vertex with the smallest $y$-coordinate.

**Step 2: Compute edge vectors.**

$$e_i^A = a_{i+1} - a_i, \quad e_j^B = b_{j+1} - b_j$$

**Step 3: Sort edges by polar angle.**

$$\theta(e) = \text{atan2}(e_y, e_x)$$

This gives two sorted lists of size $m$ and $n$ respectively.

**Step 4: Merge the edge lists in angular order.**

Using a two-pointer merge in $O(m + n)$ time, we obtain a combined list of $m + n$ edge vectors $E_0, E_1, \dots, E_{m+n-1}$, sorted by $\theta$.

**Step 5: Starting vertex.**

$$v_0 = a_0 + b_0$$

**Step 6: Walk the merged edges.**

$$v_{k+1} = v_k + E_k \quad \text{for } k = 0, 1, \dots, m+n-1$$

**Step 7: Vertex count argument.**

Since each of the $m + n$ edge vectors contributes exactly one edge to the result, and each edge contributes two endpoints but adjacent edges share an endpoint, the total vertex count is at most $m + n$. Equality holds only if the merged angle list has no collinear edges (no two edges with the same polar angle).

**Final Answer:** The Minkowski Sum $A \oplus B$ is a convex polygon with at most $m + n$ vertices, computable in $O(m + n)$ time after sorting.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Define the shapes.**

The square robot $R$ is centered at the origin with side length $2$. Its vertices are:

$$R = \{ (-1,-1), (1,-1), (1,1), (-1,1) \}$$

The reflected robot $-R$ has the same set of vertices (since the square is symmetric about the origin):

$$-R = \{ (-1,-1), (1,-1), (1,1), (-1,1) \}$$

The triangular obstacle $O$ has vertices:

$$O = \{ (3,0), (6,0), (4.5, 3) \}$$

**Step 2: Compute the C-obstacle by Minkowski Sum.**

We need $C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$. Adding the reflected robot vertices to each obstacle vertex:

$$v_0 = (3, 0) + (-1, -1) = (2, -1)$$

$$v_1 = (3, 0) + (1, -1) = (4, -1)$$

$$v_2 = (3, 0) + (1, 1) = (4, 1)$$

$$v_3 = (3, 0) + (-1, 1) = (2, 1)$$

$$v_4 = (6, 0) + (-1, -1) = (5, -1)$$

$$v_5 = (6, 0) + (1, -1) = (7, -1)$$

$$v_6 = (6, 0) + (1, 1) = (7, 1)$$

$$v_7 = (6, 0) + (-1, 1) = (5, 1)$$

$$v_8 = (4.5, 3) + (-1, -1) = (3.5, 2)$$

$$v_9 = (4.5, 3) + (1, -1) = (5.5, 2)$$

$$v_{10} = (4.5, 3) + (1, 1) = (5.5, 4)$$

$$v_{11} = (4.5, 3) + (-1, 1) = (3.5, 4)$$

Taking the convex hull of these 12 points (since the obstacle is a triangle, the C-obstacle is a hexagon with vertices):

$$C_{obs} = \text{ConvexHull}\{ (2,-1), (4,-1), (7,-1), (7,1), (5.5, 4), (3.5, 4) \}$$

This is a hexagon with 6 vertices.

**Step 3: Test the configuration $(4, 1.5)$.**

We need to check whether $(4, 1.5)$ lies inside the hexagon. The hexagon has horizontal sides at $y = -1$ and $y = 1$, and slanted sides from $(5, 1)$ to $(5.5, 4)$ on the right, and from $(2, 1)$ to $(3.5, 4)$ on the left.

Since the test point has $y = 1.5$, it is **above** the horizontal top side of the lower rectangle portion. Now check the slanted edges:

**Right slanted edge** from $(5, 1)$ to $(5.5, 4)$: parametrically, the edge satisfies:

$$x = 5 + 0.5 t, \quad y = 1 + 3t \quad \text{for } t \in [0, 1]$$

At $y = 1.5$, we get $t = 0.5/3 = 1/6$, so $x = 5 + 0.5/6 = 5.0833$. Our test point has $x = 4 < 5.0833$, so it is **inside** the right edge boundary.

**Left slanted edge** from $(2, 1)$ to $(3.5, 4)$: parametrically:

$$x = 2 + 1.5 t, \quad y = 1 + 3t \quad \text{for } t \in [0, 1]$$

At $y = 1.5$, we get $t = 1/6$, so $x = 2 + 1.5/6 = 2.25$. Our test point has $x = 4 > 2.25$, so it is **inside** the left edge boundary.

Since the test point satisfies all half-plane constraints, it lies **inside** the C-obstacle.

**Step 4: Conclusion.**

The configuration $(4, 1.5)$ is **NOT collision-free**. The robot's reference point at this configuration lies inside the inflated C-obstacle, meaning part of the robot body would penetrate the triangular obstacle.

---

### Question B (Alternative 2)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of Minkowski Sum in the context of robot motion planning. How does it enable the reduction of a shape-robot planning problem to a point-robot planning problem? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular robot of radius $1$ unit is to be planned through a workspace containing a single rectangular obstacle with corners at $(2, 2), (6, 2), (6, 5), (2, 5)$. Compute the Minkowski Sum of the obstacle and the negative robot shape, and verify whether the point $(4, 3.5)$ is inside the C-obstacle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1: Definition with diagram description.**

The Minkowski Sum of two planar sets $A$ and $B$ is the set of all points that can be expressed as the vector sum of a point in $A$ and a point in $B$:

$$A \oplus B = \{ a + b \mid a \in A, \; b \in B \}$$

Geometrically, the Minkowski Sum of an obstacle $O$ and a robot $R$ is the region traced out by every point of $R$ as $R$ is translated to cover $O$ completely.

**Step 2: Reduction mechanism.**

For a shape robot $R$ at configuration $q$ (defined by reference point position $t(q)$), the robot occupies $R(q) = R + t(q)$. The collision condition $R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset$ can be rewritten:

$$\exists r \in R, \; \exists o \in O \text{ such that } r + t(q) = o$$

Rearranging:

$$t(q) = o - r \in O \oplus (-R) = C_{obs}$$

**Step 3: Point-robot equivalence.**

A configuration $q$ is collision-free if and only if:

$$t(q) \notin C_{obs} \iff t(q) \in C_{free} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus C_{obs}$$

This transforms the *shape-vs-shape* collision check into a *point-vs-region* query, enabling direct use of graph-search planners on $C_{free}$.

**Step 4: Path correspondence.**

Any path $\pi_{point}: [0, 1] \to C_{free}$ for the point robot corresponds to a path $\pi_{shape}(s) = \pi_{point}(s) + R$ for the shape robot in the workspace, and vice versa.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Define the inputs.**

The circular robot $R$ centered at origin with radius $1$ is:

$$R = \{ (x, y) \mid x^2 + y^2 \leq 1 \}$$

The reflected robot is identical: $-R = R$ (a circle is symmetric through the origin).

The rectangular obstacle is:

$$O = [2, 6] \times [2, 5]$$

**Step 2: Compute the Minkowski Sum.**

Since $-R$ is a unit disk, the Minkowski Sum $O \oplus (-R)$ is the set of all points within distance $1$ of $O$:

$$C_{obs} = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid d(p, O) \leq 1 \}$$

This is the **Minkowski Sum of a rectangle and a disk**, which is geometrically a **"rounded rectangle"** — a rectangle with semicircular caps on each edge and quarter-circle fillets at each corner.

**Step 3: Describe the C-obstacle.**

The C-obstacle consists of:
- A central rectangle of dimensions $(6 - 2 + 2) \times (5 - 2 + 2) = 6 \times 5$ (note: the rectangle is *grown* by the diameter of the disk in both dimensions because $O \oplus (-R)$ is a dilation by the radius, not a Minkowski addition of a point).

Wait — let us recompute carefully. For a rectangle $[x_1, x_2] \times [y_1, y_2]$ and a unit disk, the Minkowski Sum is:

$$C_{obs} = \{ (x, y) \mid \max(x_1 - x, x - x_2, 0)^2 + \max(y_1 - y, y - y_2, 0)^2 \leq 1 \}$$

Equivalently, in geometric terms:
- A **central rectangle** $[x_1 - 1, x_2 + 1] \times [y_1 - 1, y_2 + 1] = [1, 7] \times [1, 6]$.
- Four **quarter-circle fillets** at the corners of the original rectangle, each of radius $1$.

The total shape is a rectangle of size $6 \times 5$ centered at $(4, 3.5)$, with rounded corners of radius $1$.

**Step 4: Test the point $(4, 3.5)$.**

The point $(4, 3.5)$ is the geometric center of the inflated rectangle. We check whether it is inside the C-obstacle:

$$(4 - 1) - 0 = 3 \geq 1 \text{ (true, far from left edge)}$$

$$(7) - 4 = 3 \geq 1 \text{ (true, far from right edge)}$$

$$(3.5 - 1) - 0 = 2.5 \geq 1 \text{ (true, far from bottom edge)}$$

$$6 - 3.5 = 2.5 \geq 1 \text{ (true, far from top edge)}$$

All four distances to the rectangle edges exceed the radius $1$, so the point is **well within the central rectangle** portion of the C-obstacle.

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

The point $(4, 3.5)$ lies **inside** the C-obstacle. This means the robot's center at this configuration is inside the inflated obstacle region, which implies the robot (a disk of radius $1$) is **colliding** with the rectangular obstacle. The reference point is at the center of the obstacle, and any unit disk placed there is obviously fully inside the obstacle region.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Negation Sign:** Writing $C_{obs} = O \oplus R$ instead of $O \oplus (-R)$ is the most frequent error. This produces an *incorrectly translated* region. The correct form is $C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$ because we are "growing the obstacle by the robot footprint from the perspective of the reference point."
>
> 2. **Confusing Minkowski Sum with Set Union:** Students sometimes compute $A \cup B$ instead of $A \oplus B$. Remember: Minkowski Sum is a *vector sum* of point pairs, not a logical OR of set membership.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Centroid Check:** Examiners often award partial credit for the "sanity check" using $\text{centroid}(A \oplus B) = \text{centroid}(A) + \text{centroid}(B)$. Skipping this loses easy marks.
>
> 4. **Wrong Vertex Ordering:** When computing the Minkowski Sum of two polygons by hand, students often order vertices clockwise instead of counter-clockwise. Always use CCW ordering for both inputs.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Convex Hull for Non-Convex Cases:** When one of the inputs is non-convex, the Minkowski Sum vertex set must be wrapped with $\text{ConvexHull}(\cdot)$ in the final step. Forgetting this gives an *under-approximated* (non-convex) result, which is wrong — the actual Minkowski Sum is always **closed under convex combinations**.
>
> 6. **Mixing Units:** The Minkowski Sum of a robot in meters and an obstacle in centimeters will silently produce incorrect results. Always normalize units at the start of the problem.

## 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

- The **Minkowski Sum** of sets $A$ and $B$ is defined as $A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A, b \in B\}$.
- In robot motion planning, the **Configuration Space Obstacle** is computed as $C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$, where $O$ is the workspace obstacle and $R$ is the robot shape.
- The Minkowski Sum enables the **shape-to-point reduction**: a shape-robot path planning problem in the workspace is equivalent to a point-robot path planning problem in the configuration space.
- **Key properties** to memorize: Commutativity, Associativity, Translation Invariance, Linear Scaling, and the fact that the result of summing two convex polygons is a convex polygon with at most $m + n$ vertices.
- For **convex polygons**, the Minkowski Sum can be computed in $O(m + n)$ time using the **rotating calipers** / angular merge method.
- For **non-convex shapes**, decompose into convex components, compute pairwise sums, and take the union (followed by the convex hull for the final C-obstacle).
- The **centroid of the sum** equals the sum of the centroids — a quick sanity check during exams.
- The Minkowski Sum is **symmetric with respect to reflection**: $A \oplus B = A \oplus (-B)$'s reflection about the origin... wait, more precisely: $\text{Reflect}(A \oplus B) = \text{Reflect}(A) \oplus \text{Reflect}(B)$.
- Applications span **autonomous vehicles, warehouse robotics, surgical robotics, drone swarms, manipulator arms, and maritime robotics**.
- The Minkowski Sum is the **canonical bridge** between workspace geometry and configuration space planning — every KTU 2024 Scheme question on this topic will test both the formal definition and at least one practical computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Potential Field # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition — Potential Field Method (PFM)** > The **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method, introduced by **Khatib (1986)**, is a reactive, gradient-based local path planning technique in which the robot is modeled as a point mass moving in a **scalar potential field** $U(q)$ defined over the robot's configuration space $\mathcal{C} \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$. The field is constructed as the **superposition** of an **attractive potential** $U_{att}(q)$ that pulls the robot toward the goal configuration $q_{goal}$, and a **repulsive potential** $U_{rep}(q)$ that pushes the robot away from obstacles $\mathcal{O}_{i}$. The robot navigates by following the **negative gradient** of the total potential, i.e., $\vec{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q)$, descending the field like a ball rolling downhill into a goal-valley while being deflected by obstacle-hills. Formally, the total potential and force are: $$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i} U_{rep,i}(q)$$ $$\vec{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \sum_{i} \nabla U_{rep,i}(q)$$ ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Charged Particle in a Hilly Landscape" Imagine a tiny, positively charged marble released on a **hilly terrain map** drawn on a piece of paper. The map has: - A **deep valley (well)** at the location of the **goal**. Gravity (the attractive field) pulls the marble into the valley. - A series of **smooth hills (bumps)** at the location of every **obstacle**. The marble is repelled away from these hills. - The marble simply rolls in the direction of **steepest descent** of the terrain at every instant. The path traced by the marble — sliding toward the goal, swerving around bumps — is the robot's planned trajectory. The "height" of the terrain is the **potential $U(q)$**, and the slope (negative gradient) is the **force $\vec{F}(q)$**. > [!NOTE] > **Why this works for real robots:** Computing the potential and its gradient is computationally cheap ($O(n)$ per obstacle, where $n$ is the configuration space dimension), so PFM is well-suited for **real-time, reactive obstacle avoidance** in unknown or dynamic environments. ## 1.3 Core Building Blocks of the Potential Field | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Symbol Used in KTU Texts | |---|---|---| | $q$ | Robot configuration (typically $q \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ for planar, $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ for 3D) | Position vector | | $q_{goal}$ | Goal configuration | Target point | | $\mathcal{O}_{i}$ | The $i$-th obstacle | Cylindrical/spherical region | | $d(q, q_{goal})$ | Euclidean distance to goal | $\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ | | $d(q, \mathcal{O}_{i})$ | Distance to the nearest point on obstacle $i$ | $\rho_i$ | | $d^{*}$ | Threshold of influence (safety radius) | $\rho_0$ | | $\zeta$ | Attractive gain constant | Scaling factor | | $\eta$ | Repulsive gain constant | Scaling factor | | $\nabla$ | Gradient operator with respect to $q$ | $\partial / \partial q$ | > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Heuristic for Choosing Gains:** Typical starting values are $\zeta \in [0.5,\, 5.0]$ and $\eta \in [100,\, 1000]$ for a workspace of side ~10 m. The ratio $\eta/\zeta$ must be tuned so that obstacle repulsion always dominates near an obstacle. A common rule is $\eta / \zeta \geq 10 \cdot d^{*}$. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of the Total Potential Field The Artificial Potential Field is the **linear superposition** of two independent scalar fields: ### 2.1.1 Attractive Potential Field $U_{att}(q)$ Drives the robot toward $q_{goal}$. Two canonical forms are prescribed by KTU syllabus: **Form A — Quadratic (Conic) Attractive Potential** *(used when far-field convergence speed is desired)* $$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\zeta \, d^{2}(q, q_{goal}) = \frac{1}{2}\,\zeta \, \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}$$ The corresponding attractive force is the **linear restoring force** of a virtual spring: $$\vec{F}_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta \, (q - q_{goal})$$ **Why quadratic?** Its gradient is **constant magnitude scaling with distance** — the farther from the goal, the stronger the pull. This guarantees smooth, unbounded convergence but produces a **non-zero force even at the goal**, which can cause oscillation. **Form B — Conic (Linear) Attractive Potential** *(used near the goal for stability)* $$U_{att}(q) = \zeta \, d(q, q_{goal}) = \zeta \, \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$$ $$\vec{F}_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta \, \frac{(q - q_{goal})}{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert}$$ **Why conic?** The gradient magnitude is **constant (equal to $\zeta$)** regardless of distance, eliminating overshoot/oscillation at the goal. However, far from the goal, convergence is sluggish. **Form C — Hybrid (Khatib's Standard)** — combine both: $$U_{att}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta \, d^{2} & \text{if } d \leq d^{*}_{att} \\[6pt] \zeta \, d & \text{if } d > d^{*}_{att} \end{cases}$$ This is the **most commonly examined form** in KTU papers. The boundary distance is $d^{*}_{att} = \zeta / \eta$ in some textbooks. ### 2.1.2 Repulsive Potential Field $U_{rep}(q)$ Active **only** within the safety radius $d^{*}$. Standard form (Khatib, 1986): $$U_{rep,i}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left( \dfrac{1}{d(q, \mathcal{O}_{i})} - \dfrac{1}{d^{*}} \right)^{2} & \text{if } d(q, \mathcal{O}_{i}) \leq d^{*} \\[8pt] 0 & \text{if } d(q, \mathcal{O}_{i}) > d^{*} \end{cases}$$ The corresponding repulsive force (vector pointing **from obstacle to robot**): $$\vec{F}_{rep,i}(q) = \begin{cases} \eta \left( \dfrac{1}{d(q, \mathcal{O}_{i})} - \dfrac{1}{d^{*}} \right) \dfrac{1}{d^{2}(q, \mathcal{O}_{i})} \, \dfrac{(q - q_{obs,i})}{d(q, \mathcal{O}_{i})} & \text{if } d \leq d^{*} \\[8pt] 0 & \text{if } d > d^{*} \end{cases}$$ where $q_{obs,i}$ is the closest point on obstacle $i$. **Why this form?** The factor $(1/d - 1/d^{*})^{2}$ guarantees: 1. $U_{rep} = 0$ at the boundary $d = d^{*}$ (continuous potential). 2. $U_{rep} \to \infty$ as $d \to 0$ (infinite barrier at the obstacle surface). 3. $\nabla U_{rep} = 0$ at $d = d^{*}$ (smooth force profile). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following **cheat-sheet** is the single most-tested content on this topic. Memorize every cell. | # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Range of Validity | Units (SI) | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total Potential | $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i} U_{rep,i}(q)$ | Always | J (Joule) | Scalar "energy landscape" | | 2 | Total Force | $\vec{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ | Always | N (Newton) | Steering command for robot | | 3 | Quadratic Attractive | $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\zeta \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}$ | Always | J | Paraboloid well at goal | | 4 | Conic Attractive | $U_{att} = \zeta \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ | Always | J | Cone well at goal | | 5 | Attractive Force (Quad.) | $\vec{F}_{att} = -\zeta(q - q_{goal})$ | Always | N | Linear spring restoring force | | 6 | Attractive Force (Conic) | $\vec{F}_{att} = -\zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal})/\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ | $q \neq q_{goal}$ | N | Unit-vector pull | | 7 | Repulsive Potential | $U_{rep,i} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta (1/d_{i} - 1/d^{*})^{2}$ | $d_{i} \leq d^{*}$ | J | Inverted bump at obstacle | | 8 | Repulsive Force | $\vec{F}_{rep,i} = \eta (1/d_{i} - 1/d^{*}) \cdot (q - q_{obs,i}) / d_{i}^{3}$ | $d_{i} \leq d^{*}$ | N | Deflection vector | | 9 | Local Minima Condition | $\vec{F}_{att} + \vec{F}_{rep} = \vec{0}$, $U \neq 0$ | At trap points | — | Failure mode of APF | | 10 | Navigation Function (Rimon–Koditschek) | $\varphi(q) = (\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}) / ((\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q))^{1/k})$ | Sphere-world | — | Provably free of local minima | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder on Notation:** In all written work for KTU, use $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ (NOT the vertical bar) for Euclidean norm to avoid markdown/LaTeX ambiguity. ## 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering | Application Domain | How APF is Used | |---|---| | **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMR)** in warehouses | Real-time obstacle avoidance with dynamic rerouting | | **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars)** | Lane-keeping + pedestrian avoidance (often hybrid with RRT*) | | **UAV / Drone Swarms** | Collision-free trajectory shaping, formation flight | | **Surgical / Medical Robots** | Safe navigation around organs (da Vinci, bronchoscopy robots) | | **Industrial Manipulators** | Real-time end-effector obstacle avoidance in cluttered cells | | **Underwater ROVs** | Reactive navigation around coral reefs, shipwrecks | > [!TIP] > **Engineer's Note:** In industry, APF is rarely used *standalone* — it is almost always embedded inside a **hybrid planner** (e.g., APF as a local planner, with a global planner like A* or RRT* providing a coarse path). The classical APF is the **theoretical cornerstone** that every KTU student must master, even if commercial systems extend it. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Attractive Force from Quadratic Potential **Problem Setup.** Define the attractive potential as $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\zeta (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})$, where $q, q_{goal} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$. Derive $\vec{F}_{att}(q)$. ### Step 1 — Expand the squared norm. In $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, write $q = (x, y)^{T}$ and $q_{goal} = (x_{g}, y_{g})^{T}$. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} U_{att}(x, y) &= \frac{1}{2}\,\zeta \left[ (x - x_{g})^{2} + (y - y_{g})^{2} \right] \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Take the partial derivative with respect to $x$. The derivative of $(x - x_{g})^{2}$ is $2(x - x_{g})$; the derivative of $(y - y_{g})^{2}$ is zero. So: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} &= \frac{1}{2}\,\zeta \cdot 2(x - x_{g}) = \zeta (x - x_{g}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Take the partial derivative with respect to $y$. By symmetry: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} &= \zeta (y - y_{g}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Assemble the gradient vector. $$ \begin{aligned} \nabla U_{att} &= \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix} = \zeta \begin{bmatrix} x - x_{g} \\[4pt] y - y_{g} \end{bmatrix} = \zeta (q - q_{goal}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Apply the force definition. $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{F}_{att}(q) &= -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta (q - q_{goal}) \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation.** The attractive force is a **linear spring** anchored at the goal with stiffness $\zeta$. The farther the robot, the larger the force, with **direction** always pointing from current position toward the goal. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Repulsive Force with Detailed Gradient Computation **Problem Setup.** Given $U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta \left( \tfrac{1}{d(q, \mathcal{O})} - \tfrac{1}{d^{*}} \right)^{2}$ for $d \leq d^{*}$, where $d(q, \mathcal{O}) = \Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert$. Derive $\vec{F}_{rep}(q)$. ### Step 1 — Introduce substitution for clarity. Let $g(q) = \dfrac{1}{d} - \dfrac{1}{d^{*}}$ where $d = \Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert$. Then $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta\, g^{2}(q)$. ### Step 2 — Apply the chain rule. $$ \begin{aligned} \nabla U_{rep} &= \eta \, g(q) \cdot \nabla g(q) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute $\nabla g(q)$. Since $d^{*}$ is constant, $\nabla(1/d^{*}) = 0$. So: $$ \begin{aligned} \nabla g(q) &= \nabla \!\left( \frac{1}{d} \right) = \nabla \!\left( d^{-1} \right) = -d^{-2} \, \nabla d \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute $\nabla d$. Using $d = \Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert = \left[ (q - q_{obs})^{T}(q - q_{obs}) \right]^{1/2}$ and the chain rule: $$ \begin{aligned} \nabla d &= \frac{1}{2}\left[(q - q_{obs})^{T}(q - q_{obs})\right]^{-1/2} \cdot 2(q - q_{obs}) = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{d} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute back. $$ \begin{aligned} \nabla g(q) &= -d^{-2} \cdot \frac{q - q_{obs}}{d} = -\frac{q - q_{obs}}{d^{3}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Assemble $\nabla U_{rep}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \nabla U_{rep} &= \eta \, g(q) \cdot \left( -\frac{q - q_{obs}}{d^{3}} \right) = -\eta \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d^{*}} \right) \frac{q - q_{obs}}{d^{3}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Apply the force definition. $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{F}_{rep}(q) &= -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d^{*}} \right) \frac{q - q_{obs}}{d^{3}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 8 — Verify the boundary and limit behavior. - At $d = d^{*}$: $(1/d - 1/d^{*}) = 0 \Rightarrow \vec{F}_{rep} = \vec{0}$. ✓ (No force outside influence zone.) - As $d \to 0$: $(1/d - 1/d^{*}) \to \infty$ and $1/d^{3} \to \infty \Rightarrow \vec{F}_{rep} \to \infty$. ✓ (Infinite repulsion at contact.) - Direction: $(q - q_{obs})/d^{3}$ points **from obstacle to robot** (i.e., away from obstacle). ✓ **Interpretation.** The repulsive force is **normal to the obstacle surface**, increasing superlinearly (as $1/d^{3}$) as the robot approaches the obstacle. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Force Behavior at Special Points | Configuration | $\vec{F}_{att}$ | $\vec{F}_{rep}$ | $\vec{F}_{total}$ | Outcome | |---|---|---|---|---| | Robot at goal, no nearby obstacle | $\vec{0}$ | $\vec{0}$ | $\vec{0}$ | **Stable equilibrium** ✓ | | Far from goal, no obstacle | Large, points to goal | $\vec{0}$ | Large, points to goal | Robot moves to goal | | Near obstacle, far from goal | Weak, points to goal | Large, points away from obstacle | Combined direction | Robot **deflects** around obstacle | | Robot between two obstacles equidistant | $\vec{F}_{att}$ | $\vec{F}_{rep,1} + \vec{F}_{rep,2} = -\vec{F}_{att}$ (cancel out) | $\vec{0}$, but $U \neq 0$ | **LOCAL MINIMUM — TRAP** ✗ | | Inside narrow U-shaped corridor | $\vec{F}_{att}$ points to goal | $\vec{F}_{rep}$ from both walls cancels | May become zero or oscillate | **Possible trap / oscillation** ✗ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Watch — Local Minima Problem:** The single most important **limitation** of classical APF is the **local minima problem**. A robot can get stuck at configurations where $\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{0}$ but $q \neq q_{goal}$. This is the **#1 cause of APF failure** and is a guaranteed question in Part B (14 marks). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Total Force Vector for a Multi-Obstacle Scenario **Worked Numerical Setup (typical 7-mark KTU problem).** A 2D mobile robot is at $q = (2, 3)^{T}$. The goal is $q_{goal} = (10, 8)^{T}$. One circular obstacle of radius $r = 1$ has its center at $q_{obs} = (4, 4)^{T}$. The safety radius is $d^{*} = 3$. Gains: $\zeta = 1$, $\eta = 50$. Compute the total force. ### Step 1 — Compute the distance to the goal. $$ \begin{aligned} d_{g} &= \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert = \sqrt{(2-10)^{2} + (3-8)^{2}} = \sqrt{64 + 25} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.434 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the distance to the obstacle surface. First, distance to center: $\Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert = \sqrt{(2-4)^{2} + (3-4)^{2}} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$. Subtract obstacle radius: $d = 2.236 - 1 = 1.236$. ### Step 3 — Check if within influence zone. $d = 1.236 \leq d^{*} = 3$? **Yes**, repulsive force is active. ### Step 4 — Compute the attractive force (using quadratic form). $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{F}_{att} &= -\zeta (q - q_{goal}) = -1 \cdot (2-10,\ 3-8) = (8,\ 5)^{T} \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute the repulsive force. First, the scalar factor: $$ \begin{aligned} k &= \eta \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d^{*}} \right) \frac{1}{d^{2}} = 50 \left( \frac{1}{1.236} - \frac{1}{3} \right) \frac{1}{(1.236)^{2}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} k &= 50 \cdot (0.8091 - 0.3333) \cdot (0.6546) = 50 \cdot 0.4758 \cdot 0.6546 \approx 15.57 \end{aligned} $$ Unit vector from obstacle to robot: $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{n} &= \frac{q - q_{obs}}{d_{center}} = \frac{(2-4,\ 3-4)}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{(-2, -1)}{2.236} = (-0.8944,\ -0.4472)^{T} \end{aligned} $$ (Note: We use $d_{center} = \sqrt{5}$ in the unit vector, not $d$, so the vector points from obstacle to robot.) Repulsive force: $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{F}_{rep} &= k \cdot \hat{n} = 15.57 \cdot (-0.8944,\ -0.4472)^{T} = (-13.93,\ -6.96)^{T} \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Total force. $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{F}_{total} &= \vec{F}_{att} + \vec{F}_{rep} = (8 - 13.93,\ 5 - 6.96)^{T} = (-5.93,\ -1.96)^{T} \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Magnitude and direction. $$ \begin{aligned} \Vert \vec{F}_{total} \Vert &= \sqrt{5.93^{2} + 1.96^{2}} = \sqrt{35.16 + 3.84} = \sqrt{39.0} \approx 6.25 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \text{atan2}(-1.96,\ -5.93) \approx 180° + \tan^{-1}(1.96/5.93) \approx 198.3° \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation.** The robot is being **pushed away from the obstacle** (west-southwest), which makes physical sense: the obstacle is northeast of the robot, and the robot must detour around it before continuing to the goal. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Attractive Potential Forms | Property | Quadratic $\tfrac{1}{2}\zeta d^{2}$ | Conic $\zeta d$ | Hybrid (Khatib) | |---|---|---|---| | Gradient magnitude at goal | $0$ ✓ (well-behaved) | **Undefined (singular)** ✗ | $0$ (matches goal behavior) | | Gradient magnitude far from goal | $\propto d$ (unbounded) | $\zeta$ (bounded, constant) | $\propto d$ (quadratic region) | | Convergence speed | Fast far, slow near | Slow far, smooth near | Best of both | | Oscillation at goal | Possible | Severe (chattering) | Eliminated | | KTU exam frequency | Very High | High | High | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the APF Planner ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Robot Current Configuration q] --> B[Workspace Map Input] B --> C[Goal Configuration q_goal] B --> D[Obstacle List O_1 ... O_n] C --> E[Attractive Potential U_att] D --> F[Repulsive Potential U_rep for each O_i] E --> G[Superposition: U_total = U_att + Sum U_rep_i] F --> G G --> H[Gradient Computation: nabla U_total] H --> I[Force Vector: F = minus nabla U_total] I --> J{Is F = 0?} J -->|Yes, at goal| K[STOP - Goal Reached] J -->|Yes, not at goal| L[LOCAL MINIMUM TRAP - Activate Recovery] J -->|No| M[Velocity Command: v = k * F] M --> N[Robot Actuator Update] N --> O[New Configuration q_new] O --> A L --> P[Recovery Strategies: Random Walk / Virtual Goal / Wall Following] P --> A ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — 2D Potential Field Landscape (Conceptual Topography) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Workspace["Configuration Space C in R squared"] direction LR H1((Obstacle 1
Repulsive Hill)) H2((Obstacle 2
Repulsive Hill)) H3((Obstacle 3
Repulsive Hill)) V((Goal q_goal
Attractive Well)) S((Start q_start
High Potential Region)) end S -->|Force Vector F_att| V H1 -.->|Deflects path| S H2 -.->|Deflects path| S H3 -.->|Deflects path| S S -->|Robot trajectory| V ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Local Minima Recovery Strategies ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Local Minimum Detected] --> B{F_recovery > F_threshold?} B -->|No| C[Apply Virtual Sub-goal Method] B -->|Yes| D[Apply Random Walk Escape] C --> E[Move toward temporary target] D --> F[Move in random direction for T seconds] E --> G[Re-evaluate APF] F --> G G --> H{New local minimum?} H -->|No| I[Resume Normal APF Navigation] H -->|Yes| J[Increment recovery counter] J --> K{Counter > N_max?} K -->|No| A K -->|Yes| L[Declare Planning Failure - Switch to Global Planner] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Hybrid Attractive Potential (Quadratic + Conic Switch) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Distance d to goal] --> B{d <= d_star_att?} B -->|Yes - Near Goal| C[Conic Form: U_att = zeta * d] B -->|No - Far from Goal| D[Quadratic Form: U_att = 0.5 * zeta * d squared] C --> E[Output U_att] D --> E E --> F[Gradient: F_att = minus nabla U_att] F --> G[Robot Steering Command] ``` ## 4.5 Data Flow — Obstacle Distance and Repulsive Force Computation ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Robot Position q] --> B[Iterate Over Obstacles O_1 to O_n] B --> C[Compute Distance d_i to each obstacle] C --> D{d_i less than or equal to d_star?} D -->|No - Outside influence| E[U_rep_i = 0] D -->|Yes - Inside influence| F[Compute Scalar Factor: eta * 1_over_d - 1_over_d_star times 1_over_d_squared] F --> G[Compute Direction Vector: q minus q_obs over d] G --> H[Repulsive Force: F_rep_i = Scalar Factor times Direction] E --> I[Accumulate: F_rep_total = Sum F_rep_i] H --> I I --> J[Output F_rep_total to Superposition Block] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** The flowcharts above describe the **computational pipeline** of an APF planner, not the physical geometry of the field. The actual field geometry is a 3D surface (height = potential) over the 2D workspace, which is best visualized using MATLAB's `mesh()` or Python's `matplotlib` `contourf()` — out of scope for this textual note. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method for robot path planning. State its two main components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method, introduced by Khatib (1986), is a **reactive, gradient-based local path planning technique** in which the robot is treated as a point moving in a **scalar potential field** $U(q)$ defined over its configuration space. The robot's motion follows the **negative gradient** $\vec{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ of this field. The two main components are: 1. **Attractive Potential $U_{att}(q)$** — A "well" centered at the goal configuration $q_{goal}$ that pulls the robot toward the target. 2. **Repulsive Potential $U_{rep}(q)$** — A "bump" centered at each obstacle $\mathcal{O}_{i}$ that pushes the robot away from obstacles, active only within a threshold distance $d^{*}$. The total potential is $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i} U_{rep,i}(q)$, and the robot moves along $-\nabla U(q)$ to reach the goal while avoiding obstacles. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper] **List any three limitations of the classical Artificial Potential Field method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Local Minima Problem:** The robot can get trapped at configurations where $\vec{F}_{att} + \vec{F}_{rep} = \vec{0}$ but $q \neq q_{goal}$, causing planning failure. 2. **Goal Non-Reachability with Nearby Obstacles (GNRON):** When the goal is in close proximity to an obstacle, the repulsive force can dominate and prevent the robot from reaching the goal. 3. **Oscillation in Narrow Passages:** In corridors or U-shaped obstacles, the robot can experience oscillatory motion between opposing repulsive forces, leading to slow or non-convergent behavior. *(Other valid limitations: No Passage Between Closely Spaced Obstacles, Oscillation in the Presence of Obstacles, parameter sensitivity, lack of kinematic constraints.)* **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style] **Q.A (a)** With suitable diagrams and mathematical expressions, explain the **Artificial Potential Field (APF) method** for mobile robot path planning. Derive the **attractive and repulsive force** equations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### 1. Introduction and Concept (1.5 marks) The APF method, proposed by Khatib (1986), is a **reactive, gradient-descent** path planning technique. The robot is modeled as a point under the influence of artificial forces in a **scalar potential field** $U(q)$ constructed over the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$. The robot's velocity is proportional to the **negative gradient** of this field: $$\vec{v}(q) = -k_{v} \, \nabla U(q)$$ #### 2. Attractive Potential (2 marks) Defined as a function that increases with distance to the goal. The most common form is the **quadratic attractive potential**: $$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\zeta \left\Vert q - q_{goal} \right\Vert^{2}$$ where $\zeta > 0$ is the attractive gain. #### 3. Attractive Force Derivation (1.5 marks) The force is the negative gradient: $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{F}_{att}(q) &= -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\nabla \!\left[ \frac{1}{2}\zeta (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal}) \right] \end{aligned} $$ Applying the matrix calculus identity $\nabla(x^{T} x) = 2x$: $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{F}_{att}(q) &= -\zeta (q - q_{goal}) \end{aligned} $$ This is a **linear spring-like restoring force** pointing from the current position toward the goal. #### 4. Repulsive Potential and Force (2 marks) The standard Khatib repulsive potential, active only within $d \leq d^{*}$: $$ U_{rep,i}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left( \dfrac{1}{d_i} - \dfrac{1}{d^{*}} \right)^{2} & d_i \leq d^{*} \\[6pt] 0 & d_i > d^{*} \end{cases} $$ Differentiating with the chain rule (as shown in Section 3.2 of this note): $$ \vec{F}_{rep,i}(q) = \eta \left( \frac{1}{d_i} - \frac{1}{d^{*}} \right) \frac{q - q_{obs,i}}{d_i^{3}}, \quad d_i \leq d^{*} $$ The force vector points **away from the obstacle**, with magnitude $\to \infty$ as $d_i \to 0$ and $\to 0$ as $d_i \to d^{*}$. **(7 marks total)** --- **Q.A (b)** A 2D mobile robot is at position $q = (1, 2)^{T}$ with goal $q_{goal} = (8, 6)^{T}$. One circular obstacle of radius $r = 0.5$ has its center at $q_{obs} = (3, 3)^{T}$. The influence distance is $d^{*} = 2.5$, with gains $\zeta = 1.5$ and $\eta = 80$. Compute the **total force** on the robot using the standard APF formulation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Step 1 — Distance to goal. (1 mark) $$ d_{g} = \sqrt{(1-8)^{2} + (2-6)^{2}} = \sqrt{49 + 16} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.062 \text{ m} $$ #### Step 2 — Distance to obstacle surface. (1 mark) Distance to center: $d_{center} = \sqrt{(1-3)^{2} + (2-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$ m. Distance to surface: $d = 2.236 - 0.5 = 1.736$ m. #### Step 3 — Check influence zone. (0.5 marks) $d = 1.736 \leq d^{*} = 2.5$ ✓ → Repulsive force is **active**. #### Step 4 — Attractive force. (1 mark) $$ \vec{F}_{att} = -\zeta (q - q_{goal}) = -1.5 (1-8,\ 2-6) = -1.5(-7, -4) = (10.5,\ 6.0)^{T} \text{ N} $$ Magnitude: $\Vert \vec{F}_{att} \Vert = \sqrt{10.5^{2} + 6.0^{2}} = \sqrt{110.25 + 36} = \sqrt{146.25} \approx 12.09$ N. #### Step 5 — Repulsive force. (2 marks) Scalar factor: $$ k = \eta \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d^{*}} \right) \frac{1}{d^{2}} = 80 \left( \frac{1}{1.736} - \frac{1}{2.5} \right) \frac{1}{(1.736)^{2}} $$ $$ k = 80 \cdot (0.5760 - 0.4000) \cdot (0.3319) = 80 \cdot 0.176 \cdot 0.3319 \approx 4.673 $$ Unit vector from obstacle to robot: $$ \hat{n} = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{d_{center}} = \frac{(1-3, 2-3)}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{(-2, -1)}{2.236} = (-0.8944, -0.4472)^{T} $$ Repulsive force: $$ \vec{F}_{rep} = k \cdot \hat{n} = 4.673 \cdot (-0.8944, -0.4472) = (-4.180, -2.090)^{T} \text{ N} $$ #### Step 6 — Total force. (1 mark) $$ \vec{F}_{total} = (10.5 - 4.180, 6.0 - 2.090) = (6.320, 3.910)^{T} \text{ N} $$ #### Step 7 — Magnitude and direction. (0.5 marks) $$ \Vert \vec{F}_{total} \Vert = \sqrt{6.320^{2} + 3.910^{2}} = \sqrt{39.94 + 15.29} = \sqrt{55.23} \approx 7.43 \text{ N} $$ $$ \theta = \text{atan2}(3.910, 6.320) \approx 31.7° \text{ (measured from positive x-axis)} $$ **Result:** The robot experiences a **net force of ~7.43 N at ~31.7°**, indicating it is being pulled toward the goal (northeast) while being partially deflected southwest by the obstacle. **(7 marks total)** --- ### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [Alternative 14-mark question for internal choice] **Q.B (a)** Explain the **local minima problem** in the Artificial Potential Field method. Discuss at least **three recovery strategies** to overcome it. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### 1. Definition of Local Minima (1.5 marks) A **local minimum** in the potential field $U(q)$ is a configuration $q_{LM}$ satisfying: - $\nabla U(q_{LM}) = \vec{0}$ (zero net force, robot stops). - $U(q_{LM}) > U(q_{goal})$ (potential is not at the global minimum). Mathematically: $$ \vec{F}_{att}(q_{LM}) + \sum_{i} \vec{F}_{rep,i}(q_{LM}) = \vec{0}, \quad q_{LM} \neq q_{goal} $$ This causes the robot to be **stuck** at a non-goal point, which is a planning failure. #### 2. Causes of Local Minima (1.5 marks) - **Concave obstacles** with U-shaped or C-shaped geometry. - **Symmetrically placed obstacles** equidistant from a point — the resultant repulsion cancels with attraction. - **Goal in close proximity to obstacles** — repulsion dominates at the goal. - **Narrow passages** between two close obstacles. #### 3. Recovery Strategies (4 marks) **Strategy 1 — Random Walk / Brownian Motion:** Inject random velocity perturbations $\vec{v}_{rand}$ (sampled from a uniform or Gaussian distribution) into the controller for a fixed duration $T$: $$\vec{v}_{total} = -\nabla U(q) + \vec{v}_{rand}, \quad t \in [t_0, t_0 + T]$$ The robot "wiggles" out of the local minimum. The probability of escape scales with $T$ and the perturbation amplitude. **Strategy 2 — Virtual Sub-goal (Waypoint Insertion):** Insert a temporary intermediate goal $q_{sub}$ (placed using a heuristic — e.g., perpendicular to the obstacle surface, or along the local gradient) to "pull" the robot out of the trap: $$U'_{total}(q) = U(q) + U_{att}^{sub}(q)$$ After the sub-goal is reached, revert to the original $U_{total}$. **Strategy 3 — Wall-Following / Tangential Navigation:** Instead of the gradient, follow a vector **tangential to the obstacle boundary**. Define a new control law: $$\vec{v}_{tan} = \vec{F}_{rep} \times \hat{k} \quad \text{(rotate repulsive force by 90°)}$$ The robot "slides" along the obstacle wall until it escapes the trap region. **Strategy 4 — Navigation Function (Rimon–Koditschek, 1992):** Replace the APF with a mathematically constructed **navigation function** $\varphi(q)$ that is guaranteed to have a **single global minimum at the goal** and no local minima. Defined as: $$\varphi(q) = \frac{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}}{(\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q))^{1/k}}$$ where $\beta(q)$ encodes the obstacle geometry. **(7 marks total)** --- **Q.B (b)** A 2D point robot must navigate from $q_{start} = (0, 0)$ to $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$. One circular obstacle of radius $r = 1$ is centered at $q_{obs} = (5, 0)$. The robot is allowed to come within $d^{*} = 3$ of the obstacle surface. Using the **conic attractive potential** $U_{att} = \zeta d_{g}$ with $\zeta = 2$, and the standard Khatib repulsive potential with $\eta = 50$: - **(i)** Compute the force on the robot at $q = (4, 0)$. - **(ii)** Explain why the robot can or cannot reach the goal at $(10, 0)$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### (i) Force computation at $q = (4, 0)$ (4 marks) **Step 1 — Distance to goal:** $$ d_{g} = \sqrt{(4-10)^{2} + 0^{2}} = 6 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Distance to obstacle surface:** $d_{center} = \sqrt{(4-5)^{2} + 0^{2}} = 1$ m, so $d = 1 - 1 = 0$ m. ⚠ **The robot is in contact with the obstacle surface.** The repulsive force is formally infinite — physical collision. **Step 3 — Slightly perturbed location: Re-evaluate at $q = (3.5, 0)$ (more meaningful).** $d_{center} = 1.5$, $d = 1.5 - 1 = 0.5$ m. Within $d^{*}=3$, repulsion is active. **Attractive force (conic form):** $$ \vec{F}_{att} = -\zeta \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert} = -2 \cdot \frac{(3.5 - 10, 0)}{6.5} = -2 \cdot (-1, 0) = (2, 0)^{T} \text{ N} $$ **Repulsive force:** $$ k = \eta \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d^{*}} \right) \frac{1}{d^{2}} = 50 \left( \frac{1}{0.5} - \frac{1}{3} \right) \frac{1}{0.25} = 50 \cdot 1.667 \cdot 4 = 333.3 $$ $$ \hat{n} = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{d_{center}} = \frac{(3.5 - 5, 0)}{1.5} = (-1, 0)^{T} $$ $$ \vec{F}_{rep} = 333.3 \cdot (-1, 0) = (-333.3, 0)^{T} \text{ N} $$ **Total force:** $$ \vec{F}_{total} = (2 - 333.3, 0) = (-331.3, 0)^{T} \text{ N} $$ **Direction:** Strongly **away from the goal**, purely in $-x$ direction. #### (ii) Why the goal is unreachable? (3 marks) The obstacle is centered on the line $y = 0$ between the start and the goal. The straight line from $(0, 0)$ to $(10, 0)$ **passes through the obstacle center**. For the robot to reach $(10, 0)$, it would have to cross the line $x = 5$, where it would be **inside the obstacle** (infinite repulsion). Furthermore, the goal at $(10, 0)$ is at distance $d = 10 - 5 - 1 = 4 > d^{*}=3$ from the obstacle surface, so the repulsive force at the goal itself is zero — but the robot must navigate **around** the obstacle. **Conclusion:** The goal **is theoretically reachable** by going around the obstacle (e.g., $y > 1$ or $y < -1$), but **on the $y = 0$ line, it is unreachable**. The APF method, being a local planner, may or may not find the detoured path depending on initial conditions and gains. The local minimum problem can also occur at $(5, 0)$ if the geometry produces a balance of forces. **(7 marks total)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!)** > > 1. **Forgetting the minus sign in force** — $\vec{F} = -\nabla U$, not $+\nabla U$. A single sign error forfeits 2–3 marks. > 2. **Using $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ (single pipe) in markdown/LaTeX** — Always use `\|` (double bar) or `\Vert` to avoid pipe-syntax conflicts. > 3. **Confusing distance to center vs distance to surface** — For circular obstacles, the repulsive distance is to the **surface**, $d = \Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert - r$, not to the center. > 4. **Forgetting to check $d \leq d^{*}$** — If the robot is outside the influence zone, the repulsive force is exactly **zero**, not "small." > 5. **Not stating units** — KTU marks explicitly reward units (N for force, J for potential, m for distance). > 6. **Omitting the obstacle radius in numerical problems** — Always read carefully whether the obstacle is given by center+radius or as a point. > 7. **Forgetting to discuss local minima** — The local minima problem is a **mandatory** discussion in any 7-mark APF question. Skipping it loses 1.5–2 marks. > 8. **Not drawing the force vector diagram** — Always include a free-body diagram showing $\vec{F}_{att}$, $\vec{F}_{rep}$, and $\vec{F}_{total}$ on the robot. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Read This Twice Before the Exam!** - [x] **APF = Gradient Descent on a potential landscape** that is the sum of attractive (toward goal) and repulsive (away from obstacles) fields. - [x] **Force equation:** $\vec{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ — **negative gradient** of total potential. - [x] **Quadratic attractive** $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\zeta d^{2} \Rightarrow \vec{F}_{att} = -\zeta(q - q_{goal})$. - [x] **Conic attractive** $U_{att} = \zeta d \Rightarrow \vec{F}_{att} = -\zeta \cdot \hat{u}_{goal}$. - [x] **Repulsive potential (Khatib)** $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta(1/d - 1/d^{*})^{2}$, valid **only** for $d \leq d^{*}$. - [x] **Repulsive force** has magnitude scaling as $\sim 1/d^{3}$ and direction **from obstacle to robot**. - [x] **Distance to circular obstacle** $d = \Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert - r$ (subtract the radius!). - [x] **Total potential** $U = U_{att} + \sum U_{rep,i}$ — additive superposition. - [x] **Local minima** are the **#1 failure mode** of APF — must be discussed in any long answer. - [x] **GNRON** (Goal Non-Reachability with Nearby Obstacles) is the **#2 failure mode** — fix with modified repulsive potential. - [x] **Recovery strategies:** Random walk, virtual sub-goal, wall-following, navigation functions. - [x] **Navigation function (Rimon–Koditschek)** is provably free of local minima — guaranteed correct convergence. - [x] **Harmonic potential functions** $\nabla^{2}\varphi = 0$ also avoid local minima but are expensive to compute. - [x] **Advantages of APF:** Real-time, computationally cheap, smooth trajectories, simple to implement. - [x] **Disadvantages of APF:** Local minima, GNRON, oscillations, no global optimality guarantee. - [x] **Tuning rule of thumb:** $\eta/\zeta \geq 10 \cdot d^{*}$ to ensure repulsion always dominates near obstacles. - [x] **Notation:** Use $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ for norms, not single pipe `|`. - [x] **Always draw** a free-body diagram of the robot showing all forces. - [x] **Units:** Potential in J, Force in N, Distances in m, Gains $\zeta, \eta$ in N/m and J/m² respectively. - [x] **Industry usage:** APF is almost always a **local planner inside a hybrid system** (with A*, RRT*, or D* as global planner). > **End of Notes — Potential Field (Module 3, OEBRT723 — Robot Navigation, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Navigation Function - in Static Deterministic Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation Function - in Static Deterministic Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (Koditschek & Rimon, 1990)

A **Navigation Function (NF)** is a real-valued, scalar, artificial potential function $\varphi(q)$ defined over the configuration space (or free space) of a robot, which simultaneously encodes the goal-attractive behavior and the obstacle-repulsive behavior into a single smooth mathematical construct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A Navigation Function $\varphi : \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ is a scalar potential field that is admissible, proper, smooth (i.e., $C^{k}$ continuous, typically $C^{2}$), and whose unique critical point (other than the goal) lies at the boundary of obstacles — thereby guaranteeing a globally convergent, collision-free trajectory."*

Formally, an NF must satisfy **three mandatory properties** on a compact, connected, analytic manifold-with-boundary representing the free space $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$:

$$
\varphi : \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \longrightarrow [0,\, 1]
$$

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Physical Interpretation |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Boundary Properness** | $\varphi(q) = 0 \iff q = q_{\text{goal}}$ | Global minimum coincides **exactly** with the target configuration |
| **Obstacle Admissibility** | $\varphi(q) \rightarrow 1$ as $q \rightarrow \partial \mathcal{O}_i$ | Function approaches unity at every obstacle boundary |
| **Polar (Saddle) Structure** | $\varphi$ has only **one** non-degenerate critical point at the goal | All other critical points are non-degenerate saddles on $\partial \mathcal{O}$ |

### 1.2 What is a "Static Deterministic Environment"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Environment Classification (Foundational to KTU Module 3):**
> - **Static** → The environment (obstacles, boundaries) is time-invariant. $\partial \mathcal{O}(t) = \partial \mathcal{O}$.
> - **Deterministic** → The robot has perfect, noise-free knowledge of its own state $q$ and the obstacle geometry. There is **no stochastic uncertainty** in the perception or actuation model.

A **static deterministic environment** is, therefore, a perfectly known, unchanging world. This is the classical *Worst-Case Motion Planning* assumption and is precisely the setting where Navigation Functions possess a **guaranteed global convergence** proof.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Glow-Floor Hospital"

Imagine a hospital corridor at night. The floor is painted with a **luminescent gradient** that smoothly intensifies from the patient's bedside (goal) outward, and **intensifies to a maximum brightness (white-hot)** near every wall and piece of furniture (obstacles).

- A small autonomous wheelchair with a light-following wheel will naturally roll from **bright → dim** to reach the patient.
- It will **never climb the white-hot walls** because those are maxima, not minima.
- The brightness function $\varphi(q)$ is a **navigation function**.
- The hospital layout never changes (static) and is fully known (deterministic).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy is powerful for KTU vivas:** It captures the three pillars — *Goal Attraction*, *Obstacle Repulsion*, and *Smoothness* — in one image. A 3-mark question can be answered with this mental model.

### 1.4 Why Navigation Functions Matter in KTU Robotics

A feedback controller is derived as the **negative gradient flow** of the NF:

$$
\dot{q} = -\, \nabla_{q}\,\varphi(q)
$$

Because $\varphi$ is provably Morse (only isolated non-degenerate critical points) on a compact free space, almost every initial condition produces a trajectory that **converges to the goal without getting trapped in local minima** — unlike the classical Artificial Potential Field (APF) method of Khatib (1986) which suffers from the *local-minima problem*.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction Pipeline (Logical Decomposition)

The construction of a valid navigation function follows a rigorous **5-step pipeline**:

1. **Workspace Discretization** — Decompose the free space $\mathcal{W} \setminus \mathcal{O}$ into star-shaped regions (e.g., generalized Voronoi diagram or sphere-world partitions).
2. **Define Goal-Distance Function** — Compute the squared Euclidean distance to the target:
   $$d_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q) = \| q - q_{\text{goal}} \|^{2}$$
3. **Define Obstacle Function $\beta_i(q)$** — For each obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$, define a smooth signed-distance-like term that vanishes on $\partial \mathcal{O}_i$.
4. **Aggregate $\beta(q)$** — Multiply all obstacle functions to get a *joint* boundary function:
   $$\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \beta_i(q)$$
5. **Apply the Tuning Function** — Combine $d_{\text{goal}}$ and $\beta$ through a *tuning function* with parameter $\kappa$.

### 2.2 The Koditschek–Rimon Sphere-World Navigation Function

The **canonical closed-form NF** for a *sphere world* (point robot among $m$ spherical obstacles) is given by:

$$
\varphi(q) \;=\; \frac{\, d_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q)\,}{\,\Big( d_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q)\Big)^{\kappa} \;+\; \beta(q)\,}
$$

where:

- $d_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q) = \Vert q - q_{\text{goal}}\Vert^{2}$ is the squared distance to the goal.
- $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \beta_i(q)$ is the product of individual obstacle functions.
- $\kappa \in \mathbb{R},\; \kappa > 0$ is the **tuning parameter** (sometimes called *navigation function gain*).

For a spherical obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$ of radius $r_i$ centered at $c_i$:

$$
\beta_i(q) \;=\; \Vert q - c_i \Vert^{2} - r_i^{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\kappa$ is called the "tuning" parameter:**
> As $\kappa$ increases, the term $(d_{\text{goal}}^{2})^{\kappa}$ dominates in regions far from both the goal and the obstacles, effectively **flattening** the landscape and **breaking the saddle points** that would otherwise trap the robot. There exists a threshold $\kappa^{\star}$ above which $\varphi$ becomes a *true* navigation function.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable for the 14-mark KTU university question. Memorize the column "Why it matters".**

| # | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Domain | Why It Matters in KTU |
|---|------------------|------------|--------|---------------------|
