$$\sin 10.025^\circ = 0.174\,043\,469$$

**Step 2.** Compute $\sin^2 \phi$.

$$\sin^2 \phi = (0.174\,043\,469)^2 = 0.030\,291\,117$$

**Step 3.** Compute $1 - e^2 \sin^2\phi$.

$$1 - (0.00669438)(0.030291117) = 1 - 0.000202792 = 0.999797208$$

**Step 4.** Compute $\sqrt{1 - e^2 \sin^2\phi}$.

$$\sqrt{0.999797208} = 0.999898603$$

**Step 5.** Compute $N$.

$$N = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{1 - e^2 \sin^2 \phi}} = \dfrac{6\,378\,137}{0.999898603}$$

$$\boxed{N = 6\,378\,645.69 \text{ m}}$$

Notice that $N$ is **slightly larger** than $a$ — the prime-vertical radius of curvature grows as you move away from the equator because the ellipsoid bulges.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Meridional Arc $M(\phi)$ (Clenshaw-style expansion simplified)

For exam problems with $\phi < 20°$, a 4-term expansion gives better than 1 mm accuracy:

$$M(\phi) = a\!\left[(1 - \tfrac{e^2}{4} - \tfrac{3e^4}{64} - \tfrac{5e^6}{256})\phi - (\tfrac{3e^2}{8} + \tfrac{3e^4}{32} + \tfrac{45e^6}{1024})\sin 2\phi + (\tfrac{15e^4}{256} + \tfrac{45e^6}{1024})\sin 4\phi - \tfrac{35e^6}{3072}\sin 6\phi\right]$$

**Step 1.** Pre-compute the small constants for WGS-84.

$$c_1 = 1 - \tfrac{e^2}{4} - \tfrac{3e^4}{64} = 0.998324298$$

$$c_2 = \tfrac{3e^2}{8} + \tfrac{3e^4}{32} = 0.002510075$$

$$c_3 = \tfrac{15e^4}{256} = 0.000003488$$

$$c_4 = \tfrac{35e^6}{3072} \approx 0$$

**Step 2.** With $\phi = 10.025^\circ$:

- $\phi$ in radians $= 10.025^\circ \times \pi/180 = 0.174\,989\,210$ rad
- $\sin 2\phi = \sin 20.05^\circ = 0.342\,898$
- $\sin 4\phi = \sin 40.10^\circ = 0.644\,217$
- $\sin 6\phi = \sin 60.15^\circ = 0.866\,676$

**Step 3.** Substitute.

$$M(\phi) = 6\,378\,137 \bigl[ 0.998324298(0.174989210) - 0.002510075(0.342898) + 0.000003488(0.644217) \bigr]$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate inside the brackets.

$$= 6\,378\,137 \bigl[ 0.174703 - 0.000861 + 0.0000022 \bigr]$$

$$= 6\,378\,137 \times 0.173844$$

$$\boxed{M(\phi) \approx 1\,108\,724.5 \text{ m} \;\;(\approx 1108.72 \text{ km})}$$

This is exactly the meridional distance from the equator to 10° N latitude — and it matches the textbook value of ~1109 km to four significant figures. ✓

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — The Reduced Longitude $A$ and Full UTM Conversion

**Given:** A robot GPS reports $(\phi, \lambda) = (10.025^\circ, 76.89^\circ)$. We have just found $N$ and $M(\phi)$, and the central meridian $\lambda_0 = 75^\circ$ E.

**Step 1.** Convert the longitude difference to radians (always convert to radians before feeding trig functions in UTM formulae).

$$\Delta\lambda = \lambda - \lambda_0 = 76.89^\circ - 75.00^\circ = 1.89^\circ$$

$$\Delta\lambda_{\text{rad}} = 1.89 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.032\,986\,724 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2.** Compute $\cos\phi$.

$$\cos 10.025^\circ = 0.984\,720$$

**Step 3.** Compute the reduced longitude $A$.

$$A = (\lambda - \lambda_0)\cos\phi = 0.032\,986\,724 \times 0.984\,720 = 0.032\,484\,422 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4.** Compute the small auxiliary quantities.

$$T = \tan^2\phi = (\tan 10.025^\circ)^2 = (0.176\,710)^2 = 0.031\,226$$

$$C = e'^2 \cos^2 \phi$$
with $e'^2 = e^2/(1-e^2) = 0.0067394967$.

$$C = 0.0067394967 \times (0.984\,720)^2 = 0.0067394967 \times 0.969673 = 0.006534$$

**Step 5.** Compute zone-local Easting $x$ (formula #9).

$$x = k_0 N \left[ A + (1 - T + C)\dfrac{A^3}{6} \right]$$

$$= 0.9996 \times 6\,378\,645.69 \left[ 0.032484 + (1 - 0.031226 + 0.006534)\dfrac{(0.032484)^3}{6} \right]$$

$$= 6\,375\,698.65 \left[ 0.032484 + (0.975308)\dfrac{3.428 \times 10^{-5}}{6} \right]$$

$$= 6\,375\,698.65 \left[ 0.032484 + 5.572 \times 10^{-6} \right]$$

$$= 6\,375\,698.65 \times 0.032490$$

$$\boxed{x \approx 207\,153.5 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 6.** Add false easting (formula #11).

$$E = 500\,000 + 207\,153.5 = 707\,153.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7.** Compute zone-local Northing $y$ (formula #10).

$$y = k_0\left[ M(\phi) + N \tan\phi \left( \dfrac{A^2}{2} + (5 - T + 9C)\dfrac{A^4}{24} \right) \right]$$

$$= 0.9996 \left[ 1\,108\,724.5 + 6\,378\,645.69 \times 0.176710 \times \left( \dfrac{0.001055}{2} + (5 - 0.031226 + 9 \times 0.006534)\dfrac{1.114 \times 10^{-6}}{24} \right) \right]$$

$$= 0.9996 \left[ 1\,108\,724.5 + 1\,127\,195.9 \times \left( 0.000528 + (5.027)\dfrac{1.114 \times 10^{-6}}{24} \right) \right]$$

$$= 0.9996 \left[ 1\,108\,724.5 + 1\,127\,195.9 \times (0.000528 + 2.334 \times 10^{-7}) \right]$$

$$= 0.9996 \left[ 1\,108\,724.5 + 1\,127\,195.9 \times 0.000528 \right]$$

$$= 0.9996 \left[ 1\,108\,724.5 + 595.16 \right]$$

$$\boxed{y \approx 1\,108\,615.0 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 8.** Hemisphere check. The latitude is **N**, so no false-northing offset is needed:

$$N_{utm} = 1\,108\,615.0 \text{ m}$$

**Final UTM coordinate of the robot:**

$$\boxed{(E, N, Z) = (707\,153.5 \text{ m},\; 1\,108\,615.0 \text{ m},\; 43\text{N})}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — How Each Step Couples to the KTU Outcomes

| KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Step that Demonstrates Mastery |
|---|---|---|
| CO4: Apply map projections to robot navigation data | Apply | Derivation 5 (full conversion) |
| CO5: Analyse distortion in map projection systems | Analyse | Derivation 2, 3 (e² and N) |
| CO6: Evaluate UTM suitability for a navigation task | Evaluate | Step 1–2 of §3.1 (zone selection) |
| CO3: Implement coordinate transforms (conceptually) | Apply | Substitution chain in §3.5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — UTM Pipeline in a Robot Navigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["GNSS Receiver Module"] --> B["Raw NMEA Sentence\nGPGGA: lat, lon, alt, fix"]
    B --> C["Datum Selector\nWGS-84 Default"]
    C --> D["Zone Calculator\nZ = floor((lon+180)/6) + 1"]
    D --> E["Central Meridian lambda0\n= -177 + (Z-1)*6 deg"]
    E --> F["Auxiliary Quantities\ne2, N, M, T, C, A"]
    F --> G["Transverse Mercator\nForward Equations"]
    G --> H["False Easting 500000 m\n+ x  -->  Easting"]
    G --> I["False Northing 0 / 10M m\n+ y  -->  Northing"]
    H --> J["Robot Pose in UTM Frame\n(E, N, U)"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Path Planner\nA* / RRT in metric grid"]
    J --> L["SLAM / Localization\nEKF in UTM"]
    J --> M["Mission Control Waypoint Set\n(E, N) targets"]
```

## 4.2 UTM Zone Layout (Schematic Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z60["Zone 60  174E-180E"]
    P0[("CM = 177E")] --- P0
    end
    subgraph Z1["Zone 1  180W-174W"]
    P00[("CM = -177")] --- P00
    end
    subgraph Z2["Zone 2  174W-168W"]
    P1[("CM = -171")] --- P1
    end
    subgraph ZK["Zone K  ..."]
    P2[("CM = ...")] --- P2
    end
    subgraph Z43["Zone 43  72E-78E"]
    P3[("CM = 75E")] --- P3
    end
    Z60 --- Z1 --- Z2 --- ZK --- Z43
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every box is a 6° longitudinal strip. The arrow inside each zone (→) marks the **central meridian** where the scale factor is at its minimum value $k_0 = 0.9996$. Two parallel lines ±1.67° away (not drawn to scale) are the **lines of true scale** (k = 1.0000).

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — UTM as a Coordinate Adapter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Geodetic Input Domain"]
        A1["phi  in  -90..+90 deg"]
        A2["lambda  in  -180..+180 deg"]
        A3["h  ellipsoidal height m"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["UTM Forward Transform Block"]
        B1["Pre-compute constants\na, b, e2, k0"]
        B2["Ellipsoidal helper functions\nN phi, M phi, T, C, A"]
        B3["Transverse Mercator series"]
        B4["False offsets and zone handling"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["Projected Output Domain"]
        C1["Easting E  in  100k..900k m"]
        C2["Northing N  in  0..10M m"]
        C3["Zone number Z  1..60"]
        C4["Hemisphere flag H"]
    end
    IN --> CORE --> OUT
    OUT --> D1["2D Grid Map for Planner"]
    OUT --> D2["Waypoint List for Mission"]
    OUT --> D3["Geofence in UTM"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's interpretation guide for the diagram:** A block-level functional architecture (as opposed to a literal Earth drawing) is the *preferred* KTU 2024 answer style when a question asks to "explain the working of UTM in robot navigation." It explicitly shows **input → transformation → output**, which is what rubric marks reward.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to add 500 000 m false easting** — most common single mark-loss. The *projected* $x$ is the offset from the central meridian; the *final* $E = 500\,000 + x$.
> 2. **Mixing up hemisphere false-northing rules** — students often add 10 000 000 m to the Northern Hemisphere, doubling the northing. Remember: **south → 10 M offset, north → 0 offset**.
> 3. **Degree vs. radian input to trig functions** — UTM series expansions **must** receive radians. A 76.89° fed directly into $\cos$ and $\tan$ will silently produce garbage without throwing an error.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong zone for a longitude near 6° boundaries** — always verify that $\lambda_0 - 3^\circ \le \lambda < \lambda_0 + 3^\circ$.
> 5. **Confusing $e^2$ and $e'^2$** — $e^2$ uses $a^2$ in the denominator, $e'^2$ uses $b^2$. A mix-up in the $C$ term causes ~10 m errors at the kilometre scale.
> 6. **Ignoring the central scale factor $k_0$** — leaving it out inflates distances by 0.04 %.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]
**Define the Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) projection. Mention any two advantages it offers for robot navigation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*UTM is a conformal, transverse cylindrical map projection that divides the Earth into 60 zones, each 6° wide, and transforms geographic coordinates $(\phi, \lambda)$ into metric grid coordinates (Easting, Northing) on a flat plane.* (1.5 marks)

*Two advantages for robot navigation:*
1. *It provides **metric (meter) distances** directly, allowing path planners and EKF localizers to operate in a true Euclidean grid rather than in angular degrees whose physical length varies with latitude.* (1 mark)
2. *Within a single 6° zone, the **scale distortion is below 0.1 %**, so distances computed by $\sqrt{\Delta E^2 + \Delta N^2}$ are accurate enough for GPS-waypoint following, geofencing, and SLAM.* (0.5 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]
**State the meaning of (a) False Easting, (b) Central Scale Factor $k_0$, and (c) Convergence of Meridians in the UTM system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(a) **False Easting** (1 mark): A constant offset of **500 000 m** added to the projected $x$-coordinate of every UTM point so that all eastings within a zone are **positive**, avoiding negative numbers in the western half of the zone.

(b) **Central Scale Factor $k_0$** (1 mark): A scaling factor of **0.9996** applied at the central meridian, making the projection *secant* (slightly smaller than the true Earth) and distributing distortion evenly across the 6° zone so that no part of the zone is more than ~0.1 % off.

(c) **Convergence of Meridians $\gamma$** (1 mark): The small angular difference at a point between **true north** (along the geographic meridian) and **grid north** (along the UTM northing axis); it grows with distance from the central meridian and is important when aligning a robot's heading sensor with the map grid.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark ESE Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Module-4 Question]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* A delivery robot in Kerala receives a GPS fix of latitude $\phi = 10.025^\circ$ N and longitude $\lambda = 76.89^\circ$ E on the WGS-84 ellipsoid. **Determine (i) the UTM zone number, (ii) the central meridian, and (iii) the value of the prime-vertical radius of curvature $N$.**

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Using the values from part (a) and the standard UTM forward series, **compute the projected Easting and Northing** of the robot, clearly showing every substitution.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Zone, central meridian, $N$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Zone number.

$$Z = \left\lfloor \dfrac{76.89 + 180}{6} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 42.815 \right\rfloor + 1 = 43$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Central meridian.

$$\lambda_0 = -177 + (43 - 1) \cdot 6 = -177 + 252 = 75^\circ \text{ E}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Verify bounds. $\lambda_0 \pm 3^\circ = (72^\circ, 78^\circ)$; $76.89^\circ \in [72^\circ, 78^\circ]$ ✓

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Eccentricity squared (WGS-84).

$$e^2 = 0.006694380$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Compute $N$.

$$\sin 10.025^\circ = 0.174043, \quad \sin^2\phi = 0.030291$$

$$1 - e^2\sin^2\phi = 1 - (0.006694380)(0.030291) = 0.999797$$

$$N = \dfrac{6\,378\,137}{\sqrt{0.999797}} = \dfrac{6\,378\,137}{0.999899} = 6\,378\,645.7 \text{ m}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Easting and Northing (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Reduced longitude.

$$A = (\lambda - \lambda_0)\cos\phi = (1.89^\circ)\cos 10.025^\circ = (0.032987)(0.984720) = 0.032484 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Helpers.

$$T = \tan^2\phi = (0.17671)^2 = 0.03123, \qquad C = e'^2\cos^2\phi = 0.006534$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Easting (zone-local $x$).

$$x = k_0 N \left[ A + (1 - T + C)\dfrac{A^3}{6} \right]$$

$$x = 0.9996 \times 6\,378\,645.7 \times \left[ 0.032484 + (0.97531)\dfrac{3.428 \times 10^{-5}}{6} \right]$$

$$x = 6\,375\,698.7 \times 0.032490 = 207\,153.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply false easting.

$$E = 500\,000 + 207\,153.5 = 707\,153.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Meridional arc.

$$M(\phi) = 1\,108\,724.5 \text{ m (from §3.4)}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Northing (zone-local $y$).

$$y = k_0\left[ M + N\tan\phi \left( \dfrac{A^2}{2} + (5 - T + 9C)\dfrac{A^4}{24} \right) \right]$$

$$y = 0.9996\left[ 1\,108\,724.5 + 595.16 \right] = 1\,108\,615.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 (mandatory final line — 0 marks but required for completeness):** Since latitude is **N**, $N_{utm} = y = 1\,108\,615.0$ m. If latitude were **S**, $N_{utm} = 10\,000\,000 + y$.

**Final UTM pose of the robot:**

$$\boxed{(E,\; N,\; Z) = (707\,153.5 \text{ m},\; 1\,108\,615.0 \text{ m},\; 43\text{N})}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Module-4 Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of neat block diagrams, **explain the working of the UTM projection system** as used in a robot navigation pipeline. Clearly identify the inputs, intermediate computations, and the final outputs.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A surveying robot is deployed at $(\phi, \lambda) = (60.0^\circ \text{ N},\; 10.0^\circ \text{ E})$ on WGS-84. **Compute (i) the UTM zone number and central meridian, (ii) the prime-vertical radius of curvature $N$, and (iii) the value of the convergence of meridians $\gamma$** (use the first-order approximation $\gamma = (\lambda - \lambda_0)\sin\phi$).

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — UTM Working Explanation (7 marks)

**Inputs (1 mark):** Geographic coordinates $(\phi, \lambda)$ in degrees from GPS, and the WGS-84 reference ellipsoid constants $(a, b, e^2, k_0)$.

**Step 1 — Zone selection (1 mark):** Compute zone $Z = \left\lfloor (\lambda + 180)/6 \right\rfloor + 1$ and central meridian $\lambda_0 = -177 + (Z-1)\cdot 6$.

**Step 2 — Auxiliary quantities (2 marks):** Compute $N(\phi)$, $M(\phi)$, $T = \tan^2\phi$, $C = e'^2\cos^2\phi$, and the reduced longitude $A = (\lambda - \lambda_0)\cos\phi$.

**Step 3 — Transverse Mercator series (2 marks):** Plug into the forward equations

$$x = k_0 N\!\left[A + (1 - T + C)\dfrac{A^3}{6} + \dots\right]$$
$$y = k_0\!\left[M(\phi) + N\tan\phi \!\left(\dfrac{A^2}{2} + (5 - T + 9C)\dfrac{A^4}{24} + \dots\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — False offsets (1 mark):** Apply $E = 500\,000 + x$; for the Southern Hemisphere, $N_{utm} = 10\,000\,000 + y$; for the Northern Hemisphere, $N_{utm} = y$.

**Output:** The robot's planar pose $(E, N, Z, H)$ in metric units, ready for the planner/SLAM stack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-allocation hint (KTU 2024):** Examiners allocate marks roughly equally across the four sub-steps. A labelled block diagram (similar to Section 4.3) earns **at least 2 of the 7 marks** even before any formula is written.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Zone and central meridian.

$$Z = \left\lfloor \dfrac{10 + 180}{6} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 31.667 \right\rfloor + 1 = 32$$

$$\lambda_0 = -177 + 31 \cdot 6 = -177 + 186 = 9^\circ \text{ E}$$

**Step 2 (0.5 mark):** Check bounds: $9^\circ \pm 3^\circ = (6^\circ, 12^\circ)$; $10^\circ \in [6^\circ, 12^\circ]$ ✓

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Prime-vertical radius of curvature at $\phi = 60.0^\circ$.

$$\sin 60^\circ = 0.866025, \quad \sin^2 60^\circ = 0.75$$

$$1 - e^2\sin^2\phi = 1 - (0.006694380)(0.75) = 1 - 0.005020785 = 0.994979215$$

$$N = \dfrac{6\,378\,137}{\sqrt{0.994979215}} = \dfrac{6\,378\,137}{0.997486} = 6\,394\,290.3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Convergence of meridians (first-order).

$$\Delta\lambda = 10^\circ - 9^\circ = 1.0^\circ = \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.0174533 \text{ rad}$$

$$\gamma = \Delta\lambda \cdot \sin\phi = 0.0174533 \times 0.866025$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = 0.015114 \text{ rad} = 0.866^\circ \approx 51.9 \text{ arc-min}}$$

**Interpretation (mandatory closing line):** Grid north at this point lies 0.866° east of true north — the robot's compass must be rotated by this amount when aligning to the UTM grid for surveying work.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must be at the fingertip during the exam)**
> - UTM is a **transverse cylindrical, conformal, secant** projection with **$k_0 = 0.9996$**.
> - Earth is divided into **60 zones** of **6° longitude each**; **Zone 1** starts at **180° W**.
> - **False Easting = 500 000 m** (always); **False Northing = 0 m (N)** or **10 000 000 m (S)**.
> - WGS-84 constants: $a = 6\,378\,137$ m, $e^2 \approx 0.006694380$.
> - Convert **degrees to radians** before applying any trig function in UTM series.
> - Reduced longitude: $A = (\lambda - \lambda_0)\cos\phi$ — small, dimensionless, and in **radians**.
> - Helpers to remember: $T = \tan^2\phi$, $C = e'^2\cos^2\phi$, where $e'^2 = e^2/(1-e^2) \approx 0.006739497$.
> - The **first three terms** of the Easting/Northing series (powers $A^3$ and $A^2$ respectively) are sufficient for sub-millimetre accuracy inside a 6° zone.
> - Maximum scale distortion inside a UTM zone is **±0.1 %** (1 part in 1000) — a 1 km path is accurate to ±1 m.
> - For **robot navigation**, UTM gives metric distances, enabling Euclidean path planning, A*, RRT, and EKF-based SLAM.
> - **Convergence of meridians** $\gamma$ must be applied when converting a magnetic/true heading to a UTM grid heading — vital for surveying and aerial-robot georeferencing.
> - UTM is **not defined** for the polar regions (latitudes > 84° N and < 80° S); the **UPS (Universal Polar Stereographic)** system is used there.
> - A common exam trap: do **not** confuse **zone number** with **UTM latitude band letter** — robotics typically uses only the zone number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Cartesian Coordinates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Local Cartesian Coordinates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile robotics, a **Local Cartesian Coordinate Frame (also called the body frame, robot frame, or vehicle frame)** is a right-handed orthogonal coordinate system whose **origin is rigidly attached to the robot's geometric center** (typically the midpoint of the drive axle for a differential-drive robot) and whose axes move and rotate *with* the robot as it navigates through its environment.

The three principal axes of the local Cartesian frame are conventionally defined as:

| Axis | Conventional Name | Direction |
|------|-------------------|-----------|
| $X_L$ | Forward / Longitudinal axis | Points along the robot's forward heading direction |
| $Y_L$ | Lateral / Transverse axis | Points to the robot's left (right-handed convention) |
| $Z_L$ | Vertical / Normal axis | Points upward, completing the right-hand rule |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Textbook Terminology):**
> A *local* Cartesian coordinate system is a **non-inertial, body-fixed reference frame** that translates and rotates synchronously with the robot. Every point observed by the robot's onboard sensors is first expressed in this local frame before being transformed into the **Global (World) Cartesian Frame** $\{W\}$ for navigation, mapping, and path planning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in the driver's seat of a car wearing a **VR headset that completely blocks the outside world**. You can still see a cup-holder directly in front of you, a window to your left, and the roof above you. From *your* perspective:

- "Forward" is always wherever your nose points.
- "Left" is always toward the left door.
- "Up" is always toward the roof.

This **first-person, ego-centric perspective is exactly the Local Cartesian Frame**. The car's windshield becomes the $X_L$ axis, your left shoulder becomes the $Y_L$ axis, and the roof becomes the $Z_L$ axis.

Now imagine a **satellite tracking your car from space**. The satellite uses latitude, longitude, and altitude (a Global Frame) — it does not care which way the car is facing. To merge these two perspectives, the car's onboard computer must continuously compute a **coordinate transformation** between the Local Frame and the Global Frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Cartesian"?** Cartesian refers to the perpendicular, orthogonal axes invented by René Descartes. Unlike polar or spherical coordinates, Cartesian coordinates make **vector arithmetic, matrix rotations, and Jacobian-based velocity transformations** algebraically simple — which is why every robot navigation stack uses them.

## 1.3 The Global vs. Local Frame Distinction

| Property | Global Frame $\{W\}$ | Local Frame $\{R\}$ |
|----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Origin** | Fixed (e.g., map corner, GPS datum) | Rigidly attached to the robot |
| **Motion** | Stationary (inertial) | Translates *and* rotates with the robot |
| **Typical Use** | Long-term path planning, map storage | Sensor data interpretation, control |
| **Standard Notation** | $(x, y, \theta)$ world pose | $(x_L, y_L, z_L)$ body coordinates |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Constant (Standard Convention):**
> Throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus and the broader ROS (Robot Operating System) community standard **REP-103**, the local frame follows the **right-hand rule** with $X_L$ forward, $Y_L$ left, and $Z_L$ up. Some aerospace and automotive standards (e.g., **ISO 8855**) instead use $X_L$ forward, $Y_L$ right, and $Z_L$ down — students must **always verify the convention** before solving numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

A robot's onboard sensors (LiDAR, camera, IMU, wheel encoders) inherently measure quantities in the **local frame**. To make these measurements useful for navigation, the robot must perform four fundamental operations:

### Step 1 — Sensor Data Acquisition in Local Frame
Each sensor reports the position of detected features (obstacles, landmarks, waypoints) **relative to the robot body**.
- A LiDAR point cloud arrives as a set of vectors $\{p_i^L = (x_i^L, y_i^L, z_i^L)\}$.
- A wheel encoder reports the rotation of each wheel $\Delta \phi_r, \Delta \phi_l$.

**Why?** Sensors are physically mounted on the robot — they cannot "see" the world in any coordinate system other than their own.

### Step 2 — Pose Estimation in Global Frame
The robot estimates its global pose $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (x, y, \theta)^T$ using either **odometry**, **GPS**, or **SLAM**.
- $x, y$ = position of the local frame's origin in the global frame.
- $\theta$ = heading angle of $X_L$ axis measured from the global $X_W$ axis (CCW positive).

**Why?** Without a global pose, the robot cannot decide *where to go* on a stored map.

### Step 3 — Coordinate Transformation (Local → Global)
To convert a local point into a global point, the robot applies a **2D rotation-translation**:

$$
\mathbf{p}^W = \mathbf{R}(\theta)\,\mathbf{p}^L + \mathbf{t}
$$

where $\mathbf{R}(\theta)$ is the planar rotation matrix and $\mathbf{t} = (x, y)^T$ is the robot's global position.

**Why?** This is the mathematical bridge that allows sensor data to be overlaid on a global map.

### Step 4 — Control Command Generation in Local Frame
The motion controller computes desired linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ — **both are expressed in the local frame** because the wheels respond to *body-frame* commands, not world-frame ones.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Physical Meaning / Units |
|---|---------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Planar Rotation Matrix | $\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | Rotates a local-frame vector into the global frame. Unitless, **orthogonal** ($\mathbf{R}^T = \mathbf{R}^{-1}$). |
| 2 | Local-to-Global Point Transform | $\begin{bmatrix} x^W \\ y^W \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x^L \\ y^L \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$ | Converts sensor-measured point into global coordinates. Units: **meters**. |
| 3 | Global-to-Local Point Transform | $\begin{bmatrix} x^L \\ y^L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x^W - x \\ y^W - y \end{bmatrix}$ | Converts a global map point into robot-centric coordinates. Units: **meters**. |
| 4 | Robot Pose Vector | $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (x,\, y,\, \theta)^T$ | Encodes position (m) and orientation (rad) in global frame. |
| 5 | Forward Kinematics (Differential Drive) | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}(\omega_r + \omega_l), \quad \omega = \dfrac{r}{L}(\omega_r - \omega_l)$ | Computes body-frame velocities from wheel angular speeds. Units: m/s and rad/s. |
| 6 | Odometry Update | $\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} &= x_k + v\cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t \\ y_{k+1} &= y_k + v\sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t \\ \theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{aligned}$ | Integrates local-frame velocities to advance the global pose. Units: meters, radians. |
| 7 | Velocity Jacobian | $\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$ | Maps body-frame velocities $(v, \omega)$ to world-frame twist $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$. |
| 8 | Wheelbase Constraint | $L = \text{distance between left and right wheels}$ | Geometric constant of the robot, **measured in meters (m)**. |
| 9 | Wheel Radius | $r$ | Geometric constant, **measured in meters (m)**. |
| 10 | Homogeneous Transform (2D) | $\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Compact $3\times3$ matrix for combined rotation + translation. |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Units & Standards Used in KTU 2024:**
> - Distances in **meters (m)**
> - Angles in **radians (rad)**
> - Time in **seconds (s)**
> - Linear velocity in **m/s**
> - Angular velocity in **rad/s**
> - Wheel radius and wheelbase are **fixed physical constants** of the robot, measured once during calibration.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Local Cartesian coordinates are the **lingua franca** of every commercial mobile robot:

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** The vehicle's planning module outputs a trajectory in the global frame (e.g., GPS coordinates), while the controller tracks that trajectory using body-frame commands (accelerator, brake, steering angle).
- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Amazon's Kiva robots use a local frame to interpret LiDAR scans, then transform obstacle positions into the global map frame to update SLAM.
- **Aerial Drones (UAVs):** The PX4 and Ardupilot flight stacks use NED (North-East-Down) or ENU (East-North-Up) local frames for body-rate control, with continuous transformation to a global geodetic frame.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Local Cartesian coordinates enable stable station-keeping against ocean currents, since current vectors are first measured in the body frame and then cancelled by thruster commands also issued in the body frame.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Local-to-Global Point Transformation

**Goal:** Show that a point $\mathbf{p}^L$ measured in the robot's local frame can be expressed in the global frame as $\mathbf{p}^W = \mathbf{R}(\theta)\,\mathbf{p}^L + \mathbf{t}$.

### Setup

Let the global frame have origin $O_W$ at axes $(X_W, Y_W)$. The local frame has origin $O_R$ at axes $(X_L, Y_L)$. The local frame's origin is located at global coordinates $(x, y)$ and its $X_L$ axis is rotated by angle $\theta$ counter-clockwise from $X_W$.

Consider a landmark $P$ that the robot's sensor reports as having local coordinates $\mathbf{p}^L = (p_x^L, p_y^L)^T$. We wish to find $\mathbf{p}^W = (p_x^W, p_y^W)^T$.

### Step 1 — Express Local Components Along Global Axes

The unit vector along $X_L$, when projected onto the global axes, has components $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$.
The unit vector along $Y_L$, when projected onto the global axes, has components $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$.

> **Logic:** A vector of length 1 pointing in the $X_L$ direction tilts by $\theta$ from $X_W$. Its horizontal projection is $\cos\theta$ and its vertical projection is $\sin\theta$. Similarly for $Y_L$, which is $90^\circ$ CCW from $X_L$, giving the negative-sine and positive-cosine components.

### Step 2 — Write the Linear Combination

The global coordinate of $P$ is the sum of:
(a) the displacement from $O_W$ to $O_R$ (translation $\mathbf{t}$), and
(b) the projection of $\mathbf{p}^L$ onto global axes (rotation).

$$
\mathbf{p}^W = \mathbf{t} + p_x^L \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \end{bmatrix} + p_y^L \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Factor into Matrix Form

Grouping the coefficients of $p_x^L$ and $p_y^L$:

$$
\mathbf{p}^W = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} p_x^L \\ p_y^L \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{p}^W = \mathbf{R}(\theta)\,\mathbf{p}^L + \mathbf{t}}
$$

where

$$
\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{t} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic Check:** If $\theta = 0$ (robot aligned with global frame), then $\mathbf{R}(0) = \mathbf{I}$, and we recover $\mathbf{p}^W = \mathbf{p}^L + \mathbf{t}$ — pure translation. This matches physical intuition: a non-rotated robot simply shifts its sensor reading by its own position. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a Differential-Drive Robot

**Goal:** Express the body-frame linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ in terms of wheel angular velocities $\omega_r, \omega_l$.

### Setup

A differential-drive robot has:
- Two drive wheels of radius $r$ (m), separated by axle length $L$ (m).
- Right wheel angular speed $\omega_r$ (rad/s), left wheel angular speed $\omega_l$ (rad/s).

### Step 1 — Wheel-Contact Linear Velocities

Each wheel rolling without slipping produces a linear velocity at its contact point:

$$
v_r = r\,\omega_r, \qquad v_l = r\,\omega_l
$$

> **Logic:** The arc length swept by a wheel in one second equals radius $\times$ angular displacement — a standard rigid-body rolling relation.

### Step 2 — Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

The robot pivots about a point on the axle line. The body-frame linear velocity (at the center) is the **average** of the two wheel velocities (by symmetry):

$$
v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_r + \omega_l)
$$

> **Logic:** The midpoint of the axle is equidistant from both wheels, so it inherits the average translational speed.

### Step 3 — Angular Velocity of the Body

The difference in wheel speeds causes the body to rotate. The angular velocity equals the velocity difference divided by the axle length:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_r - \omega_l)
$$

> **Logic:** If the right wheel spins faster, the robot yaws CCW (positive $\omega$). The lever arm is $L$, and the tangential speed difference across that lever arm is $v_r - v_l$. Dividing by the lever arm yields angular velocity.

### Step 4 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned} v &= \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_r + \omega_l) \\ \omega &= \tfrac{r}{L}(\omega_r - \omega_l) \end{aligned}}
$$

> **Logic Check:** If $\omega_r = \omega_l$ (straight-line motion), then $\omega = 0$ and $v = r\omega_r$ — the robot moves straight at the wheel speed, as expected. If $\omega_r = -\omega_l$ (in-place spin), then $v = 0$ and $\omega = 2r\omega_r/L$ — pure rotation, also as expected. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Odometry Update from Body-Frame Velocities

**Goal:** Given $v(t)$ and $\omega(t)$ measured in the body frame, compute the change in global pose over a small time step $\Delta t$.

### Step 1 — Express Body Velocities in Global Frame

A body-frame linear velocity $v$ pointing along $X_L$ has global-frame components:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \qquad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

The body-frame angular velocity $\omega$ about $Z_L$ equals the rate of change of the heading:

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

> **Logic:** The body-frame $X_L$ axis is tilted by $\theta$ from the global $X_W$ axis, so the projection of a forward velocity $v$ onto the $X_W$ axis is $v\cos\theta$ and onto $Y_W$ is $v\sin\theta$.

### Step 2 — Write the Differential Equations

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **velocity Jacobian** of the differential-drive system.

### Step 3 — Discretize Using Euler Integration

For a small time step $\Delta t$ and approximately constant $(v, \omega)$ over that interval:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v\cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v\sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Euler integration approximates the continuous derivative as a finite difference: $\dot{x} \approx \Delta x / \Delta t$, so $\Delta x = v\cos\theta \cdot \Delta t$. The approximation is exact in the limit $\Delta t \to 0$.

### Step 4 — Final Odometry Equation

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\xi}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\xi}_k + \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta_k \\ v\sin\theta_k \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}\Delta t}
$$

> **Logic Check:** If $v = 1$ m/s, $\omega = 0$, and $\theta = 0$ for 1 second, then the pose advances by $(1, 0, 0)$ — pure forward motion of 1 m, matching intuition. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m. At time $t = 0$, the robot's global pose is $\boldsymbol{\xi}_0 = (0,\, 0,\, 0)^T$. The wheel encoders report $\omega_r = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_l = 6$ rad/s for $\Delta t = 1$ s. Compute the new pose.

### Step 1 — Compute Body-Frame Velocities

$$
v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_r + \omega_l) = \frac{0.05}{2}(10 + 6) = 0.025 \times 16 = 0.40 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_r - \omega_l) = \frac{0.05}{0.30}(10 - 6) = 0.1667 \times 4 = 0.6667 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> **Logic:** $v$ is the average of the two wheel speeds scaled by radius; $\omega$ is the speed difference scaled by radius/axle-length ratio.

### Step 2 — Apply Odometry Update

With $\theta_0 = 0$, $\cos\theta_0 = 1$, $\sin\theta_0 = 0$:

$$
x_1 = 0 + 0.40 \times 1 \times 1 = 0.40 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_1 = 0 + 0.40 \times 1 \times 0 = 0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 0 + 0.6667 \times 1 = 0.6667 \text{ rad} \approx 38.2^\circ
$$

### Step 3 — Final Pose

$$
\boldsymbol{\xi}_1 = (0.40 \text{ m},\ 0 \text{ m},\ 0.6667 \text{ rad})^T
$$

> **Logic Check:** The robot moved 0.40 m forward and rotated about $38.2^\circ$ CCW. Since the right wheel was faster, CCW rotation is correct. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Coordinate Frame Relationship (Geometric Schematic)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the geometric relationship between the Global Frame $\{W\}$ and the Local Frame $\{R\}$ attached to a mobile robot, including the transformation data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GLOBAL["GLOBAL FRAME W  origin OW  axes XW YW"]
        OW["OW at 0,0"]
        XW["XW East"]
        YW["YW North"]
    end

    subgraph ROBOT["ROBOT BODY  local frame R"]
        OR["OR at global pose x y theta"]
        XL["XL Forward heading"]
        YL["YL Left lateral"]
    end

    subgraph SENSORS["ONBOARD SENSORS  measure in R"]
        LIDAR["LiDAR  points p_i to the left"]
        CAM["Camera  landmark at front"]
        ENC["Wheel encoders  omega_r omega_l"]
    end

    subgraph NAVSTACK["NAVIGATION STACK"]
        FWD["Forward kinematics  v omega"]
        ODOM["Odometry update  xi_k to xi_k+1"]
        INV["Inverse transform  R transpose of p minus t"]
    end

    LIDAR --> OR
    CAM --> OR
    ENC --> FWD
    FWD --> ODOM
    ODOM --> OR
    OR --> INV
    INV --> LIDAR
    INV --> CAM
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Coordinate Transformations

The following Mermaid diagram details the **mathematical pipeline** by which raw sensor data (in the local frame) is converted into globally meaningful information for path planning, and how global commands are converted back into local actuator commands.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["RAW SENSOR DATA  local frame R"] --> B["PREPROCESSING  noise filtering  outlier removal"]
    B --> C["POINT TRANSFORM  pW = R theta times pL + t"]
    C --> D["GLOBAL MAP UPDATE  SLAM module  occupancy grid"]
    D --> E["PATH PLANNER  A star  RRT  global trajectory"]
    E --> F["COMMAND INVERSE TRANSFORM  pL = R transpose theta times pW minus t"]
    F --> G["LOCAL CONTROLLER  PID on linear and angular velocity"]
    G --> H["ACTUATOR SIGNALS  omega_r omega_l sent to motor drivers"]
    H --> I["WHEEL ROTATION  produces new odometry"]
    I --> J["POSE UPDATE  new global pose xi new"]
    J --> C
```

## 4.3 Frame Convention Reference Card

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REP103["ROS REP 103 STANDARD"]
        A1["X forward"]
        A2["Y left"]
        A3["Z up"]
    end
    subgraph ISO8855["ISO 8855 VEHICLE STANDARD"]
        B1["X forward"]
        B2["Y right"]
        B3["Z down"]
    end
    REP103 --- ISO8855
```

> [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows that **sensors and encoders feed the local frame** directly, while the **odometry block updates the global pose**, which then re-orients the local frame.
> - The second diagram illustrates the **closed-loop nature** of robot navigation: the global planner produces a desired trajectory, which is converted to a local-frame reference for the controller, which issues actuator signals, which close the loop through new odometry.
> - The third diagram is a quick-reference card summarizing the two most common axis conventions students will encounter in KTU examinations and in real-world robotics code.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Define a Local Cartesian Coordinate Frame in the context of a mobile robot. How does it differ from a Global Cartesian Coordinate Frame?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Local Cartesian Coordinate Frame is a right-handed orthogonal coordinate system whose origin is rigidly attached to the robot body and whose axes translate and rotate with the robot as it moves (1 mark). It is the reference frame in which onboard sensors (LiDAR, camera, IMU) natively report measurements (1 mark). In contrast, the Global Cartesian Frame is a fixed, inertial reference frame anchored to the environment, used for long-term path planning, map storage, and global pose representation (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Write the 2D rotation matrix that transforms a point from the local frame $\{R\}$ to the global frame $\{W\}$ for a robot with heading angle $\theta$. What is the inverse of this matrix?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The 2D rotation matrix from local to global frame is (2 marks):

$$
\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

The inverse of a rotation matrix is its transpose, which corresponds to rotation by $-\theta$ (1 mark):

$$
\mathbf{R}^{-1}(\theta) = \mathbf{R}^T(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Model Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the forward kinematics of a differential-drive mobile robot expressing the body-frame linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ in terms of the wheel angular velocities $\omega_r$ and $\omega_l$, the wheel radius $r$, and the wheelbase $L$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A differential-drive robot has $r = 0.04$ m and $L = 0.20$ m. Starting from global pose $(0, 0, 0)^T$, it applies $\omega_r = 8$ rad/s and $\omega_l = 4$ rad/s for $2$ seconds. Using Euler odometry, compute the final global pose. Also determine the global coordinates of an obstacle that the on-board LiDAR reports at local coordinates $(1.0, 0.5)$ m at the final pose.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Each wheel rolling without slipping produces a linear velocity at the contact patch: $v_r = r\omega_r$ and $v_l = r\omega_l$.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* The body-frame linear velocity (at the axle midpoint) is the arithmetic mean of the two wheel speeds:

$$
v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_r + \omega_l)
$$

*Step 3 (2 marks):* The velocity difference between the two wheels, divided by the axle length, yields the body angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_r - \omega_l)
$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* These two equations together form the **forward kinematics** of the differential-drive robot. The first maps wheel speeds to body translation; the second maps wheel speeds to body rotation. They are invertible, so the **inverse kinematics** is also uniquely defined.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]:**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Compute the body-frame velocities.

$$
v = \frac{0.04}{2}(8 + 4) = 0.02 \times 12 = 0.24 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{0.04}{0.20}(8 - 4) = 0.20 \times 4 = 0.80 \text{ rad/s}
$$

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Apply Euler odometry with $\Delta t = 2$ s. Since $\theta_0 = 0$, $\cos 0 = 1$ and $\sin 0 = 0$:

$$
x_1 = 0 + 0.24 \times 1 \times 2 = 0.48 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_1 = 0 + 0.24 \times 0 \times 2 = 0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 0 + 0.80 \times 2 = 1.60 \text{ rad} \approx 91.67^\circ
$$

Final global pose: $\boldsymbol{\xi}_1 = (0.48 \text{ m},\ 0 \text{ m},\ 1.60 \text{ rad})^T$.

*Step 3 (3 marks):* Transform the local obstacle point $(1.0, 0.5)$ m into the global frame.

The rotation matrix at $\theta_1 = 1.60$ rad is:

$$
\mathbf{R}(1.60) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 1.60 & -\sin 1.60 \\ \sin 1.60 & \cos 1.60 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0292 & -0.9996 \\ 0.9996 & -0.0292 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply the transformation $\mathbf{p}^W = \mathbf{R}(\theta_1)\,\mathbf{p}^L + \mathbf{t}$:

$$
\mathbf{p}^W = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0292 & -0.9996 \\ 0.9996 & -0.0292 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.48 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

First, compute the rotated point:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} -0.0292 \times 1.0 + (-0.9996) \times 0.5 \\ 0.9996 \times 1.0 + (-0.0292) \times 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0292 - 0.4998 \\ 0.9996 - 0.0146 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5290 \\ 0.9850 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then add the translation:

$$
\mathbf{p}^W = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5290 + 0.48 \\ 0.9850 + 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0490 \\ 0.9850 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}
$$

**The obstacle is located at approximately $(-0.049,\, 0.985)$ m in the global frame.**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Model Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of the **robot pose vector** $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (x, y, \theta)^T$. Why is this vector sufficient to fully describe the configuration of a planar mobile robot, and how does it relate to the local Cartesian frame?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **inverse coordinate transform** that takes a global-frame point $\mathbf{p}^W$ and returns its local-frame coordinates $\mathbf{p}^L$, given the robot's global pose $(x, y, \theta)$. Show that your result reduces to the identity transform when $\theta = 0$ and $x = y = 0$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Concept Explanation [7 Marks]:**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* The pose vector $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (x, y, \theta)^T$ encodes the **complete planar configuration** of a rigid-body mobile robot. The scalars $(x, y)$ specify the position of the local frame's origin in the global frame, and the scalar $\theta$ specifies the orientation (heading angle) of the local $X_L$ axis measured CCW from the global $X_W$ axis.

*Step 2 (3 marks):* For a **planar** (2D) rigid body, the configuration space is $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ — two translational degrees of freedom and one rotational degree of freedom. The pose vector is therefore the **minimal sufficient representation**: it captures all three DOFs without redundancy. For a 3D robot, one would need a 6-DOF pose (position + roll/pitch/yaw), but for a ground robot moving on a flat surface, three components are enough.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* The pose vector **defines the rigid-body transformation** that maps the local frame to the global frame:

$$
\mathbf{T}(\boldsymbol{\xi}) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This homogeneous transform is the cornerstone of every sensor-fusion, motion-planning, and control algorithm in the robot's software stack.

**Part (b) — Derivation of Inverse Transform [7 Marks]:**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Begin with the forward transform derived in Section 3.1:

$$
\mathbf{p}^W = \mathbf{R}(\theta)\,\mathbf{p}^L + \mathbf{t}
$$

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Subtract the translation from both sides:

$$
\mathbf{p}^W - \mathbf{t} = \mathbf{R}(\theta)\,\mathbf{p}^L
$$

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Left-multiply both sides by $\mathbf{R}^{-1}(\theta) = \mathbf{R}^T(\theta)$:

$$
\mathbf{p}^L = \mathbf{R}^T(\theta)\bigl(\mathbf{p}^W - \mathbf{t}\bigr)
$$

Expanding explicitly:

$$
\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} p_x^L \\ p_y^L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} p_x^W - x \\ p_y^W - y \end{bmatrix}}
$$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* **Sanity check:** When $\theta = 0$, $x = 0$, $y = 0$, we have $\mathbf{R}^T(0) = \mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{0}$, giving $\mathbf{p}^L = \mathbf{p}^W$. The local and global frames coincide, so the transform is the identity. ✓

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Rotation Matrix:** A frequent mistake is writing $\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ with a **positive** off-diagonal. The correct matrix has a **negative** $\sin\theta$ in the top-right position. Marks lost: **1–2 marks per occurrence**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Add the Translation Vector:** When transforming a point, students often compute $\mathbf{R}(\theta)\,\mathbf{p}^L$ but **omit the $+(x, y)$ translation**. This produces a point that is rotated correctly but not displaced to the robot's actual position. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing Local-to-Global with Global-to-Local:** The two transforms use $\mathbf{R}(\theta)$ and $\mathbf{R}^T(\theta)$ respectively. Transposing incorrectly is a very common error. Marks lost: **up to 3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Radians vs. Degrees in Numerical Problems:** Computing $\cos(1.6)$ instead of $\cos(1.6 \text{ rad})$ — or worse, mixing $\sin/\cos$ of degree values into a radian-based problem. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Inverting the Wheel Speed Formulas:** Confusing forward kinematics ($v, \omega$ from $\omega_r, \omega_l$) with inverse kinematics ($\omega_r, \omega_l$ from $v, \omega$). Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Omitting Units in the Final Answer:** The KTU rubric explicitly awards partial credit for correct units (m, m/s, rad/s). Always annotate numerical answers. Marks lost: **0.5–1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Local Cartesian Frame = Robot Body Frame:** Origin fixed on the robot; axes move with the robot. Right-handed, typically $X_L$ forward, $Y_L$ left, $Z_L$ up (ROS REP-103).
- [x] **Global Cartesian Frame = World Frame:** Fixed, inertial reference for maps and long-term planning.
- [x] **Robot Pose:** $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (x, y, \theta)^T$ — position $(x, y)$ in meters and heading $\theta$ in **radians**, CCW positive.
- [x] **Planar Rotation Matrix:** $\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ — note the **negative** off-diagonal.
- [x] **Local-to-Global Point:** $\mathbf{p}^W = \mathbf{R}(\theta)\mathbf{p}^L + \mathbf{t}$.
- [x] **Global-to-Local Point:** $\mathbf{p}^L = \mathbf{R}^T(\theta)(\mathbf{p}^W - \mathbf{t})$.
- [x] **Differential-Drive Forward Kinematics:** $v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_r + \omega_l)$ and $\omega = \tfrac{r}{L}(\omega_r - \omega_l)$.
- [x] **Differential-Drive Inverse Kinematics:** $\omega_r = \tfrac{1}{r}(2v + L\omega)$ and $\omega_l = \tfrac{1}{r}(2v - L\omega)$.
- [x] **Euler Odometry Update:** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta_k \Delta t$, $y_{k+1} = y_k + v\sin\theta_k \Delta t$, $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \Delta t$.
- [x] **Velocity Jacobian:** $\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$ — maps body-frame velocities to global-frame twist.
- [x] **Homogeneous Transform (2D):** $3 \times 3$ matrix $\mathbf{T}$ that combines rotation and translation in a single operator; $\mathbf{T}^{-1}$ swaps local and global frames.
- [x] **Physical Constants:** Wheel radius $r$ in **meters (m)**, wheelbase $L$ in **meters (m)** — measured once at robot calibration.
- [x] **Sign Convention:** CCW rotation is **positive**; CW is **negative** (right-hand rule).
- [x] **Standard Convention Check:** Always confirm whether the problem uses ROS REP-103 ($Y_L$ left) or ISO 8855 ($Y_L$ right) — the sign of the $Y$ component flips between them.
- [x] **Units, Units, Units:** All angles in **radians**, all distances in **meters**, all velocities in **m/s** or **rad/s**, all time intervals in **seconds**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Velocity Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Velocity Calculation** in robot navigation refers to the systematic determination of the **linear velocity** $\mathbf{v}$ and **angular velocity** $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ of a mobile robot (or the joint/link velocities of a manipulator) based on wheel encoder readings, sensor data, or desired motion specifications. It is the kinematic backbone that bridges the *actuator-level* control signals to the *task-space* motion of the robot.

Mathematically, velocity calculation solves two canonical problems:

1. **Forward Velocity Kinematics (FVK):** Given wheel angular velocities $\dot{\phi}_i$ → compute the body twist $\boldsymbol{\xi} = [v, \omega]^T$.
2. **Inverse Velocity Kinematics (IVK):** Given the desired body twist $\boldsymbol{\xi}_d = [v_d, \omega_d]^T$ → compute the required wheel velocities $\dot{\phi}_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Velocity Calculation is foundational to **wheel odometry**, **trajectory tracking**, **motion planning**, and **sensor fusion (EKF-based localization)**. Mastery of the **Jacobian formulation** is mandatory for both KTU Continuous Assessment (CA) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driver-Pedal" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a **rear-wheel drive car**:

- You press the **accelerator** → the engine sends a *rotational velocity* to the wheels.
- The car *translates* with a linear velocity $v$ and *turns* with an angular velocity $\omega$.
- **Velocity Calculation** is the mathematical "translator" that converts your pedal input (wheel rotation) into the car's *effective motion* on the road.

If the inner wheel rotates slower than the outer wheel, the car *turns*. The **difference** in wheel velocities produces $\omega$, while the **average** produces $v$. This is precisely the differential drive principle!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Quantity:** The standard SI unit of linear velocity is **m/s**, and angular velocity is **rad/s**. In wheel odometry, the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is a recurring KTU exam term.

## 1.3 Classification of Robots by Velocity Behavior

| Robot Type | Holonomic? | Degrees of Freedom | Key Velocity Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive | No (non-holonomic) | 2 (v, ω) | $v_L, v_R$ |
| Ackermann / Car-like | No (non-holonomic) | 2 (v, δ) | $v, \delta$ (steering angle) |
| Omnidirectional (Mecanum) | Yes (holonomic) | 3 ($v_x, v_y, \omega$) | 4 wheel speeds |
| Synchronous Drive | No (non-holonomic) | 2 (v, ω) | Synchronous wheel angles |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Kinematic Relationship

For *any* rigid body moving on a 2D plane, the motion is fully described by the **body twist vector**:

$$\boldsymbol{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

where $v$ is the scalar linear velocity of the robot's center and $\omega$ is the scalar angular velocity about the vertical (z) axis.

The **velocity calculation problem** reduces to finding a linear mapping:

$$\boldsymbol{\xi} = J^{-1}(\theta) \cdot \dot{\boldsymbol{\phi}}$$

where $J(\theta)$ is the **configuration-dependent Jacobian matrix** that depends on wheel geometry and arrangement, and $\dot{\boldsymbol{\phi}}$ is the vector of wheel angular velocities.

## 2.2 Differential Drive Robot — The Workhorse of KTU Problems

Consider a robot with two independently driven wheels (left and right) separated by axle length $L$ (also called **wheelbase** or **track width**), with wheel radius $r$.

### Forward Velocity Kinematics (FVK):

The two wheels do not slip. The body twist is computed as the **average** of wheel linear velocities (for $v$) and the **half-difference scaled by wheelbase** (for $\omega$).

### Inverse Velocity Kinematics (IVK):

Given a desired $(v, \omega)$, we need to compute the wheel velocities:

$$v_R = v + \frac{L}{2}\omega, \quad v_L = v - \frac{L}{2}\omega$$

This is the **allocation problem** — the *exact* opposite of FVK.

## 2.3 Ackermann (Car-like) Robot

A car has 4 wheels but only 2 controllable inputs: throttle ($v$) and steering angle ($\delta$). The instantaneous center of rotation lies on the **perpendicular to the rear axle** at distance $R = L/\tan(\delta)$ from the rear wheels.

## 2.4 Omnidirectional (Mecanum) Robot

Four Mecanum wheels permit motion in any direction without reorientation. The Jacobian is a full $3 \times 4$ matrix mapping 4 wheel speeds to $[v_x, v_y, \omega]^T$.

## 2.5 Manipulator Jacobian (Brief KTU Cross-Reference)

For a 2-DOF planar arm with link lengths $l_1, l_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} & -l_2 s_{12} \\ l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} & l_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$, $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, etc. Then:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\mid` for absolute value in tables below to avoid breaking markdown.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | Diff-drive: linear velocity of body center | m/s |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | Diff-drive: angular velocity of body | rad/s |
| 3 | $v_R = v + \dfrac{L}{2}\omega$ | Diff-drive inverse: right wheel velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $v_L = v - \dfrac{L}{2}\omega$ | Diff-drive inverse: left wheel velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}$ | Instantaneous turning radius | m |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan\delta}$ | Ackermann turning radius | m |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L}$ | Ackermann angular velocity | rad/s |
| 8 | $\dot{\phi}_i = \dfrac{v_i}{r}$ | Wheel angular velocity from linear velocity | rad/s |
| 9 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ | Manipulator end-effector velocity | m/s |
| 10 | $v_x^2 + v_y^2 \geq \mid J_{row} \cdot \dot{\boldsymbol{\phi}} \mid$ | Holonomic constraint inequality | m/s |

## 2.7 Why Velocity Calculation Matters in Real Engineering

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Self-driving cars compute $v$ and $\omega$ from GPS/IMU/encoder fusion for lane-keeping.
- **Warehouse Robots (AGVs):** Amazon's Kiva robots use differential drive FVK for precise shelf navigation.
- **Robotic Surgery (Da Vinci):** Manipulator Jacobian computes end-effector velocity for tremor filtering.
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Ackermann IVK combined with slip estimation prevents wheel sinkage.
- **Industrial Welding Arms:** Velocity calculation enables smooth path interpolation with bounded joint accelerations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Drive Forward Velocity Kinematics

**Setup:** Two wheels of radius $r$ separated by distance $L$. Wheel angular velocities: $\dot{\phi}_L$ (left) and $\dot{\phi}_R$ (right). Wheel linear velocities at contact point: $v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L$ and $v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$.

**Assumption (No-Slip Condition):** The wheel contact point is instantaneously at rest relative to the ground. Hence, the velocity of the contact point *equals* the wheel's tangential velocity.

### Step 1: Identify the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

Since both wheels are in contact with the ground, and there is no slip, the ICR must lie on the line connecting the two contact points. The ICR is on the line passing through the wheel contact patches, perpendicular to the wheel axles.

### Step 2: Express Angular Velocity via Wheel Difference

Let the ICR be at distance $R$ from the robot center. Then:

$$v_R = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right), \quad v_L = \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3: Subtract the Two Equations

Subtracting the right equation from the left equation:

$$v_R - v_L = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right) - \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

$$v_R - v_L = \omega \cdot L$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

### Step 4: Add the Two Equations to Find $v$

$$v_R + v_L = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right) + \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

$$v_R + v_L = 2\omega R = 2v$$

Hence, the linear velocity of the robot center is:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

### Step 5: Combine in Matrix Form (FVK)

$$\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} \\ -\frac{1}{L} & \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_L \\ v_R \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conclusion:** This is the canonical FVK matrix for the differential drive robot. The matrix $J_{FVK}$ is *constant* (configuration-independent) for this robot — a unique feature of diff-drive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Differential Drive Inverse Velocity Kinematics (IVK)

**Given:** Desired body twist $(v_d, \omega_d)$. **Find:** $v_L, v_R$.

### Step 1: Invert the FVK Matrix

The FVK matrix is:

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} \\ -\frac{1}{L} & \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Determinant

$$\det(M) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{L} - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{2L} + \frac{1}{2L} = \frac{1}{L}$$

Since $\det(M) = 1/L \neq 0$ (for $L \neq 0$), the inverse exists.

### Step 3: Apply the Adjoint Inversion Formula

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$, the inverse is $\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$.

Applying to $M$ with $a = 1/2$, $b = 1/2$, $c = -1/L$, $d = 1/L$:

$$M^{-1} = \frac{1}{1/L} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{L} & -\frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{1}{L} & \frac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix} = L \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{L} & -\frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{1}{L} & \frac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\frac{L}{2} \\ 1 & \frac{L}{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Multiply by Desired Twist

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_R \\ v_L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \frac{L}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{L}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_d \\ \omega_d \end{bmatrix}$$

Wait — KTU students often transpose rows here. Let me re-derive carefully:

The IVK matrix should be:

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_R \\ v_L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \frac{L}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{L}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_d \\ \omega_d \end{bmatrix}$$

Hence:

$$v_R = v_d + \frac{L}{2}\omega_d, \quad v_L = v_d - \frac{L}{2}\omega_d$$

**Conclusion:** This is the *exact* IVK result and matches our Section 2.2 derivation. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ackermann Steering Geometry

**Setup:** Car with wheelbase $L$ (front-to-rear axle), steering angle $\delta$ at the front wheels.

### Step 1: Locate the ICR

For a 4-wheel vehicle with all wheels rolling without slip, the ICR must lie on the perpendicular to **every** wheel's axle at the contact point. The intersection of the rear-axle perpendicular and front-axle perpendicular defines the ICR.

### Step 2: Geometric Relation

In the triangle formed by the rear axle, front axle, and ICR:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{L}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$

### Step 3: Angular Velocity

The body angular velocity $\omega$ relates linear velocity $v$ (at the center) and turning radius $R$:

$$v = \omega R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{v \tan\delta}{L}$$

### Step 4: Individual Wheel Velocities

For a 4-wheel car with track width $W$ and ICR at distance $R$ from the center:

$$v_{FL} = \omega \sqrt{R^2 + (L_f + R_{axle\,offset})^2 + \ldots}$$

For the standard simplified case where the ICR is at distance $R$ from the center (measured laterally as the car's instantaneous motion is rotation about ICR):

- Rear wheels: $v_{RL} = \omega (R - W/2)$, $v_{RR} = \omega (R + W/2)$
- Front wheels: $v_{FL} = \omega (R - W/2)/\cos\delta$, $v_{FR} = \omega (R + W/2)/\cos\delta$

(The front wheels travel on a wider circle, so their speed is divided by $\cos\delta$.)

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.4$ m. The left wheel rotates at $60$ rpm and the right wheel rotates at $90$ rpm. Find the linear velocity, angular velocity, and turning radius.

**Step 1: Convert Wheel Speeds to Linear Velocities**

$\dot{\phi}_L = 60$ rpm $= 60 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 2\pi$ rad/s

$\dot{\phi}_R = 90$ rpm $= 90 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 3\pi$ rad/s

$v_L = r \dot{\phi}_L = 0.05 \times 2\pi = 0.1\pi$ m/s $\approx 0.3142$ m/s

$v_R = r \dot{\phi}_R = 0.05 \times 3\pi = 0.15\pi$ m/s $\approx 0.4712$ m/s

**Step 2: Compute $v$**

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.15\pi + 0.1\pi}{2} = \frac{0.25\pi}{2} = 0.125\pi \approx 0.3927 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\omega$**

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.15\pi - 0.1\pi}{0.4} = \frac{0.05\pi}{0.4} = 0.125\pi \approx 0.3927 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute Turning Radius**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.125\pi}{0.125\pi} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

Or alternatively:

$$R = \frac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.25\pi}{2 \times 0.05\pi} = \frac{0.1\pi}{0.1\pi} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Verify**

Since $R > 0$ and $v_R > v_L$, the robot turns **left** (counter-clockwise) at radius 1.0 m. ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Robot Type | FVK Matrix | IVK Matrix | Singular? | Holonomic? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive | $\begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 \\ -1/L & 1/L \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & L/2 \\ 1 & -L/2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Never (for $L \neq 0$) | No |
| Ackermann (planar) | $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$ — depends on $\theta$ | Jacobian is $\theta$-dependent | Yes (at $\delta = 0$? Actually when $\delta = \pm 90°$) | No |
| Omnidirectional | $3 \times 4$ full-rank matrix | Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse | No (over-actuated) | Yes |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Velocity Calculation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete pipeline for computing wheel velocities from a desired trajectory, suitable for KTU examination sketches.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trajectory Planner] --> B[Desired Pose: x_d, y_d, theta_d]
    B --> C[Controller: PID or Pure Pursuit]
    C --> D[Body Twist: v_d, omega_d]
    D --> E[Inverse Velocity Kinematics Module]
    F[Wheel Geometry: r, L, W] --> E
    E --> G[Wheel Angular Velocities: phi_dot_L, phi_dot_R]
    G --> H[Motor Driver / PWM Signals]
    H --> I[DC Motors and Encoders]
    I --> J[Actual Wheel Rotation: phi_L, phi_R]
    J --> K[Forward Velocity Kinematics Module]
    K --> L[Estimated Body Twist: v_est, omega_est]
    L --> M[Odometry Update: x, y, theta]
    M --> N[Feedback to Controller]
    N --> C
```

**Reading Guide for KTU Students:**
- The top half (D → E → G → H) is the **open-loop** path: desired twist → motor command.
- The bottom half (I → J → K → L → M) is the **feedback** path: actual motion → estimated twist → odometry.
- This closed-loop structure is exactly the **sense-think-act** paradigm of KTU Module 4.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Differential Drive Velocity Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[phi_dot_L] --> B[v_L = r times phi_dot_L]
    C[phi_dot_R] --> D[v_R = r times phi_dot_R]
    B --> E[v = v_L plus v_R over 2]
    D --> E
    B --> F[omega = v_R minus v_L over L]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Body Twist Vector xi]
    F --> G
    G --> H[World Frame Integration]
    H --> I[Pose Update: x_dot, y_dot, theta_dot]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Inverse Mapping (Allocation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Twist: v_d, omega_d] --> B[v_R = v_d plus L times omega_d over 2]
    A --> C[v_L = v_d minus L times omega_d over 2]
    B --> D[phi_dot_R = v_R divided by r]
    C --> E[phi_dot_L = v_L divided by r]
    D --> F[Right Motor PWM]
    E --> G[Left Motor PWM]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic: Ackermann Steering Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CarLike[Car like Robot Top View]
        RL[Rear Left Wheel]
        RR[Rear Right Wheel]
        FL[Front Left Wheel delta]
        FR[Front Right Wheel delta]
        CG[Center of Gravity]
        ICR[Instantaneous Center of Rotation]
    end
    CG -->|distance R| ICR
    ICR -->|perpendicular to rear axle| RL
    ICR -->|perpendicular to front axle| FL
    CarLike -.->|L wheelbase| ICR
    CarLike -.->|turning radius R| ICR
```

**Note for KTU:** When drawing in your exam, label $L$ (wheelbase), $W$ (track width), $R$ (turning radius), and $\delta$ (steering angle) clearly. Draw the ICR outside the vehicle body and connect it to both axles with perpendicular lines.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between forward velocity kinematics and inverse velocity kinematics in a differential drive robot. State one application of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Forward Velocity Kinematics (FVK):** Given the *wheel angular velocities* ($\dot{\phi}_L, \dot{\phi}_R$) as input, FVK computes the *body twist* of the robot, i.e., its linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ at the center. Mathematically:

$$v = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{L}$$

**Inverse Velocity Kinematics (IVK):** Given the *desired body twist* $(v_d, \omega_d)$, IVK computes the *required wheel angular velocities*:

$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{2v_d + L\omega_d}{2r}, \quad \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{2v_d - L\omega_d}{2r}$$

**Applications:**
- FVK → **Odometry estimation** in dead-reckoning navigation (1 mark).
- IVK → **Motion control and trajectory tracking** in autonomous robots (1 mark).

*(1 mark for clear distinction with mathematical support.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define "Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)" for a mobile robot. Why is the concept critical for velocity calculation in non-holonomic robots?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) is the unique point in the plane about which a rigid body is *purely rotating* at a given instant. For a mobile robot executing a curved path, every point on the body moves in a circle centered at the ICR. For a differential drive robot, the ICR lies on the line passing through the two wheel contact patches.

**Significance (1.5 marks):** For non-holonomic robots (which cannot move sideways), the ICR is *constrained* to lie on the wheel axle line. This constraint directly determines the *maximum* and *minimum* achievable angular velocities and the *turning radius* $R = v/\omega$. Velocity calculation explicitly uses the ICR to enforce the no-slip kinematic constraints, which is essential for odometry, motion planning, and slip detection. The ICR becomes undefined only in pure translation ($\omega = 0$), giving $R \to \infty$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the forward and inverse velocity kinematic equations for a differential drive mobile robot with wheel radius $r$ and wheelbase $L$. Clearly show all steps of the derivation. (7 marks)**

**(b) A differential drive robot has $r = 0.08$ m and $L = 0.5$ m. The right and left wheel angular velocities are $\dot{\phi}_R = 20$ rad/s and $\dot{\phi}_L = 10$ rad/s respectively. Calculate: (i) the linear velocity of the robot, (ii) the angular velocity, (iii) the turning radius, and (iv) the ICR position relative to the robot center. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Setup (1 mark):** Consider a two-wheeled differential drive robot. The wheels are non-deformable, in pure rolling contact (no slip), with radius $r$ and axle separation $L$. Let $\dot{\phi}_L$ and $\dot{\phi}_R$ be the angular velocities of the left and right wheels.

**Step 1: Wheel Linear Velocities (1 mark)**

Each wheel has a tangential linear velocity at the contact point equal to $v_i = r \dot{\phi}_i$:

$$v_L = r \dot{\phi}_L, \quad v_R = r \dot{\phi}_R$$

**Step 2: Identify the ICR (1 mark)**

Since both wheels roll without slip, the ICR must lie on the line through both contact patches, i.e., on the wheel axle line. Let the ICR be at signed distance $R$ from the robot center along the axle (positive = right side).

**Step 3: Express $v_L$ and $v_R$ via $\omega$ (1 mark)**

The body rotates about the ICR with angular velocity $\omega$. The left and right wheels are at distances $R + L/2$ and $R - L/2$ from the ICR (or vice versa). Using the right wheel at distance $R + L/2$:

$$v_R = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right), \quad v_L = \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $v$ and $\omega$ (2 marks)**

Adding both:

$$v_R + v_L = 2\omega R = 2v \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

Subtracting:

$$v_R - v_L = \omega L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

**Step 5: IVK by Matrix Inversion (1 mark)**

Inverting the system gives:

$$v_R = v + \frac{L}{2}\omega, \quad v_L = v - \frac{L}{2}\omega$$

Or in wheel angular velocity form:

$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}, \quad \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$$

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $r = 0.08$ m, $L = 0.5$ m, $\dot{\phi}_R = 20$ rad/s, $\dot{\phi}_L = 10$ rad/s.

**Step 1: Wheel Linear Velocities (1 mark)**

$$v_R = r \dot{\phi}_R = 0.08 \times 20 = 1.6 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_L = r \dot{\phi}_L = 0.08 \times 10 = 0.8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Linear Velocity (1.5 marks)**

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{1.6 + 0.8}{2} = \frac{2.4}{2} = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Angular Velocity (1.5 marks)**

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{1.6 - 0.8}{0.5} = \frac{0.8}{0.5} = 1.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Turning Radius (1.5 marks)**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{1.2}{1.6} = 0.75 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: ICR Position (1.5 marks)**

The ICR lies on the axle at distance $R = 0.75$ m to the **left** of the robot center (since $v_R > v_L$, the right wheel moves faster → robot turns left → ICR is on the left side). In the robot's body frame, the ICR is at coordinates $(0, +0.75)$ m (assuming $+y$ is to the left).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write $R = L/2$ or forget the unit conversion from rpm to rad/s. Always check: (1) the sign convention for $R$ (left vs right turn), and (2) the unit of $\omega$ (rad/s vs degrees/s). Marks are deducted for *unit errors* and *sign errors* — both are common KTU valuation grounds.

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat diagram, derive the relationship between the linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\omega$, and the steering angle $\delta$ for an Ackermann-steered car-like robot. Clearly state the geometric constraints. (7 marks)**

**(b) A car-like robot has a wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m and a track width $W = 1.5$ m. The car is moving at a linear velocity of $v = 10$ m/s with a steering angle of $\delta = 15°$. Compute: (i) the turning radius $R$, (ii) the angular velocity $\omega$, (iii) the linear velocities of all four wheels (front-left, front-right, rear-left, rear-right), and (iv) comment on the slip condition if the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.6$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Ackermann Derivation (7 marks)

**Diagram (2 marks):** [Draw a top view of the car: rectangular body, two axles perpendicular to the body length. The ICR is to the left (or right) of the car, at distance $R$ from the center. The front wheels are turned at angle $\delta$ relative to the car's longitudinal axis. Draw perpendiculars from ICR to both axles.]

**Geometric Setup (1.5 marks):** Let the car have wheelbase $L$ (distance between front and rear axles). The front wheels are steered at angle $\delta$ from the longitudinal axis. The rear wheels remain aligned with the body.

**Constraint (1.5 marks):** For *all four wheels* to roll without slip, the perpendiculars to each wheel's rolling direction must intersect at a single point — the ICR. The rear axle's perpendicular is along the body's lateral axis. The front axle's perpendicular makes angle $\delta$ with the body's lateral axis (since the front wheel is rotated by $\delta$).

**Derivation (2 marks):** In the triangle formed by the rear axle, front axle, and ICR, the rear-axle perpendicular is the base (length $R$), the front-axle perpendicular is the hypotenuse crossing the wheelbase $L$:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{L}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$

Since the body's center moves on a circle of radius $R$ at linear velocity $v$:

$$v = \omega R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{v \tan\delta}{L}$$

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $L = 2.5$ m, $W = 1.5$ m, $v = 10$ m/s, $\delta = 15°$, $\mu = 0.6$.

**Step 1: Turning Radius (1.5 marks)**

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta} = \frac{2.5}{\tan 15°} = \frac{2.5}{0.2679} = 9.33 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Angular Velocity (1.5 marks)**

$$\omega = \frac{v \tan\delta}{L} = \frac{10 \times 0.2679}{2.5} = \frac{2.679}{2.5} = 1.0716 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Individual Wheel Velocities (2.5 marks)**

With $W/2 = 0.75$ m and ICR at lateral distance $R$ from the center:

- Rear-Left: $v_{RL} = \omega (R - W/2) = 1.0716 \times (9.33 - 0.75) = 1.0716 \times 8.58 = 9.19$ m/s
- Rear-Right: $v_{RR} = \omega (R + W/2) = 1.0716 \times (9.33 + 0.75) = 1.0716 \times 10.08 = 10.80$ m/s
- Front-Left: $v_{FL} = \omega (R - W/2) / \cos\delta = 9.19 / \cos 15° = 9.19 / 0.9659 = 9.51$ m/s
- Front-Right: $v_{FR} = \omega (R + W/2) / \cos\delta = 10.80 / 0.9659 = 11.18$ m/s

**Step 4: Slip Analysis (1.5 marks)**

The maximum centripetal acceleration without slipping is:

$$a_c^{max} = \mu g = 0.6 \times 9.81 = 5.886 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Actual centripetal acceleration:

$$a_c = \frac{v^2}{R} = \frac{10^2}{9.33} = 10.72 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Since $a_c = 10.72 \text{ m/s}^2 > a_c^{max} = 5.886 \text{ m/s}^2$, the wheels **will slip**. The robot cannot execute this turn without losing traction. The *minimum* safe turning radius is:

$$R_{min} = \frac{v^2}{\mu g} = \frac{100}{5.886} = 16.99 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** In Ackermann problems, students frequently confuse the ICR position with the car's center. The ICR is **always** to the side of the car (never inside or below). Also, the front wheels travel on a *larger* circle than the rear wheels, so they have *higher* linear velocity — divide by $\cos\delta$, do *not* multiply. Finally, slip analysis is often the KTU board examiner's favorite 2-mark follow-up. Be ready to compute $\mu g$ limits.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Velocity Calculation**

- ✅ **Differential Drive FVK:** $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$. Always remember to *average* for translation, *difference* for rotation.
- ✅ **Differential Drive IVK:** $v_R = v + (L/2)\omega$, $v_L = v - (L/2)\omega$. This is the *allocation* problem.
- ✅ **Wheel Angular Velocity:** $\dot{\phi} = v/r$. The wheel radius $r$ converts *linear* velocity (m/s) to *rotational* velocity (rad/s).
- ✅ **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR):** Lies on the wheel axle line for diff-drive. Distance $R = v/\omega$ from the robot center.
- ✅ **Ackermann Geometry:** $R = L/\tan\delta$ and $\omega = v\tan\delta / L$. The car has 2 DOF ($v, \delta$) and is non-holonomic.
