```mermaid
    P --> Q[ABSOLUTE SENSOR FUSION GPS BEACON SLAM]
    Q --> P
```

### Block-by-Block Interpretation (for exam-writing)

1. **IMU Sensor Unit** — Hardware cluster providing 3-axis accelerometer, gyroscope, and (optionally) magnetometer data at fixed sample rate $f_s$.
2. **Body-frame specific force $a_b$** — Raw accelerometer output, expressed in the sensor's body frame.
3. **Body-frame angular velocity $\omega_b$** — Raw gyroscope output.
4. **Gravity Compensation** — Subtracts $R_{nb}^{\top} \vec{g}_n$ from accelerometer reading so that only the kinematic (non-gravitational) acceleration remains.
5. **Integrator 1 (Velocity)** — Numerically integrates acceleration to give linear velocity.
6. **Integrator 2 (Position)** — Numerically integrates velocity to give position.
7. **Rotation Matrix Update** — Updates the orientation using $\dot{R}_{nb} = R_{nb} [\omega_b]_{\times}$.
8. **Attitude Output** — Extracts roll, pitch, yaw.
9. **Kalman Filter / Error Compensation** — Fuses IMU state with absolute sensors (GPS, LiDAR SLAM, UWB beacons) to bound the drift.
10. **Feedback arrows** — The attitude output is fed back into the gravity compensation, since the gravity vector must be rotated into the current body frame at every step.

## 4.2 Error-Propagation Schematic

The following mermaid diagram shows the **causal chain of error growth** in pure inertial dead reckoning — useful for "explain why DR drifts" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[GYRO BIAS BW] --> S2[ORIENTATION ERROR DT]
    S2 --> S3[WRONG GRAVITY PROJECTION]
    S3 --> S4[FICTITIOUS ACCELERATION]
    S4 --> S5[VELOCITY ERROR DT]
    S5 --> S6[POSITION ERROR DT SQUARED]
    A1[ACCEL BIAS BA] --> S5
    S2 --> S7[TRANSLATIONAL COUPLING]
    S7 --> S6
    N1[GYRO WHITE NOISE SIGMA W] --> S2
    N2[ACCEL WHITE NOISE SIGMA A] --> S5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram visually proves the **error-order hierarchy** stated in Section 3.3: a small gyro bias $b_\omega$ produces an *orientation* error $\propto t$, which *couples* into an *acceleration* error, producing a *velocity* error $\propto t^2$, and finally a *position* error $\propto t^3$. This is the **Achilles' heel of pure inertial DR** and is the single most-likely 14-mark question.

## 4.3 Sensor-Fusion Architecture (INS + Absolute Sensor)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph P[PROPRIOCEPTIVE]
        IMU1[IMU ACCEL GYRO]
        ENC1[WHEEL ENCODERS]
    end
    subgraph X[EXTEROCEPTIVE]
        GPS1[GPS RECEIVER]
        LID1[LiDAR SLAM]
        UW1[UWB BEACONS]
    end
    P --> DR1[DEAD RECKONING ESTIMATOR]
    DR1 --> PRED[PREDICTION STEP]
    X --> UPD[UPDATE STEP]
    PRED --> FUS[KALMAN FILTER FUSION CORE]
    UPD --> FUS
    FUS --> OUT[CORRECTED POSE ESTIMATE]
    OUT --> PRED
```

This is the **complementary filter architecture** (or EKF) that modern robots use to **mitigate drift** by re-correcting the inertial state with absolute measurements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The question patterns below mirror the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern for a 14-week elective course (credit 3): **Part A (3 marks × 2)** and **Part B (14 marks, with internal choice)**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]`
**Define Dead Reckoning. Why is it called a "relative" localization method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dead Reckoning is the process of estimating a robot's current position by integrating its measured motion (velocity, acceleration, or angular rate) over time, starting from a known initial pose. It is called *relative* localization because the output is the position **relative to a previously known reference point** — it does not yield an absolute position in a global frame, and any error in the initial pose propagates forward through the entire trajectory. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the sensors typically present in an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU). What does each measure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An IMU typically contains:
1. **3-axis Accelerometer** — measures *specific force* (non-gravitational linear acceleration) in m/s².
2. **3-axis Gyroscope** — measures *angular velocity* about the body axes in rad/s.
3. **(Optional) 3-axis Magnetometer** — measures the Earth's magnetic field vector, used as an absolute heading reference. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> For 14-mark questions, students commonly lose marks by:
> - Writing the **formula but skipping the derivation** (loses 2–3 marks).
> - **Forgetting units** in the final numerical answer.
> - **Ignoring the gravity compensation step** when working with accelerometers.
> - **Confusing body frame vs. navigation frame** in the kinematic equations.
> - **Not drawing the block diagram** when the question explicitly asks "with a neat sketch".
> - **Reproducing a memorised answer verbatim** without addressing the question's specific sub-parts.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of inertial dead reckoning with the help of a block diagram. Discuss the role of accelerometers and gyroscopes in the navigation loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the position-update and orientation-update equations for a strapdown inertial navigation system. Show how the body-frame measurements are transformed into the navigation frame. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Principle and Block Diagram (7 marks):**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** Inertial dead reckoning estimates a vehicle's pose by integrating the outputs of an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) containing accelerometers and gyroscopes. The accelerometer output is double-integrated to obtain position, while the gyroscope output is integrated to obtain orientation. The integration is performed in the **navigation (global) frame**, which requires the body-frame measurements to be rotated by the current attitude estimate $R_{nb}$.

2. **Role of Accelerometer (2 marks):** A 3-axis accelerometer measures the **specific force** $\vec{f}_b$ — i.e., the non-gravitational acceleration acting on the body. To obtain the kinematic acceleration in the navigation frame, the gravity vector rotated into the body frame must first be subtracted:
   $$\vec{a}_n^{\text{kinetic}} = R_{nb} \left( \vec{a}_b^{\text{meas}} - R_{nb}^{\top} \vec{g}_n \right)$$
   This is then integrated once to yield velocity, and a second time to yield position.

3. **Role of Gyroscope (2 marks):** A 3-axis gyroscope measures the body-frame **angular velocity** $\vec{\omega}_b$. The orientation update is governed by Poisson's equation:
   $$\dot{R}_{nb} = R_{nb}\, [\vec{\omega}_b]_{\times}$$
   where $[\vec{\omega}_b]_{\times}$ is the skew-symmetric cross-product matrix. The integration of this equation yields the time-varying rotation matrix, from which Euler angles (roll, pitch, yaw) or quaternions are extracted.

4. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes. The examiner expects: *IMU → gravity compensation → integration → pose output*, with feedback of attitude into the gravity block.

**Part (b) — Derivation of Update Equations (7 marks):**

The full derivation appears in **Section 3.1 and Section 3.2** of these notes. The expected steps for full marks are:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the velocity-time relation:
$$\vec{v}_n(t) = \vec{v}_n(0) + \int_{0}^{t} \vec{a}_n(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the position-time relation:
$$\vec{p}_n(t) = \vec{p}_n(0) + \int_{0}^{t} \vec{v}_n(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Combine the two to obtain the position-double-integral form:
$$\vec{p}_n(t) = \vec{p}_n(0) + \vec{v}_n(0)\, t + \int_{0}^{t} \int_{0}^{\tau} \vec{a}_n(u)\, du\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the rotation-update differential equation and define the skew-symmetric matrix:
$$\dot{R}_{nb} = R_{nb}\, [\vec{\omega}_b]_{\times} \quad \text{where} \quad [\vec{\omega}_b]_{\times} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega_z & \omega_y \\ \omega_z & 0 & -\omega_x \\ -\omega_y & \omega_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Integrate to get the orientation update law:
$$R_{nb}(t) = R_{nb}(0) + \int_{0}^{t} R_{nb}(\tau)\, [\vec{\omega}_b(\tau)]_{\times}\, d\tau$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Write the discrete-time form used in software:
$$\vec{v}_k = \vec{v}_{k-1} + \vec{a}_k\, T, \quad \vec{p}_k = \vec{p}_{k-1} + \vec{v}_k\, T, \quad R_k = R_{k-1}(I + [\vec{\omega}_k T]_{\times})$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Show the body-to-navigation transformation:
$$\vec{a}_n = R_{nb}\, \vec{a}_b, \quad \text{with gravity compensation} \quad \vec{a}_b^{\text{kinetic}} = \vec{a}_b^{\text{meas}} - R_{nb}^{\top} \vec{g}_n$$
Mention the constant $\vec{g}_n = [0, 0, -9.81]^{\top}$ m/s² in the NED frame.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With suitable equations, explain the error growth in pure inertial dead reckoning. Derive the position-error expression in the presence of a constant accelerometer bias. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ground robot starts at position $(0, 0)$ with heading $\psi = 0°$ and forward velocity $v = 1$ m/s. Its gyroscope reports a constant yaw rate $\omega = 0.1$ rad/s and the accelerometer reports a constant $a_x = 0.2$ m/s² in the body $x$-direction for a duration of $T = 10$ s. Compute the final position $(x, y)$ and final heading $\psi$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Error Growth Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State that pure inertial dead reckoning is **open-loop** — it has no external reference for correction. Therefore, any sensor error integrates and grows without bound.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Let the true acceleration be $\vec{a}_{\text{true}} = \vec{a}_{\text{meas}} - b_a$, where $b_a$ is the accelerometer bias (assumed constant).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Integrate once to find the velocity error:
$$\delta \vec{v}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} b_a\, d\tau = b_a\, t$$
The velocity error grows **linearly** with time.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Integrate again to find the position error:
$$\delta \vec{p}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \delta \vec{v}(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} b_a \tau\, d\tau = \frac{1}{2} b_a\, t^{2}$$
The position error grows **quadratically** with time. This is the famous **$t^2$ drift law** of inertial navigation.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Mention the additional coupling due to gyroscope bias:
$$\delta \theta(t) = b_\omega\, t \quad \text{(orientation error grows linearly)}$$
and the resulting position error becomes $\delta p \propto t^3$ after cross-coupling with gravity.

> [!TIP]
> Draw a simple **error-growth vs. time graph** showing parabolas — examiners give bonus marks for visualisation.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Initial state:
$\vec{p}(0) = (0, 0)$ m, $\psi(0) = 0°$, $\vec{v}_b(0) = [1.0, 0]^{\top}$ m/s.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Heading update:
$\Delta \psi = \omega T = 0.1 \text{ rad/s} \times 10 \text{ s} = 1.0 \text{ rad} = 57.2958°$.
Final heading $\psi = 0° + 57.30° = 57.30°$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Body-frame velocity at $t = T$:
$\vec{v}_b(T) = \vec{v}_b(0) + \vec{a}_b T = [1.0, 0]^{\top} + [0.2, 0]^{\top} \cdot 10 = [3.0, 0]^{\top}$ m/s.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Average body-frame velocity:
$\bar{\vec{v}}_b = \dfrac{\vec{v}_b(0) + \vec{v}_b(T)}{2} = \dfrac{[1.0, 0]^{\top} + [3.0, 0]^{\top}}{2} = [2.0, 0]^{\top}$ m/s.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Body-frame displacement:
$\Delta \vec{p}_b = \bar{\vec{v}}_b \cdot T = [2.0, 0]^{\top} \cdot 10 = [20.0, 0]^{\top}$ m.

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Rotate to navigation frame:
$$\Delta \vec{p}_n = R(\psi)\, \Delta \vec{p}_b = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 57.30° & -\sin 57.30° \\ \sin 57.30° & \cos 57.30° \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 20.0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
$\cos 57.30° \approx 0.5403$, $\sin 57.30° \approx 0.8415$.
$\Delta x = 20.0 \times 0.5403 = 10.806$ m.
$\Delta y = 20.0 \times 0.8415 = 16.830$ m.

**Step 7 (Final answer — 1 mark):**
$$\boxed{\vec{p}(T) \approx (10.81,\, 16.83)\text{ m}, \quad \psi(T) \approx 57.30°}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist on the last page of your answer book for the last-minute revision before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Dead Reckoning = relative localization from an initial pose, integrating motion over time.**
- [ ] **IMU components:** 3-axis accelerometer (m/s²) + 3-axis gyroscope (rad/s) ± 3-axis magnetometer.
- [ ] **Specific force** ≠ acceleration; gravity must be subtracted using the current attitude.
- [ ] **Position** is obtained by **double integration** of acceleration.
- [ ] **Orientation** is obtained by **single integration** of angular velocity (Poisson's equation).
- [ ] **Frame transformation:** $\vec{a}_n = R_{nb}\, \vec{a}_b$.
- [ ] **Standard gravity** $g = 9.81$ m/s², **NED frame** convention for $\vec{g} = [0, 0, -g]^{\top}$.
- [ ] **Error growth laws:** velocity $\propto t$, position $\propto t^2$ (accel bias), orientation $\propto t$ (gyro bias), position $\propto t^3$ (coupled).
- [ ] **Pure inertial DR is open-loop** → error drifts unboundedly → must be fused with absolute sensors (GPS, SLAM, beacons) via Kalman filter.
- [ ] **Euler-angle representation** has **gimbal-lock singularity** at pitch $\theta = \pm 90°$ — prefer quaternions in software.
- [ ] **Skew-symmetric matrix** $[\vec{\omega}]_{\times}$ is the cross-product operator: $[\vec{\omega}]_{\times} \vec{v} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}$.
- [ ] **Strapdown** = IMU rigidly fixed to vehicle body (modern). **Gimbaled** = IMU on stabilized gimbals (older, expensive).
- [ ] **Schuler period** $\approx 84.4$ minutes is the natural oscillation period of a vertical accelerometer under gravity in an unforced INS.
- [ ] **Real-world uses:** aircraft INS, drone flight controllers, submarine SINS, smartphone AR (ARCore/ARKit), automotive ESC.
- [ ] **Exam-favourite phrases to memorise:**
    - "Dead reckoning provides *high-bandwidth* but *unbounded* pose estimates."
    - "Inertial dead reckoning is **complementary** to absolute methods — never a replacement."
    - "The **double integration** of accelerometer noise is the root cause of $t^2$ drift."
- [ ] **Common board-exam trap:** never write $\vec{a}_n = R_{nb} \vec{a}_b$ **without** first performing gravity compensation — you will lose 2 marks.
- [ ] **Quaternions**: $\dot{\vec{q}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vec{q} \otimes \vec{\omega}_b$ (where $\otimes$ is the Hamilton product) — useful one-liner for 3-mark sub-questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Robot Navigation* (OEBRT723), the integration of **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)** data with an **Inertial Navigation System (INS)** using a **Modified Particle Filter (MPF)** is defined as a *nonlinear, Bayesian state-estimation framework* in which the INS mechanization provides the **time-update (prediction)** of the vehicle's navigation state, while GNSS pseudo-range / pseudo-range-rate measurements are fused into the system through a **modified importance-sampling and resampling mechanism** that explicitly compensates for the limitations of the generic Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) Particle Filter — namely *particle degeneracy*, *sample impoverishment*, and *computational cost in high-dimensional state spaces*.

Mathematically, the MPF recursively approximates the posterior probability density function (PDF) of the augmented navigation state

$$ \mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{p}_k \\ \mathbf{v}_k \\ \mathbf{\psi}_k \\ \mathbf{b}_a \\ \mathbf{b}_g \end{bmatrix} $$

where $\mathbf{p}_k \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is position, $\mathbf{v}_k \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is velocity, $\mathbf{\psi}_k \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is attitude (roll, pitch, yaw), $\mathbf{b}_a$ is the accelerometer bias, and $\mathbf{b}_g$ is the gyroscope bias, conditioned on all GNSS measurements up to time $k$, $\mathbf{Z}_{1:k} = \{\mathbf{z}_1, \mathbf{z}_2, \dots, \mathbf{z}_k\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 explicitly mandates the study of *nonlinear filtering* for GNSS/INS integration, with the **Modified Particle Filter** listed as a key advanced technique beyond the standard EKF/UKF. Students must know both the *predict* and *update* recursions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Search Party for a Lost Hiker"

Imagine a lost hiker in a dense Kerala forest (Wayanad, say). A search party has **two information sources**:

1. **The hiker's pedometer (INS analogue):** It counts every step with some drift — accurate *in the short run*, but the error grows with every passing minute.
2. **A satellite phone that occasionally receives a noisy GPS fix (GNSS analogue):** It gives an *absolute* position, but only once every few minutes, and the signal is noisy under the canopy.

Now imagine **200 search-and-rescue volunteers** spread across the forest, each holding a "guess" of where the hiker *might* be. The "modified particle filter" is the chief coordinator:

- Each volunteer's guess is **propagated forward** using the pedometer (INS prediction).
- When a noisy GPS fix arrives, the coordinator **re-weights** every volunteer based on how close their guess was to the GPS reading (importance sampling — the **modification** uses Mahalanobis distance in measurement space, not raw likelihood, to be more robust).
- Volunteers whose guesses are wildly off are **discarded**, and new volunteers are *cloned* from those whose guesses were close (resampling — but *regularized* to prevent all clones from being identical).

This is precisely what a Modified Particle Filter does for GNSS/INS integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A *standard* Particle Filter (PF) can become degenerate — almost all particles collapse onto a single point, wasting compute. The **Modified Particle Filter** introduces fixes (e.g., *regularized resampling*, *Rao-Blackwellization*, *auxiliary particle filter* structure) to keep the particle cloud healthy. In KTU parlance, this robustness is the *modification*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |

| WGS-84 Earth semi-major axis | $a$ | **6 378 137.0 m** |
| WGS-84 Earth flattening | $f$ | **1 / 298.257 223 563** |
| WGS-84 Earth eccentricity² | $e^2$ | **0.006 694 379 990 14** |
| Earth's rotation rate | $\Omega_e$ | **7.292 115 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s** |
| Gravitational acceleration (mean) | $g$ | **9.806 65 m/s²** |
| Speed of light (GNSS signal) | $c$ | **299 792 458 m/s** |
| Number of particles (typical MPF) | $N$ | **100 – 1000** |
| Effective sample size threshold | $N_{thr}$ | **0.5 N to 0.6 N** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Modified Particle Filter

The MPF for GNSS/INS is built on **three functional blocks** that the student must master:

### Block A — INS-Based Prediction (Time Update)
1. Read inertial sensor outputs: specific force $\mathbf{f}_{ib}^b$ and angular rate $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ib}^b$ from the IMU body frame.
2. Mechanize the inertial navigation equations in the **local navigation frame (n-frame: North-East-Down or East-North-Up)**:
   - $\dot{\mathbf{p}}^n = \mathbf{v}^n$
   - $\dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}_{ib}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\Omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\Omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n$
   - $\dot{\mathbf{C}}_b^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_{ib}^b \times \right) - \left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_{in}^n \times \right) \mathbf{C}_b^n$
3. Propagate **each particle** $i = 1, \dots, N$ through the same nonlinear mechanization, but with **perturbed process noise** $\mathbf{w}_k^i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q}_k)$ sampled freshly for every particle.
4. Why? This gives the particle cloud a *spread* that is consistent with the *true* INS error growth model (Schuler loop, coning, sculling).

### Block B — GNSS-Based Importance Weighting (Measurement Update)
1. When a GNSS pseudo-range $\rho_j$ to satellite $j$ arrives at time $k$, form the **measurement innovation** for particle $i$:
   - $\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j} = \rho_j^{GNSS} - \left( \Vert \mathbf{p}_{sv,j} - \hat{\mathbf{p}}_k^i \Vert + c \cdot \delta t_u^i \right)$
2. The **modification** enters here. The importance weight is computed using the **Mahalanobis distance** in measurement space:
   - $d_{M,k}^{i,j} = \sqrt{(\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j})^T (\mathbf{R}_k)^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j}}$
   - $w_k^i \propto w_{k-1}^i \cdot \exp\left( -\tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{M} (d_{M,k}^{i,j})^2 \right)$
3. Why the *modification*? Standard PFs use a coarse likelihood; the **MPF** uses the **full measurement-noise covariance** $\mathbf{R}_k$ (typically derived from satellite elevation, GDOP, and carrier-to-noise ratio $C/N_0$).

### Block C — Regularized Resampling
1. Compute the **Effective Sample Size**:
   - $\hat{N}_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_k^i)^2}$
2. If $\hat{N}_{eff} < N_{thr}$ (say, $0.5 N$), trigger **regularized resampling**:
   - Draw new particle positions from a *kernel-smoothed* (Epanechnikov / Gaussian) approximation of the posterior, not from the discrete empirical distribution.
   - This prevents *sample impoverishment* — a failure mode of vanilla PFs in GNSS/INS where particles collapse onto the GNSS fix.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Used For |
|---|----------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_k) + \mathbf{w}_k$ | Nonlinear state transition (INS mechanization) | Prediction step |
| 2 | $\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{v}_k$ | GNSS measurement model | Update step |
| 3 | $\mathbf{x}_k^i \sim p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i, \mathbf{u}_k)$ | Particle propagation from INS process model | INS prediction |
| 4 | $w_k^i \propto w_{k-1}^i \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^i)$ | Importance weight update (Bayes' rule) | Measurement update |
| 5 | $p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^M \det \mathbf{R}_k}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i\,T} \mathbf{R}_k^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu}_k^i\right)$ | Gaussian likelihood (GNSS) | Likelihood evaluation |
| 6 | $\hat{N}_{eff} = 1 \big/ \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\tilde{w}_k^i)^2$ | Effective sample size (ESS) | Resampling trigger |
| 7 | $K_h(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{h^d} K\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x}}{h}\right)$, $K(t)=\tfrac{3}{4}(1-t^2)\mathbf{1}_{\vert t \vert \le 1}$ | Epanechnikov kernel (regularized resampling) | Sample enrichment |
| 8 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_k^i \, \mathbf{x}_k^i$ | Posterior mean estimate | Navigation output |
| 9 | $\mathbf{P}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_k^i (\mathbf{x}_k^i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k)(\mathbf{x}_k^i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k)^T$ | Posterior covariance | Uncertainty output |
| 10 | $\dot{\boldsymbol{\psi}} = \mathbf{C}_b^n (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ib}^b - \mathbf{C}_n^b \boldsymbol{\omega}_{in}^n)$ | Attitude kinematics | INS mechanization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This MPF is the workhorse of *urban canyon GNSS/INS fusion* for autonomous vehicles in Indian cities, *UAV swarms* for Kerala's terrain mapping, and *precision agriculture* tractors in Palakkad. The robustness to GNSS multipath and outages (bridges, tunnels) is what makes the "modification" industrially critical.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Importance-Weight Update in the MPF

We begin from the **Bayesian filtering recursion** for the posterior PDF of the navigation state $\mathbf{x}_k$ given all GNSS measurements up to $k$.

### Step 1 — Chapman–Kolmogorov Prediction

The *a priori* (predicted) density at time $k$, using only the INS mechanization, is

$$
p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k-1}) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_k)\, p(\mathbf{x}_{k-1} \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k-1})\, d\mathbf{x}_{k-1}
$$

where $\mathbf{u}_k = [\mathbf{f}_{ib}^b, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{ib}^b]^T$ is the IMU input vector, and the transition density is the INS error-propagation kernel.

### Step 2 — Bayes' Measurement Update

When the GNSS observation $\mathbf{z}_k$ arrives, the *a posteriori* density is

$$
p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k)\, p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k-1})}{p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k-1})}
$$

with the normalizing constant

$$
p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k-1}) = \int p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k)\, p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k-1})\, d\mathbf{x}_k
$$

### Step 3 — Monte-Carlo Approximation of the Prior

Because the integrals above are **intractable** in the nonlinear GNSS/INS system, we approximate the prior by an *empirical particle cloud*:

$$
p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k-1}) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta\!\left( \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^i \right)
$$

where $\mathbf{x}_k^i \sim p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i, \mathbf{u}_k)$ is the propagated particle.

### Step 4 — Importance Sampling (the "Modification" Kernel)

We *cannot* sample directly from the true posterior $p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k})$. Instead, we draw from a **proposal density** $q(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i, \mathbf{z}_k)$ and correct via importance weights:

$$
w_k^i \propto w_{k-1}^i \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^i)\, p(\mathbf{x}_k^i \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i)}{q(\mathbf{x}_k^i \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i, \mathbf{z}_k)}
$$

In the **MPF**, the proposal is the INS transition prior:

$$
q(\mathbf{x}_k^i \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i, \mathbf{z}_k) = p(\mathbf{x}_k^i \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i, \mathbf{u}_k)
$$

Therefore, the *modified* weight update reduces to the **likelihood-only** form:

$$
\boxed{\, w_k^i \propto w_{k-1}^i \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^i) \,}
$$

### Step 5 — Gaussian Likelihood for GNSS Pseudo-Range

For each particle $i$, evaluate the innovation against satellite $j$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j} = \rho_j^{GNSS} - \left( \sqrt{(x_{sv,j}-x_k^i)^2 + (y_{sv,j}-y_k^i)^2 + (z_{sv,j}-z_k^i)^2} + c\,\delta t_u^i \right)
$$

The likelihood (with $M$ visible satellites) is

$$
p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^M \det \mathbf{R}_k}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{M} (\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j})\, \mathbf{R}_k^{-1}\, (\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j})^T \right)
$$

Taking the natural log (for numerical stability):

$$
\ln w_k^i = \ln w_{k-1}^i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{M} (\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j})^T \mathbf{R}_k^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j} + \text{const}
$$

### Step 6 — Normalization and Effective Sample Size

Normalize so that the weights sum to unity:

$$
\tilde{w}_k^i = \frac{w_k^i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_k^i}
$$

The **effective sample size** is

$$
\hat{N}_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\tilde{w}_k^i)^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Suppose $N=200$ particles, $M=6$ GNSS satellites, $\mathbf{R}_k = \text{diag}(4, 4, 4, 4, 4, 4)\ \text{m}^2$ (i.e., $\sigma_\rho = 2$ m per satellite). For one particle, the sum of squared Mahalanobis innovations is $S^i = 12.4$. Then its log-weight increment is $-\tfrac{1}{2}(12.4) = -6.2$. The ratio of weights between this particle and one with $S^i = 8.0$ is $\exp(-6.2 + 4.0) = e^{-2.2} \approx 0.111$. The high-innovation particle is therefore *down-weighted* by a factor of 9 — the **MPF automatically rejects particles inconsistent with GNSS**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Regularized Resampling Step

If $\hat{N}_{eff} < N_{thr}$, the standard resampler draws indices from the multinomial distribution of weights. The **modification** is to jitter each resampled particle with Epanechnikov-kernel noise.

### Step 7 — Kernel Density Approximation of the Posterior

The discrete empirical posterior

$$
\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_k^i \, \delta(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^i)
$$

is replaced by the *continuous* kernel density estimate

$$
\tilde{p}(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_k^i \, K_h(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^i)
$$

with bandwidth $h = \left( \tfrac{4}{N(d+2)} \right)^{1/(d+4)} \Sigma_k^{1/2}$ where $d$ is the state dimension and $\Sigma_k$ is the sample covariance.

### Step 8 — Resampling + Jittering

For each new index $i^*$ chosen by multinomial sampling:

$$
\mathbf{x}_k^{i^*, \text{new}} = \mathbf{x}_k^{i^*} + h \cdot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}^i, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon}^i \sim K(\cdot)
$$

This is the *modification* — it adds diversity back into the particle cloud, curing sample impoverishment.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Modified Particle Filter for GNSS/INS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IMU Sensors: Accel + Gyro] --> B[INS Mechanization Engine]
    B --> C[Particle Cloud Propagation: x_k^i for i=1..N]
    C --> D{Is GNSS Measurement z_k Available?}
    D -- No --> J[Output: Weighted Mean x_hat_k and Covariance P_k]
    D -- Yes --> E[Innovation Computation: nu_k^i = z_k - h x_k^i]
    E --> F[Mahalanobis Distance: d_M = sqrt nu^T R^-1 nu]
    F --> G[Weight Update: w_k^i propto w_km1^i * exp -1/2 d_M^2]
    G --> H[Normalize: tilde_w_k^i = w_k^i / sum w_k^i]
    H --> I{ESS N_eff less than N_thr?}
    I -- No --> J
    I -- Yes --> K[Regularized Resampling: x_k^i_new = x_k^i + h * epsilon]
    K --> L[Reset Uniform Weights: w_k^i = 1/N]
    L --> J
    J --> M[Navigation Solution: Position, Velocity, Attitude]
    M --> N[Feedback to INS for Bias Correction]
```

## 4.2 Particle-Cloud Evolution Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[Initial Cloud: N particles around prior guess] -->|INS propagation| P1[Predicted Cloud: spread along INS drift direction]
    P1 -->|GNSS fix arrival| P2[Weighted Cloud: weights computed from likelihood]
    P2 -->|Regularized resample| P3[Posterior Cloud: concentrated near true state, with diversity preserved]
    P3 -->|Next INS step| P1
```

## 4.3 Functional Block: INS Mechanization inside the MPF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[F_b: Specific Force, body frame] --> M1[Transform to nav frame: C_b^n F_b]
    M1 --> M2[Subtract gravity and Coriolis]
    M2 --> M3[Integrate to get v^n]
    M3 --> M4[Integrate to get p^n]
    B1[omega_ib^b: Angular rate, body] --> M5[Subtract earth and transport rates]
    M5 --> M6[Update DCM C_b^n via quaternion or Euler]
    M6 --> M7[Extract attitude psi]
    M7 --> A1
    M3 --> A1
    M4 --> M3
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the Modified Particle Filter as applied to GNSS/INS integration. List two key modifications it introduces over the standard Particle Filter.**

**Model Answer:**
The *Modified Particle Filter (MPF)* is a nonlinear Bayesian estimator that fuses Inertial Navigation System (INS) mechanization with Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) pseudo-range measurements by representing the posterior state distribution as a weighted set of *particles* (samples) and recursively updating them through prediction, importance-weighted update, and resampling.

**Two key modifications over the standard PF:**

1. **Regularized resampling:** Replaces discrete multinomial resampling with a *kernel-smoothed* (e.g., Epanechnikov) resampling step. This injects controlled jitter into the resampled particles, preventing *sample impoverishment* — a critical failure mode during sustained GNSS availability.
2. **Mahalanobis-distance-based importance weighting:** Uses the *full* GNSS measurement-noise covariance $\mathbf{R}_k$ (often made elevation- and $C/N_0$-dependent) inside an exponential likelihood, instead of a coarse scalar weight. This yields better discrimination between particles in dense urban multipath environments.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for the two modifications.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the *effective sample size* $N_{eff}$ in a particle filter, and why is it important for GNSS/INS integration?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Effective Sample Size (ESS)** is a scalar measure of particle-cloud degeneracy, defined as

$$
\hat{N}_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\tilde{w}_k^i)^2}
$$

where $\tilde{w}_k^i$ is the normalized importance weight of the $i$-th particle. $N_{eff}$ ranges from $1$ (one particle carries all the weight — total degeneracy) to $N$ (uniform weights — maximum diversity).

**Importance in GNSS/INS integration:**
- If $N_{eff}$ falls below a threshold (typically $0.5N$ or $0.6N$), *resampling is triggered*. Without this, the cloud collapses onto a few high-weight particles, and the INS-only dead-reckoning that follows will quickly diverge.
- In *GNSS-denied* segments (tunnels, urban canyons), maintaining a diverse cloud is essential to bridge the outage; the ESS feedback loop guarantees this diversity is monitored and restored.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition+formula, 1.5 for importance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Part B carries 14 marks per question. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must attempt **one full question** out of the choice.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Modified Particle Filter for GNSS/INS integration. Derive the importance-weight update equation and explain how it differs from the EKF measurement update.**

#### Part (a) — Architecture and Block Diagram (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The MPF has three principal blocks:

1. **INS-Based Prediction Block:** Each particle $i$ is propagated through the INS error model:
   $$\mathbf{x}_k^i = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i, \mathbf{u}_k) + \mathbf{w}_k^i, \quad \mathbf{w}_k^i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q}_k)$$
   The state vector typically includes position, velocity, attitude, and IMU biases.

2. **GNSS-Based Measurement-Update Block:** For each particle, compute innovations against all visible satellites and form the Gaussian likelihood:
   $$\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j} = \rho_j^{GNSS} - \Vert \mathbf{p}_{sv,j} - \hat{\mathbf{p}}_k^i \Vert - c\,\delta t_u^i$$
   $$w_k^i \propto w_{k-1}^i \cdot \exp\!\left( -\tfrac{1}{2} \sum_j (\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j})^T \mathbf{R}_k^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i,j} \right)$$

3. **Resampling Block:** Compute $N_{eff}$. If below threshold, perform *regularized* resampling with Epanechnikov-kernel jittering to maintain cloud diversity.

The block diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. (Mermaid flow from IMU → INS mechanization → particle propagation → GNSS innovation → weight update → resampling → navigation output.)

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for block descriptions, 2 for equations.)*

#### Part (b) — Derivation and Comparison with EKF (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the Bayesian recursion (Step 1–Step 3 in Section 3.1), we obtain the importance-weight update as:

$$
w_k^i \propto w_{k-1}^i \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^i)
$$

with the Gaussian likelihood:

$$
p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^M \det \mathbf{R}_k}} \exp\!\left( -\tfrac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{i\,T} \mathbf{R}_k^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu}_k^i \right)
$$

**Comparison with the EKF measurement update:**

| Aspect | EKF | Modified Particle Filter |
|--------|-----|--------------------------|
| Posterior representation | Gaussian (mean + covariance) | Empirical (weighted particle cloud) |
| Linearization | First-order Taylor series of $\mathbf{f}$ and $\mathbf{h}$ | None — fully nonlinear |
| Multi-modal handling | Fails (collapses to one mode) | Naturally handles multi-modal posteriors (e.g., urban multipath) |
| Computational cost | Low ($O(d^3)$ per step) | High ($N$ particles per step) |
| Robustness to GNSS outliers | Sensitive to outliers in $\mathbf{R}_k$ | Outliers naturally down-weighted via likelihood |
| Tuning burden | Jacobian derivation for every model | Only proposal + likelihood + kernel bandwidth |

The **modification** in the MPF is that the weights are computed in the *full* nonlinear measurement space without any Jacobian — making it robust to the highly nonlinear GNSS geometry in obstructed environments.

*(7 marks — 3 for derivation, 4 for comparison table.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the phenomenon of *particle degeneracy* in a standard particle filter used for GNSS/INS integration. Describe with mathematical justification how the Modified Particle Filter overcomes it through *regularized resampling* and *effective sample size monitoring*.**

#### Part (a) — Degeneracy Phenomenon and Mathematical Basis (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Particle degeneracy* is the condition in which, after a few GNSS measurement updates, almost all particles in the cloud carry negligible weight, while a single (or a few) particle(s) carry weight $\approx 1$. The variance of the importance weights grows monotonically with $k$, as proven by the following lemma:

**Geweke's Diagnostic / Kong-Liu Theorem:**

$$
\text{Var}(w_k^i) = \text{Var}_{q(\cdot \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k-1})}[p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^i)] \cdot \text{Var}(w_{k-1}^i) + [\text{E}(w_{k-1}^i)]^2
$$

Since the variance of weights *cannot decrease* without resampling, the cloud inevitably degenerates.

**Why it is critical for GNSS/INS:**

- After degeneracy, when GNSS becomes unavailable (tunnel), the *single high-weight particle* drives the INS dead-reckoning — any bias in that particle propagates without correction.
- The cloud loses its ability to represent *uncertainty*, making the navigation filter overconfident.

The quantitative measure is the **Effective Sample Size**:

$$
\hat{N}_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\tilde{w}_k^i)^2}
$$

When $\hat{N}_{eff} \to 1$, degeneracy is total.

*(7 marks — 2 for definition, 3 for mathematical justification, 2 for GNSS/INS relevance.)*

#### Part (b) — Regularized Resampling and ESS Monitoring (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Triggering criterion.** Compute $N_{eff}$ at every epoch. If $N_{eff} < N_{thr}$ (e.g., $0.5N$), trigger resampling.

**Step 2 — Standard (Multinomial) Resampling.** Draw $N$ indices $i^* \in \{1,\dots,N\}$ from the categorical distribution defined by $\{\tilde{w}_k^i\}$. This produces a *new* cloud of $N$ particles, all with equal weight $1/N$.

**Step 3 — The MPF Modification — Regularization.** Replace the discrete empirical density $\hat{p}$ with a *kernel-smoothed* density:

$$
\tilde{p}(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{Z}_{1:k}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_k^i \, K_h(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^i)
$$

with optimal bandwidth (Silverman's rule for the multivariate case):

$$
h_i = \left( \frac{4}{N(d+2)} \right)^{1/(d+4)} \, \sigma_i
$$

where $\sigma_i$ is the standard deviation along the $i$-th principal component. For each resampled particle $\mathbf{x}_k^{i^*}$, add jitter:

$$
\mathbf{x}_k^{i^*,\text{new}} = \mathbf{x}_k^{i^*} + h \cdot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \text{Epanechnikov}
$$

**Why this cures impoverishment:** Standard resampling produces *exact clones* of high-weight particles — the cloud loses diversity because all clones follow the same INS trajectory. The Epanechnikov jitter adds controlled, statistically valid diversity, ensuring that the cloud continues to explore the state space during subsequent INS-only prediction.

**Mathematical justification:** The Mean Integrated Squared Error (MISE) of the kernel-density estimate is minimized by the Epanechnikov kernel, guaranteeing the *most efficient* use of the resampled particles.

**Algorithm summary (one-line pseudo-code for the KTU exam):**
1. Propagate particles via INS.
2. Compute GNSS innovations and Mahalanobis distances.
3. Update and normalize weights.
4. Compute $N_{eff}$; if below threshold, regularized resample with Epanechnikov jitter.
5. Output $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k = \sum_i \tilde{w}_k^i \mathbf{x}_k^i$ and $\mathbf{P}_k$.

*(7 marks — 2 for ESS criterion, 3 for regularization math, 2 for justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PF with EKF:** Many students write the *Jacobians* of the measurement matrix in their MPF derivation. There are **no Jacobians** in a particle filter — this is an automatic 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the Mahalanobis structure:** Writing $w_k^i \propto \exp(-(z-h(x))^2)$ *without* the $\mathbf{R}_k^{-1}$ weighting loses 1–2 marks. Always show the full covariance form.
> 3. **Skipping the "Modified" part:** If the question explicitly says *Modified* Particle Filter, you MUST discuss (a) the modification (regularization / Rao-Blackwellization / auxiliary PF) and (b) the *reason* for it (degeneracy / sample impoverishment). A generic PF answer gets at most 60% of the marks.
> 4. **No numerical units:** Always state the *units* of $N_{eff}$ (dimensionless), $\mathbf{R}_k$ (m² for pseudo-ranges), and $h$ (m for position, rad for attitude).
> 5. **No diagram in 14-mark questions:** A labelled block diagram is worth 3 marks and is *non-negotiable* in Part B of KTU ESE.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **MPF = Bayesian non-linear filter** representing the posterior by a cloud of $N$ weighted particles.
- ✅ **Three blocks:** INS *prediction*, GNSS *importance-weight update*, *regularized resampling*.
- ✅ **State vector:** position, velocity, attitude, accelerometer bias, gyro bias.
- ✅ **Importance weight:** $w_k^i \propto w_{k-1}^i \cdot \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^i; \mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_k)$.
- ✅ **ESS formula:** $\hat{N}_{eff} = 1 / \sum (\tilde{w}_k^i)^2$. Threshold ≈ $0.5N$ to $0.6N$.
- ✅ **Modification 1:** *Regularized resampling* using Epanechnikov-kernel jitter — cures sample impoverishment.
- ✅ **Modification 2:** *Full-covariance Mahalanobis likelihood* — robust to GNSS multipath and elevation-dependent noise.
- ✅ **INS propagation:** Each particle carries its own $\mathbf{Q}_k$-perturbed state — gives the cloud a Schuler-loop-consistent spread.
- ✅ **No Jacobians** anywhere in the MPF (contrast with EKF/UKF).
- ✅ **Posterior mean & covariance** are the navigation outputs: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k$ and $\mathbf{P}_k$.
- ✅ **Cost:** $N \times$ the cost of a single mechanization run. Typical $N = 100$–$1000$.
- ✅ **KTU keywords to use in answers:** "non-Gaussian posterior", "importance sampling", "particle degeneracy", "sample impoverishment", "kernel density estimation", "Mahalanobis distance", "regularized resampling".
- ✅ **GNSS measurement model:** $\rho_j = \Vert \mathbf{p}_{sv,j} - \mathbf{p} \Vert + c \cdot \delta t_u + I_j + T_j + \varepsilon_j$ (with ionospheric, tropospheric, and noise terms).
- ✅ **WGS-84 constants** to memorize: $a = 6378137$ m, $f = 1/298.257223563$, $\Omega_e = 7.292115 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS** is a **sensor-fusion** procedure in which the long-term *absolute* velocity information from a **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)** receiver is statistically blended with the *short-term high-bandwidth* velocity derived from an **Inertial Navigation System (INS)** using a **Kalman Filter (KF)** or its variants (EKF/UKF), in order to obtain a navigation solution whose velocity estimate is simultaneously **stable**, **accurate**, and **high-rate** — overcoming the complementary weaknesses of each standalone sensor.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the procedure is formally called a **GNSS/INS Integrated Navigation System** and is implemented through one of three classical architectural paradigms:

| Architecture | Coupling Level | What GNSS Provides to the Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Loosely-Coupled** | Filter level | Position & velocity (PVT) from receiver's internal solution |
| **Tightly-Coupled** | Measurement level | Raw pseudoranges and Doppler (pseudorange-rates) |
| **Ultra-Tight / Deep Integration** | Signal tracking level | I/Q correlator outputs into the INS feedback loop |

The *velocity estimation* problem is the cornerstone of Module 4 because velocity is the **first integral of acceleration** (which the IMU measures natively) and the **first derivative of position** (which GNSS measures natively), so velocity is the natural state where both sensors overlap mathematically.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two Walkers

Imagine two friends walking together across a foggy field to reach a tower whose location is fixed:

- **Walker A — "GNSS"** — has a *satellite map* of the tower. Every **1 second** he tells the group, "The tower is exactly 100 m North-East." His answer is *globally accurate* but slow, and he *trembles* slightly (≈0.5 m noise) each time he speaks. If a tree blocks the sky, he *goes silent* (signal outage).
- **Walker B — "INS"** — has a *pedometer strapped to his leg* and a *compass*. He can estimate his walking speed **1000 times per second** (very smooth, very fast), but his pedometer drifts — after 10 minutes his estimate of how far the tower is could be off by 50 m. Without a reference he eventually becomes lost.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Kalman Filter acts as the "team coordinator"**. It trusts Walker's B fast-but-drifting steps in the short run, but **automatically re-anchors** to Walker's A absolute map-reading whenever A is available. The *blended* velocity estimate is smooth, drift-free, and high-rate.

In engineering terms, INS is **complementary** to GNSS in both the *time domain* and *frequency domain*:
- INS → high frequency (≥ 100 Hz), low long-term accuracy.
- GNSS → low frequency (1–10 Hz), high long-term accuracy.
- KF → produces an estimate valid in **both** domains.

---

## 1.3 Why Velocity is the Critical State

Velocity is the *pivot* of the entire navigation state vector. Mathematically, the *navigation triad* obeys:

$$\vec{p}(t) \xrightarrow{\ \dot{\ }\ } \vec{v}(t) \xrightarrow{\ \dot{\ }\ } \vec{a}(t)$$

The IMU measures $\vec{a}$ (specific force) and $\vec{\omega}$. GNSS measures $\vec{p}$ and can measure $\vec{v}$ via the **Doppler shift**. So velocity is the *only* state that **both** sensors can directly observe — making it the most natural variable for fusion.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently test the student's ability to *identify which sensor contributes which component of the state vector* (position → GNSS, attitude → INS, velocity → BOTH). Memorize: *velocity is the shared observable*.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Earth's rotation rate | $\omega_{ie}$ | **7.292115 × 10⁻⁵** | rad/s |
| Gravity at sea level | $g$ | **9.80665** | m/s² |
| WGS-84 semi-major axis | $a_e$ | **6 378 137.0** | m |
| WGS-84 flattening | $f$ | **1 / 298.257223563** | — |
| Speed of light | $c$ | **2.998 × 10⁸** | m/s |
| INS update rate | $f_{INS}$ | 100 – 1000 | Hz |
| GNSS update rate | $f_{GNSS}$ | 1 – 20 | Hz |
| L1 carrier frequency | $f_{L1}$ | **1.57542 × 10⁹** | Hz |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Sensor Models

### 2.1.1 GNSS Velocity Model (Measurement Equation)

A GNSS receiver determines velocity from the **Doppler shift** of the carrier frequency. The pseudorange-rate $\dot{\rho}^{(s)}$ for satellite $s$ is:

$$\dot{\rho}^{(s)} = (\vec{u}^{(s)})^T (\vec{v}_{r} - \vec{v}^{(s)}) + \dot{b}_{clk,r} + \epsilon_{\dot{\rho}}$$

where:

- $\vec{u}^{(s)}$ = **line-of-sight unit vector** from receiver to satellite $s$.
- $\vec{v}_r$ = receiver velocity in ECEF.
- $\vec{v}^{(s)}$ = satellite velocity computed from broadcast ephemeris.
- $\dot{b}_{clk,r}$ = **receiver clock-drift** (in metres/second).
- $\epsilon_{\dot{\rho}}$ = measurement noise (≈ 0.05 m/s for civil code, ≈ 0.005 m/s for carrier-phase).

Stacking measurements from $n$ satellites (typically $n \geq 4$):

$$\vec{z}_{GNSS} = H_{GNSS}\,\vec{v}_r + \vec{\eta}, \quad \vec{\eta} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_{GNSS})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Combine"?** The filter treats $\vec{z}_{GNSS}$ as the **external measurement** that periodically *corrects* the INS-propagated velocity. Between GNSS updates, the filter relies purely on INS integration — which is why INS must be mechanised correctly first.

### 2.1.2 INS Velocity Mechanisation (Process Equation)

The specific-force equation in the **local navigation frame (n-frame: North-East-Down)** is:

$$\vec{v}^n = \vec{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b + \vec{g}^n - (\vec{\omega}_{en}^n + 2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n) \times \vec{v}^n$$

where:
- $\vec{C}_b^n$ = direction-cosine matrix from body to navigation frame.
- $\vec{f}^b$ = specific force measured by the **accelerometer triad**.
- $\vec{g}^n$ = gravity vector (≈ **9.81 m/s²** pointing down).
- $\vec{\omega}_{en}^n$ = **transport rate** = $\vec{v}^n / (R_e + h)$.
- $2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n$ = **Coriolis term** (twice Earth's rotation).

This is integrated once per IMU epoch (e.g., 10 ms) to give $\vec{v}^n$, then integrated again to give position $\vec{p}^n$.

---

## 2.2 INS Error Dynamics (Linearised)

The KF uses **error-state** models. Define errors $\delta\vec{p}, \delta\vec{v}, \vec{\phi}$ (position, velocity, attitude-tilt). Linearising the mechanisation about a nominal trajectory yields the **Titterton–Weston PSI (Phi) model**:

$$\delta\dot{\vec{p}} = \delta\vec{v}$$

$$\delta\dot{\vec{v}} = -\vec{\phi} \times \vec{f}^n + \vec{C}_b^n \delta\vec{f}^b - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \delta\vec{v} + \delta\vec{g}$$

$$\dot{\vec{\phi}} = -\vec{\omega}_{in}^n \times \vec{\phi} + \vec{C}_b^n \delta\vec{\omega}_{ib}^b$$

with sensor-error models:

$$\delta\dot{\vec{f}}^b = 0 \quad (\text{constant accelerometer bias } \vec{b}_a)$$

$$\delta\dot{\vec{\omega}}_{ib}^b = 0 \quad (\text{constant gyro bias } \vec{b}_g)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The state vector is therefore typically:
> $$\vec{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta\vec{p} & \delta\vec{v} & \vec{\phi} & \vec{b}_a & \vec{b}_g \end{bmatrix}^T \quad \text{(15 or 17 states)}$$

---

## 2.3 Kalman Filter Cycle

The discrete KF runs at the *GNSS rate* (1 Hz typical) using the *INS rate* (100 Hz) for prediction:

| Step | Equation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Predict (state)** | $\hat{\vec{x}}_{k\|k-1} = F_k \hat{\vec{x}}_{k-1}$ | INS-driven propagation |
| **Predict (covariance)** | $P_{k\|k-1} = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$ | Add process noise $Q_k$ |
| **Gain** | $K_k = P_{k\|k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k\|k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$ | Weight GNSS vs INS |
| **Update (state)** | $\hat{\vec{x}}_k = \hat{\vec{x}}_{k\|k-1} + K_k (\vec{z}_k - H_k \hat{\vec{x}}_{k\|k-1})$ | Correct with GNSS |
| **Update (covariance)** | $P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\|k-1}$ | Reduce uncertainty |

The *measurement innovation* $\vec{\nu}_k = \vec{z}_k - H_k \hat{\vec{x}}_{k\|k-1}$ is the **velocity discrepancy** between what INS predicted and what GNSS observed.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $\vec{v}^n = \vec{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)\times\vec{v}^n + \vec{g}^n$ | INS velocity mechanisation | m/s² |
| F2 | $\vec{p}^n(t+\Delta t) = \vec{p}^n(t) + \int \vec{v}^n dt$ | Position from velocity | m |
| F3 | $\dot{\rho}^{(s)} = \vec{u}^{(s)T}(\vec{v}_r - \vec{v}^{(s)}) + \dot{b}_{clk} + \epsilon$ | Pseudorange-rate (Doppler) | m/s |
| F4 | $H = \begin{bmatrix} 0_{3\times3} & I_3 & 0_{3\times9} \end{bmatrix}$ | Velocity measurement matrix | — |
| F5 | $\delta\dot{\vec{v}} = -\vec{\phi}\times\vec{f}^n - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n+\vec{\omega}_{en}^n)\times\delta\vec{v} + \vec{C}_b^n\vec{b}_a$ | Velocity error dynamics | m/s² |
| F6 | $\dot{\vec{\phi}} = -\vec{\omega}_{in}^n\times\vec{\phi} + \vec{C}_b^n\vec{b}_g$ | Attitude error dynamics | rad/s |
| F7 | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | — |
| F8 | $\hat{\vec{x}}_k = \hat{\vec{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (\vec{z}_{GNSS} - H_k \hat{\vec{x}}_{k\mid k-1})$ | State update | — |
| F9 | $P_{k\mid k-1} = \Phi_k P_{k-1} \Phi_k^T + Q_k$ | Covariance prediction | — |
| F10 | $\vec{\omega}_{en}^n = \left[ \dfrac{v_E}{R_e + h},\ -\dfrac{v_N}{R_e + h},\ -\dfrac{v_E \tan\phi}{R_e + h} \right]^T$ | Transport rate | rad/s |
| F11 | $\vec{f}^b = \vec{a}^b - \vec{g}^b$ | Accelerometer output (specific force) | m/s² |
| F12 | $\vec{\nu}_k = \vec{z}_{GNSS} - H_k \hat{\vec{x}}_{k\mid k-1}$ | Velocity innovation | m/s |

**Boundary conditions / typical values for the KTU exam:**

- Process noise $Q$: tune so that INS-only drift corresponds to diagonal elements $\approx (0.05\ \text{m/s}^2)^2$.
- Measurement noise $R$: $\approx \text{diag}((0.1)^2, (0.1)^2, (0.1)^2)\ \text{(m/s)}^2$ for civil-code Doppler.
- Initial covariance $P_0$: $\text{diag}(\sigma_p^2,\ \sigma_v^2,\ \sigma_\phi^2,\ \sigma_{b_a}^2,\ \sigma_{b_g}^2)$ with typical values $(\textbf{10 m})^2, (\textbf{1 m/s})^2, (\textbf{1°})^2, (\textbf{0.1 m/s}^2)^2, (\textbf{0.01°/s})^2$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane-level velocity for adaptive cruise control & emergency braking |
| **UAV / Drone Delivery** | Hover-stability in GNSS-denied corners of warehouses |
| **Marine Navigation** | Smooth heading & speed through waves, without GNSS jitter |
| **Precision Agriculture** | cm/s-level velocity for row-following tractors |
| **Aerospace** | Re-entry vehicles (no GNSS blackout only) — INS-only phases |
| **Robotics (Module context)** | SLAM initialisation, odometry, dead-reckoning between LiDAR scans |

> [!TIP]
> The single most important sentence to write in any KTU answer is: *"The Kalman filter blends INS and GNSS in a statistically optimal manner by minimising the mean-square velocity error, exploiting the high-bandwidth of INS and the long-term stability of GNSS."* This single sentence carries ≥ 4 marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Specific Force to Velocity Update

**Step 1: Start from Newton's second law in an ECEF (Earth-Centred Earth-Fixed) frame.**

The true acceleration of the vehicle in the inertial frame is the sum of gravity and the reaction to applied forces. Transforming into the rotating ECEF frame introduces Coriolis and centripetal terms:

$$\vec{a}^e = \vec{C}_b^e \vec{f}^b + \vec{g}^e - 2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^e \times \vec{v}^e - \vec{\omega}_{ie}^e \times (\vec{\omega}_{ie}^e \times \vec{r}^e)$$

**Step 2: Apply the n-frame (North-East-Down) — the local tangent frame used by INS.**

The n-frame rotates relative to ECEF at rate $\vec{\omega}_{en}^n$. Using the rotating-frame derivative identity:

$$\left.\dfrac{d\vec{v}^n}{dt}\right|_n = \left.\dfrac{d\vec{v}^n}{dt}\right|_e - \vec{\omega}_{en}^n \times \vec{v}^n$$

Substituting and rearranging gives the **INS velocity equation**:

$$\dot{\vec{v}}^n = \vec{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \vec{v}^n + \vec{g}^n$$

**Step 3: Re-write with the Schuler / Coriolis / gravity lumped vector $\vec{a}_{corr}^n$.**

$$\dot{\vec{v}}^n = \vec{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b + \vec{a}_{corr}^n$$

**Step 4: Discretise using first-order Euler integration over $\Delta t = 1/f_{INS}$.**

$$\vec{v}_{k+1}^n = \vec{v}_k^n + \left[ \vec{C}_{b_k}^n \vec{f}_k^b + \vec{a}_{corr,k}^n \right] \Delta t$$

Or higher-order with trapezoidal rule:

$$\vec{v}_{k+1}^n = \vec{v}_k^n + \dfrac{\Delta t}{2} \left[ \vec{C}_{b_k}^n \vec{f}_k^b + \vec{C}_{b_{k+1}}^n \vec{f}_{k+1}^b \right] + \vec{a}_{corr}^n \Delta t$$

This is the **INS-only velocity** $\hat{\vec{v}}_{INS}^-$. The KF then **adds a correction** $\delta\vec{v}^+$ when GNSS arrives.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — The Velocity Error Equation

**Step 1: Decompose true $\vec{v}^n$ into INS-estimated $\hat{\vec{v}}^n$ plus error $\delta\vec{v}^n$:**

$$\vec{v}^n = \hat{\vec{v}}^n + \delta\vec{v}^n$$

Similarly $\vec{C}_b^n = \hat{\vec{C}}_b^n (I - [\vec{\phi}\times])$, where $[\vec{\phi}\times]$ is the skew-symmetric matrix of small attitude error.

**Step 2: Substitute into the velocity equation and subtract the INS-mechanised version:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta\dot{\vec{v}}^n &= \hat{\vec{C}}_b^n(I - [\vec{\phi}\times])(\vec{f}^b + \delta\vec{f}^b) - \hat{\vec{C}}_b^n \vec{f}^b \\
&\quad - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)\times \delta\vec{v}^n + \delta\vec{g}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Drop second-order small products $[\vec{\phi}\times]\delta\vec{f}^b \approx 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta\dot{\vec{v}}^n &= -\hat{\vec{C}}_b^n [\vec{\phi}\times] \vec{f}^b + \hat{\vec{C}}_b^n \delta\vec{f}^b - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)\times \delta\vec{v}^n + \delta\vec{g}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Use the identity $[\vec{\phi}\times]\vec{f}^b = -\vec{\phi}\times\vec{f}^b$ and define accelerometer bias $\vec{b}_a := \delta\vec{f}^b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta\dot{\vec{v}}^n &= \vec{\phi} \times \vec{f}^n + \hat{\vec{C}}_b^n \vec{b}_a - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)\times \delta\vec{v}^n + \delta\vec{g}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **F5** in the cheat sheet — the velocity error is driven by **tilt × specific force** plus **Coriolis coupling** of velocity errors plus **accelerometer bias**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Measurement Equation Linking Velocity to State

**Step 1: At a GNSS update epoch, the filter's predicted velocity is $\hat{\vec{v}}_{k|k-1} = \hat{\vec{v}}_{k-1}^+ + \int \dot{\vec{v}}^n dt$.**

**Step 2: The GNSS receiver's velocity in the n-frame is obtained from Doppler-solved $\vec{v}_{GNSS}^n$:**

$$\vec{v}_{GNSS}^n = \vec{v}_{true}^n + \vec{\eta}_{GNSS}, \quad \vec{\eta}_{GNSS} \sim \mathcal{N}(\vec{0}, R)$$

**Step 3: The innovation (residual) is the difference between GNSS and INS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{\nu}_k &= \vec{z}_k - H_k \hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k-1} \\
&= \vec{v}_{GNSS}^n - \hat{\vec{v}}_{k|k-1}^n - \delta\vec{v}_{predicted} \\
&\approx \delta\vec{v}^n + \vec{\eta}_{GNSS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: The Kalman gain is then computed for the velocity block:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_k^{vel} &= P_{k|k-1}^{vv} (P_{k|k-1}^{vv} + R_k)^{-1} \\
&= \dfrac{\sigma_{INS,v}^2}{\sigma_{INS,v}^2 + \sigma_{GNSS,v}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the scalar "blend factor". When $\sigma_{GNSS} \ll \sigma_{INS}$ (clean GNSS, drifted INS), $K \to 1$ and the filter *trusts GNSS* fully. When $\sigma_{GNSS}$ is large (jamming/multi-path), $K \to 0$ and INS is trusted.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Question (KTU-style):** A vehicle has an INS that drifts in velocity at $\sigma_{INS,v} = 1.0$ m/s. A GNSS receiver supplies velocity with $\sigma_{GNSS,v} = 0.1$ m/s, updating at 1 Hz. Compute the Kalman gain for the velocity channel and the post-update velocity error variance.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert to variances.**

$$\sigma_{INS,v}^2 = 1.0^2 = 1.0\ \text{(m/s)}^2$$

$$\sigma_{GNSS,v}^2 = 0.1^2 = 0.01\ \text{(m/s)}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute scalar Kalman gain.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{\sigma_{INS}^2}{\sigma_{INS}^2 + \sigma_{GNSS}^2} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.0 + 0.01} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.01} = 0.9901
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Post-update variance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{post}^2 &= (1 - K)\sigma_{INS}^2 = (1 - 0.9901)(1.0) = 0.0099\ \text{(m/s)}^2 \\
\sigma_{post} &= \sqrt{0.0099} \approx 0.0995\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

> The post-update velocity accuracy (≈ 0.1 m/s) is essentially **equal to the GNSS accuracy**, because the GNSS is much more accurate than the INS drift. The filter has successfully *re-anchored* the INS.

**Step 5: Repeat for the case of a jammed GNSS (σ = 10 m/s):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{1.0}{1.0 + 100} = 0.0099 \approx 0.01 \\
\sigma_{post}^2 &= (0.99)(1.0) = 0.99 \\
\sigma_{post} &= 0.995\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now the filter relies almost entirely on the INS.

> [!TIP]
> **This numerical example is gold for a 14-mark answer.** Examiners love to ask: *"If GNSS noise increases by a factor of 100, what happens to the Kalman gain and why?"* — the above is your ready-made model answer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Strengths vs Weaknesses

| Property | INS-only | GNSS-only | INS+GNSS (KF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Update rate | **100 – 1000 Hz** ✓ | 1 – 20 Hz ✗ | **100 – 1000 Hz** ✓ |
| Long-term accuracy | drifts ✗ | **excellent** ✓ | **excellent** ✓ |
| Short-term noise | very low ✓ | jittery ✗ | very low ✓ |
| GNSS-denied operation | works ✓ | fails ✗ | works (degrades gracefully) ✓ |
| Cost / SWaP | moderate | low | moderate |
| Initialisation time | short ✓ | 30 – 60 s ✗ | **short** ✓ |
| Velocity accuracy | 0.1 m/s (short), 5+ m/s (10 min) | 0.05 m/s (good sky) | **< 0.1 m/s sustained** ✓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Functional Architecture of GNSS/INS Velocity Fusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[IMU Accel Gyro 100 Hz] --> A1[INS Mechanisation Strapdown]
    A1 --> A2[Velocity v INS plus Position p INS plus Attitude Cb n]
    A2 --> A3[INS Error State delta p delta v phi]
    A3 --> A4[Kalman Filter Prediction Phi Qk]

    B0[GNSS Receiver Pseudorange plus Doppler 1 Hz] --> B1[GNSS PVT Solver]
    B1 --> B2[Velocity v GNSS plus Position p GNSS]
    B2 --> B3[Measurement Residual nu k]

    A4 --> A5[Kalman Gain Kk P Ht H P Ht plus R inv]
    B3 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[State Update x plus equals K nu]
    A6 --> A7[Feedback Correction to INS Mechanisation]
    A7 --> A1

    A4 --> A8[Covariance Update Pk I minus K H P]
    A5 --> A8

    A8 --> A9[Output Best Velocity Estimate v fused 100 Hz]
    A6 --> A9

    style A0 fill:#FFD580
    style B0 fill:#80C0FF
    style A5 fill:#C0FFC0
    style A9 fill:#FF80C0
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **yellow path** is the INS-only branch (100 Hz).
- The **blue path** is the GNSS branch (1 Hz).
- The **green node** is the Kalman-filter gain computation — *where fusion physically happens*.
- The **pink node** is the *final* output that the robot's controller actually consumes.

---

## 4.2 Coupling-Architecture Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Loosely Coupled INS plus GNSS Position Velocity] --> F1[Filter on PVT]
    T[Tightly Coupled INS plus GNSS Pseudorange Pseudorange Rate] --> F2[Filter on Raw Measurements]
    U[Ultra Tight Deep Integration INS plus GNSS I and Q Correlator] --> F3[Filter Drives NCO]

    F1 --> O1[Easy Implementation]
    F2 --> O2[Works with 3 plus satellites]
    F3 --> O3[Best Anti Jam Performance]

    style L fill:#FFE0B0
    style T fill:#B0E0FF
    style U fill:#FFB0E0
```

---

## 4.3 Kalman Filter State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialise
    Initialise --> Predict: Set x0 P0
    Predict --> Predict: IMU arrives dt 10 ms
    Predict --> Update: GNSS arrives dt 1 s
    Update --> Predict: Correct x and P
    Update --> Outage: Few Satellites
    Outage --> Predict: Coast on INS only
    Outage --> Update: GNSS recovered
```

This state diagram is what examiners want to see in a 14-mark architecture question.

---

## 4.4 Velocity-Fusion Time-Line

```mermaid
gantt
    title GNSS plus INS Velocity Fusion Time Multiplexing
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %S
    section INS
    INS v update 1 :a1, 0, 10
    INS v update 2 :a2, 10, 10
    INS v update 3 :a3, 20, 10
    INS v update 4 :a4, 30, 10
    INS v update 5 :a5, 40, 10
    INS v update 6 :a6, 50, 10
    section GNSS
    GNSS Doppler solve :b1, 0, 100
    GNSS next solve :b2, 100, 100
    section KF Update
    KF correct at t0 :c1, 0, 1
    KF correct at t1 :c2, 100, 1
```

The horizontal time-axis (in deciseconds) shows that **INS runs ten times per GNSS cycle** — exactly the multiplexing structure the KF exploits.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State any two reasons why a stand-alone GNSS receiver is insufficient for high-rate velocity estimation in a robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Low update rate** — GNSS receivers typically output velocity at **1 – 10 Hz**, which is too slow for fast robot dynamics (e.g., a quadrotor needs > 100 Hz). → *1 mark*
2. **Velocity jitter / noise** — Doppler-derived velocity contains multipath and receiver-clock noise, giving a noisy signal unsuitable for direct control. → *1 mark*
3. **Outages** — GNSS fails in tunnels, urban canyons and under foliage; velocity becomes unavailable. → *1 mark* *(any two of the three)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define "Innovation" in a Kalman filter used for GNSS/INS velocity fusion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *innovation* (or measurement residual) is the difference between the **actual GNSS velocity measurement** $\vec{z}_k$ and the **INS-predicted velocity** $H_k \hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k-1}$ at the GNSS update epoch:

$$\vec{\nu}_k = \vec{z}_k - H_k \hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k-1}$$

It is a zero-mean white sequence if the filter model is correct. Its covariance $\mathbf{S}_k = H_k P_{k|k-1} H_k^T + R_k$ is used to compute the Kalman gain $K_k$ and to detect filter divergence (via chi-square test). → *3 marks for full statement and formula*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice:** *Answer ONE full question from Q3A or Q3B.*

---

### **Q3A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **INS velocity equation** in the local navigation frame (n-frame) starting from Newton's second law in the ECEF frame. Clearly identify the Coriolis, centripetal and gravity terms.

**(b) [7 marks]** A loosely-coupled GNSS/INS Kalman filter uses a 15-state error model. Write the **complete velocity-channel state-transition sub-matrix** (i.e., the $3 \times 3$ block that updates $\delta\vec{v}^n$ from $\delta\vec{p}^n$, $\delta\vec{v}^n$, $\vec{\phi}$, $\vec{b}_a$, $\vec{b}_g$ between two GNSS epochs of $\Delta t = 1$ s.

---

#### Model Solution to Q3A(a)

**Step 1: Start in ECEF.** Newton's second law for an object on Earth's surface in the inertial (i) frame gives $\vec{a}^i = \vec{a}_{applied} + \vec{g}^i$. The accelerometer measures **specific force** $\vec{f} = \vec{a} - \vec{g}$. In ECEF (rotating at $\vec{\omega}_{ie}$), the inertial acceleration is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{a}^i &= \vec{C}_b^e \vec{f}^b + \vec{g}^e \\
&= \left.\dfrac{d\vec{v}^e}{dt}\right|_e + 2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^e \times \vec{v}^e + \vec{\omega}_{ie}^e \times (\vec{\omega}_{ie}^e \times \vec{r}^e)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Transform to n-frame** (which rotates at $\vec{\omega}_{en}$ relative to ECEF). The rate transformation gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left.\dfrac{d\vec{v}^n}{dt}\right|_n &= \left.\dfrac{d\vec{v}^n}{dt}\right|_e - \vec{\omega}_{en}^n \times \vec{v}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Combine and rearrange** to obtain the standard INS velocity equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\vec{v}}^n &= \vec{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)\times \vec{v}^n + \vec{g}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

Term identification → *Coriolis = $2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \vec{v}^n$* (3 marks), *transport = $\vec{\omega}_{en}^n \times \vec{v}^n$* (2 marks), *gravity = $\vec{g}^n$* (1 mark), *sensor term = $\vec{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b$* (1 mark). **Total = 7 marks.**

---

#### Model Solution to Q3A(b)

**Step 1: Write the continuous-time error equation** for the velocity channel (F5):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta\dot{\vec{v}}^n &= -\vec{F}^n \vec{\phi} - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)\times \delta\vec{v}^n + \vec{C}_b^n \vec{b}_a + \vec{w}_a
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\vec{F}^n = [\vec{f}^n \times]$ (skew-symmetric form of specific force).

**Step 2: Order the state as** $\vec{x} = [\delta\vec{p}, \delta\vec{v}, \vec{\phi}, \vec{b}_a, \vec{b}_g]^T$. The $3 \times 3$ sub-matrices that act on $\delta\vec{v}^n$ are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{vv} &= -[ (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times ] \\
F_{v\phi} &= -[ \vec{f}^n \times ] \\
F_{v b_a} &= \hat{\vec{C}}_b^n \\
F_{v b_g} &= 0_{3 \times 3} \\
F_{v p} &= -\dfrac{\gamma}{R_e} I_3 \quad (\text{gravity-gradient term, small})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Form the discrete transition sub-matrix** for the velocity block via $\Phi_{vv} = I_3 + F_{vv} \Delta t + \frac{1}{2}F_{vv}^2 \Delta t^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{vv}(\Delta t = 1) &\approx I_3 - [ (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)\times ] \\
&\approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2\omega_{ie}\sin\phi \cdot \Delta t & 0 \\ -2\omega_{ie}\sin\phi \cdot \Delta t & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $\omega_{ie} = 7.292 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s, $\phi = 10°$ (Kerala latitude), $\sin\phi \approx 0.174$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{vv} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2.54 \times 10^{-5} & 0 \\ -2.54 \times 10^{-5} & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross-coupling to other states is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{v\phi} &= -[\vec{f}^n \times] \Delta t \\
\Phi_{v b_a} &= \hat{\vec{C}}_b^n \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marks distribution:** continuous-time eqn = 2, sub-matrix identification = 2, discrete-time form = 2, numerical substitution = 1. **Total = 7 marks.**

---

### **Q3B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a **block diagram**, describe the working of a **loosely-coupled GNSS/INS integration architecture** for velocity estimation. Indicate the data flow and update rates.

**(b) [7 marks]** An INS drifts in velocity at $\sigma_{INS} = 0.8$ m/s. The GNSS Doppler-derived velocity has $\sigma_{GNSS} = 0.2$ m/s. Calculate the **Kalman gain** and the **post-update velocity standard deviation** at each GNSS update. Discuss the effect on velocity accuracy if the GNSS noise rises to 2 m/s.

---

#### Model Solution to Q3B(a)

**Step 1: Define the architecture** — *Loosely-coupled integration uses the GNSS receiver's internal PVT (position-velocity-time) solution as the measurement to an external Kalman filter, while INS runs in parallel.* → *1 mark*

**Step 2: Block diagram (textual, since this is a written answer).**

```
IMU ──► INS Mechanisation ──► (v_INS, p_INS, C_b^n)
                                      │
                                      ▼
                          Kalman Filter Prediction
                                      ▲
                                      │ Innovation ν = v_GNSS − v_INS
                                      │
GNSS Receiver ──► PVT Solver ──► (v_GNSS, p_GNSS) ──► Measurement Update
```

**Step 3: State the rates** — INS at 100 Hz, GNSS at 1 Hz, KF update at 1 Hz, output at 100 Hz. → *2 marks*

**Step 4: Mathematical update at GNSS epoch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k} &= \hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k-1} + K_k(\vec{v}_{GNSS} - \hat{\vec{v}}_{INS}) \\
\hat{\vec{v}}^n &\leftarrow \hat{\vec{v}}^n + \delta\vec{v}_{corrected}
\end{aligned}
$$

→ *2 marks*

**Step 5: List the architecture's advantages and disadvantages.** Advantages: modular, simple, easy to retrofit. Disadvantages: needs ≥ 4 satellites; filter fails if GNSS PVT solution is lost. → *2 marks*

**Total = 7 marks.**

---

#### Model Solution to Q3B(b)

**Step 1: Convert to variances.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{INS}^2 = (0.8)^2 = 0.64\ \text{(m/s)}^2 \\
\sigma_{GNSS}^2 = (0.2)^2 = 0.04\ \text{(m/s)}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute scalar Kalman gain.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{\sigma_{INS}^2}{\sigma_{INS}^2 + \sigma_{GNSS}^2} = \dfrac{0.64}{0.64 + 0.04} = \dfrac{0.64}{0.68} \\
K &\approx 0.941
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Post-update variance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{post}^2 &= (1 - K)\,\sigma_{INS}^2 = (0.059)(0.64) = 0.0378 \\
\sigma_{post} &= \sqrt{0.0378} \approx 0.194\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Repeat with $\sigma_{GNSS} = 2$ m/s.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{0.64}{0.64 + 4.0} = \dfrac{0.64}{4.64} = 0.138 \\
\sigma_{post}^2 &= (0.862)(0.64) = 0.552 \\
\sigma_{post} &= 0.743\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Discussion.** With good GNSS ($0.2$ m/s) the filter is dominated by GNSS — gain $\approx 0.94$, post-update $\sigma \approx 0.194$ m/s. With degraded GNSS ($2$ m/s) the gain drops to $0.138$ and the filter essentially reverts to INS — post-update $\sigma \approx 0.74$ m/s. The filter **gracefully degrades**, which is precisely the engineering value of GNSS/INS fusion. → *2 marks for discussion*

**Marks distribution:** variances = 1, K = 2, σ_post = 2, repeated calculation = 1, discussion = 1. **Total = 7 marks.**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-deduction traps reported by KTU examiners:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Coriolis term $2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \vec{v}^n$** — costs **2 marks** every time. Always write it.
> 2. **Writing velocity error equation without the skew-symmetric $[\cdot\times]$ form** — examiners want either the explicit $3\times3$ matrix or the cross-product form. Never write "INS drifts" without an equation.
> 3. **Confusing the *innovation* with the *residual*** — innovation is *predicted* (i.e., uses $\hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k-1}$), residual is *updated* (uses $\hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k}$). KTU strictly expects the innovation definition.
> 4. **Skipping units** — velocities in m/s, accelerations in m/s², angles in rad — the unit audit alone has cost students 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply $\Phi \approx I + F\Delta t$ discretisation** — the velocity block in the discrete transition matrix is **not** the same as the continuous-time $F_{vv}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (must-memorise before exam day):**

- [x] The INS velocity equation in the n-frame: $\dot{\vec{v}}^n = \vec{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)\times\vec{v}^n + \vec{g}^n$
- [x] GNSS velocity comes from **Doppler shift** (pseudorange-rate), not from differencing positions.
- [x] The Kalman filter for GNSS/INS is run at the **GNSS rate**; INS is the *predictor* between updates.
- [x] State vector typical 15 states: $[\delta\vec{p},\ \delta\vec{v},\ \vec{\phi},\ \vec{b}_a,\ \vec{b}_g]^T$.
- [x] Measurement matrix for velocity-only fusion: $H = [0_{3\times3}\ \vert\ I_3\ \vert\ 0_{3\times9}]$ — *velocity is the 4th–6th state slot*.
- [x] Innovation $\vec{\nu}_k = \vec{z}_{GNSS} - H_k\hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k-1}$ is *the* key equation examiners test.
- [x] Scalar Kalman gain $K = \sigma_{INS}^2 / (\sigma_{INS}^2 + \sigma_{GNSS}^2)$ — *memorise this 1-line formula*.
- [x] Earth's rotation rate: $\omega_{ie} = 7.292115 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s.
- [x] Gravity: $g = 9.80665$ m/s² (downward in n-frame).
- [x] Loosely-coupled = filter on PVT; tightly-coupled = filter on raw pseudoranges; ultra-tight = filter drives the code/carrier NCO.
- [x] Velocity accuracy of integrated system is typically **0.05 – 0.10 m/s** sustained.
- [x] KF is *optimal only* under the assumptions of Gaussian noise and linear model — for non-linear use EKF/UKF.
- [x] The filter **gracefully degrades** — when GNSS fails, the INS coasts (no divergence, just drift).

> **End of Notes — Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS (Module 4).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A-GPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** A-GPS # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Assisted Global Positioning System (A-GPS)** is a satellite-based positioning enhancement technique that augments a standard Global Positioning System (GPS) receiver with externally supplied auxiliary data — known as **Assistance Data** — delivered via a terrestrial communication network (e.g., cellular GSM, UMTS, LTE, or Wi-Fi). The primary objective of A-GPS is to drastically reduce the **Time-To-First-Fix (TTFF)**, improve **receiver sensitivity**, and enable reliable position computation in challenging signal environments such as **indoor**, **urban canyons**, and **dense foliage** where standalone GPS would otherwise fail. Mathematically, A-GPS can be defined as a hybrid positioning system that combines: $$\text{A-GPS} = \{\text{GPS Satellite Signals}\} \cup \{\text{Assistance Data from Reference Network}\}$$ delivered through an assistance server to a Mobile Station (MS) / User Equipment (UE) to compute a position fix $(x, y, z, t)$ with improved latency and accuracy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Terminology per KTU 2024 Module 4 Syllabus:** > - **MS (Mobile Station)** → The user device requesting position > - **SMLC / SLP (SUPL Location Platform)** → The assistance server > - **RRLP / RRC / SUPL** → Communication protocols used to deliver assistance data > - **TTFF** → Time to acquire first valid position fix (warm start, cold start, hot start) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "Finding a Friend in a Noisy Stadium"** > > Imagine you are trying to locate a friend inside a **massive, noisy cricket stadium** using only your own eyes (this is *standalone GPS*). The crowd is loud, lights are flickering, and you cannot see well — you would have to **scan the entire stadium slowly**, which takes a lot of time (long TTFF). > > Now, imagine you have a **walkie-talkie connected to stadium staff** who already know exactly where your friend is sitting (rough row, approximate section, last known seat). The staff broadcasts **hints and clues** to you — *"look toward Block B, upper tier, row 12"* — and you immediately find your friend in seconds. > > The walkie-talkie hints are the **Assistance Data**, the stadium staff is the **A-GPS Server**, and you (with your eyes) are the **GPS receiver chip**. A-GPS is simply **"external eyes"** helping the GPS chip locate satellites faster. --- ## 1.3 Why A-GPS Exists — The Standalone GPS Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > A standard cold-start GPS receiver must search **all possible satellite ranges**, code phases, and Doppler frequencies blindly. The search space is enormous. The total number of search bins for a single satellite is given by: $$N_{\text{bins}} = N_{\text{code-phases}} \times N_{\text{Doppler}} \times N_{\text{satellites}}$$ where typically $N_{\text{code-phases}} \approx 1023$ (for C/A code) and $N_{\text{Doppler}} \approx 30$ to $40$ bins across $\pm 5$ kHz. With **24 to 32 satellites** in the constellation, this is computationally intensive and consumes significant battery power.
