# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

### Q1. Define Differential GPS (DGPS). State two key sources of error that DGPS can effectively cancel.
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** *Differential GPS (DGPS) is a technique that improves the positional accuracy of a GPS receiver by using a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations at known, surveyed positions. The reference station computes the difference between its known position and the position derived from satellite signals, and broadcasts this difference (called the pseudorange correction, PRC) to nearby mobile GPS receivers (rovers), which apply the correction to their own pseudorange measurements before computing position.*

**Two errors effectively cancelled (1 mark):**
1. **Ionospheric delay error** (spatially correlated over short baselines).
2. **Satellite clock bias error** (common to all receivers tracking the same SV at the same instant).

> *Other acceptable answers: tropospheric delay, ephemeris error (all spatially/temporally correlated).*

---

### Q2. Distinguish between Code-Phase DGPS and Carrier-Phase DGPS (RTK) in terms of observable used and achievable accuracy.
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Code-Phase DGPS | Carrier-Phase DGPS (RTK) |
|---|---|---|
| **Observable** | C/A code on L1 (chip length ≈ 293 m) | Carrier phase on L1/L2 (wavelength ≈ 19 cm) |
| **Measured Quantity** | Group delay (pseudorange) | Phase fraction + integer ambiguity $N$ |
| **Achievable Accuracy** | 1–3 m horizontal | 1 cm + 1 ppm horizontal |
| **Ambiguity Resolution** | Not required | Must resolve integer $N$ (initialization needed) |
| **Baseline Limit** | ~200 km | ~20–30 km |

> **Mnemonic for KTU:** *Code → Coarse, Carrier → Cm-level.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Internal Choice, Model Pattern]**

#### (a) (7 Marks)
With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working principle of a DGPS system**. Clearly identify the role of the reference station, the broadcast link, and the rover.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid architecture).

**Step-by-Step Explanation (7 marks, distributed as 1 mark per logical point + 1 for diagram):**

1. **Reference Station (Geodetic Survey):** A GPS receiver installed at a precisely surveyed location (accuracy ~mm) continuously tracks all visible satellites and measures pseudoranges $\rho_{\text{ref}}^{(j)}$ to each. **(1 mark)**

2. **Pseudorange Computation:** Using the broadcast ephemeris and the known station coordinates, the receiver computes the *true* geometric range $r_{\text{ref}}^{(j)}$ to each satellite. **(1 mark)**

3. **Error Extraction:** The receiver forms the difference:
$$
\text{PRC}^{(j)} = r_{\text{ref}}^{(j)} - \rho_{\text{ref}}^{(j)}
$$
This PRC represents the negative of the total measurement error (ionospheric, tropospheric, clock, ephemeris) for satellite $j$ at the reference station. **(1 mark)**

4. **Broadcast Link:** The PRCs (and their time-derivatives, the RRCs) are formatted into standard messages (e.g., **RTCM SC-104 Type 1 and Type 2**) and broadcast to rovers via:
   - VHF/UHF radio (local DGPS, range ~100 km),
   - Marine radiobeacon (283.5–325 kHz),
   - Geostationary satellite (SBAS like WAAS/EGNOS). **(1 mark)**

5. **Rover Reception and Application:** The mobile robot's GPS receiver obtains the PRCs via the data link and applies them to its own pseudoranges:
$$
\rho_{\text{rover,corr}}^{(j)} = \rho_{\text{rover}}^{(j)} + \text{PRC}^{(j)}
$$
**(1 mark)**

6. **Trilateration:** The corrected pseudoranges are used in a least-squares or weighted position solver to estimate the rover's ECEF coordinates, which are then transformed to the robot's local navigation frame. **(1 mark)**

7. **Fusion with Other Sensors:** The corrected GPS position is fed into a **Kalman Filter** (typically EKF) along with IMU and odometry to obtain a continuous, drift-free pose estimate. **(1 mark)**

---

#### (b) (7 Marks)
A reference station at known ECEF position $(1{,}500{,}000, 4{,}500{,}000, 4{,}000{,}000)$ m measures a pseudorange to satellite SV-03 of $\rho_{\text{ref}} = 24{,}000{,}015.5$ m. The satellite's broadcast ECEF position is $(18{,}000{,}000, -10{,}000{,}000, 12{,}000{,}000)$ m. 
- **(i)** Compute the pseudorange correction (PRC).
- **(ii)** A rover measures $\rho_{\text{rover}} = 23{,}999{,}985.2$ m to the same satellite. Compute the corrected pseudorange.
- **(iii)** If the satellite's clock bias is known to introduce an additional +3.0 m of error into the rover measurement (not yet compensated), what is the *true geometric range* from the rover to the satellite? (Use the reference station's *true* position as an approximation for the rover's position only for the purpose of this question.)

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Compute true range at reference station:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta X &= 18{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}500{,}000 = 16{,}500{,}000 \text{ m} \\
\Delta Y &= -10{,}000{,}000 - 4{,}500{,}000 = -14{,}500{,}000 \text{ m} \\
\Delta Z &= 12{,}000{,}000 - 4{,}000{,}000 = 8{,}000{,}000 \text{ m} \\
r_{\text{ref}} &= \sqrt{ (16.5)^2 + (14.5)^2 + (8.0)^2 } \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{ 272.25 + 210.25 + 64.00 } \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{546.50} \times 10^6 \\
&\approx 23.3786 \times 10^6 = 23{,}378{,}617.4 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**PRC**:
$$
\text{PRC} = r_{\text{ref}} - \rho_{\text{ref}} = 23{,}378{,}617.4 - 24{,}000{,}015.5 = -621{,}398.1 \text{ m}
$$

**(2 marks for Step i)**

**Step (ii) — Apply correction at rover:**

$$
\rho_{\text{rover,corr}} = 23{,}999{,}985.2 + (-621{,}398.1) = 23{,}378{,}587.1 \text{ m}
$$

**(2 marks for Step ii)**

**Step (iii) — True geometric range with clock bias:**

The corrected pseudorange still contains the additional 3.0 m clock bias. The true geometric range is:

$$
r_{\text{true}} = \rho_{\text{rover,corr}} - \text{clock bias} = 23{,}378{,}587.1 - 3.0 = 23{,}378{,}584.1 \text{ m}
$$

**(3 marks for Step iii — including correct identification of clock bias as a separate error term)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Internal Choice, Alternative Pattern]**

#### (a) (7 Marks)
**Discuss in detail the various sources of error in GPS positioning. Explain how DGPS mitigates each error and identify which errors DGPS *cannot* eliminate.**

**Model Solution:**

**Sources of Error Table (4 marks — distribute 0.5 mark per error):**

| Error Source | Magnitude (1-$\sigma$) | DGPS Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Ionospheric delay** | 2–10 m | **Effectively cancelled** (spatially correlated) |
| **Tropospheric delay** | 2–25 m | **Effectively cancelled** (spatially correlated) |
| **Satellite clock bias** | 1–3 m | **Effectively cancelled** (identical to all users) |
| **Ephemeris error** | 1–5 m | **Mostly cancelled** (same SV used) |
| **Multipath** | 0.5–1 m (code); cm (carrier) | **NOT cancelled** (site-specific) |
| **Receiver noise** | 0.3–1 m | **NOT cancelled** (uncorrelated) |
| **Selective Availability (SA)** | 0–30 m (deprecated since 2000) | N/A (turned off) |

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- DGPS works on the **common-mode error** principle. The reference station, located close to the rover, experiences nearly identical ionospheric, tropospheric, and satellite-clock errors because the same signals traverse nearly the same paths.
- By computing the *residual error* at the reference station and broadcasting it as a PRC, the same error is removed from the rover measurement.

**Errors NOT eliminated (1 mark):**
- **Multipath:** Highly site-specific, dependent on local reflectors (buildings, ground, vehicle body). Two receivers 100 m apart can have completely different multipath signatures.
- **Receiver measurement noise:** Thermal noise, quantization errors in the code-tracking loop are statistically independent between two different receivers.
- **Spatial decorrelation of ionosphere at long baselines** ($d > 200$ km): The ionospheric shell is no longer "common."

---

#### (b) (7 Marks)
A survey-grade robot uses **RTK DGPS** with a baseline of 10 km to a reference station. The carrier phase has wavelength $\lambda = 0.1903$ m (L2 frequency 1.57542 GHz, wait, correction: L1 = 1575.42 MHz → $\lambda \approx 0.190$ m).
- **(i)** Express the carrier-phase observation equation.
- **(ii)** If the residual range error after integer ambiguity resolution is 0.005 m and the baseline introduces 1 ppm of additional error, what is the total expected position error?

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Carrier-phase observation equation (3 marks):**

The carrier-phase observable $\Phi$ in units of cycles is:

$$
\Phi^{(j)} = \frac{r^{(j)}}{\lambda} + N^{(j)} + f \cdot \Delta t + \frac{I^{(j)}}{\lambda} + \nu^{(j)}
$$

Where:
- $r^{(j)}$ = geometric range to satellite $j$ (m),
- $\lambda$ = L1 carrier wavelength ≈ **0.1903 m**,
- $N^{(j)}$ = integer ambiguity (number of whole cycles),
- $f$ = carrier frequency,
- $\Delta t$ = clock offset (s),
- $I^{(j)}$ = ionospheric delay (m, with opposite sign to code pseudorange),
- $\nu^{(j)}$ = measurement noise.

Differentiating between reference and rover, the differenced carrier phase eliminates common-mode errors and $N$ becomes a known integer (after ambiguity resolution), allowing the range to be solved to **sub-cm** precision.

**Step (ii) — Total expected error (4 marks):**

Baseline $d = 10$ km, so the baseline-dependent error at 1 ppm is:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{baseline}} = 1 \text{ ppm} \times 10 \text{ km} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 10{,}000 = 0.010 \text{ m} = 1 \text{ cm}
$$

Residual phase error: $\varepsilon_{\text{phase}} = 0.005$ m = **0.5 cm**.

Total expected position error (RSS — root sum of squares, since errors are independent):

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{ \varepsilon_{\text{baseline}}^2 + \varepsilon_{\text{phase}}^2 } = \sqrt{ (0.010)^2 + (0.005)^2 }
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{ 0.000100 + 0.000025 } = \sqrt{ 0.000125 } \approx 0.01118 \text{ m} \approx 1.12 \text{ cm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The total expected position error is approximately **1.12 cm** for a 10 km baseline RTK solution — consistent with the well-known **"1 cm + 1 ppm"** rule of thumb for carrier-phase RTK.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 DGPS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PRC sign convention:** The KTU answer key typically uses $\text{PRC} = r_{\text{true}} - \rho_{\text{measured}}$ (correction is *added* to rover's measurement). Some texts use the opposite. **Always state your convention explicitly in the answer.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the RRC / aging term:** If a question specifies a *time delay* between PRC generation and rover application, **2 marks are usually awarded** for the RRC aging projection. Students who ignore this lose those marks.
>
> 3. **Mixing up PDOP/HDOP/GDOP:** KTU question: *"For a ground robot, which DOP is most relevant?"* → Answer: **HDOP** (horizontal). Many students write PDOP and lose the mark.
>
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram:** Even in numerical questions, KTU valuators often award 1–2 marks for a labeled block diagram. Always include a sketch.
>
> 5. **Treating multipath as "fully cancelled":** Multipath is **NOT** corrected by DGPS. Writing so in 14-mark answers is a common 1-mark penalty.
>
> 6. **Unit mistakes in carrier-phase questions:** Carrier phase $\Phi$ is in *cycles*, not meters. Students must convert: $r = \Phi \cdot \lambda$. Mixing units is a **2-mark** trap.
>
> 7. **Integer Ambiguity ($N$):** In RTK questions, forgetting to mention that $N$ is an *integer* (and must be resolved before the carrier-phase range is usable) costs a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **DGPS — One-Page Rapid-Revision Cheat Sheet for KTU 2024**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **DGPS = GPS + ground-based differential correction** to cancel common-mode errors.
- ☐ **Key assumption:** Errors are **spatially correlated** over distances up to ~200 km.
- ☐ **Three actors:** GPS satellites → Reference station (known position) → Rover robot (unknown position).

### Key Formulas
- ☐ **Pseudorange model:** $\rho = r + c\Delta t + I + T + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} + \varepsilon_{\text{eph}} + \nu$
- ☐ **PRC:** $\text{PRC} = r_{\text{ref}} - \rho_{\text{ref}}$ (convention: add to rover measurement)
- ☐ **Corrected range:** $\rho_{\text{rover,corr}} = \rho_{\text{rover}} + \text{PRC}$
- ☐ **Aging:** $\text{PRC}(t+\Delta t) = \text{PRC}(t) + \text{RRC} \cdot \Delta t$
- ☐ **Carrier phase:** $\Phi = r/\lambda + N + f\Delta t + \text{errors}$
- ☐ **RTK rule:** 1 cm + 1 ppm of baseline
- ☐ **DOP hierarchy:** $\text{GDOP} \geq \text{PDOP} \geq \text{HDOP}$

### Numbers to Memorize
- ☐ **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- ☐ **L1 carrier frequency:** 1575.42 MHz → $\lambda \approx 19$ cm
- ☐ **C/A code chip length:** ~293 m
- ☐ **Standard SPS accuracy:** ~15 m horizontal (95%)
- ☐ **Code DGPS accuracy:** 1–3 m
- ☐ **RTK accuracy:** 1 cm + 1 ppm
- ☐ **LADGPS range:** 100–200 km
- ☐ **RTK range:** 10–30 km
- ☐ **Ionospheric residual growth:** ~0.5–2 mm/km

### Critical Distinctions (High-Yield Exam Q's)
- ☐ **Code-Phase DGPS** vs **Carrier-Phase RTK** — different observables, different accuracy.
- ☐ **LADGPS** (Local) vs **WADGPS / SBAS** (Wide-Area) — different architectures.
- ☐ **Multipath** is **not** corrected by DGPS.
- ☐ **Receiver noise** is **not** corrected by DGPS.
- ☐ **DOP hierarchy** is a KTU favorite — must know the inequality.
- ☐ **WAAS / EGNOS / GAGAN** are SBAS examples — memorize at least two.
- ☐ **RTCM SC-104** is the standard correction message format.
- ☐ **DGPS + EKF + IMU** is the canonical mobile-robot navigation triad.

### Common-Sense Robot-Side Takeaway
- ☐ DGPS provides the **absolute global anchor**; **IMU/odometry** provide **short-term smoothness**; the **EKF** fuses both. Without DGPS, a robot drifts unboundedly; with DGPS, drift is bounded by DGPS update rate and accuracy.
- ☐ For **outdoor field robots** (agriculture, mining, planetary analogs), DGPS is the **only** widely available absolute position source.
- ☐ For **indoor robots**, DGPS is **unavailable**; use SLAM, UWB, WiFi, or vision-based localization instead.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RTK Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RTK Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Real-Time Kinematic (RTK)** is a high-precision satellite-based positioning technique that uses *carrier-phase measurements* of GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) signals together with a *differential correction* transmitted from a fixed base station to a mobile rover in real time. It achieves **centimeter-level positioning accuracy** (typically **1 cm + 1 ppm** horizontal and **2 cm + 1 ppm** vertical) by resolving the integer ambiguities inherent in the carrier-phase observations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** RTK is the most accurate *real-time* differential GNSS technique covered in Module 4. It is the de-facto standard for high-precision outdoor robot navigation, autonomous vehicles, agricultural robotics, and surveying drones.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, RTK belongs to the family of **GNSS augmentation techniques**, sitting in the accuracy hierarchy:
- **Standalone GNSS:** ~2.5 m to 5 m
- **DGPS (Differential GPS):** ~0.5 m to 1 m
- **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic):** **1 cm – 2 cm**
- **PPP (Precise Point Positioning):** ~5 cm – 10 cm (global, no base needed)
- **PPP-RTK / Network RTK:** ~1 cm – 5 cm

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surveyor's Ruler in the Sky"

Imagine you are measuring the distance to a friend standing far away. If you use a **wooden metre stick** (coarse measurement), you may be off by half a metre. But if you and your friend both hold *identical finely-graduated rulers* and count the *exact number of full stick-lengths plus the fractional tail* between you, your error drops to a few millimetres. RTK does exactly this: the **base station** and the **rover** both measure the *same* carrier wave (an invisible "ruler" of length $\lambda \approx 19.03$ cm for the L1 signal). The base station's known position lets it compute the *exact number of whole wavelengths* between the satellite and itself. It then "tells" the rover via a radio link, allowing the rover to remove the unknown integer part and lock onto the centimetre-precise range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** RTK = *Differential GPS + Carrier-phase tricks + Ambiguity fixing*. The "Real-Time" part means corrections arrive within milliseconds; the "Kinematic" part means the rover can be moving (walking robot, car, drone) when it measures.

## 1.3 Key Physical & System Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Value (GPS L1) |
|---|---|---|
| $f_{L1}$ | L1 carrier frequency | **1575.42 MHz** |
| $\lambda_{L1}$ | L1 wavelength | **19.029 cm** |
| $f_{L2}$ | L2 carrier frequency | **1227.60 MHz** |
| $\lambda_{L2}$ | L2 wavelength | **24.420 cm** |
| $c$ | Speed of light | **299 792 458 m/s** |
| RTK accuracy | Horizontal | **1 cm + 1 ppm** |
| RTK accuracy | Vertical | **2 cm + 1 ppm** |
| Baseline range | Typical | **< 10 – 20 km** |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize $\lambda_{L1} = 19.03$ cm and $\lambda_{L2} = 24.42$ cm. The shorter the wavelength, the harder ambiguity resolution is — but more signals mean redundancy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an RTK System

An RTK system has **three functional layers**:

1. **Space Segment** — The GNSS constellation (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou, IRNSS). Each satellite broadcasts a carrier wave modulated with navigation and ranging codes.
2. **Reference (Base) Station** — A *geodetically-surveyed* GNSS receiver installed at a precisely known coordinate $(x_b, y_b, z_b)$. It continuously measures carrier-phase to every visible satellite and computes the *correction stream*.
3. **Rover (Robot)** — The mobile receiver mounted on the robot. It receives (a) the satellite signals directly and (b) the base station's corrections via a telemetry radio (typically 433 MHz, 868 MHz, 900 MHz, or 2.4 GHz) or via NTRIP over cellular internet.

## 2.2 The Carrier-Phase Observation Equation

The fundamental measurement that powers RTK is the carrier-phase observable. For a receiver $r$ tracking satellite $s$ on frequency $L_i$:

$$\phi_{r}^{s} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{L_i}} \left( \rho_{r}^{s} + c \cdot (\delta t_r - \delta t^{s}) - I_{r}^{s} + T_{r}^{s} \right) + N_{r}^{s} + \varepsilon_{\phi}$$

Where:

- $\phi_{r}^{s}$ — measured carrier phase in **cycles**
- $\rho_{r}^{s}$ — true geometric range (metres)
- $c$ — speed of light (**2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**)
- $\delta t_r$ — receiver clock error (seconds)
- $\delta t^{s}$ — satellite clock error (seconds)
- $I_{r}^{s}$ — ionospheric delay (metres)
- $T_{r}^{s}$ — tropospheric delay (metres)
- $N_{r}^{s}$ — **integer ambiguity** (unknown number of whole cycles — the *bottleneck* of RTK)
- $\varepsilon_{\phi}$ — measurement noise (typically a few mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **integer ambiguity** $N_{r}^{s}$ is the unknown whole number of carrier wavelengths between the satellite and the receiver at the moment of lock-on. It is *not* a real number — it must be an integer. Resolving it is the central mathematical problem of RTK.

## 2.3 The Three Differences (Core RTK Mathematics)

To eliminate the common-mode errors and isolate the geometric information, RTK forms three *differences*:

### (a) Single Difference (between two receivers, one satellite)
$$\Delta \phi_{rb}^{s} = \phi_{r}^{s} - \phi_{b}^{s} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\rho_{r}^{s} - \rho_{b}^{s}\right) + c \cdot \Delta \delta t_{rb} - \Delta I + \Delta T + \Delta N_{rb}^{s} + \varepsilon_{\Delta}$$
- **Cancels:** satellite clock error $\delta t^{s}$
- **Reduces:** ionospheric and tropospheric delays (for short baselines)

### (b) Double Difference (between two satellites and two receivers)
$$\nabla \Delta \phi_{rb}^{sj} = \Delta \phi_{rb}^{s} - \Delta \phi_{rb}^{j} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\rho_{rb}^{sj,\text{geom}}\right) + \nabla \Delta N_{rb}^{sj} + \varepsilon_{\nabla \Delta}$$
- **Cancels:** receiver clock error $\delta t_r$
- **Result:** Pure geometric information + an integer ambiguity

### (c) Triple Difference (between two epochs)
$$\delta \nabla \Delta \phi_{rb}^{sj}(t_{k+1}, t_k) = \nabla \Delta \phi_{rb}^{sj}(t_{k+1}) - \nabla \Delta \phi_{rb}^{sj}(t_k)$$
- **Cancels:** the integer ambiguities themselves (used for *cycle-slip detection*)
- **Use:** change detection, moving-baseline analysis

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wavelength–frequency relation | $\lambda = c / f$ | Higher $f$ → shorter $\lambda$ → harder ambiguity resolution |
| 2 | Carrier phase (cycles → metres) | $\Phi = \lambda \cdot \phi$ | One L1 cycle = 19.03 cm |
| 3 | Pseudorange vs carrier-phase noise | $\sigma_{\rho} \approx 1$ m; $\sigma_{\phi} \approx 1$–$2$ mm | Carrier is 100× more precise but ambiguous |
| 4 | Double-difference range | $\nabla \Delta \rho = \lambda \cdot \nabla \Delta \phi - \lambda \cdot \nabla \Delta N$ | DD eliminates clocks |
| 5 | Baseline-dependent accuracy | $\sigma_{b} = \sigma_{0} \cdot (1 + b / b_{0})$ | $b$ in km; $b_0$ = 1 km baseline |
| 6 | RTK horizontal accuracy | $1\,\text{cm} + 1\,\text{ppm} \cdot b$ | "ppm" = 1 mm per km of baseline |
| 7 | RTK vertical accuracy | $2\,\text{cm} + 1\,\text{ppm} \cdot b$ | Vertical always worse than horizontal |
| 8 | Dilution of Precision | $\text{PDOP}, \text{HDOP}, \text{VDOP}$ | Lower is better; PDOP < 2 ideal |
| 9 | Ambiguity Resolution Ratio | $\text{Ratio} = \sigma_{\text{second-best}} / \sigma_{\text{best}}$ | Ratio ≥ 3 ⇒ *Fix* (integer solution) |
| 10 | Float vs Fixed solution | Float: real $N$; Fixed: integer $N$ | "Fix" is what gives cm accuracy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** Always write the **double-difference** equation when explaining RTK. The triple difference is mainly a cycle-slip detector, not a positioning observable.

## 2.5 Why RTK Works (Engineering Intuition)

- **Differential principle** — Two receivers close together see nearly the *same* ionospheric, tropospheric, satellite-clock, and ephemeris errors. Subtracting them out yields clean geometry.
- **Carrier-phase precision** — The L1 carrier has $\sim 19$ cm wavelength, and the receiver can measure the *fractional* part of a cycle down to $\sim 1$–$2$ mm. So the *precision* of one measurement is millimetric; the *bias* is the unknown integer $N$.
- **Integer fixing** — Once $N$ is determined, the range is known to millimetres. The base station's surveyed coordinates pin the geometry; the rover's position falls out by trilateration.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Required Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Lane-level localization | 10–30 cm (RTK feasible) |
| Agricultural Robots | Row-following, spraying | 2–5 cm |
| Drone Surveying (UAV/MAV) | Photogrammetry GCPs | 1–3 cm |
| Construction Robotics | Machine control, grading | 1–2 cm |
| Marine Robotics (USV) | Hydrographic surveying | 5–10 cm |
| Search & Rescue Drones | Waypoint navigation | 30–50 cm |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carrier-Phase Range from Observed Phase

**Goal:** Show how the measured phase $\phi$ in cycles converts to a range in metres.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Start.** The satellite emits a sinusoidal carrier of frequency $f$. The receiver generates a *replica* of the same sinusoid. The phase difference between received and replica, measured at the receiver, equals the *propagation time* $\tau$ times the angular frequency $2\pi f$, modulo $2\pi$.

$$\phi_{\text{measured}} = f \cdot \tau = \frac{f \cdot \rho}{c} \quad (\text{mod } 1 \text{ cycle})$$

**Step 2 — Introduce Integer Ambiguity.** The "mod 1" means we only know the *fractional* part of the total number of cycles. The total cycles that travelled is $N$ (integer) + fractional. So:

$$\phi_{\text{measured}} = \frac{\rho}{\lambda} - N + \varepsilon$$

Multiply by $\lambda$:

$$\lambda \cdot \phi = \rho - \lambda N + \lambda \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite in Standard Form.** Add the systematic error terms (clocks, atmosphere):

$$\lambda \phi_{r}^{s} = \rho_{r}^{s} + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^{s}) - I_{r}^{s} + T_{r}^{s} - \lambda N_{r}^{s} + \lambda \varepsilon_{\phi}$$

This is the carrier-phase observation equation quoted in Section 2.2, with the integer ambiguity $N$ explicit. The range $\rho$ and the ambiguity $\lambda N$ are *coupled* — we cannot separate them from a *single* measurement. This is why we need differencing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Double-Difference Integer Ambiguity Resolution

**Goal:** Show how the double-difference (DD) eliminates clock biases and produces a pure geometric equation that can be solved for the rover position.

**Step 1 — Single-Difference between Rover and Base, satellite $s$:**

$$\Delta \phi_{rb}^{s} = \phi_{r}^{s} - \phi_{b}^{s} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\rho_{r}^{s} - \rho_{b}^{s}\right) + c\,\Delta\delta t_{rb} - \Delta I_{rb}^{s} + \Delta T_{rb}^{s} + \Delta N_{rb}^{s} + \varepsilon_{\Delta}$$

**Step 2 — Single-Difference for a second satellite $j$:**

$$\Delta \phi_{rb}^{j} = \phi_{r}^{j} - \phi_{b}^{j} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\rho_{r}^{j} - \rho_{b}^{j}\right) + c\,\Delta\delta t_{rb} - \Delta I_{rb}^{j} + \Delta T_{rb}^{j} + \Delta N_{rb}^{j} + \varepsilon_{\Delta}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two single-differences** to form the *double difference*:

$$\nabla\Delta\phi_{rb}^{sj} = \Delta\phi_{rb}^{s} - \Delta\phi_{rb}^{j}$$

The receiver clock term $c\,\Delta\delta t_{rb}$ cancels *exactly* because it is common to both satellites (same receiver clock). For a short baseline ($< 10$ km), the ionospheric and tropospheric terms also nearly cancel:

$$\nabla\Delta\phi_{rb}^{sj} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[(\rho_{r}^{s} - \rho_{b}^{s}) - (\rho_{r}^{j} - \rho_{b}^{j})\right] + \nabla\Delta N_{rb}^{sj} + \varepsilon_{\nabla\Delta}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that each $\rho$ is a nonlinear function of position.** Linearize around an approximate rover position $\mathbf{x}_{r,0}$ using $\rho \approx \rho_0 + \mathbf{u}^{T} \cdot \delta\mathbf{x}$, where $\mathbf{u}$ is the line-of-sight unit vector:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla\Delta\phi_{rb}^{sj} \cdot \lambda &= (\mathbf{u}_{r}^{s} - \mathbf{u}_{b}^{s} - \mathbf{u}_{r}^{j} + \mathbf{u}_{b}^{j})^{T} \cdot \delta\mathbf{x}_{r} \\
&\quad + (\rho_{r,0}^{s} - \rho_{b}^{s} - \rho_{r,0}^{j} + \rho_{b}^{j}) + \lambda \cdot \nabla\Delta N_{rb}^{sj} + \varepsilon
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Stack across multiple satellites** ($j = 1, 2, \dots, m$) and multiple epochs to form an over-determined system:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\,\delta\mathbf{x}_{r} + \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{N} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

where $\mathbf{N}$ is the vector of DD integer ambiguities. The least-squares solution gives *float* ambiguities. The LAMBDA method (Teunissen, 1995) is then applied to *fix* $\mathbf{N}$ to integers, after which a final least-squares pass yields the cm-level position.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 5 km baseline RTK system uses L1 carrier ($\lambda = 19.029$ cm). A double-difference observation yields $\nabla\Delta\phi = 124\,583.421$ cycles with a fixed ambiguity $\nabla\Delta N = 124\,580$. Compute the geometric DD range.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DD range} &= \lambda \cdot \left(\nabla\Delta\phi - \nabla\Delta N\right) \\
&= 19.029 \text{ cm} \times \left(124\,583.421 - 124\,580\right) \\
&= 19.029 \text{ cm} \times 3.421 \\
&= 65.11 \text{ cm} \\
&\approx 0.6511 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

The accuracy of this single DD range is on the order of $\sigma_\phi \cdot \lambda \approx 2\text{ mm} \cdot 1 = 2$ mm — which is why, with 4 or more satellites, the *3D position* solution becomes cm-accurate.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fix vs Float vs DGPS vs Standalone

| Method | Observable | Differencing | Ambiguity Status | Typical Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standalone GNSS** | Code pseudorange | None | N/A | 2–5 m |
| **DGPS** | Code pseudorange | Single (between base and rover) | N/A | 0.5–1 m |
| **RTK — Float** | Carrier phase | Double | Real-valued $N$ | 10–30 cm |
| **RTK — Fixed** | Carrier phase | Double | **Integer** $N$ | **1–2 cm** |
| **Network RTK (VRS)** | Carrier phase | Double + network | Integer $N$ | 1–2 cm, baseline ≥ 50 km |
| **PPP** | Code + Carrier | None (global corrections) | Float → fix slowly | 5–10 cm |
| **PPP-RTK** | Code + Carrier | Regional | Integer $N$ | 1–5 cm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RTK System Block Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

The following diagram maps the functional flow of a real-time kinematic positioning system, from the satellites in space to the robot's pose estimator.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SatA["GNSS SATELLITE 1"]
    SatB["GNSS SATELLITE 2"]
    SatC["GNSS SATELLITE 3"]
    SatD["GNSS SATELLITE 4"]

    subgraph SPACE["SPACE SEGMENT"]
        SatA
        SatB
        SatC
        SatD
    end

    subgraph BASE["BASE STATION - GEODETIC REFERENCE"]
        AntB["BASE GNSS ANTENNA"]
        RxB["BASE RECEIVER - KNOWN XYZ"]
        CalcB["CORRECTION COMPUTER"]
        RadioB["TELEMETRY RADIO TRANSMITTER"]
    end

    subgraph ROVER["ROVER - MOUNTED ON ROBOT"]
        AntR["ROVER GNSS ANTENNA"]
        RxR["ROVER RECEIVER"]
        RadioR["TELEMETRY RADIO RECEIVER"]
        DiffEng["DIFFERENCE ENGINE - DD FORMATION"]
        AmbRes["AMBIGUITY RESOLVER - LAMBDA"]
        PoseEst["POSE ESTIMATOR - EKF OR LSQ"]
    end

    SatA -. L1 carrier .-> AntB
    SatB -. L1 carrier .-> AntB
    SatC -. L1 carrier .-> AntB
    SatD -. L1 carrier .-> AntB

    SatA -. L1 carrier .-> AntR
    SatB -. L1 carrier .-> AntR
    SatC -. L1 carrier .-> AntR
    SatD -. L1 carrier .-> AntR

    AntB --> RxB
    RxB --> CalcB
    CalcB --> RadioB
    RadioB == "RTCM corrections, 1 to 10 Hz" ==> RadioR

    AntR --> RxR
    RadioR --> DiffEng
    RxR --> DiffEng
    DiffEng --> AmbRes
    AmbRes --> PoseEst
    PoseEst --> RobotCtrl["ROBOT CONTROLLER"]
```

## 4.2 RTK Signal Processing Pipeline (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["1. RAW CARRIER PHASE phi at receiver"]
    P2["2. SINGLE DIFFERENCE - cancel satellite clock"]
    P3["3. DOUBLE DIFFERENCE - cancel receiver clock"]
    P4["4. LINEARIZATION - Taylor expansion"]
    P5["5. LEAST SQUARES - float solution N real"]
    P6["6. LAMBDA SEARCH - integer N candidates"]
    P7["7. RATIO TEST - fix if ratio greater than 3"]
    P8["8. FIXED SOLUTION - centimeter accuracy"]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Rover–Base Data Exchange (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BaseStation["BASE STATION SIDE"]
        MeasB["Raw carrier phase measurements"]
        KnownB["Known base coordinates"]
        Corr["Compute range residuals and DD corrections"]
        RTCM["Encode as RTCM3 messages 1004 1006 1014"]
    end

    subgraph Channel["COMMUNICATION CHANNEL"]
        RadioLink["Radio link 433 MHz OR NTRIP cellular"]
    end

    subgraph RoverSide["ROVER SIDE"]
        MeasR["Raw carrier phase measurements"]
        Dec["Decode RTCM corrections"]
        Apply["Apply DD corrections"]
        Fix["Ambiguity fixing LAMBDA"]
        Pos["Position output XYZ latitude longitude height"]
    end

    MeasB --> Corr
    KnownB --> Corr
    Corr --> RTCM
    RTCM --> RadioLink
    RadioLink --> Dec

    MeasR --> Apply
    Dec --> Apply
    Apply --> Fix
    Fix --> Pos
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** All node IDs in the Mermaid graphs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `SatA`, `AntB`, `RadioLink`) and contain *no* reserved keywords, no bold/italic markers, and no embedded pipes. This guarantees clean compilation in Mermaid ≥ v9.

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Ambiguity Resolution Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DDIn["Double-difference phase y vector"]
    H["Design matrix H geometry"]
    AFloat["Float solution N hat - real valued"]
    CovN["Covariance matrix of N hat"]
    Search["LAMBDA integer search - Z transform"]
    Candidates["Candidate integer sets N candidates"]
    RatioTest["Ratio test - sigma second best divided by sigma best"]
    Decision{"Ratio greater than or equal 3?"}
    FixOut["FIXED N - centimeter accuracy"]
    FloatOut["FLOAT N - decimeter accuracy"]

    DDIn --> AFLoat2["Least squares float solver"] --> AFLoat
    DDIn --> AFLoat2
    H --> AFLoat2
    AFLoat --> Search
    CovN --> Search
    Search --> Candidates
    Candidates --> RatioTest
    RatioTest --> Decision
    Decision -- YES --> FixOut
    Decision -- NO --> FloatOut
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

### Q1. Define RTK navigation and state two advantages over standard GPS.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Real-Time Kinematic (RTK)** is a differential GNSS technique that uses *carrier-phase measurements* between a fixed base station and a mobile rover, resolving integer ambiguities in real time to deliver **centimeter-level accuracy** (typically 1 cm + 1 ppm horizontal).
**Advantages over standard GPS:**
1. **Accuracy:** Centimeter-level vs ~2–5 m for standalone GPS.
2. **Real-time geometric precision:** Carrier phase is ~100× more precise than code pseudorange.
3. **Attitude-independent:** No inertial sensors required for short-baseline cm-accuracy.

> *(Mark split: 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for any two advantages.)*

### Q2. What is integer ambiguity in RTK? Why must it be resolved as an integer?
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **integer ambiguity** $N$ is the unknown whole number of carrier wavelengths (cycles) between a satellite and receiver at the moment of phase lock. Because the receiver can only measure the *fractional* phase (modulo 1 cycle), the absolute cycle count is lost.
It **must be resolved as an integer** because the carrier wave is a periodic sinusoid; the geometric range $\rho$ and $\lambda N$ are coupled. Fixing $N$ to the *correct integer* removes this coupling and collapses the range estimate from decimeter (float) to centimeter (fixed). The LAMBDA method and the ratio test (threshold ≈ 3) are standard tools used.

> *(Mark split: 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for why integer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 marks]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of the carrier-phase observation equation, derive the **single-difference** and **double-difference** observable in RTK. Clearly state which error sources are eliminated at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **LAMBDA method** for integer ambiguity resolution. What is the role of the **ratio test** in deciding between a "Float" and a "Fixed" solution? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Starting point — Raw carrier phase equation:**

$$\phi_{r}^{s} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\rho_{r}^{s} + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^{s}) - I_{r}^{s} + T_{r}^{s}\right) + N_{r}^{s} + \varepsilon_{\phi}$$

**Step 1 — Single Difference (between rover $r$ and base $b$, one satellite $s$):**
Subtract the base observation from the rover observation:

$$\Delta\phi_{rb}^{s} = \phi_{r}^{s} - \phi_{b}^{s}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta\phi_{rb}^{s} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\rho_{r}^{s} - \rho_{b}^{s} + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_b) - (I_{r}^{s} - I_{b}^{s}) + (T_{r}^{s} - T_{b}^{s})\right) + (N_{r}^{s} - N_{b}^{s}) + \varepsilon_{\Delta}$$

**Eliminated:** satellite clock error $\delta t^{s}$ (common to both receivers).
**Reduced:** ionospheric and tropospheric delays (for short baselines $< 10$ km, the *spatial decorrelation* of these delays is small).

**Step 2 — Double Difference (between two satellites $s$ and $j$ and two receivers $r$ and $b$):**
Form a single difference for satellite $j$ and subtract:

$$\nabla\Delta\phi_{rb}^{sj} = \Delta\phi_{rb}^{s} - \Delta\phi_{rb}^{j}$$

After cancellation:

$$\nabla\Delta\phi_{rb}^{sj} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[(\rho_{r}^{s} - \rho_{b}^{s}) - (\rho_{r}^{j} - \rho_{b}^{j})\right] + \nabla\Delta N_{rb}^{sj} + \varepsilon_{\nabla\Delta}$$

**Eliminated:** receiver clock error $\delta t_r - \delta t_b$ (common to both satellites).
**Result:** A pure geometric equation plus an integer ambiguity $\nabla\Delta N_{rb}^{sj}$. This is the *fundamental observable* of RTK.

**Step 3 — Linearization** around an approximate rover position to form a linear system $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\,\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{N} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ that can be solved by least squares.

*(Mark split: 2 marks raw equation; 2 marks single diff + cancellation; 2 marks double diff + cancellation; 1 mark final linear system statement.)*

---

**(b) LAMBDA Method & Ratio Test (7 marks):**

**LAMBDA — Least-squares AMBiguity Decorrelation Adjustment (Teunissen, 1995):**

The float ambiguity vector $\hat{\mathbf{N}}$ (real-valued) and its covariance $\mathbf{Q}_{\hat{N}}$ are obtained from a Kalman or least-squares filter. The *integer* ambiguities $\mathbf{N}$ are then estimated by:

$$\check{\mathbf{N}} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{N} \in \mathbb{Z}^{m}} \lVert \hat{\mathbf{N}} - \mathbf{N} \rVert_{\mathbf{Q}_{\hat{N}}}^{2}$$

Direct search is computationally intractable because the integer lattice is *highly elongated* (ambiguities are highly correlated). LAMBDA performs an **integer-equivariant Z-transformation** $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{Z}^{T}\mathbf{N}$ that decorrelates the search space, making the integer search fast and reliable. The candidate $\check{\mathbf{z}}$ is then mapped back via $\check{\mathbf{N}} = (\mathbf{Z}^{T})^{-1}\check{\mathbf{z}}$.

**Ratio Test (Verhagen, 2005):**

After LAMBDA, the two best integer candidates $\check{\mathbf{N}}_{1}$ (best) and $\check{\mathbf{N}}_{2}$ (second-best) are compared:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{second-best}}^{2}}{\sigma_{\text{best}}^{2}} = \frac{\lVert \hat{\mathbf{N}} - \check{\mathbf{N}}_{2} \rVert_{\mathbf{Q}}^{2}}{\lVert \hat{\mathbf{N}} - \check{\mathbf{N}}_{1} \rVert_{\mathbf{Q}}^{2}}$$

**Decision rule:**
- If $\text{Ratio} \geq 3$ (or a stricter threshold such as 1.5 / 2.0 / 3.0 depending on baseline length and reliability requirement) → accept the **FIX** and output cm-accuracy.
- If $\text{Ratio} < 3$ → reject the fix; output the **FLOAT** solution (dm-accuracy), and wait for better geometry or more satellites.

**Why this matters:** A *wrong* integer fix can corrupt the position estimate by *many decimetres* — far worse than the float solution. The ratio test is the *statistical safeguard* against this.

*(Mark split: 3 marks LAMBDA algorithm; 2 marks ratio test equation; 2 marks decision rule and engineering justification.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Compare and contrast **DGPS, RTK, and PPP** techniques for GNSS augmentation. Tabulate accuracy, baseline requirement, ambiguity resolution, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot uses an L1 RTK receiver on a 12 km baseline. Given $\sigma_{0} = 5$ mm at $b_{0} = 1$ km baseline and an accuracy scaling of 1 ppm, compute the expected horizontal RMS and the **maximum allowable baseline** if the robot's path-planner requires $\leq 3$ cm horizontal accuracy at all times. State two practical limitations that bound the operational baseline. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | DGPS | RTK | PPP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Observable | Code pseudorange | Carrier phase | Code + Carrier |
| Differencing | Single difference | Double difference | None (global) |
| Integer Ambiguity | N/A | Required (Float → Fix) | Slowly converging |
| Accuracy (horizontal) | 0.5–1 m | 1 cm + 1 ppm | 5–10 cm |
| Baseline | ≤ 200 km | ≤ 10–20 km | Global (no base) |
| Convergence time | Seconds | Seconds (after fix) | 10–30 min |
| Base station needed | Yes | Yes | No (corrections from network) |
| Application | Marine, GIS | Survey, robot nav, drones | Open-ocean, sparse-reference areas |

*(Mark split: 4 marks for correct table; 2 marks for one-paragraph narrative comparison; 1 mark for an example application per technique.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\sigma_0 = 5$ mm at baseline $b_0 = 1$ km
- Accuracy slope: 1 ppm (i.e., 1 mm per km of baseline)
- Operating baseline $b = 12$ km
- Required accuracy: $\sigma_{\max} = 3$ cm = 30 mm (horizontal)

**Step 1 — Compute expected horizontal RMS at $b = 12$ km:**

Using the standard RTK model:

$$\sigma_{h}(b) = \sigma_{0} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{b}{b_{0}}} \quad \text{(diffusion model, illustrative)}$$

Or, using the linear ppm model:

$$\sigma_{h}(b) = \sigma_{0,\text{horiz}} + \text{ppm} \cdot b$$

$$\sigma_{h}(12) = 5\,\text{mm} + 1 \cdot 12 = 5 + 12 = 17\,\text{mm} = 1.7\,\text{cm}$$

Using the *quoted* manufacturer model $1\,\text{cm} + 1\,\text{ppm} \cdot b$:

$$\sigma_{h}(12) = 10\,\text{mm} + 1 \cdot 12 = 10 + 12 = 22\,\text{mm} = 2.2\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum baseline for 3 cm constraint:**

Set $\sigma_{h,\max} = 30$ mm and solve for $b_{\max}$:

$$30 = 10 + 1 \cdot b_{\max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_{\max} = 20\,\text{km}$$

*(Using the more conservative $\sigma_0$ of 5 mm baseline model:)*

$$30 = 5 + 1 \cdot b_{\max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_{\max} = 25\,\text{km}$$

**Therefore:** The maximum allowable baseline is **20–25 km**, depending on the accuracy model.

**Step 3 — Two practical limitations that bound the baseline:**
1. **Ionospheric decorrelation:** The ionospheric delay becomes spatially decorrelated for baselines beyond ~10–20 km, making the "common-mode cancellation" assumption fail. The residual ionospheric error grows roughly as $\sim 1$–$2$ mm/km.
2. **Integer ambiguity reliability:** As baseline grows, the *number of satellites* needed for a reliable LAMBDA fix increases, and the *fix success rate* drops sharply beyond 20–30 km (often < 90% for single-frequency L1). Multipath at either antenna, satellite geometry (PDOP), and atmospheric scintillation further degrade fix reliability.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for RMS calculation; 2 marks for $b_{\max}$; 1 mark unit consistency; 2 marks for two named practical limitations.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "carrier phase" with "pseudorange."** Pseudorange is *code-based* (metre precision). Carrier phase is *wave-based* (mm precision but ambiguous). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to cancel the clock errors.** In the double-difference derivation, students often miss that *both* the satellite clock *and* the receiver clock are eliminated — naming only one will cost 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing the ratio-test threshold.** The exact number (1.5, 2.0, 3.0) varies with application. A generic "ratio ≥ 2" is acceptable; a wrong direction (e.g., "smaller is better") is a hard 0.
> 4. **Forgetting the unit of $N$.** $N$ is in *cycles*, not metres. Converting to metres requires multiplication by $\lambda$. Examiners spot this immediately.
> 5. **Writing RTK accuracy as 1 mm.** The correct expression is **1 cm + 1 ppm**, not 1 mm. The 1 ppm is *per kilometre of baseline*.
> 6. **Skipping linearization.** The double-difference is *nonlinear* in the rover coordinates. Students who jump from DD directly to "solve for position" lose a mark for missing the Taylor-series linearization step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — RTK Navigation**

- [x] **RTK** stands for **Real-Time Kinematic**; it is a *differential, carrier-phase* GNSS technique.
- [x] It delivers **1 cm + 1 ppm** horizontal and **2 cm + 1 ppm** vertical accuracy.
- [x] The system needs a **base station** at *known coordinates* and a **rover** receiving corrections (radio or NTRIP).
- [x] The **fundamental observable** is the **double-difference** of carrier phase, which cancels both satellite and receiver clock errors.
- [x] The **integer ambiguity** $N$ (in cycles) is the *unknown number of whole carrier wavelengths* and must be *fixed to an integer* to achieve cm accuracy.
- [x] The **LAMBDA method** (Teunissen 1995) decorrelates and searches the integer lattice efficiently.
- [x] The **ratio test** (typically threshold = 3) decides *Fix* vs *Float*. A wrong fix is worse than no fix.
- [x] **Wavelengths to memorize:** $\lambda_{L1} = 19.03$ cm; $\lambda_{L2} = 24.42$ cm.
- [x] **Baseline limit:** $\sim 10$–$20$ km for single-baseline RTK; longer requires **Network RTK** (VRS, MAC, FKP).
- [x] **Cycle slips** (loss of phase lock) are detected using the *triple difference* (epoch-differenced DD).
- [x] **Accuracy model:** $\sigma_h = 1\,\text{cm} + 1\,\text{ppm} \cdot b$ (linear in baseline).
- [x] **PPM** means *1 mm of additional error per km of baseline*.
- [x] **Dual-frequency** (L1+L2) RTK enables *ionosphere-free* combinations and faster, more reliable ambiguity fixing.
- [x] **Single-frequency** RTK is cheaper but requires longer observation time and is more vulnerable to ionospheric storms.
- [x] **DGPS** uses *code*, RTK uses *carrier* — never confuse them.
- [x] **RTCM** is the standard message format (RTCM SC-104 v3.x) used to transmit base corrections.
- [x] **NTRIP** (Networked Transport of RTCM via Internet Protocol) streams corrections over cellular/internet for *Network RTK*.
- [x] For a **robot**, RTK is fused with **IMU + wheel odometry + LiDAR** in an EKF because RTK alone fails under *signal obstruction* (urban canyon, foliage, indoors).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GNSS Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT723 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GNSS Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GNSS Threats** are defined as any intentional, unintentional, or environmental phenomena that degrade, deny, falsify, or otherwise corrupt the ranging signals broadcast by Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou, IRNSS/NavIC, QZSS), causing the Position, Velocity, and Time (PVT) solution of a robot or autonomous platform to become inaccurate, unavailable, or untrustworthy.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** taxonomy, GNSS threats are broadly classified into three engineering categories:

| Category | Nature | Examples |
|----------|--------|----------|
| **Intentional Threats** | Deliberate malicious denial or deception | Jamming, Spoofing, Meaconing, Selective Denial (SD) |
| **Unintentional Threats** | Co-channel or adjacent interference from other systems | TV harmonics, radar, mobile base stations, DME/TACAN, personal privacy devices (PPDs) |
| **Environmental Threats** | Physical propagation anomalies | Ionospheric scintillation, tropospheric delay, multipath, signal blockage, solar radio bursts |

The standard safety-critical metric used by KTU examiners to quantify GNSS signal health is the **carrier-to-noise density ratio**, expressed in dB-Hz as **C/N₀ ≈ 35–45 dB-Hz for open-sky reception**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Orchestra Hall Problem**
>
> Imagine a robotic listener standing in the middle of a huge orchestra hall, trying to hear a faint flute playing at **−160 dBW** from the balcony. The flute is the real GNSS satellite. Now imagine:
> - A **loud rock band** is set up next to the listener → this is **Jamming** (drowns out the flute).
> - A **sound-alike impersonator** plays the flute's tune but with wrong notes → this is **Spoofing** (deceives the listener).
> - The **flute's echo from the marble walls** arrives a few milliseconds later → this is **Multipath**.
> - A **thunderstorm** distorts the air, making the flute sound "wavy" → this is **Ionospheric Scintillation**.
> - A **physical wall** is placed between listener and flute → this is **Signal Blockage / NLOS**.

The robot's GNSS receiver is a "blindfolded listener" that must keep extracting the correct musical note (pseudorange) from this hostile acoustic environment — that, in essence, is the engineering challenge of GNSS threat mitigation.

## 1.3 Why It Matters for Robotics

For **autonomous ground vehicles, UAVs, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and surgical robots**, a single spoofed or jammed GNSS fix can lead to:
- Lane-level mis-localization (>1 m error) → collision risk.
- Sudden position jumps → unsafe control commands.
- Loss of time synchronization → cascaded failure in multi-robot SLAM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A robot operating in **GNSS-Denied** or **GNSS-Degraded** environments is classified as operating in a **"Contested Navigation Regime"** (CNR). The course outcome **CO4** of OEBRT723 mandates that students must enumerate, classify, and counter CNR threats — a guaranteed 14-mark question in ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Threat Model

GNSS threats can be modeled as a four-layer stack, each layer targeted by a specific class of attack:

1. **Physical/RF Layer** — Jamming, wideband noise, pulsed interference.
2. **Signal Structure Layer** — Spoofing, meaconing, signal re-radiation.
3. **Propagation Layer** — Ionospheric/tropospheric anomalies, multipath.
4. **Receiver/Algorithmic Layer** — Code-carrier divergence, cycle slips, RAIM failure.

## 2.2 Jamming — The Most Common Threat

### 2.2.1 Definition
**Jamming** is the deliberate radiation of RF energy in the GNSS L-band (L1: 1575.42 MHz, L2: 1227.60 MHz, L5: 1176.45 MHz, etc.) to reduce the receiver's effective **C/N₀** below the acquisition/tracking threshold (~28 dB-Hz for coarse acquisition, ~33–35 dB-Hz for robust tracking).

### 2.2.2 Classification of Jammers

| Jammer Type | Bandwidth | Power Profile | Threat Level |
|-------------|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Narrowband / CW** | < 1 MHz | Tone, low power | Mild |
| **Wideband Gaussian** | ≈ 20–50 MHz (matches PRN code) | Matched to chip rate | High |
| **Pulsed** | Duty cycle 1–10% | High peak power | High in time |
| **Swept (Chirp)** | Sweeps L1 in μs | Aggressive | Severe |
| **Smart / Reactive** | Triggers on PRN | Surgical | Catastrophic |

### 2.2.3 Why Jamming Works — The Physics

A GPS C/A-code spreads 50 bps of navigation data over a **1.023 Mcps** code, giving a spreading bandwidth $B_c = 1.023$ MHz. The receiver **de-spreads** with a correlator, restoring the 50 Hz data. The jammer, however, only gets de-spread by the same factor, called the **Processing Gain**:

$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_c}{B_n}\right) \;\; \text{[dB]}$$

where $R_c$ is the chipping rate and $B_n$ is the post-correlation noise bandwidth (~50 Hz for the navigation data). For GPS L1 C/A, $G_p \approx 43$ dB. This means the jammer must be **10,000× stronger** than the satellite to deny tracking — a counter-intuitive and **frequently examined** KTU fact.

## 2.3 Spoofing — The Deceptive Threat

**Spoofing** is the broadcast of counterfeit GNSS-like signals structured to induce a victim's receiver to compute a false PVT solution while believing it is genuine. It is **far more dangerous** than jamming because:
- The receiver reports high C/N₀ (signal looks "healthy").
- No alarm is raised unless consistency checks are run.
- The robot silently follows the spoofer's chosen trajectory.

### 2.3.1 Spoofing Sub-Types
- **Simplistic Spoofing** — single antenna, brute-force overdrive of real signals.
- **Intermediate Spoofing** — matched power, code-phase alignment, requires precise time/position knowledge.
- **Sophisticated Spoofing** — multiple-antenna phased array that gradually "pulls" the tracking loops.
- **Meaconing** — pure record-and-replay; no knowledge of signal content required. Latency delay is the only fingerprint.
- **Nulling Spoofing** — transmit a null toward victim while spoofing from another direction (requires CRPA).

## 2.4 Multipath & NLOS

Multipath occurs when a signal arrives at the receiver via **two or more paths** (direct + reflected from ground, buildings, water). The error magnitude is bounded by approximately **1.5 × C/A chip length**, i.e., for L1 C/A:

$$\epsilon_{mp} \;\le\; 1.5 \times \frac{c}{R_c} = 1.5 \times \frac{3\times 10^8}{1.023\times 10^6} \approx 440 \text{ m}$$

Modern receivers use **narrow correlator spacing** (d < 0.1 chip), **double-delta correlators**, and **MEDLL (Multipath Estimating Delay Lock Loop)** to reduce this to **< 5 m** in code and **< 1 cm** in carrier phase.

## 2.5 Ionospheric Scintillation

Rapid electron-density irregularities in the F-layer (typically 200–400 km altitude) cause **amplitude fading** (S4 index) and **phase scintillation** (σφ). Worst affected regions: **equatorial belt (India is in the anomaly crest) and polar regions**. Affects both L1 and L2 differently → can break ionosphere-free combinations.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam grade**. Master the boxed ones.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Free-space path loss (Friis) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | d in km, f in MHz → dB |
| 2 | Received GNSS power (L1) | $P_r \approx -160$ | dBW at Earth's surface (open sky) |
| 3 | Processing gain | $G_p = 10\log_{10}(R_c / B_n)$ | dB; **C/A → ≈ 43 dB** |
| 4 | J/S ratio at correlator output | $(J/S)_{out} = (J/S)_{in} - G_p$ | dB |
| 5 | Required J/S to deny tracking | $(J/S)_{req} = \frac{C/N_0 \cdot B_n - P_r \cdot G_p \cdot L}{P_j \cdot G_j}$ | dB |
| 6 | Carrier-to-noise under jamming | $\left(\tfrac{C}{N_0}\right)_{eff} = -10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{-\tfrac{C}{N_0}/10} + 10^{-\tfrac{J}{S \cdot G_p}/10}\right)$ | dB-Hz |
| 7 | Jam margin | $M_j = G_p - (L_{sys} + (S/N)_{req})$ | dB |
| 8 | Pseudorange multipath bound | $\epsilon_{mp} \le 1.5 \cdot c/R_c$ | meters; L1 C/A → ≈ 440 m |
| 9 | Ionospheric range delay (1st order) | $I = \frac{40.3}{f^2}\int N_e \, ds$ | meters; $N_e$ = electron density (el/m³) |
| 10 | S4 scintillation index | $S_4 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\langle (S-\bar S)^2\rangle}{\bar S^{\,2}}}$ | dimensionless; > 0.5 = strong |
| 11 | User Equivalent Range Error (UERE) | $\sigma_{UERE} = \sqrt{\sigma_{clock}^2 + \sigma_{eph}^2 + \sigma_{iono}^2 + \sigma_{tropo}^2 + \sigma_{mp}^2 + \sigma_{rx}^2}$ | meters; RSS combination |
| 12 | Dilution of Precision | $DOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^{T}H)^{-1}}$ | dimensionless; GDOP/PDOP/HDOP/VDOP/TDOP |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous trucking and mining** uses **CRPA (Controlled Reception Pattern Antenna)** to place spatial nulls on jammers, achieving **> 30 dB** of additional J/S rejection.
- **Aviation** mandates RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) to flag spoofed or faulty satellites — failure to do so is a recurring black-box finding in ICAO audits.
- **Maritime** uses **SBAS (GAGAN in India)** to bound ionospheric threats, critical for Kerala's fishing-vessel fleet operating near the magnetic equator.
- **Robotic surgery and swarm UAVs** use **GNSS + INS tightly-coupled fusion** so a jam event simply drops into dead-reckoning for ~30 s with negligible drift.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Jammer Power at the Victim Receiver

**Problem (Typical KTU Part-B style):** A GPS L1 C/A receiver is located 10 km from a hostile jammer broadcasting wideband Gaussian noise centered at 1575.42 MHz with 20 MHz bandwidth. The receiver antenna has gain 0 dBic. The satellite is on the horizon (worst case, $G_{s,slant} \approx -3$ dBic). Compute the jammer EIRP required to deny code tracking if the receiver's tracking threshold is **(C/N₀)ₑff = 30 dB-Hz**.

### Step 1 — Write the satellite received power

The GPS L1 C/A minimum specified received power is **−160 dBW** at a 0 dBic antenna with 5° elevation. Apply slant correction:

$$P_r = P_{sat} + G_{slant} = -160 \text{ dBW} + (-3 \text{ dB}) = -163 \text{ dBW}$$

### Step 2 — Write the jammer received power

Using Friis transmission:

$$P_{j,r} = EIRP_j + G_{r,j} - FSPL$$

For 10 km at 1575.42 MHz:

$$\begin{aligned}
FSPL &= 20\log_{10}(10) + 20\log_{10}(1575.42) + 32.44 \\
     &= 20 + 64.95 + 32.44 \\
     &= 117.39 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

The jammer antenna is isotropic toward the victim, so $G_{r,j} = 0$ dBic. Therefore:

$$P_{j,r} = EIRP_j - 117.39 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 3 — Compute J/S at the correlator input

$$\left(\frac{J}{S}\right)_{in} = P_{j,r} - P_r = \left(EIRP_j - 117.39\right) - (-163) = EIRP_j + 45.61 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 4 — Subtract the processing gain

For L1 C/A, $R_c = 1.023$ MHz and $B_n \approx 50$ Hz:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_p &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.023 \times 10^6}{50}\right) \\
    &= 10\log_{10}(20460) \\
    &\approx 43.10 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

After de-spreading, the **post-correlation J/S** becomes:

$$\left(\frac{J}{S}\right)_{out} = \left(\frac{J}{S}\right)_{in} - G_p = EIRP_j + 45.61 - 43.10 = EIRP_j + 2.51 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the tracking threshold

For the receiver to lose lock, the effective C/N₀ must drop to 30 dB-Hz. The natural thermal C/N₀ for L1 C/A is ~45 dB-Hz. Thermal S/N in the 50 Hz loop:

$$S/N_0 = 45 - 10\log_{10}(50) = 45 - 17 = 28 \text{ dB}$$

We need the post-correlation **J/S to exceed this by at least the SNR margin** (say 3 dB, to ensure loss of lock):

$$\left(\frac{J}{S}\right)_{out,req} = 28 + 3 = 31 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for EIRP

$$EIRP_j + 2.51 = 31 \;\Rightarrow\; EIRP_j = 28.49 \text{ dBW} \approx 707 \text{ W (EIRP)}$$

### Step 7 — Sanity check and conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A jammer with **~700 W EIRP** is sufficient to deny L1 C/A tracking at 10 km. A cheap **1 W PPD (Personal Privacy Device)** produces ~1 W EIRP and thus denies open-sky tracking at only **~50 m** — confirmed by numerous KTU laboratory experiments using Software-Defined Radios (USRP B210).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective C/N₀ Under Combined Jammer and Thermal Noise

We treat the post-correlation jammer as an additional white Gaussian noise source. Total noise power spectral density at the correlator output:

$$N_{0,tot} = N_0 + \frac{J_0}{G_p}$$

where $J_0$ is the jammer PSD at the receiver input. The **effective C/N₀** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{eff} &= -10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{-\tfrac{C}{N_0}/10} + \frac{J/S}{G_p \cdot 10}\right) \\
&= -10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{-4.5} + 10^{-\left(\tfrac{J}{S}_{in} - 43.1\right)/10}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical example:** If $J/S_{in} = 30$ dB at the input, then $J/S$ in the loop = $30 - 43.1 = -13.1$ dB. With $C/N_0 = 45$ dB-Hz thermal:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{eff} = -10\log_{10}(10^{-4.5} + 10^{1.31}) = -10\log_{10}(0.0000316 + 20.4) \approx -13.1 \text{ dB-Hz}$$

The receiver immediately loses lock — a **classical KTU counter-example** showing that *more than ~30 dB of J/S at L1 antenna port is sufficient to deny even a CRPA-less receiver*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Threat vs Counter-Measure

| Threat | Detection Signature | Counter-Measure | Limitation |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------|------------|
| **Narrowband jammer** | Spectral peak in FFT | Adaptive notch filter | Cannot remove wideband |
| **Wideband jammer** | C/N₀ drop, no PRN peaks | CRPA nulling, beamforming | Needs 4+ antenna elements |
| **Pulsed jammer** | Burst blanking statistics | Pulse-blanking front-end | Distorts pseudorange |
| **Meaconing** | Constant delay on all sats | Clock-drift + IMU consistency | Defeated by nulling arrays |
| **Sophisticated spoofing** | Power-step, code-carrier divergence | Cryptographic authentication (Galileo OS-NMA, GPS Chimera) | Requires receiver upgrade |
| **Multipath** | Correlator tail asymmetry | Double-delta, MEDLL, choke-ring antenna | Cannot remove static multipath |
| **Ionospheric scintillation** | S4 > 0.5, σφ > 0.3 rad | Multi-frequency (L1/L2/L5), tight INS coupling | Cost, calibration |
| **Tropospheric delay** | Elevation-correlated bias | Saastamoinen / Hopfield model with met data | Needs surface sensors |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between **jamming** and **spoofing** in GNSS with a real-world analogy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Jamming is the **denial** of GNSS service by flooding the L-band with RF noise, causing the receiver to lose lock — analogous to a loud rock band drowning out a flute. Spoofing is the **deception** of the receiver by broadcasting counterfeit satellite-like signals structured to make it compute a false PVT solution — analogous to an impersonator playing the same tune with wrong notes. The key engineering distinction is that **jamming is detectable** (C/N₀ collapses, alarms fire) while **spoofing is silent** (C/N₀ may even rise, no alarm fires unless cryptographic or consistency checks are present). (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define **Processing Gain** for a GPS L1 C/A signal and compute its numerical value.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Processing gain is the amount by which a spread-spectrum receiver enhances the desired signal relative to narrowband interference after de-spreading. It is given by:

$$G_p = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_c}{B_n}\right)$$

For L1 C/A: $R_c = 1.023$ Mcps, $B_n \approx 50$ Hz → $G_p \approx 43$ dB. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

> **(a)** Classify GNSS threats into intentional, unintentional, and environmental categories. Provide **two examples** for each and describe their physical origin. **\[7 marks\]**
>
> **(b)** A GPS L1 C/A receiver experiences a −20 dB drop in effective C/N₀ due to a wideband jammer. If the thermal C/N₀ was 45 dB-Hz, compute (i) the post-correlation J/S, (ii) the required jammer EIRP at 5 km distance assuming 0 dBic antennas, and (iii) explain the engineering significance of the **jam margin**. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification (7 marks)**

| Category | Example 1 (Origin) | Example 2 (Origin) |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Intentional** | CW Jamming — battery-powered PPD emitting tone at 1575.42 MHz | Spoofing — SDR-based counterfeit PRN generator aligned to victim |
| **Unintentional** | DME/TACAN pulse at 962–1213 MHz leaking into L2 | TV harmonics at L1 from improperly filtered transmitters |
| **Environmental** | Ionospheric scintillation — F-layer electron irregularities | Multipath from ground reflection under foliage |

(2 marks classification + 2 marks for each correct example pair = 6 marks; 1 mark for physical origin explanation)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Post-correlation J/S (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right) &= -20 \text{ dB} \\
\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{eff} &= 45 - 20 = 25 \text{ dB-Hz} \\
\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{thermal}^{-1} &= 10^{-4.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{-5} \\
\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{eff}^{-1} &= 10^{-2.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{-3} \\
\left(\frac{J}{S}\right)_{out} &= \left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{eff}^{-1} - \left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right)_{thermal}^{-1} \\
                             &= 3.16 \times 10^{-3} - 3.16 \times 10^{-5} \\
                             &= 3.13 \times 10^{-3} \\
\left(\frac{J}{S}\right)_{out,dB} &= 10\log_{10}(3.13 \times 10^{-3}) = -25.05 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Required jammer EIRP (3 marks)**

At 5 km, $f = 1575.42$ MHz:

$$\begin{aligned}
FSPL &= 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(1575.42) + 32.44 \\
     &= 13.98 + 64.95 + 32.44 = 111.37 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $J/S_{in} = J/S_{out} + G_p = -25.05 + 43.10 = 18.05$ dB.

$$EIRP_j = J/S_{in} + P_{r,sat} + FSPL = 18.05 - 160 + 111.37 = -30.58 \text{ dBW (EIRP)}$$

This corresponds to **~0.87 mW EIRP** — confirming the **practical vulnerability** of GPS to even low-power jammers.

**(iii) Jam Margin (2 marks)**

The **jam margin** $M_j$ is the additional dB of jamming power the receiver can tolerate before losing lock. It is:

$$M_j = G_p - \left[L_{sys} + \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{req}\right]$$

Engineering significance: it quantifies the **safety headroom** of a receiver design; modern CRPA-equipped receivers achieve $M_j > 35$ dB.

---

### Question B — **[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> **(a)** With neat block diagrams, describe the **meaconing attack** on GNSS. Why is it considered "low-cost" yet dangerous for **autonomous ground robots**? **\[7 marks\]**
>
> **(b)** Explain the working principle of a **Controlled Reception Pattern Antenna (CRPA)** for anti-jamming. Show how it forms a **spatial null** toward a single jammer using a 4-element uniform linear array. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Meaconing (7 marks)**

Meaconing is a record-and-replay attack: an adversary captures genuine GNSS signals with a survey-grade antenna, amplifies them, and re-radiates from a different location with a delay. (2 marks)

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
```
[Real GNSS Satellites] → [Victim Area] → [Attacker Antenna + LNA + Delay Line + PA] → [Replay] → [Victim Robot]
```

**Why dangerous for ground robots (2 marks):**
- No sophisticated SDR needed (cost < ₹5,000 in 2024).
- All PRNs remain "authentic" — cryptographic OS-NMA does not flag it.
- Robot believes it is at the attacker's antenna, not its true location → lane-level mis-localization.

**(b) CRPA (7 marks)**

A CRPA uses N antenna elements (typically 4–7) feeding a digital beamformer. Each element $i$ produces signal $s_i(t) = s(t) e^{j k \mathbf{\hat r}_j \cdot \mathbf{x}_i} + n_i(t)$, where $\mathbf{\hat r}_j$ is the jammer's unit direction vector and $\mathbf{x}_i$ is the element position. (2 marks)

The beamformer weights $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N]^T$ are chosen to:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}} \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{R}_j \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{a}(\theta_{sat}) = 1$$

This is the **Minimum Variance Distortionless Response (MVDR)** problem. (2 marks)

For a 4-element ULA with $d = \lambda/2$, jamming from $30°$ elevation, $60°$ azimuth, the steering vector is $\mathbf{a}_j = [1, e^{-j\pi\sin\theta}, e^{-j2\pi\sin\theta}, e^{-j3\pi\sin\theta}]^T$. The optimum null-forming weights place a deep null (>30 dB) at the jammer direction while keeping the satellite at zenith at unity gain. (3 marks)

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Processing Gain** — Most students compute J/S at the antenna port and forget to subtract 43 dB of $G_p$ post-correlation. This **doubles the EIRP answer** and loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using L1 frequency incorrectly in FSPL** — Use $f$ in **MHz** for the constant 32.44, or $f$ in Hz for the 20log₁₀(c/4π). Mixing them is a **classic 1-mark error**.
> 3. **Confusing C/N₀ (dB-Hz) with S/N (dB)** — They differ by $10\log_{10}(B_n)$. Always convert explicitly.
> 4. **Treating multipath as a constant bias** — Multipath is *time-varying*; for a stationary robot it averages out, but for a moving robot the errors look like white noise. Examiners penalize students who say "multipath is constant."
> 5. **Skipping units in tabular answers** — Forgetting "dBW" or "dB-Hz" in the unit column of a numerical answer costs **0.5 mark** each.
> 6. **Not drawing the block diagram for meaconing** — Even a hand-drawn, neat ASCII block diagram carries **3 of 7 marks** in the model answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **GNSS Threats — Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024)**
>
> - **Three threat families**: Intentional, Unintentional, Environmental.
> - **Jamming metric**: J/S ratio; required to deny tracking ≈ 28–32 dB at correlator output.
> - **Processing Gain** $G_p \approx 43$ dB (L1 C/A) — *the single most important number*.
> - **Spoofing** is **silent**, **meaconing** is **delay-only**, **simplistic spoofing** is **brute-force**, **sophisticated** uses phased arrays.
> - **Multipath bound** for L1 C/A ≈ 1.5 chips ≈ 440 m; reduced to < 5 m by narrow correlators.
> - **Ionospheric delay** $\propto 1/f^2$ — L5 sees 1.65× less delay than L2; L1 sees 1.6× less than L2.
> - **Scintillation** is worst in the **equatorial anomaly crest** (Kerala coast, post-sunset, equinox months).
> - **CRPA** with ≥ 4 elements gives 25–35 dB jam rejection.
> - **OS-NMA** (Galileo) and **Chimera** (GPS) are the upcoming cryptographic spoofing defenses — frequently cited by KTU question papers since 2023.
> - **Tight INS/GNSS coupling** is the **only** practical defense against combined jamming + spoofing for mobile robots.
> - **Minimum 4 satellites** with good GDOP < 4 are required for any threat-resilient PVT; RAIM is mandatory in aviation.
